topics.title: Introduction to Electric and Hybrid Vehicles: History and Evolution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT631 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Electric and Hybrid Vehicles: History and Evolution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Electric Vehicle (EV):** A vehicle that is propelled by one or more electric motors, drawing energy from a rechargeable on-board electrical energy storage system (typically a battery pack), and whose primary or sole source of traction does **not** involve an internal combustion engine (ICE) burning fossil fuels.

**Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV):** A vehicle that combines two or more distinct power sources — conventionally, a heat engine (ICE) and one or more electric machines — coupled through a power-split device to achieve propulsion, where energy recovery (regenerative braking) and electric-only operation are integrated into the drivetrain.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Formal Classification Vocabulary**
> KTU examiners expect strict use of the following classification terms in any 14-mark descriptive answer:
> - **BEV** — Battery Electric Vehicle (100% electric, grid-charged)
> - **HEV** — Hybrid Electric Vehicle (no external charging)
> - **PHEV** — Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle (external charging + ICE)
> - **FCEV** — Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle (hydrogen-fed)
> - **MHEV** — Mild Hybrid Electric Vehicle (48 V architecture, no e-drive)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine the **drivetrain of a vehicle as a human pushing a swing**:
- An **ICE vehicle** is like a person pushing the swing with constant force — it works but cannot recapture the swing's back-motion energy; every push burns fresh fuel.
- A **Hybrid vehicle** is like a person who pushes the swing *and* gently catches it on the return, storing a portion of the energy to deliver the next push — efficient, but still uses muscle.
- A **pure Electric Vehicle** is like a motorised swing — the push comes from a battery (your pre-charged energy bank), and the "swing-back" energy is also recaptured into the battery.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in EV/HEV Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Performance Metrics — memorize the bolded units; they appear in every KTU numerical.**
> - **Specific Energy of Battery: $E_{sp}$ in Wh/kg**
> - **Energy Density: $E_{den}$ in Wh/L**
> - **Range: $R$ in km**
> - **Fuel Economy Equivalence: MPGe (Miles Per Gallon Equivalent) where 1 gallon gasoline ≡ 33.7 kWh**
> - **Regenerative Braking Efficiency: $\eta_{regen}$ in %**
> - **State of Charge (SoC): dimensionless, 0 to 1 (or 0% to 100%)**
> - **C-rate: charge/discharge current normalised to battery capacity (1C = full discharge in 1 hour)**

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

The introduction and historical evolution form the **theoretical foundation** of the entire PEMUT631 course. KTU examiners frequently use this topic to test **CO1 (Apply knowledge of mathematics and engineering to evaluate EV/HEV systems)** through either a Part-A (3-mark) definition or as a sub-part of a 14-mark Module-1 question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Historical Evolution — Chronological Framework

The evolution of electric and hybrid vehicles is best understood as **three distinct eras** marked by technological breakthroughs and socio-economic drivers.

#### Era 1 — The Pioneering Age (1830 – 1900)

- **1832 – 1839:** Robert Anderson (Scotland) builds the first crude electric carriage using non-rechargeable primary cells.
- **1859:** Gaston Planté invents the **lead-acid battery** — the first rechargeable electrochemical storage device. This is the watershed moment for EVs.
- **1881:** Gustave Trouvé (France) demonstrates the first electrically propelled tricycle at the Paris International Exhibition of Electricity.
- **1884:** Thomas Parker (England) builds the first practical production EV in London.
- **1890s:** Electric taxis operate in New York, London, and Berlin. EVs were *preferred* over early ICE vehicles because they were quiet, did not require hand-cranking, and produced no exhaust.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why EVs initially dominated:** Early ICE cars (Karl Benz's 1886 patent-MPV) required manual hand-cranking, were noisy, vibrated heavily, and emitted foul smells. Women especially favoured electric cars (the "Ladies' Electric" by Peugeot in 1894).

#### Era 2 — Decline and Dormancy (1910 – 1960)

- **1908:** Henry Ford introduces the **Model T** with mass-production assembly line — ICE vehicle price drops to ~$260 (1925) versus ~$1,750 for an equivalent EV.
- **1912:** Cadillac introduces the electric starter motor for ICE vehicles, eliminating the hand-crank — this single invention removed the principal advantage of EVs.
- **1920 – 1930:** Texas and other US states discover vast petroleum reserves → cheap gasoline → ICE vehicles become dominant.
- **1935 – 1960:** EV development virtually halted. Lead-acid batteries cannot match the energy density of gasoline.

#### Era 3 — The Modern Renaissance (1960 – Present)

- **1970s:** The **1973 OPEC Oil Crisis** triggers renewed interest in alternative propulsion.
- **1971:** Victor Wouk builds the first modern hybrid prototype (Buick Skylark with a 20-kW electric motor), but it fails to attract commercial interest.
- **1976:** US Congress passes the **Electric and Hybrid Vehicle Research, Development, and Demonstration Act**.
- **1990:** **GM Impact** concept car (later EV1) is unveiled at the LA Auto Show.
- **1997:** **Toyota Prius** becomes the world's first mass-produced hybrid, launched in Japan.
- **1999:** Honda Insight (first hybrid sold in the USA).
- **2008:** **Tesla Roadster** — first highway-legal serial-production EV to use lithium-ion battery cells; demonstrates >320 km range.
- **2010:** **Nissan Leaf** and **Chevy Volt** launch the mass-market EV era.
- **2015:** Volkswagen Dieselgate scandal accelerates global EV policy.
- **2015 – Present:** Paris Agreement, EU 2035 ICE ban, India's FAME-II policy, Norway's >80% EV market share.

### 2.2 Classification of Electrified Vehicles — Decision Matrix

| Vehicle Type | Energy Source | External Charging | Electric-Only Drive | Typical Battery Size |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **BEV** | Grid electricity | Yes | Yes (always) | 25 – 100 kWh |
| **HEV** | Petrol + Battery (regen only) | No | Short bursts | 1 – 2 kWh |
| **PHEV** | Grid + Petrol | Yes | Yes (up to ~50 km) | 8 – 18 kWh |
| **FCEV** | Hydrogen tank | Hydrogen refuelling | Yes (always) | 1 – 2 kWh buffer |
| **MHEV** | Petrol + 48 V battery | No | No (assist only) | < 1 kWh |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These five equations are the **foundational formulas** examiners expect you to apply in any Module-1 numerical. Memorize the symbol, the units, and the boundary conditions.

| # | Equation (LaTeX) | Symbol Legend | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{bat} = V_{nom} \cdot Q \cdot \frac{SoC}{100}$ | $V_{nom}$ = nominal voltage (V), $Q$ = capacity (Ah), $SoC$ in % | Computes usable energy stored in a battery pack. |
| 2 | $E_{sp} = \frac{E_{bat}}{m_{bat}}$ | $E_{sp}$ in Wh/kg, $m_{bat}$ in kg | Compares battery chemistries; current Li-ion ≈ 150 – 250 Wh/kg. |
| 3 | $R = \frac{E_{bat}}{\epsilon_{veh}}$ | $\epsilon_{veh}$ = energy consumption rate (kWh/km) | Estimates the all-electric range of a BEV. |
| 4 | $MPGe = \frac{33.7 \text{ kWh}}{\epsilon_{veh} \text{ in kWh/mile}}$ | US-EPA equivalence constant 33.7 | Converts EV efficiency to gasoline-equivalent fuel economy. |
| 5 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{bat} \cdot \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{transmission}$ | Each $\eta$ is a dimensionless fraction | Tank-to-wheel efficiency comparison: EV $\approx 77\%$, ICE $\approx 12\%-30\%$. |

### 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Historical Milestone

- **Lead-acid invention (1859)** → solved the "how to store electricity" problem → made EVs physically possible.
- **Electric starter (1912)** → removed the **only** advantage EVs had over ICE → triggered the 50-year EV dormancy.
- **Oil crisis (1973)** → exposed the **strategic vulnerability** of oil dependence → motivated government R&D funding.
- **Li-ion commercialisation (1991 – Sony)** → raised $E_{sp}$ from $\sim$ 40 Wh/kg (lead-acid) to $\sim$ 150 Wh/kg → made EVs competitive on range and weight.
- **Tesla Roadster (2008)** → proved EVs can be **desirable** sports cars, not just golf carts → broke consumer scepticism.
- **Government policy (2015 – 2025)** → subsidies, CO₂ mandates, ICE bans → forced OEM commitment at scale.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Energy Stored in a Battery Pack

**Statement (KTU 2024 CO1):** Derive the expression for the usable energy stored in a rechargeable battery pack as a function of its terminal voltage, capacity, and state of charge.

**Given:**
- $V_{nom}$ = nominal terminal voltage of the battery pack (V)
- $Q$ = nameplate capacity of the pack (Ah)
- $SoC$ = present state of charge (expressed as a percentage, 0 – 100)

**Step 1 — Recall the definition of Ampere-hour capacity.**

A battery of capacity $Q$ Ah can deliver a constant current of $I$ amperes for $t = Q/I$ hours. The total charge moved in coulombs is:

$$
q = I \cdot t = I \cdot \frac{Q}{I} = Q \text{ Ah} \equiv Q \cdot 3600 \text{ C}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the total chemical energy.**

The electrical energy $E$ delivered at a constant terminal voltage $V_{nom}$ is voltage × charge:

$$
E_{total} = V_{nom} \cdot q = V_{nom} \cdot Q \cdot 3600 \text{ (in Joules)}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to the engineering-friendly unit of Watt-hours.**

Since $1 \text{ Wh} = 3600 \text{ J}$, the energy in Wh simplifies to:

$$
E_{total} = V_{nom} \cdot Q \text{ Wh}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the State-of-Charge reduction.**

A battery is never fully discharged in practice (depth-of-discharge, DoD, is restricted to protect cell life). The **usable** energy is proportional to the current $SoC$:

$$
E_{usable} = V_{nom} \cdot Q \cdot \frac{SoC}{100}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This is why a "60 kWh" Tesla battery does *not* actually deliver 60 kWh to the wheels — typically only ~85% is usable ($SoC$ window 5% to 90%).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: All-Electric Range of a BEV

**Statement (KTU 2024 CO1):** Derive the range equation for a battery electric vehicle given its pack energy and average energy consumption rate.

**Given:**
- $E_{bat}$ = usable battery energy (kWh)
- $\epsilon_{veh}$ = average vehicle energy consumption (kWh/km)

**Step 1 — Define the energy consumption rate.**

Energy consumption rate $\epsilon_{veh}$ is the electrical energy drawn from the battery per kilometre travelled. It is determined experimentally and depends on speed, payload, road gradient, and auxiliary loads.

**Step 2 — Write the energy balance over a journey of range $R$.**

Total energy consumed = Consumption rate × Distance:

$$
E_{bat} = \epsilon_{veh} \cdot R
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the range $R$.**

$$
R = \frac{E_{bat}}{\epsilon_{veh}}
$$

**Step 4 — Plug in a numerical example for KTU examination style.**

A Tata Nexon EV has a $E_{bat} = 30.2$ kWh usable pack. Assume $\epsilon_{veh} = 0.125$ kWh/km (typical Indian urban drive cycle). Compute the range:

$$
R = \frac{30.2}{0.125} = 241.6 \text{ km}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Trick:** KTU examiners often ask the *reverse* — given a target range, find the required pack size. Rearranging: $E_{bat} = \epsilon_{veh} \cdot R$. This is the standard sizing equation used by EV design engineers.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Why Each Era Ended/Began

| Era | Dominant Technology | Bottleneck | Event That Ended/Started It |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1830 – 1900 | Lead-acid EV | Low energy density, no recharge infrastructure | Discovery of Texas oil, Model T mass production |
| 1910 – 1960 | ICE / Petrol | Oil dependence, urban smog | 1973 OPEC oil embargo |
| 1970 – 1995 | Experimental HEV | Lead-acid batteries, high cost | Sony commercialises Li-ion (1991) |
| 1997 – 2010 | Prius + EV1 | Limited range, niche market | Tesla Roadster proves Li-ion viability (2008) |
| 2010 – Present | Li-ion BEV/PHEV | Cost, charging infrastructure, policy | Government mandates, climate policy, cost parity with ICE |

### 3.4 Derivation: Tank-to-Wheel Efficiency Comparison

**Step 1 — Define overall drivetrain efficiency.**

For any vehicle, overall efficiency is the product of the individual subsystem efficiencies between the energy source and the wheels.

$$
\eta_{overall} = \eta_{source} \cdot \eta_{conv} \cdot \eta_{trans} \cdot \eta_{motor}
$$

**Step 2 — Insert the ICE values (typical).**

$$
\eta_{ICE} = 0.30 \cdot 1.00 \cdot 0.95 \cdot 0.95 \approx 0.27 \;\; (27\%)
$$

**Step 3 — Insert the BEV values.**

$$
\eta_{BEV} = 0.85 \cdot 0.90 \cdot 0.95 \cdot 0.92 \approx 0.67 \;\; (67\%)
$$

**Step 4 — Add grid-generation efficiency (well-to-tank).**

If the electricity comes from a thermal power station with $\eta_{gen} = 0.40$, the **well-to-wheel** EV efficiency becomes:

$$
\eta_{BEV,well-to-wheel} = 0.40 \cdot 0.67 \approx 0.27 \;\; (27\%)
$$

> **Insight:** Even with fossil-fuel-generated electricity, the well-to-wheel efficiency of an EV is **comparable** to an ICE. With renewable grid mix (solar/wind), it rises above **70%**, decisively beating ICE.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question, Module 1]**

**Q1. (3 marks)** *Define an Electric Vehicle (EV) and a Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV). State two key differences between them.*

**Model Answer:**
An Electric Vehicle (EV) is one in which traction is provided exclusively by one or more electric motors, drawing energy from a rechargeable on-board battery. A Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV) combines an internal combustion engine (ICE) with an electric motor; the battery is recharged only by the engine and regenerative braking, with no external plug-in charging.

**Two key differences:**
1. **Energy Source:** A BEV draws all its energy from the grid via a charger; an HEV draws energy from petrol, supplemented by recovered braking energy.
2. **Electric-Only Range:** A BEV can travel 200 – 600 km on electric power; an HEV operates in electric mode only for short bursts (typically 1 – 2 km) below ~40 km/h.

**Q2. (3 marks)** *Mention any three historical milestones in the evolution of electric vehicles.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **1859** — Gaston Planté invented the rechargeable lead-acid battery, making on-board energy storage feasible.
2. **1997** — Toyota launched the Prius, the world's first mass-produced hybrid electric vehicle.
3. **2008** — Tesla Motors released the Roadster, the first highway-legal serial-production EV powered by lithium-ion cells, with a range exceeding 320 km per charge.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style, Module 1 Choice Question]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) (7 marks)** *Explain in detail the historical evolution of electric and hybrid vehicles, dividing the timeline into three distinct eras. Mention at least two landmark events per era.*

**(b) (7 marks)** *A BEV has a battery pack rated at 60 kWh nominal capacity. The vehicle's nominal voltage is 400 V and the nameplate capacity is 150 Ah. The Battery Management System (BMS) limits the usable state-of-charge window to 10% – 90%. (i) Calculate the actual usable energy. (ii) If the average energy consumption is 0.16 kWh/km, estimate the all-electric range.*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Detailed Historical Evolution — 7 marks allocation:**

*Era 1 — Pioneering Age (1830 – 1900):* 2 marks
- 1832 – 1839: Robert Anderson's first electric carriage.
- 1859: Planté invents the rechargeable lead-acid battery.
- 1881: Trouvé's electric tricycle, first public EV demonstration.
- 1890s: EVs outsold ICE cars in US cities because of quietness and ease of use.

*Era 2 — Decline and Dormancy (1910 – 1960):* 2 marks
- 1908: Ford Model T mass production; ICE cars cost one-fifth of EVs.
- 1912: Cadillac's electric starter removes the hand-crank advantage of EVs.
- 1930s: Texas oil boom → cheap petrol → ICE dominance is total.

*Era 3 — Modern Renaissance (1970 – Present):* 3 marks
- 1973 OPEC oil crisis triggers global R&D in alternative propulsion.
- 1991: Sony commercialises the lithium-ion cell — energy density jumps from 40 Wh/kg to 150 Wh/kg.
- 1997: Toyota Prius, the first mass-produced HEV.
- 2008: Tesla Roadster — first long-range BEV.
- 2010 – 2025: Government mandates (FAME-II India, EU 2035 ICE ban, US IRA) and cost parity drive mass adoption.

**(b) Numerical Solution — 7 marks allocation:**

**(i) Usable energy calculation:** (3 marks)

*Step 1:* Write the total nameplate energy.

$$
E_{total} = V_{nom} \cdot Q = 400 \text{ V} \times 150 \text{ Ah} = 60{,}000 \text{ Wh} = 60 \text{ kWh}
$$

*Step 2:* Identify the usable $SoC$ window. $SoC_{window} = 90\% - 10\% = 80\%$.

*Step 3:* Compute usable energy.

$$
E_{usable} = E_{total} \cdot \frac{SoC_{window}}{100} = 60 \times 0.80 = 48 \text{ kWh}
$$

**(ii) All-electric range:** (4 marks)

*Step 4:* Apply the range equation.

$$
R = \frac{E_{usable}}{\epsilon_{veh}} = \frac{48 \text{ kWh}}{0.16 \text{ kWh/km}} = 300 \text{ km}
$$

> **Final Answer:** Usable energy = **48 kWh**; All-electric range = **300 km**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A(b):**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Students often forget to apply the BMS SoC window and write 60 kWh directly into the range formula, giving $R = 60/0.16 = 375$ km. Always quote the *usable* energy.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (–1 mark):** Mixing units — using Wh in the numerator and kWh in the denominator. Convert *before* substituting.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Omitting the unit km in the final answer. Always write the unit explicitly.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a) (7 marks)** *Compare the four major categories of electrified vehicles (BEV, HEV, PHEV, FCEV) in terms of energy source, charging method, electric-only range, and typical application.*

**(b) (7 marks)** *A student wants to convert a petrol car (fuel economy 18 km/L, tank capacity 40 L) into an equivalent battery electric vehicle. Using the EPA MPGe equivalence (1 gallon gasoline = 33.7 kWh), determine the minimum battery pack size required to match the petrol car's full-tank range. Assume the BEV consumes 0.15 kWh/km.*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table — 7 marks allocation:**

| Parameter | BEV | HEV | PHEV | FCEV |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy Source** | Grid electricity | Petrol + regen | Grid + Petrol | Hydrogen |
| **External Charging** | Yes (AC or DC) | No | Yes (AC only) | H₂ refuelling station |
| **Electric-Only Range** | 200 – 600 km | 1 – 2 km | 30 – 80 km | 400 – 650 km |
| **Refuelling Time** | 30 min (DC fast) | 2 min (petrol) | 2 min (petrol) | 5 min (H₂) |
| **Typical Application** | Urban + intercity | City commuter | Mixed-use family car | Long-haul, commercial |
| **Tailpipe Emission** | Zero | Low (still petrol) | Low (still petrol) | Zero (only water) |

*Mark split:* 1 mark per row × 6 rows = 6 marks, plus 1 mark for a clear introductory sentence defining each type.

**(b) Numerical Solution — 7 marks allocation:**

*Step 1:* Compute the petrol car's full-tank range.

$$
R_{petrol} = 18 \text{ km/L} \times 40 \text{ L} = 720 \text{ km}
$$

*Step 2:* Compute the energy content of the petrol tank. A US gallon = 3.785 L, so 40 L = 10.57 gallons. The energy content of 10.57 gallons of gasoline in kWh:

$$
E_{petrol} = 10.57 \text{ gallons} \times 33.7 \text{ kWh/gallon} = 356.2 \text{ kWh}
$$

*Step 3:* Compute the BEV energy required for the same range at 0.15 kWh/km.

$$
E_{BEV,required} = R_{petrol} \times \epsilon_{veh} = 720 \times 0.15 = 108 \text{ kWh}
$$

*Step 4:* State the final answer. (1 mark for contextual statement)

> **Final Answer:** Minimum battery pack size = **108 kWh** (with BMS usable window of 80%, the nameplate pack would be ≈ **135 kWh**).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B(b):**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (–3 marks):** Some students use the *petrol energy* (356 kWh) as the battery size. The question is about a BEV with its own consumption rate, so you must multiply range × consumption. Read the question carefully.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (–2 marks):** Forgetting that 1 US gallon = 3.785 L; some students assume 4.5 L (which is the UK/imperial gallon) and get the wrong energy value.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Not including units in the final answer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1, Introduction & History**

- **Three Eras:** Pioneering (1830 – 1900) → Dormancy (1910 – 1960) → Renaissance (1970 – Present).
- **Five Vehicle Types:** BEV, HEV, PHEV, FCEV, MHEV — know charging, range, and application for each.
- **Five Watershed Dates to Memorize:**
  1. **1859** — Planté lead-acid battery.
  2. **1908** — Ford Model T → ICE dominance.
  3. **1973** — OPEC oil crisis.
  4. **1997** — Toyota Prius (first mass HEV).
  5. **2008** — Tesla Roadster (first long-range Li-ion BEV).
- **Two Master Equations to Memorize:**
  - Energy: $E_{usable} = V_{nom} \cdot Q \cdot \frac{SoC}{100}$
  - Range: $R = \dfrac{E_{usable}}{\epsilon_{veh}}$
- **One Equivalence Constant:** 1 US gallon gasoline ≡ **33.7 kWh** (for MPGe).
- **One Efficiency Comparison:** BEV tank-to-wheel ≈ **77%**; ICE ≈ **12 – 30%**.
- **KTU Examination Hot Phrases:** "Explain the historical evolution," "Classify electrified vehicles," "Compute the all-electric range," "State the significance of [year] in EV history."
- **Examiner Favourite Definition:** "An EV is propelled by electric motors drawing energy from a rechargeable on-board storage device." Memorize verbatim.
- **One Common Student Trap:** A "60 kWh" battery is *not* a 60 kWh usable source — the BMS SoC window typically restricts usable energy to ~80% of nameplate.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

Basic Components and Architecture of Electric and Hybrid Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT631 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Components and Architecture of Electric and Hybrid Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Electric Vehicle (EV) — Formal Definition

An **Electric Vehicle (EV)** is a road vehicle whose propulsion energy is derived wholly or partially from electrical energy stored in an on-board energy storage system (ESS), converted into mechanical work by one or more **electric traction motors** through a controlled **power electronic converter**.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** (PEMUT631, Module 1), EVs are classified based on the degree of electrification and the source of electrical energy:

| Class | Full Form | Energy Source |
|---|---|---|
| **BEV** | Battery Electric Vehicle | Battery only |
| **HEV** | Hybrid Electric Vehicle | Battery + IC Engine |
| **PHEV** | Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle | Battery (grid-chargeable) + ICE |
| **FCEV** | Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle | Hydrogen Fuel Cell + Battery |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim style):** *"A vehicle in which the propulsion force is supplied wholly or in part by an electric machine fed from a rechargeable on-board electrical energy storage device forms an Electric or Hybrid Electric Vehicle."*

---

## 1.2 Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV) — Formal Definition

A **Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV)** is defined as a vehicle that employs **two or more distinct energy sources / energy converters**, of which at least one is reversible (electrical), working in conjunction through a power-branching mechanism to deliver traction power.

The most common combination is the **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) + Electric Motor + Battery**.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Modern Smartwatch vs. Analog Watch"**
> 
> - A **pure ICE vehicle** is like an **analog watch** — one mechanism (a spring/mainspring) drives everything. When the fuel runs out, it dies completely.
> - A **Hybrid** is like a **smartwatch with kinetic charging** — it has a battery (electronic movement) **and** can harvest motion (mechanical charging). You get **two complementary paths** to keep the system alive, optimizing for efficiency.
> - A **pure BEV** is like a **fully digital smartwatch** — clean, single energy source (battery), zero emissions at the tailpipe, but limited range until recharged.
> 
> In all cases, the **"energy reservoir + converter + control logic"** triad is identical — only the proportions change.

---

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics Used in EV/HEV Design

The following physical constants and standard design metrics are universally referenced:

- **Air density** $\rho$ = **1.225 kg/m³** (at sea level, 15 °C)
- **Gravitational acceleration** $g$ = **9.81 m/s²**
- **Coefficient of rolling resistance** $\mu_{rr}$ ≈ **0.008 – 0.015** (radial tyres on asphalt)
- **Aerodynamic drag coefficient** $C_d$ ≈ **0.25 – 0.35** (modern sedan)
- **Frontal area** $A$ ≈ **1.8 – 2.5 m²**
- **State of Charge (SoC)** limits: typically **20 % – 80 %** for longevity
- **Energy density** of Li-ion ≈ **150 – 250 Wh/kg** (cell level)
- **Power density** of Li-ion ≈ **500 – 1500 W/kg**

---

## 1.5 Philosophical Shift from ICE to EV

The transition from a conventional ICE vehicle to an EV/HEV is not merely a **fuel substitution**; it is an **architectural re-engineering** of the entire powertrain:

| Subsystem | ICE Vehicle | Electric / Hybrid Vehicle |
|---|---|---|
| Energy Storage | Liquid fuel tank | Battery pack / Fuel cell / UC |
| Energy Conversion | Combustion (thermal → mechanical) | Electrochemical/Electrical → Mechanical |
| Torque Generation | High-speed reciprocating engine | Low-speed high-torque motor |
| Transmission | 5–10 speed gearbox | Single-speed reducer (mostly) |
| Emissions | Tailpipe CO₂, NOₓ, PM | Zero (BEV), reduced (HEV) |
| Efficiency | 25 – 35 % | 70 – 90 % (motor + inverter) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Building Blocks — Every EV/HEV Has Six Pillars

The powertrain of any EV or HEV can be decomposed into the following **six universal subsystems**:

1. **Energy Source (ES)** — Battery pack / Fuel cell / Ultra-capacitor / ICE-driven generator
2. **Energy Storage System (ESS)** — Rechargeable electrochemical store with BMS
3. **Power Electronic Converter (PEC)** — Inverter, DC-DC converter, onboard charger
4. **Electric Traction Motor (ETM)** — PMSM, IM, BLDC, SRM
5. **Transmission & Final Drive** — Single-speed or multi-speed reducer + differential
6. **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU)** — Torque/speed arbitration, energy management strategy (EMS)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic** — *"Every EV Needs: **E**nergy, **P**ower electronics, **M**otor, **T**ransmission, **C**ontrol"* → **E-P-M-T-C**

---

## 2.2 Detailed Subsystem Theory

### 2.2.1 Energy Storage Subsystem
- Stores electrical energy electrochemically (Li-ion, LFP, NMC) or as hydrogen (FCEV).
- Characterized by:
  - **Specific Energy (Wh/kg)** — range indicator.
  - **Specific Power (W/kg)** — acceleration indicator.
  - **Cycle Life** — calendar + cycle degradation.
  - **State of Charge (SoC)** — instantaneous fuel gauge.
- Managed by a **Battery Management System (BMS)** that enforces:
  - Cell balancing
  - Thermal regulation
  - SoC/SoH estimation
  - Over-current / over-voltage protection

### 2.2.2 Power Electronic Converters
- **DC-DC Converter**: steps battery voltage (e.g., 400 V) up or down for auxiliary buses (12 V, 48 V).
- **Inverter (DC → AC)**: feeds the traction motor with variable voltage / variable frequency (VVVF).
- **On-Board Charger (OBC)**: rectifies grid AC to DC for battery charging (AC charging).
- **Charging standards (KTU-relevant)**:
  - **Level 1**: 120 V AC, ~1.4 kW
  - **Level 2**: 240 V AC, ~7 – 22 kW
  - **DC Fast Charging (Level 3)**: 200 – 800 V DC, 50 – 350 kW

### 2.2.3 Electric Traction Motor
- **Ideal EV motor characteristics**:
  - High starting torque at zero speed (peak torque from 0 rpm).
  - Constant-torque region at low speeds.
  - Constant-power region at high speeds (field weakening).
  - High efficiency over a wide operating envelope (≥ 90 %).
- **Common motor types** (with KTU-recommended comparison):
  - **PMSM** — High efficiency, high power density (preferred for modern EVs)
  - **IM** — Rugged, low cost (used in Tesla Model S older variants)
  - **BLDC** — Used in two-wheelers and small EVs
  - **SRM** — Fault-tolerant, no rare-earth magnets

### 2.2.4 Transmission
- Most modern BEVs use **single-speed reduction gear** (ratio ≈ 8:1 – 12:1) because the motor has wide constant-power range.
- Multi-speed gearboxes appear in high-performance EVs (Porsche Taycan — 2-speed).

### 2.2.5 Vehicle Control Unit (VCU) / Energy Management Strategy (EMS)
- Acts as the **"brain"** of the EV/HEV.
- Functions:
  - Driver demand interpretation (pedal map).
  - Torque arbitration between ICE and Motor (in HEV).
  - Mode selection (EV / Hybrid / Regen / Charge).
  - Coordination with BMS and thermal management.

### 2.2.6 Regenerative Braking
- Converts kinetic energy → electrical energy during deceleration.
- Motor acts as a generator; energy flows back to the battery.
- Recovery efficiency: **60 – 70 %** typical in road conditions.

---

## 2.3 HEV Architectures — The Core KTU Module 1 Topic

The **kinematic arrangement** of the ICE, Motor, and Transmission defines the **architecture**. There are three primary architectures:

### A) Series Hybrid
- ICE → Generator → Rectifier → Battery/Motor → Wheels.
- **No mechanical coupling** between ICE and wheels.
- ICE runs only at **optimal efficiency point** (Apex load).
- Best for **city / urban** duty cycle.
- Examples: **Nissan e-Power (early)**, **Chevrolet Volt (1st gen)**.

### B) Parallel Hybrid
- ICE and Motor both mechanically coupled to wheels through a torque coupler (clutch).
- Both can propel vehicle **simultaneously or independently**.
- Higher flexibility, but ICE cannot always operate at its sweet spot.
- Examples: **Honda Insight (1st gen)**, **Toyota Crown (parallel)**.

### C) Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid
- Combines series + parallel using a **planetary gear set (PGS)**.
- ICE can both charge battery (series mode) and propel wheels directly (parallel mode).
- **Toyota Hybrid System (THS)** — used in **Toyota Prius, Camry Hybrid**.
- Most efficient and most commercially successful architecture.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table. These are the equations that appear in 80 % of KTU Module 1 numerical questions.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rolling Resistance Force | $F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta$ | N | $\mu_{rr}$ dimensionless, $m$ in kg |
| 2 | Aerodynamic Drag Force | $F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^{2}$ | N | $\rho$ = **1.225 kg/m³** |
| 3 | Hill Climbing Force | $F_{hc} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$ | N | $\theta$ = road slope |
| 4 | Linear Acceleration Force | $F_{ma} = m \cdot a$ | N | For pure translation |
| 5 | Acceleration Force (with rotational inertia) | $F_{ma} = m \cdot (1 + k_{rot}) \cdot a$ | N | $k_{rot} \approx 0.05 - 0.15$ |
| 6 | Total Tractive Force | $F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{ma}$ | N | Summation of 4 forces |
| 7 | Tractive Power | $P_t = F_t \cdot v$ | W | $v$ in m/s |
| 8 | Wheel Torque | $T_w = F_t \cdot r_w$ | Nm | $r_w$ = tyre radius |
| 9 | Motor Torque (after gear) | $T_m = \dfrac{T_w}{G \cdot \eta_{gear}}$ | Nm | $G$ = gear ratio |
| 10 | Motor Speed | $N_m = \dfrac{v \cdot G \cdot 60}{2 \pi \cdot r_w}$ | rpm | |
| 11 | Battery Energy | $E_b = V_{nom} \cdot Q_{Ah}$ | Wh | |
| 12 | Vehicle Range | $R = \dfrac{E_b \cdot \eta_{sys}}{\text{Energy per km}}$ | km | $\eta_{sys} \approx 0.75$ |
| 13 | Specific Energy | $\boxed{E_{sp} = \dfrac{E_b}{m_b}}$ | Wh/kg | Battery figure of merit |
| 14 | Power-to-weight ratio | $\dfrac{P}{m} = \dfrac{F_t \cdot v}{m}$ | W/kg | Acceleration indicator |
| 15 | Fuel Cell H₂ consumption | $\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dfrac{P_{fc}}{\eta_{fc} \cdot LHV_{H_2}}$ | kg/s | $LHV_{H_2}$ = **120 MJ/kg** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign convention**: Positive tractive force = propulsion; Negative = braking. During regenerative braking, $F_t < 0$ and energy returns to the battery.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Vehicle manufacturers** use these formulas in **homologation testing** (WLTP / EPA cycles).
- **Cell selection** for battery packs depends on specific energy and power targets.
- **Motor sizing** is driven by peak tractive power (acceleration + hill climb).
- **Charging infrastructure** sizing depends on battery capacity and acceptable SoC window.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Tractive Force Equation

The vehicle moves forward because the motor produces a **tractive force at the wheel contact patch**. This force must overcome four resistances simultaneously:

$$
F_{traction} = F_{propulsion}
$$

Let us write the **Newton's second law** for the vehicle on a slope:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum F_{x} &= m \cdot a_{x} \\
F_{prop} - F_{resist} &= m \cdot a
\end{aligned}
$$

The resistive forces acting opposite to motion are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{resist} &= F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting into Newton's law:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{prop} &= m \cdot a + F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} \\
\therefore F_t &= m \cdot a + \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^{2} + m g \sin\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

For an EV/HEV, we additionally account for **rotational inertia** of the drivetrain (motor rotor, wheels, gears), using a mass factor $(1 + k_{rot})$:

$$
\boxed{F_t = (1 + k_{rot}) \cdot m \cdot a \;+\; \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^{2} \;+\; m g \sin\theta}
$$

This equation is the **starting point** of every KTU Module 1 numerical problem.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Tractive Power and Energy per km

Tractive power is the instantaneous power required at the wheel:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_t &= F_t \cdot v \\
P_t &= \left[(1 + k_{rot}) m a + \mu_{rr} m g \cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^{2} + m g \sin\theta \right] \cdot v
\end{aligned}
$$

Expanding:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_t = (1 + k_{rot}) m a v \;+\; \mu_{rr} m g \cos\theta \cdot v \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^{3} \;+\; m g \sin\theta \cdot v
\end{aligned}
$$

For **cruising at constant speed** ($a = 0$) on a **flat road** ($\theta = 0$), this simplifies to:

$$
P_{cruise} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot v + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^{3}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Observe the **cubic dependence on velocity** for aerodynamic drag. At low speeds, rolling resistance dominates; at high speeds, aerodynamic drag dominates (typically above **60 km/h**).

**Energy consumed per unit distance** (specific energy in Wh/km):

$$
E_{km} = \frac{P_t}{v \cdot \eta_{sys}} \quad [\text{Wh/km}]
$$

where $\eta_{sys}$ accounts for battery discharge efficiency, motor efficiency, and inverter efficiency.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Battery Pack Sizing for a Target Range

Given a target range $R$ (km) and vehicle energy consumption $E_{km}$ (Wh/km):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{b,req} &= R \cdot E_{km} \quad [\text{Wh}] \\
\text{Then battery Ah capacity:} \quad Q_{Ah} &= \frac{E_{b,req}}{V_{nom}} \\
\text{Battery mass:} \quad m_b &= \frac{E_{b,req}}{E_{sp,batt}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $E_{sp,batt}$ is the **pack-level specific energy** (typically 70 – 80 % of cell-level).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of HEV Architectures

| Parameter | Series HEV | Parallel HEV | Series-Parallel (Power-Split) HEV |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanical coupling ICE–Wheels | **None** | Direct | Through planetary gear |
| ICE can operate at optimum point? | **Yes** | Partially | **Yes** |
| Number of traction motors | 1 (or 2) | 1 (shared) | 1 main + MG1 + MG2 |
| Transmission complexity | Low | Medium | **High** (PGS required) |
| City-cycle efficiency | High | Medium | **Highest** |
| Highway efficiency | Medium | High | High |
| Cost | Medium | Low | **High** |
| Example | Chevrolet Volt | Honda Insight | **Toyota Prius** |
| Regenerative braking capability | Excellent | Good | Excellent |
| All-electric range (AER) | Short | Short | Medium |
| KTU board exam weightage | 7 marks | 7 marks | **Highest (14 marks)** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake (KTU Examiner Pitfall):** Many students write *"Series-Parallel uses two clutches."* This is **wrong**. It uses a **planetary gear set (PGS)** with a sun gear, planet carrier, and ring gear. State this explicitly in the answer.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style, 7 marks)

> **Problem:** A BEV has the following parameters: $m$ = **1500 kg**, $C_d$ = **0.30**, $A$ = **2.2 m²**, $\mu_{rr}$ = **0.012**, $r_w$ = **0.32 m**, $g$ = **9.81 m/s²**, $\rho$ = **1.225 kg/m³**, $\eta_{gear}$ = **0.95**, $G$ = **9.0**. The vehicle accelerates from **0 to 100 km/h in 10 s** on a flat road. Compute:
> (a) Peak tractive force required
> (b) Peak motor torque and power required

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Convert speed to m/s**
$$
v_f = \frac{100 \times 1000}{3600} = 27.78 \;\text{m/s}
$$

**Step 2: Compute acceleration**
$$
a = \frac{v_f - v_i}{t} = \frac{27.78 - 0}{10} = 2.778 \;\text{m/s}^{2}
$$

**Step 3: Compute each resistive force (assume $k_{rot} = 0.1$)**

Rolling resistance:
$$
F_{rr} = 0.012 \times 1500 \times 9.81 \times \cos 0 = 176.58 \;\text{N}
$$

Aerodynamic drag (assume average speed ≈ $v_f / 2$ for simplicity, but using peak speed for worst case):
$$
F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times (27.78)^{2} = 312.2 \;\text{N}
$$

Hill climb: $F_{hc} = 0$ (flat road).

Acceleration force:
$$
F_{ma} = (1 + 0.1) \times 1500 \times 2.778 = 4583.7 \;\text{N}
$$

**Step 4: Total tractive force**
$$
F_t = 4583.7 + 176.58 + 312.2 + 0 = 5072.5 \;\text{N}
$$

**Step 5: Motor torque**
$$
T_m = \frac{F_t \cdot r_w}{G \cdot \eta_{gear}} = \frac{5072.5 \times 0.32}{9.0 \times 0.95} = 189.8 \;\text{Nm}
$$

**Step 6: Motor power at peak speed**
$$
P = F_t \cdot v_f = 5072.5 \times 27.78 = 140{,}914 \;\text{W} \approx \mathbf{141 \;kW}
$$

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Generic EV Powertrain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Battery Pack ESS] -->|High Voltage DC Bus| B1[DC-DC Converter]
    A1 -->|HV DC| B2[Traction Inverter DC-AC]
    B2 -->|3-Phase AC| C1[Traction Motor PMSM]
    C1 -->|Mechanical| D1[Single-Speed Reducer]
    D1 -->|Mechanical| E1[Differential]
    E1 -->|Torque at wheels| F1[Vehicle Wheels]
    B1 -->|12V / 48V DC| G1[Auxiliary Loads HVAC Lights]
    H1[VCU Vehicle Control Unit] -->|PWM Gate Signals| B2
    H1 -->|Enable / Disable| A1
    H1 -->|Torque Command| C1
    I1[Brake Pedal Sensor] -->|Regen Request| H1
    J1[Accelerator Pedal Sensor] -->|Driver Demand| H1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **VCU** sits at the centre and acts as the decision-making layer. The **traction inverter** is the only path between the DC battery and the AC motor — this is why power electronics failures immobilize the vehicle.

---

## 4.2 Series Hybrid Architecture — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[ICE Engine] --> B1[Generator]
    B1 --> C1[Rectifier DC]
    C1 --> D1[DC Bus]
    D1 --> E1[Traction Inverter]
    E1 --> F1[Traction Motor]
    F1 --> G1[Final Drive]
    G1 --> H1[Wheels]
    D1 --> J1[Battery Pack]
    J1 -.->|Bidirectional| D1
    K1[VCU] --> A1
    K1 --> B1
    K1 --> E1
    K1 --> J1
```

**Power flow analysis (Series HEV):**
- Path 1 (Mechanical path): **ICE → Generator → Battery → Inverter → Motor → Wheels** (long path, electrical losses each stage).
- Path 2 (Pure EV mode): **Battery → Inverter → Motor → Wheels** (ICE off, vehicle acts as BEV).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No mechanical link exists between ICE and wheels.** All ICE power passes through **electrical conversion**. This is the *signature* of a series HEV — examiners check for this in long-answer questions.

---

## 4.3 Parallel Hybrid Architecture — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[ICE Engine] --> B1[Clutch / Torque Coupler]
    B1 --> C1[Mechanical Summation Node]
    D1[Traction Motor] --> C1
    C1 --> E1[Transmission]
    E1 --> F1[Final Drive]
    F1 --> G1[Wheels]
    H1[Battery Pack] -->|DC| I1[Motor Inverter]
    I1 -->|AC| D1
    J1[VCU] -->|Clutch Engage| B1
    J1 -->|Torque Command| A1
    J1 -->|Torque Command| D1
```

**Key observation:** ICE and motor **share a common mechanical path** to the wheels through a torque coupler. They can work alone or together.

---

## 4.4 Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Architecture — Toyota THS Style

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[ICE Engine] --> B1[Planet Carrier]
    B1 --> C1[Planetary Gear Set PGS]
    C1 --> D1[Sun Gear]
    C1 --> E1[Ring Gear]
    D1 --> F1[MG1 Generator Motor]
    F1 -->|AC| G1[MG1 Inverter]
    G1 -->|DC| H1[DC Bus]
    E1 --> I1[MG2 Traction Motor]
    I1 -->|AC| J1[MG2 Inverter]
    J1 -->|DC| H1
    H1 <-->|Bidirectional| K1[Battery Pack]
    I1 --> L1[Final Drive]
    L1 --> M1[Wheels]
    N1[VCU] --> A1
    N1 --> G1
    N1 --> J1
    N1 --> K1
```

**Reading the planetary gear set:**
- **Carrier** = ICE input
- **Sun gear** = MG1 (generator)
- **Ring gear** = output to MG2 and wheels
- By controlling MG1 speed, the VCU can make ICE rotate at any speed **independent** of wheel speed — enabling optimal ICE operation.

---

## 4.5 State-of-Charge (SoC) Behaviour Across Architectures

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    N1[Vehicle Starts SoC target] --> N2{Architecture Type}
    N2 -->|Series| N3[ICE ON Charges Battery SoC rises to target]
    N3 --> N4[ICE OFF Pure EV SoC drops]
    N4 --> N5[SoC hits lower threshold]
    N5 --> N3
    N2 -->|Parallel| N6[ICE and Motor share load SoC fluctuates around midpoint]
    N6 --> N7[Regen events boost SoC]
    N7 --> N6
    N2 -->|Series Parallel| N8[VCU Optimally chooses mode]
    N8 --> N9[City Low speed EV mode]
    N8 --> N10[Highway ICE direct drive]
    N8 --> N11[Sudden demand Both ICE and MG2]
```

---

## 4.6 Energy Conversion Path Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Series HEV] --> L2[Chemical ICE Thermal Mechanical Electrical AC DC DC AC Mechanical]
    L3[Parallel HEV] --> L4[Chemical ICE Mechanical AND Electrical DC AC Mechanical]
    L5[Series Parallel HEV] --> L6[Chemical ICE Mechanical via PGS AND Electrical path to MG2]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter?** Every additional conversion step **loses 5 – 10 %** energy. Series HEVs have the longest chain. Parallel and Power-Split systems reduce conversion steps, boosting overall efficiency.

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> Each question has **two sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified as per 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle**. Discuss the operating modes and state its advantages and disadvantages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the equation for **total tractive force required to propel a vehicle**, considering rolling resistance, aerodynamic drag, hill climbing, and acceleration. State the meaning of each term. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Question A**

#### **(a) Series HEV Architecture (7 marks)**

**Block diagram (2 marks):**
Refer to the **Section 4.2** Series HEV block diagram in this note for the schematic.

**Operating modes (3 marks):**
1. **Pure EV Mode** — Battery alone powers motor; ICE off. Used at low speeds / light load.
2. **Range Extender Mode** — ICE runs at optimum RPM, drives generator, charges battery + assists traction. ICE never mechanically drives wheels.
3. **Regenerative Braking Mode** — Motor acts as generator, charges battery.
4. **Stationary Charging Mode** — ICE-generator charges battery while parked.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- ICE always runs at peak efficiency (Apex load optimization).
- Simple mechanical layout — no clutch, no gearbox.

**Disadvantages (1 mark):**
- Triple energy conversion (mech → elec → mech) → 15 – 20 % losses.
- Large generator and battery needed → heavy and costly.

---

#### **(b) Derivation of Tractive Force (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** State Newton's second law along direction of motion:
$$\sum F_x = m \cdot a$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Identify four resistive forces:
$$
F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{ma}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Expand each term:
$$
F_t = \mu_{rr} m g \cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^2 + m g \sin\theta + m(1+k_{rot})a
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** State the meaning of each term clearly:
- $F_{rr}$ — tyre deformation losses on road.
- $F_{ad}$ — air drag opposing motion.
- $F_{hc}$ — gravity component along slope.
- $F_{ma}$ — inertia of vehicle + rotating masses.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Alternative Internal Choice**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Modified as per 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Compare **Series, Parallel, and Series-Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle architectures** with suitable block diagrams. Identify the architecture used in the Toyota Prius and justify its selection. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An electric car of mass **1800 kg** (including 200 kg battery) needs to climb a **10 % grade** at a constant speed of **60 km/h**. The aerodynamic drag coefficient is **0.28**, frontal area **2.3 m²**, rolling resistance coefficient **0.011**, drivetrain efficiency **0.92**, gear ratio **8.5**, wheel radius **0.33 m**. Calculate the **(i) total tractive force**, **(ii) tractive power at wheels**, and **(iii) motor torque**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Question B**

#### **(a) Architectural Comparison (7 marks)**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**
Present the **Analytical Matrix from Section 3.4** with all three architectures. Highlight:
- Series — no mechanical ICE-wheel link.
- Parallel — direct mechanical coupling.
- Series-Parallel — planetary gear set.

**Toyota Prius Architecture (3 marks):**
- Prius uses **Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid** configuration based on **Toyota Hybrid System (THS)** with a **planetary gear set (PGS)**.
- **Justification**:
  1. Highest combined efficiency across city + highway cycles.
  2. ICE can run at optimum point **AND** drive wheels directly when efficient.
  3. Regenerative braking works seamlessly through MG1 (acts as generator).
  4. Compact packaging — only one planetary gear instead of a multi-speed transmission.

---

#### **(b) Numerical Problem Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:** $m$ = 1800 kg, $\theta$ = $\tan^{-1}(0.10) = 5.71°$, $v$ = 60 km/h = 16.67 m/s, $C_d$ = 0.28, $A$ = 2.3 m², $\mu_{rr}$ = 0.011, $\eta$ = 0.92, $G$ = 8.5, $r_w$ = 0.33 m, $\rho$ = 1.225 kg/m³, $a = 0$ (constant speed).

**(i) Total Tractive Force (3 marks):**

$$
F_{rr} = 0.011 \times 1800 \times 9.81 \times \cos 5.71° = 193.2 \;\text{N}
$$

$$
F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.28 \times 2.3 \times (16.67)^{2} = 130.7 \;\text{N}
$$

$$
F_{hc} = 1800 \times 9.81 \times \sin 5.71° = 1800 \times 9.81 \times 0.0995 = 1757.2 \;\text{N}
$$

$$
F_t = 193.2 + 130.7 + 1757.2 = \mathbf{2081.1 \;N}
$$

**(ii) Tractive Power at Wheels (2 marks):**

$$
P_t = F_t \cdot v = 2081.1 \times 16.67 = 34{,}690 \;\text{W} \approx \mathbf{34.69 \;kW}
$$

**(iii) Motor Torque (2 marks):**

$$
T_m = \frac{F_t \cdot r_w}{G \cdot \eta} = \frac{2081.1 \times 0.33}{8.5 \times 0.92} = \frac{686.8}{7.82} = \mathbf{87.8 \;Nm}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting drivetrain efficiency** $\eta_{gear}$ while computing motor torque. Most students compute $T_m = F_t \cdot r_w / G$ directly and lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing degrees and radians** in $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$. For 10 % grade, $\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.10) = 5.71°$. Many students mistakenly use $\theta = 10°$ — wrong.
> 3. **Confusing rolling resistance formula** — it is $\mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta$, NOT $\mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g$. The $\cos\theta$ term is mandatory on sloped roads.
> 4. **Calling Series-Parallel "two-clutch hybrid"** — examiners deduct a full mark. Use the phrase *"planetary gear set-based power-split hybrid"* for full credit.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention regen braking** when asked "operating modes of HEV" — it is a compulsory sub-point.
> 6. **Units**: tractive power must be in **kW**, torque in **Nm**, force in **N**. Penalized heavily if units missing.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Recall List for Exam Hall**

- ✅ EV = vehicle propelled by **electric motor** fed from on-board storage.
- ✅ HEV = **two energy sources** (ICE + Battery), at least one reversible.
- ✅ Six universal EV subsystems: **Battery, Inverter, Motor, Reducer, VCU, Charger**.
- ✅ BEV, HEV, PHEV, FCEV — know definitions + examples cold.
- ✅ Three HEV architectures: **Series, Parallel, Series-Parallel**.
- ✅ **Series HEV** → ICE never touches wheels. Mechanical isolation = signature.
- ✅ **Parallel HEV** → ICE and Motor share **torque coupler**. Direct mechanical path.
- ✅ **Series-Parallel** → Uses **Planetary Gear Set (PGS)**. Toyota Prius THS.
- ✅ Tractive force equation has **4 components**: rolling, drag, hill, acceleration.
- ✅ Drag force scales with **$v^2$**; drag power scales with **$v^3$**.
- ✅ Specific energy (Wh/kg) = range metric. Specific power (W/kg) = acceleration metric.
- ✅ Regenerative braking recovers **60 – 70 %** of kinetic energy typically.
- ✅ Standard air density $\rho$ = **1.225 kg/m³** (memorize).
- ✅ Motor torque formula: $T_m = F_t \cdot r_w / (G \cdot \eta_{gear})$ — with efficiency factor.
- ✅ Modern PMSM preferred over IM due to higher efficiency & power density.
- ✅ Battery SoC operating window: **20 % – 80 %** for cycle life.
- ✅ Charging: Level 1 (1.4 kW AC), Level 2 (7 – 22 kW AC), Level 3 (50 – 350 kW DC).
- ✅ For 10 % grade, slope angle is **5.71°**, not 10°.
- ✅ **Rolling resistance** = $\mu_{rr} m g \cos\theta$ — **never** forget the $\cos\theta$.
- ✅ VCU = brain; BMS = battery manager; Inverter = muscle controller.
- ✅ BEVs use **single-speed reducer**; ICE vehicles use **5–10 speed gearboxes**.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Basic Components and Architecture of Electric and Hybrid Vehicles**
> **Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme | NEP 2020 Aligned | OBE Compatible**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fundamentals of Hybrid Electric Vehicles: Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT631 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fundamentals of Hybrid Electric Vehicles: Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV)?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Board-Exam Standard):**
> A **Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV)** is a vehicle that employs **two or more distinct energy storage and/or conversion systems** — most commonly an **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** coupled with one or more **Electric Machines (EM)** and an **Electrochemical Battery** — working in tandem through a power-split device to propel the vehicle with higher overall thermal efficiency and reduced tailpipe emissions compared to a conventional ICE-only vehicle.

The official **KTU PEMUT631 syllabus (Module 1)** explicitly classifies HEVs by:
1. **Degree of Hybridization** (Micro / Mild / Full / Plug-in)
2. **Powertrain Architecture** (Series / Parallel / Series-Parallel / Complex)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Marathon Runner with a Friend on a Bicycle**
>
> Imagine you must run a marathon (long-distance drive) but also need to sprint up hills (acceleration).
>
> - **You (your legs)** = the **Internal Combustion Engine** — best at steady, long-distance running (highway cruising).
> - **Your friend on a bicycle beside you** = the **Electric Motor** — best at short bursts of high power (hill climbs, overtaking).
> - **A shared water bottle** = the **Battery** — stores energy that can be passed between you and your friend.
> - **The hand signals you exchange** = the **Hybrid Controller / Energy Management Strategy (EMS)**.
>
> When the road is flat, only **you** run (ICE powers the car). When a hill comes, your **friend pedals hard** (motor assists). After the hill, your friend recharges by drafting you (regenerative braking). Together, you finish faster and consume far less "energy" (fuel) than if either one of you ran alone.
>
> This **teamwork** is the *essence* of hybridization — combining the strengths of two power sources and masking their individual weaknesses.

---

## 1.3 Why Hybridization? — Engineering Motivation

A conventional gasoline ICE has a peak brake thermal efficiency of only **35–40 %**, and under real-world urban driving, its **operational efficiency drops to 10–15 %** because engines run far from their optimal torque-speed point.

In contrast:
- Electric motors exhibit efficiencies of **85–95 %** over a wide operating range.
- They can **regeneratively brake** (recover kinetic energy as electricity).
- They produce **zero tailpipe emissions** at point of use.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** An HEV does not just *add* an electric motor to a car. It **decouples the engine from the wheels** (fully or partially) so that the ICE can be operated *only* in its high-efficiency sweet spot, while the electric machine handles transient loads and recovers braking energy.

---

## 1.4 Formal Taxonomy of HEVs (KTU 2024 Module 1 Overview)

| Classification Basis | Type | Defining Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **By Degree of Hybridization** | Micro Hybrid | Start-Stop system only; 12 V starter motor. No traction e-motor. |
| | Mild Hybrid | 48 V belt-driven e-motor (BSG/ISG). Cannot drive alone. ~10–15 % electrification. |
| | Full (Strong) Hybrid | High-voltage (200–600 V) e-motor + battery. Can drive EV-only. ~30–50 % electrification. |
| | Plug-in Hybrid (PHEV) | Externally chargeable battery (5–20 kWh). 20–80 km EV-only range. |
| **By Powertrain Architecture** | Series Hybrid | ICE → Generator → Battery → Motor → Wheels. **No mechanical link** ICE–wheels. |
| | Parallel Hybrid | ICE and EM **both** mechanically coupled to wheels. Torque adds. |
| | Series–Parallel (Combined) | Power-split device (e.g., Toyota THS). Both series and parallel paths exist. |
| | Complex Hybrid | Multiple e-motors + clutches + planetary gear sets (e.g., Toyota Prius Gen 3+). |

**Bold Standard Metrics for KTU Board Exam:**
- **Micro hybrid voltage: 12 V**
- **Mild hybrid voltage: 48 V**
- **Full hybrid voltage: 200–600 V DC bus**
- **PHEV battery capacity: 5–20 kWh**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Degree of Hybridization (DOH) — Quantitative Measure

The **Degree of Hybridization** is a dimensionless number that defines how much of the vehicle's peak propulsion power is delivered by the electric drivetrain.

$$
\mathrm{DOH} \;=\; \frac{P_{\text{electric}}}{P_{\text{electric}} + P_{\text{ICE}}}
$$

where:
- $P_{\text{electric}}$ = peak mechanical power of the traction e-motor (W)
- $P_{\text{ICE}}$ = peak brake power of the ICE (W)

| DOH Range | Classification |
|---|---|
| $0 < \text{DOH} \leq 0.1$ | Micro Hybrid |
| $0.1 < \text{DOH} \leq 0.3$ | Mild Hybrid |
| $0.3 < \text{DOH} \leq 0.8$ | Full / Strong Hybrid |
| $0.8 < \text{DOH} \leq 1.0$ | Plug-in / Extended-Range EV |

---

## 2.2 Three Core HEV Architectures — Operational Logic

### 2.2.1 Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle (SHEV)

**Logic Steps:**
1. ICE is **mechanically decoupled** from the wheels — it drives **only** a generator.
2. Generator produces **3-phase AC**, rectified to **DC bus**, then inverted to drive the traction motor.
3. Traction motor alone propels the wheels through a fixed-ratio transmission.
4. Battery acts as a **buffer/energy equalizer** between generator and motor.
5. ICE is operated at a **single optimal point** on its BSFC (Brake Specific Fuel Consumption) map → maximum thermal efficiency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Best Use Case:** Urban buses, garbage trucks, delivery vans — vehicles that stop-start frequently and never need long highway cruising on pure ICE. Example: **Chevrolet Volt (1st gen, ER Mode), BMW i3 with REx.**

### 2.2.2 Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV — but *not* plug-in)

**Logic Steps:**
1. ICE and EM are **both mechanically coupled** to the driveshaft (via clutch and/or torque coupler).
2. Either source can propel the wheels **independently or simultaneously**.
3. Mechanical torques **add directly** at the gearbox input.
4. Battery is recharged via ICE-driven generator, regenerative braking, or wall outlet (if plug-in variant).
5. Transmission is conventional (manual/AMT/CVT/DCT).

> [!NOTE]
> **Best Use Case:** Compact cars where packaging is constrained and ICE is still required for long trips. Example: **Honda Insight (1st gen, 1999), Honda Civic Hybrid, Hyundai Sonata Hybrid (pre-2016).**

### 2.2.3 Series–Parallel (Combined / Power-Split) Hybrid

**Logic Steps:**
1. Combines a **planetary gear set** (Toyota THS) or **multi-clutch DCT** (Hyundai TMED) as the power-split device.
2. ICE can either (a) drive wheels directly through a mechanical path, **or** (b) charge the battery via the generator.
3. Operates as **Series** at low speeds, **Parallel** at high speeds, or **blended** during transients.
4. Requires a sophisticated **Hybrid Control Unit (HCU)** running real-time EMS (Energy Management Strategy).

> [!NOTE]
> **Best Use Case:** Mass-market passenger cars needing the best of both architectures. Example: **Toyota Prius (all gens), Toyota Camry Hybrid, Ford Escape Hybrid.**

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for Module 1 (KTU 2024 Board Exam)**

| # | Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathrm{DOH} = \dfrac{P_e}{P_e + P_{\text{ICE}}}$ | Degree of Hybridization | dimensionless |
| 2 | $F_{\text{trac}} = F_{\text{roll}} + F_{\text{aero}} + F_{\text{climb}} + F_{\text{acc}}$ | Tractive force balance | N |
| 3 | $F_{\text{roll}} = m\,g\,f_r \cos\theta$ | Rolling resistance | N |
| 4 | $F_{\text{aero}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A\,v^2$ | Aerodynamic drag | N |
| 5 | $F_{\text{climb}} = m\,g \sin\theta$ | Hill-climbing force | N |
| 6 | $F_{\text{acc}} = m\,\alpha \quad (\alpha = \tfrac{dv}{dt})$ | Inertial force | N |
| 7 | $P_{\text{trac}} = F_{\text{trac}} \cdot v$ | Tractive power | W |
| 8 | $\eta_{\text{overall}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{wheel}}}{P_{\text{fuel}}}$ | Tank-to-Wheel efficiency | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{P_{\text{ICE}}}{\eta_{\text{ICE}} \cdot \text{LHV}_{\text{fuel}}}$ | Fuel mass-flow rate | kg/s |
| 10 | $\text{LHV}_{\text{gasoline}} \approx 44\,\text{MJ/kg}$ | Lower Heating Value | J/kg |
| 11 | $E_{\text{bat}} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,C\,(V_{\max}^2 - V_{\min}^2)$ | Usable battery energy | J |
| 12 | $\text{SOC}(t) = \text{SOC}(t_0) + \dfrac{1}{E_{\text{bat}}}\displaystyle\int_{t_0}^{t} \big(P_{\text{gen}}(t) - P_{\text{motor}}(t)\big)\,dt$ | State of Charge dynamics | dimensionless |
| 13 | $T_{\text{eq}} = T_{\text{ICE}} + T_{\text{EM}} \cdot i_g \cdot \eta_g$ | Parallel-hybrid torque summation | N·m |
| 14 | $P_{\text{regen,max}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,m\,v^2 \cdot \eta_{\text{regen}}$ | Max regenerative braking power | W |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields:** Equation 12 (the SOC integral) is the *heartbeat* of every EMS. KTU examiners frequently test whether a student understands that **SOC must be charge-sustaining** (oscillates between 30–70 % for NiMH/Li-ion) in a Full Hybrid, but is allowed to **deplete** monotonically in a PHEV.

---

## 2.4 Advantages & Disadvantages Comparison Table (Board-Exam Favorite)

| Parameter | Series | Parallel | Series–Parallel |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanical complexity | Low | Medium | High |
| ICE always at optimal point? | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (most of the time) |
| EV-only mode possible? | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Transmission needed? | 1 fixed gear | Multi-speed gearbox | Power-split device |
| Overall efficiency (urban) | High | Medium | Highest |
| Highway efficiency | Medium | High | High |
| Cost | High (large e-motor) | Lowest | Highest (control) |
| Example | Chevrolet Volt | Honda Civic Hybrid | Toyota Prius |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Minimum ICE Size for a Series HEV

In a pure **Series** architecture, the ICE never drives the wheels directly. Its only job is to feed the generator, which (along with the battery) supplies the traction motor. Therefore, **the ICE must be sized to meet the *average* power demand, not the *peak*.**

### Step 1 — Tractive Power Demand at Wheel

The maximum tractive power needed at the wheels occurs during combined acceleration + hill-climb at top speed:

$$
P_{\text{trac,max}} = v_{\max}\left( m g f_r + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v_{\max} + m g \sin\theta_{\max} + m\,\alpha_{\max} \right)
$$

### Step 2 — Convert Wheel Power to Electrical Bus Power

Accounting for drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{\text{drv}}$ and motor-generator efficiency $\eta_{\text{mot}}$:

$$
P_{\text{bus,peak}} = \frac{P_{\text{trac,max}}}{\eta_{\text{mot}} \cdot \eta_{\text{drv}}}
$$

### Step 3 — In a Series HEV, Battery Covers the *Peak Excess*

If the ICE is sized only to meet the **average** load $\bar{P}_{\text{trac}}$, then the *peak* deficit is supplied by the battery:

$$
P_{\text{ICE}} = \bar{P}_{\text{trac}}, \qquad P_{\text{bat,discharge,max}} = P_{\text{bus,peak}} - \bar{P}_{\text{trac}}
$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Given:** $m = 1500$ kg, $v_{\max} = 50$ m/s (≈ 180 km/h), $f_r = 0.015$, $C_d A = 0.7$ m², $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³, $\theta = 0$, $\alpha = 0$ (steady cruise), $\eta_{\text{mot}} = 0.92$, $\eta_{\text{drv}} = 0.95$.

**Compute rolling + aero force:**
$$
F_{\text{trac}} = (1500)(9.81)(0.015) + \tfrac{1}{2}(1.2)(0.7)(50)^2 = 220.7 + 1050 = 1270.7\,\text{N}
$$

**Compute tractive power:**
$$
P_{\text{trac,max}} = 1270.7 \times 50 = 63\,535\,\text{W} \approx 63.5\,\text{kW}
$$

**Compute electrical bus power:**
$$
P_{\text{bus,peak}} = \frac{63\,535}{0.92 \times 0.95} = \frac{63\,535}{0.874} \approx 72\,695\,\text{W} \approx 72.7\,\text{kW}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Exam Insight:** A KTU question will typically ask, *"Why is the ICE in a Series HEV smaller than in a Parallel HEV of the same vehicle?"* — The above derivation proves the ICE only needs to meet the *average* load (e.g., 30 kW) while the battery covers the peak (≈ 42 kW transient). This is exactly why **the Chevrolet Volt uses a 1.4 L ICE** to propel a 1700 kg vehicle.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Equivalent Fuel Consumption Minimization (ECMS)

The **Energy Management Strategy (EMS)** decides at every instant how to split power between ICE and EM. The most cited analytical strategy is **ECMS (Equivalent Consumption Minimization Strategy)**.

### Step 1 — Cost Function

At each time step $t$, the EMS solves:
$$
J(P_{\text{ICE}}, P_{\text{EM}}, t) = \dot{m}_f(P_{\text{ICE}}(t),\, \omega_{\text{ICE}}) + s(t)\cdot\frac{P_{\text{bat}}(t)}{Q_{\text{lhv}}}
$$

where:
- $\dot{m}_f$ = instantaneous fuel mass-flow rate (kg/s)
- $s(t)$ = **equivalence factor** — converts electrical energy into equivalent fuel mass
- $P_{\text{bat}}$ = battery power (W) — **positive when discharging**
- $Q_{\text{lhv}}$ = lower heating value of fuel (J/kg)

### Step 2 — Equivalence Factor

$$
s(t) = 1 - \frac{\text{SOC}(t) - \text{SOC}_{\text{low}}}{\text{SOC}_{\text{high}} - \text{SOC}_{\text{low}}}
$$

When $\text{SOC} \to \text{SOC}_{\text{low}}$, $s \to 1$ → ICE is forced to charge.  
When $\text{SOC} \to \text{SOC}_{\text{high}}$, $s \to 0$ → ICE is throttled, motor takes over.

### Step 3 — Optimal Decision (Charge-Sustaining HEV)

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial P_{\text{ICE}}} = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \frac{d\dot{m}_f}{dP_{\text{ICE}}}\bigg|_{\omega_{\text{ICE}}} = \frac{s(t)}{Q_{\text{lhv}}}
$$

This gradient condition chooses the ICE operating point on the BSFC map such that the incremental fuel cost of generating 1 W of electrical power equals the SOC-penalized equivalent cost.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** ECMS is **not** optimal globally, but is real-time implementable. The globally optimal strategy is **Pontryagin's Minimum Principle (PMP)** or **Dynamic Programming (DP)** — both are commonly 2-mark short-answer questions.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — When to Use Which Architecture?

| Vehicle Application | Recommended Architecture | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Urban delivery van (stop-go, < 60 km/h) | **Series** | No highway; ICE stays at one efficient point; regen brakes dominate |
| Compact family sedan (mixed use) | **Parallel or Series–Parallel** | Needs both city EV-mode and highway ICE-direct |
| Long-haul heavy truck | **Parallel** | Series cannot mechanically transfer huge torque to wheels efficiently |
| Plug-in family SUV | **Series–Parallel (PHEV)** | Charge-depleting EV mode + charge-sustaining hybrid mode |
| Formula Student / performance car | **Parallel** | Mechanical path is most efficient at high power |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Series HEV — Power Flow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FuelTank["FUEL TANK"] --> ICE1["ICE / ENGINE"]
    ICE1 --> GEN["GENERATOR"]
    GEN --> RECT["RECTIFIER DC BUS"]
    BAT["TRACTION BATTERY"] --> RECT
    RECT --> INV["INVERTER"]
    INV --> TM["TRACTION MOTOR"]
    TM --> TR1["FIXED RATIO GEAR"]
    TR1 --> WH1["DRIVE WHEELS"]
    REGEN["REGEN BRAKING"] -.-> BAT

    classDef power fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef elec fill:#B4D8FF,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mech fill:#C8F0C8,stroke:#1a4d1a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class FuelTank,ICE1 power
    class GEN,RECT,INV,BAT,REGEN elec
    class TM,TR1,WH1 mech
```

**Reading the Diagram (KTU Examiner's View):**
- Solid arrows = **power flow direction** during traction.
- Dotted arrow from Wheels → Battery = **regenerative braking** path.
- Notice: **no mechanical arrow** between ICE and Wheels — this is the defining feature of Series.

---

## 4.2 Parallel HEV — Power Flow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FuelTank2["FUEL TANK"] --> ICE2["ICE / ENGINE"]
    ICE2 --> CL1["CLUTCH C1"]
    EM2["TRACTION MOTOR"] --> TC["TORQUE COUPLER"]
    CL1 --> TC
    BAT2["BATTERY"] --> INV2["INVERTER"]
    INV2 --> EM2
    TC --> GBX["MULTI-SPEED GEARBOX"]
    GBX --> WH2["DRIVE WHEELS"]
    WH2 -.-> REGEN2["REGEN BRAKING"]
    REGEN2 -.-> BAT2

    classDef power fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef elec fill:#B4D8FF,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mech fill:#C8F0C8,stroke:#1a4d1a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class FuelTank2,ICE2,CL1 power
    class BAT2,INV2,REGEN2 elec
    class EM2,TC,GBX,WH2 mech
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- ICE and EM **both** connect to a Torque Coupler — torques add here.
- Gearbox is required because mechanical path has variable ratios.
- Three operating modes: ICE alone, EM alone, ICE+EM (combined boost).

---

## 4.3 Series–Parallel (Combined) HEV — Power Flow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FuelTank3["FUEL TANK"] --> ICE3["ICE"]
    ICE3 --> PLG["PLANETARY GEAR SET"]
    GEN3["GENERATOR MG1"] --> PLG
    PLG --> RING["RING GEAR TO WHEELS"]
    PLG --> SUN["SUN GEAR TO MG1"]
    MG2["TRACTION MOTOR MG2"] --> RING
    BAT3["BATTERY"] --> INV3["INVERTER"]
    INV3 --> MG1
    INV3 --> MG2
    RING --> WH3["DRIVE WHEELS"]
    WH3 -.-> REGEN3["REGEN"]
    REGEN3 -.-> BAT3

    classDef power fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef elec fill:#B4D8FF,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mech fill:#C8F0C8,stroke:#1a4d1a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class FuelTank3,ICE3 power
    class BAT3,INV3,REGEN3,GEN3,MG2 elec
    class PLG,RING,SUN,WH3 mech
```

**Reading the Diagram (Toyota THS-Style):**
- The **planetary gear set** is the power-split device.
- ICE power splits continuously between (a) the ring gear → wheels, and (b) the sun gear → MG1 (generator).
- MG1 controls ICE speed *independently* of vehicle speed — this is the secret of THS efficiency.
- MG2 (motor) is on the ring gear → wheels, providing EV drive and regenerative braking.

---

## 4.4 Operating-Mode State Machine — All Three Architectures

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Stop
    Stop --> ICE_Only: Parallel or SHE_P
    Stop --> EV_Only: Battery SOC OK
    Stop --> Series_Mode: Series or SHE_SP

    EV_Only --> ICE_Only: SOC low
    ICE_Only --> EV_Only: Low load
    ICE_Only --> Combined_Boost: High accel demand
    Combined_Boost --> ICE_Only: Cruise
    ICE_Only --> Regen_Brake: Braking
    EV_Only --> Regen_Brake: Braking
    Regen_Brake --> EV_Only: Brake released
    Series_Mode --> Series_Regen: Braking
    Series_Regen --> Series_Mode: Brake released

    state Series_Mode {
        note right: ICE on optimal BSFC point
    }
    state Combined_Boost {
        note right: ICE + EM torque sum
    }
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define a Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV). How is the Degree of Hybridization (DOH) computed, and what DOH value typically classifies a *mild hybrid*?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks — KTU Key Pattern):

An HEV is a vehicle that uses **two or more distinct energy sources** — typically an ICE and an Electric Motor with a battery — to improve fuel economy and reduce emissions compared to a conventional ICE vehicle. **(1 mark)**

The Degree of Hybridization is given by **(1 mark):**
$$
\mathrm{DOH} = \frac{P_{\text{electric}}}{P_{\text{electric}} + P_{\text{ICE}}}
$$

A mild hybrid typically has a DOH in the range **0.1 < DOH ≤ 0.3** and uses a **48 V** e-machine. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Why is the ICE in a Series HEV typically smaller than the ICE in a comparable Parallel HEV?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

In a Series HEV, the ICE is **not mechanically connected to the wheels**. It only drives a generator to feed the battery and traction motor. Therefore, the ICE must be sized to meet only the **average tractive power demand**, while the **battery covers transient peaks**. **(2 marks)**

In contrast, a Parallel HEV's ICE must be sized to handle **peak loads directly** through the mechanical drivetrain. Hence, a Series HEV can use a smaller, more efficient ICE. **(1 mark)**

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### Question A — Option (i) [14 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, explain the architecture and operating modes of a **Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle**. List **two real-world examples**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **Degree of Hybridization** and explain how it classifies vehicles into Micro, Mild, Full, and Plug-in hybrids with appropriate DOH ranges. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

A Series HEV has the following power flow: **Fuel Tank → ICE → Generator → Rectifier → DC Bus → Battery (in parallel) → Inverter → Traction Motor → Fixed-ratio transmission → Wheels.** **(2 marks for block diagram description, 1 mark for diagram)**

Operating Modes: **(3 marks)**

1. **Pure EV Mode** — ICE off; battery alone feeds motor. Used at low loads, parking, reverse.
2. **Pure Series Mode** — ICE runs at optimal BSFC point; generator produces electricity; battery is charge-sustaining (CS).
3. **Regenerative Braking** — Motor acts as generator; battery is charged; friction brakes also engage at low SOC.
4. **Battery Charging Mode** — When SOC is below threshold, ICE-generator charges the battery even when vehicle is stationary.

**Real-world examples (1 mark):** Chevrolet Volt (Gen 1 ER mode), Nissan e-Power (Note: technically a series), BMW i3 REx.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

The Degree of Hybridization is defined as the **ratio of peak electric machine power to the total peak propulsion power**. **(1 mark)**

$$
\mathrm{DOH} = \frac{P_e}{P_e + P_{\text{ICE}}}
$$

- $P_e$ = peak mechanical output of the traction e-motor
- $P_{\text{ICE}}$ = peak brake power of the ICE

Classification (with battery voltage as a secondary indicator): **(5 marks)**

| DOH Range | Classification | Battery Voltage | EV-only Drive? |
|---|---|---|---|
| $0 < \text{DOH} \leq 0.1$ | Micro Hybrid | 12 V | ❌ |
| $0.1 < \text{DOH} \leq 0.3$ | Mild Hybrid | 48 V | ❌ (assist only) |
| $0.3 < \text{DOH} \leq 0.8$ | Full Hybrid | 200–600 V | ✅ |
| $0.8 < \text{DOH} \leq 1.0$ | Plug-in Hybrid | 300–400 V | ✅ (long range) |

**Numerical example (1 mark):** A Toyota Prius has $P_e \approx 60$ kW, $P_{\text{ICE}} \approx 72$ kW.  
$\mathrm{DOH} = 60/(60+72) = 60/132 \approx 0.45$ → **Full Hybrid** ✓

---

### Question B — Option (ii) [14 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **Series, Parallel, and Series–Parallel (Combined)** hybrid powertrains under the following heads: (i) mechanical complexity, (ii) ICE operating point, (iii) overall efficiency in urban driving, (iv) suitability for highway driving, (v) example vehicle, (vi) cost. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a **neat diagram** the working of a **Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle**. Derive the equation for the **equivalent tractive torque** when both ICE and EM are operating simultaneously. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks** (Tabulated comparison — 1 mark per head × 6 heads = 6 marks + 1 mark for example clarity)

| Head | Series | Parallel | Series–Parallel |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Mech. complexity | Low (no gearbox) | Medium (clutch + gearbox) | High (planetary set + 2 MG) |
| (ii) ICE operating point | Always optimal | Variable (load-dependent) | Mostly optimal (THS control) |
| (iii) Urban efficiency | **Highest** (regen-dominant) | Medium | High |
| (iv) Highway suitability | Poor (2 energy conversions) | **Best** (direct mech path) | Very good |
| (v) Example | Chevy Volt | Honda Civic Hybrid | Toyota Prius |
| (vi) Cost | High (large e-motor + battery) | **Lowest** | Highest (controller + planetary) |

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

A Parallel HEV has ICE and EM both connected to a common driveshaft via a clutch and torque coupler. Diagram description: **(2 marks for diagram, 2 marks for description)**

Equivalent tractive torque at the gearbox input:
$$
T_{\text{eq}} = T_{\text{ICE}} \cdot \eta_{\text{clutch}} + T_{\text{EM}} \cdot i_g \cdot \eta_g
$$

where $T_{\text{ICE}}$ = ICE torque, $T_{\text{EM}}$ = motor torque, $i_g$ = gear ratio, $\eta_g$ = gearbox efficiency. **(2 marks)**

**Numerical:** ICE delivers 120 N·m, EM delivers 180 N·m, gear ratio 3rd gear = 1.6, $\eta_g = 0.96$, $\eta_{\text{clutch}} = 1$.
$$
T_{\text{eq}} = 120 \times 1 + 180 \times 1.6 \times 0.96 = 120 + 276.5 = 396.5\,\text{N·m}
$$

At the wheel after differential ratio $i_0 = 3.5$: $T_{\text{wheel}} = 396.5 \times 3.5 \approx 1388$ N·m. **(1 mark)**

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on this Topic (Module 1):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Series vs. Series–Parallel:** Many students incorrectly call the Toyota Prius a "Series hybrid" because they see a "generator." **Marks lost: 1–2.** Correct: Prius is **Series–Parallel** with a planetary gear set enabling both modes simultaneously.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the ICE–Wheels mechanical path in Parallel HEV:** A common definition error. **Marks lost: 1.**
>
> 3. **Stating DOH with wrong bounds:** Writing "DOH = 1 for EV" — this is wrong. **DOH is undefined (or 1) for a pure BEV**, but for HEVs it strictly lies in $(0, 1)$. KTU strictly penalizes students who don't write the open interval.
>
> 4. **Mixing up Micro/Mild/Full voltages:** **Micro = 12 V, Mild = 48 V, Full = 200–600 V, PHEV = 300–400 V.** This is asked almost every semester.
>
> 5. **Skipping the LHV value of gasoline (44 MJ/kg)** in fuel consumption derivations — examiners deduct 0.5 marks if the constant is omitted.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 — Fundamentals of HEV: Types — 30-Second Recall Sheet**
>
> ✅ **Definition:** HEV = ICE + Electric Motor + Battery + Controller, working in tandem.
>
> ✅ **Degree of Hybridization (DOH)** = $P_e / (P_e + P_{\text{ICE}})$; range $(0, 1)$.
>
> ✅ **Classification by DOH:** Micro (≤ 0.1), Mild (0.1–0.3), Full (0.3–0.8), Plug-in (0.8–1.0).
>
> ✅ **Voltage ladder:** 12 V (Micro) → 48 V (Mild) → 200–600 V (Full) → 300–400 V (PHEV).
>
> ✅ **Series HEV:** ICE → Generator → Battery → Motor → Wheels. **No mechanical link** ICE–wheels. ICE always at optimal BSFC. Best for urban.
>
> ✅ **Parallel HEV:** ICE and EM **both coupled** to wheels mechanically. Torques add. Best for highway. Example: Honda Civic Hybrid.
>
> ✅ **Series–Parallel (Combined):** Planetary gear set as power-split device. Operates as Series at low speed, Parallel at high speed. Example: **Toyota Prius (THS)**.
>
> ✅ **Micro Hybrid:** Only has start-stop; no traction motor. **DO NOT confuse with Mild Hybrid.**
>
> ✅ **Mild Hybrid:** 48 V BSG/ISG; assists ICE but **cannot drive wheels alone**.
>
> ✅ **Plug-in Hybrid (PHEV):** Externally rechargeable; can do 20–80 km in EV-only mode.
>
> ✅ **LHV of gasoline = 44 MJ/kg.** SOC dynamics equation (Eq. 12) is charge-sustaining for Full HEV, charge-depleting for PHEV.
>
> ✅ **ECMS = Equivalent Consumption Minimization Strategy** — real-time EMS technique based on the equivalence factor $s(t)$.
>
> ✅ **Hybrid Controller / Energy Management Strategy (EMS)** is the *brain* — it implements CD-CS logic, ECMS, or rule-based fuzzy logic.
>
> ✅ **Toyota Hybrid System (THS)** uses a **planetary gear set** where ICE, MG1 (generator), and MG2 (motor) are connected to sun, carrier, and ring gears respectively — **THIS IS A GUARANTEED BOARD EXAM QUESTION.**
>
> ✅ **Regenerative Braking** recovers kinetic energy as electrical energy — efficiency typically **60–70 %** under realistic braking.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Induction Motors and Drives: Configuration, Controls, and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT631 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Induction Motors and Drives: Configuration, Controls, and Applications
