<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Induction Motor (IM):** A singly-excited, brushless alternating-current (AC) machine in which the rotor currents are induced electromagnetically by relative motion between the rotating magnetic field (RMF) of the stator and the stationary rotor conductors — *no physical electrical connection to the rotor is required*. In Electric and Hybrid Electric Vehicle (EV/HEV) traction applications, the **squirrel-cage induction motor** dominates due to its ruggedness, low cost, and absence of rare-earth permanent magnets.

An **Induction Motor Drive** is the integrated electromechanical power-processing system that couples a Voltage Source Inverter (VSI), a digital controller, and the IM to deliver *controllable* torque and speed to the vehicle drivetrain across the entire operating envelope.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Conveyor-Belt in a Sled-Track"

Imagine a circular playground roundabout (the **stator rotating field**) that drags a smooth hockey puck (the **rotor**) by blowing air tangentially over its surface. The puck never touches the blower; it only *responds* to the moving wind. The faster the wind blows (higher stator frequency → higher synchronous speed), the faster the puck chases. But the puck can **never** catch up to the wind — that tiny lag, called **slip**, is exactly what creates the force (torque) on the puck. Induce the current, induce the force.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics (KTU-Exam Favorite):**
> - Supply frequency for traction: **f = 50 Hz (India) / 60 Hz (USA)**, variable from **0 Hz → 200 Hz** in drive mode
> - Standard vehicle traction voltage: **400 V DC bus** (modern EVs)
> - Peak rotor speed range: **10,000 – 20,000 rpm** (often with reduction gearbox to wheel)
> - Peak traction power: **150 kW – 750 kW** depending on vehicle class

### 1.3 Why Induction Motors for EVs?

| Property | Induction Motor | Permanent Magnet (PM) Motor |
|---|---|---|
| Rotor excitation | Induced current | Permanent magnets |
| Rare-earth usage | **None** | NdFeB required |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| High-speed operation | Excellent (no magnet retention issues) | Requires careful demag analysis |
| Low-speed efficiency | Lower | Higher |
| Control complexity | Higher (FOC/DTC needed) | Lower (often trapezoidal OK) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note for KTU:** Tesla Model S (2012) and Model X use a 3-phase AC **induction motor** on the rear axle, validating the IM as a serious automotive traction machine. Nissan Leaf, by contrast, uses a PM synchronous motor.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Configuration

**Stator (Stationary Outer Assembly):**
- Laminated **silicon-steel** core (0.35 mm – 0.5 mm lamination thickness) to limit eddy-current losses at high switching frequencies.
- **Three-phase distributed winding** placed in semi-closed slots. Each phase is spatially displaced by **120° electrical**.
- Winding may be *single-layer* or *double-layer*; double-layer permits short-pitch (chorded) coils, which suppress specific MMF harmonics.

**Rotor (Rotating Inner Assembly):**
- **Squirrel-Cage Type:** Conducting bars (aluminum or copper) cast into rotor slots, short-circuited at both ends by end-rings. No slip rings, no brushes → maintenance-free.
- **Wound Rotor Type:** Three-phase insulated winding on rotor, brought out via slip rings — used historically for rotor-resistance starting (now obsolete in EVs).
- Laminated core, slots are *semi-closed* or *closed* to reduce slot harmonics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why squirrel-cage in EVs?** No slip rings → no wear → no maintenance → no brush dust → sealed rotor → IP67+ rating possible → ideal for harsh automotive underbody environment.

### 2.2 Operating Principle — The Rotating Magnetic Field (RMF)

When a **balanced 3-phase sinusoidal supply** of frequency $f$ is applied to the stator, the three time-displaced currents produce a resultant MMF wave that **rotates in space at synchronous speed**:

$$N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P} \quad \text{[rpm]}$$

where $P$ is the number of pole-pairs × 2 (total poles).

**Slip** — the *relative speed* between RMF and rotor, normalized:

$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$

**Why does the rotor turn?**
- RMF cuts rotor bars at slip frequency $f_{sl} = s \cdot f$
- EMF is induced in rotor bars: $E_{2s} = s \cdot E_2$ (where $E_2$ is the standstill rotor EMF)
- Rotor current flows through short-circuited bars: $I_2 = \dfrac{s \, E_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}}$
- This current interacts with the air-gap flux $\Phi$ to produce **Lorentz force** $F = B \ell I$, generating torque.

### 2.3 Per-Phase Equivalent Circuit (Thevenin's from Stator Side)

| Component | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Stator copper loss | $R_1$ | $I^2R$ heating in stator winding |
| Stator leakage reactance | $X_1$ | Non-air-gap flux linking only stator |
| Magnetizing reactance | $X_m$ | Models the air-gap flux linkage |
| Rotor copper loss | $R_2$ | Resistive loss in rotor bars |
| Rotor leakage reactance | $X_2$ | Leakage flux linking only rotor |
| **Slip-scaled rotor resistance** | $R_2 / s$ | The mechanical-load equivalent resistance |

The total mechanical power developed per phase is dissipated in the *fictitious* resistance $R_2 \left( \dfrac{1-s}{s} \right)$.

### 2.4 Power-Flow Stages (Mandatory for KTU Numerical)

$$\boxed{P_{in} \rightarrow P_{stator\,Cu} \rightarrow P_{air\,gap} \rightarrow P_{rotor\,Cu} \rightarrow P_{developed} \rightarrow P_{shaft}}$$

Algebraically:

$$P_{ag} = 3 \, I_2^2 \, \frac{R_2}{s}$$

$$P_{cu,rotor} = 3 \, I_2^2 \, R_2 = s \, P_{ag}$$

$$P_{mech} = P_{ag} - P_{cu,rotor} = (1 - s) \, P_{ag}$$

$$P_{shaft} = P_{mech} - P_{friction+windage} - P_{core}$$

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — it covers ~70% of numerical questions on IM in Module 1.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | rpm | $P$ = total poles |
| 2 | Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | pu | $s=0$ ideal no-load; $s=1$ standstill |
| 3 | Slip frequency | $f_{sl} = s \cdot f$ | Hz | — |
| 4 | Rotor EMF at slip $s$ | $E_{2s} = s \, E_2$ | V | Linear with slip |
| 5 | Rotor current | $I_2 = \dfrac{s \, E_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (s X_2)^2}}$ | A | — |
| 6 | Air-gap power | $P_{ag} = 3 \, I_2^2 \, \dfrac{R_2}{s}$ | W | — |
| 7 | Electromagnetic torque | $T_{em} = \dfrac{P_{ag}}{\omega_s} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V^2 (R_2/s)}{(R_1 + R_2/s)^2 + (X_1 + X_2)^2}$ | N·m | $\omega_s = 2\pi N_s/60$ |
| 8 | Slip at max torque | $s_{max} = \dfrac{R_2}{\sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2)^2}}$ | pu | $R_1$ often neglected → $s_{max} \approx R_2 / X_2$ |
| 9 | Max (pull-out) torque | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3 V^2}{2 \omega_s (R_1 + \sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1+X_2)^2})}$ | N·m | Independent of $R_2$ |
| 10 | V/f ratio (constant flux) | $\dfrac{V}{f} = \text{const} = \dfrac{V_{rated}}{f_{rated}}$ | V/Hz | Scalar control law |
| 11 | Per-unit slip in EV mode | $s \in [-0.3,\, 0.05]$ (motoring + regen) | pu | Negative $s$ → generator mode |
| 12 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{(1-s)P_{ag} - P_{losses}}{P_{ag} + P_{stator\,Cu}}$ | % | Typical 92–96% in traction |

> [!CAUTION]
> In the table above, all instances of absolute value, modulus, and vertical separators are written as $\vert \cdot \vert$ or via parentheses for clarity. KTU examiners penalize ambiguous math; always show units.

### 2.6 Engineering Utility in EV/HEV

- **Constant-Torque Region (low speed, $0 \le N \le N_{base}$):** V/f held constant, flux held constant → torque limit is set by current limit. This is the **launch / hill-climb** regime.
- **Constant-Power Region (high speed, $N_{base} \le N \le N_{max}$):** Voltage saturates at $V_{DC\,bus}/\sqrt{3}$. Flux is *weakened* (field-weakening region). $V/f$ ratio drops; torque falls as $1/N$. This is the **highway cruising** regime.
- **Field-Weakening Ratio:** $k_{fw} = V_{rated} / (f \cdot V/f_{rated})$ — used to compute the deep-FW operating point in Tesla drivetrains.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Air-Gap Torque from First Principles

**Given:** 3-phase IM, per-phase equivalent circuit with $R_1, X_1, X_m, R_2/s, X_2$ referred to stator.

**Step 1:** Thevenin equivalent at the air-gap terminals (looking back from the magnetizing branch into the source):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{th} &= V \cdot \frac{jX_m}{R_1 + j(X_1 + X_m)} \\
Z_{th} &= \frac{jX_m (R_1 + jX_1)}{R_1 + j(X_1 + X_m)} = \frac{jX_m (R_1 + jX_1)(R_1 - j(X_1+X_m))}{\vert R_1 + j(X_1+X_m) \vert^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

For KTU problems, we **neglect $R_1$ in $Z_{th}$** to simplify (this is the standard assumption at the board level). Then:

$$V_{th} \approx V \cdot \frac{X_m}{X_1 + X_m}, \quad Z_{th} \approx \frac{j X_1 X_m}{X_1 + X_m}$$

**Step 2:** Rotor-side current (Thevenin-loop):

$$I_2 = \frac{V_{th}}{R_2/s + j X_2 + Z_{th}}$$

**Step 3:** Air-gap power is the *real power* delivered to the rotor branch:

$$P_{ag} = 3 \, \vert I_2 \vert^2 \cdot \frac{R_2}{s}$$

**Step 4:** Electromagnetic torque is air-gap power divided by synchronous angular speed $\omega_s = 2\pi f / (P/2)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{em} &= \frac{P_{ag}}{\omega_s} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V_{th}^2 (R_2/s)}{(R_2/s)^2 + (X_2 + X_{th})^2} \\
&= \frac{3 V_{th}^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{1}{s(R_2/s^2) + s(X_2 + X_{th})^2 / R_2} \quad \text{[differentiate w.r.t. } s \text{]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** To find the slip at maximum torque, set $\dfrac{dT_{em}}{ds} = 0$. After differentiation, the standard result:

$$\boxed{\, s_{max} = \frac{R_2}{\sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2)^2}}\,}$$

And substituting back:

$$T_{max} = \frac{3 V^2}{2 \, \omega_s \left[ R_1 + \sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1+X_2)^2} \right]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** $T_{max}$ is **independent of rotor resistance $R_2$**! This is why modern IMs cannot be soft-started by increasing $R_2$ at the design stage — but the *slip* at which it occurs *does* depend on $R_2$, which is exploited in wound-rotor slip-ring drives (rare in EVs).

### 3.2 Derivation — V/f Control Law for Constant Flux

The air-gap flux $\Phi$ is proportional to $V/f$ (ignoring stator impedance drop):

$$\Phi = k \cdot \frac{V}{f}$$

To maintain **rated flux** across the entire speed range (and thus rated torque capability):

$$\frac{V}{f} = \frac{V_{rated}}{f_{rated}} = \text{constant}$$

**Compensation for low-frequency stator drop** (KTU favorite sub-question):

At very low $f$, the stator drop $I_1 R_1$ and $I_1 X_1$ become comparable to $V$. So the *modified* V/f law is:

$$V = V_0 + k_v \cdot f, \quad \text{where } V_0 \approx I_1 (R_1 \cos\phi + X_1 \sin\phi)$$

This adds a small **voltage boost** $V_0$ at $f=0$ to recover the lost flux.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Operating Modes of an IM Drive in an EV

| Mode | Speed Range | Flux | V/f | Torque Limit | Power Limit | Real Vehicle Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Motoring (Launch)** | 0 – $N_{base}$ | Rated | Constant | Max (current-limited) | Ramps up | 0–60 km/h acceleration |
| **Motoring (Cruise)** | $N_{base}$ – $N_{rated}$ | Reducing (FW) | $V$ saturated | Falling $\propto 1/N$ | Constant | Highway 80 km/h |
| **Motoring (Top speed)** | $N_{rated}$ – $N_{max}$ | Deep FW | $V$ saturated | $1/N^2$ | Falling | Maximum speed run |
| **Regenerative Braking** | $N > 0$, $s < 0$ | Rated | $V/f$ | Negative (braking) | Recovered to battery | Downhill / brake pedal |
| **Plugging** | Brief, $s > 1$ | — | Reversed phase | Negative | — | Emergency stop (avoid in EV) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — IM Drive System in an EV/HEV

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    batt[Battery Pack 400 V DC] -->|DC Bus| dcdc[DC DC Converter]
    dcdc -->|12V / 24V Aux| auxAux[Vehicle Auxiliaries]
    batt -->|High Voltage DC| inv[Three Phase VSI Inverter]
    inv -->|PWM Variable V and f| term[3 Phase Stator Terminals]
    term --> im[Squirrel Cage Induction Motor]
    im -->|Mechanical Shaft| gbx[Reduction Gearbox]
    gbx -->|Drive Shaft| wheel[Vehicle Wheel]
    speedSensor[Wheelspeed Sensor] --> vcu[VCU Vehicle Control Unit]
    tps[Throttle Pedal Position] --> vcu
    brakePedal[Brake Pedal Sensor] --> vcu
    vcu -->|Torque Reference| inv
    im -->|Resolver Encoder| inv
    tempS[Stator Temp Sensor] --> vcu
    inv -->|DC Link Regen| batt
```

### 4.2 Flowchart — V/f Scalar Control Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start]) --> read[Read Speed Reference Nref]
    read --> calcF[Compute fstar = Nref times P over 120]
    calcF --> calcV[Compute Vstar = VoverF times fstar plus V0 boost]
    calcV --> mod[Generate 3 Phase SPWM or SVPWM Modulation]
    mod --> gate[Fire IGBT Gate Signals to Inverter]
    gate --> measure[Measure Actual Speed Nact and DC Bus Vdc]
    measure --> close[Close Loop: Adjust Nref and VoverF]
    close --> read
```

### 4.3 Flowchart — Indirect Field Oriented Control IFOC

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    a([Torque Request Tstar]) --> b[Park Inverse Transform Idr Iqr]
    c[Field Reference Idstar] --> b
    b --> d[PI Current Controllers Id Iq]
    d --> e[Inverse Park Vdr Vqr]
    f[Rotor Flux Angle Theta e] --> e
    e --> g[SVPWM Modulator]
    g --> h[Inverter Switching IGBT]
    h --> i[Induction Motor]
    i --> j[Encoder Resolver]
    j --> k[Slip and Speed Estimator]
    k --> f
    j --> l[Current Sensors Iabc]
    l --> m[Clarke and Park Transform]
    m --> d
```

### 4.4 Mechanical Cross-Section — Squirrel-Cage IM (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Stator
        A1[Stator Laminated Core]
        A2[Three Phase Distributed Winding in Slots]
        A3[Frame and Cooling Jacket]
    end
    subgraph AirGap
        G1[Air Gap 0.5 to 2 mm]
    end
    subgraph Rotor
        R1[Rotor Laminated Core]
        R2[Conductive Bars Aluminum or Copper]
        R3[End Ring Short Circuit]
        R4[Shaft]
    end
    A2 -->|RMF| G1
    G1 -->|Induced EMF| R2
    R2 -->|Circulating Current| R3
    R3 -->|Lorentz Force| R1
    R1 -->|Torque| R4
```

> [!NOTE]
> The cooling jacket in traction IMs typically carries **water-glycol coolant** (50:50 mix) at ~65 °C, with the stator end-windings spray-cooled or potted for thermal management during repeated launch cycles.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List the three major losses in a 3-phase induction motor and state how each varies with mechanical load (i.e., with slip $s$).*

**Model Answer:**
- **Stator copper loss** $P_{cu1} = 3 I_1^2 R_1$: nearly constant with load, varies only mildly because $I_1 \approx I_m$ at no-load and rises with load.
- **Rotor copper loss** $P_{cu2} = 3 I_2^2 R_2 = s \cdot P_{ag}$: **directly proportional to slip** $s$ — this is the defining load-dependent loss in an IM.
- **Core (iron) loss** $P_{core}$: approximately **constant** with mechanical load, depends mainly on $V$ and $f$; in V/f control it is held near-constant.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Explain why the slip of an induction motor cannot be zero under loaded conditions. What is the typical slip range of a commercial EV traction induction motor at rated torque?*

**Model Answer:**
Slip is the *relative* speed between the rotating magnetic field and the rotor. If $s = 0$, the rotor would rotate at synchronous speed $N_s$, and there would be **no relative motion** between flux and rotor conductors → no induced EMF, no rotor current, no torque. Hence, the motor *cannot* sustain load torque at $s = 0$.

For a squirrel-cage EV traction IM, the rated slip is typically $s \approx 0.01$ to $0.05$ (1–5%), much lower than industrial motors (3–7%), because traction IMs are designed for **high efficiency and high-speed operation**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> (1) When asked for *operating slip*, draw the power-flow stages and clearly distinguish $P_{ag}$ from $P_{mech}$ — losing 1 mark on this is the most common error.
> (2) In V/f derivations, **never** write $\dfrac{V}{f} = \text{const}$ *without* specifying the low-frequency boost correction; examiners test this explicitly.
> (3) In vector control, clearly state that *FOC decouples flux and torque control*, and the rotor-flux angle $\theta_e$ is the heart of the transformation — examiners allocate 2 marks for this conceptual clarity.
> (4) Show *units* in every step. Numerical errors in unit conversion cost 1 mark each.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Equivalent Circuit + Torque Derivation**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz squirrel-cage induction motor has the following per-phase parameters referred to the stator: $R_1 = 0.5 \, \Omega$, $R_2 = 0.6 \, \Omega$, $X_1 = X_2 = 1.0 \, \Omega$, $X_m = 50 \, \Omega$. The motor is fed from a 400 V (line-to-line) V/f-controlled inverter. Compute the synchronous speed, the slip at maximum torque, and the maximum torque.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Explain with a neat block diagram the working of **Indirect Field-Oriented Control (IFOC)** of an induction motor drive. What are the two PI controllers used, and what physical quantities do they regulate?

**Model Solution to Q-A (a):**

Given: $P = 4$, $f = 50$ Hz, $V_{LL} = 400$ V → $V_{ph} = 400/\sqrt{3} = 230.94$ V.

**Step 1 — Synchronous speed:**

$$N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$

$$\omega_s = \frac{2 \pi N_s}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Slip at maximum torque:**

$$s_{max} = \frac{R_2}{\sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1 + X_2)^2}} = \frac{0.6}{\sqrt{0.5^2 + (1.0 + 1.0)^2}} = \frac{0.6}{\sqrt{0.25 + 4}} = \frac{0.6}{2.0616} = 0.291$$

**Step 3 — Maximum torque:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{max} &= \frac{3 V_{ph}^2}{2 \, \omega_s \left[ R_1 + \sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1+X_2)^2} \right]} \\
&= \frac{3 \times (230.94)^2}{2 \times 157.08 \times \left[ 0.5 + 2.0616 \right]} \\
&= \frac{159{,}999.4}{2 \times 157.08 \times 2.5616} \\
&= \frac{159{,}999.4}{804.84} \\
&\approx 198.8 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answers:** $N_s = 1500$ rpm, $s_{max} = 0.291$, $T_{max} \approx 198.8$ N·m.

**Model Solution to Q-A (b):**

Indirect Field-Oriented Control (IFOC) decouples the IM's stator current into two orthogonal components in the *rotor-flux reference frame*:

- **$i_d$ (flux-producing component):** aligned with rotor-flux axis $\rightarrow$ regulates $\Phi_r$ (air-gap flux). PI controller output: $V_d$ reference.
- **$i_q$ (torque-producing component):** orthogonal to rotor-flux axis $\rightarrow$ regulates $T_{em}$ (electromagnetic torque). PI controller output: $V_q$ reference.

**Block diagram (already in Section 4.3) consists of:**

1. Outer torque/flux reference from VCU.
2. $dq$-axis PI current controllers (one for $i_d$, one for $i_q$).
3. Inverse Park transform using rotor-flux angle $\theta_e$.
4. SVPWM modulator → IGBT gating signals.
5. Encoder/resolver feedback for speed; current sensors for $i_a, i_b, i_c$ → Clarke → Park transform.

**Slip estimation:**

$$\omega_{sl} = \frac{R_2}{L_r} \cdot \frac{i_q}{i_d}, \quad \theta_e = \int (\omega_r + \omega_{sl}) \, dt$$

The two PI controllers thus independently regulate **flux** ($d$-axis) and **torque** ($q$-axis), achieving DC-machine-like dynamic response from an AC IM.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — V/f Control + EV Application**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **V/f control law** for a 3-phase induction motor drive used in an EV. Why is a low-frequency voltage boost $V_0$ necessary, and how is it computed? Sketch the V/f characteristic.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss with a comparative table the **advantages and limitations of squirrel-cage induction motors** as traction machines in Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs). Mention two real-world production EVs that use induction motors.

**Model Solution to Q-B (a):**

The air-gap flux in an IM is:

$$\Phi = \frac{1}{4.44 \, k_w N f} \cdot \frac{V}{\sqrt{(R_1 \cos\phi + R_2' \cos\phi / s)^2 + \ldots}} \approx \frac{V}{k \cdot f}$$

For **constant flux** in the constant-torque region:

$$\boxed{\frac{V}{f} = \text{const} = \frac{V_{rated}}{f_{rated}}}$$

**Low-frequency correction (voltage boost $V_0$):** At very low $f$, the stator copper and leakage-impedance drops $I_1 (R_1 + jX_1)$ become comparable to $V$, causing flux reduction. To compensate:

$$V = V_0 + \left( \frac{V_{rated}}{f_{rated}} \right) \cdot f$$

where $V_0$ is the **boost voltage**, typically computed as:

$$V_0 = I_1 \left( R_1 \cos\phi + X_1 \sin\phi \right)$$

In EV drives, $V_0$ is calibrated at $f \approx 0.5$ to $2$ Hz and applied as a fixed offset in the PWM modulator.

**Sketch of V/f characteristic:**
- Straight line with positive slope $V_{rated}/f_{rated}$ passing through origin (ideal).
- In practice: a **clipped line** with positive $V$-intercept $V_0$, going up to $(f_{rated}, V_{rated})$, then horizontal (voltage-limited, field-weakening region).

**Model Solution to Q-B (b):**

| Aspect | Advantage | Limitation |
|---|---|---|
| Construction | Rugged squirrel-cage, no brushes, no slip rings | Heavier than PM motors for same power |
| Cost | Low — no rare-earth magnets | Higher inverter cost (complex control) |
| Maintenance | Almost zero (sealed rotor) | Bearing-only wear items |
| High-speed | Excellent — no magnet demag risk | Needs reduction gearbox (typically 9:1) |
| Low-speed efficiency | Lower (~85%) than PM | Cooling demands high at low-speed launch |
| Control | Mature FOC, DTC algorithms | Sensor (encoder) often required |
| Reliability | High (no PM, no brush) | Rotor bar failure possible in harsh env. |

**Real-world EVs using induction motors:**
- **Tesla Model S / X / 3 / Y** — rear (and sometimes front) axle IM with SiC inverter.
- **Rivian R1T / R1S** — quad-motor configuration using IMs.
- **Mercedes-Benz EQS 4Matic** (rear axle variant).
- **General Motors EV1 (1996)** — historical landmark; 3-phase AC IM, 137 hp.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1 / IM Drives**

- [x] **Construction:** Stator (3-phase, distributed, laminated) + Squirrel-cage rotor (cast Al/Cu bars, end-rings) + Air gap 0.5–2 mm.
- [x] **Synchronous speed:** $N_s = 120 f / P$ (rpm).
- [x] **Slip:** $s = (N_s - N_r) / N_s$; regen mode $s < 0$.
- [x] **Power flow:** $P_{in} \rightarrow P_{sc} \rightarrow P_{ag} \rightarrow P_{rc} (= s P_{ag}) \rightarrow P_{mech} ((1-s) P_{ag}) \rightarrow P_{shaft}$.
- [x] **Torque equation:** $T = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V^2 (R_2/s)}{(R_1 + R_2/s)^2 + (X_1+X_2)^2}$.
- [x] **Max torque slip:** $s_{max} = R_2 / \sqrt{R_1^2 + (X_1+X_2)^2}$; $T_{max}$ independent of $R_2$.
- [x] **V/f control:** $V/f = $ const; low-frequency boost $V_0$ compensates stator drop.
- [x] **Field weakening:** $V$ saturates at $V_{bus}/\sqrt{3}$ above base speed; flux drops as $1/N$.
- [x] **FOC/IFOC:** Decouples flux ($d$-axis) and torque ($q$-axis) via rotor-flux angle $\theta_e$.
- [x] **DTC:** Hysteresis-based, very fast torque response (~1 ms), no PI current loops.
- [x] **EV applications:** Tesla (RWD), Rivian (quad-motor), Mercedes EQS — all use IMs.
- [x] **Typical EV traction IM specs:** 150–750 kW, 10,000–20,000 rpm, 400 V DC bus, 92–96% efficiency.
- [x] **Inverter:** 3-phase VSI with IGBT or SiC MOSFETs; SPWM or SVPWM modulation.
- [x] **Cooling:** Water-glycol jacket; stator end-windings spray-cooled.
- [x] **No rare-earth magnets** → cost and supply-chain advantage over PMSM.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Permanent Magnet Motors: Neodymium, Ferrite, and Samarium Cobalt Types and Drives Configuration
Brushless DC Motor (BLDC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT631 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Brushless DC Motor (BLDC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔵 MODULE 1 — Brushless DC Motor (BLDC)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📘 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

A **Brushless DC (BLDC) Motor** is a *synchronous* electric machine in which the **rotor** is constructed using **permanent magnets (PMs)** that produce a constant air-gap flux, while the **stator** carries a **three-phase concentrated or distributed winding**. The DC supply is electronically converted into a three-phase AC by an inverter, and the resulting stator field is rotated in synchronism with the rotor by means of a **rotor-position feedback signal** (Hall sensors, resolvers, or sensorless estimators). Because commutation is performed *electronically* rather than by carbon brushes, the motor is termed "brushless DC."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PEMUT631 — Module 1):**
> BLDC motors are listed as the *primary* traction candidate for two-wheeler and light four-wheeler EV platforms due to their **high power density (typically 3–5 kW/kg)**, **flat efficiency map (>90 % over a wide speed range)**, and **absence of brush wear** — making them the default choice for Indian EV drivetrains studied in this module.

### 💡 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Think of a **ceiling fan without its mechanical regulator**. A regular fan has **carbon brushes** sliding against a copper ring to switch the supply to its windings. A BLDC motor throws the brushes into a small electronic **microcontroller + inverter** circuit instead.

* **The rotor (magnets)** is the fan blades.
* **The stator coils** are the stationary frame.
* **The Hall sensor** is the "eye" that tells the controller where the blades are pointing at every instant.
* **The inverter switches (IGBT/MOSFET)** are the "fingers" that, based on that eye's information, push current into the *correct* stator coil at the *correct* moment — creating a rotating magnetic field that perpetually chases the rotor.

In effect, a BLDC motor is a **DC motor turned inside-out**: the magnets rotate and the coils stay still, with an *electronic brain* replacing the noisy, sparky brush assembly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Designation Rule (for KTU exams):**
> BLDC is fed from a *DC link* but produces a *trapezoidal* (or quasi-square) stator current waveform. Despite the name "DC", its internal operation is that of a *3-phase synchronous AC machine* with permanent-magnet excitation. Confusing this with a brushed DC motor costs students 1–2 marks routinely.

### 🔑 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics to Memorise

* **Air-gap flux density** in a typical BLDC rotor: **0.6 T – 1.1 T** (using **NdFeB N35–N52** magnets).
* **Supply voltage** in Indian EVs: **48 V / 72 V / 96 V** (low-voltage two-wheeler) up to **400 V / 800 V** (passenger cars).
* **Base / corner speed** of a typical 1 kW hub BLDC: **3000–5000 rpm**.
* **Torque per ampere constant (Kt)** and **Back-EMF constant (Ke)** are **numerically equal** in SI units: **Kt (Nm/A) = Ke (V·s/rad)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Architecture (Rotor Variants)

A KTU examiner expects the following classification of rotor magnet arrangements:

1. **Surface-Mounted Permanent Magnet (SPM) rotor** — magnets glued on the outer surface of a ferromagnetic core. Provides a **trapezoidal air-gap flux** and is therefore preferred for *trapezoidal back-EMF* BLDCs.
2. **Interior Permanent Magnet (IPM) rotor** — magnets buried *inside* rotor slots. Gives a *sinusoidal* flux distribution and exploits **reluctance torque** in addition to magnet torque, preferred for sinusoidal back-EMF motors.
3. **Inset / Spoke / V-shaped magnet rotors** — variants used in high-speed traction BLDCs.

### 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

* **Step 1 (Position sensing):** Three Hall-effect sensors (H_A, H_B, H_C) placed 120° electrical apart generate a 3-bit digital code that uniquely identifies the rotor's electrical quadrant.
* **Step 2 (Controller decision):** The microcontroller looks up the *commutation table* and decides which two of the three phases must be *energised* and which must be *floating* during the next 60° electrical interval.
* **Step 3 (Inverter switching):** Six MOSFET/IGBT switches in a 3-phase bridge (per IEEE std. labelling: Q1–Q6) are turned ON/OFF in a fixed sequence. Only *two phases conduct at any instant* (six-step / 120° conduction mode).
* **Step 4 (Field rotation):** The stator MMF jumps in 60° steps, dragging the PM rotor with it. The average mechanical rotation is therefore smooth, although the underlying stator field is *staircase-shaped*.
* **Step 5 (Back-EMF generation):** As the rotor magnets sweep past the stator teeth, a **trapezoidal back-EMF** is induced in each phase. The inverter must time the *current* waveform to be in phase with the *flat top* of the back-EMF for maximum torque.
* **Step 6 (Speed control):** Speed is varied by **PWM chopping** of the DC-link voltage applied to the active switches. Below base speed, PWM adjusts the average phase voltage; above base speed, *field-weakening* advances commutation to keep the back-EMF within the DC-link limit.

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Six-Step Commutation

Out of the six switches in the 3-phase inverter, only the **upper switch of one phase and the lower switch of a different phase** are turned ON simultaneously. This routes current from the **positive DC bus** through phase A (say) and returns it through phase B to the **negative DC bus**, while phase C is left floating. After 60°, the pattern shifts to A+ / C−, then B+ / C−, and so on. This *two-quadrant* energisation is what gives the BLDC its **trapezoidal current** waveform and its *brushless DC* character.

### 2.4 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Examination-Ready)

| # | Quantity | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Per-phase back-EMF | $E \;=\; K_e \,\omega$ | $K_e$ in $\mathrm{V\cdot s/rad}$, $\omega$ in $\mathrm{rad/s}$ |
| 2 | Electromagnetic Torque | $T_e \;=\; K_t \,I_a$ | $K_t$ in $\mathrm{Nm/A}$, $I_a$ in $\mathrm{A}$ |
| 3 | Numerical Identity | $K_t \;\equiv\; K_e$ | Valid in SI units (Nm/A and V·s/rad) |
| 4 | Per-phase Voltage Eqn. | $V \;=\; E \;+\; I_a R_a \;+\; L_a\,\dfrac{dI_a}{dt}$ | Steady-state: $V \approx E + I_a R_a$ |
| 5 | Steady-state Speed | $\omega \;=\; \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{K_e}$ | $\mathrm{rad/s}$ |
| 6 | No-load Speed | $\omega_0 \;=\; \dfrac{V}{K_e}$ | When $I_a \rightarrow 0$ |
| 7 | Stall Torque | $T_{stall} \;=\; \dfrac{K_t \,V}{R_a}$ | At $\omega = 0$, $E = 0$ |
| 8 | Mechanical Power | $P_{m} \;=\; T_e \,\omega$ | Watts |
| 9 | Electrical Power Input | $P_{in} \;=\; 3 \,V_{ph} \,I_{ph}$ | For 3-phase, balanced |
| 10 | Power Losses (Copper) | $P_{cu} \;=\; 3\,I_{ph}^{\,2}\,R_{ph}$ | Stator copper loss |
| 11 | Efficiency | $\eta \;=\; \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \;=\; 1 - \dfrac{P_{loss}}{P_{in}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 12 | Torque per Rotor Volume | $\dfrac{T}{V_{rot}} \;=\; 1.4\,K_0\,K_\phi\,K_i\,A\,B_g \cdot 10^{-6}$ | SI BLDC design constant, $B_g$ in Tesla |
| 13 | Electrical / Mech. Speed | $\omega_e \;=\; \dfrac{P}{2}\,\omega_m$ | $P$ = number of pole pairs |
| 14 | Reluctance Torque (IPM) | $T_{rel} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2}\,i^{\,2}\,\dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$ | Only in IPM BLDCs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Real-World Utility:** The relation $K_t \equiv K_e$ is the single most-tested identity in the KTU ESE. It allows a designer who has measured the back-EMF of a BLDC (a quick bench test) to *immediately* know its torque constant without any torque-rig experiment — saving hours of EV drivetrain calibration.

### 2.5 Speed–Torque Characteristic (Why BLDCs Suit EV Duty Cycles)

The BLDC speed–torque curve is a *straight descending line* between no-load speed $\omega_0$ and stall torque $T_{stall}$. In an EV context this is **ideal** because:

* The driver demands *high torque at low speed* (hill start, launch) — the BLDC delivers its **maximum torque** here.
* At cruising speed, the motor naturally settles to *low torque / high speed* — the **high-efficiency zone**.

This intrinsic linearity is why a BLDC requires *no transmission* in most two-wheeler EVs and only a *single-speed reduction* in many four-wheeler EVs (Tata Nexon EV, Ather 450X, Ola S1 Pro).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Back-EMF Constant from Faraday's Law

The back-EMF induced in a single stator phase is obtained by differentiating the flux linkage with respect to rotor position.

$$
e(t) \;=\; \frac{d\lambda}{dt} \;=\; \frac{d\lambda}{d\theta_{m}}\,\frac{d\theta_{m}}{dt}
$$

Here $\theta_m$ is the mechanical angle of the rotor and $\omega_m = d\theta_m/dt$ is its mechanical angular velocity.

**Step 1 (Flux-linkage gradient):** For a surface-mounted PM BLDC with $N$ turns per phase spanning a pole pitch, the flux linkage per radian of mechanical rotation is constant over the flat-trapezoidal region, so

$$
\frac{d\lambda}{d\theta_{m}} \;=\; \frac{P}{2}\,N\,\Phi_{g}
$$

where $P$ is the total number of poles, $\Phi_g$ is the air-gap flux per pole (in webers), and the factor $P/2$ converts mechanical to electrical radians.

**Step 2 (Substitute $\omega_m$):** Multiplying by the mechanical angular velocity gives the instantaneous back-EMF during the constant-flux region:

$$
E \;=\; \frac{P}{2}\,N\,\Phi_{g}\,\omega_{m}
$$

**Step 3 (Define the back-EMF constant):** Comparing with the compact form $E = K_e\,\omega_m$, we identify

$$
K_e \;=\; \frac{P}{2}\,N\,\Phi_{g} \quad \bigl[\mathrm{V\cdot s/rad}\bigr]
$$

**Step 4 (Engineering interpretation):** Doubling the *number of poles* (P) or the *flux per pole* ($\Phi_g$) linearly increases $K_e$. Designers therefore increase $K_e$ by using **rare-earth magnets (NdFeB)** with high residual flux density $B_r \approx 1.1$–$1.4\,\mathrm{T}$ to maximise $\Phi_g$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Electromagnetic Torque from Power Balance

For a lossless conversion (inverter and copper losses neglected for clarity), the electrical power entering the motor from the DC link equals the mechanical power developed at the shaft.

$$
P_{electrical} \;=\; P_{mechanical}
$$

**Step 1 (Electrical side — 3 phases, two conducting at any instant):** During each 60° sector, two phases carry current $I_a$ from the DC link, so the DC-link current equals $I_a$ and the DC-link voltage is $V_{dc}$. Electrical input is

$$
P_{in} \;=\; V_{dc}\,I_{a} \;=\; 2\,E_{ph}\,I_{a}
$$

(the factor of 2 arises because two phases simultaneously experience the back-EMF $E_{ph}$).

**Step 2 (Mechanical side):** The developed electromagnetic torque $T_e$ acting at mechanical angular velocity $\omega_m$ delivers

$$
P_{m} \;=\; T_e\,\omega_{m}
$$

**Step 3 (Equate and use $E_{ph} = K_e\,\omega_m$):**

$$
T_e\,\omega_{m} \;=\; 2\,(K_e\,\omega_m)\,I_a
$$

**Step 4 (Cancel $\omega_m$, solve for $T_e$):**

$$
T_e \;=\; 2\,K_e\,I_a
$$

**Step 5 (Phase current and effective constant):** If we re-define the per-phase current such that the *line current from the DC link* is $I_{dc}$ and the *phase current* is $I_{ph}$ (which equals $I_{dc}$ in two-quadrant conduction), and use the standard $K_t = 2 K_e$ factor for 3-phase BLDCs, we get the canonical form

$$
T_e \;=\; K_t\,I_{ph}, \quad \text{with}\;\; K_t \;=\; 2\,K_e
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the factor of 2?** A brushed DC motor uses *one* active conductor path at a time, whereas a 3-phase BLDC uses *two* phases in series. With both phases contributing torque simultaneously, the effective torque per ampere is doubled, which is part of the reason BLDCs deliver a higher torque density than their brushed counterparts.

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Steady-State Speed Equation

Starting from the per-phase steady-state voltage equation (inductance drop vanishes because $dI/dt = 0$ in steady state for a square-wave current):

$$
V_{ph} \;=\; E_{ph} \;+\; I_a R_a
$$

**Step 1 (Substitute the back-EMF identity $E_{ph} = K_e \omega$):**

$$
V_{ph} \;=\; K_e\,\omega \;+\; I_a R_a
$$

**Step 2 (Rearrange for $\omega$):**

$$
\omega \;=\; \frac{V_{ph} - I_a R_a}{K_e}
$$

**Step 3 (No-load condition $I_a = 0$):**

$$
\omega_0 \;=\; \frac{V_{ph}}{K_e}
$$

**Step 4 (Stall condition $\omega = 0$):**

$$
I_{stall} \;=\; \frac{V_{ph}}{R_a}, \quad T_{stall} \;=\; K_t\,I_{stall} \;=\; \frac{K_t\,V_{ph}}{R_a}
$$

---

### 3.4 📋 Analytical Matrix — Trapezoidal vs Sinusoidal BLDC

| Parameter | Trapezoidal BLDC | Sinusoidal BLDC (PMSM variant) |
|---|---|---|
| Back-EMF shape | Trapezoidal (flat top) | Pure sine |
| Stator current shape | Square-wave (120° conduction) | Sinusoidal (180° conduction) |
| Torque ripple | ~ 13 % (theoretical minimum) | < 1 % (theoretically) |
| Position sensor | 3 Hall sensors (low resolution) | Resolver / encoder (high resolution) |
| Controller complexity | Low (6-step commutation) | High (FOC / vector control) |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| NVH (Noise, Vibration) | Slightly higher | Very low |
| EV application | Two/three-wheelers, budget drives | Premium 4-wheelers, racing EVs |
| Stator winding | Concentrated (short-pitched) | Distributed (sinusoidal EMF) |

### 3.5 📋 Comparative Matrix — Brushed DC vs BLDC

| Feature | Brushed DC | BLDC |
|---|---|---|
| Commutation | Mechanical (brush-commutator) | Electronic (inverter + sensors) |
| Maintenance | High (brush wear) | Negligible |
| Life | 1 000 – 3 000 hrs | 10 000 – 20 000 hrs |
| Efficiency | 70 – 80 % | 85 – 95 % |
| Power density | Low | High |
| Cost | Low | Higher (inverter needed) |
| Rotor inertia | High (wound armature) | Low (PM rotor) |
| Cooling | Easier (armature in stator air gap) | More involved (stator only) |
| EMI / RFI | From brush sparking | From inverter switching |
| EV adoption | Obsolete for traction | Industry standard |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Safeguard Notes

* All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `node1`, `phaseA`, `inverter1`).
* No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, `style`) used as standalone node names.
* Node labels contain **plain uppercase alphanumeric text only** — no bold, italics, or HTML tags.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a BLDC Drive (EV Context)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    batt1["BATTERY PACK DC LINK"]
    dcdc1["DC DC CONVERTER AUXILIARY"]
    inv1["THREE PHASE VOLTAGE SOURCE INVERTER"]
    ctrl1["VEHICLE CONTROL UNIT VCU"]
    pwm1["PWM GENERATOR"]
    drv1["GATE DRIVER STAGE"]
    mot1["BLDC MOTOR STATOR THREE PHASE"]
    rot1["PM ROTOR"]
    hall1["HALL POSITION SENSORS H A H B H C"]
    tps1["THROTTLE PEDAL SENSOR"]
    brk1["BRAKE SENSOR"]
    fbk1["CURRENT SENSE RESISTORS"]
    load1["VEHICLE WHEELS VIA SINGLE SPEED GEARBOX"]

    batt1 --> dcdc1
    batt1 --> inv1
    dcdc1 --> ctrl1
    tps1 --> ctrl1
    brk1 --> ctrl1
    ctrl1 --> pwm1
    hall1 --> ctrl1
    fbk1 --> ctrl1
    pwm1 --> drv1
    drv1 --> inv1
    inv1 --> mot1
    mot1 --> rot1
    rot1 --> hall1
    mot1 --> load1
```

### 4.3 Six-Step Commutation Sequence State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> S1_STATE
    S1_STATE --> S2_STATE : HALL 101 TO 100
    S2_STATE --> S3_STATE : HALL 100 TO 110
    S3_STATE --> S4_STATE : HALL 110 TO 010
    S4_STATE --> S5_STATE : HALL 010 TO 011
    S5_STATE --> S6_STATE : HALL 011 TO 001
    S6_STATE --> S1_STATE : HALL 001 TO 101
    S1_STATE : PHASE A POSITIVE PHASE B NEGATIVE
    S2_STATE : PHASE A POSITIVE PHASE C NEGATIVE
    S3_STATE : PHASE B POSITIVE PHASE C NEGATIVE
    S4_STATE : PHASE B POSITIVE PHASE A NEGATIVE
    S5_STATE : PHASE C POSITIVE PHASE A NEGATIVE
    S6_STATE : PHASE C POSITIVE PHASE B NEGATIVE
```

### 4.4 Three-Phase Inverter Topology (Six-Switch Bridge)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DCp["DC PLUS BUS"] --> Q1["Q1 UPPER PHASE A"]
    Q1 --> Aout["PHASE A WINDING TERMINAL"]
    DCn["DC MINUS BUS"] --> Q4["Q4 LOWER PHASE A"]
    Aout --> Q4
    DCp --> Q2["Q2 UPPER PHASE B"]
    Q2 --> Bout["PHASE B WINDING TERMINAL"]
    DCn --> Q5["Q5 LOWER PHASE B"]
    Bout --> Q5
    DCp --> Q3["Q3 UPPER PHASE C"]
    Q3 --> Cout["PHASE C WINDING TERMINAL"]
    DCn --> Q6["Q6 LOWER PHASE C"]
    Cout --> Q6
```

### 4.5 Power-Flow Block Diagram (Battery → Wheel)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pack1["LITHIUM ION BATTERY PACK 48V TO 400V"] --> bms1["BMS PROTECTION AND CELL BALANCING"]
    bms1 --> dclink1["DC LINK CAPACITOR BUS"]
    dclink1 --> inv2["VSI INVERTER IGBT OR SIC MOSFET"]
    inv2 --> bldc1["BLDC TRACTION MOTOR"]
    bldc1 --> reducer1["SINGLE SPEED REDUCTION GEARBOX"]
    reducer1 --> diff1["DIFFERENTIAL UNIT"]
    diff1 --> wheel1["DRIVE WHEELS"]
    bldc1 -.FEEDBACK.-> ctrl2["VCU TORQUE AND SPEED CONTROLLER"]
    ctrl2 -.PWM GATE SIGNALS.-> inv2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mark Distribution Reference:**
> * Part A: Short answer, **3 marks each**, 30–50 words.
> * Part B: Descriptive, **14 marks each**, with *internal choice* (Q-A or Q-B). Sub-parts typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.
> * Cognitive levels: Apply (L3), Analyse (L4), Design (L5) are emphasised under NEP 2020.

---

### 📝 Part A — 3-Mark Questions

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between a Brushed DC motor and a Brushless DC (BLDC) motor in terms of construction, commutation and maintenance.**

**Model Answer:**
A Brushed DC motor has its armature winding on the *rotor* and field winding (or PM) on the *stator*; current reversal is achieved mechanically by a *brush-commutator* assembly, which is subject to wear and demands periodic maintenance. A BLDC motor *inverts* this arrangement: the permanent magnets are on the *rotor* and the three-phase windings are on the *stator*. Commutation is *electronic*, performed by a microcontroller-driven inverter using rotor-position feedback (Hall sensors). Consequently, BLDC motors have **higher efficiency (90–95 %)**, **longer life (>20 000 hrs)**, **higher power density**, and **virtually zero maintenance**, making them the standard for EV traction. (3 marks)

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why are BLDC motors preferred for Electric Vehicle traction applications? List any four reasons.**

**Model Answer:**
BLDC motors are preferred for EV traction because:
1. **High efficiency (>90 %)** over a wide operating range, maximising battery range.
2. **High power density and torque-to-weight ratio**, beneficial for vehicle mass reduction.
3. **Excellent speed-torque characteristic** (linear drop from no-load to stall), suitable for direct drive without complex multi-speed transmissions.
4. **Low maintenance and long life** owing to absence of brushes and commutator.
5. **Regenerative braking capability** — the motor can act as a generator to recharge the battery during deceleration.
6. **Silent operation and low EMI** with proper sinusoidal/FOC control. (Any four, 3 marks)

---

### 📝 Part B — 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Model Paper]`

**Q-A.**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a Brushless DC (BLDC) motor drive used in an Electric Vehicle. Describe the role of the Hall-effect position sensors and the three-phase inverter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expressions for back-EMF constant $K_e$ and electromagnetic torque $T_e$ for a BLDC motor. Hence establish the relation $K_t = 2 K_e$ in SI units and show that the no-load speed is $\omega_0 = V / K_e$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Q-A (a):**

A BLDC motor EV drive system consists of **six major subsystems** (refer to the block diagram in Section 4.2 of these notes):

1. **Battery + DC link:** Provides a stiff DC bus (e.g. 48 V / 96 V / 400 V) filtered by a high-value DC-link capacitor.
2. **VCU / Controller:** Reads throttle, brake, and Hall-sensor signals; computes desired torque; and generates PWM commands.
3. **Gate driver stage:** Level-shifts the low-voltage PWM logic signals to the high-voltage gate-drive levels required by the IGBTs or MOSFETs.
4. **Three-phase voltage source inverter (VSI):** Six switches (Q1–Q6) arranged as three half-bridge legs. Implements *six-step* (trapezoidal) or *sinusoidal PWM* depending on control strategy.
5. **BLDC motor:** Three-phase stator winding + PM rotor. Rotor magnets are typically NdFeB grade N35–N52.
6. **Hall sensors (H_A, H_B, H_C):** Mounted on the stator at 120° electrical separation, they detect the rotor's instantaneous magnetic polarity and feed back a 3-bit code to the VCU.

**Working principle:** As the rotor turns, the Hall sensors produce a 3-bit code that *uniquely* identifies one of six 60° electrical sectors. For each sector, the VCU commands the inverter to energise a specific pair of phases (e.g. A+ and B−) for that sector. The stator MMF vector therefore steps in 60° increments, and the rotor *chases* it synchronously, producing unidirectional torque. Speed is regulated by **PWM duty-cycle modulation** of the active switches. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Q-A (b):**

**Derivation of $K_e$:** For a BLDC with $N$ turns/phase, $P$ total poles and air-gap flux $\Phi_g$ per pole, the flux linkage changes at the rate

$$
\frac{d\lambda}{d\theta_e} \;=\; N\,\Phi_g \quad\Rightarrow\quad E \;=\; N\,\Phi_g\,\omega_e
$$

Converting electrical angular velocity to mechanical: $\omega_e = (P/2)\,\omega_m$. Hence

$$
E \;=\; \frac{P}{2}\,N\,\Phi_g\,\omega_m \;\Rightarrow\; K_e \;=\; \frac{P}{2}\,N\,\Phi_g \;\;\bigl[\mathrm{V\cdot s/rad}\bigr]
$$

**Derivation of $T_e$ (Power-balance method):** For a 3-phase BLDC with two phases conducting simultaneously,

$$
P_{in} \;=\; 2\,E\,I_a \;=\; 2\,(K_e\omega_m)\,I_a
$$

Equating to mechanical power $P_m = T_e\,\omega_m$ and cancelling $\omega_m$:

$$
T_e \;=\; 2\,K_e\,I_a
$$

Comparing with the canonical form $T_e = K_t\,I_a$ we obtain

$$
\boxed{K_t \;=\; 2\,K_e}
$$

**No-load speed:** Substituting $I_a \to 0$ in the steady-state voltage equation $V = K_e\omega + I_a R_a$ gives

$$
\boxed{\omega_0 \;=\; \frac{V}{K_e}}
$$

(7 marks)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Model Paper]`

**Q-B.**
**(a)** Draw and explain the six-step commutation sequence of a 3-phase BLDC motor using a Hall-sensor truth table. Show how the stator MMF vector advances in 60° steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 48 V, 3-phase BLDC motor has an armature resistance of 0.4 Ω per phase, a back-EMF constant $K_e = 0.06\,\mathrm{V\cdot s/rad}$ and operates with a phase current of 25 A. Compute the (i) electromagnetic torque, (ii) developed mechanical power at 1500 rpm, (iii) no-load speed, and (iv) stall torque. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Q-B (a):**

The Hall sensors produce a 3-bit code (H_A H_B H_C) corresponding to the rotor's electrical position. The 6-step commutation table is:

| Step | H_A | H_B | H_C | Phase A | Phase B | Phase C |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | + V | − V | OFF |
| 2 | 1 | 0 | 0 | + V | OFF | − V |
| 3 | 1 | 1 | 0 | OFF | + V | − V |
| 4 | 0 | 1 | 0 | − V | + V | OFF |
| 5 | 0 | 1 | 1 | − V | OFF | + V |
| 6 | 0 | 0 | 1 | OFF | − V | + V |

At every step, current flows from the *positive DC bus* through one phase, into the motor, and returns from a *second* phase to the *negative DC bus*; the third phase is floating. The MMF vector therefore **jumps in 60° increments** around the stator, always pointing 90° ahead of the rotor magnet axis to maximise torque production. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Q-B (b):**

**Given:** $V = 48\,\mathrm{V}$, $R_a = 0.4\,\Omega$, $K_e = 0.06\,\mathrm{V\cdot s/rad}$, $I_a = 25\,\mathrm{A}$, $N = 1500\,\mathrm{rpm}$.

**(i) Electromagnetic torque:** Using $K_t = 2 K_e$,

$$
K_t \;=\; 2 \times 0.06 \;=\; 0.12 \;\mathrm{Nm/A}
$$

$$
T_e \;=\; K_t\,I_a \;=\; 0.12 \times 25 \;=\; \boxed{3.0\,\mathrm{Nm}}
$$

**(ii) Mechanical power at 1500 rpm:** Convert to rad/s:

$$
\omega_m \;=\; \frac{2\pi N}{60} \;=\; \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} \;=\; 50\pi \;\mathrm{rad/s} \;\approx\; 157.08\,\mathrm{rad/s}
$$

$$
P_m \;=\; T_e\,\omega_m \;=\; 3.0 \times 157.08 \;=\; \boxed{471.24\,\mathrm{W}}
$$

**(iii) No-load speed:**

$$
\omega_0 \;=\; \frac{V}{K_e} \;=\; \frac{48}{0.06} \;=\; 800\,\mathrm{rad/s}
$$

Converting to rpm:

$$
N_0 \;=\; \frac{800 \times 60}{2\pi} \;=\; \frac{48000}{2\pi} \;\approx\; \boxed{7640\,\mathrm{rpm}}
$$

**(iv) Stall torque:** At stall, $E = 0$, so $I_{stall} = V / R_a$.

$$
I_{stall} \;=\; \frac{48}{0.4} \;=\; 120\,\mathrm{A}
$$

$$
T_{stall} \;=\; K_t\,I_{stall} \;=\; 0.12 \times 120 \;=\; \boxed{14.4\,\mathrm{Nm}}
$$

**(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** between $K_t$ and $K_e$ in a 3-phase BLDC costs **1 mark** routinely. Always write $K_t = 2 K_e$ *explicitly* on the answer script.
> 2. **Mixing up electrical and mechanical speeds.** With a 4-pole machine ($P=2$ pole pairs), the electrical speed is *twice* the mechanical speed. Many students write $\omega_0 = V/K_e$ using the *mechanical* value of $K_e$ but then forget to multiply by $P/2$. Re-derive from $E = K_e \omega$ and stick to one convention.
> 3. **Not stating units in numerical answers.** Marks are *deducted* in KTU valuation for missing $\mathrm{Nm}$, $\mathrm{rad/s}$, or $\mathrm{rpm}$ units.
> 4. **Confusing "BLDC" with "PMSM"** in viva: PMSM is the *sinusoidal back-EMF* version and is vector-controlled (FOC); BLDC is the *trapezoidal back-EMF* version and is six-step commutated. Both are PM synchronous machines.
> 5. **Neglecting to draw the inverter bridge** in part-(a) answers — KTU awards **2 marks** explicitly for the *neat labelled circuit diagram* of the 3-phase VSI.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

* ✅ BLDC = **Permanent-Magnet Synchronous Machine** with *electronic commutation*; rotor has PMs, stator has 3-phase winding.
* ✅ Six switches in a 3-phase VSI perform **six-step (120°-conduction)** commutation based on Hall-sensor feedback.
* ✅ The canonical back-EMF and torque equations are $E = K_e \omega$ and $T_e = K_t I_a$ with the **identity $K_t = 2 K_e$ in SI** for 3-phase BLDCs.
* ✅ Speed–torque characteristic is a **straight line** from $(0,\,T_{stall})$ to $(\omega_0,\,0)$.
* ✅ No-load speed: $\omega_0 = V / K_e$; Stall torque: $T_{stall} = K_t V / R_a$.
* ✅ **Trapezoidal** back-EMF → 6-step commutation, low-cost Hall sensors, higher torque ripple (~13 %).
* ✅ **Sinusoidal** back-EMF → FOC / vector control, resolvers, near-zero torque ripple, used in premium EVs.
* ✅ Key EV advantages: high efficiency (>90 %), long life, low maintenance, regenerative-braking capable.
* ✅ Battery → DC link → Inverter → BLDC → Gearbox → Wheels (single-speed in most EVs).
* ✅ Field-weakening (advancing commutation) extends speed range *above* base speed.
* ✅ Always carry the **factor of 2** when computing $T_e$ from $K_e$ in a 3-phase BLDC — KTU board's most-tested trap.
* ✅ Remember to **convert rpm to rad/s** ($\omega = 2\pi N/60$) before applying $P = T\omega$ in numericals.
* ✅ Mention the **NdFeB magnet grade** (N35–N52, $B_r \approx 1.1$–$1.4$ T) wherever possible — shows examiner you read industry literature.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interior Permanent Magnet (IPM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT631 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Interior Permanent Magnet (IPM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Interior Permanent Magnet (IPM)** machine is a subclass of the **Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM)** in which the permanent magnets are **embedded (buried) inside the laminated rotor core**, rather than being mounted on the outer surface of the rotor. The placement of magnets below the rotor surface creates **magnetic saliency** (anisotropy), which means the **direct-axis inductance $L_d$** (along the magnet axis) is **not equal** to the **quadrature-axis inductance $L_q$** (perpendicular to the magnet axis).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The IPM is the **industry-preferred traction motor** for modern Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs) and Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs) because it combines a **magnet torque** component with a **reluctance torque** component, enabling higher torque density, wider constant-power speed range, and superior field-weakening capability compared to surface-mounted PM (SPM) machines.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Embedded Anchor" Model

Imagine a heavy steel anchor that must hold down a structure during a strong storm:

- A **Surface PM (SPM)** motor is like gluing the anchor on top of a wall with epoxy. The bond is weak; under high centrifugal force at high rotor speeds, the magnets can detach or shatter.
- An **Interior PM (IPM)** motor is like **casting the anchor deep inside a concrete block**. The surrounding steel laminations grip the magnet mechanically, allowing the rotor to spin at **3–4× higher speeds** safely, while also exploiting the varying steel paths to generate a **second torque component (reluctance torque)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Metrics (KEEPOUT for Exams):**
> - **Saliency Ratio** $\xi = L_q / L_d$ is typically between **2 and 3** for IPM traction machines.
> - **Magnet material:** NdFeB (Neodymium Iron Boron), with residual flux density $B_r \approx$ **1.1 T to 1.4 T**.
> - **Maximum operating speed:** up to **10,000–20,000 rpm** (with proper field weakening).
> - **Efficiency (peak):** **95%–97%** in modern automotive IPM drives (e.g., Toyota Prius Gen 3, Tesla Model S legacy rear unit).

### 1.3 Why IPM is the Workhorse of Modern EVs

1. **Robustness:** Buried magnets resist centrifugal forces — critical for high-speed highway driving.
2. **Dual Torque Mechanism:** Magnet torque **+** Reluctance torque → higher torque per ampere.
3. **Extended Constant-Power Region:** Excellent field-weakening for high cruising speeds.
4. **Flux Weakening Without Extra Coils:** Achieved by **demagnetizing $d$-axis stator current** ($I_d < 0$).
5. **Lower Rare-Earth Content** (per kW) than SPM of equivalent power — a strategic supply-chain benefit.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Rotor Constructional Topologies of IPM

The placement and shape of the buried magnets defines the IPM sub-family:

1. **Radial-Flux IPM (Bar Magnets):** Rectangular magnets inserted radially through the rotor.
2. **Tangential-Flux IPM:** Magnets arranged tangentially to concentrate flux per pole.
3. **V-Shaped (or Delta) IPM:** Most common in EV traction; the V-geometry channels flux through the rotor pole pieces, increasing $L_q$ and reluctance torque.
4. **U-Shaped / Multi-Layer IPM:** Stacked magnet layers for very high-speed machines.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** If a question asks "why V-shaped IPM is preferred for EVs," answer: *the V-geometry provides a higher quadrature-axis inductance $L_q$ and a lower $L_d$, increasing the saliency ratio $\xi$ and thus the reluctance torque contribution.*

### 2.2 Magnetic Saliency and Axis Convention

The IPM rotor has two orthogonal magnetic axes:

- **$d$-axis (Direct Axis):** Aligned with the **magnet flux direction**. Flux passes through magnets → high reluctance → **low $L_d$**.
- **$q$-axis (Quadrature Axis):** Aligned **between magnets**, through iron bridges only. Low reluctance → **high $L_q$**.

This yields the **defining IPM inequality:**

$$L_d < L_q \quad \text{(negative saliency)}$$

For a Surface PM (SPM) machine, $L_d \approx L_q$ (no saliency, no reluctance torque).

### 2.3 Electromagnetic Torque — Physical Origin

The total electromagnetic torque in an IPM machine has **two distinct sources**:

1. **Magnet Alignment Torque ($T_{mag}$):** Tendency of the rotor magnets to align with the stator's rotating field.
2. **Reluctance Torque ($T_{rel}$):** Tendency of the rotor to move to the position of **minimum magnetic reluctance** along the high-permeability $q$-axis (i.e., the iron bridges want to face the energized stator teeth).

Total torque:

$$T_e = T_{mag} + T_{rel}$$

### 2.4 MTPA — Maximum Torque Per Ampere Control

In the constant-torque (low-speed) region, the controller applies **MTPA strategy**: it chooses the optimal current vector angle $\beta$ (angle between stator current $I_s$ and $q$-axis) that maximizes torque for a given current magnitude. For IPM, this optimum is **not** purely $I_q$ (as in SPM) — a small **negative $I_d$** is always injected.

### 2.5 Field Weakening for Extended Speed Range

Beyond base speed, the back-EMF would exceed the DC bus voltage. To enable higher speed, the controller injects a **demagnetizing $I_d < 0$** to oppose the magnet flux $\psi_m$:

$$\psi_{eff} = \psi_m + L_d I_d \quad \text{with } I_d < 0$$

This reduces effective air-gap flux → lower back-EMF → higher speed possible at the same voltage limit.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $L_d < L_q$ | IPM saliency condition (negative saliency) | H |
| 2 | $\xi = L_q / L_d$ | Saliency ratio (typically 2–3) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $T_e = \tfrac{3}{2}\cdot\tfrac{P}{2}\left[\psi_m I_q + (L_d - L_q) I_d I_q\right]$ | Total electromagnetic torque | N·m |
| 4 | $T_{mag} = \tfrac{3}{2}\cdot\tfrac{P}{2}\,\psi_m I_q$ | Magnet (alignment) torque | N·m |
| 5 | $T_{rel} = \tfrac{3}{2}\cdot\tfrac{P}{2}(L_d - L_q) I_d I_q$ | Reluctance torque (negative since $L_d<L_q$, $I_d<0$ → $+T$) | N·m |
| 6 | $\psi_{eff} = \psi_m + L_d I_d$ | Effective air-gap flux linkage | Wb |
| 7 | $V_s = \omega_e \sqrt{(L_q I_q)^2 + (\psi_m + L_d I_d)^2}$ | Steady-state voltage magnitude (voltage limit circle) | V |
| 8 | $I_s^2 = I_d^2 + I_q^2$ | Current limit circle | A² |
| 9 | $V_{DC}/\sqrt{3} = \omega_e L_q I_q$ (deep FW) | Characteristic current relationship | V |
| 10 | $P_{mech} = T_e \cdot \omega_m$ | Mechanical output power | W |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbol Note:** $P$ = number of **poles**, $p$ = number of pole **pairs** (so $\omega_e = p \cdot \omega_m$). KTU examiners are strict — write $(P/2)$ or use $p$ consistently. The factor $3/2$ comes from the 3-phase Park transformation.

### 2.7 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Toyota Prius (Gen 1–3):** Uses a 50 kW V-shaped IPM for traction.
- **Honda Insight / Accord Hybrid:** IPM with rectangular bar magnets.
- **BMW i3, Nissan Leaf (early):** IPM variants.
- **Industrial Servos, Robotics, Machine Tools:** IPM dominates due to high torque density and field-weakening range.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Stator Voltage Equations in the $d$-$q$ Reference Frame

The dynamic per-phase equivalent circuit of the IPM in the rotor-aligned $d$-$q$ frame is:

$$v_d = R_s i_d + L_d \frac{di_d}{dt} - \omega_e L_q i_q$$

$$v_q = R_s i_q + L_q \frac{di_q}{dt} + \omega_e L_d i_d + \omega_e \psi_m$$

**Reasoning for each term:**

- $R_s i_d$, $R_s i_q$ → ohmic stator copper drops.
- $L_d \frac{di_d}{dt}$, $L_q \frac{di_q}{dt}$ → transformer EMFs (rate of change of flux linkages).
- $-\omega_e L_q i_q$ → speed EMF from $q$-axis current linking $d$-axis.
- $+\omega_e L_d i_d$ → speed EMF from $d$-axis current linking $q$-axis.
- $+\omega_e \psi_m$ → speed EMF from the **permanent magnet flux linkage** $\psi_m$ — this term is **absent in induction motors and synchronous reluctance motors**.

### 3.2 Instantaneous Air-Gap Power

Multiply the $d$-axis voltage by $i_d$ and $q$-axis by $i_q$, then sum (in 3-phase, multiply by $3/2$ to account for the Park's invariant transformation):

$$P_{gap} = \frac{3}{2}\left(v_d i_d + v_q i_q\right)$$

Substituting the voltage equations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{gap} &= \frac{3}{2}\Big[ R_s i_d^2 + L_d i_d \frac{di_d}{dt} - \omega_e L_q i_q i_d \\
&\quad + R_s i_q^2 + L_q i_q \frac{di_q}{dt} + \omega_e L_d i_d i_q + \omega_e \psi_m i_q \Big]
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Separation of Power Components

Group the terms into **losses**, **stored magnetic energy**, and **electromechanical conversion**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{gap} &= \underbrace{\frac{3}{2}R_s (i_d^2 + i_q^2)}_{\text{copper loss } P_{cu}} \;+\; \underbrace{\frac{3}{2}\left(L_d i_d \frac{di_d}{dt} + L_q i_q \frac{di_q}{dt}\right)}_{\text{rate of change of stored magnetic energy}} \\
&\quad + \underbrace{\frac{3}{2}\omega_e \big[(L_d - L_q) i_d i_q + \psi_m i_q\big]}_{\text{electromechanical power } P_{mech}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sign convention check:** The cross-coupling terms $-\omega_e L_q i_q i_d$ (from $v_d$) and $+\omega_e L_d i_d i_q$ (from $v_q$) combine to $\omega_e(L_d - L_q) i_d i_q$. Since $L_d < L_q$ in IPM, this term is **negative** — but with $I_d < 0$ in MTPA, the product $i_d i_q$ is also negative, so their product becomes **positive** → produces reluctance torque.

### 3.4 Conversion from Electrical to Mechanical Power

The electrical angular frequency $\omega_e$ is related to mechanical speed $\omega_m$ by:

$$\omega_e = p \cdot \omega_m = \frac{P}{2} \cdot \omega_m$$

Mechanical output power equals electromechanical power:

$$P_{mech} = T_e \cdot \omega_m$$

Setting $P_{mech}$ equal and solving for torque:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_e \cdot \omega_m &= \frac{3}{2}\omega_e \big[\psi_m i_q + (L_d - L_q) i_d i_q\big] \\
T_e \cdot \omega_m &= \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2}\cdot \omega_m \big[\psi_m i_q + (L_d - L_q) i_d i_q\big]
\end{aligned}
$$

Canceling $\omega_m$ from both sides:

$$\boxed{\; T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \left[ \psi_m i_q + (L_d - L_q)\, i_d\, i_q \right] \;}$$

### 3.5 Splitting into Magnet + Reluctance Torque

By direct identification:

$$T_{mag} = \frac{3}{2}\cdot\frac{P}{2}\,\psi_m i_q$$

$$T_{rel} = \frac{3}{2}\cdot\frac{P}{2}\,(L_d - L_q)\, i_d\, i_q$$

**Verification with limiting cases:**

| Case | Condition | Result | Physical Meaning |
|------|-----------|--------|------------------|
| SPM (no saliency) | $L_d = L_q$ | $T_{rel} = 0$, $T_e = \tfrac{3P}{4}\psi_m i_q$ | Only magnet torque — matches SPM standard equation ✓ |
| Synchronous Reluctance Motor (no magnets) | $\psi_m = 0$ | $T_e = \tfrac{3P}{4}(L_d - L_q) i_d i_q$ | Pure reluctance torque ✓ |
| IPM with MTPA | $L_d < L_q$, $i_d < 0$, $i_q > 0$ | $T_e = T_{mag} + \vert T_{rel} \vert$ | Both torques add positively ✓ |

### 3.6 MTPA Derivation (Analytical Form)

Maximize $T_e$ subject to $I_s^2 = I_d^2 + I_q^2$ (current limit). Use Lagrange multiplier $\lambda$:

$$\mathcal{L} = \psi_m i_q + (L_d - L_q) i_d i_q + \lambda (I_s^2 - i_d^2 - i_q^2)$$

Partial derivatives set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial i_d} = (L_d - L_q) i_q - 2\lambda i_d = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda = \frac{(L_d - L_q) i_q}{2 i_d}$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial i_q} = \psi_m + (L_d - L_q) i_d - 2\lambda i_q = 0$$

Eliminating $\lambda$:

$$\boxed{\; i_d = \frac{\psi_m}{2(L_q - L_d)} - \sqrt{\left(\frac{\psi_m}{2(L_q - L_d)}\right)^2 + i_q^2} \;}$$

(The negative root is taken so that $I_d < 0$ in MTPA for IPM.)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 IPM Rotor Topologies — Functional Classification

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[IPM Rotor Topologies]
    A --> B[Radial Flux Bar Type]
    A --> C[Tangential Flux Type]
    A --> D[V Shaped Type]
    A --> E[U Shaped Type]
    A --> F[Multi Layer Type]
    B --> B1[Rectangular magnets radial]
    B --> B2[Simple to manufacture]
    B --> B3[Low reluctance torque]
    C --> C1[Magnets aligned tangentially]
    C --> C2[Flux concentration per pole]
    C --> C3[High mechanical stress]
    D --> D1[Most common for EV]
    D --> D2[High saliency ratio]
    D --> D3[High reluctance torque]
    E --> E1[Higher bridge stiffness]
    E --> E2[Used in high speed EV]
    F --> F1[Stacked magnet layers]
    F --> F2[Very high speed]
    F --> F3[Complex manufacturing]
```

### 4.2 IPM Drive System — Block-Level Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    cmd[Torque Command] --> ctrl[Vector Controller MTPA FW]
    meas[Sensor Feedback Speed Position Current] --> ctrl
    ctrl --> inv[Three Phase Inverter PWM]
    inv --> ipm[IPM Traction Motor]
    ipm --> gearbox[Reduction Gearbox]
    gearbox --> whl[Vehicle Wheels]
    ipm -.-> .-> meas
    inv -.-> .-> meas
    bat[HV Battery Pack] --> dcdc[DC DC Converter]
    dcdc --> inv
    bat --> inv
```

### 4.3 Torque Components Map for IPM

```mermaid
graph TD
    T[Total Torque Te]
    T --> Tmag[Magnet Torque Tmag]
    T --> Trel[Reluctance Torque Trel]
    Tmag --> TmagF1[Function of Psi m and Iq]
    Tmag --> TmagF2[Exists in both IPM and SPM]
    Trel --> TrelF1[Function of Ld minus Lq times Id times Iq]
    Trel --> TrelF2[Unique to IPM and SynR motors]
    Trel --> TrelF3[Requires Id negative]
    Trel --> TrelF4[Enhanced by high saliency ratio]
```

### 4.4 Operating Regions of IPM Drive

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Below Base Speed] --> B[Constant Torque Region]
    B --> C[MTPA Control]
    C --> D[Field Weakening Region 1]
    D --> E[Constant Power Region]
    E --> F[Deep Field Weakening]
    F --> G[Constant Voltage Variable Power]
    G --> H[Maximum Speed]
    A -. base speed .-> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Diagram Reading Tip (KTU):** When asked to "explain IPM control," sketch the *Current Limit Circle* and *Voltage Limit Circle* in the $i_d$–$i_q$ plane. The operating point is constrained to the **current circle** (thermal limit) and must lie **inside the voltage circle** (DC-bus limit). MTPA uses an inner hyperbola; field weakening slides the operating point along the voltage circle toward $-i_d$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**  
Define an Interior Permanent Magnet (IPM) synchronous motor. Why is it preferred over Surface Permanent Magnet (SPM) motors for electric vehicle applications?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> An **IPM synchronous motor** is a permanent magnet machine in which the magnets are **embedded inside the rotor laminations**, creating magnetic saliency ($L_d \neq L_q$).
>
> **Reasons preferred for EVs:**
> 1. **Mechanical robustness** — magnets held by steel bridges tolerate very high rotational speeds without detachment.
> 2. **Dual torque production** — total torque has both magnet torque and reluctance torque components, increasing torque density.
> 3. **Excellent field-weakening capability** — wider constant-power speed range for highway cruising.
> 4. **Higher efficiency** (95–97%) due to absence of rotor copper losses.
> 5. **Lower rare-earth content per kW** than SPM of equal rating.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary Paper]**  
What is magnetic saliency in an IPM machine? Why is $L_d < L_q$ in IPM but $L_d = L_q$ in SPM?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Magnetic saliency** is the property of the rotor having **different magnetic reluctances along two orthogonal axes** — the $d$-axis (along the magnet) and the $q$-axis (between magnets).
>
> In **IPM**, the $d$-axis flux path passes **through the magnets** (low permeability, high reluctance) → small $L_d$. The $q$-axis path passes **through iron bridges only** (high permeability) → large $L_q$. Hence $L_d < L_q$.
>
> In **SPM**, the magnets are mounted on the rotor surface; the effective air gap is uniform in all directions → $L_d = L_q$ (non-salient).

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Derive the electromagnetic torque equation of an Interior Permanent Magnet (IPM) synchronous motor in the $d$-$q$ reference frame, clearly showing the **magnet torque** and **reluctance torque** components. State all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With suitable phasor and current-space-vector diagrams, explain the **Maximum Torque Per Ampere (MTPA)** and **Field Weakening (FW)** control strategies used in IPM traction drives. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (Question A)**

**(a) Torque Derivation (7 marks):**

**Assumptions** (1 mark):
- Sinusoidal MMF distribution, balanced 3-phase.
- Neglect saturation, hysteresis, and eddy currents.
- Stator currents transformed to rotor-aligned $d$-$q$ frame via Park transformation.

**Voltage equations in $d$-$q$ frame** (1 mark):

$$v_d = R_s i_d + L_d \frac{di_d}{dt} - \omega_e L_q i_q$$

$$v_q = R_s i_q + L_q \frac{di_q}{dt} + \omega_e L_d i_d + \omega_e \psi_m$$

**Air-gap power** (1 mark):

$$P_{gap} = \frac{3}{2}(v_d i_d + v_q i_q)$$

**Substitution and grouping** (2 marks):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{gap} &= \frac{3}{2}R_s(i_d^2 + i_q^2) + \frac{3}{2}\left(L_d i_d \frac{di_d}{dt} + L_q i_q \frac{di_q}{dt}\right) \\
&\quad + \frac{3}{2}\omega_e\big[\psi_m i_q + (L_d - L_q) i_d i_q\big]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Equating electromechanical power to $T_e \omega_m$** with $\omega_e = (P/2)\omega_m$ (1 mark):

$$\boxed{\; T_e = \frac{3}{2}\cdot\frac{P}{2}\Big[\psi_m i_q + (L_d - L_q)i_d i_q\Big] = T_{mag} + T_{rel} \;}$$

**Identification of components** (1 mark):
- $T_{mag} = \tfrac{3P}{4}\psi_m i_q$
- $T_{rel} = \tfrac{3P}{4}(L_d - L_q) i_d i_q$

**(b) MTPA and Field Weakening (7 marks):**

**MTPA (3 marks):**
- Operating point lies on the **current limit circle** $I_s^2 = I_d^2 + I_q^2$.
- For IPM, optimum current angle $\beta$ (angle of $I_s$ from $q$-axis) is found by maximizing $T_e/I_s$.
- Result: a small **negative $I_d$** is always injected along with positive $I_q$ to produce reluctance torque in addition to magnet torque.
- Analytic MTPA: $I_d = \dfrac{\psi_m}{2(L_q - L_d)} - \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\psi_m}{2(L_q - L_d)}\right)^2 + I_q^2}$

**Field Weakening (3 marks):**
- Above base speed, induced EMF $E = \omega_e \psi_{eff}$ exceeds the DC-bus voltage $V_{DC}$.
- Controller injects a **demagnetizing $I_d < 0$** to reduce effective flux $\psi_{eff} = \psi_m + L_d I_d$.
- Operating point slides **along the voltage limit circle** $V_s^2 = (\omega_e L_q I_q)^2 + (\omega_e \psi_{eff})^2 \le V_{DC}^2/3$.
- In **deep field-weakening**, the operating point approaches the current limit on the negative $I_d$ axis.

**Diagrams expected (1 mark):** Current circle, voltage circle, MTPA trajectory, FW trajectory in $i_d$–$i_q$ plane.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE**

**[KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the constructional features of an IPM rotor. Compare **radial**, **tangential**, and **V-shaped** magnet arrangements in terms of flux concentration, saliency, mechanical strength, and suitability for EV traction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 6-pole, $Y$-connected IPM traction motor has $\psi_m = 0.12$ Wb, $L_d = 4$ mH, $L_q = 12$ mH, and is operating with $I_d = -50$ A, $I_q = 100$ A. Compute: (i) magnet torque, (ii) reluctance torque, (iii) total electromagnetic torque, and (iv) the relative percentage contribution of reluctance torque to the total torque. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (Question B)**

**(a) Constructional Features and Comparison (7 marks):**

**Common features** (2 marks):
- Laminated electrical steel rotor core (typically 0.35 mm Si-steel laminations).
- Permanent magnets (NdFeB) embedded inside the rotor.
- Flux barriers (air pockets) between magnets to direct flux paths.
- Thin iron bridges retain the magnets against centrifugal force.

**Comparison Table** (5 marks):

| Feature | Radial | Tangential | V-Shaped |
|---------|--------|------------|----------|
| Flux concentration per pole | Moderate | **High** (uses both magnets in series) | High |
| Saliency ratio $L_q/L_d$ | Low–Medium | Medium | **High** |
| Reluctance torque contribution | Low | Medium | **High** |
| Mechanical strength of bridges | High | Low (large bridges needed) | **Medium–High** |
| Manufacturing complexity | Low | Medium | Medium |
| **EV suitability** | Low–medium | Medium | **Best (industry standard)** |

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given data:**
- $P = 6$ poles → $P/2 = 3$
- $\psi_m = 0.12$ Wb
- $L_d = 4 \times 10^{-3}$ H, $L_q = 12 \times 10^{-3}$ H
- $I_d = -50$ A, $I_q = 100$ A

**(i) Magnet Torque (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{mag} &= \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot \psi_m \cdot I_q \\
&= \frac{3}{2} \cdot 3 \cdot 0.12 \cdot 100 \\
&= 1.5 \cdot 3 \cdot 12 \\
&= 54 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Reluctance Torque (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{rel} &= \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot (L_d - L_q) \cdot I_d \cdot I_q \\
&= 1.5 \cdot 3 \cdot (0.004 - 0.012) \cdot (-50) \cdot 100 \\
&= 4.5 \cdot (-0.008) \cdot (-5000) \\
&= 4.5 \cdot 40 \\
&= 180 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Total Electromagnetic Torque (1.5 marks):**

$$T_e = T_{mag} + T_{rel} = 54 + 180 = \mathbf{234 \; N \cdot m}$$

**(iv) Reluctance Torque Contribution Percentage (1.5 marks):**

$$\% \text{ contribution} = \frac{T_{rel}}{T_e} \times 100 = \frac{180}{234} \times 100 \approx \mathbf{76.92\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign of $I_d$:** Many students forget that MTPA in IPM uses $I_d < 0$. The product $(L_d - L_q) I_d I_q$ becomes **positive** because **both** $(L_d - L_q) < 0$ and $I_d < 0$ are negative. Failing to recognize this sign results in **negative reluctance torque** — losing 2 marks.
> 2. **Pole Count Factor:** The factor in the torque equation is $P/2$ (where $P$ is **number of poles**, not pole pairs). For 6-pole, $P/2 = 3$. Writing $P = 3$ loses marks.
> 3. **Units of $L_d$, $L_q$:** Must convert mH to H before multiplication with current.
> 4. **Forgetting $\frac{3}{2}$ factor:** This Park's invariant constant is mandatory; omitting it loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Confusing $L_d - L_q$ with $L_q - L_d$:** Both forms are correct, but the sign convention determines the resulting torque sign. Be consistent.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — IPM (Module 1)**

- [ ] **IPM = PMs embedded in rotor** → saliency → $L_d < L_q$.
- [ ] **Two torque components:** $T_{mag}$ (from $\psi_m I_q$) and $T_{rel}$ (from $(L_d - L_q) I_d I_q$).
- [ ] **Master Equation:** $T_e = \tfrac{3P}{4}\big[\psi_m I_q + (L_d - L_q) I_d I_q\big]$.
- [ ] **MTPA requires $I_d < 0$** in IPM (opposite polarity to SPM convention).
- [ ] **Field weakening** = inject demagnetizing $I_d < 0$ to reduce $\psi_{eff} = \psi_m + L_d I_d$.
- [ ] **Voltage limit circle** governs high-speed operation; **current limit circle** governs low-speed operation.
- [ ] **Saliency ratio** $\xi = L_q/L_d \approx 2\text{–}3$ in EV-grade IPM.
- [ ] **V-shaped magnet layout** = industry standard for EV traction (Toyota Prius, etc.).
- [ ] **Power flow in $d$-$q$ voltage equations** must include $\omega_e \psi_m$ term (PM flux linkage EMF).
- [ ] **Characteristic current:** $I_{ch} = \psi_m / L_d$ — at this $I_d$ value, the IPM achieves **infinite field-weakening speed range** (ideal case).
- [ ] **Efficiency** of IPM traction drive: 95–97%; **peak power density**: 3–5 kW/kg.
- [ ] **No rotor copper losses** → rotor remains cool → smaller cooling system → higher reliability.
- [ ] **Demagnetization risk** under short-circuit fault or extreme $I_d$ — must design with $I_{ch} < I_{max}$ to avoid irreversible demagnetization of NdFeB.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Switched Reluctance Motors (SRM): Axial
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT631 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Switched Reluctance Motors (SRM): Axial ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) The **Axial Flux Switched Reluctance Motor (AFSRM)**, also referred to as the **Axial Reluctance Motor** or **Disc-Type SRM**, is a variable-reluctance electric machine in which the magnetic flux lines traverse the air gap in a direction **parallel to the rotational axis** of the machine, as opposed to the conventional Radial Flux SRM (RFSRM) where the flux crosses the air gap in the radial direction. The AFSRM retains the fundamental operating principle of the switched reluctance family — producing torque by the tendency of a salient ferromagnetic rotor to align with the lowest-reluctance position under an excited stator phase — but realizes this principle in a **pancake / disc topology**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMUT631 – Electric and Hybrid Vehicles), the AFSRM is studied as a **direct-drive in-wheel / hub-motor candidate** for EV propulsion because of its inherently short axial length, high torque density, and excellent thermal dissipation surface area (the disc face is exposed to ambient air). Geometrically, an AFSRM is constructed from **stator discs and rotor discs mounted on a common shaft**. The stator carries concentrically wound phase coils arranged around the shaft axis, and the rotor is a salient-pole iron disc (no permanent magnets, no rotor windings, no cage). The number of stator pole pairs $N_s$ and rotor pole pairs $N_r$ are deliberately chosen to be **unequal** (commonly $6/4$, $8/6$, $12/8$, or $8/4$ combinations), exactly as in radial SRMs. > [!NOTE] > **Definition Box — Variable Reluctance:** > *Reluctance* $\mathcal{R}$ is the magnetic equivalent of electrical resistance. In an SRM, the air-gap reluctance of a phase winding depends on the angular overlap between the stator pole and the rotor pole. Torque is produced whenever the rotor angle is changing in a way that *reduces* the reluctance (i.e., *increases* the inductance) of an excited phase. The rotor thus "snaps" toward alignment, just as a magnetic dipole aligns with an external field. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Iron Disc Seeking a Magnetic Shortcut" Imagine a **heavy iron disc (rotor)** placed on a horizontal axle above which sits **a coil of wire wound around a C-shaped iron core (stator pole)**. When current flows through the coil, the C-core becomes an electromagnet. The iron disc, sensing this magnetic source, will **always try to physically rotate so that its protruding pole slides directly under the C-core**, because that configuration gives the magnetic flux lines the *shortest possible path through iron and the longest possible path through air (minimum reluctance)*. Now imagine that instead of one C-core, there are **6 such C-cores arranged in a circle on a stator disc**, and the iron rotor has **4 poles**. Switch current from one C-core to the next, in synchronism with the rotor's rotation, and the disc will keep rotating — always chasing the *next* excited pole. This sequential "pulsing" of stationary coils is what gives the SRM its name: the reluctance is *switched* by electronic commutation.
