- [x] **NCA** = highest energy → Tesla Model S/X/3.
- [x] **BMS functions:** Sense, SoC/SoH estimate, balance, protect, communicate, thermal control.
- [x] **Thermal runaway** = exothermic chain reaction starting at ~80 °C (SEI breakdown); requires multi-layer protection.
- [x] **Pack sizing formula:** $V_{pack} = n_s \times V_{cell}$, $Q_{pack} = n_p \times Q_{cell}$, $E_{pack} = V_{pack} \times Q_{pack}$.
- [x] **Heat generated:** $Q = I^2 R_{int}$; **C-rate** = $I / Q_{rated}$.
- [x] **Remember to draw** a labeled block diagram of BMS and/or Li-ion cell cross-section in any 14-mark answer — carries **2–3 marks by itself**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nickel Metal Hydride
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT631 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLES > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Nickel Metal Hydride # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Nickel Metal Hydride (NiMH) Battery** is a secondary (rechargeable) alkaline electrochemical energy storage device that uses **nickel hydroxide [Ni(OH)₂]** as the positive active material, a **hydrogen-absorbing metal alloy (Metal Hydride, MH)** as the negative active material, and an **alkaline potassium hydroxide (KOH) electrolyte** to facilitate the reversible transport of hydrogen ions between electrodes during charge and discharge cycles. In the context of **Electric and Hybrid Electric Vehicles (EV/HEV)**, NiMH is classified as a **mid-energy, high-power, thermally robust** rechargeable chemistry, particularly suited for hybrid propulsion systems where it acts as a buffer for regenerative braking energy and engine load leveling. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Hydrogen Shuttling" Analogy:** > Imagine a **delivery warehouse** where goods (hydrogen ions, H⁺) constantly shuttle between two storage zones: > - **Zone A (Warehouse A — Ni(OH)₂/NiOOH side):** A chemical warehouse that gives off hydrogen ions when discharging. > - **Zone B (Warehouse B — Metal Alloy side):** A sponge-like metal that *physically traps* hydrogen ions inside its crystal lattice like sponges trap water. > > During **discharge**, Zone A releases hydrogen "packages" through a toll-road (electrolyte) to Zone B, which absorbs them (charging its sponge). The flow of these packages is the electric current. > During **charging**, an external power supply *forces* the packages back from Zone B to Zone A. > The genius of NiMH is that **Zone B is a solid metal sponge** — no gaseous hydrogen is ever produced or pressurized — making it far safer than storing hydrogen gas in a tank. ## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU Board-Exam Frequently Tested) > [!NOTE] > The following are the **canonical specifications** for HEV-grade NiMH cells (e.g., Toyota Prius Generation 3, Panasonic/Ovonic modules): | Performance Metric | Typical Value | Significance | |---|---|---| | **Nominal Cell Voltage** | **1.2 V** | Identical to NiCd; allows drop-in replacement | | **Specific Energy** | **60 – 110 Wh/kg** | Mass-based energy storage | | **Energy Density** | **180 – 300 Wh/L** | Volume-based energy storage | | **Specific Power** | **250 – 1000 W/kg** | Critical for HEV acceleration bursts | | **Cycle Life** | **> 1000 cycles (80% DoD)** | Long calendar life suitable for vehicles | | **Self-Discharge Rate** | **20 – 30% per month** | Higher than Li-ion | | **Operating Temperature** | **−20 °C to +45 °C** | Excellent thermal tolerance | | **Memory Effect** | **Negligible (improved over NiCd)** | Less of a concern | ## 1.4 KTU Syllabus Highlight Callout > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why NiMH is still in the KTU 2024 PEMUT631 syllabus:** > Despite Li-ion dominance in modern BEVs, NiMH remains the **most widely deployed battery chemistry in mass-produced HEVs** (Toyota Prius, Camry Hybrid, Lexus hybrid lineup — millions of units). KTU examiners expect students to be able to **compare NiMH against Pb-acid and Li-ion** for hybrid powertrain applications. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Constructional Architecture of a NiMH Cell A NiMH cell is a **cylindrical (D, C, AA formats) or prismatic** sealed alkaline cell comprising the following functional layers: 1. **Positive Electrode (Cathode):** - Active material: **Nickel Hydroxide, Ni(OH)₂** - Conductive additive: **Cobalt hydroxide (Co(OH)₂)** or graphite flakes (~3–5%) - Substrate: **Foamed nickel plaque** (porous sintered nickel) for current collection - Conduction mechanism: *Proton (H⁺) insertion/extraction* via solid-state diffusion 2. **Negative Electrode (Anode):** - Active material: **Hydrogen-storage alloy (M)** of the type **AB₅ (LaNi₅-based)** or **AB₂ (Zr/Ti-Ni based)** - Hydride form: **MH** (Metal Hydride) - Function: **Reversibly absorbs and desorbs hydrogen atoms** into its intermetallic lattice - Conduction mechanism: *Solid-state hydrogen absorption/desorption* 3. **Electrolyte:** - **Aqueous Potassium Hydroxide (KOH), typically 30 wt% concentration** - Additives: **LiOH (additive to improve charge efficiency and cycle life)** - **NaOH** sometimes added to reduce cost - Function: **OH⁻ ionic conduction** between electrodes 4. **Separator:** - **Non-woven polypropylene (PP) or nylon** with hydrophilic surface treatment - Function: **Electrical insulation + electrolyte retention** 5. **Cell Casing & Safety:** - **Nickel-plated steel can** (hermetic seal) - **Safety vent** (releases H₂ gas if internal pressure exceeds ~2–3 MPa) - **Positive terminal** is insulated; **negative terminal** is the can ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Working Principle > [!NOTE] > **Core Idea:** The NiMH cell operates on the **reversible movement of hydrogen (H)** between two solid-state host lattices via an alkaline electrolyte. ### Step 1 — Discharge Mode (Cell delivers current to external load): 1. At the **positive electrode**, the active material **NiOOH (charged state)** is reduced back to **Ni(OH)₂**, releasing an OH⁻ into the electrolyte and accepting an electron from the external circuit. 2. The OH⁻ ion migrates through the KOH electrolyte to the negative electrode. 3. At the **negative electrode**, the **Metal Hydride (MH)** reacts with the incoming OH⁻ to release H₂O and an electron back to the external circuit. The metal alloy returns to its desorbed (M) state. ### Step 2 — Charge Mode (External power supply drives the reverse reaction): 1. Electrons are externally forced from the positive electrode into the negative electrode. 2. At the **negative electrode**, water is reduced, liberating H atoms that are **absorbed into the metal alloy lattice** (forming MH). 3. At the **positive electrode**, Ni(OH)₂ is oxidized back to NiOOH, releasing H⁺ into the electrolyte (which forms water with OH⁻). ### Step 3 — Sealed-Cell Recombination Cycle: - During overcharge, O₂ generated at the positive electrode diffuses to the negative electrode, where it reacts with H stored in the metal hydride to reform water — a **closed oxygen cycle** that prevents electrolyte loss. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Following Equations Are Repeatedly Tested in KTU University Examinations:** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{\text{cell}} &= V_{\text{nom}} \times Q \quad [\text{Wh}] \\ \text{Specific Energy} &= \frac{E_{\text{cell}}}{m_{\text{cell}}} \quad [\text{Wh/kg}] \\ \text{Energy Density} &= \frac{E_{\text{cell}}}{V_{\text{cell}}} \quad [\text{Wh/L}] \\ \eta_{\text{Coulombic}} &= \frac{Q_{\text{discharge}}}{Q_{\text{charge}}} \times 100\% \\ \eta_{\text{Energy}} &= \frac{E_{\text{discharge}}}{E_{\text{charge}}} \times 100\% \\ \text{State of Charge (SoC)} &= \frac{Q_{\text{remaining}}}{Q_{\text{rated}}} \times 100\% \\ P_{\text{max}} &= \frac{V_{\text{oc}}^2}{4 R_{\text{int}}} \quad [\text{Maximum Power Transfer Theorem}] \end{aligned} $$ > **Standard Boundaries for HEV NiMH:** > - $V_{\text{oc}} \approx \mathbf{1.35 \text{ V}}$ (open-circuit, fully charged) > - $V_{\text{cutoff}} \approx \mathbf{1.0 \text{ V}}$ (discharge cutoff) > - $V_{\text{nominal}} = \mathbf{1.2 \text{ V}}$ > - Operating **Depth of Discharge (DoD)** for HEV: **30 – 70%** (to maximize cycle life) ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Toyota Hybrid System (THS):** Toyota's HEVs use a **battery pack of 28 NiMH modules (201.6 V nominal)**, each module containing 6 D-size cells (1.2 V × 6 = 7.2 V). - **NASA Space Applications:** NiMH batteries powered the **Hubble Space Telescope** and **International Space Station** EVA tools due to their safety and reliability. - **Grid-Scale Storage (early adoption):** SAFT and ABB deployed NiMH for short-duration frequency regulation before Li-ion became economical. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Electrochemical Reactions During Discharge > [!NOTE] > **Assumptions:** Isothermal operation (T = 25 °C), 30 wt% KOH electrolyte, fully charged initial state. ### Step 3.1.1 — Positive Electrode (Cathode during discharge) The charged active material is **Nickel Oxyhydroxide (NiOOH)**. It is reduced by accepting one proton (H⁺) from water and one electron from the external circuit: $$ \text{NiOOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^- \longrightarrow \text{Ni(OH)}_2 + \text{OH}^- $$ **Logic:** The Ni⁺³ in NiOOH is reduced to Ni⁺² in Ni(OH)₂, gaining one electron. The proton from water combines with the oxygen to form the hydroxyl group. ### Step 3.1.2 — Negative Electrode (Anode during discharge) The metal hydride (MH) reacts with hydroxyl ion from the electrolyte to release the stored hydrogen as water, donating one electron: $$ \text{MH} + \text{OH}^- \longrightarrow \text{M} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^- $$ **Logic:** The H⁻ in the hydride is oxidized to H⁺ (formal oxidation state), donating an electron. The alloy (M) is regenerated in its desorbed state. ### Step 3.1.3 — Overall Cell Reaction (Discharge) Adding the two half-reactions, electrons and OH⁻ cancel on both sides: $$ \text{NiOOH} + \text{MH} \longrightarrow \text{Ni(OH)}_2 + \text{M} $$ **Logic:** Net result is hydrogen atom transfer from the positive electrode lattice to the negative electrode lattice — **no net electrolyte consumption**, ensuring sealed operation. ### Step 3.1.4 — Reversibility During Charge During charging, an external power supply reverses the electron flow: $$ \text{Ni(OH)}_2 + \text{M} \longrightarrow \text{NiOOH} + \text{MH} $$ ## 3.2 Derivation: Theoretical Specific Energy of NiMH Using **Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis** and the **Nernst-derived thermodynamic voltage**: ### Step 3.2.1 — Molar Mass of Active Materials $$ \begin{aligned} M_{\text{NiOOH}} &= 58.69 + 16.00 + 16.00 + 1.008 \\ &= 91.70 \text{ g/mol} \\ \\ M_{\text{MH}} &\approx 91.70 \text{ g/mol} \quad (\text{assumed for LaNi}_5\text{H}_6 \text{ hydride}) \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3.2.2 — Total Active Mass per Equivalent For a **one-electron transfer** ($n = 1$), the Faraday constant $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$: $$ m_{\text{total}} = M_{\text{NiOOH}} + M_{\text{MH}} = 91.70 + 91.70 = 183.40 \text{ g/mol} $$ ### Step 3.2.3 — Theoretical Energy per Mole $$ \begin{aligned} E_{\text{theoretical}} &= V_{\text{cell}} \times Q_{\text{molar}} \\ &= V_{\text{cell}} \times n \times F \\ &= 1.35 \text{ V} \times 1 \times 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol} \\ &= 130{,}254.75 \text{ J/mol} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3.2.4 — Convert to Wh/kg $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Specific Energy}_{\text{theoretical}} &= \frac{130{,}254.75 \text{ J/mol}}{183.40 \text{ g/mol} \times 3600 \text{ s/h} \div 1000 \text{ g/kg}} \\ &= \frac{130{,}254.75}{660.24} \\ &\approx 197.3 \text{ Wh/kg} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Reality Check:** Practical NiMH cells achieve only **30 – 55% of the theoretical specific energy** (~60–110 Wh/kg) due to inactive components — current collectors, electrolyte, separator, casing, and unused active material mass. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: NiMH vs NiCd vs Li-ion for HEV | Parameter | **NiMH** | **NiCd** | **Li-ion (NMC)** | |---|---|---|---| | Nominal Voltage | **1.2 V** | 1.2 V | 3.6 – 3.7 V | | Specific Energy (Wh/kg) | **60 – 110** | 45 – 80 | 150 – 250 | | Memory Effect | **Very Low** | Severe | None | | Toxicity | **Low (No Cd)** | High (Cd) | Low | | Self-Discharge/month | **20 – 30%** | 10 – 20% | 2 – 5% | | Cycle Life | **1000+** | 1500+ | 1000 – 3000 | | High-Temp Tolerance | **Excellent** | Good | Poor (needs TMS) | | Cost per kWh | **Medium** | Low | Medium-High | | HEV Suitability | **★★★★★** | ★★★ | ★★★★ | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: NiMH Cell — Functional Architecture Flow > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **electrochemical flow** within a sealed NiMH cell during the **discharge** half-cycle. Hydrogen migrates from the NiOOH (positive) lattice through the KOH electrolyte to the Metal Alloy (negative) lattice. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[External Load
Hybrid Powertrain] -->|Electron flow e-| B(Negative Terminal
Nickel-Plated Steel Can) B --> C[Negative Electrode
Metal Hydride MH] C -->|Releases H- as e- and H2O| D[Electrolyte
30 wt% KOH aq] D -->|OH- ion migration| E[Positive Electrode
NiOOH Lattice] E -->|NiOOH reduced to Ni OH 2| F(Positive Terminal
Insulated Cap) F -->|Electrons return via external circuit| A G[Safety Vent] -.Pressure Relief.-> C H[Separator
Non-woven PP] -.Insulation and Electrolyte Retention.-> D I[Oxygen Recombination
Cycle on Overcharge] -.Closed Loop.-> C ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Toyota HEV Battery Pack Architecture > [!NOTE] > This diagram maps the **series-parallel arrangement** of NiMH modules in a typical Toyota THS-II battery pack (e.g., Camry Hybrid, 3rd Gen Prius). ```mermaid flowchart TB Pack[HEV Battery Pack
201.6 V Nominal 1.3 kWh] --> M1[Module 1
6 cells x 1.2 V] Pack --> M2[Module 2
6 cells x 1.2 V] Pack --> M3[Module 3
6 cells x 1.2 V] Pack --> Mn[Module N
28 modules in series] M1 --> C1[D-size NiMH Cell 1] M1 --> C2[D-size NiMH Cell 2] M1 --> C3[D-size NiMH Cell 3] M1 --> C4[D-size NiMH Cell 4] M1 --> C5[D-size NiMH Cell 5] M1 --> C6[D-size NiMH Cell 6] BMS[Battery Management System
SoC SoH Monitoring] -.Cell Voltage and Temp Sensing.-> Pack TMS[Cooling System
Forced Air from Cabin] -.Thermal Regulation.-> Pack INV[Power Inverter
DC to 3 phase AC] -.Motor Drive.-> Pack ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram: Hydrogen Shuttling in NiMH ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Charged Charged --> Discharging: External Load Connected Discharging --> Discharged: V cutoff 1.0 V Discharged --> Charging: External Power Supply Charging --> Charged: V peak 1.45 V Charged --> Overcharge: Excessive Charge Overcharge --> Charged: O2 Recombination Cycle Overcharge --> Venting: Pressure exceeds 2 MPa Venting --> [*]: Safety Vent Activated ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]** *Define Nickel-Metal Hydride (NiMH) battery. List any **two advantages** of NiMH over NiCd batteries for HEV applications.* **Model Answer (3 Marks — Awarding Breakdown):** > **[1 Mark]** NiMH is a rechargeable alkaline battery in which the positive electrode is **nickel hydroxide [Ni(OH)₂]** and the negative electrode is a **hydrogen-absorbing metal alloy (MH)**, with **KOH as the electrolyte**. > **[1 Mark]** *Advantage 1:* NiMH has **higher specific energy (~80 Wh/kg vs ~50 Wh/kg for NiCd)**, providing greater EV/HEV range per unit mass. > **[1 Mark]** *Advantage 2:* NiMH is **environmentally benign** — it contains **no toxic cadmium**, satisfying RoHS directives. --- > **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]** *State the **electrochemical reactions** occurring at the positive and negative electrodes of a NiMH cell during **discharge**.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **[1.5 Marks] Positive Electrode:** > $$\text{NiOOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^- \longrightarrow \text{Ni(OH)}_2 + \text{OH}^-$$ > **[1.5 Marks] Negative Electrode:** > $$\text{MH} + \text{OH}^- \longrightarrow \text{M} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^-$$ --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module Internal Choice Rule:** Students must answer **either Question A or Question B in full.** Each carries 14 marks, split as Part (a) = 7 marks and Part (b) = 7 marks. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Question Aligned to KTU 2024 Scheme]* > **(a)** Describe the **construction and working principle** of a Nickel-Metal Hydride (NiMH) battery with a neat diagram. Explain the role of the **hydrogen-absorbing alloy** in the negative electrode. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **Construction (3.5 Marks):** 1. **Positive Electrode (Cathode):** Nickel hydroxide [Ni(OH)₂] paste impregnated into a porous **nickel foam substrate**, with cobalt additive for conductivity. (1 Mark) 2. **Negative Electrode (Anode):** **Hydrogen-absorbing alloy** (typically **AB₅ type — Mischmetal/Ni/Co/Mn/Al**) coated on a perforated nickel strip. (1 Mark) 3. **Electrolyte:** Aqueous **30 wt% KOH** with LiOH additive. (0.5 Mark) 4. **Separator:** Non-woven **polypropylene** that electrically isolates the electrodes while permitting OH⁻ ion transport. (0.5 Mark) 5. **Housing & Safety:** Nickel-plated steel can with a **resealable safety vent** that activates at ~2–3 MPa internal pressure to prevent rupture. (0.5 Mark) **Working Principle (3.5 Marks):** The NiMH cell operates on **reversible hydrogen transfer** between two solid lattices. (0.5 Mark) - **During discharge**, NiOOH at the positive electrode is reduced to Ni(OH)₂ by accepting a proton and electron; the OH⁻ migrates through the electrolyte to the negative electrode, where the metal hydride releases its stored hydrogen to form water. (1.5 Marks) - **During charge**, the external supply forces electrons to the negative electrode, where water is reduced and H atoms are absorbed into the metal alloy lattice forming MH; at the positive electrode, Ni(OH)₂ is oxidized back to NiOOH. (1.5 Marks) **Role of Hydrogen-Absorbing Alloy:** The negative electrode alloy (e.g., **LaNi₅**) possesses a unique crystalline structure with interstitial sites that **reversibly absorb hydrogen atoms**, achieving hydrogen densities exceeding that of liquid hydrogen per unit volume. This eliminates the need for high-pressure gas storage, making the cell **sealed, safe, and maintenance-free**. (Included in overall 7 marks) > **(b)** Compare NiMH batteries with **Lithium-ion batteries** for **hybrid electric vehicle (HEV)** applications. Discuss **specific energy, cycle life, safety, cost, and operating temperature range**. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** | Parameter | **NiMH** | **Li-ion (NMC)** | HEV Implication (Marks) | |---|---|---|---| | Specific Energy | **60–110 Wh/kg** | **150–250 Wh/kg** | Li-ion offers longer BEV range; NiMH adequate for HEV. (1 Mark) | | Specific Power | **250–1000 W/kg** | **300–1500 W/kg** | Comparable — both suitable for HEV acceleration. (1 Mark) | | Cycle Life | **> 1000 cycles** | **1000–3000 cycles** | Li-ion better for full BEV; NiMH adequate for HEV shallow cycling. (1 Mark) | | Safety | **Excellent** (aqueous electrolyte, no thermal runaway) | **Moderate** (risk of thermal runaway, requires BMS + TMS) | NiMH preferred for safety-critical HEVs. (1.5 Marks) | | Cost per kWh | **Lower for HEV volumes** | **Higher** (cobalt, BMS cost) | NiMH economically attractive for mass-market HEV. (1 Mark) | | Operating Temp | **−20 to +45 °C** (no cooling in many cases) | **0 to +40 °C** strict (needs active TMS) | NiMH simpler thermal management. (1 Mark) | | Memory Effect | **Negligible** | **None** | Both suitable. (0.5 Mark) | > **Examiner's Verdict:** For HEV applications where high power bursts and long calendar life matter more than absolute range, **NiMH remains competitive**; for **BEVs requiring maximum range**, Li-ion is superior. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** > **(a)** With the help of **half-cell reactions**, derive the **overall discharge reaction** of a NiMH cell. Comment on why the cell is called a **"hydrogen shuttle"** device. **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **Half-Cell Reactions (3 Marks):** Positive Electrode (Cathode, reduction): $$\text{NiOOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^- \longrightarrow \text{Ni(OH)}_2 + \text{OH}^-$$ Negative Electrode (Anode, oxidation): $$\text{MH} + \text{OH}^- \longrightarrow \text{M} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^-$$ **Overall Reaction Derivation (3 Marks):** Adding the two half-reactions (electrons and OH⁻ cancel on both sides): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{NiOOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^- + \text{MH} + \text{OH}^- &\longrightarrow \text{Ni(OH)}_2 + \text{OH}^- + \text{M} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^- \\ \text{Net: } \text{NiOOH} + \text{MH} &\longrightarrow \text{Ni(OH)}_2 + \text{M} \end{aligned} $$ **Comment on "Hydrogen Shuttle" Concept (1 Mark):** The net reaction involves **no net change in electrolyte composition** — only a **hydrogen atom migrates** from the NiOOH lattice to the metal alloy lattice via the electrolyte. This reversible shuttling of H (rather than bulk ionic mass transfer) gives the cell its name and its excellent reversibility, sealed operation, and long cycle life. > **(b)** A NiMH battery pack for a HEV consists of **28 modules in series**, each module containing **6 D-size cells in series**. Each cell has a rated capacity of **6.5 Ah** and nominal voltage of **1.2 V**. Calculate: **[7 Marks]** > > **(i)** Pack nominal voltage > **(ii)** Pack nominal energy in kWh > **(iii)** If the vehicle consumes **150 Wh/km**, estimate the **all-electric range** (assuming 100% battery use). **Model Solution:** **(i) Pack Nominal Voltage (2 Marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{\text{cell}} &= 1.2 \text{ V} \\ V_{\text{module}} &= 6 \times 1.2 = 7.2 \text{ V} \\ V_{\text{pack}} &= 28 \times 7.2 = 201.6 \text{ V} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Pack Nominal Energy (3 Marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{\text{cell}} &= V_{\text{cell}} \times Q_{\text{cell}} = 1.2 \times 6.5 = 7.8 \text{ Wh} \\ E_{\text{module}} &= 6 \times 7.8 = 46.8 \text{ Wh} \\ E_{\text{pack}} &= 28 \times 46.8 = 1310.4 \text{ Wh} = 1.31 \text{ kWh} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) All-Electric Range (2 Marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Range} &= \frac{E_{\text{pack}}}{\text{Energy per km}} \\ &= \frac{1310.4 \text{ Wh}}{150 \text{ Wh/km}} \\ &\approx 8.74 \text{ km} \end{aligned} $$ > **Practical Note (KTU Board Insight):** This short range confirms why NiMH in HEVs is used as a **power-assist buffer** (~30–70% SoC operating window) and not as the primary energy source — the **Internal Combustion Engine** handles sustained propulsion. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in NiMH Questions:** > 1. **Confusing NiMH with NiCd chemistry** — NiMH uses **metal alloy (MH)**, NOT metallic cadmium (Cd). Writing "M + 2OH⁻ → Cd(OH)₂" loses full marks. > 2. **Forgetting the role of water (H₂O)** in the positive electrode half-reaction — students often write only `NiOOH + e⁻ → Ni(OH)₂`, which is **electronically and stoichiometrically incorrect**. > 3. **Ignoring the oxygen recombination cycle** — when asked about safety/sealed design, examiners expect mention of the **closed O₂ loop** during overcharge. > 4. **Mishandling DoD in HEV applications** — for HEV NiMH, operating DoD is **NOT 100%**; it's typically **30–70%** to achieve > 1000 cycle life. > 5. **Writing nominal voltage as 1.5 V or 1.35 V** — for KTU 2024 answer scripts, use **1.2 V** as the standard nominal cell voltage unless explicitly stated otherwise. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for NiMH — KTU 2024 Module 2:** - ☐ **Definition:** Rechargeable alkaline battery with **Ni(OH)₂ cathode + Metal Hydride anode + KOH electrolyte**. - ☐ **Nominal Cell Voltage:** **1.2 V**; Open-circuit (full charge): ~1.35 V; Cutoff: 1.0 V. - ☐ **Hydrogen Storage Alloy Types:** **AB₅** (LaNi₅ — most common), **AB₂** (Zr/Ti-Ni), **AB** (TiNi), and rare-earth Mischmetal-based alloys. - ☐ **Overall Discharge Reaction:** **NiOOH + MH → Ni(OH)₂ + M** (no net electrolyte change). - ☐ **Electrolyte:** **30 wt% KOH aqueous** — alkaline (not acidic, not organic). - ☐ **Specific Energy (Practical):** **60 – 110 Wh/kg**; Theoretical max ≈ **197 Wh/kg**. - ☐ **Memory Effect:** **Negligible** (improved over NiCd). - ☐ **Self-Discharge:** **20–30% per month** (higher than Li-ion). - ☐ **Cycle Life (HEV use):** **> 1000 cycles** at 30–70% DoD. - ☐ **Safety Feature:** **Closed O₂ recombination cycle** + resealable safety vent at 2–3 MPa. - ☐ **HEV Operating SoC Window:** **30% – 70%** (shallow cycling for longevity). - ☐ **Real-World Application:** **Toyota Prius THS-II** — 201.6 V, 28-module NiMH pack. - ☐ **Comparison Edge:** NiMH wins on **safety, cost, temperature tolerance**; loses to Li-ion on **specific energy and self-discharge**. - ☐ **Electrochemistry Mnemonic:** *"**NiOOH** is the **charged** state; **Ni(OH)₂** is the **discharged** state."* ---

topics.title: Sodium-Based Batteries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT631 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sodium-Based Batteries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Sodium-Based Batteries** are a class of **secondary (rechargeable) electrochemical energy storage devices** in which **sodium ions (Na⁺)** serve as the primary charge carrier, shuttling between the cathode and anode through a sodium-conducting electrolyte during the charge–discharge cycle.

In the context of the **KTU PEMUT631 (Electric and Hybrid Vehicles, 2024 Scheme)** syllabus, sodium-based batteries are positioned as a **post-lithium alternative chemistry** for traction applications, broadly classified into three engineering-relevant families:

| Sub-Family | Cathode | Anode | Electrolyte | Operating Temp |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sodium-Ion (Na-ion / SIB)** | Layered oxide / Prussian Blue / Polyanionic | Hard carbon / Soft carbon | Organic liquid (NaPF₆ in EC/DMC) | **Ambient (~25 °C)** |
| **Sodium–Sulfur (NaS)** | Sulfur (S) | Liquid sodium (Na) | β-alumina solid electrolyte (BASE) | **300–350 °C** |
| **Sodium–Metal Chloride (ZEBRA)** | NiCl₂ | Liquid sodium (Na) | β″-alumina + NaAlCl₄ auxiliary | **250–300 °C** |

The **ZEBRA** (Zero Emission Battery Research Activities) battery is the most commercially deployed sodium system in EV demonstrators and grid storage. Its nominal cell reaction is:

$$2\,\text{Na} + \text{NiCl}_2 \;\rightleftharpoons\; 2\,\text{NaCl} + \text{Ni}$$

yielding a **theoretical open-circuit voltage of ~2.58 V** and a **theoretical specific energy of ~790 Wh/kg**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of a Sodium-Based Battery as a "Reversible Salt Bridge Pump."**

Imagine two water tanks on different floors of a building:
- **Sodium = water**
- **Discharged state = water pooled in the lower tank**
- **Charging = pumping water back up to the upper tank**
- **Electrolyte = the staircase (pipe) connecting the floors**

When the battery **discharges**, sodium ions (Na⁺) want to "fall downhill" from the high-energy anode to the lower-energy cathode, releasing electrical energy — just like water flowing downhill turns a turbine. When you **charge**, an external power source pushes the ions back uphill, storing chemical potential energy again.

In lithium-ion batteries, the working fluid is *lithium*, which is light, scarce, and geographically concentrated (Chile, Australia, China). **Sodium is the "saltwater cousin"** — heavy, but practically infinite (seawater and the Earth's crust are ~2.6% and ~2.8% sodium by mass, respectively). For a vehicle application, you trade off some energy density for **abundance, cost, and thermal robustness**.

## 1.3 Why Sodium Batteries Matter for KTU Examinations

- **Raw-material cost:** Sodium carbonate is ~**$0.10–0.30/kg** vs. lithium carbonate at ~**$10–15/kg** (highly volatile). Sodium removes a critical geopolitical and economic bottleneck.
- **Abundance:** Na is the **6th most abundant element** in the Earth's crust.
- **Safety:** Ambient-temperature Na-ion cells avoid the lithium-plating dendrite failure mode; high-temperature NaS/ZEBRA use fail-safe ceramic electrolytes.
- **KTU mark-worthy property:** Sodium's standard electrode potential is **E°(Na⁺/Na) = −2.71 V vs. SHE**, only **0.33 V more positive** than lithium's −3.04 V, meaning its thermodynamic penalty versus Li-ion is small in principle.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants for Sodium Battery Analysis**
>
> - Atomic mass of Na: **22.99 g/mol**
> - Standard electrode potential E°(Na⁺/Na): **−2.71 V vs. SHE**
> - Ionic radius of Na⁺: **1.02 Å** (vs. 0.76 Å for Li⁺)
> - Theoretical capacity of Na metal: **1166 mAh/g**
> - Melting point of Na metal: **97.8 °C**
> - Conductivity of β″-alumina at 300 °C: **~0.2–0.4 S/cm**
> - Faraday's constant: **F = 96485 C/mol**

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic

A sodium-based battery operates on **Faraday's laws of electrolysis** combined with **Gibbs free energy** considerations. The mechanism is decomposed below for the canonical ZEBRA (Na-NiCl₂) cell — the most exam-relevant sodium system in KTU PEMUT631.

### Step 1 — Identify the Electrode Reactions

**Discharge (galvanic mode):**

$${\text{Anode (oxidation):}} \quad 2\,\text{Na} \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,\text{Na}^{+} + 2e^{-}$$

$${\text{Cathode (reduction):}} \quad \text{NiCl}_2 + 2e^{-} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Ni} + 2\,\text{Cl}^{-}$$

**Overall discharge:**

$$\boxed{\;2\,\text{Na} + \text{NiCl}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,\text{NaCl} + \text{Ni}\;}$$

### Step 2 — Identify the Charge-Transport Pathway

In ZEBRA cells, two electrolyte media work in tandem:
- **β″-alumina solid electrolyte (BASE):** conducts **Na⁺** ions selectively between anode and cathode compartments.
- **NaAlCl₄ secondary electrolyte:** a molten salt that fills the cathode compartment and provides Na⁺ conductivity between the BASE ceramic and the NiCl₂ granules.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **β″-alumina** is the heart of the ZEBRA architecture. Its nominal composition is **Na₂O·5Al₂O₃** (or ~11 Al₂O₃ : 1 Na₂O for the β″ phase stabilized with MgO/Li₂O). It is a *superionic conductor* with Na⁺ conductivity approaching that of liquid electrolytes at 300 °C.

### Step 3 — Apply Gibbs Free Energy to Compute Voltage

The electromotive force (EMF) of any electrochemical cell is given by the **fundamental thermodynamic relation**:

$$E_{cell} = -\dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{n\,F}$$

For the ZEBRA cell at 300 °C, ΔG° ≈ **−498 kJ/mol of NiCl₂**, with n = 2 electrons transferred. This yields the **theoretical open-circuit voltage of ~2.58 V** — a high value for an aqueous-free chemistry.

### Step 4 — Compute Specific Energy

$$E_{sp} = \dfrac{n\,F\,E_{cell}}{\sum M_i}$$

where ΣMᵢ is the sum of molecular masses of all active species participating per mole of reaction.

### Step 5 — Analyze Overpotentials and Losses

In practice, the usable cell voltage is reduced by three loss terms:

$$V_{terminal} = E_{cell} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohmic} - \eta_{conc}$$

- **η_act (activation overpotential):** charge-transfer barrier at electrode-electrolyte interfaces.
- **η_ohmic (ohmic overpotential):** i·R drop across BASE ceramic and electrodes.
- **η_conc (concentration overpotential):** mass-transport limitation of Na⁺ in liquid Na and NaAlCl₄.

## 2.2 Comparison with Lithium-Ion (For KTU Context)

| Parameter | Na-ion (SIB) | NaS | ZEBRA (Na-NiCl₂) | Li-ion (LFP) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Nominal cell voltage | 2.5–3.3 V | **2.08 V** | **2.58 V** | 3.2–3.7 V |
| Practical specific energy | 90–160 Wh/kg | 150–240 Wh/kg | 90–120 Wh/kg | 120–180 Wh/kg |
| Cycle life | 1000–4000 | 2500+ | 3000+ | 2000–5000 |
| Operating temperature | 25 °C | 300–350 °C | 250–300 °C | 25 °C |
| Thermal runaway risk | Low | Medium | **Very Low** | Medium-High |
| Raw material cost | **Low** | Low | Low-Medium | High |
| EV commercialization | Early (CATL, HiNa) | Limited (trains, grid) | EV demonstrators | **Mass market** |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE FOR SODIUM-BASED BATTERIES — KTU PEMUT631**

| # | Formula / Equation | Description / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{cell} = -\dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{nF}$ | Theoretical EMF from Gibbs free energy | V |
| 2 | $\Delta G^\circ = -n\,F\,E_{cell}$ | Free-energy change from measured voltage | J/mol |
| 3 | $E_{cell} = E^\circ_{cathode} - E^\circ_{anode}$ | Electrode-potential difference (Nernst form) | V |
| 4 | $E = E^\circ - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$ | Full Nernst equation | V |
| 5 | $E_{sp} = \dfrac{n\,F\,E_{cell}}{\sum M_i}$ | Theoretical specific energy | Wh/kg |
| 6 | $E_{vol} = \dfrac{n\,F\,E_{cell}}{\sum V_i}$ | Theoretical volumetric energy density | Wh/L |
| 7 | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{n\,F}{M \cdot 3600}$ | Theoretical specific capacity | Ah/kg |
| 8 | $Q_{prac} = Q_{th} \cdot \eta_{coulombic}$ | Practical capacity with Coulombic efficiency | Ah/kg |
| 9 | $P_{max} = \dfrac{E_{ocv}^{\,2}}{4\,R_{internal}}$ | Maximum power transfer theorem | W |
| 10 | $\sigma_{Na^+} = \sigma_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{k_B T}\right)$ | Arrhenius conductivity of BASE | S/cm |
| 11 | $\eta_{act} = \dfrac{RT}{\alpha n F}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{i}{i_0}\right)$ | Butler–Volmer activation overpotential | V |
| 12 | $\text{SOC}(\%) = \dfrac{Q_{discharged}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100$ | State of charge definition | % |
| 13 | $\text{SOH}(\%) = \dfrac{Q_{max,\,aged}}{Q_{max,\,new}} \times 100$ | State of health | % |
| 14 | $R_{internal} = \dfrac{\Delta V}{I}$ | Internal resistance from voltage sag | Ω |
| 15 | $\eta_{energy} = \dfrac{\int V\,dQ_{dis}}{\int V\,dQ_{chg}}$ | Round-trip energy efficiency | dimensionless |

**Boundary Conditions & Notes:**
- R = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**, F = **96485 C/mol**, k_B = **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K**.
- For ZEBRA cell, use T = 573 K (300 °C) in the Nernst and Arrhenius equations.
- Q in the Nernst equation is the reaction quotient evaluated at operating conditions; for solid-phase reactions (NiCl₂ + 2 Na → 2 NaCl + Ni), activities are typically ≈ 1, simplifying E ≈ E°.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in EV Engineering

Sodium-based batteries occupy **three distinct engineering niches**:

1. **Two-/Three-Wheeler Micro-EVs (Ambient Na-ion):** Lower voltage window (2.5–3.3 V/cell) is acceptable for low-power scooters, e-bikes, and rickshaws. Major adopters in 2024: **CATL (China), Faradion (UK), HiNa Battery (China)**, and **Northvolt (Sweden)**.

2. **Stationary Grid Storage & Heavy Rail (High-Temp NaS/ZEBRA):** The thermal management burden (300 °C) is offset by long cycle life and cheap raw materials. **Mitsubishi, GE, and FZ Sonick** have deployed NaS systems in Japan (300+ MW), with NGK Insulators operating megawatt-scale installations.

3. **EV Range Extenders / Hybrid Packs:** ZEBRA batteries have been tested in **Mercedes-Benz A-Class E-Cell (2009–2011)** demonstrators and **Smart ED prototypes**, demonstrating fast charging (15-minute to 80% SOC) at high C-rates due to the molten-liquid Na anode.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A: Theoretical Specific Energy of the ZEBRA Cell

**Problem statement (typical KTU exam phrasing):**
*"Derive the theoretical specific energy of a Sodium–Nickel Chloride (ZEBRA) cell, given the cell reaction 2 Na + NiCl₂ → 2 NaCl + Ni and the standard EMF E° = 2.58 V at 300 °C."*

### Step 1 — Identify Active Species and Molar Masses

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{Na}} &= 22.99 \;\text{g/mol} \\
M_{\text{NiCl}_2} &= 58.69 + 2(35.45) = 129.59 \;\text{g/mol} \\
M_{\text{NaCl}} &= 22.99 + 35.45 = 58.44 \;\text{g/mol} \\
M_{\text{Ni}} &= 58.69 \;\text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation:** We need the sum of molecular masses of all *reactants* that produce one mole of cell reaction. In ZEBRA cells, the active mass undergoing conversion per formula unit is **2 Na + 1 NiCl₂ → 2 NaCl + 1 Ni**.

### Step 2 — Sum the Active Molar Mass

$$\sum M_i = 2 M_{\text{Na}} + M_{\text{NiCl}_2}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum M_i &= 2(22.99) + 129.59 \\
&= 45.98 + 129.59 \\
&= 175.57 \;\text{g/mol} \;\equiv\; 0.17557 \;\text{kg/mol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation:** Per mole of the overall reaction (which transfers 2 electrons), the active material weighs 175.57 g. Products (NaCl + Ni) are not subtracted because they are formed *in situ* within the cathode compartment — they do not leave the cell — but conservatively we still take only the reactant mass for a theoretical upper bound.

### Step 3 — Apply the Specific Energy Formula

$$E_{sp} = \dfrac{n \cdot F \cdot E_{cell}}{\sum M_i}$$

where **n = 2** electrons per mole of NiCl₂ reacted.

### Step 4 — Substitute Numerical Values

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{sp} &= \dfrac{2 \times 96485 \;\text{C/mol} \times 2.58 \;\text{V}}{0.17557 \;\text{kg/mol}} \\
&= \dfrac{497{,}862.6 \;\text{J/mol}}{0.17557 \;\text{kg/mol}} \\
&= 2{,}835{,}789 \;\text{J/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Convert to Wh/kg

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{sp} &= \dfrac{2{,}835{,}789}{3600} \;\text{Wh/kg} \\
&\approx 787.7 \;\text{Wh/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $\boxed{E_{sp}^{\,\text{ZEBRA}} \approx 788 \;\text{Wh/kg}}$

This is **~2.5× higher** than typical LiFePO₄ cells (theoretical ~586 Wh/kg) — though **practical deliverable specific energy is only 90–120 Wh/kg** due to packaging, electrolyte mass, current collectors, and cell hardware overheads.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B: Nernst Equation for the ZEBRA Cell

### Step 1 — Write the Reaction Quotient Q

For the cell reaction:

$$2\,\text{Na (l)} + \text{NiCl}_2 \text{(s)} \rightleftharpoons 2\,\text{NaCl (s)} + \text{Ni (s)}$$

The reaction quotient is:

$$Q = \dfrac{a_{\text{NaCl}}^{\,2} \cdot a_{\text{Ni}}}{a_{\text{Na}}^{\,2} \cdot a_{\text{NiCl}_2}}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Raoultian Activity Conventions

- **Pure condensed phases (solid Ni, solid NiCl₂, solid NaCl):** activity = 1
- **Liquid sodium at 300 °C:** activity ≈ 1 (pure liquid, fixed by reservoir)

$$\therefore \; a_{\text{Na}} = a_{\text{NiCl}_2} = a_{\text{NaCl}} = a_{\text{Ni}} \approx 1$$

### Step 3 — Solve the Nernst Equation

$$E = E^\circ - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$$

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= E^\circ - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln(1) \\
&= E^\circ - 0 \\
&= E^\circ = 2.58 \;\text{V}
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation:** For solid-phase-only ZEBRA reactions, the Nernst term vanishes — the cell voltage is essentially *flat* throughout discharge, giving ZEBRA batteries a desirable **flat discharge curve** (a major advantage for EV state-of-charge estimation).

---

## 3.3 Derivation C: Energy Density Penalty of Sodium vs. Lithium (KTU Favourite)

> [!NOTE]
> **Compare the theoretical specific energy of Na-metal vs. Li-metal anodes paired with a generic cathode at the same voltage.**

### Step 1 — Theoretical Capacity of Sodium Metal

$$Q_{th} = \dfrac{n F}{M \cdot 3600} = \dfrac{1 \times 96485}{22.99 \times 3600} = 1165.8 \;\text{mAh/g}$$

### Step 2 — Theoretical Capacity of Lithium Metal

$$Q_{th} = \dfrac{96485}{6.941 \times 3600} = 3861.5 \;\text{mAh/g}$$

### Step 3 — Capacity Penalty Ratio

$$\dfrac{Q_{\text{Na}}}{Q_{\text{Li}}} = \dfrac{1165.8}{3861.5} \approx 0.302$$

### Step 4 — Energy Penalty

Sodium's standard potential is **0.33 V less negative** than lithium. So if a hypothetical cathode gives Li an OCV of 3.7 V, Na would give 3.37 V at the same cathode chemistry. The energy density ratio is:

$$\dfrac{E_{\text{Na}}}{E_{\text{Li}}} \approx 0.302 \times \dfrac{3.37}{3.7} \approx 0.275$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Sodium's *theoretical* energy density is roughly **27–30% of lithium's** when only the anode is considered. However, when **real-world cathode mass and cell packaging** are included, this gap narrows significantly to ~50–60%, making sodium competitive in cost-sensitive applications.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Failure Modes & Mitigation Strategies

| Failure Mode | Mechanism | Mitigation (KTU-recommended answer) |
|---|---|---|
| **BASE ceramic fracture** | Thermal shock, mechanical stress during Na plating | Pre-heating protocol, MgO-stabilized β″-alumina, stress-relieved cell geometry |
| **Sodium dendrite penetration** | At high charge rates, Na filaments pierce BASE | Operating at 250–300 °C keeps Na molten & dendrite-free (ZEBRA) |
| **Polysulfide shuttle (NaS)** | Na₂Sₓ dissolves into liquid Na cathode | Use of β″-alumina + sealed cell design |
| **Cathode dissolution (Na-ion)** | Mn-based layered oxides dissolve in electrolyte | Al₂O₃ coating, electrolyte additives (FEC) |
| **Hard-carbon anode expansion** | ~10–12% volume change on (de)sodiation | Nano-structured hard carbon, binders (CMC/SBR) |
| **Thermal runaway (NaS)** | Na + S reaction at >400 °C is highly exothermic | Triple-redundant thermal cutoff, sealed insulation |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

### **Q1.** Define a Sodium-Based Battery. List any three advantages of sodium chemistry over lithium chemistry for EV applications. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer (verbatim-style KTU key):**

> A sodium-based battery is a rechargeable electrochemical cell in which sodium ions (Na⁺) act as the primary charge carrier between two electrodes through a sodium-conducting electrolyte. The general reaction during discharge is $\text{Na}_{anode} \rightarrow \text{Na}^{+} + e^{-} \rightarrow \text{intercalated Na}_{cathode}$.

**Three advantages:**

1. **Raw-material abundance and low cost:** Sodium is the 6th most abundant element in the Earth's crust; sodium carbonate costs **~$0.10–0.30/kg** versus **$10–15/kg** for lithium carbonate, dramatically reducing pack-level material cost.
2. **Use of aluminium current collectors** at both anode and cathode (because Na does not alloy with Al at low potentials), saving ~10% pack mass and cost vs. Li-ion's copper anode current collector.
3. **Enhanced safety:** Ambient Na-ion cells avoid lithium plating dendrites; high-temperature NaS/ZEBRA use fail-safe ceramic electrolytes with no organic flammables, dramatically reducing thermal-runaway risk.

---

### **Q2.** What is β″-alumina? Why is it critical for high-temperature sodium batteries? **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

> β″-alumina is a sodium-ion-conducting ceramic electrolyte with the nominal composition $\text{Na}_2\text{O}\cdot 5\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ (stabilized in the β″-phase by ~0.5–1 wt% MgO or Li₂O). It is critical because:
> 1. It exhibits **superionic Na⁺ conductivity** of ~0.2–0.4 S/cm at 300 °C — comparable to liquid electrolytes.
> 2. It is **selectively conductive to Na⁺ only**, blocking electrons and other ions, eliminating self-discharge.
> 3. It is **thermally and chemically stable** against molten sodium and liquid sulfur, making it the cornerstone of NaS and ZEBRA cells.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2 | PEMUT631]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** Explain with neat sketches the construction and working principle of a Sodium–Sulfur (NaS) battery. Discuss the role of β-alumina electrolyte and the cell reactions during charge and discharge.

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (sketch description for exam sheet):**
A NaS cell is a vertically-oriented cylindrical assembly consisting of:
- A **central sulfur electrode** (cathode during discharge) made of porous carbon-felt impregnated with elemental sulfur.
- A **sodium reservoir** filling the annular space between the sulfur compartment and the outer steel casing (acts as anode during discharge).
- A **β-alumina ceramic tube** (or disc) separating the two compartments, sealed to the casing.
- Thermal management to maintain **300–350 °C**.

**Working Principle:**

During **discharge**, sodium is oxidized at the anode:

$$2\,\text{Na} \rightarrow 2\,\text{Na}^{+} + 2e^{-}$$

The Na⁺ ions migrate through the β-alumina solid electrolyte (BASE) into the sulfur compartment, where they react with sulfur in a multi-step reduction:

$$\text{Na}^{+} + e^{-} + \tfrac{x}{2}\,\text{S} \rightarrow \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{Na}_2\text{S}_x$$

At full discharge, the final product is **Na₂S** (sodium sulfide), giving an overall reaction:

$$2\,\text{Na} + x\,\text{S} \rightleftharpoons \text{Na}_2\text{S}_x$$

The standard EMF is **E° ≈ 2.08 V** at 300 °C, and the practical specific energy is **150–240 Wh/kg**.

**Role of β-alumina:**
The β-alumina acts as a **superionic solid electrolyte**, selectively transporting Na⁺ ions while blocking electrons (preventing internal short) and physically separating the two molten reactants. Its high ionic conductivity at 300 °C (~0.25 S/cm) ensures low internal resistance and high power capability.

**During charge**, all reactions reverse; an external DC source drives Na⁺ back to the sodium compartment.

---

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** A ZEBRA cell operates on the reaction 2 Na + NiCl₂ ⇌ 2 NaCl + Ni with E° = 2.58 V. Calculate the theoretical specific energy and specific capacity. Compare it with a Li-ion cell having E° = 3.7 V and anode mass-equivalent of 6.94 g/mol.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate specific capacity of Na (anode):**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{Na} &= \dfrac{F}{M_{Na} \times 3600} \\
&= \dfrac{96485}{22.99 \times 3600} \\
&= 1165.8 \;\text{mAh/g}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate theoretical specific energy of ZEBRA:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum M_i &= 2(22.99) + 129.59 = 175.57 \;\text{g/mol} \\
E_{sp}^{\,\text{ZEBRA}} &= \dfrac{n F E_{cell}}{\sum M_i} = \dfrac{2 \times 96485 \times 2.58}{175.57} \\
&\approx 2835.8 \;\text{kJ/kg} = 787.7 \;\text{Wh/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with Li reference (per equivalent electron):**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{Li} &= \dfrac{96485}{6.94 \times 3600} = 3861.5 \;\text{mAh/g} \\
\dfrac{Q_{Na}}{Q_{Li}} &= \dfrac{1165.8}{3861.5} \approx 0.302 \\
\dfrac{E_{Na}}{E_{Li}} &= 0.302 \times \dfrac{2.58}{3.70} \approx 0.21
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** ZEBRA's theoretical specific energy is ~788 Wh/kg vs. Li-ion's reference of ~3700 Wh/kg at full theoretical limit — but practical pack-level delivery is ~100 Wh/kg vs. ~250 Wh/kg for Li-ion, narrowing the *real-world* gap to about **2.5×**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** Compare Sodium-Ion (SIB), Sodium-Sulfur (NaS), and ZEBRA (Na-NiCl₂) batteries with respect to: (i) operating temperature, (ii) electrolyte type, (iii) nominal cell voltage, (iv) specific energy, and (v) safety. State two EV applications for each.

**Model Solution (table format recommended for KTU answer sheet):**

| Parameter | SIB | NaS | ZEBRA |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Operating T | 25 °C | 300–350 °C | 250–300 °C |
| (ii) Electrolyte | NaPF₆ in EC/DMC (liquid) | β-alumina ceramic | β″-alumina + NaAlCl₄ |
| (iii) Nominal V | 2.5–3.3 V | 2.08 V | 2.58 V |
| (iv) Sp. energy | 90–160 Wh/kg | 150–240 Wh/kg | 90–120 Wh/kg |
| (v) Safety | High | Medium (thermal mgmt) | Very High |

**Applications:**
- **SIB:** Electric two-/three-wheelers (CATL, HiNa); small urban EVs; stationary storage.
- **NaS:** Grid storage (NGK Japan, 300+ MW); railway catenary regulation; UPS backup.
- **ZEBRA:** EV demonstrators (Mercedes A-Class E-Cell); range-extender modules; heavy-duty EVs.

---

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** With the help of the Nernst equation, explain why the ZEBRA cell shows an almost flat discharge voltage profile. What is the engineering advantage of this feature for EV Battery Management Systems (BMS)?

**Model Solution:**

**The Nernst equation** for the cell reaction $2\text{Na} + \text{NiCl}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\text{NaCl} + \text{Ni}$ is:

$$E = E^\circ - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q \quad\text{where}\quad Q = \dfrac{a_{\text{NaCl}}^{\,2}\cdot a_{\text{Ni}}}{a_{\text{Na}}^{\,2}\cdot a_{\text{NiCl}_2}}$$

Because all active species are **pure condensed phases** (solid Ni, solid NiCl₂, solid NaCl, liquid Na), their activities are essentially **unity**. Therefore:

$$Q = \dfrac{1 \cdot 1}{1 \cdot 1} = 1 \implies \ln Q = 0 \implies E = E^\circ = 2.58\;\text{V}$$

The voltage remains constant as long as all four phases coexist. The profile droops only near 0% and 100% SOC when a phase is depleted (transition into single-phase regions where activities deviate from unity).

**Engineering Advantage for BMS:**

A flat discharge curve enables:
1. **Simpler SOC estimation:** Voltage is a near-direct SOC indicator → Coulomb-counting drift errors are minimized.
2. **Predictable power delivery:** Constant-voltage operation simplifies inverter DC-link design.
3. **Easier cell balancing:** No complex look-up tables required for series-connected packs.
4. **Reliable fault detection:** Any voltage deviation immediately flags degradation or failure.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Missing the activity convention:** Many students forget that pure solids and pure liquids have activity = 1, leading to a non-zero (incorrect) Nernst term. **Marks lost: 1–2 in part (b).**
> 2. **Confusing ZEBRA with NaS:** ZEBRA uses **NiCl₂** (not pure S); NaS uses **pure S**. The cell reactions are *not* the same. Mixing them up costs full marks.
> 3. **Skipping the formula derivation steps:** When asked to "derive," KTU examiners expect at least **3–4 intermediate steps** before the final answer. A single-line answer = ~50% marks only.
> 4. **Forgetting units:** Always convert J/kg → Wh/kg by dividing by 3600. Writing "specific energy = 2,835,789 J/kg" without conversion is **incomplete** — final answer must be in Wh/kg.
> 5. **Neglecting the practical vs. theoretical distinction:** KTU 2024 scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) rewards students who distinguish **theoretical** (Gibbs-derived) from **practical/deliverable** (cell-level) values. Failing to make this distinction loses application-level marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST — Sodium-Based Batteries | PEMUT631 Module 2**

- ☐ Sodium is the **6th most abundant crustal element**; Na⁺ standard potential = **−2.71 V vs. SHE**, only 0.33 V above Li⁺.
- ☐ Three main sodium families: **Na-ion (ambient)**, **NaS (300–350 °C)**, **ZEBRA (250–300 °C)**.
- ☐ ZEBRA cell reaction: $2\,\text{Na} + \text{NiCl}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\,\text{NaCl} + \text{Ni}$, **E° = 2.58 V**, theoretical specific energy **~788 Wh/kg**.
- ☐ NaS cell reaction: $2\,\text{Na} + x\,\text{S} \rightleftharpoons \text{Na}_2\text{S}_x$, **E° ≈ 2.08 V**, theoretical specific energy **~760 Wh/kg**.
- ☐ **β″-alumina (BASE)** is the key solid electrolyte: composition **Na₂O·5Al₂O₃**, stabilized with MgO/Li₂O, conductivity **~0.2–0.4 S/cm** at 300 °C.
- ☐ Na-metal theoretical capacity = **1166 mAh/g**; Li-metal = **3862 mAh/g** — sodium's *intrinsic* capacity is ~30% of lithium's.
- ☐ Nernst equation simplifies to **E ≈ E°** for solid-phase ZEBRA reactions → flat discharge curve → BMS-friendly SOC estimation.
- ☐ Specific energy formula: $E_{sp} = \dfrac{n F E_{cell}}{\sum M_i}$; remember to convert J/kg to Wh/kg via division by 3600.
- ☐ ZEBRA advantages: **flat voltage profile**, **fast charge capable**, **no dendrites** (molten Na), **inherently safe** (BASE fails as insulator on crack).
- ☐ Na-ion advantages for EV: **aluminium current collectors** at anode (save cost/mass), **no cobalt**, **wide operating temperature**, suitable for **2W/3W micro-EVs**.
- ☐ Key EV/grid deployments: **NGK NaS (Japan, 300+ MW grid)**, **HiNa Battery (China, Na-ion)**, **Mercedes A-Class E-Cell (ZEBRA)**, **CATL Na-ion (2024)**.
- ☐ Practical vs. theoretical: Pack-level **deliverable** specific energy is ~10–15% of theoretical; always quote both in KTU answers.
- ☐ Activation overpotential (Butler–Volmer): $\eta_{act} = \dfrac{RT}{\alpha n F}\ln(i/i_0)$; Arrhenius conductivity: $\sigma = \sigma_0 \exp(-E_a/k_B T)$.
- ☐ Remember to use **T = 573 K** (300 °C) when applying Nernst/Arrhenius at ZEBRA operating temperature.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Metal-Air Batteries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT631 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Metal-Air Batteries ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Metal-Air Battery** is a class of **electrochemical energy storage / conversion device** in which the **anode is a pure metal (or metal alloy)** that gets oxidized during discharge, and the **cathode is ambient atmospheric oxygen (O₂) drawn from the air** through a porous gas-diffusion electrode. Because the active cathode reactant (oxygen) is *not stored inside the cell* but is **supplied continuously from the environment**, the practical energy density of the system is exceptionally high. The generic cell reaction is: $$\text{M} + \dfrac{n}{2}\,\text{O}_{2} \longrightarrow \text{MO}_{n/2}$$ In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMUT631 – Module 2)** framework, metal-air cells are positioned as **post-lithium-ion, ultra-high-energy chemistries** with strong relevance to **range-extended electric vehicles (BEVs)**, **range extender modules in HEVs**, and **military/strategic EV platforms**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Metal-Air batteries belong to the *Advanced Battery Technologies for EVs* unit. Students are expected to understand **(i) electrochemistry, (ii) the half-cell reactions at both electrodes, (iii) the reason for very high theoretical specific energy, and (iv) the practical barriers** preventing commercial EV deployment. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – "The Breathing Battery" Imagine a **conventional lithium-ion cell as a packed lunchbox**: the cell carries *both* the food (anode) and the drink (cathode) — the entire meal is sealed inside a heavy container. Now picture a **metal-air battery as a "buffet-style" dinner**: you bring only your *plate* (the metal anode), but you fetch the *oxygen/food* from the surrounding environment (air) at mealtime. Because the oxidizer is **free, weightless, and infinite**, the "container" becomes dramatically lighter. The trade-off? You must have a *well-ventilated kitchen* (open ambient air), and the *dish must be eaten quickly* (limited cycle life in rechargeable versions). This is precisely why metal-air chemistries are often called **"breathing batteries"** in the EV literature. > [!NOTE] > **Key Standard Metrics for KTU Board Exams:** > - **Theoretical Specific Energy:** 1000 – 11 000 Wh/kg > - **Faraday's Constant (F):** **96 485 C mol⁻¹** > - **Open-Circuit Voltage (OCV):** 1.0 – 3.5 V (chemistry dependent) > - **Electrolyte classes:** aqueous (alkaline / saline), non-aqueous, solid-state, and hybrid (mixed) systems. ### 1.3 Why Metal-Air Chemistries Matter for Electric Vehicles 1. **Range anxiety reduction** – Theoretical specific energies of Al-air (~8100 Wh/kg) and Li-air (~11 400 Wh/kg) are **4×–10× higher** than commercial Li-ion (~250 Wh/kg). 2. **Lightweight packaging** – Air cathode eliminates the need to carry a heavy metal-oxide cathode. 3. **Low-cost raw materials** – Zn, Al, Fe, Mg are abundant and inexpensive. 4. **Recyclability & safety** – Many metal-anode systems use non-flammable aqueous electrolytes (Zn-air, Al-air, Mg-air), eliminating thermal-runaway risk. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Generic Architecture of a Metal-Air Cell A metal-air cell physically consists of **four essential components**: - **Metal Anode (negative electrode):** Pure metal (Zn, Al, Li, Mg, Fe, Na) — supplies electrons through oxidation. - **Porous Air Cathode (positive electrode):** A **gas-diffusion electrode (GDE)** typically composed of: 1. **Active carbon layer** (catalyst support, often MnO₂ or Co₃O₄ for ORR — oxygen reduction reaction). 2. **Current collector** (Ni mesh, Cu foam, or carbon paper). 3. **Hydrophobic PTFE backing** — allows O₂ ingress while preventing electrolyte leakage. - **Electrolyte:** Alkaline (KOH), saline (NaCl), aprotic (organic carbonate), or solid-state (glass-ceramic). - **Separator:** Porous polymer (PP, PE, glass-fibre) to prevent anode-cathode shorting. ### 2.2 Operating Principle (Step-by-Step Logic) 1. **Oxygen Intake (Cathode Side):** Atmospheric O₂ diffuses through the porous GDE and dissolves in the electrolyte. 2. **Oxygen Reduction Reaction (ORR):** O₂ gains electrons supplied through the external circuit, forming hydroxide (in alkaline) or peroxide/oxide (in non-aqueous). 3. **Metal Oxidation (Anode Side):** Metal atoms lose electrons, forming metal cations that migrate into the electrolyte. 4. **Ion Migration:** OH⁻, O²⁻, or metal cations move through the electrolyte to maintain charge neutrality. 5. **External Circuit:** Electrons flow from the metal anode (–) to the air cathode (+), producing usable DC current to drive the EV traction motor. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why the very high theoretical specific energy?** Because the **cathode reactant (O₂) is not weighed** in the cell. Energy density is calculated **per unit mass of metal anode only**, and oxygen arrives "for free" from the air. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Symbol Meaning** | **Typical Units** | |---|---|---|---| | Theoretical Specific Energy | $W_{th} = \dfrac{n \cdot F \cdot E_{ocv}}{M \cdot 3600}$ | $n$ = electrons transferred per atom; $F$ = Faraday constant; $E_{ocv}$ = open-circuit voltage; $M$ = molar mass of metal | Wh kg⁻¹ | | Open-Circuit Voltage | $E_{ocv} = \dfrac{-\Delta G}{nF}$ | $\Delta G$ = Gibbs free energy of cell reaction | V | | Theoretical Capacity | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{nF}{M}$ | charge stored per unit mass | Ah kg⁻¹ | | Practical Energy Density | $W_{prac} = \eta \cdot W_{th}$ | $\eta$ = practical efficiency factor (0.10 – 0.30 typical) | Wh kg⁻¹ | | Faradaic Current Density | $j = nFk_c C_{O_2}$ | $k_c$ = kinetic rate constant; $C_{O_2}$ = O₂ concentration | A m⁻² | | Round-Trip Efficiency | $\eta_{rt} = \dfrac{E_{discharge}}{E_{charge}} \times 100$ | ratio of discharge to charge energy | % | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Use of These Equations:** The specific-energy equation is the **#1 high-yield formula for KTU board exams**. Examiners routinely test whether the student can substitute $n$, $F$, $E_{ocv}$, and $M$ correctly and arrive at the right order of magnitude (1000–11 000 Wh/kg) for different chemistries. ### 2.4 Comparison of Major Metal-Air Chemistries (KTU Hot Topic) | **Chemistry** | **Anode Reaction** | **Cathode Reaction (ORR)** | **E_ocv (V)** | **n** | **M (g/mol)** | **W_th (Wh/kg)** | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Zinc-Air | Zn + 2OH⁻ → ZnO + H₂O + 2e⁻ | ½O₂ + H₂O + 2e⁻ → 2OH⁻ | **1.65** | 2 | 65.38 | **1350** | | Aluminum-Air | Al + 3OH⁻ → Al(OH)₃ + 3e⁻ | ¾O₂ + 3/2 H₂O + 3e⁻ → 3OH⁻ | **2.71** | 3 | 26.98 | **8100** | | Lithium-Air (non-aq.) | Li → Li⁺ + e⁻ | Li⁺ + e⁻ + ½O₂ → ½Li₂O₂ | **2.96** | 1 | 6.94 | **11 400** | | Magnesium-Air | Mg + 2OH⁻ → Mg(OH)₂ + 2e⁻ | ½O₂ + H₂O + 2e⁻ → 2OH⁻ | **3.09** | 2 | 24.31 | **6800** | | Iron-Air | Fe + 2OH⁻ → Fe(OH)₂ + 2e⁻ | ½O₂ + H₂O + 2e⁻ → 2OH⁻ | **1.28** | 2 | 55.85 | **1228** | | Sodium-Air | Na → Na⁺ + e⁻ | Na⁺ + e⁻ + ¼O₂ → ½Na₂O | **1.90** | 1 | 23.00 | **2218** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-world utility:** Zn-air is already used in **hearing aids**; Al-air in **range-extender modules** (Phinergy/Phinergy-Marine demos on EVs); Li-air is still in **laboratory prototype** stage (University of Oxford, Argonne National Lab); Mg-air is being pursued by **SAIT/Papercell** for low-power IoT and EV auxiliary loads. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Theoretical Specific Energy of a Metal-Air Cell **Starting Point – Faraday's Law of Electrolysis:** The electrical charge $Q$ liberated per unit mass of metal is given by: $$Q = \dfrac{n \cdot F}{M} \quad \text{(Coulombs per gram)}$$ **Step 1 — Convert to Ampere-Hours (the EV industry's standard unit for battery capacity):** $$Q_{Ah/kg} = \dfrac{n \cdot F}{M \cdot 3600} \quad \text{(Ah per kg)}$$ *(Division by 3600 converts Coulombs → Ampere-Hours, since 1 Ah = 3600 C.)* **Step 2 — Multiply by the Open-Circuit Voltage $E_{ocv}$ to obtain energy:** $$W_{th} = E_{ocv} \times Q_{Ah/kg} = \dfrac{n \cdot F \cdot E_{ocv}}{M \cdot 3600} \quad \text{(Wh per kg)}$$ **Step 3 — The thermodynamic justification (Gibbs free energy route):** $$\Delta G = -nF \cdot E_{ocv} \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_{ocv} = \dfrac{-\Delta G}{nF}$$ **Substitute this back into the energy equation:** $$W_{th} = \dfrac{n \cdot F}{M \cdot 3600} \cdot \dfrac{-\Delta G}{nF} = \dfrac{-\Delta G}{M \cdot 3600}$$ This elegant result shows that **the maximum energy density depends only on the Gibbs free energy of formation and the metal's atomic mass** — the oxygen mass disappears from the equation because it is "free." ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Numerical Verification for Zinc-Air Cell **Given (from KTU data tables):** - $n = 2$ electrons - $E_{ocv} = 1.65$ V - $M_{Zn} = 65.38$ g mol⁻¹ - $F = 96\,485$ C mol⁻¹ **Step 1 — Substitute into the formula:** $$W_{th} = \dfrac{2 \times 96\,485 \times 1.65}{65.38 \times 3600}$$ **Step 2 — Numerator evaluation:** $$2 \times 96\,485 = 192\,970$$ $$192\,970 \times 1.65 = 318\,400.5 \;\text{J/g}$$ **Step 3 — Denominator evaluation:** $$65.38 \times 3600 = 235\,368$$ **Step 4 — Final division:** $$W_{th} = \dfrac{318\,400.5}{235\,368} \approx 1.353 \;\text{Wh/g} = 1353 \;\text{Wh/kg}$$ **Step 5 — Interpretation:** This is the **theoretical** value. Practical Zn-air coin cells deliver only **400–450 Wh/kg** (factor of ~3 lower), giving $\eta \approx 0.30$ (30% practical efficiency). KTU examiners love asking "Why is the practical value so much lower?" — the answer involves **ORR overpotential, Zn-dendrite formation, CO₂ contamination from air, and electrode polarization losses.** ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Aluminum-Air Cell — Highest Practical Promise for EV Range Extenders **Given:** - $n = 3$, $E_{ocv} = 2.71$ V, $M_{Al} = 26.98$ g mol⁻¹ **Step 1 — Numerator:** $$3 \times 96\,485 \times 2.71 = 3 \times 261\,474.35 = 784\,423.05$$ **Step 2 — Denominator:** $$26.98 \times 3600 = 97\,128$$ **Step 3 — Division:** $$W_{th} = \dfrac{784\,423.05}{97\,128} \approx 8.077 \;\text{Wh/g} = 8077 \;\text{Wh/kg}$$ **Rounded:** **W_th ≈ 8100 Wh/kg**, which is **~32× higher** than commercial Li-ion (~250 Wh/kg) — a single Al-air module can theoretically extend an EV's range by **several thousand kilometres** on a single anode fill, provided CO₂ and water ingress are managed. ### 3.4 Derivation 4: Why Lithium-Air Has Not Reached Commercial EV Production **The full electrochemistry (non-aqueous):** $$\text{Anode:}\;\;\; \text{Li} \rightarrow \text{Li}^+ + e^- \quad (E^\circ = -3.04\;\text{V vs SHE})$$ $$\text{Cathode:}\;\;\; \text{Li}^+ + e^- + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \tfrac{1}{2}\text{Li}_2\text{O}_2 \quad (E^\circ = +0.30\;\text{V vs SHE})$$ $$E_{ocv} = 0.30 - (-3.04) = 2.96\;\text{V (theoretical) — but practically 2.5–2.7 V only)}$$ **Computational result (n = 1 per Li, M_Li = 6.94 g/mol):** $$W_{th} = \dfrac{1 \times 96\,485 \times 2.96}{6.94 \times 3600} = \dfrac{285\,595.6}{24\,984} \approx 11\,430\;\text{Wh/kg}$$ **The practical blockers (high-yield KTU content):** 1. **Insoluble Li₂O₂ discharge product** clogs the porous cathode → capacity fade. 2. **Electrolyte decomposition** at the cathode interface during recharge. 3. **Oxygen evolution reaction (OER) overpotential** is huge (~1 V) — kills round-trip efficiency. 4. **Lithium dendrite formation** during charging — safety hazard. 5. **Air-contaminant sensitivity** (H₂O, CO₂, N₂) — requires ultrapure O₂ source in lab demos. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner Tip:** The single most important concept is the **gap between theoretical and practical energy density**. Always quote the **practical ratio $\eta \approx 0.10$–0.30** and the **dominant loss mechanism** for whatever chemistry is asked. ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Practical Feasibility Ranking for EV Deployment | **Rank** | **Chemistry** | **Theoretical W (Wh/kg)** | **Practical W (Wh/kg)** | **Rechargeable?** | **EV Readiness** | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 🥇 1 | **Al-Air** | 8100 | 1300 | No (mech. refill) | Range-extender demo | | 🥈 2 | **Li-Air** | 11 400 | 600 (lab) | In research | Lab prototype | | 🥉 3 | **Mg-Air** | 6800 | 680 | No | Low-power IoT | | 4 | **Zn-Air** | 1350 | 450 | Yes (limited) | Hearing aids, e-bikes | | 5 | **Fe-Air** | 1228 | 80 | Yes (100s cycles) | Grid storage pilot | | 6 | **Na-Air** | 2218 | <200 | Lab only | Pre-research | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Generic Metal-Air Cell ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Metal Anode
Zn or Al or Li or Mg or Fe] -->|Oxidation releases electrons| B[External Load
EV Traction Motor] B -->|Electrons flow through circuit| C[Porous Air Cathode
Gas Diffusion Electrode] D[Ambient Air
O2 diffuses inward] -->|Oxygen Reduction Reaction| C C -->|OH- or O2- ions migrate| E[Electrolyte
Aqueous or Non-aqueous or Solid] E -->|Charge balance via ion movement| A F[Separator
Prevents internal short] -.-> A F -.-> C C -->|Reaction byproducts: ZnO, Al OH 3, Li2O2, Mg OH 2| G[Discharge Products
Stored in cell] ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an EV Metal-Air Range-Extender Module ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PowerPack[Metal Air Range Extender Pack] M1[Al Anode Cartridge 1] --- E1[Alkaline Electrolyte Reservoir] M2[Al Anode Cartridge 2] --- E1 M3[Al Anode Cartridge 3] --- E1 E1 --- C1[Air Cathode Stack 1] E1 --- C2[Air Cathode Stack 2] C1 --> BMS[Battery Management System] C2 --> BMS BMS --> INV[DC DC Converter] end INV --> TRA[EV Traction Inverter] TRA --> MOT[3 Phase AC Motor] MOT --> WHL[Drive Wheels] AIR[Atmospheric O2 Supply] --> C1 AIR --> C2 H2O[CO2 and H2O Scrubber Filter] --> AIR BMS -. State of Charge Telemetry .-> DASH[Driver Dashboard] ``` ### 4.3 Comparative Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Right Metal-Air Chemistry for an EV ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Start: Define EV Use Case] --> Q1{Requires Rechargeable or Refillable} Q1 -->|Refillable Mech| R1[Pick Al Air or Mg Air] Q1 -->|Electrically Rechargeable| R2[Pick Zn Air or Fe Air] R1 --> Q2{High Energy per kg Critical} Q2 -->|Yes| R3[Choose Al Air 8100 Wh kg] Q2 -->|No| R4[Choose Mg Air 6800 Wh kg safer] R2 --> Q3{Cost per kWh Critical} Q3 -->|Yes| R5[Choose Fe Air cheapest] Q3 -->|No| R6[Choose Zn Air mature tech] R3 --> DEPLOY1[Range Extender Module] R4 --> DEPLOY2[Low Power Auxiliary] R5 --> DEPLOY3[Grid Scale EV Charging Buffer] R6 --> DEPLOY4[Scooter and E Bike Application] ``` ### 4.4 Schematic of Half-Cell Reactions at Each Electrode (Zinc-Air Example) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph AnodeHalfCell[Anode Side Negative] ZN[Zn Metal] -->|Zn plus 2 OH minus| ZNO[H2O plus ZnO] ZN -->|Liberates 2 e minus| E1[2 Electrons] end subgraph CathodeHalfCell[Cathode Side Positive] O2[1/2 O2 from Air] -->|Plus H2O and 2e minus| OH[2 OH minus ions] end E1 -->|Travels through external circuit| CathodeHalfCell OH -->|Migrate to anode| ZN ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Note:** Mermaid's syntax does not render superscripts/subscripts inside node labels. In the schematic above, chemical formulas use the closest text approximation. In a KTU board exam, always use **proper chemical notation with subscripts/superscripts** when writing on the answer script — *e.g.*, Zn(OH)₂, Al(OH)₃, Li₂O₂. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **"What is a metal-air battery? Why is its theoretical specific energy so much higher than a conventional lithium-ion cell?" (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** A metal-air battery is an electrochemical cell in which the **anode is a pure metal (Zn, Al, Li, Mg, Fe)** and the **cathode is ambient atmospheric oxygen** drawn through a porous gas-diffusion electrode. The theoretical specific energy is exceptionally high because **the active cathode reactant (O₂) is not stored inside the cell**, so the energy is calculated *per unit mass of the metal anode only*. As a result, systems like Al-air and Li-air can theoretically deliver **3000 – 11 000 Wh/kg**, vastly exceeding the **~250 Wh/kg** of conventional Li-ion cells. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **"State the half-cell reactions during discharge of a Zinc-Air cell. Why is this chemistry popular for hearing aids?" (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Half-cell reactions:** - **Anode (oxidation):** $\text{Zn} + 2\text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{ZnO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e^-$ - **Cathode (reduction — ORR):** $\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + 2e^- \rightarrow 2\text{OH}^-$ - **Overall:** $\text{Zn} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{ZnO}$ **Why popular for hearing aids:** Zinc-air is popular because it has a **high volumetric energy density (~1400 Wh/L)** in a flat, coin-cell form factor, **low self-discharge**, and **long shelf life (~3 years)**. The flat cell geometry fits behind the ear, and the small air-hole allows continuous O₂ ingress for steady voltage output. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Choice-Based Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]** **(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **construction and working principle of a metal-air battery**. Highlight the role of the **gas-diffusion electrode (GDE)** and the **ORR catalyst**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Derive the **theoretical specific energy equation** for a metal-air cell from first principles (Faraday's law + Gibbs free energy). **Calculate the theoretical specific energy of an Aluminum-Air cell** with $n=3$, $E_{ocv}=2.71$ V, and $M_{Al}=26.98$ g/mol. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Answer (a):** **Construction:** A metal-air cell consists of four key parts — (1) **metal anode** (Zn, Al, Li), (2) **porous air cathode (GDE)** with catalyst, (3) **electrolyte** (alkaline, saline, or non-aqueous), and (4) **separator** (porous polymer). **Working Principle:** - During discharge, the metal anode undergoes oxidation: $\text{M} \rightarrow \text{M}^{n+} + ne^-$ - The liberated electrons travel through the external circuit to the air cathode, powering the EV load. - At the cathode, atmospheric O₂ (drawn in through the porous GDE) undergoes **oxygen reduction reaction (ORR):** $\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$ (alkaline). - The OH⁻ ions migrate to the anode, where they combine with metal ions to form metal-oxide/hydroxide discharge products. **Role of the GDE:** The GDE is a *multi-layer sandwich* — a **hydrophobic PTFE backing** (lets O₂ in, keeps electrolyte out), a **catalyst-loaded carbon layer** (where ORR occurs), and a **current collector** (Ni mesh or carbon paper). Without a properly designed GDE, the cell would either flood (electrolyte leakage) or suffocate (no O₂ supply). **Role of the ORR Catalyst:** Common catalysts include **MnO₂, Co₃O₄, perovskites, and Pt/C**. The catalyst lowers the **ORR activation overpotential** (~0.3–0.4 V), which is the single largest voltage loss in metal-air cells. Better catalysts → higher practical efficiency. --- **Model Answer (b):** **Derivation (as shown in Section 3.1 above):** Starting from Faraday's law: $$Q_{Ah/kg} = \dfrac{nF}{M \times 3600}$$ Multiplying by $E_{ocv}$: $$W_{th} = E_{ocv} \cdot \dfrac{nF}{M \times 3600} = \dfrac{nFE_{ocv}}{3600\,M}$$ Equivalently, using $\Delta G = -nFE_{ocv}$: $$W_{th} = \dfrac{-\Delta G}{3600\,M}$$ **Numerical Calculation for Al-Air:** $$W_{th} = \dfrac{3 \times 96\,485 \times 2.71}{26.98 \times 3600}$$ **Numerator:** $3 \times 96\,485 = 289\,455$; $289\,455 \times 2.71 = 784\,423.05$ J/g **Denominator:** $26.98 \times 3600 = 97\,128$ $$W_{th} = \dfrac{784\,423.05}{97\,128} \approx 8.077 \;\text{Wh/g} = 8077 \;\text{Wh/kg} \approx \mathbf{8100\;Wh/kg}$$ **Conclusion:** This theoretical value is **~32× greater** than a commercial Li-ion cell (~250 Wh/kg). However, **practical** Al-air cells achieve only **~1300 Wh/kg** (efficiency ~16%) due to parasitic Al-corrosion, water consumption, and ohmic losses in the alkaline electrolyte. --- **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Alternative Module Choice]** **(a)** Compare **Zinc-Air, Aluminum-Air, and Lithium-Air batteries** in terms of electrochemistry, theoretical specific energy, advantages, and major challenges for EV application. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the **practical challenges** that prevent metal-air batteries from replacing Li-ion in mass-market electric vehicles. Discuss at least **four specific barriers** with technical reasoning. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Answer (a):** | **Parameter** | **Zn-Air** | **Al-Air** | **Li-Air** | |---|---|---|---| | Electrolyte | Alkaline (KOH) | Alkaline (KOH) or saline | Non-aqueous (organic carbonate) | | Anode Reaction | Zn + 2OH⁻ → ZnO + H₂O + 2e⁻ | Al + 3OH⁻ → Al(OH)₃ + 3e⁻ | Li → Li⁺ + e⁻ | | Cathode Reaction (ORR) | ½O₂ + H₂O + 2e⁻ → 2OH⁻ | ¾O₂ + 3/2 H₂O + 3e⁻ → 3OH⁻ | Li⁺ + e⁻ + ½O₂ → ½Li₂O₂ | | E_ocv (V) | 1.65 | 2.71 | 2.96 | | W_th (Wh/kg) | 1350 | 8100 | 11 400 | | Rechargeability | Limited | Mechanically refillable | Lab research only | | Major Advantage | Mature, low cost | Ultra-high energy | Highest theoretical | | Key Challenge | CO₂ sensitivity | Corrosion, H₂ evolution | Li₂O₂ clogging, dendrites | **Key insight:** Zn-air is the most commercially mature, Al-air offers the best *near-term* EV range-extender potential, and Li-air is the *long-term* aspirational technology. --- **Model Answer (b) — Four Practical Barriers:** 1. **Limited Rechargeability:** Most high-energy metal-air cells (Al, Mg) are **primary (non-rechargeable)**. Electrically recharging Al or Mg anodes is hampered by **parasitic hydrogen evolution** and **oxide-passivation film formation**, both of which consume efficiency irreversibly. 2. **Air-Cathode Degradation (CO₂ + H₂O poisoning):** Atmospheric CO₂ reacts with alkaline electrolytes to form **carbonates (K₂CO₃, Na₂CO₃)** that clog the GDE pores. Water vapour can also flood the hydrophobic layer. Both effects **reduce ORR activity and shorten cycle life**. 3. **Air-Electrode ORR Kinetics Slowness:** The **oxygen reduction reaction** has an intrinsically slow multi-electron-transfer mechanism. Even with the best catalysts, ORR overpotential is **0.3–0.5 V**, cutting practical energy density by **30–50%**. 4. **Safety and Dendrite Formation (Rechargeable Systems):** In rechargeable Li-air, **lithium dendrites** grow on the anode during charge, creating **internal short-circuit risk** — a serious EV safety hazard. The deposited Li₂O₂ also physically **clogs the air cathode**, terminating discharge prematurely. 5. **Manufacturing and Supply-Chain Immaturity:** Unlike Li-ion, metal-air technology has **no Gigafactory-scale production line**, **no standardised cell formats (18650/21700)**, and **no uniform BMS protocols**, making EV integration prohibitively expensive today. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Avoid These Common Mistakes):** > - **Wrong sign in $\Delta G$ equation:** Always $\Delta G = -nFE_{ocv}$. Using a *positive* sign will yield a *negative* voltage — instant zero. > - **Forgetting to divide by 3600:** Students often write $W = nFE/M$ and obtain **Wh/g × 3600 = kJ/kg** (correct magnitude), but examiner expects **Wh/kg** units; missing the divisor loses 1 mark. > - **Mixing up OCV values:** Zn-air is **1.65 V**, NOT 1.5 V; Al-air is **2.71 V**, NOT 1.9 V. Memorise the table. > - **Conflating theoretical vs. practical energy density:** Always quote *both* numbers and the efficiency ratio. Stating only theoretical makes the answer look incomplete. > - **Wrong anode half-reaction:** For Al-air, the product is Al(OH)₃, NOT Al₂O₃ (in alkaline electrolyte). For Li-air, the product is Li₂O₂ in non-aqueous systems. > - **Missing the role of the GDE:** Examiners specifically test whether the student understands *why* a gas-diffusion electrode is necessary. A vague "cathode where O₂ reacts" answer loses 2 marks. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Definition:** Metal-air battery = metal anode + atmospheric O₂ cathode (GDE) → ultra-high specific energy. - **Key Buzzword for KTU:** "**Breathing battery**" — remember the analogy. - **Core formula to memorise:** $W_{th} = \dfrac{nFE_{ocv}}{M \times 3600}$ (derive on demand). - **Faraday's constant:** **F = 96 485 C mol⁻¹** — never round to 96 000. - **OCV memorisation table:** Zn-air 1.65 V | Al-air 2.71 V | Li-air 2.96 V | Mg-air 3.09 V | Fe-air 1.28 V | Na-air 1.90 V. - **Theoretical energy density ranking (highest → lowest):** Li-air (11 400) > Al-air (8 100) > Mg-air (6 800) > Na-air (2 218) > Zn-air (1 350) > Fe-air (1 228) Wh/kg. - **Practical energy density ranking:** Al-air (1 300) > Li-air (600, lab) > Mg-air (680) > Zn-air (450) > Fe-air (80) > Na-air (<200) Wh/kg. - **Four practical barriers (high-yield):** Limited rechargeability | CO₂/H₂O poisoning | Slow ORR kinetics | Li-dendrite safety (rechargeable systems). - **EV application context:** Zn-air → hearing aids, e-bikes; Al-air → range-extender demos (Phinergy); Fe-air → grid storage buffer; Li-air → lab only. - **Three electrolyte classes:** Alkaline (KOH) | Saline (NaCl) | Non-aqueous (organic carbonate). - **Reactor hardware:** Porous GDE = PTFE hydrophobic layer + catalyst layer + current collector. - **Thermodynamic root equation:** $\Delta G = -nFE_{ocv}$ → cell voltage comes from free energy. - **Examiner trap:** Theoretical ≠ practical. Always state *both* and the *efficiency ratio* ($\eta \approx 0.10$–0.30). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU 2024 Strategy:** In the exam, when a metal-air question appears: (1) define the cell, (2) write both half-reactions, (3) state the overall reaction, (4) plug numbers into $W_{th} = nFE_{ocv}/(M \times 3600)$, and (5) comment on **practical limitations**. A 7-mark question completed in this structure is virtually guaranteed full marks.

topics.title: Supercapacitors and Flywheels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT631 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Supercapacitors and Flywheels ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Supercapacitors (Ultracapacitors) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition** > A **Supercapacitor** (also called an **Ultracapacitor** or **Electric Double-Layer Capacitor, EDLC**) is an electrochemical energy storage device that stores electrical energy **electrostatically** by means of the reversible adsorption of ions from an electrolyte onto high-surface-area, porous carbon electrodes, separated by an ion-permeable membrane and an electrolyte. Unlike a conventional electrolytic capacitor, a supercapacitor does **not** rely on a faradaic (electron-transfer) chemical reaction at the bulk electrode. Instead, charge separation occurs across the **Helmholtz double layer** — a region only a few angstroms thick at the electrode/electrolyte interface.
