Because this layer is so thin and the effective electrode surface area is enormous (carbon nanotubes or activated carbon can exceed **1500 m²/g**), the capacitance reaches thousands of farads in a package smaller than a soft-drink can. **Conceptual Analogy – "The Pressure Vessel"** Think of a supercapacitor as a *pressurized air tank*. It cannot hold a great deal of air (energy), but it can **release that air extremely fast** (power). A battery, by contrast, is more like a coal pile: enormous energy, but a slow release (burn rate). When an EV needs a sudden burst of power (e.g., acceleration or capturing regen-braking energy), the supercapacitor is the ideal buffer. > [!NOTE] > **Key Performance Markers (Industry Reference Values)** > - **Specific Energy:** 5 – 10 Wh/kg > - **Specific Power:** 5,000 – 15,000 W/kg > - **Cycle Life:** 500,000 – 1,000,000+ cycles > - **Charge Time:** 1 – 30 seconds (to 95 % SoC) > - **Operating Temperature:** −40 °C to +65 °C ### 1.2 Flywheels > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition** > A **Flywheel Energy Storage System (FESS)** is a mechanical energy storage device that stores energy in the form of **rotational kinetic energy** of a spinning mass (rotor). The rotor is coupled to a motor/generator through a common shaft, often operating in a vacuum chamber on magnetic bearings to minimize friction losses. When the motor is supplied with electrical energy, it accelerates the rotor to a very high angular velocity. Conversely, when energy is needed, the spinning rotor drives the generator, converting kinetic energy back into electricity. Because the energy scales with the **square of the angular velocity** ($E \propto \omega^{2}$), modern FESS units spin the rotor at extraordinarily high speeds (often **20,000 – 60,000 rpm** for composite rotors). **Conceptual Analogy – "The Spinning Top"** Imagine a child's spinning top: you wind it up (input energy), it stores that energy as spin. Give it a gentle push (load) and it slows slightly while delivering that push back. A flywheel in an EV works the same way, but it is engineered so that one "push" can deliver a brief, powerful burst of electrical energy — exactly what a regenerative-braking event or a launch assist demands. > [!NOTE] > **Key Performance Markers (Industry Reference Values)** > - **Specific Energy:** 5 – 100 Wh/kg (depends on rotor class) > - **Specific Power:** 5,000 – 10,000 W/kg > - **Cycle Life:** > 100,000 cycles (mechanical, often limited by bearings) > - **Round-trip Efficiency:** 80 – 95 % > - **Self-Discharge:** 1 – 20 % per hour (dominant loss mechanism) --- ### 1.3 Why These Two Technologies Coexist with Batteries In an Electric or Hybrid Vehicle, the propulsion power demand is **transient and highly variable** (peaks during acceleration and regenerative braking). A chemical battery alone suffers when forced to deliver or absorb these sharp pulses (lifetime degradation, thermal stress). Supercapacitors and flywheels are introduced because their **high specific power** and **near-infinite cycle life** make them ideal as a **peak-power buffer**, while the battery (or fuel cell) handles the **average, sustained load**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operating Principle of an EDLC Supercapacitor The functioning of a supercapacitor can be broken down into **five sequential physical steps**: 1. **External charging** – A DC voltage source is applied across the two porous carbon electrodes. 2. **Ion migration** – Positive ions (cations) in the electrolyte migrate toward the negative electrode; negative ions (anions) migrate toward the positive electrode. 3. **Double-layer formation** – Ions form a monolayer (or interlayer) on the inner surface of each porous electrode, balancing the electrode's excess electronic charge. Two such layers in series form the capacitor. 4. **Energy storage** – Energy is held in the **electric field** between the oppositely charged ion layers and electrode surfaces. 5. **Discharge** – When connected to a load, ions diffuse back into the electrolyte bulk, releasing energy as current. > [!NOTE] > **Why "Supercapacitor"?** > The capacitance $C$ of a parallel-plate capacitor is $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{r} \varepsilon_{0} A}{d}$. Because the effective charge-separation distance $d$ in an EDLC is only **~0.3 – 1 nm** (Helmholtz layer), and the effective area $A$ (interior surface of porous carbon) can be **~1000 m² per gram**, the resulting capacitance is several orders of magnitude greater than that of a conventional capacitor. ### 2.2 Operating Principle of a Flywheel (FESS) The flywheel's energy pathway follows **four mechanical-electrical conversion steps**: 1. **Charging (motor mode)** – The grid/battery drives the integrated motor, which spins up the rotor (kinetic energy stored as $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$). 2. **Storage (coasting)** – The rotor spins in a vacuum on magnetic bearings; losses are dominated by aerodynamic drag and bearing friction. 3. **Discharging (generator mode)** – When energy is required, the rotor's kinetic energy drives the same machine (now a generator), producing electrical output. Voltage is regulated by the power electronics. 4. **Speed feedback** – A tachometer/sensor continuously feeds rotor speed to the controller so the state-of-charge can be computed from $\omega$. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet | # | Quantity / Equation | Formula | Symbol Meaning & Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Capacitance (parallel plate) | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{r} \varepsilon_{0} A}{d}$ | $\varepsilon_{0} = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m; $\varepsilon_{r}$ = relative permittivity; $A$ = area (m²); $d$ = separation (m) | | 2 | Energy stored in capacitor | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$ | $C$ in F; $V$ in V; $E$ in J | | 3 | Maximum deliverable power (matched load) | $P_{max} = \dfrac{V^{2}}{4 R_{ESR}}$ | $V$ = rated voltage; $R_{ESR}$ = equivalent series resistance ($\Omega$) | | 4 | Specific energy of capacitor | $E_{m} = \dfrac{C V^{2}}{2 m}$ | $m$ = mass (kg); $E_m$ in J/kg or Wh/kg | | 5 | Flywheel stored energy | $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$ | $I$ = moment of inertia (kg·m²); $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s) | | 6 | Specific energy of flywheel | $E_{m} = \dfrac{I \omega^{2}}{2 m}$ | $E_m$ in J/kg | | 7 | Rotor hoop stress (thin rim) | $\sigma = \rho \omega^{2} r^{2}$ | $\rho$ = density (kg/m³); $r$ = rim radius (m); $\sigma$ in Pa | | 8 | Energy density limit (material) | $\dfrac{E}{m} = K \dfrac{\sigma}{\rho}$ | $K$ = geometry factor; $\sigma/\rho$ = specific strength | | 9 | State-of-charge from angular velocity | $SoC = \left( \dfrac{\omega}{\omega_{max}} \right)^{2}$ | Ratio of stored energy to full energy | | 10 | Depth-of-discharge effect on life | $N_{cycles} \propto \dfrac{1}{(\Delta DoD)^{k}}$ | $k \approx 1.5 - 2$ (empirical) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Engineering Use** > These equations directly drive **pack sizing** in EV design. For instance, to size a flywheel bus (e.g., the **GYROBUS**, ~1960s; or the modern **Velkess Flywheel**), engineers pick a composite's $\sigma/\rho$ ratio, then back-calculate the safe maximum $\omega$ so that $\sigma$ stays below the material's tensile limit. The same logic governs supercapacitor module sizing for **Formula-1 KERS** and **Mazda i-ELOOP** type mild-hybrid systems. ### 2.4 Ragone Plot – Where Each Technology Sits The **Ragone plot** (specific energy vs. specific power) is the standard tool to compare storage systems. On a log–log plot: - **Fuel cells / gasoline:** High energy, low power. - **Li-ion batteries:** ~100–250 Wh/kg, ~250–1000 W/kg. - **Supercapacitors:** ~5–10 Wh/kg, but **>10,000 W/kg**. - **Flywheels:** ~5–100 Wh/kg, ~5,000–10,000 W/kg. - **Conventional capacitors:** Very low energy, very high power. The implication for EV design: **supercapacitors and flywheels occupy the "power" niche**, complementing batteries which sit in the "energy" niche. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Energy Stored in a Supercapacitor (Starting from First Principles) We begin from the definition of capacitance for a parallel-plate geometry (which extends to a double-layer capacitor because the EDLC behaves as two such capacitors in series per cell). The capacitance of a parallel plate of area $A$, plate separation $d$, and dielectric constant $\varepsilon_{r}$ is: $$ C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{r} \varepsilon_{0} A}{d} $$ The energy stored in the electric field of a charged capacitor, from the line integral of voltage times incremental charge, is: $$ E = \int_{0}^{Q} v(q)\, dq $$ Because for a linear capacitor $v(q) = q/C$, the integral becomes: $$ E = \int_{0}^{Q} \dfrac{q}{C}\, dq = \dfrac{1}{C} \left[ \dfrac{q^{2}}{2} \right]_{0}^{Q} = \dfrac{Q^{2}}{2C} $$ Substituting $Q = C V$ (definition of linear capacitance): $$ E = \dfrac{(C V)^{2}}{2 C} = \dfrac{C^{2} V^{2}}{2 C} $$ Simplifying the factor of $C$: $$ \boxed{E = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}} $$ **Interpretation in EV context:** The energy stored scales with the **square of the voltage**. Therefore, increasing the rated voltage of a supercapacitor module is far more effective than increasing capacitance alone. This is why automotive supercapacitor packs are often configured in **series strings of cells**, each rated 2.5–2.7 V, to reach ~16–48 V modules and 100–400 V packs. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Power Transfer to a Load A real supercapacitor has an internal resistance called the **Equivalent Series Resistance (ESR)**, denoted $R_{ESR}$. When delivering current $I$ to an external load $R_L$, the closed-circuit voltage is reduced by the $I R_{ESR}$ drop. Applying KVL around the simple series loop (capacitor → ESR → load): $$ V_{cap} = I\,(R_{ESR} + R_L) $$ The instantaneous power delivered to the load is: $$ P_L = I^{2} R_L = \left( \dfrac{V_{cap}}{R_{ESR} + R_L} \right)^{2} R_L $$ To find the maximum, take $\dfrac{dP_L}{dR_L} = 0$: $$ \dfrac{dP_L}{dR_L} = V_{cap}^{2}\, \dfrac{(R_{ESR} + R_L)^{2} - 2 R_L (R_{ESR} + R_L)}{(R_{ESR} + R_L)^{4}} = 0 $$ The numerator must vanish: $$ (R_{ESR} + R_L)^{2} - 2 R_L (R_{ESR} + R_L) = 0 $$ $$ (R_{ESR} + R_L)\bigl[(R_{ESR} + R_L) - 2 R_L\bigr] = 0 $$ $$ (R_{ESR} + R_L)(R_{ESR} - R_L) = 0 $$ Therefore the **maximum power condition** is: $$ \boxed{R_L = R_{ESR}} $$ Substituting back to find $P_{max}$: $$ P_{max} = \left( \dfrac{V_{cap}}{2 R_{ESR}} \right)^{2} R_{ESR} = \dfrac{V_{cap}^{2}}{4 R_{ESR}} $$ $$ \boxed{P_{max} = \dfrac{V_{cap}^{2}}{4 R_{ESR}}} $$ **Engineering takeaway:** A supercapacitor cannot simultaneously deliver its **full open-circuit voltage** *and* its **maximum power**. To unlock $P_{max}$, the source must be allowed to droop to $V_{cap}/2$. Power-electronics converters in EV applications are designed around this trade-off. --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Energy Stored in a Flywheel Rotor Consider a rigid rotor of mass $m$ with moment of inertia $I$ rotating at angular velocity $\omega$. By definition of rotational kinetic energy: $$ E_k = \int_{0}^{\omega} \tau \, d\theta' $$ where $\tau = I \alpha = I (d\omega'/dt)$ is the applied torque. Using $\theta' = \int \omega' dt$, change of variables gives $d\theta' = \omega'\, dt$. Substituting: $$ E_k = \int_{0}^{\omega} I \dfrac{d\omega'}{dt} \cdot \omega' \, dt = \int_{0}^{\omega} I \omega'\, d\omega' $$ $$ E_k = I \left[ \dfrac{\omega'^{2}}{2} \right]_{0}^{\omega} $$ $$ \boxed{E_k = \dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}} $$ For a **solid cylindrical rotor** of mass $m$, radius $r$, and height $h$, the moment of inertia is: $$ I = \dfrac{1}{2} m r^{2} $$ Substituting: $$ E_k = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} m r^{2} \cdot \omega^{2} = \dfrac{1}{4} m r^{2} \omega^{2} $$ For a **thin rim** (mass concentrated at radius $r$), $I = m r^{2}$, giving: $$ E_k = \dfrac{1}{2} m r^{2} \omega^{2} $$ **Numerical illustration:** A 10 kg thin-rim flywheel at radius 0.2 m, spinning at $\omega = 1{,}000$ rad/s ($\approx 9{,}549$ rpm), stores: $$ E_k = 0.5 \times 10 \times (0.2)^{2} \times (1000)^{2} $$ $$ E_k = 0.5 \times 10 \times 0.04 \times 1{,}000{,}000 $$ $$ E_k = 200{,}000 \text{ J} \approx 55.6 \text{ Wh} $$ This is the stored energy available for regen capture and re-launch assist in a small EV. --- ### 3.4 Derivation: Material-Limited Energy Density of a Flywheel The flywheel's rotor is constrained by the **tensile strength** of its material. Consider a thin rim rotating at $\omega$. A small element on the rim experiences a centrifugal force balanced by hoop stress $\sigma$ in the rim material. For a thin rim, the radial force on an element of arc length $r\,d\theta$ and cross-sectional area $A_c$ is: $$ dF = (dm)\,\omega^{2} r = (\rho A_c r\,d\theta)\,\omega^{2} r = \rho A_c \omega^{2} r^{2} d\theta $$ The two tensile forces at the cut ends of the element pull inward; their net inward radial component balances $dF$: $$ 2 \sigma A_c \sin(d\theta/2) \approx \sigma A_c\, d\theta = dF $$ Equating: $$ \sigma A_c\, d\theta = \rho A_c \omega^{2} r^{2} d\theta $$ Cancelling $A_c\, d\theta$: $$ \sigma = \rho \omega^{2} r^{2} $$ Solving for $\omega^{2}$ and substituting into the energy equation $E/m = \tfrac{1}{2} r^{2} \omega^{2}$ for a thin rim: $$ \omega^{2} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\rho r^{2}} $$ $$ \dfrac{E}{m} = \dfrac{1}{2} r^{2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\rho r^{2}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{2 \rho} $$ $$ \boxed{\left(\dfrac{E}{m}\right)_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_{max}}{2 \rho}} $$ For a solid disk, the geometry factor changes (becomes $\sigma/\rho$), so in general: $$ \dfrac{E}{m} = K \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\rho} $$ where $K$ depends on rotor geometry. **Carbon-fiber composites** with $\sigma/\rho \sim 2$ MJ/kg and steel with $\sigma/\rho \sim 0.2$ MJ/kg illustrate why modern flywheels use composite rims — they store **roughly 10× more energy per kilogram** at safe stress levels. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Attribute | Li-ion Battery | Supercapacitor | Flywheel | |---|---|---|---| | Energy storage mechanism | Chemical (faradaic) | Electrostatic (EDLC) | Kinetic (rotational) | | Specific energy (Wh/kg) | 100 – 250 | 5 – 10 | 5 – 100 | | Specific power (W/kg) | 250 – 1,000 | 5,000 – 15,000 | 5,000 – 10,000 | | Cycle life | 1,000 – 3,000 | 500,000 – 1,000,000+ | 100,000+ | | Charge time | 30 min – 8 h | 1 – 30 s | Seconds – minutes | | Self-discharge | ~2 %/month | ~10 %/day | 1 – 20 %/hour | | Operating temp range | 0 – 45 °C | −40 – +65 °C | −20 – +50 °C | | Efficiency (round trip) | 85 – 95 % | 90 – 98 % | 80 – 95 % | | Aging mechanism | SEI growth, plating | Electrolyte dry-out | Bearing wear, rotor fatigue | | EV role | Energy source | Peak-power buffer | Peak-power buffer / recovery | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: Supercapacitor as a Peak-Power Buffer in a Hybrid EV Powertrain ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Battery Pack
Li-ion Energy Source] -- Avg Load --> C[DC Bus
High Voltage] C --> D[Motor Inverter VSI] D --> E[Traction Motor M] E --> F[Wheels via Transmission] F -- Regen Event --> E E --> D D -- Regen Energy --> C C -- Peak Charge --> B[Supercapacitor Module
Peak Power Buffer] C -- Peak Discharge --> B B -.Bidirectional DC-DC.-> C G[Vehicle Control Unit VCU] -- SOC Management --> B G -- SOC Management --> A ``` **Reading the diagram:** The bidirectional DC-DC converter between the supercapacitor and the DC bus lets the VCU route regen energy into the supercapacitor during braking (when the battery's charge acceptance is too slow) and pull energy *out* during launch (when the battery's discharge rate is too limited). The battery handles the average; the supercapacitor handles the transients. --- ### 4.2 Block Diagram: Flywheel Energy Storage System (FESS) Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB A1[External Power Source
Battery or Grid] -- Charging Mode --> B1[Power Electronics Converter
Rectifier Inverter] B1 -- Variable Frequency --> C1[Integrated Motor Generator IMG] C1 -- Mechanical Shaft --> D1[Rotor Assembly
Vacuum Chamber] D1 -- Spins at high omega --> E1[Magnetic Bearings
Active Suspension] E1 --> D1 D1 -- Back EMF --> C1 C1 --> B1 B1 --> A2[DC Bus or Load] F1[Controller and Sensors
Tachometer Temp Vacuum] -- Feedback --> B1 F1 -- Feedback --> C1 G1[Safety Containment
Buried Underground] --- D1 ``` **Reading the diagram:** The IMG is a single electrical machine operating in motor mode (charge) or generator mode (discharge). Magnetic bearings eliminate contact friction; the vacuum chamber eliminates air drag. The tachometer enables SOC computation using $SoC = (\omega/\omega_{max})^{2}$. For safety, large FESS units are typically buried underground to contain catastrophic rotor failure. --- ### 4.3 Charge/Discharge Characteristic: Functional Flow of a Supercapacitor Cell ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Cell at Rated Voltage V] -- Closed Circuit --> B1[Current i flows] B1 --> C1{Voltage Drop?} C1 -- Yes ESR Drop --> D1[V_terminal = V - i x R_ESR] C1 -- No Open Circuit --> E1[V_terminal = V] D1 --> F1[Power to Load P = V_terminal x i] F1 --> G1{SOC reached?} G1 -- No --> A1 G1 -- Yes --> H1[Disconnect Load] ``` --- ### 4.4 Ragone Plot – Conceptual Positioning ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Energy_High_Power_Low [HIGH ENERGY LOW POWER] A1[Gasoline] A2[Fuel Cell] end subgraph Energy_Power_Mid [MID ENERGY MID POWER] B1[Li-ion Battery] B2[NiMH Battery] end subgraph Energy_Low_Power_High [LOW ENERGY HIGH POWER] C1[Supercapacitor] C2[Flywheel] C3[Conventional Capacitor] end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading this layout:** Reading top-to-bottom = increasing specific power; left-to-right within each band represents increasing specific energy. In a hybrid EV, the system **combines one technology from the middle band (energy source) with one from the lower band (power buffer)** to obtain a composite Ragone point that is superior to either alone. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *What is meant by an Electric Double-Layer Capacitor (EDLC)? Mention any two reasons why EDLCs are preferred over batteries for capturing regenerative-braking energy in EVs.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** An EDLC, or supercapacitor, is an electrochemical capacitor that stores energy in the form of electrical charge at the interface between a porous carbon electrode and an electrolyte (the Helmholtz double layer). The charge separation distance (~0.3–1 nm) is extremely small and the effective surface area is huge (~1000 m²/g), giving capacitances of thousands of farads. Two reasons EDLCs are preferred for regen capture: 1. **High charge acceptance:** Batteries cannot accept a high current pulse during braking without plating lithium or overheating. Supercapacitors can absorb very high currents (specific power >10 kW/kg), capturing nearly all of the braking energy. 2. **Extremely long cycle life (>500,000 cycles):** Regen-braking events occur every few seconds in city driving. A battery would age in months; a supercapacitor can sustain this duty for the vehicle's entire life. *(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the two reasons – KTU valuation key pattern.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *List the main components of a Flywheel Energy Storage System (FESS) and state the function of each.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The main components of a FESS are: 1. **Rotor (flywheel mass):** Stores the rotational kinetic energy. Made of steel for low-speed units or carbon-fiber composites for high-speed units. 2. **Integrated Motor-Generator (IMG):** Operates as a motor to spin up the rotor (charge) and as a generator to extract energy (discharge). 3. **Bearings (typically magnetic / active):** Support the rotor without physical contact, eliminating mechanical friction. 4. **Vacuum enclosure:** Reduces aerodynamic drag on the spinning rotor. 5. **Power electronics converter:** Conditions the electrical output (regulates voltage, manages bidirectional flow). 6. **Controller & safety housing:** Monitors rotor speed and contains the rotor in case of failure. *(0.5 mark per component – KTU awards partial marks for each correctly listed component.)* --- ### Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 ESE Module Pattern:** Internal choice between two questions. Choose **ONE** and answer in full. Marks are split across sub-parts (typically 7 + 7). --- #### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]` **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of an Electric Double-Layer Capacitor (EDLC). Compare its Ragone-plot position with that of a Li-ion battery. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A supercapacitor module has $C = 3000$ F and an equivalent series resistance $R_{ESR} = 0.5$ m$\Omega$, rated at $V = 2.7$ V per cell. Three cells are connected in **series**. Compute: 1. Equivalent capacitance of the series stack. 2. Total energy stored at rated voltage. 3. Maximum power deliverable under matched-load condition. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Construction and Working of EDLC (7 marks)** **Construction (3 marks):** An EDLC cell consists of: - Two **porous carbon electrodes** (activated carbon, carbon aerogel, or carbon nanotubes) with extremely high internal surface area. - An **electrolyte** (aqueous KOH or organic, e.g., tetraethylammonium tetrafluoroborate in acetonitrile). - A **separator** (porous polymer membrane) that prevents direct contact between electrodes while allowing ionic conduction. - Two **current collectors** (aluminum foil) to connect to external circuitry. **Working (3 marks):** When a DC voltage is applied, ions in the electrolyte migrate and form a charged layer (Helmholtz layer) on the porous surface of each electrode. Two such layers in series form the "double layer" — the actual capacitance. Energy is stored in the resulting electric field. On discharge, ions diffuse back into the bulk electrolyte, releasing current. **Ragone comparison (1 mark):** On a log–log Ragone plot, the Li-ion battery lies at ~150 Wh/kg, ~500 W/kg (energy-oriented), while the EDLC lies at ~7 Wh/kg, ~10,000 W/kg (power-oriented). They occupy almost orthogonal regions; this is why they are combined in hybrid energy storage systems. **(b) Numerical (7 marks)** **Given:** $C = 3000$ F per cell, $R_{ESR} = 0.5$ m$\Omega$ per cell, $V = 2.7$ V per cell, 3 cells in series. **Step 1 – Equivalent capacitance of series stack (2 marks):** For $n$ identical capacitors in series: $$ C_{eq} = \dfrac{C}{n} = \dfrac{3000}{3} $$ $$ \boxed{C_{eq} = 1000 \text{ F}} $$ **Step 2 – Total energy stored (3 marks):** For a series stack, the rated voltage of the pack is the sum of the cell voltages: $$ V_{pack} = 3 \times 2.7 = 8.1 \text{ V} $$ Stored energy in the stack: $$ E_{pack} = \dfrac{1}{2} C_{eq} V_{pack}^{2} $$ $$ E_{pack} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 1000 \times (8.1)^{2} $$ $$ E_{pack} = 500 \times 65.61 $$ $$ \boxed{E_{pack} = 32{,}805 \text{ J} \approx 9.11 \text{ Wh}} $$ **Step 3 – Maximum power under matched load (2 marks):** Equivalent series resistance of the stack: $$ R_{ESR,pack} = n \cdot R_{ESR} = 3 \times 0.0005 = 0.0015 \text{ }\Omega $$ Matched-load maximum power: $$ P_{max} = \dfrac{V_{pack}^{2}}{4 R_{ESR,pack}} $$ $$ P_{max} = \dfrac{(8.1)^{2}}{4 \times 0.0015} = \dfrac{65.61}{0.006} $$ $$ \boxed{P_{max} \approx 10{,}935 \text{ W} \approx 10.9 \text{ kW}} $$ --- #### **Question B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]` **(a)** Derive the expression for the energy stored in a rotating flywheel of mass $m$, radius $r$, spinning at angular velocity $\omega$. Clearly state the assumption used. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A composite-rim flywheel for a hybrid bus has the following data: - Rotor type: thin rim, mass $m = 50$ kg, radius $r = 0.3$ m - Maximum safe speed: $\omega_{max} = 1{,}200$ rad/s - Material density: $\rho = 1{,}600$ kg/m³ - Material tensile strength: $\sigma_{max} = 2{,}000$ MPa Calculate: 1. Maximum stored kinetic energy. 2. Specific energy (Wh/kg). 3. Actual hoop stress at $\omega_{max}$ and verify it is below $\sigma_{max}$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation (7 marks)** Consider a rigid rotor of moment of inertia $I$ rotating with angular velocity $\omega$. The work done by the driving torque $\tau$ as the rotor accelerates from rest to $\omega$ equals the stored kinetic energy: $$ E_k = \int_{0}^{\omega} \tau\, d\theta' \quad \text{(work-energy theorem, 2 marks)} $$ For a rigid body, the torque required to produce angular acceleration $\alpha$ is $\tau = I \alpha = I (d\omega'/dt)$. Using $d\theta' = \omega'\, dt$, we change variables: $$ E_k = \int_{0}^{\omega} I \dfrac{d\omega'}{dt} \cdot \omega'\, dt $$ $$ E_k = \int_{0}^{\omega} I \omega'\, d\omega' $$ $$ E_k = I \left[ \dfrac{\omega'^{2}}{2} \right]_{0}^{\omega} $$ $$ \boxed{E_k = \dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}} \quad \text{(3 marks)} $$ **Assumption (2 marks):** The rotor is a rigid body (no deformation at the operating speed) and the moment of inertia $I$ is constant during charging/discharging. For a thin rim, $I = m r^{2}$, giving $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m r^{2} \omega^{2}$. **(b) Numerical (7 marks)** **Given:** $m = 50$ kg, $r = 0.3$ m, $\omega_{max} = 1{,}200$ rad/s, $\rho = 1{,}600$ kg/m³, $\sigma_{max} = 2{,}000$ MPa. **Step 1 – Maximum stored kinetic energy (3 marks):** For a thin rim, $I = m r^{2}$: $$ I = 50 \times (0.3)^{2} = 50 \times 0.09 = 4.5 \text{ kg·m}^{2} $$ $$ E_k = \dfrac{1}{2} I \omega_{max}^{2} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 4.5 \times (1200)^{2} $$ $$ E_k = 2.25 \times 1{,}440{,}000 $$ $$ \boxed{E_k = 3{,}240{,}000 \text{ J} = 3.24 \text{ MJ}} $$ **Step 2 – Specific energy (2 marks):** $$ E_{m} = \dfrac{E_k}{m} = \dfrac{3{,}240{,}000}{50} = 64{,}800 \text{ J/kg} $$ Convert to Wh/kg: divide by 3600: $$ \boxed{E_{m} = 18 \text{ Wh/kg}} $$ **Step 3 – Actual hoop stress and verification (2 marks):** $$ \sigma = \rho\, \omega_{max}^{2} r^{2} $$ $$ \sigma = 1600 \times (1200)^{2} \times (0.3)^{2} $$ $$ \sigma = 1600 \times 1{,}440{,}000 \times 0.09 $$ $$ \sigma = 207{,}360{,}000 \text{ Pa} $$ $$ \sigma \approx 207.4 \text{ MPa} $$ Since $\sigma \approx 207.4$ MPa $< \sigma_{max} = 2{,}000$ MPa, the rotor is operating safely within its material limit. The design has a comfortable safety factor of approximately **9.6×**. --- ### > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls – Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Confusing cell voltage vs. pack voltage:** When supercapacitors or flywheel generators are stacked in series, students often forget to multiply voltage (or sum ESR). KTU examiners routinely deduct 1–2 marks here. > 2. **Mixing Wh and J:** Numerical answers must carry correct units. $E_k$ from $\tfrac{1}{2} I\omega^{2}$ is in **joules**; specific energy should be converted to **Wh/kg** by dividing by 3600. Examiners deduct ½ mark for unit errors. > 3. **Forgetting the matched-load condition:** For $P_{max}$, students frequently write $P = V^{2}/R$ instead of $V^{2}/(4R)$. The "factor of 4" is a common KTU trick question. > 4. **Skipping the assumption in derivations:** Part-(a) long-answer questions demand an explicit assumption (e.g., rigid rotor, constant $I$). Omitting it costs 1–2 marks even if the algebra is correct. > 5. **Confusing EDLC and pseudocapacitor:** Examiners may give ½ mark extra if the student correctly distinguishes them. Remember: EDLC = pure electrostatic; pseudocapacitor = faradaic + electrostatic (hybrid). > 6. **Hoop stress numerical slip:** The hoop-stress formula $\sigma = \rho \omega^{2} r^{2}$ is dimensionally correct, but students sometimes write $\sigma = \rho \omega r$ or $\sigma = \rho \omega^{2} r$. Wrong exponents = full mark loss. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist** - **Supercapacitor (EDLC)** stores energy **electrostatically** in the Helmholtz double layer; capacitance from $C = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A / d$ with $d \sim$ nm. - **Energy:** $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$. - **Maximum power:** $P_{max} = V^{2}/(4 R_{ESR})$ at matched load $R_L = R_{ESR}$. - **Flywheel energy:** $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$. - **Thin rim moment of inertia:** $I = m r^{2}$; solid disk: $I = \tfrac{1}{2} m r^{2}$. - **Hoop stress limit:** $\sigma = \rho \omega^{2} r^{2}$; safe max energy density $E/m = \sigma_{max}/(2\rho)$. - **State-of-charge (flywheel):** $SoC = (\omega/\omega_{max})^{2}$. - **Supercapacitor position on Ragone plot:** low energy, very high power. - **Flywheel position on Ragone plot:** low-to-moderate energy, high power. - **EV role:** both act as **peak-power buffers** for regen capture and launch assist; battery handles average load. - **Series connection effects:** capacitance divides by $n$; voltage multiplies by $n$; ESR multiplies by $n$. - **Composite vs. steel rotors:** composite gives ~10× higher specific energy due to higher $\sigma/\rho$. - **Cycle life:** supercapacitors >500,000; flywheels >100,000; both far exceed Li-ion (~1,000–3,000). - **Self-discharge:** supercapacitors lose ~10 %/day; flywheels lose 1–20 %/hour — relevant for long-parked EVs. - **Safety note:** large flywheels are buried to contain catastrophic rotor burst failures.

topics.title: Fuel Cells and Applications: Basic Principle of Hydrogen Fuel Cell
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT631 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fuel Cells and Applications: Basic Principle of Hydrogen Fuel Cell

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydrogen Fuel Cell (HFC):** An electrochemical energy conversion device that converts the chemical energy of a fuel (hydrogen) and an oxidant (oxygen from air) directly into electrical energy, with water and heat as the only by-products, **as long as the reactants are continuously supplied**.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, a fuel cell is classified as a **galvanic cell** (like a battery) that is **open-system** (fuel is fed from outside, not stored internally). The specific device using pure **H₂** as the fuel is termed a **Hydrogen Fuel Cell**, and the most commercially relevant variant for automotive applications is the **Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC)**, which operates at low temperatures (60–100 °C) and is the centerpiece of modern Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEVs) such as the Toyota Mirai and Hyundai Nexo.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ever-Refillable Battery"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for the Student:** Imagine a normal AA battery. It produces electricity until its internal chemicals run out, after which you throw it away. Now imagine that instead of throwing it away, you simply **refill the empty battery with hydrogen gas from a nozzle**, and it works again instantly, producing only water vapor from the exhaust. That is essentially what a hydrogen fuel cell does — it is a **battery that never dies, as long as hydrogen is fed to it**.

| Feature | Conventional Battery | Hydrogen Fuel Cell |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Energy Storage | Internal (electrodes) | External (hydrogen tank) |
| Recharging | Plug-in (slow, hours) | Refuel in 3–5 minutes |
| By-products | Often toxic | Pure **H₂O** and heat |
| Energy Density | Lower (Li-ion) | Very high (H₂ is light) |
| Operating Temp | Room temp | 60–100 °C (PEMFC) |

## 1.3 Fundamental Physical Constants Used

The following constants are indispensable for any fuel cell numerical:

- **Faraday's Constant (F) = 96,485 C/mol**
- **Universal Gas Constant (R) = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- **Standard Cell Potential (E°) = 1.229 V** at 25 °C and 1 atm
- **Standard Gibbs Free Energy (ΔG°) = −237.13 kJ/mol**
- **Standard Enthalpy of Formation of H₂O (ΔH°) = −285.83 kJ/mol** (Higher Heating Value, HHV)
- **Lower Heating Value (LHV) of H₂ = −241.82 kJ/mol**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this topic in your syllabus?** Electric and Hybrid Vehicles (PEMUT631) Module 2 deals with **Alternative Energy Sources for Traction**. Since batteries alone limit the range and charging time of pure EVs, hydrogen fuel cells provide the *third* zero-emission propulsion pathway — bridging the gap between Battery EVs and Internal Combustion Engine vehicles. Toyota, Hyundai, Honda, Mercedes-Benz, and BMW all have production FCEVs on the road today.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — A Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

A hydrogen fuel cell consists of three principal layers sandwiched between two bipolar plates:

1. **Anode (Negative Electrode):** Where hydrogen is fed.
2. **Electrolyte (Proton Exchange Membrane — Nafion™):** Allows only H⁺ ions to pass; blocks electrons and gases.
3. **Cathode (Positive Electrode):** Where oxygen (from air) is fed.

The operational sequence is:

- **Step 1 — Fuel Distribution:** Pressurized H₂ gas is supplied to the anode flow channels machined into the bipolar plate.
- **Step 2 — Oxidation at Anode (Hydrogen Oxidation Reaction, HOR):** A platinum catalyst splits the H₂ molecule into two protons and two electrons.
- **Step 3 — Ion Conduction:** The protons migrate through the hydrated proton-exchange membrane toward the cathode.
- **Step 4 — Electron Conduction:** The electrons, being blocked by the membrane, are forced through the **external circuit** — this is the useful **direct current (DC)** electricity that drives the traction motor of the vehicle.
- **Step 5 — Reduction at Cathode (Oxygen Reduction Reaction, ORR):** At the cathode catalyst, the protons, electrons, and oxygen combine to form water and release heat.
- **Step 6 — Product Removal:** The product water is purged from the cathode, and exhaust heat is managed by the vehicle's thermal management system.

## 2.2 The Three Governing Half-Cell & Net Reactions

The electrochemistry of the cell is captured by three equations that **must be memorized verbatim** for the KTU board exam:

**Anode (Oxidation — electrons released):**

$$H_2 \longrightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-$$

**Cathode (Reduction — electrons consumed):**

$$\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \longrightarrow H_2O$$

**Overall Cell Reaction (Net):**

$$H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \longrightarrow H_2O \quad (+ \text{electrical work} + \text{heat})$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the **complete arsenal of equations** required for solving any 3-mark or 14-mark fuel cell problem in your ESE. Reproduce and memorize these.

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning of Symbols | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $E^\circ = \dfrac{-\Delta G^\circ}{nF}$ | Standard EMF from Gibbs energy | E° in **V**, ΔG° in **J/mol**, n = 2 (electrons per H₂), F = 96,485 C/mol |
| 2 | $E = E^\circ + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{p_{H_2} \cdot p_{O_2}^{\,1/2}}{p_{H_2 O}}\right)$ | **Nernst Equation** for the fuel cell | E = actual operating EMF; pᵢ = partial pressure (atm) |
| 3 | $E_{cell} = E^\circ - \dfrac{RT}{2F}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{p_{H_2 O}}{p_{H_2}\sqrt{p_{O_2}}}\right)$ | Alternative form of Nernst | Often used in exam problems |
| 4 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{\Delta H^\circ}$ | Theoretical (thermodynamic) efficiency | Ratio, dimensionless, 0 < η < 1 |
| 5 | $\eta_{th,\,LHV} = \dfrac{237.13}{241.82} = 0.9805$ | Efficiency w.r.t. LHV of H₂ | 98.05 % at standard conditions |
| 6 | $\eta_{th,\,HHV} = \dfrac{237.13}{285.83} = 0.8296$ | Efficiency w.r.t. HHV of H₂ | 82.96 % at standard conditions |
| 7 | $V_{cell,\,ideal} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{nF}$ | Ideal reversible cell voltage | ≈ **1.229 V** at 25 °C |
| 8 | $V_{cell} = E^\circ - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohm} - \eta_{conc}$ | Actual cell voltage after losses | Each η is a voltage **loss** term |
| 9 | $P_{stack} = n_{cells} \cdot V_{cell} \cdot I$ | Stack power output | n_cells = number of cells in stack; I = current in **A** |
| 10 | $\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dfrac{I \cdot M_{H_2}}{n \cdot F}$ | Mass flow rate of H₂ required | M_H₂ = 2.016 g/mol, n = 2 |
| 11 | $w_{elec,\,max} = -\Delta G$ | Maximum reversible electrical work | Per mole of H₂ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Reference — KTU Examiner's hint:** For PEMFC problems, the board almost always uses **T = 298.15 K** (25 °C) and the value **RT/F = 0.02569 V**. Memorize $\dfrac{RT}{F} = 0.02569$ V at 25 °C — it speeds up derivations dramatically.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Traction:** PEMFC stacks rated **50–120 kW** drive passenger FCEVs, offering ranges of 500–700 km per 5-minute refuel.
- **Stationary Power:** Used as backup units for telecom towers and data centers — silent, reliable, and emission-free at point of use.
- **Space Applications:** NASA has used alkaline and PEM-type fuel cells in Apollo, Space Shuttle, and Orion programs to provide both electricity and drinking water.
- **Portable Power:** Military field equipment, drones, and laptop chargers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Standard EMF from Gibbs Free Energy

> **Goal:** Derive $E^\circ = \dfrac{-\Delta G^\circ}{nF}$ from first principles.

**Starting Principle:** The maximum reversible electrical work obtainable from a constant-temperature, constant-pressure electrochemical cell is given by the decrease in Gibbs free energy of the system.

$$w_{elec,\,max} = -\Delta G$$

**Step 1 — Express electrical work in terms of charge and voltage:** The electrical work done by the cell when $n$ moles of electrons circulate through the external circuit under a potential difference $E$ is:

$$w_{elec} = Q \cdot E = (n \cdot F) \cdot E$$

**Step 2 — Equate the two expressions for maximum work:** Since both expressions represent the same maximum electrical work, we set them equal to each other:

$$n \cdot F \cdot E^\circ = -\Delta G^\circ$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the standard EMF:** Rearranging the equation to isolate $E^\circ$ on the left-hand side:

$$E^\circ = \frac{-\Delta G^\circ}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Substitution for the H₂/O₂ Cell:** Substituting $\Delta G^\circ = -237{,}130$ J/mol, $n = 2$ electrons per mole of H₂, and $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol:

$$E^\circ = \frac{-(-237{,}130)}{2 \times 96{,}485}$$

$$E^\circ = \frac{237{,}130}{192{,}970}$$

$$\boxed{E^\circ = 1.229 \text{ V at 25 °C, 1 atm}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Nernst Equation for the Hydrogen Fuel Cell

> **Goal:** Derive the operating cell voltage as a function of reactant partial pressures and temperature.

**Starting Point — General Nernst Equation:**

$$E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

where $Q$ is the reaction quotient. For the cell reaction $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$, the reaction quotient (products over reactants, each raised to stoichiometric power) is:

$$Q = \frac{a_{H_2 O}}{a_{H_2} \cdot a_{O_2}^{\,1/2}}$$

**Step 1 — Replace activities with partial pressures (assuming ideal gas behavior):** For gaseous species, activity $a_i \approx p_i$ (in atm). For liquid water product, $a_{H_2 O} \approx 1$ (pure liquid reference state).

$$Q = \frac{1}{p_{H_2} \cdot p_{O_2}^{\,1/2}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the Nernst equation:**

$$E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \frac{1}{p_{H_2} \cdot p_{O_2}^{\,1/2}} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Use the logarithm identity $\ln(1/x) = -\ln(x)$:** The two negatives cancel, yielding the conventional form:

$$E = E^\circ + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( p_{H_2} \cdot p_{O_2}^{\,1/2} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $n = 2$ for the H₂/O₂ couple:**

$$\boxed{E = E^\circ + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln \left( p_{H_2} \cdot p_{O_2}^{\,1/2} \right)}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example (a common KTU problem):** A fuel cell operates at 80 °C (353 K) with $p_{H_2} = 3$ atm and $p_{O_2} = 1$ atm. Compute the open-circuit voltage.

$$E = 1.229 + \frac{8.314 \times 353}{2 \times 96{,}485} \ln \left( 3 \times 1^{1/2} \right)$$

$$E = 1.229 + \frac{2934.84}{192{,}970} \ln(3)$$

$$E = 1.229 + (0.01521)(1.0986)$$

$$E = 1.229 + 0.0167$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 1.2457 \text{ V}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why did E increase?** The Nernst term is **positive** when reactant partial pressures exceed 1 atm. Pressurizing the reactants above atmospheric pressure is one of the engineering strategies used in automotive fuel cell stacks to extract slightly more voltage from each cell.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Thermodynamic (Theoretical) Efficiency

> **Goal:** Show why a fuel cell is theoretically more efficient than a heat engine.

**Step 1 — Carnot (Heat Engine) Reference:** The maximum efficiency of any heat engine operating between $T_{hot}$ and $T_{cold}$ is:

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_{cold}}{T_{hot}}$$

For a hydrogen flame at ~2400 K exhausting to 300 K surroundings, $\eta_{Carnot,\,ICE} \approx 0.875$ *only* if it were a perfect heat engine. In practice, gasoline engines achieve 25–35 %.

**Step 2 — Fuel Cell Efficiency Definition:** The fraction of the fuel's chemical energy (heating value) that is converted to useful electrical work:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{w_{elec}}{\Delta H} = \frac{-\Delta G}{-\Delta H} = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H}$$

**Step 3 — Compute with HHV (water produced as liquid):**

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{237.13 \text{ kJ/mol}}{285.83 \text{ kJ/mol}} = 0.8296 = 82.96\,\%$$

**Step 4 — Compute with LHV (water leaves as vapor at 25 °C):**

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{237.13 \text{ kJ/mol}}{241.82 \text{ kJ/mol}} = 0.9805 = 98.05\,\%$$

**Step 5 — Why this matters:** A fuel cell **directly** converts chemical → electrical energy, bypassing the heat → mechanical → electrical route of an ICE. This is why FCEVs can achieve real-world drivetrain efficiencies of 50–60 %, compared to 25–35 % for gasoline hybrids.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Actual Cell Voltage Including Polarization Losses

The real-world terminal voltage of a fuel cell is always **less than** 1.229 V due to three irreversible losses:

$$V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohm} - \eta_{conc}$$

where:

- **$\eta_{act}$ — Activation Loss:** Energy barrier of the ORR/HOR reactions, dominant at low current densities. Modeled by the **Butler-Volmer equation** (linearized at low current gives the **Tafel equation**: $\eta_{act} = a + b \log i$).
- **$\eta_{ohm}$ — Ohmic Loss:** Resistive drop across the membrane, electrodes, and interconnects. Modeled as $\eta_{ohm} = I \cdot R_{internal}$ (linear with current).
- **$\eta_{conc}$ — Concentration Loss:** Mass-transport limitation when reactants cannot reach catalyst sites fast enough at high current densities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key result for the examiner:** A single PEMFC at practical operating current density (~1 A/cm²) delivers about **0.6–0.7 V**, NOT 1.229 V. The drop of ~0.5 V is entirely due to the three losses above. A vehicle stack of 300–400 such cells in series produces 250–450 V DC for the traction inverter.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Single PEM Hydrogen Fuel Cell Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the *physical layer-by-layer construction* of a single hydrogen fuel cell, the interactions between the layers, and the flow paths of the reactants, products, ions, and electrons.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ANODE["ANODE BIPOLAR PLATE (H2 Inlet)"]
        A1[H2 Gas Channel]
    end

    subgraph ACL["ANODE CATALYST LAYER (Pt)"]
        A2[HOR: H2 splits into 2H plus 2e]
    end

    subgraph MEM["PROTON EXCHANGE MEMBRANE (Nafion)"]
        A3[H plus ions migrate L to R]
    end

    subgraph CCL["CATHODE CATALYST LAYER (Pt)"]
        A4[ORR: O2 plus 2H plus 2e gives H2O]
    end

    subgraph CATH["CATHODE BIPOLAR PLATE (Air Inlet)"]
        A5[O2 from Air plus H2O Exhaust]
    end

    EXT["EXTERNAL CIRCUIT (Vehicle Traction Load)"]

    A1 --> A2
    A2 -- 2H plus ions --> A3
    A3 -- 2H plus --> A4
    A2 -- 2e blocked by membrane --> EXT
    A4 -- electrons consumed --> EXT
    A4 -- H2O product --> A5
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Fuel Cell Vehicle Powertrain

This higher-level diagram shows how the fuel cell stack interfaces with the rest of the electric/hybrid vehicle drive system. It is the **type of diagram examiners love** for 7-mark questions on "Explain the fuel cell vehicle architecture."

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TANK["Hydrogen Storage Subsystem"]
        T1[High Pressure H2 Tank 350 or 700 bar]
    end

    subgraph FC["Fuel Cell Stack Module"]
        F1[Compressor and Humidifier]
        F2[PEMFC Stack 300 to 400 cells in series]
        F3[Recirculation Blower]
        F4[Thermal Management Radiator]
    end

    subgraph ELEC["Power Electronics Subsystem"]
        E1[DC DC Boost Converter]
        E2[High Voltage Traction Inverter]
        E3[Vehicle Control Unit VCU]
    end

    subgraph DRV["Drivetrain Subsystem"]
        D1[3 Phase AC Traction Motor]
        D2[Single Speed Transmission]
        D3[Wheels]
    end

    subgraph AUX["Auxiliary Subsystem"]
        X1[12V Auxiliary Battery]
        X2[Brake Vacuum Pump and HVAC]
    end

    T1 --> F1
    F1 --> F2
    F2 --> F3
    F2 --> F4
    F2 -- 250 to 450 V DC --> E1
    E1 -- 400 to 650 V DC --> E2
    E3 -- control signals --> E2
    E1 -- 12 to 14 V DC --> X1
    X1 --> X2
    E2 -- 3 phase AC --> D1
    D1 --> D2 --> D3
```

## 4.3 Polarization Curve — Voltage vs Current Density (Descriptive Schematic)

The classic "polarization curve" plots cell voltage $V$ on the Y-axis against current density $j$ (A/cm²) on the X-axis. Three distinct regions appear:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P1["Region 1: Activation Losses (0 to 0.1 A per cm2)"]
        R1[Steep initial drop due to sluggish ORR kinetics]
    end
    subgraph P2["Region 2: Ohmic Losses (0.1 to 1.0 A per cm2)"]
        R2[Linear decline dominated by membrane resistance]
    end
    subgraph P3["Region 3: Concentration Losses (above 1.0 A per cm2)"]
        R3[Sharp drop as mass transport limits reactant supply]
    end
    P1 --> P2 --> P3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **What the examiner wants you to label on this curve:** Mark the open-circuit voltage at $E^\circ = 1.229$ V, the activation-controlled kink, the linear ohmic region, and the limiting current density $j_L$ where the voltage collapses to zero. This single graph often earns the 7 marks in a "Discuss polarization losses" question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a hydrogen fuel cell. Write the anode and cathode half-reactions and the overall cell reaction.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A hydrogen fuel cell is an **electrochemical device** that continuously converts the chemical energy of hydrogen and oxygen directly into electrical energy, producing water and heat as by-products, as long as reactants are supplied.

**Anode (oxidation):**

$$H_2 \longrightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^- \quad (E_a^\circ = 0.00 \text{ V vs SHE})$$

**Cathode (reduction):**

$$\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \longrightarrow H_2O \quad (E_c^\circ = 1.229 \text{ V vs SHE})$$

**Overall:**

$$H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \longrightarrow H_2O \quad (E_{cell}^\circ = 1.229 \text{ V})$$

*(Examiner's note: A clean diagram showing the membrane-electrode-assembly earns the third mark.)*

---

### **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Compare a hydrogen fuel cell and a lithium-ion battery as energy sources for electric vehicles (any three points).**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per valid point):**

| Parameter | Hydrogen Fuel Cell | Li-ion Battery |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Energy Storage | External (H₂ tank) | Internal (electrodes) |
| Refuel/Recharge Time | 3–5 min (like petrol) | 30 min – 8 hr |
| Specific Energy | ~120–140 Wh/kg (system) | 150–250 Wh/kg (cell) |
| Range | 500–700 km | 200–500 km |
| Tail-pipe Emissions | Only water | Zero |
| Cold-Start Below 0 °C | Difficult (water freezing) | Excellent |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do **not** write that a fuel cell "stores electricity." It is an **energy conversion device**, not a storage device. The hydrogen tank stores the *chemical* energy. Losing 1 mark on this distinction is the most common error in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper PEMUT631]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the construction and working principle of a Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM) hydrogen fuel cell. Derive the Nernst equation for the cell.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A PEM fuel cell operates at 65 °C with $p_{H_2} = 2.5$ atm and $p_{O_2} = 0.21$ atm (oxygen mole fraction in air). Compute the open-circuit voltage of the cell.

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — Construction, Working, and Nernst Derivation [7 marks]**

**Construction (2 marks):** A single PEM fuel cell is a Membrane Electrode Assembly (MEA) sandwiched between two bipolar plates. The MEA consists of:

- **Anode catalyst layer** (Pt nanoparticles on carbon)
- **Proton exchange membrane** (Nafion-117, ~50 µm thick, perfluorosulfonic acid)
- **Cathode catalyst layer** (Pt nanoparticles on carbon)

**Working (2 marks):** Hydrogen enters the anode flow field, dissociates at the Pt catalyst into 2H⁺ + 2e⁻. Protons migrate through the hydrated membrane; electrons take the external circuit (producing current). At the cathode, O₂, H⁺, and e⁻ combine to form H₂O, which is expelled.

**Nernst Equation Derivation (3 marks):** As derived in Section 3.2 above:

$$E = E^\circ + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln \left( p_{H_2} \cdot p_{O_2}^{\,1/2} \right)$$

> *Steps: Start from $E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$, identify $Q = 1/(p_{H_2} p_{O_2}^{1/2})$ for the H₂/O₂ cell, apply $\ln(1/x) = -\ln x$, simplify.*

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Problem [7 marks]**

**Given:**

- Operating temperature: $T = 65\,^\circ C = 338.15$ K
- $p_{H_2} = 2.5$ atm
- $p_{O_2} = 0.21$ atm
- $E^\circ = 1.229$ V

**Step 1 — Compute the Nernst temperature factor:**

$$\frac{RT}{2F} = \frac{8.314 \times 338.15}{2 \times 96{,}485} = \frac{2811.34}{192{,}970} = 0.01457 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the logarithmic term:**

$$p_{H_2} \cdot p_{O_2}^{\,1/2} = 2.5 \times (0.21)^{0.5} = 2.5 \times 0.4583 = 1.1457$$

$$\ln(1.1457) = 0.1361$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the Nernst equation:**

$$E = 1.229 + (0.01457)(0.1361)$$

$$E = 1.229 + 0.00198$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 1.231 \text{ V}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** The voltage is only marginally above 1.229 V because the air side is at low partial pressure (oxygen is only 21 % of air). Automotive fuel cell systems use **air compressors** to raise the air pressure to 2–3 atm, boosting the Nernst term substantially.

---

### **Question B.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the three major polarization losses in a fuel cell. Sketch and label the polarization curve showing these regions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A fuel cell stack has 350 cells in series, each delivering a mean operating voltage of 0.65 V at a current of 200 A. Calculate (i) the stack power output, (ii) the mass flow rate of hydrogen required, and (iii) the overall stack efficiency if the HHV of hydrogen is used.

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — Polarization Losses [7 marks]**

The actual terminal voltage of a fuel cell is less than the reversible voltage $E^\circ = 1.229$ V due to **three irreversible losses** (2 marks each):

1. **Activation Polarization ($\eta_{act}$):** Caused by the **sluggish kinetics of the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR)** at the cathode. Even with platinum catalyst, the ORR has a high activation energy. Modeled by the **Tafel equation** $\eta_{act} = a + b \log j$, where $a$ and $b$ are Tafel constants and $j$ is current density. **Dominant at low current densities** (0 to 0.1 A/cm²). (2 marks)

2. **Ohmic Polarization ($\eta_{ohm}$):** Caused by **ionic resistance of the membrane** and **electronic resistance of electrodes, bipolar plates, and contact interfaces**. Modeled by **Ohm's law** $\eta_{ohm} = I \cdot R_{internal}$, where $R_{internal}$ is the total cell area-specific resistance (ASR). Produces a **linear** voltage drop with current. **Dominant in the mid-range** (0.1 to 1.0 A/cm²). (2 marks)

3. **Concentration Polarization ($\eta_{conc}$):** Caused by **mass-transport limitations** when reactant gases cannot diffuse to the catalyst sites fast enough at high current densities. Modeled as $\eta_{conc} = -\frac{RT}{nF}\ln\left(1 - \frac{j}{j_L}\right)$, where $j_L$ is the limiting current density. Produces a **sharp voltage drop** at high currents. (2 marks)

**Net equation:** $V_{cell} = E_{Nernst} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohm} - \eta_{conc}$

(Polarization curve sketch — refer to Section 4.3 for the labelled Mermaid diagram. 1 mark for diagram)

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Problem [7 marks]**

**Given:** $n_{cells} = 350$, $V_{cell} = 0.65$ V, $I = 200$ A.

**(i) Stack Power Output (2 marks):**

$$P = n_{cells} \times V_{cell} \times I = 350 \times 0.65 \times 200$$

$$P = 45{,}500 \text{ W} = 45.5 \text{ kW}$$

**(ii) Hydrogen Mass Flow Rate (3 marks):**

Using Faraday's law: $\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dfrac{I \cdot M_{H_2}}{n \cdot F}$

With $M_{H_2} = 2.016 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/mol, $n = 2$, $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol:

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = \frac{200 \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 96{,}485} = \frac{0.4032}{192{,}970} = 2.089 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/s}$$

Per hour: $\dot{m}_{H_2} = 2.089 \times 10^{-6} \times 3600 = 0.00752$ kg/h = **7.52 g/h**

**(iii) Overall Stack Efficiency (2 marks):**

Rate of hydrogen energy input (HHV basis):

$$\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_{H_2} \times \Delta H_{HHV} = (2.089 \times 10^{-6}) \times (285.83 \times 10^3) = 0.5971 \text{ W}$$

Stack efficiency:

$$\eta_{stack} = \frac{P_{stack}}{\dot{Q}_{in}} = \frac{45{,}500}{0.5971} \times 10^{-3}$$

Wait — let us check the calculation more carefully. The denominator must equal the energy input rate:

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} \times \Delta H_{HHV} = (2.089 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/s}) \times (285{,}830 \text{ J/g} \times 1000 \text{ g/kg}/\text{kg-conversion})$$

Using consistent units: $\Delta H_{HHV} = 285{,}830$ J/g × 1000 g/kg = 285.83 × 10⁶ J/kg.

$$\dot{Q}_{in} = (2.089 \times 10^{-6}) \times (285.83 \times 10^6) = 597.1 \text{ W}$$

Stack efficiency:

$$\eta_{stack} = \frac{45{,}500}{597.1} \times 100\,\% \quad \text{... recheck}$$

Recheck using HHV per mole: For 200 A, moles of H₂ per second = $I/(nF) = 200/(2 \times 96{,}485) = 1.0364 \times 10^{-3}$ mol/s. Energy input rate = $1.0364 \times 10^{-3} \times 285{,}830 = 296.2$ kW. Then:

$$\eta_{stack} = \frac{45.5 \text{ kW}}{296.2 \text{ kW}} = 0.1536 = 15.4\,\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{stack} \approx 15.4\,\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Frequent Mistakes:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in (b)(ii):** Some students forget to convert grams → kilograms, giving an answer off by a factor of 1000. Always write units next to every number.
> 2. **Using $n=1$ instead of $n=2$ in Faraday's law:** Each H₂ molecule releases **2** electrons. Using $n=1$ halves your answer and loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing LHV and HHV:** For PEMFC operating below 100 °C, water leaves as **vapor**, so LHV is more appropriate, but KTU board problems often explicitly state which to use. Always re-read the question.
> 4. **In (a), writing $\eta_{act} = V \cdot I$ or some wrong formula:** The activation loss is a **voltage** drop, not a power loss. Be precise with the symbol $\eta$ (eta) vs. $\eta$ for efficiency.
> 5. **Skipping the Nernst derivation in Question A(a):** A 3-mark sub-part demands the **derivation steps**, not just the final formula. Examiners will deduct 1–2 marks if you only state the Nernst equation without showing the reaction quotient manipulation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this checklist — it is your last-minute revision sheet for the KTU board exam.**

- ☐ A **hydrogen fuel cell** is an **open** galvanic cell that converts H₂ + O₂ → H₂O + electricity + heat continuously.
- ☐ The three **must-memorize** reactions:
  - Anode: $H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-$
  - Cathode: $\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O$
  - Overall: $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$
- ☐ Standard reversible voltage: $E^\circ = 1.229$ V at 25 °C, 1 atm.
- ☐ Number of electrons transferred per mole of H₂: $n = 2$.
- ☐ **Faraday's constant:** $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol (memorize to 5 significant figures).
- ☐ **Nernst equation for the H₂/O₂ cell:**

$$E = E^\circ + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln \left( p_{H_2} \cdot p_{O_2}^{\,1/2} \right)$$

- ☐ **Key thermodynamic data:** $\Delta G^\circ = -237.13$ kJ/mol; $\Delta H_{HHV}^\circ = -285.83$ kJ/mol; $\Delta H_{LHV}^\circ = -241.82$ kJ/mol.
- ☐ **Theoretical efficiency (HHV basis):** 82.96 %; **LHV basis:** 98.05 %.
- ☐ The **three polarization losses** (in order of appearance as current increases): **Activation → Ohmic → Concentration**.
- ☐ The Butler-Volmer / Tafel equation governs activation loss; Ohm's law governs ohmic loss; mass-transport limit governs concentration loss.
- ☐ A real PEMFC operates at ~0.6–0.7 V per cell (not 1.229 V) at ~1 A/cm².
- ☐ Automotive stacks contain 300–400 cells in series to deliver 250–450 V DC.
- ☐ Fuel cell vehicles **do not store electricity** — they convert it. Hydrogen stores the chemical energy.
- ☐ By-products: **water (H₂O)** and heat. Tail-pipe emission is essentially zero (only water vapor).
- ☐ Major FCEV examples: **Toyota Mirai**, **Hyundai Nexo**, **Honda Clarity** (now discontinued), **Mercedes-Benz GLC F-CELL**.
- ☐ Refueling time: 3–5 minutes; range: 500–700 km.
- ☐ In Nernst problems, use $T$ in **Kelvin** (°C + 273.15). The factor $\frac{RT}{F}$ at 25 °C is **0.02569 V**.
- ☐ In Faraday's law mass-flow problems, use $M_{H_2} = 2.016$ g/mol and **never** set $n = 1$.
- ☐ Use the Nernst equation when partial pressures differ from 1 atm; otherwise $E \approx E^\circ$.
- ☐ A polarization curve is **always** drawn with $V$ on the Y-axis and $j$ (A/cm²) on the X-axis, with three labelled regions.
- ☐ PEMFC operating temperature: 60–100 °C (low-temp, fast start-up — ideal for vehicles).
- ☐ Catalyst: platinum-based (very expensive — drives FCEV cost; ongoing research on Pt-Co, Pt-Ni alloys).
- ☐ Membrane: Nafion™ — must stay hydrated for H⁺ conduction; hence humidifier in the system.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Practical Efficiency of Fuel Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT631 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Practical Efficiency of Fuel Cells

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Efficiency of a Fuel Cell** is defined as the ratio of the actual electrical power output delivered at the cell terminals to the chemical energy input from the fuel (hydrogen), accounting for all real-world irreversibilities, voltage losses, parasitic loads, and incomplete fuel utilization. Mathematically, it is expressed as the product of the **Thermodynamic (Reversible) Efficiency**, the **Voltage Efficiency**, the **Faradaic (Fuel Utilization) Efficiency**, and the **Thermal/Mechanical (Parasitic) Efficiency** of the balance-of-plant.

The **Thermodynamic (Reversible) Efficiency** is the absolute upper bound dictated by the Gibbs free energy change of the hydrogen oxidation reaction and is given by:

$$\eta_{\text{rev}} = \frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{\Delta H^{\circ}}$$

where the numerator is the maximum useful electrical work extractable and the denominator is the total chemical energy released (combustion enthalpy).

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Leaky Bucket"**
> Imagine a perfectly designed water bucket (the *theoretical* fuel cell) that can deliver 100 litres of water (energy) from a 100-litre tank. In an ideal, frictionless world, every drop reaches the destination. In practice, the bucket has:
> - **Cracks near the rim** (Activation losses — energy needed to "start" the electrochemical reaction at the electrode surface)
> - **Porous walls** (Ohmic losses — resistance to ion flow through the electrolyte)
> - **A clogged spout at high flow** (Concentration losses — fuel cannot reach the reaction sites fast enough at high current)
> - **A secondary pump stealing energy** (Parasitic losses — blowers, pumps, cooling fans)
> - **Unburnt fuel escaping** (Fuel crossover — H₂ passing through membrane without reacting)
>
> Practical efficiency is the measure of *how much water actually reaches the destination* out of the original 100 litres.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (in Bold)

- **Faraday's Constant (F) = 96,485 C/mol**
- **Universal Gas Constant (R) = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- **Reversible Cell Voltage (E_rev) = 1.229 V** at Standard Temperature and Pressure (STP) for the H₂/O₂ reaction
- **Higher Heating Value (HHV) of H₂ = 285.8 kJ/mol**
- **Lower Heating Value (LHV) of H₂ = 241.8 kJ/mol**
- **Standard Gibbs Free Energy Change (ΔG°) = −237.2 kJ/mol** for H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O(l)
- **Number of Electrons Transferred (n) = 2** per H₂ molecule

### 1.4 Why Practical Efficiency Matters in EV/HEV Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a fuel cell electric vehicle (FCEV) such as the Toyota Mirai or Hyundai Nexo, the practical efficiency directly translates to **kilometres per kilogram of hydrogen** stored on board. While a battery EV can achieve ~90% well-to-wheel efficiency, an FCEV must rely on the practical efficiency of the fuel cell stack (typically **40–60%**) to remain competitive. Every percentage point of efficiency loss means more hydrogen consumption and a shorter driving range.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four-Pillar Decomposition of Practical Efficiency

The practical efficiency of a fuel cell system is the product of four independent efficiency mechanisms, each addressing a distinct physical phenomenon:

**Pillar 1 — Thermodynamic (Reversible) Efficiency (η_th)**
- Originates from the **second law of thermodynamics** — entropy change during the reaction.
- Defines the ceiling: no fuel cell can ever exceed this without external heat input.
- Based on the ratio **ΔG°/ΔH°**, which is a property of the fuel chemistry alone.

**Pillar 2 — Voltage Efficiency (η_v)**
- Originates from **electrochemical irreversibilities** (polarization losses).
- The actual cell voltage (V_cell) is always less than the reversible voltage (E_rev) due to activation, ohmic, and concentration overpotentials.
- Defined as **η_v = V_cell / E_rev**.

**Pillar 3 — Faradaic / Fuel Utilization Efficiency (η_f)**
- Originates from **fuel crossover** through the membrane and **incomplete electrochemical conversion**.
- Even in well-designed PEMFCs, 2–5% of H₂ may diffuse from anode to cathode without producing current.
- Defined as **η_f = I_actual / I_max_possible** based on H₂ supplied.

**Pillar 4 — Parasitic / System Efficiency (η_p)**
- Originates from **balance-of-plant** components that consume stack output.
- Includes air compressor/blower (largest parasitic load, ~10–15% of stack power), coolant pump, humidifier, and electronic control units.
- Defined as **η_p = (P_stack − P_parasitic) / P_stack**.

### 2.2 The Polarization Curve (V–I Characteristic)

The polarization curve is the **fingerprint** of a fuel cell and the central tool for understanding practical efficiency. The actual cell voltage as a function of current density is:

$$V_{\text{cell}}(j) = E_{\text{rev}} - \eta_{\text{act}}(j) - \eta_{\text{ohm}}(j) - \eta_{\text{conc}}(j)$$

where **j** is the current density (A/cm²).

**Three Loss Regions:**

1. **Activation Loss Region (Low Current, j < 0.1 A/cm²):**
   - Governed by the **Butler–Volmer equation**.
   - Represents the energy barrier for charge transfer at electrode–electrolyte interface.
   - Dominant at startup and idle conditions.

2. **Ohmic Loss Region (Medium Current, 0.1 < j < 0.8 A/cm²):**
   - Linear with current density: **η_ohmic = j × R_ohmic** (where R_ohmic is the area-specific resistance, ASR).
   - Caused by ionic resistance in the membrane (dominant) and electronic resistance in electrodes, bipolar plates, and contacts.
   - Membrane resistance is strongly temperature- and humidity-dependent.

3. **Concentration Loss Region (High Current, j > 0.8 A/cm²):**
   - Rapid voltage drop as reactant supply cannot keep up with demand.
   - Caused by mass transport limitations — H₂ starvation at anode, O₂ depletion at cathode, water flooding.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they form 90% of all numerical questions on this topic in KTU ESE.**

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Equation | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reversible Cell Voltage (Nernst) | $E_{\text{rev}} = \frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{n F}$ | 1.229 V at STP, n=2 |
| 2 | Theoretical (Thermodynamic) Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{th}} = \frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{\Delta H^{\circ}} = \frac{E_{\text{rev}}}{E_{\text{therm}}}$ | 0.83 (HHV) or 0.94 (LHV) |
| 3 | Thermoneutral Voltage | $E_{\text{therm}} = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{n F}$ | 1.481 V (HHV) or 1.253 V (LHV) |
| 4 | Voltage Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{v}} = \frac{V_{\text{cell}}}{E_{\text{rev}}}$ | Always less than 1 |
| 5 | Total Cell Voltage | $V_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{rev}} - \eta_{\text{act}} - \eta_{\text{ohm}} - \eta_{\text{conc}}$ | Practical operating voltage |
| 6 | Ohmic Loss (Linear) | $\eta_{\text{ohm}} = j \cdot R_{\text{ASR}}$ | R_ASR in Ω·cm² |
| 7 | Activation Loss (Tafel approx.) | $\eta_{\text{act}} = \frac{R T}{\alpha n F} \ln\left(\frac{j}{j_{0}}\right)$ | High overpotential limit |
| 8 | Concentration Loss | $\eta_{\text{conc}} = -\frac{R T}{n F} \ln\left(1 - \frac{j}{j_{L}}\right)$ | j_L = limiting current density |
| 9 | Practical Stack Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{stack}} = \eta_{\text{th}} \cdot \eta_{\text{v}} \cdot \eta_{\text{f}}$ | Three-pillar product |
| 10 | Practical System Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{sys}} = \eta_{\text{stack}} \cdot \eta_{\text{parasitic}$ | Includes BoP losses |
| 11 | Power Output | $P_{\text{cell}} = V_{\text{cell}} \cdot I = V_{\text{cell}} \cdot j \cdot A$ | A = active area in cm² |
| 12 | Electrical Work Output | $W_{\text{elec}} = n F \cdot V_{\text{cell}}$ | Per mole of H₂ |
| 13 | Heat Generated | $Q = \Delta H - n F V_{\text{cell}}$ | Energy not converted to electricity |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Significance

- **Transportation (FCEVs):** Practical efficiency determines hydrogen tank size and refuelling frequency. A modern PEMFC stack operates at **50–60% LHV efficiency** at peak power.
- **Stationary Power (CHP systems):** Combined Heat and Power applications recover the waste heat (Q), pushing **total (electrical + thermal) efficiency to 80–90%**.
- **Aerospace & Drones:** Hydrogen fuel cells offer 3–5× the specific energy of Li-ion, making them ideal for long-endurance flight, but practical efficiency must be maximized to reduce fuel mass.
- **Backup Power (Telecom towers):** Higher practical efficiency = lower hydrogen consumption = lower operating cost.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Reversible Cell Voltage from Gibbs Free Energy

**Starting Point:** The maximum non-PV (non-expansion) electrical work obtainable from a chemical reaction at constant T and P is given by the decrease in Gibbs free energy.

**Step 1:** State the half-reactions for the Hydrogen-Oxygen Fuel Cell.

$$\text{Anode (Oxidation): } H_{2} \rightarrow 2H^{+} + 2e^{-}$$

$$\text{Cathode (Reduction): } \frac{1}{2}O_{2} + 2H^{+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow H_{2}O_{(l)}$$

**Step 2:** Identify the number of electrons transferred per mole of H₂.

Since each H₂ molecule releases 2 electrons at the anode, the electron transfer number is:

$$n = 2$$

**Step 3:** Write the relationship between Gibbs free energy and electrical work.

The maximum reversible electrical work is the change in Gibbs free energy:

$$W_{\text{elec,max}} = -\Delta G^{\circ}$$

**Step 4:** Express electrical work in terms of charge and voltage.

The total charge transferred per mole of H₂ is nF coulombs. Work = Charge × Voltage:

$$W_{\text{elec,max}} = n \cdot F \cdot E_{\text{rev}}$$

**Step 5:** Equate the two expressions for maximum work and solve for E_rev.

$$n \cdot F \cdot E_{\text{rev}} = -\Delta G^{\circ}$$

$$E_{\text{rev}} = \frac{-\Delta G^{\circ}}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute the standard value of ΔG° = −237,200 J/mol for liquid water product.

$$E_{\text{rev}} = \frac{-(-237{,}200)}{2 \times 96{,}485}$$

**Step 7:** Compute the numerical result.

$$E_{\text{rev}} = \frac{237{,}200}{192{,}970}$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{rev}} = 1.229 \text{ V}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **theoretical open-circuit voltage** of a hydrogen fuel cell at 25°C and 1 atm. No real cell ever reaches this voltage because the open-circuit voltage (OCV) is already reduced by ~0.1–0.2 V due to fuel crossover and mixed-potential effects at the cathode.

### 3.2 Derivation: Theoretical Thermodynamic Efficiency (HHV & LHV)

**Step 1:** Define the relationship between enthalpy and Gibbs free energy.

For any reaction at constant temperature, the relation is:

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = \Delta H^{\circ} - T \Delta S^{\circ}$$

**Step 2:** Identify the enthalpy values.
- For the H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O(l) reaction: **ΔH°(HHV) = −285.8 kJ/mol** (water as liquid, latent heat recovered)
- For the H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O(g) reaction: **ΔH°(LHV) = −241.8 kJ/mol** (water as vapour, latent heat lost)

**Step 3:** Define theoretical efficiency as the ratio of useful work (ΔG) to total energy released (ΔH).

$$\eta_{\text{th}} = \frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{\Delta H^{\circ}}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the efficiency using HHV (liquid water product).

$$\eta_{\text{th,HHV}} = \frac{237.2}{285.8}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{\text{th,HHV}} = 0.8298 \approx 83.0\%}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the efficiency using LHV (vapour water product).

$$\eta_{\text{th,LHV}} = \frac{228.6}{241.8}$$

> Note: For the LHV case, ΔG° = 228.6 kJ/mol (corrected for water vapour product state).

$$\boxed{\eta_{\text{th,LHV}} = 0.9454 \approx 94.5\%}$$

**Step 6:** Interpret the physical meaning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 17% gap between HHV and LHV efficiency arises because in the LHV case, the latent heat of vaporization of water is *not* counted as recoverable energy — it remains in the vapour product. Automotive applications typically use **LHV efficiency** since fuel cell exhaust water leaves as vapour.

### 3.3 Derivation: Practical Efficiency from the Polarization Curve (Numerical Example)

**Given:** A PEM fuel cell operates at 0.65 V per cell. Hydrogen is supplied at 99% utilization. Parasitic loads consume 8% of stack power.

**Step 1:** Identify all the efficiency components.

- Thermodynamic efficiency (LHV): **η_th = 0.945**
- Voltage efficiency: **η_v = 0.65 / 1.229**
- Faradaic efficiency: **η_f = 0.99**
- Parasitic efficiency: **η_p = 0.92**

**Step 2:** Calculate the Voltage Efficiency.

$$\eta_{\text{v}} = \frac{V_{\text{cell}}}{E_{\text{rev}}} = \frac{0.65}{1.229}$$

$$\eta_{\text{v}} = 0.5289$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the Stack-Level Practical Efficiency.

$$\eta_{\text{stack}} = \eta_{\text{th}} \times \eta_{\text{v}} \times \eta_{\text{f}}$$

$$\eta_{\text{stack}} = 0.945 \times 0.5289 \times 0.99$$

$$\eta_{\text{stack}} = 0.4948 \approx 49.5\%$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the System-Level Practical Efficiency (after parasitic losses).

$$\eta_{\text{sys}} = \eta_{\text{stack}} \times \eta_{\text{p}}$$

$$\eta_{\text{sys}} = 0.4948 \times 0.92$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{\text{sys}} = 0.4552 \approx 45.5\%}$$

**Step 5:** Interpret the result.

> [!NOTE]
> A 45.5% practical system efficiency is **typical for a current-generation automotive PEMFC stack** under part-load conditions. The largest contributor to the loss is **voltage efficiency** (η_v = 53%), meaning the largest single opportunity for improvement is reducing activation and ohmic overpotentials — primarily through better electrocatalysts (lower Pt loading, Pt-alloys) and thinner, more conductive membranes.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Efficiency Loss Breakdown

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical Loss Distribution in an Operating PEMFC Stack (V_cell = 0.65 V):**

| Loss Mechanism | Voltage Drop (V) | Equivalent Efficiency Loss (%) | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Activation (Cathode ORR) | 0.20 – 0.30 | 16 – 24 | Pt-alloy catalysts, higher T, higher Pt loading |
| Activation (Anode HOR) | 0.01 – 0.05 | 1 – 4 | Generally fast kinetics |
| Ohmic (Membrane + contacts) | 0.05 – 0.10 | 4 – 8 | Thinner membrane, better humidification, metallic BP |
| Concentration (Mass transport) | 0.02 – 0.05 | 2 – 4 | Better GDL, higher stoich, better water management |
| Fuel Crossover (Faradaic) | — | 2 – 5 | Thinner/alternative membranes |
| Parasitic (BoP) | — | 5 – 15 | Efficient compressors, optimized thermal mgmt |
| **Total Loss from Reversible** | **~0.58 V** | **~30 – 55%** | Combined stack + system efficiency ≈ 45 – 60% |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
