## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is Field Oriented Control? List its main advantages over scalar V/f control of induction motors.**

**Model Answer:**
Field Oriented Control (FOC), or vector control, is a technique that decouples the flux-producing ($i_d$) and torque-producing ($i_q$) stator current components of an AC machine by transforming them into a synchronously rotating $dq$ reference frame. This allows independent control of flux and torque, similar to a separately excited DC motor.

**Main advantages over scalar V/f control:**
1. **Independent flux and torque control** — enables fast torque response.
2. **Excellent low-speed performance** including zero-speed full torque.
3. **Wide speed range** with field-weakening capability.
4. **Fast dynamic response** for EV acceleration/braking.
5. **Higher efficiency** at partial loads.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**What is Space Vector PWM? Why is it preferred over sinusoidal PWM in EV traction inverters?**

**Model Answer:**
SVPWM is a modulation technique where the three-phase reference voltage is treated as a single rotating space vector in the $\alpha\beta$ plane, and synthesized by time-averaging the discrete switching states of a two-level VSI over a sampling period $T_s$.

**Why preferred in EV traction inverters:**
1. **~15.47% higher DC-bus utilization** ($V_{ref,max} = V_{DC}/\sqrt{3}$ vs $V_{DC}/2$ in SPWM) — extends EV range.
2. **Lower total harmonic distortion** in line currents and torque.
3. **Easier digital implementation** in DSPs/FPGAs.
4. **Better utilisation of DC bus** reduces battery stress.
5. **Symmetric seven-segment switching** minimises switching losses.

---

### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — KTU Internal Choice Pattern**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Explain the Park and Clarke transformations used in FOC of a three-phase PMSM. Derive the corresponding transformation matrices.**

**Model Solution:**

**Clarke Transformation ($abc \rightarrow \alpha\beta$):**
The three-phase stator currents $i_a, i_b, i_c$ are projected onto two orthogonal stationary axes $\alpha$ and $\beta$ (with $\alpha$ along phase-$a$):

$$i_\alpha = i_a - \tfrac{1}{2}(i_b + i_c) = \tfrac{3}{2}i_a \quad \text{(for balanced system)}$$

$$i_\beta = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(i_a + 2i_b)$$

In matrix form (amplitude-invariant):

$$\begin{bmatrix} i_\alpha \\ i_\beta \end{bmatrix} = \tfrac{2}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -\tfrac{1}{2} & -\tfrac{1}{2} \\ 0 & \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} & -\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} i_a \\ i_b \\ i_c \end{bmatrix}$$

**Park Transformation ($\alpha\beta \rightarrow dq$):**
The $\alpha\beta$ frame is rotated by the synchronous angle $\theta_e$ to align the $d$-axis with the rotor flux:

$$\begin{bmatrix} i_d \\ i_q \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_e & \sin\theta_e \\ -\sin\theta_e & \cos\theta_e \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} i_\alpha \\ i_\beta \end{bmatrix}$$

**Combined transform ($abc \rightarrow dq$):**
Substituting the Clarke matrix into the Park matrix:

$$\begin{bmatrix} i_d \\ i_q \end{bmatrix} = \tfrac{2}{3}\begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_e & \cos(\theta_e - 120^\circ) & \cos(\theta_e + 120^\circ) \\ -\sin\theta_e & -\sin(\theta_e - 120^\circ) & -\sin(\theta_e + 120^\circ) \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} i_a \\ i_b \\ i_c \end{bmatrix}$$

In the $dq$ frame, $i_d$ controls the field and $i_q$ controls the torque independently.

---

**(b)** [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**With a neat block diagram, explain the indirect field-oriented control of a three-phase PMSM. Derive the expression for electromagnetic torque.**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram Description:** The indirect FOC uses the rotor position $\theta_r$ from an encoder plus the computed slip angle $\theta_{sl}$ to obtain the synchronous angle $\theta_e = \theta_r + \theta_{sl}$.

**Speed Loop:** Reference speed $\omega^*$ is compared with measured speed $\omega_r$ and the error is fed to a PI controller whose output is $i_q^*$.

**Current Loops:** $i_d^*$ is set to zero (for surface PMSM, MTPA) and $i_q^*$ comes from the speed PI. Measured phase currents are transformed via Clarke and Park to obtain $i_d$ and $i_q$, which are then compared with their references. The errors are fed to two PI controllers producing $V_d^*$ and $V_q^*$.

**Inverse Park + SVPWM:** $V_d^*, V_q^*$ are converted back to $\alpha\beta$ frame and then to switching pulses via SVPWM, which drive the 2-level VSI feeding the PMSM.

**Torque Derivation for PMSM:**
The electromagnetic torque of a PMSM in the $dq$ frame is given by:

$$T_e = \tfrac{3}{2}\,p\left[\psi_f\,i_q + (L_d - L_q)\,i_d\,i_q\right]$$

For a **surface-mounted PMSM** (SPM) where $L_d = L_q = L_s$:

$$T_e = \tfrac{3}{2}\,p\,\psi_f\,i_q$$

For an **interior PMSM** (IPM) with $L_d \ne L_q$, the **reluctance torque** term $(L_d - L_q)i_d i_q$ is non-zero and is exploited for **field-weakening** and **flux-weakening** operation above base speed.

With $i_d^* = 0$ control, only the magnet torque exists, giving a linear torque-$i_q$ relationship — a key advantage of FOC.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2022]
**Explain Space Vector PWM for a two-level voltage source inverter. Derive expressions for active and zero vector dwell times in Sector-1.**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept:** A 2-level VSI has 8 switching states. Six states (100, 110, 010, 011, 001, 101) produce six active voltage vectors of magnitude $\tfrac{2}{3}V_{DC}$ equally spaced at $60^\circ$ apart, forming a hexagon. Two states (000 and 111) produce the zero vector at the origin.

**Reference Vector in Sector-1:** Let $V_{ref} = V_m\angle\theta$ with $\theta \in [0, 60^\circ]$.

**Volt-Second Balance:**

$$T_s\,\vec{V}_{ref} = T_1\,\vec{V}_1 + T_2\,\vec{V}_2 + T_0\,\vec{V}_0$$

Since $V_0 = 0$, decomposing into $\alpha$ and $\beta$ components:

$$T_s V_m \cos\theta = T_1\,\tfrac{2}{3}V_{DC} + T_2\,\tfrac{2}{3}V_{DC}\cos 60^\circ$$

$$T_s V_m \sin\theta = T_2\,\tfrac{2}{3}V_{DC}\sin 60^\circ$$

**Solving:**

$$T_1 = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}\,T_s V_m}{V_{DC}}\sin(60^\circ - \theta)$$

$$T_2 = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}\,T_s V_m}{V_{DC}}\sin\theta$$

$$T_0 = T_s - T_1 - T_2$$

**Switching Sequence (Sector-1):** $V_0 \rightarrow V_1 \rightarrow V_2 \rightarrow V_7 \rightarrow V_2 \rightarrow V_1 \rightarrow V_0$ (7 segments, symmetric).

---

**(b)** [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2022]
**Compare SPWM and SVPWM. Show that SVPWM provides 15.47% higher DC bus utilisation.**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | SPWM | SVPWM |
|---|---|---|
| DC bus utilization | $V_{DC}/2$ | $V_{DC}/\sqrt{3}$ |
| Modulation index $m$ max | 1.0 | 1.1547 (overmodulation) |
| Implementation | Per-phase | Vector-based |
| THD | Higher | Lower |
| Switching losses | Higher | Lower (7-segment symmetric) |
| Suitability for EV | Limited | Excellent |

**Proof of 15.47% Higher DC Bus Utilization:**

In **SPWM**, the peak of the fundamental phase voltage is:

$$V_{ph,peak}^{SPWM} = \dfrac{V_{DC}}{2}$$

In **SVPWM**, the maximum reference magnitude within the linear region is the radius of the inscribed circle of the hexagon:

$$V_{ref,max}^{SVPWM} = \dfrac{V_{DC}}{\sqrt{3}}$$

(equivalently, the phase peak is $V_{DC}/\sqrt{3}$ for the line-to-neutral voltage at $m = 1$).

**Ratio:**

$$\dfrac{V_{SVPWM}}{V_{SPWM}} = \dfrac{V_{DC}/\sqrt{3}}{V_{DC}/2} = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}} = 1.1547$$

**Percentage gain:** $1.1547 - 1.0 = 0.1547 = \mathbf{15.47\%}$ ✓

This means for the same DC bus voltage, an EV traction motor can deliver **15.47% more torque and power** when using SVPWM, directly translating to **better acceleration and longer range** — a decisive factor in commercial EVs.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign of Park transform:** Many students write $i_d = i_\alpha\cos\theta - i_\beta\sin\theta$ (wrong sign). The correct form is $i_d = i_\alpha\cos\theta + i_\beta\sin\theta$.
> 2. **$2/3$ factor in Clarke:** The amplitude-invariant Clarke uses factor $2/3$; the power-invariant form uses $\sqrt{2/3}$. Mixing these leads to wrong torque values.
> 3. **$T_0 = T_s - T_1 - T_2$:** Students often forget the zero-vector dwell time and write only $T_s = T_1 + T_2$, leading to overshoot and saturation.
> 4. **Sector identification:** If $\theta = 75^\circ$, students mistakenly put it in Sector-1 — it is actually in **Sector-2** (since Sector-2 spans $60^\circ$ to $120^\circ$).
> 5. **"SVPWM gives 15% more"** without derivation: Examiners award marks only when the ratio $2/\sqrt{3} = 1.1547$ is **explicitly shown**.

---

### **TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Save before exam!)**

- ☐ FOC = **decoupled control of flux & torque** in rotating $dq$ frame.
- ☐ Clarke = $abc \to \alpha\beta$; Park = $\alpha\beta \to dq$ (DC quantities in steady state).
- ☐ Inverse Park + SVPWM = bridge from $dq$ commands to real inverter gating.
- ☐ For SPM-PMSM: $T_e = \tfrac{3}{2}p\psi_f i_q$, set $i_d^* = 0$ (MTPA).
- ☐ For IPM-PMSM: $T_e$ has **reluctance term** $(L_d - L_q)i_d i_q$ — exploit negative $i_d$ for field-weakening.
- ☐ Indirect FOC needs slip $\theta_{sl} = \int \omega_{sl} dt$ where $\omega_{sl} = R_r i_q / (L_r i_d)$ for IM.
- ☐ Direct FOC needs flux observer (sensorless-friendly) but more complex.
- ☐ SVPWM = 8 states, hexagon, 6 sectors, 7-segment symmetric sequence.
- ☐ Max linear $V_{ref} = V_{DC}/\sqrt{3}$ (vs $V_{DC}/2$ in SPWM) → **+15.47% bus utilization**.
- ☐ Modulation index $m = \sqrt{3}\,V_{ref}/V_{DC}$, max $= 1$ in linear region.
- ☐ Switching times: $T_1 = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}T_s V_m}{V_{DC}}\sin(60^\circ-\theta)$, $T_2 = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}T_s V_m}{V_{DC}}\sin\theta$, $T_0 = T_s - T_1 - T_2$.
- ☐ PI current loops operate on **DC** $i_d, i_q$ — easy tuning, high bandwidth (~kHz).
- ☐ Outer speed loop bandwidth $\sim$ 1/10th of inner current loop bandwidth.
- ☐ Cascaded structure: **Speed PI → Current PI → SVPWM → VSI → Motor**.
- ☐ EV applications: Tesla, Nissan Leaf, Toyota Prius, BMW i3 — all use FOC + SVPWM.
- ☐ Wide-bandgap SiC/GaN devices enable $f_{sw} > 50\text{ kHz}$ → quieter, smaller EV inverters.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gearbox
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT631 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Gearbox

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **gearbox** (also called a transmission or reducer) in an Electric and Hybrid Vehicle (EV/HEV) is a mechanical power-transmission device that converts the rotational speed and torque of the electric traction motor into a usable output at the drive wheels. It employs a set of meshing gears of varying sizes to provide specific **gear ratios**, allowing the motor's high-RPM, low-torque output to be adapted to the vehicle's low-RPM, high-torque requirements during launch and hill-climbing, while still permitting high-speed cruising without over-revving the motor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> "The gearbox in an EV/HEV is an electromechanical device that modifies the speed-torque characteristic of the electric motor to match the tractive effort requirements of the vehicle, while improving overall drivetrain efficiency, and may consist of single-stage, multi-stage, or continuously variable configurations."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine riding a **bicycle**. When you start pedaling from a standstill, climbing a steep hill, you shift to a **low gear** (small chainring, large sprocket). This makes pedaling easy but your feet spin slowly. Conversely, when cruising downhill on a flat road, you shift to a **high gear** (large chainring, small sprocket), making one pedal stroke move the bike a long distance.

The **electric motor is the leg muscle**, and the **gearbox is the derailleur**. The muscle (motor) always wants to spin fast in its "sweet spot," but the wheel needs different "leverage" depending on the situation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Metrics Used in KTU Board Exams:**
> - Standard efficiency of EV single-speed gearbox: **0.96 – 0.98 (96%–98%)**
> - Typical EV single-speed reduction ratio: **8:1 to 12:1**
> - Common multi-speed EV gear ratios (Tesla Model S 2-speed): **1st: 9.34, 2nd: 4.81**
> - Universal physical constant: $\pi \approx 3.14159$

## 1.3 Why an EV Needs a Gearbox (or a Simple Reducer)

Unlike Internal Combustion Engines (ICE) which have a narrow useful RPM band, **electric motors produce maximum torque at zero RPM** and have a wide constant-power region. This gives EVs the *theoretical* ability to operate without a gearbox (single-speed direct drive). However, a gearbox is still preferred for:

1. **Optimizing Motor Operating Point** — keeps the motor in its high-efficiency zone.
2. **Increasing Starting Torque at the Wheels** — without it, high-RPM motors cannot launch a heavy vehicle smoothly.
3. **Reducing Motor Size and Cost** — a high reduction ratio allows use of smaller, lighter, cheaper motors.
4. **Improving Range** — by matching motor speed to road load, reducing electrical energy consumption.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Most modern mass-market EVs (e.g., Nissan Leaf, Tata Nexon EV) use a single-speed fixed-ratio gearbox with a ratio of approximately 8:1 to 10:1. Performance EVs (Porsche Taycan, Audi e-tron GT) use a 2-speed gearbox for both launch torque and high-speed efficiency.**

## 1.4 Types of Gearboxes Used in EV/HEVs

| Type | Application | Typical Use |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Single-Speed Reducer** | Most modern EVs | Compact, high-efficiency city/highway cars |
| **Two-Speed Gearbox** | Performance & Premium EVs | Tesla Model S Plaid, Porsche Taycan |
| **Multi-Speed (3–7 gears)** | Hybrid Synergy Drives | Toyota THS, Honda i-MMD |
| **Planetary Gear Set** | Power-split HEVs | Toyota Prius, Ford Escape Hybrid |
| **AMT (Automated Manual)** | Low-cost EVs | Mahindra eVerito |
| **DCT (Dual-Clutch)** | High-performance HEVs | BMW i8, Koenigsegg Regera |
| **CVT (e-CVT)** | Hybrid synergy | Nissan e-Power system |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle of a Gearbox

A gearbox operates on the principle of **meshing gear pairs** where two gears of different tooth counts engage to transfer rotational motion. When a smaller gear (pinion) drives a larger gear (wheel), the output rotates slower but with proportionally greater torque.

### 2.1.1 Core Logic Steps

1. **Input from Motor** — The electric motor delivers rotational power $(P_{in})$ at angular speed $\omega_m$ and torque $T_m$.
2. **Gear Pair Engagement** — Power flows through gear teeth. The contact transmits tangential force $F_t$ at the pitch circle radius $r$.
3. **Speed Reduction** — Driven gear rotates at $\omega_{out} = \omega_m / i_g$, where $i_g$ is the gear ratio.
4. **Torque Amplification** — Output torque is amplified: $T_{out} = T_m \cdot i_g \cdot \eta_g$, where $\eta_g$ is gearbox efficiency.
5. **Power Loss** — Small fraction of power is lost to friction, churning, and windage; efficiency typically **96%–98%** for a single-stage reducer.
6. **Output to Differential & Wheels** — Output is fed to a differential (in most layouts) and then to the drive wheels.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why' Behind Each Step:** Without speed reduction, the small, fast-spinning motor could not move a 1500+ kg vehicle from rest, because the contact force at the tire-road interface would be insufficient. The gearbox multiplies force through the mechanical advantage of gear ratios.

## 2.2 Gear Ratio Definitions

For a simple two-gear pair:
- **Velocity Ratio (VR) / Gear Ratio** $i_g = N_2 / N_1 = Z_2 / Z_1 = \omega_1 / \omega_2$
- Where $N_1$ = teeth on driver (pinion), $N_2$ = teeth on driven (wheel), $\omega_1$ = input speed, $\omega_2$ = output speed.

For a **multi-stage gearbox** (compound gear train with $k$ stages):

$$
i_{total} = \prod_{j=1}^{k} i_j = \frac{\omega_{in}}{\omega_{out}}
$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table for the 2024 ESE — these formulas are tested every year.**

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | $i_g = N_2 / N_1$ | Gear ratio (driven / driver) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\omega_{out} = \omega_{in} / i_g$ | Output angular speed | rad/s |
| 3 | $T_{out} = T_{in} \cdot i_g \cdot \eta_g$ | Output torque (with losses) | N·m |
| 4 | $T_{out} = T_{in} \cdot i_g$ | Output torque (ideal, $\eta_g=1$) | N·m |
| 5 | $P_{out} = T_{out} \cdot \omega_{out}$ | Output power | W |
| 6 | $P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot \eta_g$ | Power output with efficiency | W |
| 7 | $\eta_g = P_{out} / P_{in}$ | Gearbox efficiency | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 8 | $v = \omega_{wheel} \cdot r_{wheel}$ | Vehicle linear speed | m/s |
| 9 | $\omega_{wheel} = \omega_m / (i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot i_d)$ | Wheel speed from motor | rad/s |
| 10 | $F_t = T_{wheel} / r_{wheel}$ | Tractive force at wheel | N |
| 11 | $MVA = (Z_1 + Z_2) \cdot m \cdot \cos\phi$ | Minimum teeth to avoid interference | dimensionless |
| 12 | $P_d = M_t \cdot \omega$ | Design power for thermal rating | W |

Where:
- $i_0$ = final drive ratio (if separate)
- $i_d$ = differential ratio
- $r_{wheel}$ = effective rolling radius of tire
- $Z_1, Z_2$ = number of teeth on pinion and wheel
- $m$ = module of the gear
- $\phi$ = pressure angle (typically 20°)
- $M_t$ = transmitted torque

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Table Safety:** Notice the use of $\vert$ in formula representations inside the table — never use the pipe symbol `|` as it breaks markdown table syntax.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

In automotive industries, the gearbox enables:
- **Vehicle launch** from standstill under full load.
- **Efficiency optimization** by keeping the motor in its **high-efficiency constant-power region**.
- **NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness) tuning** through proper gear tooth profile selection (e.g., helical gears for quiet operation).
- **Torque vectoring** in multi-motor EVs (using differential gear sets in each axle).

## 2.5 Single-Speed vs Multi-Speed Gearbox — Comparative Analysis

| Parameter | Single-Speed | Multi-Speed |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|
| Complexity | Low | High |
| Weight | Light (~25–40 kg) | Heavy (~70–120 kg) |
| Efficiency | 96–98% | 92–96% (multiple meshes) |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Shift Quality | No shift shock | Requires synchronizers/actuators |
| Top Speed | Limited by motor max RPM | Higher achievable |
| Efficiency Band | Narrow optimum | Wide optimum |
| Used In | Nissan Leaf, Tata Nexon EV | Tesla Model S, Porsche Taycan |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Output Torque of a Single-Stage Gearbox with Losses

**Problem Statement:** Derive the relationship between output torque $T_{out}$, input torque $T_{in}$, gear ratio $i_g$, and gearbox efficiency $\eta_g$.

**Starting Point — Conservation of Power:**

In an *ideal* gearbox (no losses), power in = power out. In a *real* gearbox, output power is less than input power due to friction, churning, and windage losses.

$$
P_{in} = T_{in} \cdot \omega_{in}
$$

$$
P_{out} = T_{out} \cdot \omega_{out}
$$

**Step 1 — Define Efficiency**

By definition of mechanical efficiency:

$$
\eta_g = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{T_{out} \cdot \omega_{out}}{T_{in} \cdot \omega_{in}}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Gear Ratio Relation**

The gear ratio is defined as:

$$
i_g = \frac{\omega_{in}}{\omega_{out}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{\omega_{out}}{\omega_{in}} = \frac{1}{i_g}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the Efficiency Equation**

$$
\eta_g = \frac{T_{out}}{T_{in}} \cdot \frac{\omega_{out}}{\omega_{in}} = \frac{T_{out}}{T_{in}} \cdot \frac{1}{i_g}
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange for Output Torque**

$$
\frac{T_{out}}{T_{in}} = \eta_g \cdot i_g
$$

$$
T_{out} = T_{in} \cdot i_g \cdot \eta_g
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,T_{out} = T_{in} \cdot i_g \cdot \eta_g\,}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:** For every 1 N·m of motor torque, with a gear ratio of 10:1 and 97% efficiency, the wheels receive 9.7 N·m of usable torque (10 - 0.3 N·m lost to friction).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Vehicle Speed from Motor RPM

**Problem Statement:** A single-speed EV has a motor peak speed of $N_m = 12{,}000$ RPM, a gearbox ratio of $i_g = 9.5$, and a tire effective radius of $r_{wheel} = 0.32$ m. Find the maximum vehicle speed in km/h.

**Step 1 — Convert Motor Speed to Angular Velocity**

$$
\omega_m = \frac{2 \pi N_m}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 12{,}000}{60}
$$

$$
\omega_m = 400 \pi \text{ rad/s} \approx 1256.64 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Gear Ratio to Find Wheel Angular Speed**

$$
\omega_{wheel} = \frac{\omega_m}{i_g} = \frac{400 \pi}{9.5}
$$

$$
\omega_{wheel} \approx 42.105 \pi \approx 132.28 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert Angular Wheel Speed to Linear Vehicle Speed**

$$
v = \omega_{wheel} \cdot r_{wheel}
$$

$$
v = 132.28 \times 0.32 \approx 42.33 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to km/h**

$$
v_{km/h} = v_{m/s} \times 3.6
$$

$$
v_{km/h} = 42.33 \times 3.6 \approx 152.4 \text{ km/h}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,v_{max} \approx 152.4 \text{ km/h}\,}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Total Drivetrain Ratio and Tractive Force

**Problem Statement:** An EV powertrain has: motor torque $T_m = 250$ N·m, gearbox ratio $i_g = 8.5$, final drive ratio $i_0 = 1$ (combined in EV), and tire radius $r = 0.31$ m. Find the tractive force at the wheels.

**Step 1 — Total Drivetrain Ratio**

$$
i_{total} = i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot i_d
$$

For most modern EVs, the gearbox and final drive are integrated, so $i_{total} = i_g$. Here, taking the total system ratio as 8.5.

**Step 2 — Compute Wheel Torque (Assuming 97% Drivetrain Efficiency)**

$$
T_{wheel} = T_m \cdot i_{total} \cdot \eta
$$

$$
T_{wheel} = 250 \times 8.5 \times 0.97
$$

$$
T_{wheel} = 250 \times 8.245
$$

$$
T_{wheel} = 2061.25 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Tractive Force at the Tire Contact Patch**

$$
F_t = \frac{T_{wheel}}{r_{wheel}}
$$

$$
F_t = \frac{2061.25}{0.31}
$$

$$
F_t \approx 6649.2 \text{ N}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,F_t \approx 6.65 \text{ kN per wheel pair}\,}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Gearbox Design Trade-offs

| Design Decision | Effect on Torque | Effect on Speed | Effect on Efficiency | Effect on Cost |
|-----------------|------------------|-----------------|----------------------|----------------|
| Increase $i_g$ (e.g., 8 → 12) | Increases | Decreases top speed | Slight decrease | Marginal increase |
| Single-stage | Moderate | Limited by motor RPM | Highest (96–98%) | Lowest |
| Two-stage (2-speed) | Flexible | Higher top speed | Slightly lower (94–96%) | Higher |
| Use helical gears | Slight increase | Same | +1–2% gain | Slightly higher |
| Use spur gears | Same | Same | Slightly lower | Lowest |
| Increase module size | Higher capacity | Same | Same | Higher |
| Better lubrication | Same | Same | +1–2% gain | Marginal |

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Power Loss in a Gearbox

**Problem Statement:** A gearbox transmits $P_{in} = 50$ kW with an efficiency of 96%. Find (a) output power, and (b) power loss in kW and as a percentage.

**Step 1 — Compute Output Power**

$$
P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot \eta_g = 50 \times 0.96 = 48 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Power Loss**

$$
P_{loss} = P_{in} - P_{out} = 50 - 48 = 2 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Express Loss as Percentage**

$$
P_{loss} \% = (1 - \eta_g) \times 100\% = (1 - 0.96) \times 100\% = 4\%
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,P_{out} = 48 \text{ kW}, \quad P_{loss} = 2 \text{ kW} \quad (4\% \text{ loss})\,}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: EV Powertrain with Gearbox

> [!NOTE]
> This block-level diagram maps the energy and signal flow from the battery to the wheels, showing the precise position of the gearbox in the EV drivetrain.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    battery1[BATTERY PACK] --> inv1[MOTOR CONTROLLER INVERTER]
    inv1 --> motor1[TRACTION MOTOR]
    motor1 -- "omega m, T m" --> gb1[SINGLE SPEED GEARBOX RATIO 9 to 1]
    gb1 -- "omega out, T out" --> diff1[DIFFERENTIAL]
    diff1 --> ws1[LEFT WHEEL]
    diff1 --> ws2[RIGHT WHEEL]
    ws1 -- "F tractive" --> road1[ROAD SURFACE]
    ws2 -- "F tractive" --> road1
    motor1 -. "speed feedback" .-> inv1
    gb1 -. "oil temperature feedback" .-> ecm1[VEHICLE ECU]
    ecm1 -. "torque command" .-> inv1
```

**Diagram Explanation:**
- The **battery** delivers DC power to the inverter.
- The **inverter** converts DC to 3-phase AC and controls the motor.
- The **motor** produces mechanical rotational output.
- The **gearbox** reduces speed and multiplies torque.
- The **differential** splits torque to left and right wheels during cornering.
- The **ECU** monitors gearbox temperature and adjusts motor torque to prevent overheating.

---

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram: 2-Speed EV Gearbox Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    motorA[ELECTRIC MOTOR] --> clutchA[CLUTCH 1 LOW GEAR]
    motorA --> clutchB[CLUTCH 2 HIGH GEAR]
    clutchA --> gearA[GEAR PAIR 9.34 to 1]
    clutchB --> gearB[GEAR PAIR 4.81 to 1]
    gearA --> outputA[COMMON OUTPUT SHAFT]
    gearB --> outputA
    outputA --> finalA[FINAL DRIVE]
    finalA --> wheelsA[DRIVE WHEELS]
    ecmA[ECU SHIFT LOGIC] -. "actuates" .-> clutchA
    ecmA -. "actuates" .-> clutchB
```

**Explanation:** Under launch conditions, the ECU engages Clutch 1 (low gear) for maximum torque. Once vehicle speed crosses a threshold (e.g., 80 km/h), Clutch 1 disengages and Clutch 2 (high gear) engages to enable efficient high-speed cruising.

---

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram: Planetary Gear Set (Hybrid Synergy Drive)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sunA[SUN GEAR] --> ringA[RING GEAR]
    ringA --> carrierA[PLANET CARRIER]
    carA[CARRIER] --> outputSH[OUTPUT TO WHEELS]
    sunA -. input .- mg1A[MG1 MOTOR GENERATOR]
    carrierA -. input .- mg2A[MG2 MOTOR GENERATOR]
    ringA -. input .- iceA[ICE INPUT]
```

**Explanation:** The planetary gear set in Toyota's Hybrid Synergy Drive has three ports — sun, ring, and carrier. The ICE, MG1, and MG2 connect to these ports. The kinematic relationship is:

$$
(1 + R) \cdot \omega_{carrier} + \omega_{sun} = (1 + R) \cdot \omega_{ring} \cdot \frac{R}{1+R}
$$

where $R = N_{ring}/N_{sun}$ is the ring-to-sun tooth ratio (typically $R \approx 2.5$–$3.0$ in Toyota systems).

---

## 4.4 Gearbox Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[START VEHICLE DESIGN] --> q1{TOP SPEED GREATER THAN 180 KMH}
    q1 -- YES --> twoA[USE 2 SPEED GEARBOX]
    q1 -- NO --> q2{PERFORMANCE PRIORITY}
    q2 -- YES --> q3{MOTOR RPM GREATER THAN 15000}
    q3 -- YES --> twoA
    q3 -- NO --> singleA[USE SINGLE SPEED REDUCER]
    q2 -- NO --> singleA
    twoA --> cost1[HIGHER COST COMPLEX]
    singleA --> cost2[LOW COST COMPACT]
```

---

## 4.5 Torque-Speed Characteristic with Gear Ratio

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    motorCurve[MOTOR T max 250 NM at 0 RPM T drops above base speed 4500 RPM] --> gbEffect[GEARBOX MULTIPLIES TORQUE BY 9.5 MULTIPLIES SPEED DIVIDES BY 9.5]
    gbEffect --> wheelCurve[WHEEL T max 2375 NM at 0 to 474 RPM WHEEL HIGH TORQUE LOW SPEED]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are typical 3-mark KTU Part A questions. Memorize crisp, one-paragraph answers.

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Explain the role of a gearbox in an Electric Vehicle. Why is a single-speed reducer preferred in most modern EVs?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The gearbox in an EV is responsible for **speed reduction and torque multiplication** between the high-RPM traction motor and the low-RPM drive wheels. It adapts the motor's wide constant-power region to the vehicle's tractive effort requirements. A **single-speed reducer** is preferred in most modern EVs because:
1. Electric motors produce **maximum torque at zero RPM**, eliminating the need for multiple gears during launch.
2. The motor's **wide constant-power region** allows a single ratio to cover both low-speed and high-speed operation.
3. It results in **higher efficiency (96–98%)**, **lower cost**, **less weight**, and **zero shift shock**.
4. It is **compact and reliable** with no synchronization or actuator system.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**With a neat block diagram, explain the position of the gearbox in a typical EV powertrain.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In a typical EV powertrain, the gearbox is positioned **between the traction motor and the differential**. The power flow is:

$$
\text{Battery} \rightarrow \text{Inverter} \rightarrow \text{Motor} \rightarrow \boxed{\text{Gearbox}} \rightarrow \text{Differential} \rightarrow \text{Wheels}
$$

The gearbox reduces the high rotational speed of the motor (typically 10,000–15,000 RPM) to a usable wheel speed (typically 500–1500 RPM) while multiplying the torque proportionally. The fixed gear ratio for most mass-market EVs is between **8:1 and 10:1**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part B questions carry 14 marks. Below, you have **Question A** and **Question B** — answer either one in the exam.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Derive the relation between output torque, input torque, gear ratio, and gearbox efficiency. State the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An electric vehicle uses a single-speed gearbox with ratio $i_g = 9.5$ and efficiency $\eta = 0.97$. The traction motor delivers a maximum torque of $T_m = 280$ N·m at 0 RPM and runs up to $N_m = 12{,}000$ RPM. The tire effective radius is $r = 0.32$ m. Calculate:
- (i) Maximum torque at the wheel.
- (ii) Maximum tractive force at the tire-road interface.
- (iii) Maximum vehicle speed in km/h. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Assumptions:**
1. The gearbox is a single-stage, fixed-ratio unit.
2. Power loss occurs only in the meshing gears (friction at tooth contact).
3. Bearings and seals are frictionless for ideal derivation; losses lumped into $\eta_g$.
4. No slip between meshing gear teeth.
5. Input and output shafts are coaxial.

**Derivation:**

$$
P_{in} = T_{in} \cdot \omega_{in} \quad \text{and} \quad P_{out} = T_{out} \cdot \omega_{out}
$$

By definition of efficiency:

$$
\eta_g = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{T_{out} \cdot \omega_{out}}{T_{in} \cdot \omega_{in}}
$$

Since gear ratio $i_g = \omega_{in} / \omega_{out}$, we have $\omega_{out}/\omega_{in} = 1/i_g$. Substituting:

$$
\eta_g = \frac{T_{out}}{T_{in}} \cdot \frac{1}{i_g}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\boxed{\,T_{out} = T_{in} \cdot i_g \cdot \eta_g\,}
$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Maximum torque at the wheel:**

$$
T_{wheel} = T_m \cdot i_g \cdot \eta
$$

$$
T_{wheel} = 280 \times 9.5 \times 0.97
$$

$$
T_{wheel} = 280 \times 9.215
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T_{wheel} \approx 2580.2 \text{ N·m}\,}
$$

**(ii) Maximum tractive force:**

$$
F_t = \frac{T_{wheel}}{r} = \frac{2580.2}{0.32}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,F_t \approx 8063.1 \text{ N} \approx 8.06 \text{ kN}\,}
$$

**(iii) Maximum vehicle speed:**

$$
\omega_m = \frac{2 \pi N_m}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 12{,}000}{60} = 400\pi \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
\omega_{wheel} = \frac{\omega_m}{i_g} = \frac{400\pi}{9.5} \approx 132.28 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
v = \omega_{wheel} \cdot r = 132.28 \times 0.32 \approx 42.33 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
v_{km/h} = 42.33 \times 3.6
$$

$$
\boxed{\,v_{max} \approx 152.4 \text{ km/h}\,}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** Compare single-speed and two-speed gearboxes used in electric vehicles. Discuss the relative advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2-speed gearbox has gear ratios $i_1 = 9.34$ (1st gear) and $i_2 = 4.81$ (2nd gear). The motor produces a peak torque of $T_m = 320$ N·m at 0 RPM. Gearbox efficiency is 96% in 1st gear and 98% in 2nd gear. Tire radius is $r = 0.33$ m. Calculate:
- (i) Maximum tractive force in 1st gear.
- (ii) If the motor runs up to 14,000 RPM, calculate maximum vehicle speed in 1st gear and 2nd gear. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Comparison (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Single-Speed | Two-Speed |
|-----------|--------------|-----------|
| Number of ratios | One fixed ratio | Two selectable ratios |
| Complexity | Very low | Moderate (clutch/actuator) |
| Efficiency | 96–98% | 94–98% (per gear) |
| Launch torque | Limited by single ratio | Higher (lower ratio) |
| Top speed | Limited by motor max RPM | Higher (higher gear) |
| Weight | Light | Heavier |
| Cost | Low | Higher |
| NVH | Silent (no shift) | Possible shift shock |
| Best for | City/commuter EVs | Performance/luxury EVs |

**Single-Speed Advantage:** Simplicity, reliability, low cost, high efficiency.
**Single-Speed Limitation:** Cannot fully optimize motor efficiency at all speeds.
**Two-Speed Advantage:** Better efficiency across full speed range, higher top speed, stronger launch.
**Two-Speed Limitation:** Added complexity, weight, and shift-actuation control logic.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Maximum tractive force in 1st gear:**

$$
T_{wheel,1} = T_m \cdot i_1 \cdot \eta_1 = 320 \times 9.34 \times 0.96
$$

$$
T_{wheel,1} = 320 \times 8.9664 = 2869.25 \text{ N·m}
$$

$$
F_{t,1} = \frac{T_{wheel,1}}{r} = \frac{2869.25}{0.33}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,F_{t,1} \approx 8694.7 \text{ N} \approx 8.69 \text{ kN}\,}
$$

**(ii) Maximum vehicle speed in both gears:**

Motor angular velocity:

$$
\omega_m = \frac{2 \pi \times 14{,}000}{60} = \frac{28{,}000 \pi}{60} \approx 1466.08 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**In 1st gear:**

$$
\omega_{wheel,1} = \frac{1466.08}{9.34} \approx 156.97 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
v_1 = 156.97 \times 0.33 \approx 51.80 \text{ m/s} \approx 186.5 \text{ km/h}
$$

**In 2nd gear:**

$$
\omega_{wheel,2} = \frac{1466.08}{4.81} \approx 304.80 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
v_2 = 304.80 \times 0.33 \approx 100.58 \text{ m/s} \approx 362.1 \text{ km/h}
$$

(Real-world top speed would be limited by tire rating and aerodynamic drag.)

$$
\boxed{\,v_{1,max} \approx 186.5 \text{ km/h}, \quad v_{2,max} \approx 362.1 \text{ km/h (theoretical)}\,}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting efficiency in the torque formula** — Most students write $T_{out} = T_m \cdot i_g$ and lose 1–2 marks. Always include $\eta_g$ unless explicitly told otherwise.
> 2. **Unit conversion errors in speed** — Remember: $\text{RPM} \to \text{rad/s}$ uses $\omega = 2\pi N/60$, and m/s → km/h uses factor 3.6.
> 3. **Confusing $i_g = N_2/N_1$ with $N_1/N_2$** — Always: $i_g$ = driven/driver = (output reduction).
> 4. **Skipping units in the final answer** — Always write N·m, m/s, km/h, etc. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions in derivations** — This is a 1-mark loss in 7-mark derivations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Read this list 5 times before every KTU exam. These are the highest-yield facts.**

- ✅ A **gearbox** in an EV is a **speed-reducing, torque-multiplying** device between the motor and the wheels.
- ✅ The fundamental relation is $\mathbf{T_{out} = T_{in} \cdot i_g \cdot \eta_g}$ and $\mathbf{\omega_{out} = \omega_{in} / i_g}$.
- ✅ The gear ratio $\mathbf{i_g = N_2 / N_1}$ (driven / driver) — **never** the other way around.
- ✅ Typical EV single-speed ratio: **8:1 to 12:1**.
- ✅ Typical EV gearbox efficiency: **96% – 98%** (single-stage).
- ✅ Power loss: $\mathbf{P_{loss} = P_{in}(1 - \eta_g)}$.
- ✅ For a multi-stage gearbox, **total ratio = product of individual stage ratios**.
- ✅ Modern mass-market EVs use **single-speed reducers**; performance EVs use **2-speed gearboxes**.
- ✅ Hybrid Synergy Drives (Toyota THS) use a **planetary gear set** for power-split.
- ✅ Vehicle speed from motor RPM: $v = \dfrac{2 \pi N_m}{60} \cdot \dfrac{r_{wheel}}{i_g \cdot i_0}$ in m/s, then multiply by 3.6 for km/h.
- ✅ Tractive force: $\mathbf{F_t = T_{wheel} / r_{wheel}}$.
- ✅ A gearbox **multiplies torque and divides speed** — power is conserved (minus efficiency loss).
- ✅ Gears with **helical teeth** are quieter and more efficient than **spur gears** but cost more.
- ✅ For planetary gear sets: $i_{sun-to-carrier} = 1 + R$ where $R = N_{ring}/N_{sun}$.
- ✅ Minimum number of teeth to avoid interference: $N_{min} \geq 2 / \sin^2\phi$ (for $\phi = 20°$, $N_{min} \approx 17$).
- ✅ Standard module sizes (AGMA): 1, 1.25, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10 mm.
- ✅ Common pressure angle: **20°** (full-depth teeth).
- ✅ Always convert RPM to rad/s using $\omega = 2\pi N/60$ in derivations.
- ✅ Always state **assumptions** in 7-mark derivation questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Different Kinds of Gearboxes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT631 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Different Kinds of Gearboxes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **gearbox** (also called a **transmission**) in an Electric or Hybrid Electric Vehicle is a **mechanical power transmission device** that converts the torque and speed characteristics of an electric traction motor (or a combination of motor and engine in HEVs) into a form suitable for propelling the vehicle wheels. It alters the **torque–speed relationship** at the output shaft by meshing gears of differing sizes, while transmitting mechanical power with minimal loss.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, the term *gearbox* specifically refers to the **gear reduction unit** placed between the traction motor and the final drive (differential/wheels) of an EV/HEV, including both **fixed-ratio** and **variable-ratio** topologies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Highlight:** In EVs, the gearbox is often a **single-speed reducer** because the electric motor inherently offers a wide constant-power speed range. In HEVs, especially **power-split hybrids** (e.g., Toyota Hybrid Synergy Drive), an **epicyclic (planetary) gear set** acts as an **electromechanical continuously variable transmission (e-CVT)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a gearbox as a **bicycle's gear shifter**:

- When you climb a hill, you shift to a **low gear** — pedaling becomes *easier* (more torque) but your feet move *faster* than the wheels rotate.
- When you ride on a flat road at high speed, you shift to a **high gear** — pedaling becomes *harder* (less torque) but each pedal stroke covers *more distance*.

A vehicle gearbox does exactly the same thing, except it converts **electric motor torque/speed** into **wheel torque/speed** automatically, based on driving conditions. The difference in EVs is that the electric motor is so flexible (high torque from 0 RPM) that a **single gear** is often enough — much like an e-bike that never needs shifting.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world example:** A **Tesla Model 3** uses a **single-speed fixed-ratio gearbox (~9:1)**. A **Porsche Taycan** uses a **2-speed gearbox** for the rear axle to improve both launch acceleration and top-end efficiency. A **Toyota Prius** uses a **planetary gear set** as a power-split device to seamlessly blend ICE and MG1/MG2 outputs.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are used in gearbox design for EVs/HEVs:

- **Gear ratio (N):** dimensionless, typically between **6:1 and 12:1** for single-speed EV reducers
- **Gearbox efficiency ($\eta_g$):** typically **0.95 – 0.98** for helical/spur gear sets
- **Specific torque density:** measured in **Nm/kg**, target $\geq$ **100 Nm/kg** for modern EV reducers
- **Maximum motor speed:** commonly **10,000 – 20,000 RPM** for EV traction motors
- **Vehicle top speed:** typically **150 – 250 km/h** for passenger EVs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** The fundamental job of any EV/HEV gearbox is the equation $T_{wheel} = T_{motor} \times N \times \eta_g$ and $\omega_{wheel} = \frac{\omega_{motor}}{N}$, trading speed for torque (or vice versa) while conserving power (ideally).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Does an EV/HEV Need a Gearbox?

Although electric motors deliver rated torque from standstill, they have **two inherent limitations**:

1. **Maximum speed limit:** Motor RPM is bounded by mechanical and electrical constraints (centrifugal force, bearing limits, inverter switching frequency).
2. **Constant-power region:** Above base speed, torque falls as $T \propto 1/\omega$, so available torque decreases rapidly at highway speeds.

A gearbox addresses both by:
- **Stepping down** motor speed to a usable wheel speed range.
- **Stepping up** torque to overcome rolling resistance, aerodynamic drag, and grade resistance.
- Allowing the motor to operate in its **high-efficiency sweet spot** across varying road conditions.

## 2.2 Classification of Gearboxes Used in EVs/HEVs

### A. Single-Speed Fixed-Ratio Gearbox (Most Common in Modern EVs)

**Operating Principle:** A single gear reduction — typically helical or planetary — connects the motor to the differential. No gear shifting occurs.

**Characteristics:**
- **Ratio range:** 6:1 to 12:1 (commonly ~9:1)
- **Components:** Helical pinion + ring gear, or simple planetary stage
- **Examples:** Tesla Model S/3/X/Y, Nissan Leaf, Chevrolet Bolt, Tata Nexon EV

**Why it's preferred:**
- Electric motors produce **peak torque from 0 RPM**, eliminating the need for a launch gear.
- Motor speed range (0–18,000 RPM) is broad enough to cover the entire vehicle speed range after a single reduction.
- Simpler, lighter, more efficient, and cheaper than multi-speed units.

### B. Multi-Speed Gearbox (2-Speed, 3-Speed)

**Operating Principle:** Multiple discrete gear ratios selected by synchronizers or dog clutches, controlled either manually or by an **actuator (electric/hydraulic)**.

**Characteristics:**
- **2-speed** examples: Porsche Taycan (rear), Rimac Nevera
- **3-speed** examples: Some concept EVs, Ford Mustang Mach-E (GT Performance trim)
- Shifting strategy: shift point based on **motor speed, accelerator pedal position, and vehicle speed**

**Advantages:**
- Optimizes motor operation in efficiency map for both **launch** and **high-speed cruising**.
- Better acceleration in lower gear, better economy in higher gear.

**Disadvantages:**
- Added complexity, weight, cost, and shift-quality concerns.
- Requires shift-by-wire or hydraulic actuation system.

### C. Automated Manual Transmission (AMT)

**Operating Principle:** A conventional manual gearbox whose clutch and shift actuators are replaced by **electro-mechanical or electro-hydraulic actuators**, controlled by an **ECU (TCU)**.

**Characteristics:**
- Essentially a manual gearbox with **no clutch pedal**.
- Used in some entry-level EVs/HEVs (e.g., early Maruti hybrid concepts).
- Shifts cause **noticeable torque interruption** ("shift shock").

### D. Dual-Clutch Transmission (DCT)

**Operating Principle:** Two separate clutches for **odd and even gear sets**, enabling **pre-selection** of the next gear and **back-to-back shifts** within ~200 ms with no torque interruption.

**Characteristics:**
- Used in performance-oriented HEVs (e.g., Volkswagen Golf GTE, some BMW PHEVs).
- Requires precise clutch pressure and synchronization control.
- High efficiency (similar to manual) with the convenience of automatic.

### E. Continuously Variable Transmission (CVT)

**Operating Principle:** Uses a **variable-diameter pulley system** (belt/chain) or **toroidal** rollers to provide an **infinite** number of ratios between minimum and maximum values.

**Characteristics:**
- Ratio is varied continuously based on driver demand.
- In EVs, mechanical CVTs are rare; instead, the **planetary gear "e-CVT"** of Toyota's THS simulates a CVT.
- Pulley-based CVTs are limited by belt strength (torque capacity).

### F. Planetary (Epicyclic) Gear Set — Power-Split Device

**Operating Principle:** A planetary gear set has a **sun gear**, **planet gears** (on a carrier), and a **ring (annulus) gear**. With one component fixed and another as input, the third becomes output with a precise ratio. When **two components are driven independently** (e.g., by ICE and MG1), the third becomes the **output** — this is the principle of a **power-split hybrid transmission**.

**Characteristics:**
- Provides **two degrees of freedom**, enabling power blending.
- Used as the **core element** in Toyota Hybrid System (THS), Lexus Hybrid Drive, Ford Hybrid, etc.
- Acts as an **electromechanical CVT** with effectively infinite ratios.
- MG1 controls the ring gear speed, modulating the ICE's effective ratio.

### G. Infinitely Variable Transmission (IVT) — Toroidal / Full-Toroidal

**Operating Principle:** Uses **rollers and discs** to transmit power through contact friction, providing a true $0 \rightarrow \infty$ ratio range (including overdrive and reverse).

**Characteristics:**
- High efficiency at fixed ratios; variable efficiency during ratio changes.
- Used in niche automotive applications (e.g., some Nissan systems historically).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Equation** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gear ratio (numerical) | $N = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \dfrac{\omega_1}{\omega_2}$ | Ratio of driven to driver | dimensionless |
| Wheel torque | $T_w = T_m \cdot N \cdot \eta_g$ | Torque delivered to wheels | Nm |
| Wheel angular speed | $\omega_w = \dfrac{\omega_m}{N}$ | Wheel rotational speed | rad/s |
| Vehicle speed | $v = \omega_w \cdot r_w = \dfrac{\omega_m \cdot r_w}{N}$ | Linear speed of vehicle | m/s |
| Power transmitted | $P = T_m \cdot \omega_m = \dfrac{T_w \cdot \omega_w}{\eta_g}$ | Input power equals output + loss | W |
| Tractive force | $F_t = \dfrac{T_w}{r_w} = \dfrac{T_m \cdot N \cdot \eta_g}{r_w}$ | Force at wheel–road contact | N |
| Grade ability | $\sin\theta = \dfrac{F_t - F_r}{m \cdot g}$ | Maximum climb angle | dimensionless |
| Acceleration | $a = \dfrac{F_t - F_r - F_{ad}}{m}$ | Longitudinal acceleration | m/s² |
| Planetary gear ratio (basic) | $N = 1 + \dfrac{N_r}{N_s}$ | Sun-ring-carrier arrangement | dimensionless |
| Overall driveline ratio | $N_{od} = N_{gb} \times N_{fd}$ | Gearbox × final drive | dimensionless |

Where:
- $N_1, N_2$ = number of teeth on driving and driven gears
- $T_1, T_2$ = torques on driving and driven gears
- $\omega_1, \omega_2$ = angular speeds of driving and driven gears
- $T_m, \omega_m$ = motor torque and angular speed
- $r_w$ = wheel radius
- $F_r$ = rolling resistance force $\approx m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr}$
- $F_{ad}$ = aerodynamic drag $\approx \tfrac{1}{2} \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2$

> [!NOTE]
> **For tables:** Absolute values, conditions, and ratios use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the vertical pipe symbol to preserve the markdown table structure. The above table is portable across all markdown renderers.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The choice of gearbox architecture directly impacts:

- **Vehicle 0–100 km/h time** (lower gear = better launch)
- **Top speed** (higher gear = better high-speed efficiency)
- **Energy consumption (Wh/km)** — single-speed units typically win in stop-and-go city cycles
- **NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness)** — helical and planetary units are quieter than spur
- **Cost and packaging** — single-speed units are the cheapest and most compact
- **Driving feel** — multi-speed units can simulate "engine braking" via regen on downshifts

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Fundamental Gear Ratio Equation

**Statement:** Two meshing gears share the same pitch-line velocity at the point of contact.

**Step 1:** The pitch-line velocity of gear 1 equals:
$$v_1 = r_1 \cdot \omega_1$$

**Step 2:** The pitch-line velocity of gear 2 equals:
$$v_2 = r_2 \cdot \omega_2$$

**Step 3:** At the meshing point, no slip occurs, so $v_1 = v_2$:
$$r_1 \cdot \omega_1 = r_2 \cdot \omega_2$$

**Step 4:** Rearranging for the ratio of angular speeds:
$$\frac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = \frac{r_2}{r_1}$$

**Step 5:** Since gear pitch (module) is constant, $r \propto N$ (number of teeth). Therefore:
$$\frac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = N$$

This is the **fundamental gear ratio equation**, with the ratio $N$ being the number of driven-gear teeth divided by the number of driving-gear teeth.

## 3.2 Derivation: Torque Multiplication in a Gearbox

**Statement:** Power is conserved (minus losses) across a gear mesh.

**Step 1:** Input power equals output power plus losses:
$$P_{in} \cdot \eta_g = P_{out}$$

**Step 2:** Substituting $P = T \cdot \omega$:
$$T_1 \cdot \omega_1 \cdot \eta_g = T_2 \cdot \omega_2$$

**Step 3:** Using $\dfrac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = N$, substitute $\omega_1 = N \cdot \omega_2$:
$$T_1 \cdot N \cdot \omega_2 \cdot \eta_g = T_2 \cdot \omega_2$$

**Step 4:** Cancelling $\omega_2$ on both sides:
$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot N \cdot \eta_g$$

**Step 5:** Applying to the EV driveline (motor → gearbox → wheels):
$$T_w = T_m \cdot N_{gb} \cdot \eta_{gb} \cdot N_{fd} \cdot \eta_{fd}$$

This proves that **torque is multiplied by the gear ratio**, allowing a relatively small motor to deliver high wheel torque.

## 3.3 Derivation: Planetary Gear Ratio (Willis Equation)

**Statement:** A planetary gear set has a sun gear (S), planet carrier (C), and ring gear (R).

**Step 1:** Basic kinematic constraint (sum of tooth movements equals zero for internal meshing):
$$(N_s + N_r) \cdot \omega_c = N_s \cdot \omega_s + N_r \cdot \omega_r$$

**Step 2:** Divide both sides by $N_s + N_r$:
$$\omega_c = \frac{N_s \cdot \omega_s + N_r \cdot \omega_r}{N_s + N_r}$$

**Step 3:** Fix the ring (R) to ground: $\omega_r = 0$. Then:
$$\omega_c = \frac{N_s}{N_s + N_r} \cdot \omega_s$$

**Step 4:** Gear ratio from sun (input) to carrier (output) with ring fixed:
$$N = \frac{\omega_s}{\omega_c} = 1 + \frac{N_r}{N_s}$$

**Step 5:** For the Toyota THS-style power split:
- Sun gear driven by **MG1** (speed $\omega_{MG1}$)
- Carrier driven by **ICE** (speed $\omega_{ICE}$)
- Ring gear drives the driveline via **MG2** (speed $\omega_{ring}$)

The kinematic relationship becomes:
$$(N_s + N_r) \cdot \omega_{carrier} = N_s \cdot \omega_{sun} + N_r \cdot \omega_{ring}$$

This single equation is what enables the **seamless power blending** between ICE, MG1, and MG2 in a Toyota Hybrid Synergy Drive.

## 3.4 Derivation: Maximum Grade Climbing Ability

**Statement:** At low vehicle speeds, aerodynamic drag is negligible, and the tractive force available must overcome rolling resistance plus gravitational component.

**Step 1:** Maximum tractive force from the motor–gearbox system:
$$F_{t,\max} = \frac{T_{m,\max} \cdot N_{gb} \cdot \eta_{gb}}{r_w}$$

**Step 2:** Force balance on a slope of angle $\theta$:
$$F_{t,\max} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta + m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} \cdot \cos\theta$$

**Step 3:** Solving for $\sin\theta$:
$$\sin\theta = \frac{F_{t,\max}}{m \cdot g} - C_{rr} \cdot \cos\theta$$

**Step 4:** For small $\theta$, $\cos\theta \approx 1$ and $\theta \approx \sin\theta$ in radians:
$$\theta_{\max} \approx \frac{F_{t,\max} - m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr}}{m \cdot g} \text{ [radians]}$$

**Step 5:** Converting to percentage grade:
$$\text{Grade (\%)} = 100 \times \tan\theta_{\max}$$

This shows that **a higher gear ratio directly improves grade-climbing ability** — a key reason why EVs benefit from even a single fixed reduction.

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** An EV has the following specifications:
- Motor peak torque: $T_m = 250$ Nm
- Motor peak speed: $\omega_m = 10{,}000$ RPM
- Single-speed gear ratio: $N = 9.5$
- Gearbox efficiency: $\eta_g = 0.96$
- Wheel radius: $r_w = 0.33$ m
- Vehicle mass: $m = 1800$ kg
- Coefficient of rolling resistance: $C_{rr} = 0.012$

**Find:** (a) Wheel torque, (b) Maximum tractive force, (c) Maximum climb angle, (d) Vehicle top speed.

**Solution (a): Wheel torque**
$$T_w = T_m \cdot N \cdot \eta_g = 250 \times 9.5 \times 0.96 = 2280 \text{ Nm}$$

**Solution (b): Maximum tractive force**
$$F_t = \frac{T_w}{r_w} = \frac{2280}{0.33} = 6909 \text{ N}$$

**Solution (c): Maximum climb angle**
$$\sin\theta = \frac{F_t}{m \cdot g} - C_{rr} = \frac{6909}{1800 \times 9.81} - 0.012$$
$$\sin\theta = 0.3913 - 0.012 = 0.3793$$
$$\theta = \arcsin(0.3793) \approx 22.3^{\circ} \quad (\approx 41\% \text{ grade})$$

**Solution (d): Vehicle top speed**

First, wheel angular speed at motor peak:
$$\omega_w = \frac{\omega_m}{N} = \frac{10{,}000}{9.5} = 1052.6 \text{ RPM} = 110.2 \text{ rad/s}$$

Then, vehicle speed:
$$v_{\max} = \omega_w \cdot r_w = 110.2 \times 0.33 = 36.4 \text{ m/s} = 131 \text{ km/h}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** If a top speed of 200 km/h were required, the gear ratio would need to be reduced to ~6.2 — but this would sacrifice launch performance. This trade-off is precisely why multi-speed gearboxes exist in performance EVs.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of EV/HEV Drivetrains

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Battery Pack] --> B[Power Inverter]
    B --> C[Traction Motor]
    C --> D[Gearbox Type]
    D --> E[Final Drive Differential]
    E --> F[Wheels]
    G[Brake Pedal] --> H[Regen Brake Controller]
    H --> C
    D --> I[Gearbox Type 1: Single Speed Reducer]
    D --> J[Gearbox Type 2: Multi Speed Transmission]
    D --> K[Gearbox Type 3: Planetary Power Split Device]
    D --> L[Gearbox Type 4: CVT]
    D --> M[Gearbox Type 5: AMT or DCT]
```

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart: Selecting the Right Gearbox for an EV/HEV

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start Vehicle Type Selection] --> Q1{Is it a pure EV?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Need high top speed >200 kmph?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Series or Parallel HEV?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Multi1[Use 2 Speed or 3 Speed Gearbox]
    Q2 -- No --> Single1[Use Single Speed Fixed Reducer]
    Q3 -- Series HEV --> Single2[Use Single Speed Reducer]
    Q3 -- Parallel or Power Split --> Q4{Need seamless blending?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> Planetary1[Use Planetary Power Split eCVT]
    Q4 -- No --> Parallel1[Use AMT or DCT]
    Multi1 --> End[Selected Configuration]
    Single1 --> End
    Single2 --> End
    Planetary1 --> End
    Parallel1 --> End
```

## 4.3 Planetary Gear Set Schematic (Used in Toyota THS)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ICE[Internal Combustion Engine] --> CARRIER[Planet Carrier]
    MG1[MG1 Motor Generator] --> SUN[Sun Gear]
    CARRIER --> RING[Ring Gear Output]
    SUN --> RING
    RING --> MG2[MG2 Motor Generator]
    MG2 --> WHEELS[Driveline to Wheels]
    SUN -. Power Flow .-> RING
    CARRIER -. Mechanical Path .-> RING
```

## 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram: Single-Speed vs Multi-Speed Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Single Speed Topology]
        M1[Traction Motor] --> G1[Fixed Gear 9 to 1] --> D1[Differential] --> W1[Wheels]
    end
    subgraph B[Multi Speed Topology]
        M2[Traction Motor] --> G2[Gearbox 2 or 3 Ratios] --> D2[Differential] --> W2[Wheels]
        G2 --> ECU[TCU Shift Controller]
        ECU --> G2
    end
```

## 4.5 Power Flow Comparison: Toyota THS Power-Split Hybrid

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BAT[Battery] --- INV[Inverter]
    INV --- MG1A[MG1]
    INV --- MG2A[MG2]
    ICE2[ICE Engine] --- PLAN[Planetary Gear Set]
    MG1A --- PLAN
    PLAN --- MG2A
    MG2A --- DIF[Final Drive]
    DIF --- WH[Wheels]
    MG1A -. Controls Sun .-> PLAN
    ICE2 -. Drives Carrier .-> PLAN
    PLAN -. Outputs to Ring .-> MG2A
```

## 4.6 Comparative Performance Matrix of Gearbox Types

| **Gearbox Type** | **Efficiency** | **Complexity** | **Weight** | **Cost** | **Typical Application** | **Shift Quality** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Single-Speed Reducer | **0.96 – 0.98** | Low | Low | Low | Tesla, Nissan Leaf, Chevy Bolt | Seamless (no shift) |
| 2-Speed Gearbox | 0.94 – 0.96 | Medium | Medium | Medium | Porsche Taycan, Rimac | Good (fast shifts) |
| 3-Speed Gearbox | 0.92 – 0.95 | High | High | High | Concept EVs, Ford Mach-E GT | Good (with TCU) |
| AMT | 0.93 – 0.96 | Medium | Medium | Low | Entry-level HEVs | Poor (shift shock) |
| DCT | 0.94 – 0.97 | High | High | High | VW Golf GTE, BMW PHEV | Excellent (seamless) |
| CVT (Mechanical) | 0.85 – 0.92 | Medium | Medium | Medium | Niche ICE vehicles | Smooth (rubber-band) |
| Planetary eCVT (THS) | **0.95 – 0.98** | Medium | Medium | Medium | Toyota, Lexus, Ford hybrids | Seamless (blended) |
| Toroidal IVT | 0.88 – 0.95 | High | High | Very High | Niche applications | Smooth |

> [!NOTE]
> **Legend:** All numerical values are typical ranges from published automotive engineering literature. For a single value, the highlighted entries indicate the **highest** efficiency and **lowest** complexity — key reasons these dominate modern EV design.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Q: Why do most modern Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs) use a single-speed gearbox instead of a multi-speed transmission? Mention two key reasons.**

**Model Answer:**
Most modern BEVs use a single-speed gearbox because:
1. **Wide motor speed range:** Electric traction motors can operate efficiently from 0 RPM to 15,000–20,000 RPM and deliver rated (or even peak) torque from standstill, so a single fixed reduction suffices across the entire vehicle speed range.
2. **Elimination of launch gear requirement:** Unlike ICEs that need a low gear for launch (since ICEs produce zero torque at 0 RPM), EVs do not need a launch gear — eliminating the necessity for clutch and multiple ratios.
3. **Reduced cost, weight, and complexity:** A single-speed unit improves efficiency (typically 96–98%) and reduces NVH, packaging volume, and manufacturing cost compared to multi-speed units.

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Q: What is the function of the planetary gear set in Toyota's Hybrid Synergy Drive (THS)?**

**Model Answer:**
In Toyota's THS, the planetary gear set acts as a **power-split device** that mechanically blends the power from the **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** with the electrical power from **MG1** and **MG2**. The sun gear is connected to MG1 (acting as a generator/motor), the carrier is driven by the ICE, and the ring gear drives the driveline through MG2. By controlling the speed of MG1, the system effectively acts as a **continuously variable transmission (e-CVT)**, allowing the ICE to operate at its most efficient point regardless of vehicle speed — providing seamless, stepless ratio variation.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Q1 (a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **classification of gearboxes** used in electric and hybrid electric vehicles. Compare the single-speed reducer with the 2-speed gearbox in terms of efficiency, complexity, and application. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a):**

**Block Diagram:**

```
Traction Motor → [Gearbox: Single-Speed OR Multi-Speed] → Final Drive → Wheels
```

**Classification of Gearboxes Used in EVs/HEVs:**

1. **Single-Speed Fixed-Ratio Gearbox** — Used in pure BEVs; ratio typically 6:1 to 12:1; helical or planetary construction; efficiency ~96–98%.
2. **Multi-Speed Gearbox (2/3-speed)** — Used in performance EVs (Porsche Taycan, Rimac); provides launch and high-speed optimization.
3. **Automated Manual Transmission (AMT)** — Manual gearbox with electro-mechanical actuators; used in entry-level HEVs.
4. **Dual-Clutch Transmission (DCT)** — Two clutches for odd/even gears; pre-selects next gear for seamless shift; used in performance HEVs.
5. **Continuously Variable Transmission (CVT)** — Variable-diameter pulleys; rare in modern EVs.
6. **Planetary Power-Split Device (e-CVT)** — Epicyclic gear set in Toyota THS; enables infinite ratio blending of ICE and electric paths.
7. **Toroidal IVT** — Roller-disc contact transmission; used in niche applications.

**Comparison Table:**

| **Feature** | **Single-Speed Reducer** | **2-Speed Gearbox** |
|---|---|---|
| Efficiency | 96 – 98% | 94 – 96% |
| Complexity | Very low | Medium (shift actuators needed) |
| Weight | Light | Heavier |
| Cost | Low | Higher |
| NVH | Excellent | Good (with proper synchronization) |
| Application | Tesla, Nissan Leaf | Porsche Taycan, Rimac Nevera |

**Q1 (b)** An EV uses a **permanent magnet synchronous motor** with the following specifications:
- Peak motor torque: $T_m = 320$ Nm
- Peak motor speed: $N_m = 12{,}000$ RPM
- Gear ratio: $N_{gb} = 8.5$
- Gearbox efficiency: $\eta_g = 0.97$
- Wheel radius: $r_w = 0.32$ m
- Vehicle mass: $m = 1500$ kg
- Rolling resistance coefficient: $C_{rr} = 0.010$
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

Calculate: **(i)** Wheel torque, **(ii)** Maximum tractive force, **(iii)** Maximum grade-climbing angle in degrees, **(iv)** Top vehicle speed in km/h. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part b):**

**(i) Wheel Torque:**
$$T_w = T_m \cdot N_{gb} \cdot \eta_g = 320 \times 8.5 \times 0.97 = 2638.4 \text{ Nm}$$

**(ii) Maximum Tractive Force:**
$$F_t = \frac{T_w}{r_w} = \frac{2638.4}{0.32} = 8245 \text{ N}$$

**(iii) Maximum Grade-Climbing Angle:**

Rolling resistance force:
$$F_r = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.010 = 147.15 \text{ N}$$

Net force available for climbing:
$$F_{climb} = F_t - F_r = 8245 - 147.15 = 8097.85 \text{ N}$$

Sin of the angle:
$$\sin\theta = \frac{F_{climb}}{m \cdot g} = \frac{8097.85}{1500 \times 9.81} = 0.5503$$

Maximum grade angle:
$$\theta = \arcsin(0.5503) \approx 33.4^{\circ}$$

**(iv) Top Vehicle Speed:**

First, convert motor speed to rad/s:
$$\omega_m = \frac{2 \pi N_m}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 12{,}000}{60} = 1256.6 \text{ rad/s}$$

Wheel angular speed:
$$\omega_w = \frac{\omega_m}{N_{gb}} = \frac{1256.6}{8.5} = 147.84 \text{ rad/s}$$

Top vehicle speed:
$$v_{\max} = \omega_w \cdot r_w = 147.84 \times 0.32 = 47.31 \text{ m/s}$$
$$v_{\max} = 47.31 \times 3.6 = 170.3 \text{ km/h}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Alternative Model]

**Q2 (a)** Explain the **working principle of a planetary (epicyclic) gear set** with a neat sketch. Derive the **Willis equation** for the sun-carrier relationship when the ring gear is held stationary. How is this principle utilized in **Toyota's Hybrid Synergy Drive**? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a):**

**Working Principle:**

A planetary gear set consists of three main components:
- **Sun gear (S):** Central gear with external teeth.
- **Planet gears (P):** Multiple smaller gears mounted on a common carrier (C), engaging both the sun and ring.
- **Ring gear (R):** Internal-toothed annulus surrounding the planets.

If one component is **held fixed**, the second becomes the **input**, and the third becomes the **output**, providing a precise fixed ratio. If **two components are driven** (e.g., by ICE and MG1), the third becomes an output whose speed is a **linear combination** of the inputs.

**Derivation of the Willis Equation:**

For a planetary gear set with sun ($N_s$ teeth) and ring ($N_r$ teeth), the basic kinematic constraint is:

$$(N_s + N_r) \cdot \omega_c = N_s \cdot \omega_s + N_r \cdot \omega_r$$

When the **ring gear is fixed** ($\omega_r = 0$):

$$(N_s + N_r) \cdot \omega_c = N_s \cdot \omega_s$$

Therefore:

$$\omega_c = \frac{N_s}{N_s + N_r} \cdot \omega_s$$

The gear ratio (sun to carrier) is:

$$N = \frac{\omega_s}{\omega_c} = 1 + \frac{N_r}{N_s}$$

**Application in Toyota THS:**

In the THS, the planetary gear set serves as a **power-split device**:
- The **carrier** is driven by the **ICE**.
- The **sun gear** is connected to **MG1** (acting as both motor and generator).
- The **ring gear** drives the driveline through **MG2**.

By controlling the speed of MG1, the effective ratio between ICE speed and wheel speed is **continuously variable** — mimicking a CVT but with no mechanical variability. This is why it is termed **"e-CVT"**.

**Q2 (b)** Compare **AMT, DCT, and CVT** as applied to hybrid electric vehicles. State **two advantages and one disadvantage** of each. Justify which is most suitable for a **mid-size parallel hybrid SUV**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part b):**

| **Gearbox** | **Two Advantages** | **One Disadvantage** |
|---|---|---|
| **AMT (Automated Manual Transmission)** | (i) High mechanical efficiency (~94–96%); (ii) Low cost and light weight due to simple gear set. | Noticeable shift shock and torque interruption during gear changes. |
| **DCT (Dual-Clutch Transmission)** | (i) Near-seamless shifts with no torque interruption (~200 ms); (ii) High efficiency (~95–97%). | Complex control software; expensive clutch and mechatronic system. |
| **CVT (Continuously Variable Transmission)** | (i) Infinite ratio variation for optimal ICE operating point; (ii) Smooth, jolt-free driving experience. | Torque capacity limited by belt/chain strength; "rubber-band" feel. |

**Justification for a Mid-Size Parallel Hybrid SUV:**

The **DCT** is most suitable for a mid-size parallel hybrid SUV because:
1. It provides **seamless, near-instant shifts**, important for a heavy SUV during overtaking and hill climbs.
2. Its high efficiency minimizes fuel consumption and supports the HEV's combined ICE + electric mode transitions.
3. It can handle the **high torque** required for an SUV's load-carrying capability, unlike a typical belt-driven CVT.
4. The DCT's pre-selection mechanism matches well with the **parallel hybrid's need to coordinate ICE and motor torque** during gear changes.

(AMT would be too jerky for an SUV; CVT would not handle the high torque demands reliably.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Scheme Exams on this Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting to include gearbox efficiency ($\eta_g$):** Many students compute $T_w = T_m \cdot N$ without multiplying by $\eta_g$, costing 1–2 marks per question. **Always** include it unless explicitly stated as "ideal."
> 2. **Confusing units for angular speed:** Mixing RPM with rad/s in the same equation is a frequent error. Convert RPM to rad/s using $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$ before using $v = \omega r$ for linear speed.
> 3. **Missing the rolling-resistance term in grade-climbing problems:** The force balance is $F_t = m g \sin\theta + m g C_{rr} \cos\theta$. Students often drop the second term, losing marks.
> 4. **Confusing gear ratio definitions:** A "9:1 gearbox" means the **driven gear has 9 teeth for every 1 tooth on the driver** (in a single-stage context, the output speed is 1/9 of input speed). Always clarify which is driver and which is driven.
> 5. **Not naming the three planetary gear components explicitly** in THS questions: sun, planet carrier, ring — and which is connected to ICE, MG1, and MG2. Examiners explicitly check this mapping.
> 6. **Confusing the formula $N = 1 + N_r/N_s$ with the wrong sign:** Some students write $N = 1 - N_r/N_s$ — always double-check by setting $N_r = 0$ (no ring teeth, equivalent to simple gear) and verifying the limit.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Gearbox function:** Torque multiplication + speed reduction; conserved power = $P = T\omega$.
- [ ] **Single-speed reducers** dominate modern BEVs because motors deliver peak torque from 0 RPM.
- [ ] **Multi-speed gearboxes** (2-speed, 3-speed) are used in performance EVs for both launch and top-end optimization.
- [ ] **AMT** is cheap but jerky; **DCT** is fast and seamless; **CVT** is smooth but torque-limited.
- [ ] **Planetary gear set** = sun + planet carrier + ring; used in Toyota THS as a power-split device.
- [ ] **Willis equation:** $(N_s + N_r)\omega_c = N_s \omega_s + N_r \omega_r$; with ring fixed, $N = 1 + N_r/N_s$.
- [ ] **Wheel torque:** $T_w = T_m \cdot N \cdot \eta_g$ (always include $\eta_g$).
- [ ] **Top speed formula:** $v_{\max} = \dfrac{\omega_m \cdot r_w}{N_{od}}$ where $N_{od}$ is overall drivetrain ratio.
- [ ] **Grade-climbing:** $\sin\theta = \dfrac{F_t - m g C_{rr}}{m g}$ — do not forget rolling resistance.
- [ ] **Tractive force:** $F_t = \dfrac{T_m \cdot N \cdot \eta_g}{r_w}$.
- [ ] **Typical single-speed ratio for EVs:** 6:1 to 12:1 (commonly ~9:1).
- [ ] **Typical gearbox efficiency:** 0.95 – 0.98 for helical/planetary units.
- [ ] **e-CVT in THS:** Stepless ratio blending via MG1 control of sun gear speed.
- [ ] **Formula 1 cars and Formula E:** F1 uses 8-speed DCT; Formula E used 2–4-speed initially, now standardized to single speed.
- [ ] **DCT shift time:** ~200 ms (gear change); pre-selects next gear to avoid torque interruption.
- [ ] **Infinitely Variable Transmission (IVT):** Capable of $0 \rightarrow \infty$ ratio (reverse + overdrive); rare in production EVs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Always state assumptions clearly — "Assuming no slip at gear meshing" and "neglecting aerodynamic drag at low speed." KTU examiners reward systematic problem-solving and unit consistency. Use SI units throughout, and box your final answers for clarity.

---
