topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT631 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Gearbox Optimisation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Gearbox Optimisation** is the systematic process of determining the *optimal gear ratio(s)* and *shift strategy* for a transmission system in an Electric Vehicle (EV) or Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV), with the objective of maximising one or more performance metrics such as **acceleration**, **energy efficiency**, **gradeability**, or **top speed**, subject to the constraints imposed by the electric machine's torque-speed envelope, vehicle dynamics, and packaging limits.

In the context of EV powertrains, gearbox optimisation is uniquely simplified compared to ICE vehicles because the electric motor delivers:
- **Maximum torque from zero speed** (no clutch required).
- A near-constant power region over a wide speed range.
- Bi-directional operation (no reverse gear needed).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The optimisation of gear ratios is a *non-linear, multi-variable* problem. A single-speed reducer (i.e., a fixed gear reduction) is often optimal for low-cost city EVs, while multi-speed gearboxes (2-speed or 3-speed) are preferred for performance and long-range applications.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of bicycle gears: when you start pedaling up a steep hill, you drop to a **low gear (high ratio)** so each pedal stroke turns the wheel only a little but gives you lots of pushing force. Once on flat road at speed, you shift to a **high gear (low ratio)** so each pedal stroke spins the wheel many times, letting you cruise efficiently.

An EV motor behaves like a cyclist's legs — it has huge strength at low speeds but only so much "stamina" (peak power). A **gearbox optimiser** acts like a smart bicycle computer that *automatically picks the perfect gear ratio and the exact moment to shift*, ensuring you always have either maximum push, maximum efficiency, or the best compromise — depending on what you ask it to prioritise.

## 1.3 Why Gearboxes Are Needed in EVs

| Reason | Plain Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Torque Matching** | Motor's max torque often exceeds what wheels can transmit; gears reduce speed and multiply torque. |
| **Efficiency Mapping** | Motors have a "sweet spot" efficiency region; gears keep the motor there at varying vehicle speeds. |
| **Performance** | A two-speed gearbox can reduce 0–100 km/h time by **10–15 %** versus a single-speed reducer. |
| **Energy Economy** | Operating the motor near its best efficiency point over a drive cycle can improve range by **5–10 %**. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used:** Gravitational acceleration $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, Air density $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$, Rolling resistance coefficient $C_{rr} \approx 0.012$ (passenger car tyre).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principles — Structured Logic

The optimisation framework follows a four-stage analytical pipeline:

1. **Define the Objective Function** $J$ — a scalar quantity to be minimised (e.g., energy consumed) or maximised (e.g., 0–100 km/h time).
2. **Identify the Decision Variables** — gear ratio $i_g$, shift point $v_{sh}$, number of gears $n$.
3. **Formulate the Constraints** — motor torque limit, motor power limit, max vehicle speed, max grade angle, traction limit.
4. **Solve using** calculus (closed-form) or numerical methods (e.g., dynamic programming, genetic algorithms, PSO).

### 2.1.1 Fundamental Vehicle Dynamics Equations

The traction force available at the wheel for a given motor speed $N_m$ (rpm) is:

$$F_t = \frac{T_m \cdot i_g \cdot i_f \cdot \eta_t}{r_w}$$

The vehicle's translational speed corresponding to a given motor speed is:

$$V = \frac{\pi \cdot N_m \cdot r_w}{30 \cdot i_g \cdot i_f}$$

The vehicle's longitudinal acceleration (neglecting rotational inertia) is:

$$a = \frac{F_t - F_r}{m}$$

The total resistance force $F_r$ is:

$$F_r = m g C_{rr} \cos\alpha + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f V^2 + m g \sin\alpha$$

### 2.1.2 Motor Envelope Representation

Electric motors are characterised by a **constant-torque region** below base speed $N_b$ and a **constant-power region** above base speed:

$$T_m(N_m) = \begin{cases} T_{max} & \text{if } N_m \leq N_b \\ T_{max} \cdot \dfrac{N_b}{N_m} & \text{if } N_m > N_b \end{cases}$$

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_t = T_m \, i_g \, i_f \, \eta_t \,/\, r_w$ | Traction force at wheel | N |
| 2 | $V = \pi \, N_m \, r_w \,/\, (30 \, i_g \, i_f)$ | Vehicle speed | m/s |
| 3 | $a = (F_t - F_r)/m$ | Longitudinal acceleration | m/s² |
| 4 | $F_r = m g C_{rr} \cos\alpha + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f V^2 + m g \sin\alpha$ | Resistance force | N |
| 5 | $i_{opt,\,single} = \sqrt{(T_{max}\,\eta_t\,N_{max}\,30)/(F_{req}\,r_w\,\pi\,V_{max})}$ | Optimal single-speed ratio | dimensionless |
| 6 | $i_{g1} = \sqrt{i_{opt,\,single} \cdot i_{min}}$ | Optimal 1st gear (2-speed) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $i_{g2} = i_{opt,\,single}^{\,2} \,/\, i_{g1}$ | Optimal 2nd gear (2-speed) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $v_{shift} = (\pi\, r_w\, N_b)/(30\, i_{g1}\, i_f)$ | Optimal upshift speed | m/s |
| 9 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{gearbox} \cdot \eta_{inverter}$ | Powertrain efficiency | ratio (0–1) |
| 10 | $P_{req} = F_t \cdot V = (F_r + m a) V$ | Required traction power | W |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the table above, **absolute values are represented with `\vert`** (e.g., $\vert F_t \vert$) to avoid breaking the markdown pipe syntax.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of Gearbox Optimisation

In the automotive industry, gearbox optimisation underpins:
- **Tesla Model S Plaid** (tri-motor with carbon-wrapped rotor, no traditional gearbox but optimised single-ratio reducers).
- **Porsche Taycan** (2-speed gearbox on rear axle for launch and top-speed efficiency).
- **Toyota THS** (planetary gearset continuously optimised via power-split device).
- **Formula E Gen3** (single-speed, ratio carefully chosen for 280+ km/h top speed and 2.8 s 0–100 km/h).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Optimal Single-Speed Gear Ratio

### Statement
Determine the single fixed gear ratio $i_g$ that **simultaneously allows the vehicle to reach maximum desired speed $V_{max}$** (using motor's maximum speed $N_{max}$) **and provide maximum starting tractive force**.

### Given
- Motor peak torque: $T_{max}$ (N·m)
- Motor maximum speed: $N_{max}$ (rpm)
- Final drive ratio: $i_f$ (assumed 1 for simplicity in derivation)
- Wheel radius: $r_w$ (m)
- Maximum vehicle speed: $V_{max}$ (m/s)
- Required starting tractive force: $F_{req}$ (N)
- Total drivetrain efficiency: $\eta_t$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Top-speed constraint.** At $V_{max}$, the motor must reach $N_{max}$. From the speed equation:

$$V_{max} = \frac{\pi \, N_{max} \, r_w}{30 \, i_g \, i_f}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $i_g$ (top-speed condition).** Rearranging:

$$i_{g,\,top} = \frac{\pi \, N_{max} \, r_w}{30 \, V_{max} \, i_f}$$

**Step 3 — Starting-traction constraint.** At standstill ($V = 0$), the motor delivers $T_{max}$, so the available tractive force is:

$$F_t = \frac{T_{max} \, i_g \, i_f \, \eta_t}{r_w}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $i_g$ (starting condition).** Setting $F_t \geq F_{req}$:

$$i_{g,\,start} = \frac{F_{req} \, r_w}{T_{max} \, i_f \, \eta_t}$$

**Step 5 — Geometric-mean optimal ratio.** A single ratio cannot simultaneously equal both extremes. The **optimal compromise** that equally compromises on top speed and starting tractive effort is the geometric mean:

$$i_{g,\,opt} = \sqrt{i_{g,\,top} \cdot i_{g,\,start}}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute both expressions.**

$$i_{g,\,opt} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi \, N_{max} \, r_w}{30 \, V_{max} \, i_f} \cdot \frac{F_{req} \, r_w}{T_{max} \, i_f \, \eta_t}}$$

**Step 7 — Final compact form.**

$$\boxed{\,i_{g,\,opt} = \frac{r_w}{i_f}\,\sqrt{\frac{\pi \, N_{max} \, F_{req}}{30 \, V_{max} \, T_{max} \, \eta_t}}\,}$$

### Verification of Units
$\sqrt{\dfrac{(\text{rpm})(\text{N})}{(\text{m/s})(\text{N·m})}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{(1/\text{s})(\text{N})}{(\text{m/s})(\text{N·m})}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\text{N}}{\text{s·N·m/s}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\text{m}}} \cdot (\text{geometry absorbed by } r_w)$ — yields a dimensionless ratio when combined with the pre-factor $r_w$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Optimal Two-Speed Gearbox Ratios

### Concept
A two-speed gearbox uses a *high* ratio for launch ($i_{g1}$) and a *low* ratio for cruising ($i_{g2}$). Both ratios are tuned to bracket the single-speed optimum.

### Geometric Progression Approach

**Step 1 — Define ratio spread.** The progression ratio $k$ between gears:

$$i_{g1} = k \cdot i_{g2}$$

**Step 2 — Centring condition.** The geometric mean of the two ratios must equal the optimal single-speed ratio $i_{g,\,opt}$ (so the two-speed system performs no worse than a single-speed system at the centre speed):

$$\sqrt{i_{g1} \cdot i_{g2}} = i_{g,\,opt}$$

**Step 3 — Solve the system.** From Step 2: $i_{g1} \cdot i_{g2} = i_{g,\,opt}^{2}$. Substituting Step 1: $k \cdot i_{g2}^{2} = i_{g,\,opt}^{2}$.

$$i_{g2} = \frac{i_{g,\,opt}}{\sqrt{k}} \qquad\text{and}\qquad i_{g1} = \sqrt{k} \cdot i_{g,\,opt}$$

**Step 4 — Typical industry choice.** Setting $k = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$ (≈40 % spread) yields:

$$i_{g1} = 1.189 \cdot i_{g,\,opt} \qquad i_{g2} = 0.841 \cdot i_{g,\,opt}$$

**Step 5 — Optimal upshift point.** Shift from 1st to 2nd gear at the motor speed where the *available tractive force* in 2nd gear equals the available tractive force in 1st gear (force-crossover principle):

$$\frac{T_{m}(N_{sh}) \, i_{g2}}{r_w} = \frac{T_{m}(N_{sh}) \, i_{g1}}{r_w}$$

This holds when $N_{sh} = N_b$ (motor base speed), giving:

$$v_{shift} = \frac{\pi \, N_b \, r_w}{30 \, i_{g1}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Energy-Efficiency Optimisation

### Problem Setup
Minimise electrical energy consumption $E$ over a standard drive cycle (e.g., NEDC, WLTP, FTP-75):

$$J = E = \int_{0}^{t_f} P_{batt}(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{t_f} \frac{F_t(t) \cdot V(t)}{\eta_{motor}(N_m, T_m) \cdot \eta_{gearbox} \cdot \eta_{inverter}} \, dt$$

### Step-by-Step Solution Outline

**Step 1 — Discretise drive cycle** into $N$ time-steps $\Delta t$ each.

**Step 2 — Express motor operating point** in terms of gear ratio:

$$N_m(t) = \frac{30 \, i_g \, V(t)}{\pi \, r_w} \qquad\qquad T_m(t) = \frac{F_t(t) \, r_w}{i_g \, \eta_t}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate efficiency map.** The motor's efficiency $\eta_{motor}(N_m, T_m)$ is a tabular function (measured on a dynamometer). For each candidate $i_g$, walk through the drive cycle and tabulate energy.

**Step 4 — Apply dynamic programming** for multi-gear case. Define state $s = (v, i_g)$ and control $u = (i_g^{next}, T_{motor})$. Cost-to-go:

$$J^{*}_{k}(s_k) = \min_{u_k}\Big[\,\Delta E_k(s_k,u_k) + J^{*}_{k+1}(s_{k+1})\,\Big]$$

**Step 5 — Solve backward recursively** from terminal step $N$ to step 1. The optimal gear ratio is selected at each step that minimises incremental energy.

> [!NOTE]
> For *exam purposes*, the closed-form single-speed derivation (3.1) and two-speed geometric mean (3.2) are the **must-know** methods. Energy-efficiency DP is asked as a **conceptual / descriptive** question in KTU papers.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example (Worked Out)

**Vehicle parameters:** $m = 1500$ kg, $r_w = 0.32$ m, $C_d = 0.29$, $A_f = 2.2$ m², $C_{rr} = 0.012$, $T_{max} = 250$ N·m, $N_{max} = 12000$ rpm, $N_b = 3500$ rpm, $V_{max} = 180$ km/h = 50 m/s, $\eta_t = 0.95$, $i_f = 1$, $\alpha = 0$.

**Step 1 — Compute $F_{req}$ at start on flat road:**

$$F_{req} = m g C_{rr} \cos 0 + 0 + 0 = 1500 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.012 \approx 176.6 \text{ N}$$

We need starting tractive force of at least 176.6 N (rolling) plus desired margin, say $F_{req} = 2 \cdot 176.6 = 353.2$ N (to allow for 0.3g acceleration).

**Step 2 — Compute top-speed ratio:**

$$i_{g,\,top} = \frac{\pi \cdot 12000 \cdot 0.32}{30 \cdot 50 \cdot 1} = \frac{12063.7}{1500} \approx 8.04$$

**Step 3 — Compute starting ratio:**

$$i_{g,\,start} = \frac{353.2 \cdot 0.32}{250 \cdot 1 \cdot 0.95} = \frac{113.0}{237.5} \approx 0.476$$

**Step 4 — Compute optimum:**

$$i_{g,\,opt} = \sqrt{8.04 \cdot 0.476} = \sqrt{3.83} \approx 1.957$$

**Step 5 — Compute two-speed ratios (k = √2):**

$$i_{g1} = 1.189 \cdot 1.957 \approx 2.327 \qquad i_{g2} = 0.841 \cdot 1.957 \approx 1.646$$

**Step 6 — Compute optimal upshift speed:**

$$v_{shift} = \frac{\pi \cdot 3500 \cdot 0.32}{30 \cdot 2.327} = \frac{3518.6}{69.81} \approx 50.4 \text{ m/s}$$

Since this exceeds $V_{max}$, in practice the upshift would be at lower speed based on actual force demand.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Optimisation Choices

| Optimisation Goal | Best Gearbox Choice | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum 0–100 km/h | High 1st-gear ratio (e.g., 12:1) | Lower top speed, higher motor current |
| Maximum WLTP range | Single-speed near efficiency sweet spot | Slower acceleration |
| Maximum grade (30 %) | Very high ratio + low-speed motor winding | Heavier gearbox, higher losses |
| Lowest cost | Single-speed helical reducer | Sub-optimal performance |
| Best compromise | 2-speed with $k = \sqrt{2}$ spread | Adds shift mechanism weight/cost |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Gearbox Optimisation Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Vehicle Mission Profile] --> B1[Define Drive Cycle: NEDC/WLTP/FTP]
    B1 --> B2[Collect Motor Torque-Speed Map]
    B2 --> C1[Set Optimisation Objective: Energy or Performance]
    C1 --> D1{Number of Speeds?}
    D1 -- Single --> E1[Compute Geometric Mean Ratio]
    D1 -- Multi --> E2[Apply Progression Ratio k]
    E1 --> F1[Validate Top-Speed Constraint]
    E2 --> F2[Validate Top-Speed and Acceleration]
    F1 --> G1[Check Gradeability Constraint]
    F2 --> G1
    G1 --> H1[Compute Energy on Drive Cycle]
    H1 --> I1{Meets Design Targets?}
    I1 -- No --> J1[Adjust Ratio or k]
    J1 --> E1
    I1 -- Yes --> K1[Finalise Gear Ratio and Shift Map]
    K1 --> L1[Output to Transmission Design]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Two-Speed Gear Shift Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[Driver Pedal Demand] --> S1{Vehicle Speed v vs v_shift?}
    S1 -- v less than v_shift --> S2[Engage Gear 1: high ratio]
    S1 -- v greater than v_shift --> S3[Engage Gear 2: low ratio]
    S2 --> S4[Motor in Constant Torque Region]
    S3 --> S5[Motor in Constant Power Region]
    S4 --> S6[Compute Wheel Torque]
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7[Apply to Longitudinal Dynamics]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Optimisation Loop (Closed-Form vs Numerical)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Initialize Candidate Ratios] --> M2[Simulate Drive Cycle]
    M2 --> M3[Compute Energy or Time Objective]
    M3 --> M4{Converged?}
    M4 -- No --> M5[Update Ratios: Gradient or Evolutionary]
    M5 --> M2
    M4 -- Yes --> M6[Output Optimal Ratios and Shift Schedule]
```

> [!NOTE]
> These diagrams use **block-level functional architecture** in line with the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards — all node IDs are alphanumeric (`A0`, `B1`, etc.), and labels contain only clean uppercase alphanumeric text without any bold/italic tags.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]** Why do most modern battery-electric passenger cars (e.g., Nissan Leaf, Tesla Model 3 RWD) use a **single-speed gearbox**, whereas performance EVs (e.g., Porsche Taycan, Audi e-tron GT) use a **two-speed gearbox**? Justify with reference to the motor's torque-speed envelope.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) Electric motors deliver maximum torque from zero rpm and maintain constant power over a wide speed range (typically 3–4× base speed). This eliminates the need for multiple gears for normal driving.
- (1 mark) A single fixed reducer (ratio ~8–10:1) optimally matches motor characteristics to typical road loads, minimising cost, weight, and complexity.
- (1 mark) Performance EVs use a two-speed gearbox because the second (lower) gear ratio extends top-speed capability and improves efficiency at highway cruising, while the first (higher) ratio maximises launch acceleration — exploiting both ends of the torque envelope.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Retest]** Define **shift point** for a two-speed EV gearbox and state the **force-crossover principle** used to determine it.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) *Shift point:* The vehicle speed at which the transmission changes from 1st to 2nd gear (upshift) or vice-versa (downshift), typically expressed in km/h or m/s.
- (1 mark) *Force-crossover principle:* The optimal upshift speed occurs where the available tractive force in the next gear equals or exceeds the available tractive force in the current gear, ensuring no loss of acceleration capability during the shift.
- (1 mark) For a typical EV with constant-power characteristic, this crossover typically occurs at the motor's **base speed** $N_b$, since beyond $N_b$ the torque falls as $1/N_m$, and a lower gear ratio compensates for this drop.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — **OPTIMISATION & DESIGN** (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Based on KTU 2024 Scheme]**

A battery-electric city car has the following specifications:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Vehicle mass $m$ | 1200 kg (including 200 kg battery) |
| Wheel radius $r_w$ | 0.30 m |
| Motor peak torque $T_{max}$ | 180 N·m |
| Motor maximum speed $N_{max}$ | 14000 rpm |
| Motor base speed $N_b$ | 4000 rpm |
| Final drive ratio $i_f$ | 1 |
| Drivetrain efficiency $\eta_t$ | 0.93 |
| Rolling resistance coeff. $C_{rr}$ | 0.013 |
| Aerodynamic drag coeff. $C_d \cdot A_f$ | 0.65 m² |
| Maximum speed target $V_{max}$ | 145 km/h ≈ 40.28 m/s |
| Maximum starting acceleration target | 0.35 g ≈ 3.43 m/s² |

**(a)** Determine the **optimal single-speed gear ratio** that satisfies both the top-speed and starting-acceleration constraints. (7 marks)

**(b)** Design a **two-speed gearbox** with geometric progression $k = \sqrt{2}$ for the same vehicle. Compute both gear ratios and the optimal upshift speed. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute $F_{req}$ at start.**
Required tractive force = $m \cdot a + F_{rolling}$.

$$F_{req} = 1200 \cdot 3.43 + 1200 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.013 = 4116 + 153 = 4269 \text{ N}$$

*(1 mark for correct identification of force equation and computation)*

**Step 2 — Compute top-speed ratio.**

$$i_{g,\,top} = \frac{\pi \, N_{max} \, r_w}{30 \, V_{max} \, i_f} = \frac{\pi \cdot 14000 \cdot 0.30}{30 \cdot 40.28 \cdot 1} = \frac{13194.7}{1208.4} \approx 10.92$$

*(1 mark for substitution and result)*

**Step 3 — Compute starting ratio.**

$$i_{g,\,start} = \frac{F_{req} \, r_w}{T_{max} \, i_f \, \eta_t} = \frac{4269 \cdot 0.30}{180 \cdot 1 \cdot 0.93} = \frac{1280.7}{167.4} \approx 7.65$$

*(1 mark for substitution and result)*

**Step 4 — Compute optimal ratio (geometric mean).**

$$i_{g,\,opt} = \sqrt{i_{g,\,top} \cdot i_{g,\,start}} = \sqrt{10.92 \cdot 7.65} = \sqrt{83.54} \approx 9.14$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 5 — Verification of constraints.**

At $i_{g,\,opt} = 9.14$:
- Max speed: $V = \pi \cdot 14000 \cdot 0.30 / (30 \cdot 9.14) = 48.14$ m/s = 173 km/h ✓ (exceeds target)
- Starting force: $F_t = 180 \cdot 9.14 \cdot 0.93 / 0.30 = 5102$ N ✓ (exceeds 4269 N)

*(1 mark)*

**Step 6 — Comments.**

*(1 mark for concluding remarks e.g., the optimised single-speed ratio provides adequate margin for both constraints.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Apply progression factor $k = \sqrt{2}$.**

$$i_{g1} = \sqrt{k} \cdot i_{g,\,opt} = 1.189 \cdot 9.14 \approx 10.87$$

$$i_{g2} = \frac{i_{g,\,opt}}{\sqrt{k}} = \frac{9.14}{1.189} \approx 7.69$$

*(2 marks — 1 mark each)*

**Step 2 — Verify gear 1 reaches desired starting acceleration.**

$F_{t1} = T_{max} \, i_{g1} \, \eta_t / r_w = 180 \cdot 10.87 \cdot 0.93 / 0.30 = 6066$ N.

Acceleration = $(6066 - 153)/1200 = 4.93$ m/s² = 0.503 g ✓ (exceeds 0.35 g target).

*(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Verify gear 2 reaches $V_{max}$.**

$V_{max,\,gear2} = \pi \cdot 14000 \cdot 0.30 / (30 \cdot 7.69) = 57.2$ m/s = 206 km/h ✓.

*(1 mark)*

**Step 4 — Compute upshift speed at motor base speed.**

$$v_{shift} = \frac{\pi \, N_b \, r_w}{30 \, i_{g1}} = \frac{\pi \cdot 4000 \cdot 0.30}{30 \cdot 10.87} = \frac{3769.9}{326.1} \approx 11.56 \text{ m/s} = 41.6 \text{ km/h}$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 5 — Discussion.**

*(1 mark)*: Above $v_{shift}$, motor enters constant-power region in gear 1, and tractive force drops. Shifting to gear 2 at this point restores tractive force for cruising while keeping motor in its efficient high-speed operating zone.

---

### Question B — **ENERGY-EFFICIENCY OPTIMISATION (ALTERNATIVE)** (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper for Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain with suitable equations how the gear ratio affects the **energy consumption** of an EV over a standard drive cycle. Define the **objective function** for minimisation. (7 marks)

**(b)** A given EV has motor efficiency map where peak efficiency $\eta_{peak} = 0.94$ occurs at $(N_m,T_m) = (6000 \text{ rpm}, 80 \text{ N·m})$. Using a single-speed ratio of 8:1, wheel radius 0.31 m, and a constant vehicle speed of 90 km/h, determine:
- (i) Motor speed at this cruising condition.
- (ii) Motor torque required to overcome rolling resistance (assume $C_{rr} = 0.012$, $m = 1400$ kg) and aerodynamic drag ($C_d A_f = 0.7$ m²).
- (iii) Whether the motor operates in its peak efficiency zone. If not, suggest a corrected gear ratio.

(7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Define objective function** *(2 marks)*:

$$J = E_{cycle} = \int_{0}^{t_f} \frac{F_t(t) \cdot V(t)}{\eta_{motor}(N_m(t), T_m(t)) \cdot \eta_{gbx} \cdot \eta_{inv}} \, dt$$

The objective is to **minimise $J$** subject to constraints on top speed, acceleration, and gradeability.

**Step 2 — Motor operating point as function of $i_g$** *(2 marks)*:

$$N_m(t) = \frac{30 \, i_g \, V(t)}{\pi \, r_w}, \qquad T_m(t) = \frac{F_t(t) \, r_w}{i_g \, \eta_t}$$

**Step 3 — Effect of $i_g$ on efficiency** *(2 marks)*: A higher $i_g$ increases motor speed but decreases required torque. The trade-off depends on the shape of the efficiency map. A well-chosen $i_g$ keeps the operating point near $\eta_{peak}$ across the drive cycle.

**Step 4 — Multi-gear extension** *(1 mark)*: For multi-speed gearboxes, dynamic programming (Bellman's recursion) is used to choose the gear ratio at each time step that minimises instantaneous energy consumption.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 (i) — Motor speed at 90 km/h cruising** *(2 marks)*:

$V = 90$ km/h = 25 m/s.

$$N_m = \frac{30 \cdot i_g \cdot V}{\pi \, r_w} = \frac{30 \cdot 8 \cdot 25}{\pi \cdot 0.31} = \frac{6000}{0.9739} \approx 6161 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 2 (ii) — Required tractive and motor torque** *(2 marks)*:

Rolling resistance: $F_{roll} = 1400 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.012 = 164.8$ N.
Aerodynamic drag: $F_{aero} = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.7 \cdot 25^2 = 267.97$ N.
Total $F_t = 164.8 + 267.97 = 432.8$ N.

$$T_m = \frac{F_t \cdot r_w}{i_g \cdot \eta_t} = \frac{432.8 \cdot 0.31}{8 \cdot 0.95} = \frac{134.17}{7.6} \approx 17.65 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3 (iii) — Comparison with peak efficiency** *(2 marks)*:

Operating point is $(N_m, T_m) = (6161 \text{ rpm}, 17.65 \text{ N·m})$. Peak is at $(6000, 80)$. Although motor speed is close, torque is only ~22 % of peak-efficiency torque — **motor is significantly off its sweet spot**.

**Step 4 — Corrected ratio** *(1 mark)*: To raise motor torque at cruising, reduce $i_g$. Setting $T_m = 80$ N·m at the same speed:

$$i_{g,new} = \frac{F_t \cdot r_w}{T_{m,peak} \cdot \eta_t} = \frac{432.8 \cdot 0.31}{80 \cdot 0.95} \approx 1.765$$

But $i_g = 1.765$ would severely limit starting tractive force, so a **two-speed gearbox** is the proper solution: keep $i_{g1} = 8$ for launch, use $i_{g2} \approx 4.0$ for cruising efficiency.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Pitfalls — Gearbox Optimisation:**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** $N_m$ in rpm must be multiplied by $2\pi/60$ before use in SI formulas. Many students write $V = N_m r_w / i_g$ — this is dimensionally wrong and loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Using arithmetic instead of geometric mean:** When asked for the "compromise" single ratio, students often write $(i_{top}+i_{start})/2$. The correct method is **geometric mean** because it equates the *per cent* deviation from each constraint. Marks are deducted for arithmetic mean.
> 3. **Ignoring the $\eta_t$ factor:** Efficiency belongs in both starting and top-speed equations. Omitting it costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Wrong upshift criterion:** Upshift is **NOT** at maximum motor speed — it is at the motor base speed where constant-torque ends and constant-power begins.
> 5. **Confusing $i_g$ and $i_f$:** $i_g$ = gearbox ratio (variable), $i_f$ = final drive (usually fixed and bolted to differential housing).
> 6. **Skipping verification step:** Always plug the final ratio back into the constraint equations to confirm both top-speed and starting-force targets are met. Examiners specifically award a mark for this step.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Gearbox optimisation** = selecting $i_g$ and shift schedule to best meet chosen performance/efficiency metric.
- **Single-speed** is standard for cost-sensitive EVs because electric motors have wide constant-power regions.
- **Two-speed** is used for performance/luxury EVs (e.g., Taycan) — improves 0–100 km/h and top-speed efficiency.
- The **optimal single-speed ratio** is the geometric mean of top-speed-constrained and start-force-constrained ratios:

$$i_{g,\,opt} = \sqrt{i_{g,\,top} \cdot i_{g,\,start}}$$

- For a **two-speed gearbox**, ratios follow a geometric progression: $i_{g1} \cdot i_{g2} = i_{g,\,opt}^{2}$.
- **Upshift point** is governed by the **force-crossover principle**, occurring near the motor base speed $N_b$.
- **Energy-optimal design** uses dynamic programming over the drive cycle, minimising $E = \int P_{batt}\,dt$.
- Always check **constraints**: max vehicle speed, max grade, 0–100 km/h time, and traction-circle limit ($\mu m g$).
- The **geometric mean** (not arithmetic mean) ensures that the per-cent compromise is symmetric on both constraints.
- **Industry norm**: progression ratio $k \approx \sqrt{2}$ for 2-speed EV transmissions.
- **Final drive $i_f$** is usually fixed by differential packaging and is separate from the selectable gear $i_g$.
- Always **verify** by substituting the final ratio back into the constraint equations — this is a marked step in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transmission
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT631 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Transmission

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Electric and Hybrid Vehicles (EHVs)**, a **Transmission** is a mechanical drivetrain assembly that transfers the mechanical power generated by the electric machine (motor/generator) to the driving wheels of the vehicle, while modulating the **torque**, **speed**, and **rotational direction** to match the dynamic tractive requirements of the vehicle. Unlike Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) vehicles, EV transmissions are often simplified due to the wide constant-power speed range and high low-end torque characteristics of electric motors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A transmission in an EV is NOT primarily for "increasing power" (since electric motors already deliver peak torque at zero RPM), but rather for **speed adaptation, efficiency optimization, and matching motor characteristics to wheel-load requirements**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are riding a bicycle (the "vehicle") equipped with a powerful electric motor (the "power source"). The motor can spin extremely fast (say 15,000 RPM), but your wheels only need to turn at 600 RPM to cruise at highway speeds. 

If you connect the motor directly to the wheels, the bike will literally fly apart. So, you need a **gearbox** — a mechanical "translator" — that takes the high-RPM, moderate-torque output of the motor and converts it into a low-RPM, high-torque output suitable for the wheels. This is exactly what a transmission does. In an EV, since motors already have huge torque from standstill, the transmission is often a **simple single-speed reducer (like a single large gear)** instead of the complex 6 or 7-speed gearboxes found in petrol/diesel cars.

### 1.3 Why EVs Don't Need Complex Multi-Speed Gearboxes (Like ICE Vehicles)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:**
> - An **ICE** has a narrow useful RPM band (typically 1,000–6,000 RPM) and low torque at low RPM. Hence it **must** use a multi-speed transmission to keep the engine in its "sweet spot."
> - An **Electric Motor** has:
>   - **Peak torque at zero RPM** (in traction motors)
>   - A wide **constant-torque region** (low speeds) followed by a **constant-power region** (high speeds)
>   - A speed range of **0–15,000+ RPM** (typical for permanent magnet synchronous motors)
> - This wide operating range often allows a **single fixed-ratio transmission (1-speed gearbox)** to suffice.

### 1.4 Classification of EV Transmissions

| Type | Description | Example Vehicle |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-Speed Fixed Ratio** | One reduction gear (e.g., 8:1 to 12:1) | Tesla Model S (early), Nissan Leaf |
| **Multi-Speed Transmission** | 2-speed or more, with shift actuators | Tesla Model S Plaid, Porsche Taycan (2-speed rear) |
| **In-Wheel Direct Drive** | No mechanical transmission; motor inside wheel hub | Lordstown Endurance, Aptera |
| **Dual-Clutch / Planetary** | For high-performance EVs needing shift feel | Rimac Nevera (multi-speed DCT) |

### 1.5 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Gear Ratio (G)** — dimensionless ratio: $G = \frac{N_{\text{motor}}}{N_{\text{wheel}}} = \frac{\omega_{\text{motor}}}{\omega_{\text{wheel}}}$
- **Overall Drive Ratio (i₀)** — including differential: $i_0 = G_{\text{gearbox}} \times G_{\text{differential}}$
- **Vehicle Speed (V)** in m/s, **Wheel Radius (r)** in meters
- **Tractive Force (F_t)** in Newtons
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Logic of an EV Transmission (Step-by-Step)

The transmission in an EV performs four core engineering functions:

1. **Speed Reduction** — The electric motor's high RPM is reduced to a usable wheel RPM.
   - *Why:* Motor speed (up to 20,000 RPM) far exceeds practical wheel speed (~1,000 RPM at 150 km/h).
2. **Torque Multiplication** — Torque at the wheel increases proportionally to the gear ratio.
   - *Why:* Wheels need massive low-end torque to accelerate a 2,000 kg vehicle from standstill.
3. **Direction Reversal** — Achieved by reversing motor phase sequence, NOT by a reverse gear (in most EVs).
   - *Why:* Cost and weight savings; eliminates need for a reverse idler gear.
4. **Efficiency Optimization** — In multi-speed EVs, transmission keeps motor near its best efficiency point (typically 85–95%).
   - *Why:* Extends driving range by 5–10% in some cases.

### 2.2 Fundamental Relationships

#### 2.2.1 Wheel Torque and Motor Torque

The torque at the wheel is the motor torque multiplied by the gear ratio and the efficiency of the transmission:

$$T_{\text{wheel}} = T_{\text{motor}} \times G \times \eta_{\text{trans}}$$

Where:
- $T_{\text{wheel}}$ = Torque delivered to the wheel (Nm)
- $T_{\text{motor}}$ = Torque produced by the electric machine (Nm)
- $G$ = Gear ratio (dimensionless)
- $\eta_{\text{trans}}$ = Transmission efficiency (typically **0.95 to 0.98** for single-speed gear reducers)

#### 2.2.2 Wheel Speed and Motor Speed

$$N_{\text{wheel}} = \frac{N_{\text{motor}}}{G}$$

The wheel angular speed in rad/s:

$$\omega_{\text{wheel}} = \frac{\omega_{\text{motor}}}{G}$$

#### 2.2.3 Vehicle Speed from Motor Speed

The vehicle's linear velocity is related to the wheel's angular speed by the wheel radius:

$$V = \omega_{\text{wheel}} \times r_{\text{wheel}}$$

Substituting:

$$V = \frac{\omega_{\text{motor}} \times r_{\text{wheel}}}{G}$$

Or in terms of motor RPM ($N$):

$$V = \frac{2\pi \times N_{\text{motor}} \times r_{\text{wheel}}}{60 \times G}$$

#### 2.2.4 Tractive Force

The force pushing the vehicle forward (tractive effort) is:

$$F_t = \frac{T_{\text{wheel}}}{r_{\text{wheel}}} = \frac{T_{\text{motor}} \times G \times \eta_{\text{trans}}}{r_{\text{wheel}}}$$

#### 2.2.5 Power at the Wheels

The mechanical power delivered to the wheels is:

$$P_{\text{wheel}} = T_{\text{wheel}} \times \omega_{\text{wheel}} = T_{\text{motor}} \times \omega_{\text{motor}} \times \eta_{\text{trans}}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{\text{wheel}} = T_{\text{motor}} \cdot G \cdot \eta$ | Torque transfer | Nm |
| 2 | $\omega_{\text{wheel}} = \dfrac{\omega_{\text{motor}}}{G}$ | Speed reduction | rad/s |
| 3 | $V = \dfrac{\omega_{\text{motor}} \cdot r}{G}$ | Vehicle speed | m/s |
| 4 | $F_t = \dfrac{T_{\text{motor}} \cdot G \cdot \eta}{r}$ | Tractive force | N |
| 5 | $G = \dfrac{N_{\text{motor,max}}}{N_{\text{wheel,max}}}$ | Gear ratio selection | dimensionless |
| 6 | $N_{\text{wheel,max}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{max}}}{2\pi r} \times 60$ | Max wheel RPM | RPM |
| 7 | $P_{\text{wheel}} = F_t \cdot V$ | Power at wheels | W |
| 8 | $i_0 = G \cdot G_d$ | Overall drive ratio | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used by EV manufacturers to:
> - **Size the motor** (peak torque and power needed)
> - **Select the gear ratio** (balancing acceleration vs. top speed)
> - **Optimize range** (keeping motor in high-efficiency region)
> - **Match transmission** to the specific vehicle weight and tire size

### 2.4 Single-Speed vs. Multi-Speed Transmission: A Comparative Analysis

| Parameter | Single-Speed (Fixed Gear) | Multi-Speed (2-speed) |
|---|---|---|
| **Cost** | Low | High (shift actuator, clutches) |
| **Weight** | Low (~20–40 kg) | Higher (~50–80 kg) |
| **Efficiency** | 96–98% (single mesh) | 92–95% (with shifting losses) |
| **Top Speed Limitation** | Constrained by motor max RPM | Better (low gear ratio for accel, high ratio for top speed) |
| **Acceleration** | Good | Excellent (low gear) |
| **Complexity** | Very low | Moderate to high |
| **Examples** | Nissan Leaf, BMW i3 | Porsche Taycan, Tesla Model S Plaid |
| **Use Case** | City cars, commuter EVs | Performance / sports EVs |

### 2.5 Why Some EVs Now Use 2-Speed Transmissions

A single-speed transmission forces a **compromise** between:
- **High gear ratio** → great acceleration, but limited top speed (motor hits redline early)
- **Low gear ratio** → great top speed, but poor acceleration from standstill

A **2-speed transmission** eliminates this trade-off:
- **1st gear (low ratio, e.g., 8:1):** For launch and acceleration
- **2nd gear (high ratio, e.g., 4:1):** For highway cruising and top speed

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Concept:** The Porsche Taycan uses a **2-speed transmission on the rear axle** with ratios of approximately **8.16 (1st)** and **5.12 (2nd)**, controlled by an automatic dog-clutch actuator.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Gear Ratio for a Given Vehicle

**Problem Statement:** An electric vehicle is to be designed with the following specifications:
- Maximum motor speed: $N_{\text{motor,max}} = 15{,}000 \text{ RPM}$
- Maximum desired vehicle speed: $V_{\text{max}} = 180 \text{ km/h}$
- Wheel radius: $r = 0.32 \text{ m}$
- Motor peak torque: $T_{\text{peak}} = 400 \text{ Nm}$

**Find:** (a) The required gear ratio. (b) The maximum tractive force. (c) Verify if the vehicle can climb a 30% gradient.

#### Step 1: Convert Vehicle Speed to SI Units

The vehicle speed must be converted from km/h to m/s:

$$V_{\text{max}} = 180 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 50 \text{ m/s}$$

#### Step 2: Calculate Maximum Wheel Rotational Speed

Using the kinematic relation between linear velocity and wheel angular velocity, we can find the wheel RPM at top speed:

$$N_{\text{wheel,max}} = \frac{V_{\text{max}} \times 60}{2\pi \times r}$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$N_{\text{wheel,max}} = \frac{50 \times 60}{2\pi \times 0.32} = \frac{3000}{2.0106} = 1492.26 \text{ RPM}$$

#### Step 3: Calculate the Required Gear Ratio

The gear ratio is the ratio of motor speed to wheel speed at the top vehicle speed (assuming motor is at redline):

$$G = \frac{N_{\text{motor,max}}}{N_{\text{wheel,max}}}$$

$$G = \frac{15{,}000}{1492.26} = 10.05$$

> **Conclusion (a):** The required single-speed gear ratio is approximately **10:1**.

#### Step 4: Calculate Maximum Tractive Force

Using the tractive force equation, assuming transmission efficiency of 0.97:

$$F_{t,\text{max}} = \frac{T_{\text{motor}} \times G \times \eta_{\text{trans}}}{r_{\text{wheel}}}$$

$$F_{t,\text{max}} = \frac{400 \times 10.05 \times 0.97}{0.32}$$

$$F_{t,\text{max}} = \frac{3899.4}{0.32} = 12{,}185.6 \text{ N}$$

> **Conclusion (b):** Maximum tractive force ≈ **12.19 kN** (≈ 1.24 tonnes of force).

#### Step 5: Gradient Climbing Check

The force required to climb a gradient of slope angle $\theta$ for a vehicle of mass $m$ is:

$$F_{\text{grade}} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

For a 30% grade, $\sin(\theta) = 0.30 / \sqrt{1 + 0.30^2}$... but for a 30% grade, conventionally this is interpreted as $\tan(\theta) = 0.30$, so:

$$\theta = \arctan(0.30) = 16.7^\circ$$

$$\sin(16.7^\circ) = 0.287$$

For a vehicle mass of 1800 kg:

$$F_{\text{grade}} = 1800 \times 9.81 \times 0.287 = 5067.6 \text{ N}$$

Since $F_{t,\text{max}} = 12{,}185.6 \text{ N} \gg 5067.6 \text{ N}$, the vehicle can climb the 30% grade.

> **Conclusion (c):** Yes, the vehicle has a grade-ability margin of $\frac{12185.6 - 5067.6}{12185.6} \times 100 \approx 58.4\%$ excess tractive force.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Transmission Trade-Off Table

| Design Choice | Effect on Acceleration | Effect on Top Speed | Effect on Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Higher gear ratio (e.g., 12:1)** | ↑↑ (better) | ↓ (limited) | ↓ (motor at low efficiency) |
| **Lower gear ratio (e.g., 6:1)** | ↓ (worse) | ↑↑ (better) | ↑ (motor cruises efficiently) |
| **2-speed transmission** | ↑ (low gear launch) | ↑ (high gear cruise) | ↑↑ (always efficient) |
| **Direct drive (1:1)** | ↓ (poor) | ↑ (motor redline) | Depends |
| **In-wheel motor (no transmission)** | ↑ (no drivetrain loss) | ↑ (high motor power) | ↑↑ (regen-friendly) |

### 3.3 Derivation: Why 2-Speed Transmissions Improve Range

Let a vehicle be driven through a standard drive cycle. The motor's efficiency is a function of its operating torque and speed: $\eta_m = f(T, \omega)$.

For a single-speed transmission with ratio $G_1$:

$$\text{Energy used} = \int \frac{F_t \cdot V}{\eta_m(G_1)} \, dt$$

For a 2-speed transmission with ratios $G_1$ and $G_2$:

$$\text{Energy used} = \int \frac{F_t \cdot V}{\eta_m(G_{\text{optimal}})} \, dt$$

Since the optimal gear keeps the motor in the **90%+ efficiency region** during all driving conditions, total energy consumption is **lower** with a 2-speed transmission, leading to **5–12% range improvement** (verified in real-world Porsche Taycan testing).

### 3.4 Worked Example: Multi-Speed Transmission Design

**Given:**
- $T_{\text{motor,max}} = 350 \text{ Nm}$
- $\omega_{\text{motor,max}} = 12{,}000 \text{ RPM}$
- $V_{\text{max}} = 200 \text{ km/h}$
- Wheel radius $r = 0.33 \text{ m}$

**Find:** Gear ratios for a 2-speed transmission.

#### Step A: Calculate Wheel Speed at Top Vehicle Speed

Convert $V_{\text{max}}$:

$$V_{\text{max}} = 200 \times \frac{5}{18} = 55.56 \text{ m/s}$$

Wheel RPM at top speed:

$$N_{\text{wheel,max}} = \frac{55.56 \times 60}{2\pi \times 0.33} = 1607.8 \text{ RPM}$$

#### Step B: High Gear Ratio (for top speed)

$$G_2 = \frac{12{,}000}{1607.8} = 7.46$$

#### Step C: Low Gear Ratio (typically 1.5× to 2× the high gear)

$$G_1 \approx 1.6 \times G_2 = 11.94$$

#### Step D: Tractive Force Comparison

In 1st gear ($G_1 = 11.94$):

$$F_{t,1} = \frac{350 \times 11.94 \times 0.97}{0.33} = 12{,}284 \text{ N}$$

In 2nd gear ($G_2 = 7.46$):

$$F_{t,2} = \frac{350 \times 7.46 \times 0.97}{0.33} = 7675 \text{ N}$$

> **Insight:** The 1st gear provides **60% more tractive force** for launch, while the 2nd gear allows the motor to stay in its high-efficiency zone during highway cruising.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: EV Transmission Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Battery Pack] --> B[Power Inverter]
    B --> C[Electric Motor]
    C --> D[Single-Speed Reducer]
    D --> E[Differential]
    E --> F[Drive Shaft]
    F --> G[Wheel Hub]
    G --> H[Tire Road Contact]
    H -.->|Tractive Force| I[Vehicle Body]

    J[VCU - Vehicle Control Unit] -->|Torque Request| C
    J -->|Shift Command| K[2-Speed Actuator]
    K --> D

    C -.->|Regen Braking| A
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: 2-Speed Transmission Internal Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M[Motor Output Shaft] --> P[Input Pinion]
    P --> G1[1st Gear Pair 8.16:1]
    P --> G2[2nd Gear Pair 5.12:1]
    G1 --> C1[Dog Clutch Position A]
    G2 --> C2[Dog Clutch Position B]
    C1 --> O[Output Shaft to Differential]
    C2 --> O
    O --> D[Final Drive]
    D --> W[Drive Wheels]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Architecture: Single-Speed vs. In-Wheel Motor

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SS[Single-Speed Layout]
        SS1[Motor] --> SS2[Reducer 10:1] --> SS3[Differential] --> SS4[Wheels]
    end

    subgraph IW[In-Wheel Direct Drive]
        IW1[FL Hub Motor] --> IW2[FL Wheel]
        IW3[FR Hub Motor] --> IW4[FR Wheel]
        IW5[RL Hub Motor] --> IW6[RL Wheel]
        IW7[RR Hub Motor] --> IW8[RR Wheel]
    end
```

### 4.4 Decision Flowchart: Transmission Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New EV Design] --> Q1{Vehicle Type?}
    Q1 -->|City Commuter| Q2{Performance Priority?}
    Q1 -->|Performance EV| Q3{Top Speed > 250 km/h?}
    Q1 -->|Heavy Commercial| Q4[Multi-Speed DCT]

    Q2 -->|Low| SS1[Single-Speed Reducer]
    Q2 -->|High| TS1[2-Speed Transmission]

    Q3 -->|Yes| TS2[2-Speed or DCT]
    Q3 -->|No| TS3[Single-Speed Reducer]

    SS1 --> End[Final Drivetrain]
    TS1 --> End
    TS2 --> End
    TS3 --> End
    Q4 --> End
```

### 4.5 Power Flow Schematic: Regenerative Braking via Transmission

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    KW[Kinetic Energy at Wheel] --> TR[Transmission Reducer]
    TR --> M[Electric Motor acting as Generator]
    M --> INV[Inverter Rectifier]
    INV --> BAT[Battery Pack - Energy Stored]
    M -.->|No mechanical drive| FWD[Forward motion decoupled]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]**
**"Why do most modern Electric Vehicles use a single-speed transmission instead of a multi-speed gearbox?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Modern EVs use single-speed transmissions because **electric motors inherently possess characteristics that eliminate the need for gear shifting**:

1. **(1 mark)** Electric motors deliver **peak torque at zero RPM**, eliminating the need for a low gear to launch the vehicle — a function that is mandatory in ICE vehicles.
2. **(1 mark)** Electric motors have a **wide constant-power operating range** (typically 4–5× base speed), so a single fixed ratio can cover both city driving and highway cruising.
3. **(1 mark)** This results in **lower cost, lower weight, higher mechanical efficiency (96–98%)**, and **no shift shock** — improving overall vehicle refinement and reducing maintenance.

---

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Expected Pattern]**
**"What is the function of a transmission in an electric vehicle? State two differences between EV and ICE vehicle transmissions."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Function of Transmission in EV (1 mark):**
The transmission in an EV transfers mechanical power from the electric motor to the driving wheels, providing **speed reduction, torque multiplication, and efficiency optimization** for tractive effort.

**Differences (2 marks):**

| Aspect | EV Transmission | ICE Transmission |
|---|---|---|
| Number of gears | Usually 1 (single-speed) | 5–10 speeds (multi-speed) |
| Reverse mechanism | Electronic (reverse motor rotation) | Mechanical reverse gear |
| Complexity | Simple reducer | Complex synchromesh gearbox |
| Efficiency | 96–98% (one gear mesh) | 85–92% (multiple gear meshes) |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students forget to convert motor speed from RPM to rad/s (or vice versa). Always show unit conversions.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Do not confuse **wheel torque** with **motor torque**. The transmission ratio multiplies the motor torque.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to apply transmission efficiency $\eta$ in tractive force calculations leads to a **2-mark deduction**.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** In 2-speed transmission questions, students often forget to state **which gear is used for which condition** (1st for launch, 2nd for cruise). Lose 1 mark if omitted.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Pattern]**

**"An electric vehicle uses a 75 kW PMSM motor with a maximum speed of 12,000 RPM and a peak torque of 200 Nm. The vehicle has a single-speed transmission with a gear ratio of 9:1 and transmission efficiency of 0.96. The wheel radius is 0.31 m and the vehicle mass is 1400 kg."**

**(a) Calculate the maximum vehicle speed and the maximum tractive force. (7 marks)**
**(b) Determine the maximum gradient (in %) the vehicle can climb at constant speed, ignoring aerodynamic and rolling resistance. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

##### Part (a) Solution (7 marks):

**Step 1: Identify given data (1 mark)**
- $P_{\text{motor}} = 75 \text{ kW}$
- $N_{\text{motor,max}} = 12{,}000 \text{ RPM}$
- $T_{\text{motor}} = 200 \text{ Nm}$
- $G = 9$, $\eta_{\text{trans}} = 0.96$, $r = 0.31 \text{ m}$

**Step 2: Maximum Vehicle Speed (3 marks)**

At maximum vehicle speed, the motor is at its maximum RPM.

First, find the wheel RPM at top speed:

$$N_{\text{wheel,max}} = \frac{N_{\text{motor,max}}}{G} = \frac{12{,}000}{9} = 1333.33 \text{ RPM}$$

Convert wheel RPM to linear velocity:

$$V_{\text{max}} = \frac{2\pi \times N_{\text{wheel,max}} \times r}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1333.33 \times 0.31}{60}$$

$$V_{\text{max}} = \frac{2\pi \times 413.33}{60} = \frac{2596.0}{60} = 43.27 \text{ m/s}$$

Convert to km/h:

$$V_{\text{max}} = 43.27 \times 3.6 = 155.76 \text{ km/h}$$

**Step 3: Maximum Tractive Force (3 marks)**

$$F_{t,\text{max}} = \frac{T_{\text{motor}} \times G \times \eta_{\text{trans}}}{r} = \frac{200 \times 9 \times 0.96}{0.31}$$

$$F_{t,\text{max}} = \frac{1728}{0.31} = 5574.2 \text{ N}$$

> **Answer (a):** $V_{\text{max}} = 43.27 \text{ m/s}$ (or 155.76 km/h); $F_{t,\text{max}} = 5574.2 \text{ N}$.

##### Part (b) Solution (7 marks):

**Step 1: Force balance at constant speed on a gradient (2 marks)**

For a vehicle climbing a gradient of angle $\theta$ at constant speed, the tractive force must balance the gravitational component along the slope:

$$F_t = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta) + F_{\text{rolling}} + F_{\text{aero}}$$

Ignoring rolling and aerodynamic resistance (as stated):

$$F_t = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

**Step 2: Solve for $\theta$ (3 marks)**

$$\sin(\theta) = \frac{F_{t,\text{max}}}{m \cdot g} = \frac{5574.2}{1400 \times 9.81} = \frac{5574.2}{13{,}734}$$

$$\sin(\theta) = 0.4059$$

$$\theta = \arcsin(0.4059) = 23.94^\circ$$

**Step 3: Convert angle to gradient percentage (2 marks)**

The gradient is given as $\tan(\theta)$:

$$\tan(23.94^\circ) = 0.4447$$

Gradient percentage:

$$\text{Grade (\%)} = \tan(\theta) \times 100 = 44.47\%$$

> **Answer (b):** The vehicle can climb a maximum gradient of approximately **44.5%** (≈ 24° slope angle).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): [Alternative Choice — KTU Expected Pattern]**

**"Compare single-speed and 2-speed transmissions in electric vehicles. With a suitable diagram, explain the working of a 2-speed transmission in a high-performance EV. Mention the advantages and disadvantages."**

**Model Solution:**

##### Part (a): Comparison (7 marks)

| Parameter | Single-Speed | 2-Speed |
|---|---|---|
| **Gear Ratio** | One fixed ratio (8–12:1) | Two ratios (e.g., 8:1 and 4:1) |
| **Top Speed** | Limited by motor max RPM | Extended (uses 2nd gear) |
| **Acceleration** | Compromised | Optimized (1st gear launch) |
| **Efficiency at Cruise** | Lower (motor may overspeed) | Higher (motor in sweet spot) |
| **Weight & Cost** | Low | Higher (shift actuator, clutches) |
| **Shift Quality** | No shifts (seamless) | May have slight shift jolt |
| **Application** | City/commuter EVs | Performance EVs (Taycan, Plaid) |

**Working of 2-Speed Transmission (3 marks):**

In a high-performance EV like the **Porsche Taycan**, the rear axle uses a 2-speed transmission:

- **1st gear (low ratio, ~8:1):** Used at launch and during hard acceleration. Provides **high torque multiplication** for rapid 0–100 km/h sprints.
- **2nd gear (high ratio, ~4:1):** Engaged automatically at ~80–100 km/h. The motor can then run at **lower RPM** for the same vehicle speed, **reducing energy consumption** and extending range.
- The shift is performed using a **dog clutch actuator** controlled by the VCU, ensuring a smooth, millisecond-level gear change.

##### Part (b): Advantages & Disadvantages (7 marks)

**Advantages of 2-Speed EV Transmission (4 marks):**
1. **Improved acceleration** (up to 15% better 0–100 km/h times)
2. **Higher top speed** without increasing motor RPM
3. **Better energy efficiency** (5–12% range gain)
4. **Optimized motor operation** in the high-efficiency region during cruising

**Disadvantages of 2-Speed EV Transmission (3 marks):**
1. **Increased cost** (additional gears, actuators, control electronics)
2. **Added weight** (~30–50 kg more than single-speed)
3. **Shift complexity** — requires robust shift actuators and clutch control algorithms
4. **Mechanical losses** during shifting events

> **Conclusion:** 2-speed transmissions are favored in **performance and luxury EVs** where the trade-off between cost/complexity and performance/range is justified. For mass-market commuter EVs, single-speed reducers remain the industry standard.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **📋 Rapid Revision Checklist — Transmission in EVs**

- ✅ EV transmission is primarily for **speed reduction and torque multiplication**, NOT for managing narrow power bands (unlike ICE).
- ✅ **Single-speed fixed-ratio reducers** (ratios typically 8:1 to 12:1) dominate the EV market due to motor characteristics.
- ✅ **Key formulas** (must memorize):
  - $T_{\text{wheel}} = T_{\text{motor}} \cdot G \cdot \eta$
  - $V_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot N_{\text{motor}} \cdot r}{60 \cdot G}$
  - $F_t = \dfrac{T_{\text{motor}} \cdot G \cdot \eta}{r}$
- ✅ **Reverse gear in EVs** is achieved electronically by reversing the motor phase sequence — **no mechanical reverse gear needed**.
- ✅ **2-speed transmissions** (Porsche Taycan, Tesla Plaid) are used when **top speed > 250 km/h** or **sub-3-second 0–100 km/h** is desired.
- ✅ **In-wheel motors** (Lordstown, REE) eliminate the transmission entirely, reducing drivetrain losses by 10–15%.
- ✅ **Transmission efficiency** for single-stage gear reducers is **96–98%**; for multi-speed with shifting, **92–95%**.
- ✅ **Grade-ability calculation** requires: $F_t = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta)$ at constant speed; report as $\tan(\theta) \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **Common KTU mistakes**: forgetting unit conversion (RPM ↔ rad/s, km/h ↔ m/s), ignoring transmission efficiency, confusing tractive force with motor torque.
- ✅ **Tractive force** must overcome: $F_{\text{grade}} + F_{\text{rolling}} + F_{\text{aero}} + F_{\text{acceleration}}$ (Newton's 2nd law for vehicles).
- ✅ **Power at wheels** = $F_t \cdot V$ (W), and equals $T_{\text{motor}} \cdot \omega_{\text{motor}} \cdot \eta_{\text{trans}}$.
- ✅ **Overall drive ratio** $i_0 = G_{\text{gearbox}} \cdot G_{\text{differential}}$ (used for tractive force calculations when final drive is included).
- ✅ **Constant-torque region** of motor (low speed) and **constant-power region** (high speed) are why EVs avoid multi-speed gearboxes.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Exam Tip:** If a question asks "design a transmission for an EV," always start with: (1) state motor specs, (2) calculate $V_{\text{max}}$ target, (3) compute wheel RPM, (4) derive $G = N_{\text{motor,max}} / N_{\text{wheel,max}}$, (5) check tractive force and grade-ability. This 5-step framework guarantees full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: EV Charging Infrastructure and Technology: Fast Charger
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT631 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** EV Charging Infrastructure and Technology: Fast Charger**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standardized)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DC Fast Charger (Level 3 / Mode 4 Charger):** An off-board high-power Electric Vehicle Supply Equipment (EVSE) that converts **three-phase AC grid supply** into a regulated **DC current** and delivers it **directly to the traction battery**, bypassing the vehicle's onboard charger (OBC). It operates under the **IEC 61851-23** standard and falls within the **Mode 4 (DC charging)** classification of conductive charging.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** (Module 3), a Fast Charger is defined as a charging station capable of delivering power typically in the range of **50 kW to 400 kW** (with cutting-edge systems reaching **350 kW–600 kW**), allowing an EV to be charged from **20% to 80% State of Charge (SoC)** in approximately **20 to 30 minutes**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Codification:**
> - **IEC 61851-1** – General conductive charging system
> - **IEC 61851-23** – DC electric vehicle charging station
> - **IEC 62196-3** – DC charging connector specifications
> - **ISO 15118** – Vehicle-to-Grid Communication Interface (V2G)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The High-Pressure Diesel Pump vs. a Slow-Speed Petrol Dispenser":**
>
> Imagine you are filling water into a giant tank:
> - **Level 1 (Slow AC Charger, ~3 kW)** is like a *kitchen tap* — it can fill a bucket overnight, but you cannot use it to fill a swimming pool quickly.
> - **Level 2 (AC Fast Charger, ~7–22 kW)** is like a *garden hose* — works well at home, takes 4–6 hours.
> - **Level 3 (DC Fast Charger, ~50–350 kW)** is like a *fire hydrant hose attached directly to the municipal supply line* — water (energy) gushes directly into the tank bypassing your house's small pipe (the onboard charger). It is **powerful, industrial-grade, and bypasses intermediate conversion stages** to push massive current directly into the battery.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Benchmark Parameters for Fast Charging:**
> - **Nominal DC Voltage Range:** 200 V to 1000 V (Class A) and up to **1500 V** (Class B, IEC 61851-23:2023)
> - **Maximum Continuous Current:** 200 A (standard), 500 A (high-power cooled systems)
> - **Charging Efficiency:** **92% to 96%** (from grid to battery terminals)
> - **Power Factor (at PCC):** > **0.95** (mandated by grid codes)
> - **THD (Total Harmonic Distortion) at input:** < **8%** (IEEE 519 compliance)
> - **Communication Protocol:** CAN-based (CHAdeMO), PLC-based (ISO 15118 / CCS)
> - **Grid Frequency Tolerance:** 50 Hz ± **0.5 Hz** (Indian grid context)

### 1.4 Classification of Fast Chargers by Power Rating

| Class | Power Range | Charging Time (20→80% SoC) | Typical Application |
|-------|-------------|----------------------------|---------------------|
| **Mid-Power DCFC** | 50–75 kW | 30–45 min | Public stations, fleet depots |
| **High-Power DCFC** | 150–200 kW | 15–20 min | Highway corridors |
| **Ultra-Fast / HPC** | 350–600 kW | 8–12 min | Premium highway hubs, Tesla V4 |
| **Megawatt Charging (MCS)** | 1000+ kW | < 5 min | Heavy-duty trucks (EU/US) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture of a DC Fast Charger

A DC fast charger is functionally a **multi-stage power electronic converter** coupled with a **supervisory communication controller**. The breakdown is as follows:

#### Stage 1: AC Input & EMI Filtering
- A **three-phase 415 V, 50 Hz** AC supply is fed through an **LC/RC EMI filter** to suppress conducted noise.
- An **input circuit breaker (MCB/MCCB)** provides short-circuit and overcurrent protection.
- A **surge protection device (SPD, Type 2)** is installed to clamp grid transients.

#### Stage 2: Active Power Factor Correction (PFC) Stage
- A **three-phase 6-pulse or Vienna rectifier** acts as the PFC front-end.
- It boosts the DC bus voltage to **700 V – 800 V DC**.
- It forces the input current waveform to be sinusoidal and in-phase with the voltage, achieving **PF > 0.99** at full load.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why PFC is Mandatory:** EV chargers are non-linear loads. Without PFC, they would draw peaky current pulses rich in **3rd, 5th, and 7th harmonics**, violating **IEEE 519** and **IEC 61000-3-12** grid codes.

#### Stage 3: Isolated DC-DC Converter Stage
- An **LLC resonant converter** or **Dual-Active-Bridge (DAB)** topology provides galvanic isolation (mandatory by safety standards).
- It steps down the **800 V bus** to the **dynamic battery voltage** (200 V – 1000 V).
- High-frequency isolation transformer operates at **50 kHz – 200 kHz** to minimize size and weight.

#### Stage 4: Output Filter & Cable Management
- An **LC output filter** smooths the DC ripple to < **±1%** of setpoint.
- **Liquid-cooled charging cables** carry 500 A continuously without overheating.

#### Stage 5: Controller & Communication Module
- A **DSP / FPGA-based controller** executes the **CC-CV (Constant Current – Constant Voltage)** charging profile.
- It communicates with the **Battery Management System (BMS)** via:
  - **CAN bus** (CHAdeMO, GB/T)
  - **PLC over pilot wire** (CCS / ISO 15118)

### 2.2 The CC-CV Charging Profile (Heart of Fast Charging)

The BMS dictates the charging profile based on SoC, temperature, and chemistry:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phase 1 — Constant Current (Bulk Phase):** From 0% to ~70–80% SoC, the charger delivers **maximum rated current** while voltage ramps up. Power = $V \times I$ is approximately constant.
>
> **Phase 2 — Constant Voltage (Absorption Phase):** Beyond ~80% SoC, voltage is clamped at the **maximum charge voltage** (e.g., 4.2 V/cell for NMC), and current tapers exponentially to prevent **lithium plating**.
>
> **Phase 3 — Float / Cutoff:** Once current falls below a threshold (typically C/20), charging terminates.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Equation | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------------------|----------|-------|---------|
| 1 | **Energy Stored in Battery** | $E = V_{nom} \times C_{Ah}$ | Wh | Nominal pack voltage × Capacity |
| 2 | **Charging Time (Average Power Method)** | $t_c = \dfrac{\Delta SoC \times E_{pack}}{100 \times P_{avg} \times \eta_{ch}}$ | hours | Most common KTU question |
| 3 | **DC Output Power** | $P_{dc} = V_{batt} \times I_{batt}$ | W | Instantaneous battery-side power |
| 4 | **Input Power from Grid** | $P_{in} = \dfrac{P_{dc}}{\eta_{ch}}$ | W | Includes PFC + DC-DC losses |
| 5 | **Energy Conversion Efficiency** | $\eta_{ch} = \dfrac{P_{dc}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | % | Typically **92–96%** |
| 6 | **Apparent Power (3-φ AC)** | $S = \sqrt{3} \times V_L \times I_L$ | VA | For AC input side |
| 7 | **Real Power (3-φ AC)** | $P = \sqrt{3} \times V_L \times I_L \times \cos\phi$ | W | $\cos\phi$ is displacement PF |
| 8 | **Reactive Power** | $Q = \sqrt{3} \times V_L \times I_L \times \sin\phi$ | VAR | Compensated by PFC |
| 9 | **C-Rate (Charging)** | $C_{rate} = \dfrac{I_{charge}}{C_{Ah}}$ | dimensionless | E.g., 1C = full charge in 1 hour |
| 10 | **Cabling Power Loss** | $P_{loss} = I^2 \times R_{cable}$ | W | Dominates at high current |
| 11 | **Thermal Heat Generation** | $Q_{heat} = P_{loss} \times t$ | J | Cooled via liquid or forced air |
| 12 | **Charging Cost** | $C_{cost} = E_{delivered} \times \text{Unit Price}$ | ₹ / kWh | Energy billing parameter |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where Fast Chargers Are Deployed:**
> - **Highway Corridors (NHAI Expressways in India)** — Ultra-fast 150 kW+ hubs every 50–100 km
> - **Urban Fleet Hubs** — 60 kW DCFC for last-mile delivery EVs (Amazon, Flipkart fleets)
> - **Commercial EV Bus Depots** — 240 kW overnight opportunity charging (pantograph type)
> - **Service Stations (IOCL, BPCL, HPCL)** — Co-located with fuel pumps as per India’s "EV Charging on Highway" policy
> - **Workplace Charging** — Mid-power 30–50 kW DC units for staff vehicles
> - **Grid Services** — V2G-enabled fast chargers feeding power back to grid during peak hours (pilot stage in India)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Charging Time for a Given State-of-Charge Window

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Framing:** Given a battery pack with nominal energy $E_{pack}$, charger delivers an *average* power $P_{avg}$ at efficiency $\eta_{ch}$, find the time required to charge from $SoC_1$ to $SoC_2$.

**Step 1: Define the required energy delta**

The energy needed to be pumped into the battery is the difference in stored energy between the target SoC and the initial SoC:

$$
\Delta E = \frac{(SoC_2 - SoC_1)}{100} \times E_{pack}
$$

**Step 2: Account for charger efficiency**

Since the charger is not 100% efficient, the *grid energy* needed is higher than the *battery energy* gained. The grid energy required is:

$$
E_{grid} = \frac{\Delta E}{\eta_{ch}}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the time-energy-power relation**

Time is the ratio of energy to average power delivered by the charger:

$$
t_c = \frac{E_{grid}}{P_{avg}} = \frac{(SoC_2 - SoC_1) \times E_{pack}}{100 \times P_{avg} \times \eta_{ch}}
$$

**Step 4: State the final boxed result**

$$
\boxed{\,t_c \;=\; \frac{(SoC_2 - SoC_1) \times V_{nom} \times C_{Ah}}{100 \times P_{avg} \times \eta_{ch}}\,}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: AC Input Current Required for a Given DC Output

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** For a 150 kW DC fast charger operating at 96% efficiency and a 3-phase 415 V input, calculate the line current.

**Step 1: Compute input power**

$$
P_{in} = \frac{P_{dc}}{\eta_{ch}} = \frac{150{,}000}{0.96} = 156{,}250 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 2: Apply 3-phase real power formula with PF = 1 (after PFC)**

$$
P_{in} = \sqrt{3} \times V_L \times I_L \times \cos\phi
$$

**Step 3: Solve for line current**

$$
I_L = \frac{P_{in}}{\sqrt{3} \times V_L \times \cos\phi} = \frac{156{,}250}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 1.0}
$$

**Step 4: Evaluate numerically**

$$
I_L = \frac{156{,}250}{1.732 \times 415} = \frac{156{,}250}{718.78} = 217.4 \text{ A}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,I_L \approx 217.4 \text{ A per phase (line current)}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is precisely why fast charging stations need **dedicated transformer feeders** of 250 kVA to 500 kVA, and cannot be tapped from a household distribution transformer.

### 3.3 Derivation: Cable Power Loss for a 350 A Liquid-Cooled Cable

**Step 1: Cable resistance per meter (typical)**

For a 70 mm² liquid-cooled charging cable:

$$
R_{cable} \approx 0.28 \text{ m}\Omega/\text{m}
$$

**Step 2: Total cable loop length (positive + return)**

For a 5 m cable:

$$
R_{total} = 2 \times 0.28 \times 10^{-3} \times 5 = 2.8 \text{ m}\Omega
$$

**Step 3: Power loss at 350 A**

$$
P_{loss} = I^2 \times R_{total} = 350^2 \times 2.8 \times 10^{-3} = 122{,}500 \times 0.0028 = 343 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 4: Converted to heat per second**

$$
Q = 343 \text{ J/s} = 343 \text{ W}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is precisely why **liquid cooling is mandatory** above 200 A — without it, the cable insulation would melt within minutes.

### 3.4 Numerical Example (Comprehensive)

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **A 60 kWh NMC battery EV is fast-charged from 20% SoC to 80% SoC using a 150 kW DCFC with overall efficiency of 94%. Calculate the charging time.**

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Energy required**

$$
\Delta E = \frac{(80 - 20)}{100} \times 60 = 36 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 2: Energy from grid (efficiency-adjusted)**

$$
E_{grid} = \frac{36}{0.94} = 38.30 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 3: Time at 150 kW average**

$$
t_c = \frac{38.30 \times 1000}{150{,}000} = 0.2553 \text{ h} = 15.32 \text{ min}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,t_c \approx 15.3 \text{ minutes}\,}
$$

> [!TIP]
> In practice, the actual time is slightly longer (~20 min) because the BMS tapers current after 70% SoC to protect the battery — this is the **CC-CV taper effect**.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Charging Standards

| Standard | Region | Max Power | Max Current | Connector Type | Communication |
|----------|--------|-----------|-------------|----------------|----------------|
| **CHAdeMO** | Japan, Global | 400 kW | 400 A | Unique round | CAN |
| **CCS2 (Combo 2)** | EU, India (adopted) | 350 kW | 500 A | Type 2 + 2 DC pins | PLC / ISO 15118 |
| **Tesla NACS** | North America (adopted) | 250 kW | 631 A (V4) | Compact 5-pin | CAN |
| **GB/T** | China | 250 kW | 250 A | 9-pin | CAN |
| **Bharat DC-001** | India (legacy) | 15 kW | 57 A | GB/T variant | CAN |
| **IS 17017 / IEC 61851-23** | India (new) | Up to 230 kW | 200 A | CCS2 mandated | PLC + CAN |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Internal Architecture of a DC Fast Charger

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[3-Phase AC Grid Input 415V 50Hz] --> B1[EMI Filter and SPD]
    B1 --> C1[MCCB Circuit Breaker]
    C1 --> D1[Three Phase Vienna Rectifier with PFC]
    D1 --> E1[DC Link Capacitor Bank 700 to 800V]
    E1 --> F1[Dual Active Bridge DAB Isolated DC-DC]
    E1 --> F2[LLC Resonant Converter Stage]
    F1 --> G1[Output LC Filter]
    F2 --> G1
    G1 --> H1[Liquid Cooled Charging Cable]
    H1 --> I1[EV Battery Pack via CCS or CHAdeMO]
    J1[Controller DSP FPGA] --> D1
    J1 --> F1
    J1 --> F2
    K1[CAN Communication Module] --> J1
    L1[PLC Modem ISO 15118] --> J1
    M1[HMI Touchscreen Display] --> J1
    N1[Cloud Backend OCPP 1.6 or 2.0.1] --> J1
```

### 4.2 Flowchart: Fast Charging Sequence (Handshake Protocol)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start Driver Plugs CCS2 Connector into EV] --> S2[Charger Applies Low Voltage Pilot Signal 9V to Detect Connection]
    S2 --> S3{HVIL High Voltage Interlock Loop Closed?}
    S3 -- No --> S4[Abort and Display Fault on HMI]
    S3 -- Yes --> S5[Charger and BMS Perform Parameter Exchange via PLC or CAN]
    S5 --> S6[BMS Sends Battery Voltage Max Current Max SoC and Temperature Limits]
    S6 --> S7[Charger Computes Optimal V and I within BMS Envelope]
    S7 --> S8[Charger Closes Internal Contactors and Starts Constant Current Mode]
    S8 --> S9[Battery SoC Increasing Voltage Rising]
    S9 --> S10{SoC Reaches 80 percent Threshold?}
    S10 -- No --> S8
    S10 -- Yes --> S11[Transition to Constant Voltage Mode Current Tapers]
    S11 --> S12{Charging Current less than C by 20 cutoff?}
    S12 -- No --> S11
    S12 -- Yes --> S13[Charger Opens Contactors and Displays End of Session]
    S13 --> S14[Driver Unplugs Cable and Departs]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: CC-CV Charging Profile Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Phase 1 Constant Current 0 to 70 percent SoC] --> P2[Phase 2 Constant Voltage 70 to 100 percent SoC]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3 Termination or Float]
    P1 -. Current Constant Voltage Rising .-> P1
    P2 -. Voltage Constant Current Falling .-> P2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of Diagram 4.3:**
> - **Phase 1 (CC):** Current is clamped at $I_{max}$; voltage rises from $V_{min}$ to $V_{max}$.
> - **Phase 2 (CV):** Voltage is clamped at $V_{max}$; current tapers exponentially.
> - **Phase 3:** Current falls below C/20 cutoff → charging terminates safely.

### 4.4 Schematic: Communication Stack of a CCS2 Fast Charger

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Application Layer ISO 15118 Plug and Charge and EIM] --> L2[Transport Layer TCP and IP over Power Line Communication]
    L2 --> L3[Data Link Layer HomePlug Green PHY]
    L3 --> L4[Physical Layer Combined Charging System Pilot Pin]
    L4 --> L5[EVSE Hardware CCS2 Inlet]
    L5 --> L6[Vehicle Communication Controller VCC]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The CCS2 standard uses **Power Line Communication (HomePlug Green PHY)** to send IP-based data over the **Control Pilot (CP) pin**, allowing high-bandwidth **Plug & Charge** authentication (no RFID card needed). CHAdeMO, in contrast, uses a **dedicated CAN bus** — simpler but lower bandwidth.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*Define a DC Fast Charger. List any two international standards governing fast charging of electric vehicles.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** A DC Fast Charger is an off-board EV supply equipment that directly delivers DC power to the vehicle's traction battery, bypassing the onboard charger. It typically operates in the power range of **50 kW to 350 kW**, enabling 20–80% SoC charging in 20–30 minutes.

> **Standards (1 mark):**
> 1. **IEC 61851-23** – DC charging station requirements
> 2. **CHAdeMO / CCS / ISO 15118** – Communication and connector protocols

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Compare AC Level 2 charging with DC Level 3 fast charging in terms of converter location, charging time, and power range.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | AC Level 2 | DC Level 3 (Fast) |
|-----------|------------|-------------------|
| **Converter Location** | Onboard (inside vehicle) | Off-board (in station) |
| **Power Range** | 7–22 kW | 50–350 kW |
| **Charging Time (20→80% SoC)** | 4–6 hours | 15–30 minutes |
| **Connector Type** | Type 1 / Type 2 | CCS2 / CHAdeMO / NACS |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A [14 Marks] — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model — Module 3 Carry Over]**

> **a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **DC fast charging station**. Clearly show the **PFC stage, isolation DC-DC converter, output filter, and controller**. *(7 marks)*
>
> **b)** A **50 kWh lithium-ion battery EV** is fast-charged from **15% to 80% SoC** using a **120 kW DCFC** with an overall efficiency of **93%**. Calculate the **charging time, energy drawn from the grid (in kWh), and the AC input line current** (assume 3-phase 415 V supply, PF = 1). *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

> **Step 1: Introduction (1 mark)**
> A DC fast charging station converts grid AC into a regulated DC output and supplies it **directly** to the EV's traction battery. The OBC is bypassed, allowing high power delivery.

> **Step 2: Block Diagram Explanation (4 marks)**
> *(Refer to SECTION 4.1 above for the full schematic.)*
> - **PFC Rectifier Stage:** Vienna/6-pulse rectifier boosts PF to >0.99 and provides 800 V DC bus.
> - **Isolated DC-DC Stage:** DAB or LLC topology provides galvanic isolation and regulates output to battery voltage (200–1000 V).
> - **Output LC Filter:** Smooths ripple to < ±1%.
> - **Liquid-Cooled Cable:** Carries 200–500 A without overheating.
> - **Controller (DSP/FPGA):** Executes CC-CV profile, communicates via CAN or PLC.

> **Step 3: Communication Protocol (2 marks)**
> - **CHAdeMO** uses CAN bus; **CCS2** uses PLC over pilot pin (ISO 15118).
> - The BMS dictates maximum allowable voltage and current based on SoC, temperature, and SoH.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

> **Step 1: Energy required (1 mark)**

$$
\Delta E = \frac{(80 - 15)}{100} \times 50 = 32.5 \text{ kWh}
$$

> **Step 2: Grid energy required (1 mark)**

$$
E_{grid} = \frac{32.5}{0.93} = 34.95 \text{ kWh}
$$

> **Step 3: Charging time (2 marks)**

$$
t_c = \frac{34.95 \times 1000}{120{,}000} = 0.2912 \text{ h} = 17.47 \text{ min}
$$

> **Step 4: Input power (1 mark)**

$$
P_{in} = \frac{120{,}000}{0.93} = 129{,}032 \text{ W}
$$

> **Step 5: AC line current (2 marks)**

$$
I_L = \frac{P_{in}}{\sqrt{3} \times V_L} = \frac{129{,}032}{1.732 \times 415} = \frac{129{,}032}{718.78} = 179.5 \text{ A}
$$

> **Final Answer Box:**

$$
\boxed{\,t_c = 17.47 \text{ min} \quad E_{grid} = 34.95 \text{ kWh} \quad I_L = 179.5 \text{ A}\,}
$$

---

### **Question B [14 Marks] — Option 2 (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model — Module 3]**

> **a)** Explain the **CC-CV charging profile** with a suitable voltage-current diagram. Why is the current tapered after 80% SoC? *(7 marks)*
>
> **b)** Derive the expression for the **charging time** of an EV battery in terms of battery energy, SoC change, charger power, and efficiency. A **350 V, 75 Ah EV battery** is to be charged from **20% to 90% SoC** using a **50 kW DCFC** with **94% efficiency**. Compute the charging time. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

> **Step 1: CC Phase (3 marks)**
> During the **Constant Current (CC) phase**, the charger delivers a fixed maximum current (e.g., 2C) while battery voltage rises linearly. This phase covers roughly **0% to 70–80% SoC** and is the fastest regime because power is at its peak ($P = V \times I$, both at maximum).

> **Step 2: CV Phase (2 marks)**
> Once the battery reaches its **maximum safe voltage** (e.g., 4.2 V/cell for NMC = 400 V for a 96-cell pack), the charger holds the voltage constant. Current begins to decay exponentially as the cell's internal resistance impedes further charge acceptance.

> **Step 3: Why Taper? (2 marks)**
> - **Lithium Plating Prevention:** Sustained high current near 100% SoC causes metallic lithium to deposit on the anode, permanently degrading capacity and posing a thermal runaway risk.
> - **Anode Potential Protection:** Lowering current keeps the anode potential above 0 V vs. Li/Li⁺, the safe threshold.
> - **Cycle Life Enhancement:** Tapering reduces mechanical stress on the SEI layer, extending cycle life.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

> **Step 1: Derivation (3 marks)**

Starting from energy balance:

$$
t_c = \frac{\Delta E_{batt}}{P_{dc} \times \eta_{ch}} = \frac{(SoC_2 - SoC_1) \times E_{pack}}{100 \times P_{avg} \times \eta_{ch}}
$$

Substituting $E_{pack} = V_{nom} \times C_{Ah}$:

$$
\boxed{\,t_c = \frac{(SoC_2 - SoC_1) \times V_{nom} \times C_{Ah}}{100 \times P_{avg} \times \eta_{ch}}\,}
$$

> **Step 2: Numerical substitution (2 marks)**

Given: $V_{nom} = 350$ V, $C_{Ah} = 75$ Ah, $P_{avg} = 50$ kW, $\eta_{ch} = 0.94$, $SoC_2 - SoC_1 = 90 - 20 = 70$%

$$
E_{pack} = 350 \times 75 = 26{,}250 \text{ Wh} = 26.25 \text{ kWh}
$$

$$
\Delta E = \frac{70}{100} \times 26.25 = 18.375 \text{ kWh}
$$

> **Step 3: Final time calculation (2 marks)**

$$
t_c = \frac{18.375}{50 \times 0.94} = \frac{18.375}{47} = 0.3910 \text{ h} = 23.46 \text{ min}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,t_c \approx 23.46 \text{ minutes}\,}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing **kW with Wh** (or hours with seconds) is the #1 mark-loss reason. Always convert to **kW·h ÷ kW = h** before multiplying by 60 for minutes.
> 2. **Forgetting Efficiency:** Many students write $t_c = \frac{\Delta E}{P}$ and skip the $\eta_{ch}$ term, losing 1 full mark.
> 3. **Confusing AC vs DC Power:** The "AC input current" question requires $P_{in} = P_{dc}/\eta_{ch}$, not $P_{dc}$ itself.
> 4. **Ignoring CC-CV Taper:** Never claim the charging time is purely $E/P$. The taper region (~70→100% SoC) takes nearly 40% of the total time despite transferring only 20% of energy.
> 5. **No Standards Mentioned:** Writing about fast chargers without naming **IEC 61851-23 / ISO 15118** is treated as incomplete by KTU evaluators.
> 6. **Wrong $\sqrt{3}$ Factor:** For 3-phase current, the formula $I = P/(\sqrt{3} V_L \cos\phi)$ is mandatory; using $I = P/V$ (single-phase) loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Fast Charger**
>
> ✅ Fast charger = **off-board DC supply** bypassing the **onboard charger (OBC)**.
>
> ✅ Classified as **Mode 4** charging per **IEC 61851-1**; detailed in **IEC 61851-23**.
>
> ✅ **Power range:** 50 kW (Level 1 DCFC) → 350 kW (HPC) → 1000+ kW (MCS for trucks).
>
> ✅ **Connector standards:** CHAdeMO (Japan), **CCS2 (EU/India)**, NACS (Tesla/US), GB/T (China).
>
> ✅ **India-mandated standard:** **Bharat DC-001** (legacy) → **CCS2** (current adoption for >15 kW).
>
> ✅ **Power electronics stages:** AC → EMI filter → PFC (Vienna rectifier, PF > 0.95) → DC link (800 V) → Isolated DAB/LLC DC-DC → Output filter → Cable → Battery.
>
> ✅ **CC-CV profile:** Constant current up to ~80% SoC, then constant voltage taper.
>
> ✅ **Communication:** CHAdeMO = CAN; CCS2 = PLC over pilot (HomePlug Green PHY, ISO 15118).
>
> ✅ **Cloud protocol:** **OCPP 1.6 / 2.0.1** (Open Charge Point Protocol) for backend management.
>
> ✅ **Critical formulas:**
> - $t_c = \frac{\Delta SoC \times E_{pack}}{100 \times P_{avg} \times \eta_{ch}}$
> - $I_L = \frac{P_{in}}{\sqrt{3} \times V_L \times \cos\phi}$
> - $P_{loss,cable} = I^2 \times R_{cable}$
>
> ✅ **Efficiency:** 92–96% grid-to-battery.
>
> ✅ **Cooling:** Liquid cooling mandatory for > 200 A operation; air cooling only up to ~150 A.
>
> ✅ **Bidirectional (V2G):** Modern fast chargers support **ISO 15118-20 V2G** for vehicle-to-grid feedback.
>
> ✅ **Safety:** HVIL loop, isolation monitoring (≤ 100 Ω/V), ground fault detection, emergency stop, IP54 enclosure rating.
>
> ✅ **Indian context:** Government has mandated **Bharat Public EV Charging** standards; IS 17017 aligned with IEC 61851; CEA notification on EV charging infrastructure (2024 amendment) requires **40 kW minimum at every highway station**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DC Charger
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT631 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLES
