> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** DC Charger

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DC Charger (DC Fast Charger / Level 3 EVSE)**: A stationary off-board electric vehicle supply equipment (EVSE) that converts **grid-side Alternating Current (AC)** into **regulated Direct Current (DC)** and delivers it directly to the Electric Vehicle's (EV) high-voltage traction battery, **bypassing the limited-capacity onboard charger (OBC)**. It operates typically in the power range of **50 kW to 350 kW**, enabling rapid replenishment of battery State of Charge (SoC).

According to the **IEC 61851-23** standard, a DC charger is officially classified as "Mode 4" charging, characterized by an external AC-DC converter with a dedicated EV-EVSE digital communication protocol (such as **CHAdeMO**, **CCS-Combo 2**, or **GB/T**).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Direct Pipeline vs. The Bucket Relay"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Real-world Analogy)**: Imagine filling a swimming pool with water.
> - **AC Level 1/2 (Slow Charging)** is like carrying water in a small bucket from a distant tap (Grid → Onboard Charger → Battery). The bucket size (OBC rating, typically 3.3 kW to 22 kW) limits the flow rate.
> - **DC Fast Charging (Level 3)** is like running a high-pressure fire hose directly from a water tanker straight into the pool (Grid → External Rectifier → Battery). The bottleneck of the onboard bucket is completely eliminated, allowing water (current) to pour in at a tremendous rate.
>
> The "fire hose" is the **thick DC charging cable** with heavy-duty liquid cooling, and the "high-pressure pump" is the **high-power silicon-carbide (SiC) or IGBT-based rectifier** sitting inside the charging station cabinet.

### 1.3 Why DC Charging is Essential — Key Metrics

| Parameter | Slow AC Charging | DC Fast Charging |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Power Rating** | **3.3 kW – 22 kW** | **50 kW – 350 kW** |
| **Location of Rectifier** | Inside the car (OBC) | **Inside the charger** |
| **Time to charge 100 kWh** | 5 – 30 hours | **15 – 60 minutes** |
| **Cable Thickness** | Thin, light | Very thick, **liquid-cooled** |
| **Typical Use Case** | Home/Overnight | Highway corridors, Fleets |

### 1.4 Key Engineering Constants & Standards

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Standards Governing DC Charging:**
> - **IEC 61851-23**: DC charging system requirements.
> - **IEC 61851-24**: Digital communication for DC charging control.
> - **ISO 15118**: Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) communication interface.
> - **CHAdeMO Protocol**: Japanese-origin DC standard, max **400 V / 1000 V** architectures.
> - **CCS-Combo 2 (Combined Charging System)**: European/American standard supporting up to **350 kW**.
> - **GB/T 27930**: Chinese national DC standard.

### 1.5 Primary Functional Block Summary

A DC Charger fundamentally consists of four mandatory subsystems:
1. **Input Section**: 3-phase AC grid connection with isolation transformer and surge protection.
2. **Power Conversion Stage (AC-DC)**: Active rectifier with Power Factor Correction (PFC).
3. **Output Stage (DC-DC)**: Isolated high-frequency DC-DC converter for galvanic isolation and voltage matching to the EV battery.
4. **Supervisory Controller**: Embedded processor running the high-level CAN/PLC communication stack to negotiate voltage/current with the Battery Management System (BMS) of the EV.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — "The Why and The How"

#### Step 1: Handshake & Authentication
The user inserts the DC connector. The charger's **EVSE Controller** establishes a high-level digital link (CAN for CHAdeMO/GB-T or **PLC over CP/PP** for CCS) with the vehicle's **Battery Management System (BMS)**.
> **Why?** The BMS is the only authoritative source that knows the exact battery voltage, SoC, cell temperature, and maximum safe charging current. The DC charger *cannot* push current blindly; it must be commanded by the battery.

#### Step 2: Parameter Negotiation
The BMS transmits a charging profile:
- $V_{\text{max}}$ (maximum battery terminal voltage allowed, e.g., **400 V** or **800 V** architecture)
- $I_{\text{max}}$ (maximum C-rate, e.g., 2C or 3C)
- $P_{\text{max}}$ (thermal limit of cable/connector)
- $T_{\text{cutoff}}$ (temperature derating thresholds)

#### Step 3: Pre-charge & Insulation Check
The charger's output contactors close against a pre-charge resistor, and an **Insulation Monitoring Device (IMD)** verifies there is no leakage to chassis ground (typically requiring **> 100 kΩ/V** of insulation resistance).

#### Step 4: Constant Current / Constant Voltage (CC-CV) Power Delivery
The charger executes a closed-loop control:
- **CC Phase**: The current is held constant at $I_{\text{max}}$. The battery voltage rises gradually.
- **CV Phase**: Once $V_{\text{batt}}$ reaches the cutoff (e.g., 4.2 V/cell × 96s = 403 V), current tapers off exponentially.

#### Step 5: Session Termination
When the BMS signals SoC ≥ target% or when current drops below a threshold (e.g., 5% of rated), the EVSE ramps down to zero, opens contactors, and the session ends.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following mathematical models are essential for solving KTU numerical problems on DC charger design and analysis. Master these derivations.

| # | Formula / Equation | Physical Meaning / Application | Variables & Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $P_{\text{ch}} = V_{\text{batt}} \cdot I_{\text{ch}}$ | Instantaneous charging power delivered to battery | $P$ in **kW**, $V$ in **V**, $I$ in **A** |
| 2 | $E_{\text{del}} = \int_{0}^{t} V_{\text{batt}}(t) \cdot I_{\text{ch}}(t) \, dt$ | Total energy delivered during the session | $E$ in **kWh** |
| 3 | $t_{\text{ch}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{bat}} \cdot \Delta \text{SoC}}{P_{\text{ch}} \cdot \eta_{\text{ch}}}$ | Approximate charging time from initial to final SoC | $Q$ in **kWh**, $t$ in **h**, $\eta$ dimensionless |
| 4 | $\eta_{\text{total}} = \eta_{\text{PFC}} \cdot \eta_{\text{DC-DC}}$ | Cascaded efficiency of the two-stage converter | $\eta$ dimensionless |
| 5 | $C\text{-rate} = \dfrac{I_{\text{ch}}}{Q_{\text{bat}}}$ | Charging aggressiveness; 1C = full charge in 1 h | $I$ in **A**, $Q$ in **Ah** |
| 6 | $P_{\text{loss}} = P_{\text{in}} \left( 1 - \eta_{\text{total}} \right)$ | Heat that must be dissipated by the cabinet cooling system | $P$ in **W** |
| 7 | $\text{THD}_I = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} I_h^2}}{I_1} \times 100\%$ | Total Harmonic Distortion of input current (must be < 5% per IEEE 519) | Dimensionless (%) |
| 8 | $\text{PF} = \cos \phi_1 \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \text{THD}_I^2}}$ | Displacement and distortion power factor of the rectifier | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $R_{\text{insul, min}} = 100 \text{ k}\Omega / V_{\text{DC,max}}$ | IEC 61851 minimum insulation resistance threshold | $R$ in **Ω**, $V$ in **V** |
| 10 | $I_{\text{out, max}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{out, max}}}{V_{\text{batt, min}}}$ | Worst-case output current demand at low SoC | $I$ in **A**, $P$ in **W** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**: These equations are used directly in KTU numerical problems to calculate (a) charging time for a given battery capacity, (b) sizing of the PFC inductor, (c) thermal design of the heat sink, and (d) verifying grid code compliance for harmonic injection.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Trade-offs

| Design Choice | Advantage | Disadvantage |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Two-Stage (PFC + DAB)** | High efficiency (>96%), excellent isolation, modular | More components, higher cost |
| **Single-Stage (Matrix Charger)** | Compact, lower BOM cost | Complex control, no galvanic isolation (needs grid transformer) |
| **SiC MOSFETs** | High switching frequency, low loss | Expensive wafers |
| **Air Cooling** | Cheap, simple | Limited to ~50 kW; noisy |
| **Liquid Cooling** | Enables 350 kW+ in compact enclosure | Pumps, leaks, maintenance overhead |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Charging Time for a CC-CV Profile

**Problem Context**: An EV with a **75 kWh** traction battery is to be charged from **20% SoC to 80% SoC** using a **150 kW** DC fast charger. The overall charger-to-battery efficiency is **94%**. Compute the approximate charging time, assuming the charger delivers constant **150 kW** throughout the bulk phase.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 1 — Identify the Energy Gap**
> The amount of energy that must be replenished is the capacity multiplied by the SoC change.
> Let $\Delta \text{SoC} = 80\% - 20\% = 60\% = 0.60$.

$$E_{\text{required}} = Q_{\text{bat}} \cdot \Delta \text{SoC}$$

$$E_{\text{required}} = 75 \text{ kWh} \times 0.60 = 45 \text{ kWh}$$

> **Step 2 — Account for Conversion Losses**
> The charger input power $P_{\text{in}}$ must overcome the wall-to-battery efficiency. The power that *actually reaches* the battery is the output power.

$$P_{\text{out}} = P_{\text{in}} \cdot \eta_{\text{ch}}$$

$$P_{\text{out}} = 150 \text{ kW} \times 0.94 = 141 \text{ kW}$$

> **Step 3 — Solve for Time**
> Since energy is the product of power and time, $E = P \cdot t$, rearrange for $t$:

$$t = \dfrac{E_{\text{required}}}{P_{\text{out}}}$$

$$t = \dfrac{45 \text{ kWh}}{141 \text{ kW}}$$

$$\boxed{t = 0.319 \text{ h} \approx 19.15 \text{ minutes}}$$

> **Step 4 — Sanity Check & Engineering Insight**
> In reality, the last 20% (above 80% SoC) takes almost as long as the first 60% because the BMS throttles current to protect the cells. So real-world time = 19 min (bulk) + ~25 min (tapering) ≈ **44 minutes total**.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Worst-Case Output Current & Conductor Sizing

**Problem Context**: A DC fast charger is rated at **125 kW** DC output. It must charge an EV operating on a **400 V** nominal battery architecture. Determine (a) the maximum output current the converter must deliver, and (b) the minimum cross-sectional area of copper cable required if current density is limited to **5 A/mm²**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 1 — Determine the Lowest Battery Voltage**
> The highest current demand occurs at the lowest battery voltage (lowest SoC), because $I = P / V$. For a 400 V nominal Li-ion pack, the minimum is typically around **350 V** (deepest discharge state).

$$V_{\text{batt, min}} = 350 \text{ V}$$

> **Step 2 — Calculate Maximum Output Current**
> Apply Ohm's law rearranged for power:

$$I_{\text{out, max}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{max}}}{V_{\text{batt, min}}}$$

$$I_{\text{out, max}} = \dfrac{125{,}000 \text{ W}}{350 \text{ V}}$$

$$\boxed{I_{\text{out, max}} \approx 357.14 \text{ A}}$$

> **Step 3 — Calculate Required Conductor Cross-Section**
> The current density $J$ is current per unit area. The area needed to safely carry 357.14 A is:

$$A_{\text{cond}} = \dfrac{I_{\text{out, max}}}{J}$$

$$A_{\text{cond}} = \dfrac{357.14 \text{ A}}{5 \text{ A/mm}^2}$$

$$\boxed{A_{\text{cond}} = 71.43 \text{ mm}^2}$$

> **Step 4 — Engineering Justification for Liquid Cooling**
> A copper conductor of 71 mm² cross-section is roughly the diameter of a human thumb. This is why the CHAdeMO/CCS connector pins and cables are actively liquid-cooled — otherwise the I²R heating would melt the insulation in seconds.

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3 — Input Current Harmonic Compliance

**Problem Context**: A 50 kW DC charger draws from a 3-phase, **415 V (line-to-line)** grid. It uses an active PFC front-end. Calculate (a) the RMS line current, and (b) the maximum allowable 5th harmonic component such that the THD remains below **5%** (IEEE 519 limit for equipment > 20 kW).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 1 — Apparent and Real Power (assuming PF ≈ 1.0 with active PFC)**

$$S \approx P = 50 \text{ kW}$$

$$I_{\text{line}} = \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{LL} \cdot \text{PF}}$$

$$I_{\text{line}} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 1.0}$$

$$I_{\text{line}} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{718.66}$$

$$I_{\text{line}} \approx 69.6 \text{ A}$$

> **Step 2 — Defining the THD Constraint**
> Per IEEE 519, THD is defined as:

$$\text{THD}_I = \dfrac{\sqrt{I_5^2 + I_7^2 + I_{11}^2 + \cdots}}{I_1} \leq 0.05$$

> **Step 3 — Worst-Case Single-Harmonic Approximation**
> If we assume the dominant harmonic is the 5th (typical for 6-pulse rectifiers), and all others are negligible, then:

$$0.05 = \dfrac{I_5}{I_1}$$

$$I_{5, \text{max}} = 0.05 \times 69.6 \text{ A}$$

$$\boxed{I_{5, \text{max}} = 3.48 \text{ A}}$$

> **Step 4 — Engineering Insight**
> A passive 6-pulse diode bridge produces a 5th harmonic of roughly **20–25%** of fundamental. To bring this down to 5%, the front-end *must* be an **active Vienna rectifier or 3-level NPC topology** with PWM control — this is the reason modern DC fast chargers are not simple diode bridges.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Charger Topology Comparison

| Topology | Galvanic Isolation | Peak Efficiency | Power Density | Used In |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **2-Stage: PFC + DAB** | Yes (HF transformer) | 96–97% | Medium | Tesla V3, ABB Terra HP |
| **2-Stage: PFC + LLC** | Yes (resonant) | 97–98% | High | Most 150 kW CCS stations |
| **Single-Stage Matrix** | No (requires LF transformer) | 95% | Very High | Research prototypes |
| **Modular Multilevel (MMC)** | Yes | 98%+ | Low | Ultra-fast 350–600 kW |
| **3-Level NPC PFC** | Built into topology | 97% | High | European CCS hardware |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Functional Architecture of a DC Fast Charger

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete signal, power, and communication flow of a 150 kW DC fast charger, from the 3-phase AC grid input all the way to the EV battery terminals.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[GRID 3-PHASE 415V AC] --> B[INPUT EMI FILTER AND SURGE PROTECTION]
    B --> C[ISOLATION TRANSFORMER 11KV TO 415V]
    C --> D[ACTIVE VIENNA RECTIFIER PFC STAGE]
    D --> E[DC LINK CAPACITOR BANK 800V DC]
    E --> F[ISOLATED DC DC LLC CONVERTER]
    F --> G[OUTPUT FILTER INDUCTOR AND CAPACITOR]
    G --> H[OUTPUT CONTACTOR WITH PRECHARGE]
    H --> I[INSULATION MONITORING DEVICE IMD]
    I --> J[LIQUID COOLED DC GUN CCS OR CHADEMO]
    J --> K[EV BATTERY PACK 400V OR 800V]
    L[SUPERVISORY CONTROLLER DSP FPGA] --> D
    L --> F
    L --> H
    M[HMI TOUCH DISPLAY AND RFID] --> L
    N[BACKEND OCPP SERVER CLOUD] --> L
    O[EV BMS] -. CAN OR PLC .-> L
```

### 4.2 State Machine — DC Charging Session Lifecycle

This diagram shows the discrete handshake states the charger and vehicle transition through during a typical charging session. This is a *high-yield* topic for KTU short-answer questions.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> A_IDLE
    A_IDLE --> B_CONNECTED: User inserts gun and connector latch detected
    B_CONNECTED --> C_HANDSHAKE: EVSE initiates digital communication via PLC or CAN
    C_HANDSHAKE --> D_PARAMETER_EXCHANGE: BMS publishes limits Vmax Imax Tmax
    D_PARAMETER_EXCHANGE --> E_CABLE_CHECK: Proximity pilot and insulation test
    E_CABLE_CHECK --> F_PRECHARGE: Output capacitor ramped to match battery voltage
    F_PRECHARGE --> G_ENERGY_TRANSFER: Closed loop CC CV control active
    G_ENERGY_TRANSFER --> H_TAPER: BMS reduces current as SoC approaches 100 percent
    H_TAPER --> G_ENERGY_TRANSFER: Current above 5 percent threshold
    G_ENERGY_TRANSFER --> I_TERMINATION: SoC target reached or user stop
    I_TERMINATION --> J_SESSION_BILLING: OCPP transaction finalized
    J_SESSION_BILLING --> K_DISCONNECT: Contactors open and gun unlocked
    K_DISCONNECT --> A_IDLE
```

### 4.3 Power Electronics — Two-Stage Converter Signal Path

The block diagram below illustrates the typical control loops of a 2-stage AC-DC + DC-DC fast charger. The PFC stage regulates the DC-link voltage, while the DC-DC stage regulates the charging current/voltage command from the BMS.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FrontEnd [ACTIVE PFC STAGE]
        pfcIn[3PH AC INPUT] --> pfcRec[VIENNA RECTIFIER SIC MOSFET]
        pfcRec --> pfcSense[CURRENT SENSOR LEM LA 100P]
        pfcSense --> pfcCtrl[VOLTAGE MODE CONTROLLER PI LOOP]
        pfcCtrl --> pfcGate[GATE DRIVER SI8271]
        pfcGate --> pfcRec
    end
    subgraph DCBus [INTERMEDIATE DC LINK]
        dclink[800V DC BUS CAPACITOR 2 X 4.7mF]
        pfcRec --> dclink
    end
    subgraph DCDCDwn [ISOLATED DC DC STAGE]
        dclink --> llcInv[LLC FULL BRIDGE INVERTER]
        llcInv --> hftx[HF TRANSFORMER 1 TO 2 RATIO]
        hftx --> llcRec[SECONDARY SYNCHRONOUS RECTIFIER]
        llcRec --> outFilter[LC OUTPUT FILTER]
        outFilter --> battOut[BATTERY TERMINALS 400V NOMINAL]
        outSense[VOLTAGE AND CURRENT SENSE] --> battOut
        outSense --> dcCtrl[CURRENT AND VOLTAGE CONTROLLER]
        dcCtrl --> llcInv
    end
```

### 4.4 Communication Protocol Stack

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[PHYSICAL LAYER CAN 500 KBPS OR PLC HOMEPLUG GREEN PHY] --> L2[DATA LINK LAYER ISO 11898]
    L2 --> L3[TRANSPORT LAYER ISO 15770 OR SAE J2847]
    L3 --> L4[APPLICATION LAYER DIN 70121 OR ISO 15118]
    L4 --> L5[BMS PARAMETER MESSAGE CHARGE STATUS SOC]
    L4 --> L6[EVSE COMMAND MESSAGE VOLTAGE CURRENT]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
*Define a DC fast charger for an electric vehicle. How does it differ functionally from a Level 2 AC charger?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A DC fast charger (Level 3 / Mode 4 per IEC 61851-23) is an off-board EV supply equipment that directly delivers DC power to the traction battery, **bypassing the onboard charger (OBC)**. **(1 mark)**
- The AC-DC rectification and isolation happen inside the external charger cabinet. **(1 mark)**
- Functionally, it differs from Level 2 AC charging in three ways: (i) power rating is much higher (50–350 kW vs. ≤ 22 kW); (ii) it requires digital handshake (CAN/PLC) with the BMS; (iii) the cable is liquid-cooled due to high current (> 200 A). **(1 mark)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Exam]**
*List any three DC charging standards/connectors used globally and state their maximum power rating.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **CHAdeMO** (Japan-origin) — up to **400 kW** (CHAdeMO 2.0). **(1 mark)**
2. **CCS-Combo 2** (Europe/US) — up to **350 kW** as per IEC 61851-23. **(1 mark)**
3. **GB/T 27930** (China) — up to **250 kW** in current revision. **(1 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**: Students often write "Tesla Supercharger" as a *standard*. It is a *proprietary connector* used only by Tesla vehicles (now opening up to CCS in some markets). Do not list it as an international DC charging standard.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Extended Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Slot]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a 2-stage DC fast charger for an electric vehicle. Discuss the function of each block in detail. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**
A 2-stage DC fast charger consists of an **AC-DC front-end (PFC stage)** followed by an **isolated DC-DC stage**. The block diagram is as shown in Section 4.1 of these notes.

*Stage 1 — PFC Rectifier (AC to DC):* A 3-phase, 415 V AC supply from the grid is first passed through an **EMI filter** to suppress conducted noise, then through a **surge protection** MOV. A step-down **isolation transformer** (11 kV/415 V) provides galvanic isolation from the utility. The **active Vienna rectifier** using SiC MOSFETs rectifies the AC into an intermediate **800 V DC link** while maintaining a near-unity power factor (> 0.99) and input current THD < 5% per IEEE 519. **(3 marks)**

*Stage 2 — Isolated DC-DC (DC to regulated DC):* The 800 V DC link is fed to an **LLC resonant converter** (or Dual Active Bridge) which provides (i) high-frequency (50–100 kHz) galvanic isolation via an HF transformer, and (ii) step-down/step-up voltage matching to the battery (200–800 V range). The **secondary synchronous rectifier** with low-voltage Si MOSFETs converts HF AC back to DC, followed by an **LC output filter** to remove switching ripple. **(2 marks)**

*Control & Communication:* A **DSP/FPGA-based supervisory controller** runs a dual-loop PI control: the outer voltage loop regulates DC-link to 800 V, and the inner current loop regulates output current per the BMS command. The **CAN/PLC communication stack** (ISO 15118/DIN 70121) continuously exchanges parameters (V, I, T) with the vehicle's BMS every ~100 ms. **(2 marks)**

**(b)** A 60 kWh EV battery is to be charged from 10% SoC to 80% SoC using a 120 kW DC fast charger with a wall-to-battery efficiency of 92%. Calculate (i) the total energy to be supplied by the charger, (ii) the energy delivered to the battery, and (iii) the approximate charging time. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 1 — Total Energy to be Supplied by the Charger (Input Energy)**
> The battery requires $Q \times \Delta \text{SoC}$ of energy. The charger must supply this *plus* its own losses.

$$\Delta \text{SoC} = 80\% - 10\% = 70\% = 0.70$$

$$E_{\text{batt, req}} = 60 \text{ kWh} \times 0.70 = 42 \text{ kWh}$$

$$E_{\text{input}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{batt, req}}}{\eta_{\text{ch}}} = \dfrac{42}{0.92}$$

$$E_{\text{input}} = 45.65 \text{ kWh} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}$$

> **Step 2 — Energy Delivered to the Battery**
> This is simply the requirement from Step 1.

$$\boxed{E_{\text{batt, delivered}} = 42 \text{ kWh}} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

> **Step 3 — Charging Time**
> Assuming the charger delivers its rated 120 kW throughout the bulk CC phase:

$$t = \dfrac{E_{\text{batt, req}}}{P_{\text{ch}} \cdot \eta_{\text{ch}}} = \dfrac{42}{120 \times 0.92}$$

$$t = \dfrac{42}{110.4}$$

$$\boxed{t = 0.380 \text{ h} \approx 22.8 \text{ minutes}} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Main Slot]

**(a)** Compare the CHAdeMO and CCS-Combo 2 DC fast charging standards with respect to (i) communication protocol, (ii) maximum supported voltage and current, (iii) connector design, and (iv) V2G capability. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | **CHAdeMO** | **CCS-Combo 2** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (i) Communication | Dedicated **CAN bus** (CAN 2.0B at 500 kbps) over a separate pin pair. | **Power Line Communication (PLC)** modulated on the **CP/PP pilot pins** (HomePlug Green PHY). No extra pins. **(2 marks)** |
| (ii) Max V / I | Up to **1000 V / 400 A** (CHAdeMO 2.0), max **400 kW**. | Up to **1000 V / 500 A** (CCS 2.0), max **350–500 kW** in production. **(2 marks)** |
| (iii) Connector | Large, **round** connector with **separate** AC and DC ports. | **Combo** design — AC + DC pins integrated into a single Type-2 inlet on the vehicle. More compact. **(1.5 marks)** |
| (iv) V2G | **Native V2G** supported since CHAdeMO 1.0 (2012). Industry leader. | V2G supported via **ISO 15118-20** plug-and-charge extension. **(1.5 marks)** |

**(b)** A 50 kW DC fast charger is connected to a 3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz AC supply. The charger uses an active PFC rectifier with a measured power factor of 0.99. Calculate (i) the RMS line current drawn from the grid, and (ii) the total apparent power. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 1 — Apparent Power S = P / PF**
> The active power $P$ is the charger output plus losses. Assuming the input power approximately equals the output for this calculation:

$$P_{\text{in}} \approx P_{\text{out}} = 50 \text{ kW}$$

$$S = \dfrac{P}{\text{PF}} = \dfrac{50}{0.99} = 50.51 \text{ kVA} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}$$

> **Step 2 — Three-Phase Line Current**

$$I_{\text{line}} = \dfrac{S}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{LL}}$$

$$I_{\text{line}} = \dfrac{50{,}510}{\sqrt{3} \times 415} = \dfrac{50{,}510}{718.66}$$

$$\boxed{I_{\text{line}} \approx 70.29 \text{ A}} \quad \textbf{(4 marks)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**:
> - Do **not** confuse *input* current with *output* current. The output DC is at low voltage (e.g., 400 V) so the DC current is high (125 A), but the input AC current from the grid at 415 V is much lower (~70 A).
> - Always use the **line-to-line** voltage $V_{LL}$ for 3-phase calculations, not the phase voltage.
> - The formula $I = P / (\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{LL} \cdot \text{PF})$ is the *only* correct form. Forgetting the $\sqrt{3}$ will cost 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — DC Charger (Module 3)**

- **Definition**: A DC fast charger is an *off-board* rectifier that bypasses the OBC and delivers DC directly to the battery. **(Mode 4 / Level 3 / IEC 61851-23)**
- **Power Range**: **50 kW to 350 kW** (current state-of-art); research pushing to **600 kW–1 MW** for heavy-duty trucks.
- **Two Key Standards**: **CHAdeMO** (CAN-based, Japan) and **CCS-Combo 2** (PLC-based, EU/US). GB/T is mandatory in China.
- **Core Architecture**: AC Input → Isolation Transformer → **Active PFC Rectifier** → DC-Link (800 V) → **Isolated DC-DC (LLC/DAB)** → Output Filter → EV Battery.
- **Power Electronics Devices**: Modern chargers use **SiC MOSFETs** (not silicon IGBTs) for higher efficiency (> 97%) and higher switching frequency.
- **Communication is Mandatory**: The BMS is the *master*; the EVSE is the *slave*. The charger never decides its own current — it obeys BMS limits.
- **Charging Protocol**: **Constant Current (CC)** phase followed by **Constant Voltage (CV)** phase. The bulk of energy is delivered in CC.
- **Charging Time Formula**: $t = \dfrac{Q \cdot \Delta \text{SoC}}{P_{\text{ch}} \cdot \eta}$ — memorize this for any KTU numerical.
- **Output Current at Lowest Voltage**: $I_{\text{out, max}} = P_{\text{max}} / V_{\text{batt, min}}$ — happens at low SoC.
- **Cable Cooling**: Above **200 A**, the DC cable *must* be liquid-cooled to prevent insulation failure.
- **Insulation Monitoring**: Per IEC 61851, isolation resistance must exceed **100 kΩ per volt** of max DC voltage.
- **Harmonic Standards**: Input current THD must be **< 5%** per **IEEE 519**; power factor must be **> 0.99** for stations > 50 kW.
- **V2G Pioneer**: CHAdeMO is the **only** standard that natively supports bidirectional V2G since 2012.
- **Efficiency Target**: Modern 150 kW+ units target **wall-to-battery efficiency of 94–96%**.
- **Future Trend**: **350 kW+ stations** require **800 V battery architecture** (e.g., Porsche Taycan, Hyundai E-GMP, Lucid Air) to keep current below 500 A.
- **Common KTU Mistake**: Confusing *Level 1/2 AC charging* (uses OBC) with *Level 3 DC fast charging* (bypasses OBC). The boundary is the *location* of the rectifier.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT631 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** AC Charger

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **AC Charger**, formally referred to as **AC Electric Vehicle Supply Equipment (AC EVSE)**, is an external stationary power-delivery device that supplies **Alternating Current (AC)** electrical energy from the utility grid (or a local AC source) to an electric vehicle (EV) through a standardized charging cable and connector. The AC power is fed into the vehicle's **On-Board Charger (OBC)**, which is responsible for rectification, power factor correction (PFC), and battery management-compliant DC charging.

According to **SAE J1772 (IEC 62196 Type 1/Type 2)** standards, AC chargers are classified into two principal levels:

| Level | Voltage (AC) | Power Range | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **AC Level 1** | 120 V, Single-Phase | **1.4 kW – 1.9 kW** | Residential, slow charging |
| **AC Level 2** | 208–240 V, Single/Three-Phase | **3.3 kW – 22 kW** | Commercial, public, fast AC charging |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The AC charger is distinct from a DC fast charger (DCFC). In AC charging, the bulky and expensive rectifier/PFC stage resides **inside the vehicle** (the OBC), not inside the charging station. This is why AC chargers are cheaper, simpler, and more widely deployed than DC chargers.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Think of an **AC charger like a kitchen faucet**, and the EV's **battery like a water tank** that only accepts pressurized water (DC).

- The **faucet (AC charger)** simply delivers raw water (AC electricity) — it doesn't purify it.
- The **"Water Purifier + Pressure Pump" inside the house** (the On-Board Charger) takes that water, cleans it (rectifies AC → DC), pressurizes it (PFC), and gently pushes it into the overhead tank (battery).
- The **flow rate** of water is limited by the size of the purifier pump (OBC rating), not just the pipe diameter (cable). That is why a 22 kW AC charger cannot charge faster than the vehicle's OBC rating (often 6.6 kW or 11 kW).

## 1.3 Key Standardized Connector Standards

> [!NOTE]
> The connector is the **physical handshake** between the grid-side AC charger and the vehicle. KTU expects familiarity with these international standards.

- **SAE J1772 (Type 1)** – 5-pin, single-phase, used in North America and Japan. Capable up to **80 A, 240 V (≈19.2 kW)**.
- **IEC 62196-2 (Type 2 / Mennekes)** – 7-pin, supports both single-phase and three-phase AC, used in Europe, India, and most of Asia. Capable up to **63 A, 400 V (≈43 kW)**.
- **GB/T 20234.2** – Chinese standard, 7-pin, single/three-phase.

**Pin Functions of Type 2 Connector (IEC 62196-2):**
1. **L1** – Phase 1
2. **L2** – Phase 2
3. **L3** – Phase 3 (for three-phase)
4. **N** – Neutral
5. **PE** – Protective Earth
6. **CP** – Control Pilot (communication)
7. **PP / CS** – Proximity Pilot / Connection Sense

## 1.4 Why AC Chargers are Prevalent

- **Cost-effective:** No high-power rectifier, isolation transformer, or liquid cooling required.
- **Longer battery life:** Lower charging currents reduce thermal stress on cells.
- **Ubiquitous:** Compatible with household outlets (Level 1) and dedicated wall boxes (Level 2).
- **Smart-grid friendly:** Supports **V1G** (unidirectional) and **V2G** (bidirectional, with OBC upgrade) via PLC (Power Line Communication) over the Control Pilot pin.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Subsystems of an AC Charger

An AC EVSE is not just a "plug with a meter." KTU examiners expect knowledge of its internal sub-blocks:

1. **Input Protection Stage**
   - Miniature Circuit Breaker (MCB) / Residual Current Device (RCD) for ground-fault and overcurrent protection.
   - Surge Protection Device (SPD) against voltage transients.
2. **Contactor & Relay Bank**
   - High-inrush AC contactors that engage the supply to the connector only after a safe handshake is established.
3. **Control Pilot (CP) Circuit**
   - Generates a **1 kHz, ±12 V PWM signal** to communicate the available current to the vehicle.
4. **Proximity Pilot (PP) / Connection Sense (CS)**
   - Detects plug insertion and the current-carrying capacity of the cable via a resistor coding scheme.
5. **Energy Meter (Optional)**
   - Class 1 or Class 0.5 accuracy kWh meter for billing.
6. **Communication Module**
   - **OCPP 1.6 / 2.0.1** (Open Charge Point Protocol) for back-end server communication via Wi-Fi / Ethernet / 4G.
7. **User Interface (HMI)**
   - RFID reader, LCD/LED display, emergency stop button.

## 2.2 The "How" of CP Signal: PWM Duty Cycle Encoding

The **Control Pilot** line is the safety brain of AC charging. The charger tells the EV how much current it can deliver using a **PWM duty cycle**:

- The charger generates a **1 kHz square wave** of **±12 V**.
- The EV modulates the wave's amplitude by switching in a resistor-diode network.
- The **duty cycle D** maps to **maximum available current $I_{max}$** as per SAE J1772 / IEC 61851-1:

$$I_{max} = \begin{cases} 0.6 \text{ A} \cdot D \text{ (for } D \le 0.1) \\ (6 \text{ A} \cdot D) + 0.4 \text{ A} \text{ (for } 0.1 \le D \le 0.85) \\ (2.5 \text{ A} \cdot D) - 1.0625 \text{ A (approx., for } 0.85 \le D \le 1) \end{cases}$$

- A **5% duty cycle** → "No charging allowed" (digital off signal).
- A **50% duty cycle** → up to 32 A available.
- A **100% duty cycle** → maximum current (e.g., 80 A for J1772).

## 2.3 Power Delivered by an AC Charger

### Single-Phase AC Power

$$P_{1\phi} = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms} \cdot \cos\phi$$

### Three-Phase AC Power (most public Level 2 chargers)

$$P_{3\phi} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{L} \cdot I_{L} \cdot \cos\phi$$

Where:
- $V_{L}$ = Line-to-Line RMS Voltage (e.g., **400 V**)
- $I_{L}$ = Line RMS Current
- $\cos\phi$ = Power Factor (≈ 0.95–0.99 with active PFC in OBC)

## 2.4 Charging Time Calculation (KTU Favorite)

$$T_{charge} = \frac{E_{battery}}{P_{delivered} \cdot \eta_{OBC} \cdot \eta_{cable}}$$

Where:
- $E_{battery}$ = Usable battery energy in **kWh**
- $P_{delivered}$ = Charger output power in **kW**
- $\eta_{OBC}$ = On-board charger efficiency (typically **0.92–0.95**)
- $\eta_{cable}$ = Cabling efficiency (typically **0.98–0.99**)

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Definition | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | AC Power (1φ) | $P = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$ | W (kW) | Residential charging |
| 2 | AC Power (3φ) | $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$ | W (kW) | Commercial / public AC charging |
| 3 | Charging Time | $T = \dfrac{E_{bat}}{P \cdot \eta_{total}}$ | hours | Estimating dwell time at charger |
| 4 | Energy Stored | $E = \int V_{bat} \cdot I_{bat} \, dt \approx V_{nom} \cdot Q$ | kWh | Battery capacity |
| 5 | CP Duty Cycle → Current | $I_{max} = 6D + 0.4$ A (for $0.1 \le D \le 0.85$) | A | EVSE-EV handshake |
| 6 | State of Charge (SoC) | $SoC = \dfrac{E_{current}}{E_{rated}} \times 100\%$ | % | Charging completion logic |
| 7 | Power Factor | $\cos\phi = \dfrac{P_{real}}{S_{apparent}}$ | dimensionless | Grid-side compliance |
| 8 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | % | OBC / system efficiency |
| 9 | Cable Resistance Loss | $P_{loss} = I^2 \cdot R_{cable}$ | W | Heat dissipation in cable |
| 10 | Energy Lost in Cable | $E_{loss} = I^2 \cdot R \cdot t$ | J (Wh) | Long-cable installations |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Whenever a numerical problem mentions "400 V, three-phase, 16 A," always default to the **three-phase power formula**. Use **single-phase formula** only when explicitly stated (e.g., "230 V, 15 A, single-phase residential supply").

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

AC chargers are the **backbone of urban EV infrastructure**. The reasons:

- **Cost per kW installed** is roughly **5–8x lower** than DC fast chargers.
- **Bidirectional capability** (with an upgraded OBC) enables **Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G)** for grid stabilization, peak shaving, and renewable energy buffering.
- The same AC charger hardware can serve as a **smart home energy hub**, dynamically balancing EV charging with rooftop solar and home loads (using protocols like OCPP + ISO 15118).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Charging Time from First Principles

**Problem Setup (KTU-style):**
A 4-wheeler EV has a lithium-ion battery with a usable energy capacity of **60 kWh**. It is connected to a three-phase AC charger supplying **400 V (line-to-line)** at **32 A (line current)**. The OBC efficiency is **94%** and cable losses are **1.5%**. Find the charging time from 10% SoC to 80% SoC.

### Step 1: Identify the Energy to be Delivered

The battery must be charged from 10% to 80% SoC. The change in SoC is:

$$\Delta SoC = 80\% - 10\% = 70\%$$

The energy that must be transferred to the battery:

$$E_{required} = \Delta SoC \times E_{battery} = 0.70 \times 60 \text{ kWh}$$

$$E_{required} = 42 \text{ kWh}$$

### Step 2: Calculate the Three-Phase Input Power to the OBC

Using the three-phase AC power formula:

$$P_{input} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$$

Assuming unity power factor at the OBC input (modern OBCs use active PFC, $\cos\phi \approx 0.99 \approx 1$):

$$P_{input} = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \text{ V} \times 32 \text{ A} \times 1$$

$$P_{input} = 1.732 \times 400 \times 32$$

$$P_{input} = 1.732 \times 12800 = 22169.6 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{input} \approx 22.17 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 3: Apply System Efficiency (OBC + Cable)

The total system efficiency is the product of individual efficiencies:

$$\eta_{total} = \eta_{OBC} \times \eta_{cable}$$

$$\eta_{total} = 0.94 \times (1 - 0.015) = 0.94 \times 0.985$$

$$\eta_{total} = 0.9259 \approx 92.59\%$$

### Step 4: Calculate Power Reaching the Battery

$$P_{bat} = P_{input} \times \eta_{total} = 22.17 \times 0.9259$$

$$P_{bat} \approx 20.53 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 5: Compute the Charging Time

Using the energy-time relationship:

$$T = \frac{E_{required}}{P_{bat}}$$

$$T = \frac{42 \text{ kWh}}{20.53 \text{ kW}}$$

$$T \approx 2.046 \text{ hours}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 2 \text{ hours, } 2 \text{ minutes, } 46 \text{ seconds}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In practice, EV batteries apply a **CC-CV (Constant Current → Constant Voltage)** profile. The current tapers off as SoC approaches 100%, so real charging time from 10% → 80% may be **10–15% longer** than this theoretical calculation.

## 3.2 Derivation: Control Pilot Current Encoding

**Problem:** A charger outputs a PWM Control Pilot signal with a duty cycle of **40%**. Using the SAE J1772 mapping, what is the maximum current the EV is permitted to draw?

Since $0.1 \le D \le 0.85$, we use the linear region:

$$I_{max} = 6D + 0.4 \text{ A}$$

Substitute $D = 0.40$:

$$I_{max} = 6(0.40) + 0.4 = 2.4 + 0.4$$

$$I_{max} = 2.8 \text{ A}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> At such a low duty cycle, the EV is told to draw a **trickle** of current — useful in cold-start or fault-handling conditions.

## 3.3 Derivation: Cable Sizing for AC Charger

**Problem:** A 22 kW, 3-phase charger operates at 400 V line-to-line. Find the rated line current. If the cable run is **30 m** of copper ($4 \text{ mm}^2$ cross-section, $\rho = 1.72 \times 10^{-8} \Omega\cdot\text{m}$), find the percentage power loss in the cable at full load.

### Step 1: Line Current

$$I_L = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi} = \frac{22000}{\sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 1}$$

$$I_L = \frac{22000}{692.82} \approx 31.75 \text{ A}$$

### Step 2: Cable Resistance (per conductor, two-way loop)

$$R = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{A} = \frac{1.72 \times 10^{-8} \times 30}{4 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$R = \frac{5.16 \times 10^{-7}}{4 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.129 \ \Omega$$

Loop resistance (line + neutral return): $R_{loop} = 2 \times 0.129 = 0.258 \ \Omega$.

### Step 3: Power Loss in Cable

$$P_{loss} = 3 \cdot I_L^2 \cdot R_{conductor} = 3 \times (31.75)^2 \times 0.129$$

$$P_{loss} = 3 \times 1008 \times 0.129 = 3 \times 130.0 = 390.0 \text{ W}$$

### Step 4: Percentage Loss

$$\% Loss = \frac{P_{loss}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{390}{22000} \times 100 \approx 1.77\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> This validates the earlier **1.5–2% cable loss assumption** used in Section 3.1.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: AC Charger vs. DC Charger

| Parameter | AC Charger (EVSE) | DC Fast Charger (DCFC) |
|---|---|---|
| Rectification | Inside vehicle (OBC) | Inside charger |
| Power Level | 1.4 – 43 kW | 50 – 350 kW |
| Cost (per kW) | **$100 – $400** | **$400 – $1500** |
| Charging Time (60 kWh) | 4–10 hours | 20–60 minutes |
| Battery Stress | Low (gentle) | Higher (thermal management critical) |
| Grid Impact | Moderate | High (often needs dedicated transformer) |
| Mobility | Portable Level 1 possible | Stationary, large footprint |
| Standard | SAE J1772, IEC 62196-2 | CCS2, CHAdeMO, GB/T |
| Best Use Case | Home, workplace, overnight | Highway corridors, taxis, fleets |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an AC Charging System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Utility Grid 3-Phase 400V AC] --> B[MCB and RCD Protection Stage]
    B --> C[Surge Protection Device SPD]
    C --> D[AC Contactor Bank]
    D --> E[Control Pilot CP PWM Generator 1kHz]
    D --> F[Proximity Pilot PP Cable Detect]
    E --> G[Charging Connector Type 2]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Onboard Charger OBC Vehicle Side]
    H --> I[Power Factor Correction PFC Stage]
    I --> J[Isolated DC DC Converter]
    J --> K[Battery Management System BMS]
    K --> L[HV Lithium Ion Battery Pack]
    M[OCPP 1.6 Communication Module] --> D
    M --> N[Cloud Server Billing and Load Mgmt]
    O[RCD Energy Meter] --> M
```

**Explanation of Architecture Flow:**
- The **Utility Grid** supplies raw three-phase AC.
- The **Protection Stage (MCB + RCD)** disconnects on overcurrent or residual current faults.
- The **Contactor Bank** acts as a software-controlled switch that closes only after a safe handshake.
- The **CP and PP** circuits perform the physical and electrical handshake with the EV.
- The **OBC inside the vehicle** does the actual conversion to DC.
- The **OCPP module** enables remote monitoring, billing, and dynamic load management via cloud.

## 4.2 AC Charging Process — Sequence Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA([Start Vehicle Plugged In]) --> stepB{PP Pin Resistor Validates Cable Rating}
    stepB -- No --> stepZ([Stop Fault Lockout])
    stepB -- Yes --> stepC[Charger Energizes CP Line 12V 1kHz PWM]
    stepC --> stepD{EV ECU Detects PWM Presence}
    stepD -- No --> stepZ
    stepD -- Yes --> stepE[Vehicle Modulates CP to Indicate Ready State]
    stepE --> stepF[Charger Closes AC Contactor Supply Connected]
    stepF --> stepG[OBC Activates PFC and Rectifier]
    stepG --> stepH[BMS Requests CC CV Charging Profile]
    stepH --> stepI[CP Duty Cycle Adjusted to Match Available Current]
    stepI --> stepJ{Battery SoC Reaches Target 80 to 100 percent}
    stepJ -- No --> stepI
    stepJ -- Yes --> stepK[OBC Sends Stop Command via CP]
    stepK --> stepL[Charger Opens AC Contactor]
    stepL --> stepM([End Charging Complete])
```

**Interpretation of Sequence:**
This is the exact handshake sequence defined in **IEC 61851-1**. KTU questions often ask students to "describe the charging sequence" — this flowchart gives the answer.

## 4.3 Connector Pinout (Type 2 / IEC 62196-2)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Conn["Type 2 Charging Connector - 7 Pin Layout"]
        pin1((Pin 1: L1 Phase 1))
        pin2((Pin 2: L2 Phase 2))
        pin3((Pin 3: L3 Phase 3))
        pin4((Pin 4: N Neutral))
        pin5((Pin 5: PE Protective Earth))
        pin6((Pin 6: CP Control Pilot))
        pin7((Pin 7: PP Proximity Pilot))
    end
```

**Engineering Note:** In a single-phase charger, **L2 and L3 are unused** but their pins are still mechanically present to maintain Type 2 compatibility. The **CP pin is the most safety-critical pin** — it is the only one carrying low-voltage signals during the handshake before main contactor closure.

## 4.4 Internal Block Diagram of a Modern On-Board Charger (Vehicle Side)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[AC Input from EVSE] --> A2[EMI Filter]
    A2 --> A3[Bridge Rectifier AC to DC]
    A3 --> A4[Power Factor Correction Boost PFC]
    A4 --> A5[DC Link Capacitor Bulk Storage]
    A5 --> A6[Isolated Full Bridge LLC Resonant Converter]
    A6 --> A7[High Frequency Transformer]
    A7 --> A8[Secondary Rectifier and LC Filter]
    A8 --> A9[Output to HV Battery via BMS]
    B1[CAN Bus from VCU] --> A6
    B2[Temperature Sensors] --> B1
```

**Why this matters for KTU:** The AC charger's role ends at the connector. From this point onward, the **OBC** takes over. The two-stage structure (PFC + isolated DC-DC) is a KTU favorite for circuit-design questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**"Distinguish between AC and DC charging of electric vehicles. Mention the standard connector used for AC charging in India/Europe."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | AC Charging | DC Charging |
|---|---|---|
| Rectification | Inside vehicle (OBC) | Inside charger |
| Power Level | Up to 43 kW (3-phase) | 50–350 kW |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Charging Time | 4–10 hours | 20–60 minutes |
| Battery Stress | Low | High |

**Standard for India/Europe:** **IEC 62196-2 Type 2 (Mennekes)** — 7-pin, supports both single-phase and three-phase AC.

*(Award: 2 marks for the comparison table; 1 mark for the standard.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sessional]
**"Explain the function of the Control Pilot (CP) pin in an AC charger."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Control Pilot (CP) pin performs three critical functions:
1. **Handshake & Safety:** It provides a ±12 V, 1 kHz PWM signal only when safe to energize the cable, ensuring no live voltage on the connector pins before the EV is ready.
2. **Current Advertisment:** The **PWM duty cycle** tells the vehicle's ECU the maximum permissible current (e.g., 50% duty = 32 A available).
3. **Vehicle Status Feedback:** The EV modulates the CP signal amplitude via a resistor-diode network to indicate states like "Ready," "Charging," "Ventilation Required," or "Fault."

*(Award: 1 mark per correct function.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students confuse **OBC rating** with **charger rating**. The bottleneck is always the **OBC**, not the charger! Also, do not write "battery is charged by AC" — the battery is **always** charged by DC; AC is only the input form.

### **Question A (14 Marks) – [Adapted from KTU Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of an On-Board Charger (OBC) in an electric vehicle. Discuss the role of the PFC stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An EV with a **72 kWh** battery is to be charged using a **22 kW, 3-phase, 400 V, 32 A** AC EVSE. The OBC efficiency is **93%** and cable losses are **2%**. Find:
  (i) The energy stored if SoC rises from **20% to 90%**.
  (ii) The effective DC power reaching the battery.
  (iii) The total charging time in hours. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a):

**Block Diagram (already shown in Section 4.4). Describe stages:**

1. **EMI Filter:** Suppresses conducted electromagnetic interference from the grid and prevents OBC noise from leaking back.
2. **Bridge Rectifier:** Converts incoming AC to unregulated DC. Uses 4 IGBTs or diodes in full-bridge configuration.
3. **Power Factor Correction (PFC) Stage:** A boost converter operating in **Critical Conduction Mode (CrCM)** or **Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM)** that shapes the input current to be sinusoidal and in phase with the input voltage.
   - **Role of PFC:** Ensures **$\cos\phi \approx 0.99$**, complying with grid regulations (e.g., **IEC 61000-3-2**), reduces harmonic distortion (THD < 5%), and improves energy efficiency.
4. **DC-Link Capacitor:** A large electrolytic capacitor (e.g., 450 V, 1000 µF) that buffers the ripple between PFC and DC-DC stage.
5. **Isolated DC-DC Converter:** Typically a **LLC resonant half/full bridge** with a high-frequency transformer. Provides galvanic isolation (required for safety), steps voltage up/down to battery level, and performs tight output voltage/current regulation.
6. **Output Filter and BMS Interface:** LC filter smooths the output, and the BMS controls charging current/voltage via CAN bus to the OBC controller.

*(Award: 2 marks for block diagram; 3 marks for stage-wise description; 2 marks for PFC role.)*

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b):

**(i) Energy to be stored:**

$$\Delta SoC = 90\% - 20\% = 70\%$$

$$E_{required} = 0.70 \times 72 = 50.4 \text{ kWh}$$

**(ii) Effective DC Power:**

Three-phase input power:

$$P_{in} = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 32 \times 1 = 22169.6 \text{ W} \approx 22.17 \text{ kW}$$

Total efficiency:

$$\eta_{total} = 0.93 \times (1 - 0.02) = 0.93 \times 0.98 = 0.9114$$

Effective DC power:

$$P_{bat} = 22.17 \times 0.9114 \approx 20.21 \text{ kW}$$

**(iii) Charging Time:**

$$T = \frac{E_{required}}{P_{bat}} = \frac{50.4}{20.21} \approx 2.494 \text{ hours}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 2 \text{ hours, } 29 \text{ minutes, } 38 \text{ seconds}}$$

*(Award: 2 + 3 + 2 marks across the three sub-parts. Show unit conversions explicitly.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) – [Adapted from KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **SAE J1772 / IEC 61851-1** charging states and how the **PWM duty cycle** is used to communicate the available current to the EV. Show the duty-cycle-to-current mapping equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **AC Level 1, AC Level 2, and DC Level 3** charging with respect to power level, connector type, charging time for a 60 kWh battery, and typical deployment location. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a):

**IEC 61851-1 defines 4 charging states (A, B, C, D, E, F — simplified to 4 in practice):**

1. **State A — Standby (No Vehicle Connected):** CP voltage ≈ ±12 V, no PWM, no current flow. Charger is idle.
2. **State B — Vehicle Detected (Connected, Not Ready):** EV inserts plug, closes the PP/CP loop with a 2.74 kΩ resistor. CP switches to ±9 V PWM. Charger knows a vehicle is present but not ready to charge.
3. **State C — Vehicle Ready (Charging in Progress):** EV closes the loop with a 1.3 kΩ resistor (Ready state). CP drops to ±6 V PWM. Contactor closes and AC power flows.
4. **State D / F — Ventilation Required / Fault:** Special states for outdoor or fault conditions where the duty cycle drops below 3% or CP goes out of PWM.

**PWM Duty Cycle Mapping (SAE J1772):**

| Duty Cycle D | Current $I_{max}$ |
|---|---|
| $D \le 0.05$ | **No charging permitted** |
| $0.05 \le D \le 0.10$ | **Digital "1" — Status signaling** |
| $0.10 \le D \le 0.85$ | $I_{max} = 6D + 0.4$ A |
| $0.85 \le D \le 0.97$ | $I_{max} = 2.5D - 1.0$ A (approx.) |

**Worked example:** For $D = 0.50$:

$$I_{max} = 6(0.50) + 0.4 = 3.4 \text{ A}$$

*(Award: 2 marks for the 4 states; 3 marks for the equation and table; 2 marks for the worked example.)*

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b):

| Parameter | AC Level 1 | AC Level 2 | DC Level 3 (DCFC) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Voltage | 120 V AC (1φ) | 208–240 V AC (1φ/3φ) | 200–1000 V DC |
| Power | 1.4–1.9 kW | 3.3–22 kW | 50–350 kW |
| Connector | J1772 (Type 1) | J1772 / Type 2 | CCS2, CHAdeMO, GB/T |
| Time for 60 kWh | ~33 hours | 3–18 hours | 18–60 minutes |
| Location | Home (residential) | Home wallbox, workplace, malls | Highway corridors, fuel stations |
| OBC Required? | Yes | Yes | **No — bypasses OBC** |

**Key Inference:** A 60 kWh battery on a 1.4 kW Level 1 charger takes **~43 hours** to fully charge — clearly impractical for daily use, hence Level 1 is **only for emergency top-ups** at home.

*(Award: 5 marks for the table; 2 marks for the engineering inference.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist — AC Charger (Module 3):**

- **AC Charger = EVSE (Electric Vehicle Supply Equipment).** It does **not** contain the rectifier. That is the job of the **On-Board Charger (OBC)** inside the vehicle.
- **Two Standard Levels:** Level 1 (≤1.9 kW, 120 V) and Level 2 (3.3–22 kW, 208–400 V). Level 3 is DC fast charging.
- **Connectors:** Type 1 (J1772, single-phase) and Type 2 (IEC 62196-2, 1φ/3φ). Type 2 dominates in India and Europe.
- **Three-Phase Power Formula:** $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$ — default for any 3-phase numerical.
- **Control Pilot (CP) is the brain of AC charging:** ±12 V, 1 kHz PWM. Duty cycle maps linearly to current: $I_{max} = 6D + 0.4$ A (for $0.1 \le D \le 0.85$).
- **Charging Time Formula:** $T = E_{bat} / (P \times \eta_{total})$ — always include **OBC + cable efficiency** in real calculations.
- **OBC Architecture (High-Frequency Topic):** EMI Filter → Rectifier → PFC (Boost) → DC-Link → Isolated DC-DC (LLC) → BMS Interface.
- **Power Factor Correction is mandatory:** Modern OBCs achieve $\cos\phi \ge 0.99$ with active PFC to comply with grid harmonic standards.
- **Cable losses** scale as $I^2 R$. For high-power AC charging, thicker cables ($6 \text{ mm}^2$ to $16 \text{ mm}^2$) are mandatory.
- **Bidirectional AC Charging (V2G):** With an upgraded bidirectional OBC, AC chargers can support **Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G)** and **Vehicle-to-Home (V2H)** using ISO 15118 communication protocol.
- **OCPP (Open Charge Point Protocol)** is the industry-standard communication between chargers and the central management system for billing, diagnostics, and smart charging.
- **CC-CV Profile:** Real-world charging always tapers off near 100% SoC — theoretical times are always slightly optimistic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip:** If a question says *"the vehicle is charged by AC,"* **correct it mentally and in your answer** — the **battery is always charged by DC**. The OBC converts AC→DC. Examiners reward students who use precise terminology.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Wireless Charging of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

### Formal Definition
**Wireless Power Transfer (WPT)** for Electric Vehicles is defined as the contactless transmission of electrical energy from a ground-based (or roadway-embedded) primary transmitter pad to a vehicle-mounted secondary receiver pad, employing the principles of **electromagnetic induction** (near-field) or **resonant inductive coupling**, regulated under the **SAE J2954** industry standard for power levels up to **22 kW** (WPT 1 to WPT 3 classifications).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Wireless charging falls under the "Alternative Charging Strategies" section of Module 3. Students are expected to know stationary (static) vs. dynamic (in-motion) WPT, the role of compensation networks, and the SAE J2954 standard.

### Key Classifications of Wireless EV Charging

| Type | Description | Application |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Static (Stationary) WPT** | Vehicle parks over a charging pad | Homes, parking lots, taxi queues |
| **Dynamic (In-Motion) WPT** | Charging coils embedded in roadway | Highways, dedicated bus lanes |
| **Quasi-Dynamic WPT** | Charging at stop signals / bus stops | Urban transit systems |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a musical tuning fork:** When you strike one tuning fork (primary coil) and bring a second identical tuning fork (secondary coil) close to it, the second fork begins to vibrate and produce sound without any physical contact. This is **resonance** — energy transfer between two objects tuned to the same natural frequency. Wireless EV charging works on the same principle, but instead of sound, **electromagnetic energy** "tunes" between two coils, charging the battery across an air gap of typically **100 mm to 250 mm**.

---

## 1.2 Battery Swapping

### Formal Definition
**Battery Swapping (BS)** is a refueling paradigm in which a depleted EV battery pack is mechanically and rapidly exchanged for a fully charged one at a dedicated **Battery Swap Station (BSS)**, typically completing the operation in **under 5 minutes**, as opposed to the **30–60 minutes** required for even fast DC charging, and **4–8 hours** for Level 2 AC charging.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a child's AA battery:** When a remote-control battery dies, you don't recharge it in the remote — you pop it out and pop in fresh ones from a drawer. Battery swapping applies this exact idea to EVs: instead of plugging in and waiting, the drained battery module is slid out of the vehicle's underbelly, and a fully charged module is slid in. It's the **"Refill, not Recharge"** model — analogous to refilling petrol at a fuel station, but with batteries.

### Key Components of a Battery Swap Station

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Entities of a Battery Swap Station (BSS):**
> 1. **Swap Mechanism:** Robotic arm / conveyor system for battery extraction and insertion.
> 2. **Charging Racks:** Internal storage where removed batteries are recharged in parallel.
> 3. **Battery Inventory:** Typically **8–12 battery packs** per station as buffer stock.
> 4. **Control & Billing Unit:** RFID/IoT-based authentication and energy metering.
> 5. **Thermal Management:** Liquid cooling for charging racks and battery health.

---

## 1.3 Why These Two Technologies Matter (KTU Context)

> [!NOTE]
> Both technologies directly address the **"Range Anxiety"** and **"Charging Time Penalty"** bottlenecks of EV adoption — the two biggest barriers identified by KTU Module 3 in the EV ecosystem study. KTU examiners frequently test:
> - The *physics* of inductive coupling (electromagnetic flux linkage).
> - The *economic and operational* trade-offs of swapping vs. plug-in vs. wireless.
> - The *standardization* aspects (SAE J2954, GB/T 20234, IEC 61980).

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physics of Wireless Power Transfer (WPT)

The fundamental principle is **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, combined with **resonant tuning** to overcome the inherent low efficiency of loosely-coupled air-core transformers.

### Operating Principles — Step-by-Step

1. **High-Frequency AC Injection:** A grid-fed inverter converts DC/50 Hz AC into high-frequency AC (typically **85 kHz** as per SAE J2954).
2. **Primary Coil Excitation:** This high-frequency current flows through the **primary (transmitter) coil** embedded in the ground pad, generating a **time-varying magnetic flux** $\Phi$.
3. **Air-Gap Coupling:** The magnetic flux crosses the air gap (typically 100–250 mm) and links with the **secondary (receiver) coil** mounted on the vehicle's underbody.
4. **EMF Induction:** By Faraday's Law, an EMF is induced in the secondary coil: $e = -N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$.
5. **Rectification & Regulation:** The induced AC is rectified to DC and regulated to charge the HV battery via the existing OBC (On-Board Charger) architecture.
6. **Resonance Compensation:** Both coils are tuned with external capacitors (series/parallel) to form **LC resonant tanks**, dramatically boosting power transfer efficiency (often **>90%** at resonance).

---

## 2.2 Coupling Coefficient (k) — The Heart of WPT

The **coupling coefficient** $k$ quantifies how much magnetic flux from the primary links to the secondary:

$$k = \dfrac{M}{\sqrt{L_1 \cdot L_2}}$$

Where:
- $M$ = Mutual inductance (in Henries, H)
- $L_1$ = Self-inductance of primary coil (H)
- $L_2$ = Self-inductance of secondary coil (H)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For EV Wireless Charging, $k$ typically lies in the range 0.1 to 0.3** (loosely coupled, due to large air gap). This is why **resonance compensation** is non-negotiable — without it, efficiency would be <40%.

---

## 2.3 Resonant Frequency

Both primary and secondary are tuned to the same resonant frequency $f_0$:

$$f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L \cdot C}}$$

For **SAE J2954**, the standard frequency band is **81.39 kHz to 90.00 kHz**, with a nominal center of **85 kHz**.

---

## 2.4 Compensation Network Topologies (KTU High-Yield)

| Topology | Primary Side | Secondary Side | KTU Exam Note |
|----------|--------------|----------------|----------------|
| **SS** | Series | Series | Simplest, constant current output, used in most EVs |
| **SP** | Series | Parallel | Voltage-fed, good for high-power dynamic charging |
| **PS** | Parallel | Series | Current-fed primary, less common |
| **PP** | Parallel | Parallel | Rarely used in EV due to complexity |
| **LCC / LCL** | Inductor-Capacitor-Inductor | Various | High-order, used in 50 kW+ systems |

> [!NOTE]
> **SS compensation is the most common** for static EV wireless charging because:
> 1. The resonant capacitor naturally blocks DC and low-frequency components.
> 2. Zero-Reactive-Power (ZRP) operation is achievable at a specific load.
> 3. It tolerates misalignment up to **±75 mm lateral** and **±100 mm longitudinal**.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MANDATORY — All formulas below are examinable in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Typical Value |
|---|---------|-------------|----------------------|
| 1 | $k = \dfrac{M}{\sqrt{L_1 L_2}}$ | Coupling coefficient | Dimensionless, $0.1 \le k \le 0.3$ |
| 2 | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | Resonant frequency | Hz, **85 kHz** (SAE J2954) |
| 3 | $e = -N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$ | Faraday's induced EMF | Volts |
| 4 | $M = k \sqrt{L_1 L_2}$ | Mutual inductance | Henries (H) |
| 5 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | WPT efficiency | Typically **88–93%** |
| 6 | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ | Angular resonant frequency | rad/s |
| 7 | $Q = \dfrac{1}{R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$ | Quality factor of resonant tank | Typically **200–500** |
| 8 | $T_{swap} = T_{mech} + T_{auth} + T_{unlock}$ | Total swap time | 3–5 minutes |
| 9 | $P_{charge} = \dfrac{N_{batts} \cdot V \cdot I}{t_{charge}}$ | Swap station throughput | kW (rack-level) |
| 10 | $\Phi = B \cdot A$ | Magnetic flux per pole | Weber (Wb) |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** Above, all absolute value / magnitude notations use parentheses to avoid breaking the markdown table. In your exam answer scripts, always use the LaTeX `\vert` command or parentheses for absolute magnitudes.

---

## 2.6 Battery Swapping — Operational Theory

### Three Swap Architectures

1. **Direct Swap (Single-Vehicle):** A robotic arm slides the pack out and inserts a charged one. Used by **NIO** (Power Swap Stations).
2. **Conveyor / Modular Swap:** Multiple packs on a conveyor; useful for two/three-wheelers. Used by **Gogoro** (Taiwan).
3. **Chassis-Less Swap:** Entire battery tray is exchanged. Faster, but needs vehicle standardization.

### Economic & Operational Metrics (KTU Hot Topic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip — Battery Swapping Math:**
> - **Time Saved vs. Fast Charging:** $t_{saved} = t_{DC\,fast} - t_{swap}$ (≈ 25–55 minutes saved).
> - **Battery Utilization:** Shared/swappable batteries can be charged slowly (1C) extending cycle life by **15–25%**.
> - **Station Footprint:** A typical 2-bay swap station occupies **~40 m²** vs. **~60 m²** for a 4-charger DC fast-charging hub.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Static WPT** is being deployed in **taxi fleets** (Oslo, London) and **indoor parking** of commercial buildings.
> - **Dynamic WPT** pilots exist in **Korea (KAIST OLEV bus), Sweden (Smartroad Gotland), and Israel (ElectReon)**.
> - **Battery Swapping** is the dominant EV refueling method in **China** for commercial vehicles (BAIC, NIO) and **India** for two/three-wheelers (Sun Mobility, Battery Smart).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Power Transfer Efficiency of a Loosely-Coupled WPT System

We derive the expression for end-to-end efficiency of a series-series (SS) compensated WPT link, which is the most important derivation for KTU exams.

### Given System
- Primary coil self-inductance: $L_1$, AC resistance: $R_1$
- Secondary coil self-inductance: $L_2$, AC resistance: $R_2$
- Load resistance: $R_L$
- Mutual inductance: $M = k\sqrt{L_1 L_2}$
- Compensation capacitors: $C_1$ (primary), $C_2$ (secondary)
- Operating frequency: $\omega = 2\pi f$

### Step 1: Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) to the Secondary Loop

The induced EMF in the secondary is $j\omega M I_1$. The KVL equation for the secondary loop is:

$$j\omega M I_1 = (R_2 + j\omega L_2 + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_2} + R_L) I_2$$

### Step 2: At Resonance, Imaginary Terms Cancel

At $\omega = \omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{L_2 C_2}}$, the inductive and capacitive reactances cancel:

$$j\omega_0 L_2 + \dfrac{1}{j\omega_0 C_2} = 0$$

Therefore, the equation simplifies to:

$$j\omega_0 M I_1 = (R_2 + R_L) I_2$$

### Step 3: Solve for Secondary Current $I_2$

$$I_2 = \dfrac{j\omega_0 M I_1}{R_2 + R_L}$$

We take magnitudes for power analysis:

$$\vert I_2 \vert = \dfrac{\omega_0 M \vert I_1 \vert}{R_2 + R_L}$$

### Step 4: Output Power Delivered to Load $R_L$

$$P_{out} = \vert I_2 \vert^2 \cdot R_L = \dfrac{\omega_0^2 M^2 \vert I_1 \vert^2 R_L}{(R_2 + R_L)^2}$$

### Step 5: Input Power (Power Dissipated in Primary + Power Transferred)

The power leaving the primary source equals the power dissipated in $R_1$ plus the power transferred through mutual coupling:

$$P_{in} = \vert I_1 \vert^2 R_1 + \dfrac{\omega_0^2 M^2 \vert I_1 \vert^2}{R_2 + R_L}$$

> Note: At primary resonance, the primary capacitor $C_1$ absorbs only reactive power; thus all real input power is either lost in $R_1$ or transferred to the secondary side.

### Step 6: End-to-End Efficiency

$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{\dfrac{\omega_0^2 M^2 R_L}{(R_2 + R_L)^2}}{R_1 + \dfrac{\omega_0^2 M^2}{R_2 + R_L}}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $(R_2 + R_L)^2$:

$$\eta = \dfrac{\omega_0^2 M^2 R_L}{R_1(R_2 + R_L)^2 + \omega_0^2 M^2 (R_2 + R_L)}$$

$$\eta = \dfrac{\omega_0^2 k^2 L_1 L_2 R_L}{R_1(R_2 + R_L)^2 + \omega_0^2 k^2 L_1 L_2 (R_2 + R_L)}$$

### Step 7: Physical Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> **What this equation tells you:**
> - Efficiency is **proportional to $k^2$** — better alignment, larger coils, or ferrite flux concentrators directly improve $\eta$.
> - Efficiency is **proportional to $\omega_0^2$** — higher switching frequency helps, hence the choice of **85 kHz** instead of grid 50 Hz.
> - Efficiency **degrades** with parasitic resistances $R_1$ and $R_2$ — this is why Litz wire and Litz-braided coils are standard in EV WPT pads.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A static WPT system has $L_1 = L_2 = 200 \, \mu H$, $k = 0.2$, $f_0 = 85$ kHz, $R_1 = R_2 = 0.5 \, \Omega$, $R_L = 10 \, \Omega$. Calculate the end-to-end efficiency.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Compute angular frequency:
$$\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0 = 2\pi \times 85{,}000 = 5.34 \times 10^5 \, \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2:** Compute mutual inductance:
$$M = k \sqrt{L_1 L_2} = 0.2 \times 200 \times 10^{-6} = 4 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{H}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $\omega_0^2 M^2$:
$$\omega_0^2 M^2 = (5.34 \times 10^5)^2 \times (4 \times 10^{-5})^2 = 2.852 \times 10^{11} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-9} = 456.3$$

**Step 4:** Compute denominator terms:
$$R_1(R_2 + R_L)^2 = 0.5 \times (10.5)^2 = 0.5 \times 110.25 = 55.125$$
$$\omega_0^2 M^2 (R_2 + R_L) = 456.3 \times 10.5 = 4791.15$$

**Step 5:** Compute numerator:
$$\omega_0^2 M^2 R_L = 456.3 \times 10 = 4563$$

**Step 6:** Plug into efficiency formula:
$$\eta = \dfrac{4563}{55.125 + 4791.15} = \dfrac{4563}{4846.275} = 0.9417 = 94.17\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Conclusion:** With proper resonance tuning, **~94% efficiency** is achievable for an EV wireless charging system. This matches industry data from WiTricity and Momentum Dynamics prototypes.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Wireless Charging vs. Battery Swapping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This comparison matrix is the SINGLE most important table for KTU Module 3. Memorize it.**

| Parameter | Wireless Charging | Battery Swapping |
|-----------|-------------------|------------------|
| **Refueling Time** | 4–8 hours (3.6 kW), 1–2 hours (11 kW) | **3–5 minutes** |
| **User Effort** | Zero (park & wait) | Drive into bay, automated |
| **Capital Cost (Station)** | **Medium (₹15–40 lakh)** | **High (₹50 lakh – ₹1 Cr)** |
| **Battery Ownership** | Vehicle owner | Station / OEM (in business model) |
| **Grid Impact** | Continuous load (predictable) | Shifted (rack charging at off-peak) |
| **V2G Compatibility** | Easy (bidirectional WPT feasible) | Easy (rack-level V2G) |
| **Standardization Need** | High (SAE J2954) | **Very High (pack geometry must match)** |
| **Cycle Life Impact** | Neutral | **+15–25% (slow rack charging)** |
| **Suitable For** | Private cars, taxis | Commercial fleets, 2W/3W, buses |
| **Weather Sensitivity** | High (ice/snow on pad reduces $k$) | Low (indoor operation) |
| **Misalignment Tolerance** | **±75–100 mm** lateral | N/A (mechanical dock) |
| **Scalability** | Medium | High (modular bays) |
