# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define wireless charging of EVs. Mention the relevant international standard and its operating frequency band.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Wireless charging of EVs is the contactless transfer of electrical energy from a ground-mounted primary pad to a vehicle-mounted secondary pad using the principle of **electromagnetic induction** and **resonant inductive coupling**. The relevant international standard is **SAE J2954**, which specifies an operating frequency band of **81.39 kHz to 90 kHz** with a nominal center frequency of **85 kHz**. It classifies systems into power levels WPT1 (**3.7 kW**), WPT2 (**7.7 kW**), and WPT3 (**11 kW**), with WPT4 (**22 kW**) under development.

> **Mark Distribution:** Definition (1M) + Principle (1M) + SAE J2954 detail (1M).

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**What is battery swapping? List any two advantages and one major challenge.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Battery swapping is a refueling method in which a depleted EV battery is **mechanically exchanged for a fully charged one** at a dedicated swap station, completing the operation in **3–5 minutes**.

**Advantages:**
1. **Time efficiency:** Refueling time comparable to petrol stations, eliminating range anxiety.
2. **Battery life extension:** Swapped batteries are charged slowly at **1C**, improving cycle life by **15–25%**.

**Major Challenge:** **Standardization of battery packs** across OEMs — without uniform pack dimensions, connectors, and BMS protocols, a single station cannot serve multiple vehicle brands.

> **Mark Distribution:** Definition (1M) + Two advantages (1M) + One challenge (1M).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a static wireless EV charging system. Discuss the role of compensation networks and identify the most common topology used, justifying your answer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A static WPT system operates at $f_0 = 85$ kHz with $L_1 = L_2 = 250 \, \mu H$, coupling coefficient $k = 0.18$, primary resistance $R_1 = 0.4 \, \Omega$, secondary resistance $R_2 = 0.6 \, \Omega$, and load $R_L = 12 \, \Omega$. Calculate the end-to-end power transfer efficiency. State one practical factor that reduces this efficiency in real-world deployment. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**
Refer to the architecture shown in **Section 4.1** of these notes. The system consists of:
1. **Grid AC → PFC Stage** — corrects power factor to near unity.
2. **PFC → High-Frequency Inverter** — converts to **85 kHz** AC.
3. **Inverter → Primary Compensation $C_1$** — tunes the primary to resonance.
4. **Primary Pad → Air Gap → Secondary Pad** — inductive coupling occurs here.
5. **Secondary Pad → Secondary Compensation $C_2$ → Rectifier → DC-DC → Battery.**

**Role of Compensation Networks (2 marks):**
At resonance ($\omega_0 L = 1/\omega_0 C$), the inductive and capacitive reactances cancel, making the system present a **purely resistive** impedance to the source. This:
- Maximizes power transfer across the air gap.
- Eliminates circulating reactive currents, reducing inverter VA rating.
- Enables **Zero-Voltage Switching (ZVS)** in the inverter, improving efficiency.

**Most Common Topology — SS (Series-Series) (2 marks):**
The **SS** (Series-Primary, Series-Secondary) topology is most widely used because:
- The resonant capacitor naturally blocks DC and grid-frequency harmonics.
- The system operates at a **load-independent resonant frequency**, simplifying control.
- It is tolerant of coil misalignment up to **±100 mm** without significant detuning.
- It allows constant-current output, ideal for battery charging profiles.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
$f_0 = 85$ kHz, $L_1 = L_2 = 250 \, \mu H = 250 \times 10^{-6}$ H, $k = 0.18$, $R_1 = 0.4 \, \Omega$, $R_2 = 0.6 \, \Omega$, $R_L = 12 \, \Omega$

**Step 1:** Angular frequency:
$$\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0 = 2\pi \times 85{,}000 = 5.34 \times 10^5 \, \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2:** Mutual inductance:
$$M = k \sqrt{L_1 L_2} = 0.18 \times 250 \times 10^{-6} = 4.5 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{H}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $\omega_0^2 M^2$:
$$\omega_0^2 = (5.34 \times 10^5)^2 = 2.852 \times 10^{11}$$
$$\omega_0^2 M^2 = 2.852 \times 10^{11} \times (4.5 \times 10^{-5})^2 = 2.852 \times 10^{11} \times 2.025 \times 10^{-9} = 577.5$$

**Step 4:** Numerator of efficiency:
$$\omega_0^2 M^2 R_L = 577.5 \times 12 = 6930$$

**Step 5:** Denominator terms:
$$R_1 (R_2 + R_L)^2 = 0.4 \times (12.6)^2 = 0.4 \times 158.76 = 63.5$$
$$\omega_0^2 M^2 (R_2 + R_L) = 577.5 \times 12.6 = 7276.5$$

**Step 6:** Denominator total:
$$63.5 + 7276.5 = 7340$$

**Step 7:** Efficiency:
$$\eta = \dfrac{6930}{7340} = 0.9441 = 94.41\%$$

**Practical factor reducing real-world efficiency (1 mark):**
**Coil misalignment (lateral or vertical)** reduces the coupling coefficient $k$ from its nominal 0.18 to as low as 0.05–0.10, dropping efficiency by 10–20%. Ferrite flux concentrators and self-aligning parking guidance systems mitigate this.

> **Mark Distribution:** 6 steps of calculation (1M each) + practical factor (1M).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of battery swapping in EVs. With a neat sketch, describe the operation of an automated battery swap station. Compare battery swapping with DC fast charging in terms of time, infrastructure cost, and battery life. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A battery swap station maintains **10 battery packs** in inventory. Each pack has a capacity of **40 kWh** and is charged at a rate of **0.5C** using a constant-current constant-voltage (CCCV) protocol. If the average charging time is **2 hours**, calculate: (i) the charging power per pack, (ii) the total station charging load, and (iii) the maximum number of vehicles that can be served in a **10-hour** operating window, assuming each vehicle exchanges one pack. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Concept (2 marks):**
Battery swapping is the **mechanized exchange** of a depleted EV battery for a fully charged one at a **Battery Swap Station (BSS)**, completing in **3–5 minutes**. It converts a "charging problem" into a "logistics problem" — the EV user does not wait for charging; the station recharges batteries in the background at optimal (slow) rates.

**Operation of Automated Swap Station (3 marks):** (Refer to Section 4.2 diagram)
1. **Vehicle arrives** at the swap bay; RFID scans the VIN.
2. **Authentication & SOC check** — BMS reports state-of-charge and health.
3. **Robotic arm** locates the battery tray mounting bolts and unbolts them.
4. **Depleted battery extracted** via a conveyor to a charging rack.
5. **Charged battery retrieved** from the rack and inserted into the vehicle.
6. **Verification** of mechanical lock and electrical connectors.
7. **Billing** based on kWh delta and time; user departs.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Parameter | DC Fast Charging | Battery Swapping |
|-----------|------------------|------------------|
| **Time** | 30–60 min | 3–5 min |
| **Infrastructure Cost** | ₹10–20 lakh per charger | ₹50 lakh – ₹1 Cr per station |
| **Battery Life Impact** | Fast charging (2C) reduces cycle life by 10–20% | Slow rack charging (0.5C) **extends** life by 15–25% |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $N = 10$ packs, $E_{pack} = 40$ kWh, C-rate = 0.5C, $t_{charge} = 2$ hours.

**(i) Charging power per pack (2 marks):**
$$P_{pack} = 0.5C \times E_{pack} / t_{charge} = \dfrac{0.5 \times 40 \, \text{kWh}}{2 \, \text{h}} = 10 \, \text{kW}$$

Alternative: $P_{pack} = V \times I$. For a 400V pack, 0.5C means $I = 0.5 \times 100 = 50$ A, so $P = 400 \times 50 = 20$ kW. We use the energy-based definition here:
$$P_{pack} = \dfrac{E_{pack}}{t_{charge}} = \dfrac{40}{2} = 20 \, \text{kW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Correcting Step (i):** Power = Energy / Time = 40 kWh / 2 h = **20 kW per pack**.

**(ii) Total station charging load (2 marks):**
$$P_{total} = N \times P_{pack} = 10 \times 20 = 200 \, \text{kW}$$

**(iii) Maximum vehicles served in 10 hours (3 marks):**
Each charging cycle takes 2 hours; in a 10-hour window, each rack slot completes $10/2 = 5$ charge cycles. With 10 packs:
$$\text{Total charged packs} = 10 \times 5 = 50 \, \text{packs}$$

Assuming 1 pack per vehicle:
$$\text{Vehicles served} = 50$$

> **Mark Distribution:** (i) Formula + answer (2M), (ii) Multiplication + answer (2M), (iii) Logic + answer (3M).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** — kW vs. W confusion costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing WPT frequency (85 kHz) with grid frequency (50 Hz)** — examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **Not stating SS compensation topology explicitly** — losing 1 mark on the 7-mark architecture question.
> 4. **Writing "battery swapping is fast" without quantifying (3–5 minutes)** — vague answers get partial credit only.
> 5. **Skipping the standardization challenge** in battery swap answers — KTU Module 3 specifically tests this.
> 6. **In efficiency calculations, mixing up $R_1$ and $R_2$ roles** — the formula is asymmetric. Re-derive from KVL if unsure.
> 7. **Forgetting to mention SAE J2954** — every WPT question expects this standard to be cited.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Wireless Charging & Battery Swapping (Module 3)**

- [ ] **Wireless Power Transfer (WPT)** = contactless energy transfer via **electromagnetic induction + resonance**.
- [ ] **SAE J2954** standard regulates EV WPT at **85 kHz** (range 81.39–90 kHz).
- [ ] Power classes: **WPT1 (3.7 kW)**, **WPT2 (7.7 kW)**, **WPT3 (11 kW)**, **WPT4 (22 kW)**.
- [ ] **Coupling coefficient $k$** for EVs is **0.1 to 0.3** (loosely coupled due to air gap).
- [ ] Air gap for static WPT: **100–250 mm**.
- [ ] **Resonant frequency formula:** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ — **memorize**.
- [ ] **SS compensation** is the most common topology for EV WPT.
- [ ] **Mutual inductance:** $M = k\sqrt{L_1 L_2}$ — **memorize**.
- [ ] End-to-end WPT efficiency in practice: **88–94%**.
- [ ] **Battery swapping** completes in **3–5 minutes** (vs. 30–60 min DC fast charging).
- [ ] Swappable batteries are charged slowly at **0.5–1C**, extending cycle life by **15–25%**.
- [ ] **Major challenge of swapping: pack standardization** across OEMs.
- [ ] **Static WPT** = parking pad; **Dynamic WPT** = roadway-embedded coils.
- [ ] **Ferrite flux concentrators** are used in WPT pads to enhance $k$ and shield EMF.
- [ ] **V2G (Vehicle-to-Grid)** is feasible via both bidirectional WPT and swap-station rack discharge.
- [ ] Real-world deployment leaders: **WiTricity, Momentum Dynamics (WPT)**; **NIO, Gogoro, Sun Mobility (Swap)**.
- [ ] **Misalignment tolerance** for WPT pads: lateral **±75–100 mm**; vertical **±25–50 mm**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Different Charging Protocols: CHAdeMO
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT631 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Different Charging Protocols: CHAdeMO ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **CHAdeMO** (an abbreviation of **"CHArge de MOve"**, derived from the Japanese phrase *"O cha demo ikaga desuka"* meaning *"How about a cup of tea?"* — referencing the time required to charge) is a **DC fast charging protocol and connector standard** specifically developed for Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs) and Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicles (PHEVs). It was originally conceived by the **Tokyo Electric Power Company (TEPCO)** along with five major Japanese automakers (Nissan, Toyota, Mitsubishi, Fuji Heavy Industries — now Subaru, and Honda) and released commercially in **2009**. The standard is officially designated as **JEVS G105** (Japan Electric Vehicle Standard G105) under the IEC 62196-3 framework and is administered globally by the **CHAdeMO Association**, headquartered in Tokyo, Japan. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMUT631 Module 3):** > CHAdeMO is a Level 3 (DC Fast Charging) protocol. Students must remember it is **fundamentally different** from the AC-based Level 1/Level 2 protocols, because it features **direct high-voltage DC injection** into the traction battery, completely bypassing the on-board charger (OBC). ### 1.2 Key Technical Specifications (Across Versions) | Version | Year | Max Power | Max Voltage | Max Current | Key Innovation | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---| | CHAdeMO 1.0 | 2009 | **62.5 kW** | 500 V | 125 A | Baseline DC fast charging | | CHAdeMO 1.1 | 2012 | 62.5 kW | 500 V | 125 A | Enhanced diagnostics | | CHAdeMO 1.2 | 2017 | **200 kW** | 500 V | 400 A | High-power charging | | CHAdeMO 2.0 | 2018 | **400 kW** | 1000 V | 400 A | **V2X (V2G/V2H) capability** | | CHAdeMO 3.0 (ChaoJi) | 2020 | **900 kW** (asymmetric) | 1500 V | 600 A | Joint harmonization with GB/T | ### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture) > [!TIP] > **Analogy: "The Specialized Fuel Pump"** > Imagine two petrol stations across the street. Station A uses a thin nozzle that delivers petrol slowly (this is **AC Level 2 charging**, max ~22 kW). Station B uses a massive industrial-grade hose that pumps fuel directly into the tank at very high pressure (this is **CHAdeMO DC Fast Charging**). > > The "specialized hose" of CHAdeMO is engineered with a **unique connector (JEVS G105)** that physically cannot be confused with CCS or any AC plug. Just like industrial fuel pumps, CHAdeMO communicates continuously with the vehicle's brain (**Battery Management System — BMS**) through a dedicated "data cable" embedded in the same handle. This data line uses the **CAN bus protocol** (Controller Area Network), the same robust communication standard used inside cars for critical safety systems like airbags and ABS. > > The result? You can charge a Nissan Leaf from **20% to 80% in about 30 minutes** with a CHAdeMO 50 kW station — roughly the time it takes to enjoy a cup of tea! ☕ ### 1.4 Critical Distinction from AC Protocols > [!IMPORTANT] > **AC Charging (SAE J1772 / Type 2 / GB/T AC):** AC supply → On-Board Charger (OBC) → DC battery. > **CHAdeMO (DC Fast):** AC grid → **External Off-board Rectifier** → High-voltage DC → Directly into battery. > > This is why CHAdeMO is called a **"DC-coupled"** protocol. The rectification happens **outside the vehicle**, in the charging station itself. This architecture allows the use of much larger, liquid-cooled power electronics in the station, enabling power levels far exceeding the 6.6–22 kW typical of on-board chargers. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The CHAdeMO Communication Architecture CHAdeMO employs a **master-slave communication topology** built on the **CAN 2.0B** (Controller Area Network, ISO 11898) standard operating at a **baud rate of 500 kbps**. The two primary participants are: - **EVCC (Electric Vehicle Communication Controller):** The "master" in CHAdeMO. It initiates the session, sends battery parameters (max voltage, max current, SOC, temperature), and issues the **charge permission / termination** commands. - **SECC (Supply Equipment Communication Controller):** The "slave". It receives the EV's request and modulates the high-voltage DC output accordingly. > [!NOTE] > **Critical KTU Point:** The CAN identifier range used by CHAdeMO is **0x100 to 0x1FF** for charger-to-vehicle messages, and **0x200 to 0x2FF** for vehicle-to-charger messages. This isolation prevents cross-talk with the vehicle's internal CAN bus (which typically uses 0x7XX range for powertrain). ### 2.2 The 6-Phase CHAdeMO Charging Sequence The CHAdeMO charging session follows a strict deterministic state machine: 1. **Phase 1 — Initiation & Handshake:** - Connector is detected (proximity pilot pin shorts to ground). - Locking electromagnet on the connector is engaged. - Isolation monitoring circuit verifies galvanic isolation between grid and battery. - CAN bus wake-up is performed. 2. **Phase 2 — Parameter Exchange:** - EVCC broadcasts EV parameters: **Target Battery Voltage (V_bat)**, **Maximum Allowable Current (I_max)**, **State of Charge (SOC)**, **Available Charging Time**. - SECC broadcasts station capabilities: **Maximum Output Voltage (V_station)**, **Maximum Output Current (I_station)**, **Cable Current Rating**. 3. **Phase 3 — Insulation & Compatibility Check:** - The charger injects a small test voltage (typically 500 V DC) to measure **insulation resistance (R_iso)** between high-voltage lines and chassis ground. - **Rule:** R_iso must exceed **100 Ω/V of nominal system voltage** (per IEC 61851-23). 4. **Phase 4 — Pre-charge Ramp-up:** - The charger's output contactor closes. - The output voltage **ramps from 0 V up to the EV's reported battery voltage** at a controlled rate (typically **≤ 50 V/s**), to prevent inrush current damage. 5. **Phase 5 — Constant Current / Constant Voltage (CC/CV) Loop:** - The charger operates in a feedback loop, every **100 ms** sending updated current setpoints and every **1 s** receiving updated EV parameters. - Charging follows the standard **CC-CV profile** dictated by the BMS. 6. **Phase 6 — Termination:** - Either SOC reaches 100%, or EVCC issues a stop command. - Charger ramps down current at ≤ 50 A/s. - Output contactor opens. - CAN bus enters sleep mode. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why CAN bus and not PLC (Power Line Communication)?** > Unlike CCS which uses PLC (HomePlug Green PHY), CHAdeMO uses dedicated signal pins. This makes CHAdeMO communication more deterministic and lower-latency, but requires 2 extra large pins in the connector for the CAN-H and CAN-L signals. ### 2.3 CHAdeMO Connector Anatomy (JEVS G105) The connector has **10 pins** in total, organized as follows: | Pin # | Function | Type | Rating | |:---:|:---|:---:|:---| | 1 | DC Positive (+) | Power | 125 A (1.0) / 400 A (1.2+) | | 2 | DC Negative (−) | Power | 125 A / 400 A | | 3 | Protective Earth (PE) | Safety | — | | 4 | Charge Enable / Start | Signal | Low-voltage | | 5 | CAN-H | Communication | Differential pair | | 6 | CAN-L | Communication | Differential pair | | 7 | Proximity Detection | Signal | 5 V logic | | 8 | Charger Present | Signal | — | | 9 | Locking Motor (+) | Control | 12 V / 24 V | | 10 | Locking Motor (−) | Control | — | ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | Boundary / Notes | |:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---| | 1 | $P_{DC} = V_{bat} \times I_{chg}$ | Instantaneous DC charging power | **kW** | $V_{bat}$ in V, $I_{chg}$ in A | | 2 | $t_{charge} = \dfrac{E_{required}}{P_{DC} \times \eta_{sys}}$ | Total charging time | **hours (h)** | $\eta_{sys}$ typically 0.85–0.92 | | 3 | $E_{required} = C_{bat} \times V_{nom} \times (SOC_{final} - SOC_{initial})$ | Energy to be transferred | **kWh** | $C_{bat}$ in Ah, $V_{nom}$ in V | | 4 | $\Delta SOC = \dfrac{P_{DC} \times t}{E_{bat,usable}}$ | Percentage SOC change | **dimensionless (%)** | Useful for fast SOC estimation | | 5 | $I_{max,derated} = I_{rated} \times \left(1 - k_T \cdot (T_{cell} - T_{ref})\right)$ | Current derating due to battery temperature | **A** | $k_T$ is thermal coefficient | | 6 | $R_{iso,min} = 100 \times V_{nom}$ | Minimum insulation resistance | **Ω** | Per IEC 61851-23 | | 7 | $I_{cable,max} = \dfrac{P_{station,max}}{V_{bat,min}}$ | Maximum cable current draw | **A** | Ensures cable current rating not exceeded | | 8 | $P_{peak,CHAdeMO1.0} = 500 \text{ V} \times 125 \text{ A} = 62.5 \text{ kW}$ | Original CHAdeMO peak power | **kW** | First-generation benchmark | | 9 | $P_{peak,CHAdeMO2.0} = 1000 \text{ V} \times 400 \text{ A} = 400 \text{ kW}$ | Modern CHAdeMO peak power | **kW** | Liquid-cooled connectors required | | 10 | $E_{V2G} = V_{nom} \times I_{disch} \times t_{disch} \times \eta_{round}$ | Energy discharged back to grid (V2G) | **kWh** | $\eta_{round}$ is round-trip efficiency (~0.85) | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Real-World Utility:** > The V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) feature introduced in CHAdeMO 2.0 is **the world's first commercially deployed bidirectional DC charging protocol**. The Nissan LEAF was the first production car (in 2013) to support V2G, enabling it to power a house for ~2 days during blackouts — a critical feature that drove CHAdeMO's adoption in Japan post-Fukushima disaster. ### 2.5 CHAdeMO vs. Other DC Fast Charging Standards (Engineering Comparison) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters for KTU:** Module 3 specifically covers multiple charging protocols. Examiners frequently ask comparison-based questions. | Parameter | CHAdeMO | CCS2 (Combo 2) | Tesla Supercharger (NACS) | GB/T DC | |:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | Origin | Japan | Germany/EU | USA | China | | Communication | CAN bus | PLC (HomePlug) | Proprietary CAN | CAN bus | | Max Power | 400 kW (900 kW ChaoJi) | 350 kW (1000 V) | 250 kW (V3) | 250 kW | | V2G | **Yes (native)** | Limited rollout | No (planned) | Yes (planned) | | Connector size | Large | Largest | Compact | Medium | | Vehicle support | Nissan, Mitsubishi, Kia (legacy), Toyota | BMW, VW, Hyundai, Mercedes, Tata | Tesla (US), Ford, GM (adopting) | Chinese OEMs | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Time Required to Charge an EV from 20% to 80% SOC Using a 50 kW CHAdeMO Station **Given Data:** - Battery nominal capacity: $C_{bat} = 40 \text{ kWh}$ (typical mid-size EV battery) - Initial State of Charge: $SOC_{initial} = 20\%$ - Final State of Charge: $SOC_{final} = 80\%$ - CHAdeMO 1.0 station power: $P_{DC} = 50 \text{ kW}$ - System (charger + battery) efficiency: $\eta_{sys} = 0.90$ - Battery nominal voltage: $V_{nom} = 360 \text{ V}$ **Step 1: Calculate the required energy to be transferred.** The energy stored in a battery is given by $E = V \times Q$ where $Q$ is charge in Ah, or simply the capacity in kWh. $$ E_{required} = C_{bat} \times (SOC_{final} - SOC_{initial}) $$ Substitute values: $$ E_{required} = 40 \text{ kWh} \times (0.80 - 0.20) $$ $$ E_{required} = 40 \text{ kWh} \times 0.60 $$ $$ E_{required} = 24 \text{ kWh} $$ **Step 2: Calculate the actual charging time using the charging power equation.** Rearranging the power equation $P = E / t$ to solve for $t$: $$ t_{charge} = \dfrac{E_{required}}{P_{DC} \times \eta_{sys}} $$ Substitute values: $$ t_{charge} = \dfrac{24 \text{ kWh}}{50 \text{ kW} \times 0.90} $$ $$ t_{charge} = \dfrac{24 \text{ kWh}}{45 \text{ kW}} $$ $$ t_{charge} = 0.5333 \text{ hours} $$ **Step 3: Convert to minutes for engineering intuition.** $$ t_{charge} = 0.5333 \text{ hours} \times 60 \text{ min/hour} = 32 \text{ minutes} $$ **Final Result:** $\boxed{t_{charge} \approx 32 \text{ minutes}}$ **Engineering Insight:** This matches the famous CHAdeMO marketing claim — a "tea break" (~30 minutes) of charging adds ~60% SOC, the optimal range extension without significantly accelerating battery degradation (which is non-linear above 80% SOC). --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Cable Current Draw for a CHAdeMO 2.0 Station Charging a 400 V Battery Pack **Given Data:** - CHAdeMO 2.0 max station power: $P_{station,max} = 400 \text{ kW}$ - EV battery minimum voltage: $V_{bat,min} = 320 \text{ V}$ (at very low SOC) - EV battery maximum voltage: $V_{bat,max} = 480 \text{ V}$ (at 100% SOC) **Step 1: Calculate the maximum current the cable must conduct.** In a constant-power region, the current is inversely proportional to the battery voltage. The **worst case (highest current)** occurs at the **lowest battery voltage**. $$ I_{cable,max} = \dfrac{P_{station,max}}{V_{bat,min}} $$ Substitute values: $$ I_{cable,max} = \dfrac{400{,}000 \text{ W}}{320 \text{ V}} $$ $$ I_{cable,max} = 1250 \text{ A} $$ **Step 2: Compare against cable rating.** The standard CHAdeMO 2.0 cable is rated for **400 A continuous**. At $I = 1250 \text{ A}$, the cable would thermally fail. **Step 3: Conclude operating constraint.** To stay within the 400 A cable limit, the maximum charge power at low battery voltage must be limited to: $$ P_{limited} = V_{bat,min} \times I_{cable,rated} $$ $$ P_{limited} = 320 \text{ V} \times 400 \text{ A} = 128 \text{ kW} $$ **Step 4: Calculate the minimum battery voltage at which the full 400 kW can be delivered.** $$ V_{bat,threshold} = \dfrac{P_{station,max}}{I_{cable,rated}} $$ $$ V_{bat,threshold} = \dfrac{400{,}000 \text{ W}}{400 \text{ A}} = 1000 \text{ V} $$ **Final Conclusion:** The full **400 kW power is only achievable if $V_{bat} \geq 1000 \text{ V}$**. For typical 400 V class EVs, the practical maximum is ~128 kW. This is precisely why **800 V architecture vehicles** (e.g., Porsche Taycan, Hyundai E-GMP, Kia EV6) can leverage CHAdeMO 2.0's high-power capabilities — they reach the 1000 V threshold naturally during the CV phase. > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistake:** Many students assume "CHAdeMO 2.0 = 400 kW" is always delivered. **NO** — power is limited by the minimum of (Station max, Cable max, BMS-reported max). This is the **"Min-Function Rule"** of EV charging: > $$P_{actual} = \min(P_{station}, P_{cable}, P_{BMS})$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: V2G Energy Discharge and Grid Revenue Calculation **Given Data:** - EV battery: 60 kWh usable capacity - Discharge window: 8 hours (overnight, 11 PM to 7 AM) - Average discharge power: $P_{disch} = 7 \text{ kW}$ - Round-trip efficiency: $\eta_{round} = 0.85$ - Grid electricity price differential (peak vs. off-peak): $C_{grid} = \text{INR } 8 \text{ per kWh}$ **Step 1: Calculate the DC energy leaving the battery.** $$ E_{DC,out} = P_{disch} \times t_{disch} $$ $$ E_{DC,out} = 7 \text{ kW} \times 8 \text{ h} = 56 \text{ kWh} $$ **Step 2: Calculate the AC energy actually injected into the grid after inverter losses.** $$ E_{AC,grid} = E_{DC,out} \times \eta_{round} $$ $$ E_{AC,grid} = 56 \text{ kWh} \times 0.85 = 47.6 \text{ kWh} $$ **Step 3: Calculate the revenue earned.** $$ R = E_{AC,grid} \times C_{grid} $$ $$ R = 47.6 \text{ kWh} \times 8 \text{ INR/kWh} = \text{INR } 380.8 $$ **Step 4: Calculate the daily cycle degradation impact.** If V2G is performed every night for 1 year, the equivalent full cycles added to the battery are: $$ N_{cycles} = \dfrac{365 \text{ days} \times E_{AC,grid}}{C_{bat,usable}} = \dfrac{365 \times 47.6}{60} \approx 290 \text{ cycles} $$ For a battery rated at 2000 cycles, this represents **14.5%** of its calendar cycle life consumed in one year of V2G service. **Final Engineering Verdict:** V2G via CHAdeMO is technically and economically feasible, but battery warranty terms must explicitly allow bidirectional cycling — a current industry challenge. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 CHAdeMO System-Level Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB GRID[AC Grid Supply
415V 3-phase] --> RECT[Off-board Rectifier
AC to DC Converter] RECT --> PWR[Power Factor Correction
PFC Stage] PWR --> DCBUS[High Voltage DC Bus
500V to 1000V] DCBUS --> CONTACTOR[Output Contactor
High-voltage switch] CONTACTOR --> CABLE[Liquid Cooled Cable
CHAdeMO JEVS G105] CABLE --> EVBAT[EV Traction Battery Pack] EVBAT --> BMS[Battery Management System
EVCC Master] BMS --> CAN1[CAN Bus Transceiver] CAN1 --> CONNECTOR[Connector Signal Pins
CAN-H and CAN-L] CONNECTOR --> CAN2[Charger CAN Controller
SECC Slave] CAN2 --> CTRL[Charger Control Unit] CTRL --> RECT USER[User Interface
Touch Screen] --> CTRL CTRL --> METER[Billing and Metering] ISOMON[Insulation Monitor
Detects leakage to chassis] -.-> CONTACTOR TEMPSENSE[Temperature Sensors
Cable and connector] -.-> CTRL ``` ### 4.2 CHAdeMO 6-Phase Charging Sequence Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TB A[User Plugs Connector into EV] --> B[Proximity Pin Detected
Locking Motor Engaged] B --> C[CAN Bus Wake-up
500 kbps] C --> D[Phase 1: Parameter Exchange
EV sends SOC, Vmax, Imax, Tmax] D --> E[Phase 2: Insulation Test
R-iso must be greater than 100 ohms per V] E --> F{Isolation OK?} F -- No --> ERR1[Abort Session
Display Error] F -- Yes --> G[Phase 3: Pre-charge Ramp
Voltage rises at less than 50 V per second] G --> H[Phase 4: CC-CV Charging Loop
Update every 100 ms] H --> I{Termination Trigger?
SOC equals 100% OR User Stop OR Fault} I -- No --> H I -- Yes --> J[Phase 5: Ramp Down
Current drops at less than 50 A per second] J --> K[Phase 6: Session End
Contactor Opens, CAN Sleep] K --> L[User Unplugs Connector] ``` ### 4.3 CHAdeMO vs. CCS Communication Protocol Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CHADEMO[CHAdeMO Architecture] EV1[EV BMS] -->|CAN bus 500 kbps
Dedicated pins| CHG1[Charger Controller] end subgraph CCS[CCS Architecture] EV2[EV BMS] -->|PLC over pilot line
HomePlug Green PHY| CHG2[Charger Controller] end style CHADEMO fill:#e1f5ff style CCS fill:#fff4e1 ``` ### 4.4 CHAdeMO Connector Pinout Schematic (Text-Based Architecture Map) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CONN[JEVS G105 Connector - Front View] P1[Pin 1: DC Positive
Power pin] P2[Pin 2: DC Negative
Power pin] P3[Pin 3: Protective Earth
Chassis ground] P4[Pin 4: Charge Permission Signal
Low voltage logic] P5[Pin 5: CAN High
Communication] P6[Pin 6: CAN Low
Communication] P7[Pin 7: Proximity Detection
Cable presence] P8[Pin 8: Charger Present
Station handshake] P9[Pin 9: Lock Motor Plus
12V supply] P10[Pin 10: Lock Motor Minus
Return path] end P1 -->|500V to 1000V DC| BATT[To Traction Battery] P2 -->|Return path| BATT P5 <-->|CAN 2.0B| ECUBMS[To EVCC BMS] P6 <-->|CAN 2.0B| ECUBMS P3 -->|Safety| CHASSIS[To Vehicle Chassis] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define CHAdeMO. What is the meaning of the word "CHAdeMO"?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** CHAdeMO is a DC fast charging protocol standard for electric vehicles, developed originally by TEPCO and Japanese automakers in 2009, with the formal designation JEVS G105 **(1 mark)**. The acronym stands for **"CHArge de MOve"** **(1 mark)**, derived from the Japanese phrase *"O cha demo ikaga desuka"* meaning *"How about a cup of tea?"* — symbolizing the short time (~30 minutes) required to fast-charge an EV **(1 mark)**. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **List any three salient features of the CHAdeMO 2.0 charging standard.** **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):** 1. Supports up to **400 kW** DC fast charging at **1000 V** and **400 A**. 2. Introduces **native V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) bidirectional capability**, enabling V2G and V2H. 3. Maintains **CAN bus communication** (500 kbps) for deterministic, low-latency control, unlike CCS which uses PLC. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]** **Q. (a)** Explain the complete 6-phase charging sequence of the CHAdeMO protocol with a neat block diagram. Clearly state the role of the CAN bus communication. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 50 kWh electric vehicle battery is to be charged from 15% to 85% SOC using a CHAdeMO 50 kW DC fast charger. If the system efficiency is 92%, calculate: (i) The total energy to be transferred. (ii) The total charging time in minutes. (iii) The average DC current supplied if the battery voltage is 360 V. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Question A **(a) 6-Phase CHAdeMO Charging Sequence (7 marks):** **(Phase 1 — Initiation & Handshake — 1 mark):** When the user inserts the CHAdeMO connector, the proximity pin completes a circuit. The locking electromagnet in the connector is energized to prevent accidental disconnection during the high-current session. The CAN transceivers in both EV and charger are woken from sleep mode. **(Phase 2 — Parameter Exchange — 1 mark):** The EV's **EVCC (Electric Vehicle Communication Controller)** broadcasts a set of CAN messages at 100 ms intervals, containing: - Target battery voltage (V_target) - Maximum allowable charging current (I_max) - Current State of Charge (SOC) - Estimated remaining charging time - Battery temperature flags The **SECC (Supply Equipment Communication Controller)** replies with station parameters (V_station_max, I_station_max, cable rating, pricing). **(Phase 3 — Insulation Test — 1 mark):** Before any high voltage is applied, the station injects a small test voltage (~500 V DC) between the high-voltage lines and protective earth. The leakage current is measured and the **insulation resistance R_iso** is calculated. The session aborts if $R_{iso} < 100 \times V_{nom}$ (per IEC 61851-23). **(Phase 4 — Pre-charge Ramp-up — 1 mark):** The output contactor closes. The charger voltage ramps from 0 V upward to match the EV battery voltage at a controlled rate $\le 50$ V/s to prevent inrush currents. This is analogous to slowly opening a high-pressure valve. **(Phase 5 — CC-CV Charging Loop — 2 marks):** The charger now operates in a closed-loop feedback mode. The BMS continuously monitors each cell's voltage, temperature, and SOC. The charger follows a **Constant Current (CC)** profile until the battery reaches a manufacturer-defined voltage threshold (typically 4.2 V/cell for NMC), then transitions to **Constant Voltage (CV)** mode where current tapers exponentially as SOC approaches 100%. CAN messages are exchanged every 100 ms (current setpoints) and 1 s (battery telemetry). This is the "active heart" of the charging session. **(Phase 6 — Termination — 1 mark):** When SOC = 100%, OR the user presses stop, OR a fault occurs (over-temperature, isolation drop, communication timeout), the charger ramps down current at $\le 50$ A/s, opens the output contactor, and the CAN bus returns to sleep. The locking motor is de-energized only after current has ceased, ensuring safe disconnection. **Role of CAN bus (built into above):** CAN 2.0B at 500 kbps provides **deterministic, real-time, differential signaling** between the EV and charger. It is robust against electromagnetic noise generated by the high-current DC switching — critical for safety-critical messages like emergency stop. --- **(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):** **Given:** - $C_{bat} = 50 \text{ kWh}$ - $SOC_{initial} = 15\%$ - $SOC_{final} = 85\%$ - $P_{DC} = 50 \text{ kW}$ - $\eta_{sys} = 0.92$ - $V_{bat} = 360 \text{ V}$ **(i) Energy to be transferred (2 marks):** $$ E_{required} = C_{bat} \times (SOC_{final} - SOC_{initial}) $$ $$ E_{required} = 50 \text{ kWh} \times (0.85 - 0.15) = 50 \times 0.70 $$ $$ \boxed{E_{required} = 35 \text{ kWh}} $$ **(ii) Total charging time (3 marks):** $$ t_{charge} = \dfrac{E_{required}}{P_{DC} \times \eta_{sys}} $$ $$ t_{charge} = \dfrac{35 \text{ kWh}}{50 \text{ kW} \times 0.92} = \dfrac{35}{46} \text{ h} $$ $$ t_{charge} = 0.7609 \text{ h} $$ Converting to minutes: $$ \boxed{t_{charge} = 0.7609 \times 60 = 45.65 \text{ minutes}} $$ **(iii) Average DC current (2 marks):** $$ I_{avg} = \dfrac{P_{DC}}{V_{bat}} = \dfrac{50{,}000 \text{ W}}{360 \text{ V}} $$ $$ \boxed{I_{avg} = 138.89 \text{ A}} $$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **Q. (a)** Discuss the evolution of the CHAdeMO standard from version 1.0 to 3.0 (ChaoJi). Compare CHAdeMO and CCS2 with respect to communication protocol, maximum power, and V2G capability. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A CHAdeMO 2.0 station rated at 200 kW and 1000 V is connected to an EV whose battery pack is rated 80 kWh, 800 V nominal. The BMS reports a maximum charging current of 350 A. Calculate: (i) The maximum possible charging power. (ii) The minimum time required to charge from 10% to 90% SOC assuming 90% efficiency. (iii) Comment on whether the station's full rated power can be utilized. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Question B **(a) Evolution & Comparison (7 marks):** **Evolution (4 marks):** - **CHAdeMO 1.0 (2009):** Original 62.5 kW max (500 V × 125 A), designed for early Nissan LEAF. **1 mark** - **CHAdeMO 1.2 (2017):** Power doubled to 200 kW with 400 A capability. Improved connector cooling. **1 mark** - **CHAdeMO 2.0 (2018):** 400 kW / 1000 V support. **First-ever commercial V2G** bidirectional protocol, validated in Japan post-Fukushima. **1 mark** - **CHAdeMO 3.0 / ChaoJi (2020):** Joint harmonization with China's GB/T. Up to 900 kW asymmetric (CHAdeMO → GB/T) and 500 kW symmetric. Uses a new lightweight connector derived from GB/T mechanical design with CHAdeMO protocol. **1 mark** **Comparison with CCS2 (3 marks — 1 mark each):** | Feature | CHAdeMO 2.0 | CCS2 | |:---|:---:|:---:| | Communication | CAN bus (500 kbps) | PLC via HomePlug Green PHY | | Max Power | 400 kW (1000 V, 400 A) | 350 kW (1000 V, 350 A) | | V2G | **Native, commercially deployed** | Limited (e.g., pilot projects in EU) | --- **(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):** **Given:** - $P_{station,max} = 200 \text{ kW}$ - $V_{station,max} = 1000 \text{ V}$ - $C_{bat} = 80 \text{ kWh}$ - $V_{bat,nom} = 800 \text{ V}$ - $I_{BMS,max} = 350 \text{ A}$ - $\eta_{sys} = 0.90$ **(i) Maximum possible charging power (2 marks):** Apply the **Min-Function Rule:** $$ P_{actual} = \min(P_{station}, P_{cable}, P_{BMS}) $$ Calculate each limit: - $P_{station} = 200 \text{ kW}$ (given) - $P_{cable}$ for 400 A rated cable: $P_{cable} = 800 \text{ V} \times 400 \text{ A} = 320 \text{ kW}$ (not binding) - $P_{BMS} = V_{bat} \times I_{BMS,max} = 800 \text{ V} \times 350 \text{ A} = 280 \text{ kW}$ (not binding) $$ \boxed{P_{actual} = \min(200, 320, 280) = 200 \text{ kW}} $$ **The station itself is the bottleneck.** **(ii) Minimum charging time (3 marks):** $$ E_{required} = 80 \times (0.90 - 0.10) = 80 \times 0.80 = 64 \text{ kWh} $$ $$ t_{charge} = \dfrac{E_{required}}{P_{actual} \times \eta_{sys}} = \dfrac{64}{200 \times 0.90} $$ $$ t_{charge} = \dfrac{64}{180} = 0.3556 \text{ h} $$ $$ \boxed{t_{charge} = 0.3556 \times 60 \approx 21.33 \text{ minutes}} $$ **(iii) Comment (2 marks):** Yes, the station's full **200 kW rated power can be utilized** in this scenario because: - The cable rating (400 A) allows up to 320 kW — exceeds 200 kW. - The BMS allows up to 280 kW — exceeds 200 kW. - Therefore, the station's 200 kW rating becomes the actual limiting factor. - **Engineering implication:** For this EV, an even higher-power station (e.g., 280 kW) would still be limited to 280 kW by the BMS, not the station. Vehicle-side limitations are often the true bottleneck in modern EVs. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Watch Your Score!):** > 1. **Confusing AC and DC protocols:** CHAdeMO is **DC fast charging**. Students who write "CHAdeMO uses the on-board charger" lose **2 marks** immediately. > 2. **Ignoring the Min-Function Rule:** In numerical problems, simply taking the station's rated power as the actual delivered power (without checking cable and BMS limits) results in **partial credit only** (1 of 3 marks). > 3. **Forgetting the system efficiency ($\eta_{sys}$):** Charging time calculations must include $\eta_{sys} \approx 0.85 - 0.92$. Without it, your answer will be wrong by ~10–15%. Examiners **specifically test** this in numericals. > 4. **Mixing up CAN bus and PLC:** CHAdeMO = CAN, CCS = PLC. Confusing them is a 2-mark deduction in comparison questions. > 5. **Ignoring units:** Writing $t = 35/46$ without converting hours to minutes loses 1 mark. Always state the final unit clearly. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Final 60-Second Revision Bulletins — Memorize Before Exam!** - [x] **CHAdeMO = DC fast charging, originated in Japan (2009, TEPCO + 5 OEMs).** - [x] Acronym etymology: **CHArge de MOve** = "How about tea?" (~30 min charge time). - [x] Formal standard ID: **JEVS G105** (under IEC 62196-3). - [x] Communication: **CAN 2.0B at 500 kbps** (NOT PLC). - [x] Connector: **10 pins** including 2 power, CAN-H, CAN-L, PE, proximity, lock motor. - [x] Version 1.0 → 62.5 kW; Version 2.0 → 400 kW @ 1000 V; ChaoJi 3.0 → up to 900 kW. - [x] **CHAdeMO 2.0 is the world's first commercially deployed V2G protocol.** - [x] Charging sequence has **6 phases**: Handshake → Parameter Exchange → Insulation Test → Pre-charge Ramp → CC-CV Loop → Termination. - [x] Insulation resistance rule: $R_{iso} \geq 100 \times V_{nom}$ (Ω). - [x] Voltage ramp rate limit: $\leq 50$ V/s. Current ramp-down: $\leq 50$ A/s. - [x] **Min-Function Rule:** $P_{actual} = \min(P_{station}, P_{cable}, P_{BMS})$. - [x] **8 V vs 400 V architecture:** 800 V EVs (Taycan, EV6, Ioniq 5) benefit more from CHAdeMO 2.0 high power because they reach the 1000 V threshold needed to draw full 400 kW at 400 A cable limit. - [x] Time formula: $t = E_{required} / (P \times \eta_{sys})$ — always include efficiency. - [x] Real-world charging target: **20% → 80% SOC in ~30 minutes** with 50 kW CHAdeMO (optimal for battery longevity). - [x] **EVCC = Master (vehicle side); SECC = Slave (charger side).** - [x] **Bidirectional V2G** is the killer feature differentiating CHAdeMO from CCS in the global market.

topics.title: CCS2
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT631 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** CCS2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Combined Charging System 2 (CCS2)** — also known as **Combo 2** — is the European-origin, internationally harmonized DC fast-charging standard for Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs) and Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicles (PHEVs). It is formally standardized under **IEC 62196-3 (Configuration FF)** and uses **ISO 15118** as its high-level digital communication protocol.

Physically, the CCS2 connector is an evolution of the **IEC 62196 Type 2 (Mennekes) AC plug**, augmented with **two large additional DC power pins (DC+ and DC−)** mounted directly beneath the AC pin array. This enables the same physical inlet on the vehicle to support both:
- **AC Level 1 / Level 2 charging** (via the upper Type 2 contacts), and
- **DC Level 1 / Level 2 / Level 3 fast charging** (via the lower DC contacts).

This dual-capability, single-inlet architecture is the defining hallmark of CCS2 and the reason it is called the "Combined" Charging System.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The European Union's *Alternative Fuels Infrastructure Regulation (AFIR)*, in force since 2024, mandates that **every new public DC fast charger ≥ 50 kW installed in the EU must offer a CCS2 connector**. This makes CCS2 effectively the global default for new EV deployments outside of Japan, China, and North America.

## 1.2 Key Standardized Performance Metrics

| Metric | Standardized Value |
|---|---|
| Maximum DC Voltage | **1000 V DC** (practical operation typically up to **920 V DC**) |
| Maximum DC Current | **500 A** (continuous, liquid-cooled cable) |
| Maximum Power Transfer | **350 kW** (current practical ceiling); theoretical ceiling **500 kW** |
| AC Mode (backward compatible) | Up to **43 kW** (3-phase, 63 A) on Type 2 contacts |
| Communication Protocol | **ISO 15118-2** (Plug & Charge), **ISO 15118-20** (next-gen) |
| Signaling | **PLC (HomePlug Green PHY)** over Control Pilot pin |
| Locking Actuator | **Electromechanical latch** (12 V / 9 V / 6 V signaling) |
| Vehicle Inlet Standard | **IEC 62196-3 Configuration FF** |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "All-in-One Power Socket"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Universal Power Socket of EVs"**

Imagine your home had only a single wall socket that could intelligently accept *both* a slow overnight phone charger *and* a high-power industrial welding plug — without you ever changing the wall plate. That is exactly what CCS2 does for an EV.

- The **upper half** of the CCS2 inlet is like a regular household socket — it accepts the slow AC plug you use at home overnight.
- The **lower half** (the two chunky DC pins) is like the heavy-duty industrial three-phase plug — used only at a highway service station for a 15-minute rapid top-up.

When you plug in a CCS2 DC fast-charging cable at a highway station, the vehicle's inlet automatically detects the heavy DC current, locks the connector electro-mechanically (so nobody can yank it out while **500 A** is flowing!), and begins high-bandwidth digital handshaking via the **Control Pilot (CP)** pin using Power Line Communication. The AC pins remain physically present but are electrically inactive during DC mode.

## 1.4 Why CCS2 Matters — Engineering Significance

1. **Single-inlet simplicity** — automakers no longer need separate AC and DC ports on the vehicle body.
2. **Backward compatibility** — a CCS2-equipped vehicle can still use any Type 2 AC destination charger.
3. **High power ceiling** — supports both today's 150 kW chargers and tomorrow's 350 kW+ highway hubs.
4. **Smart-grid ready** — ISO 15118 supports **Plug & Charge**, **bidirectional charging (V2G)**, and **autonomous authentication**, all critical for future EV-grid integration.
5. **Global harmonization** — adopted in EU, UK, India, Australia, South Korea, UAE, Saudi Arabia, and parts of South America.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the CCS2 Connector (Pin-by-Pin)

The CCS2 coupler (per **IEC 62196-3 Configuration FF**) consists of **seven active contacts**:

| Pin # | Name | Function | DC Mode | AC Mode |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **L1** | AC Phase 1 | Inactive | Active (carry AC phase) |
| 2 | **L2** | AC Phase 2 | Inactive | Active |
| 3 | **L3** | AC Phase 3 | Inactive | Active |
| 4 | **N** | AC Neutral | Inactive | Active |
| 5 | **PE** | Protective Earth | Active | Active |
| 6 | **CP** | Control Pilot | Active (PLC over CP) | Active (PWM pilot) |
| 7 | **PP** | Proximity Pilot | Active | Active |
| 8 | **DC+** | DC Positive Power | Active (up to 920 V, 500 A) | Inactive |
| 9 | **DC−** | DC Negative Power | Active | Inactive |
| — | **Locking Actuator** | Electromechanical latch (12 V/9 V/6 V) | Active | Active |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** Students often confuse **CP (Control Pilot)** and **PP (Proximity Pilot)**. CP carries the *communication signal* (PWM in AC, PLC in DC). PP is a simple resistor-divider that tells the EV *whether a plug is physically inserted* and *what current-carrying capacity the cable has* — it is the **safety interlock**, not the data channel.

## 2.2 CCS2 Charging Modes & Power Classes

Per IEC 61851-23, DC charging via CCS2 is categorized into four modes, but the **KTU-relevant** distinction is:

| Mode | Voltage Range | Current Range | Typical Power | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mode 4 (DC Level 1) | 50–500 V | ≤ 80 A | ≤ 40 kW | Legacy / depot |
| Mode 4 (DC Level 2) | 200–920 V | ≤ 200 A | ≤ 150 kW | Urban fast charging |
| Mode 4 (DC Level 3) | 200–920 V | ≤ 375 A | ≤ 350 kW | Highway HPC (High Power Charging) |
| Mode 4 (Future) | up to 1000 V | up to 500 A | up to 500 kW | Megawatt Charging System (MCS for trucks) |

## 2.3 The CCS2 Charging Handshake — Stepwise Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-Step Operational Flow** (this sequence is **directly examinable** in KTU Part B questions):

**Step 1 — Physical Engagement & PP Detection**
- The CCS2 coupler is inserted into the vehicle inlet. A mechanical push rod actuates a switch inside the PP circuit.
- The EV measures the **PP resistor value** to determine:
  - That a connector is present (PP ≠ open circuit)
  - The **current rating of the attached cable** (e.g., 150 A, 200 A, 375 A, 500 A)
  - The **temperature of the cable** (via additional PP pin on some implementations)

**Step 2 — Electromechanical Locking**
- The EV applies a voltage to the locking actuator circuit.
- The station receives a **12 V** "lock request" signal, activates the latch, and confirms by switching to **9 V** (or **6 V** in some vehicles) to indicate a *securely locked* state.
- If the latch fails, charging will **not start** — this is a hard safety interlock.

**Step 3 — Pilot Signal (CP) Handshake**
- In AC mode: a **±12 V, 1 kHz PWM signal** with a duty cycle encoding current limit (e.g., 10% = 6 A, 90% = 63 A) is sent on CP.
- In DC mode: a **±12 V digital signal** indicates "ready to communicate," after which **PLC (HomePlug Green PHY)** is overlaid on the same CP conductor using OFDM in the 1.3–30 MHz band.

**Step 4 — ISO 15118 Digital Communication**
- **SLAC (Signal Level Attenuation Characterization):** EV and EVSE exchange signals to discover each other on the PLC network.
- **SDP (SECC Discovery Protocol):** Match IPv6 multicast addresses.
- **TCP/TLS handshake** over IPv6 establishes a secure encrypted channel.
- **V2G session**: EV identifies itself (either by **Plug & Charge** — ISO 15118-2 certificate-based auth — or via **EIM** — External Identification Means like an RFID card).
- **ChargeParameterDiscovery**: EV sends its **battery State of Charge (SoC)**, target SoC, maximum voltage, maximum current, and requested power.

**Step 5 — Cable Check & Insulation Test**
- The EVSE performs an **isolation resistance measurement** between DC+ and PE (must typically be ≥ **100 kΩ/V** of nominal voltage, e.g., ≥ 100 MΩ for 1000 V systems).
- The EVSE verifies the DC contactor's welded status (pre-charge test).

**Step 6 — Pre-charge**
- EVSE closes the internal DC contactor, bringing DC+ to a voltage just below the EV's pack voltage (typically 5–20 V lower) to prevent inrush arcing when the vehicle's contactors close.

**Step 7 — Energy Transfer**
- EV closes its internal HV contactors. Current ramps up per the agreed **Current Demand Loop** (updated every ~100 ms via ISO 15118 messages).
- **Continuous insulation monitoring** and **thermistor monitoring** of cable/connector pins occur throughout.

**Step 8 — Termination**
- Either SoC target reached or user stops the session.
- EVSE ramps current to 0, opens its contactors, EV opens its contactors.
- The lock releases, PLC session terminates, CP returns to standby state.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **mathematical backbone of CCS2 power-flow analysis**, frequently asked in numerical problems.

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{DC} = V_{batt} \times I_{DC}$ | Instantaneous DC charging power (kW) |
| 2 | $E_{delivered} = \int_{t_0}^{t_f} V_{batt}(t)\,I_{DC}(t)\,dt$ | Energy transferred during session |
| 3 | $R_{iso,min} = 100\,k\Omega / V \times V_{nom}$ | Minimum allowable insulation resistance (per IEC 61851-23) |
| 4 | $I_{cable,max} = \frac{P_{max}}{\eta_{cable} \times V_{batt,min}}$ | Maximum cable current during pre-charge |
| 5 | $\Delta V_{precharge} = V_{EVSE} - V_{pack} \leq 20\,V$ | Pre-charge voltage offset to limit inrush |
| 6 | $t_{charge} = \frac{C_{pack} \times (SoC_f - SoC_i)}{P_{avg} \times \eta_{chg}}$ | Time-to-charge estimate (with Coulombic efficiency $\eta_{chg}$) |
| 7 | $C_{rate} = \frac{I_{DC}}{C_{pack,Ah}}$ | Pack stress indicator (e.g., 2C, 3C fast-charging) |
| 8 | $P_{loss,cable} = I_{DC}^2 \times R_{cable}$ | I²R cable losses — quadratic with current |
| 9 | $\eta_{overall} = \frac{E_{DC,out}}{E_{AC,in}}$ | Station efficiency (rectifier + auxiliary) |
| 10 | $Q_{cool} = \dot{m} \times c_p \times \Delta T$ | Liquid-cooling heat removal from HPC cable |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** In numerical problems, you will almost always be asked to compute either $P_{DC}$, $t_{charge}$, or $R_{iso,min}$. Memorize the **three primary formulas** (1, 3, and 6) above; they cover ~80% of marks.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The CCS2 standard is not merely academic — it directly enables:
- **Highway corridor fast-charging networks** (e.g., IONITY, Tesla Supercharger v3 in EU, BP Pulse, Tata Power EZ Charge in India).
- **Fleet electrification** for last-mile delivery vans and buses.
- **V2G (Vehicle-to-Grid)** bidirectional pilots — CCS2 with ISO 15118-20 supports reverse power flow for grid stabilization.
- **Trucking electrification** — the **Megawatt Charging System (MCS)** is being developed as an extension of CCS2 principles for Class 8 trucks, targeting **1000 V / 3000 A** (≈ 3 MW).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Minimum Charging Time for a Given Pack and Charger

This is the **most common 7-mark numerical** in KTU Module 3 examinations.

**Problem Setup:** A BEV has a lithium-ion pack with the following parameters:
- Pack nominal capacity: $C_{pack} = 75\,kWh$
- Initial State of Charge: $SoC_i = 20\%$
- Target State of Charge: $SoC_f = 80\%$
- Available DC fast charger power: $P_{charger} = 150\,kW$
- Average pack voltage during charging: $V_{pack,avg} = 400\,V$
- Coulombic charging efficiency: $\eta_{chg} = 0.95$
- Cable and contactor losses: $\eta_{cable} = 0.98$
- Vehicle's BMS enforces a tapering charge profile: the charger delivers $P_{charger}$ only until $SoC = 60\%$, after which it linearly tapers to 50 kW at $SoC = 80\%$.

**Find:** Total charging time $t_{charge}$.

---

### Step 1 — Compute the energy that must be delivered to the battery cells

The *useful* energy required by the cells is:

$$
E_{cell} = C_{pack} \times (SoC_f - SoC_i)
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
E_{cell} = 75\,kWh \times (0.80 - 0.20)
$$

$$
E_{cell} = 75 \times 0.60 = 45\,kWh
$$

**Logic note:** This is the gross energy that the *battery* must absorb; losses in the cable, contactors, and conversion inefficiencies must be supplied *in addition* by the EVSE.

---

### Step 2 — Adjust for charging efficiency

To deliver $E_{cell}$ to the cells, the EVSE must supply $E_{cell} / \eta_{chg}$ (because $\eta_{chg} < 1$ accounts for heat loss in the electrochemical reaction — e.g., overpotential losses at the electrodes):

$$
E_{pack,delivered} = \frac{E_{cell}}{\eta_{chg}}
$$

$$
E_{pack,delivered} = \frac{45}{0.95} \approx 47.368\,kWh
$$

---

### Step 3 — Split the session into two phases

**Phase 1:** $SoC_i = 20\% \rightarrow SoC_a = 60\%$ at constant $P_{1} = 150\,kW$

The energy delivered during Phase 1:

$$
E_1 = C_{pack} \times (SoC_a - SoC_i) = 75 \times (0.60 - 0.20) = 30\,kWh
$$

Dividing by the power:

$$
t_1 = \frac{E_1}{P_1} = \frac{30}{150} = 0.20\,h = 12\,minutes
$$

**Phase 2:** $SoC = 60\% \rightarrow 80\%$ with linear tapering from $P_{1} = 150\,kW$ to $P_{2} = 50\,kW$.

The average power during Phase 2 is the arithmetic mean:

$$
P_{avg,2} = \frac{P_1 + P_2}{2} = \frac{150 + 50}{2} = 100\,kW
$$

The energy delivered during Phase 2:

$$
E_2 = 75 \times (0.80 - 0.60) = 15\,kWh
$$

The time for Phase 2:

$$
t_2 = \frac{E_2}{P_{avg,2}} = \frac{15}{100} = 0.15\,h = 9\,minutes
$$

---

### Step 4 — Sum the phases and adjust for cable losses

The total *gross* energy the EVSE must output is larger than $E_1 + E_2$ because of cable losses. The energy that the EVSE actually delivers (before losses):

$$
E_{EVSE} = \frac{E_1 + E_2}{\eta_{cable}}
$$

$$
E_{EVSE} = \frac{30 + 15}{0.98} = \frac{45}{0.98} \approx 45.918\,kWh
$$

But because the power profile is piecewise, we approximate the time directly by dividing each phase by its *effective* power after cable loss. For Phase 1:

$$
t_1^{eff} = \frac{E_1}{P_1 \times \eta_{cable}} = \frac{30}{150 \times 0.98} \approx 0.2041\,h \approx 12.24\,min
$$

For Phase 2:

$$
t_2^{eff} = \frac{E_2}{P_{avg,2} \times \eta_{cable}} = \frac{15}{100 \times 0.98} \approx 0.1531\,h \approx 9.18\,min
$$

---

### Step 5 — Total time

$$
t_{total} = t_1^{eff} + t_2^{eff}
$$

$$
t_{total} = 12.24 + 9.18 \approx 21.42\,minutes
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{t_{charge} \approx 21.4\,\text{minutes}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice how the **tapering profile** dramatically affects total time. If we *ignored* the taper and assumed a constant 150 kW throughout, we would compute $t = 45/150 = 0.30\,h = 18$ min — a **~15% underestimate** of real-world charging time. This is exactly why KTU examiners love this question.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Insulation Resistance Threshold Calculation

**Problem:** A CCS2 DC fast charger is rated for **920 V** nominal DC output. Per IEC 61851-23, what is the minimum acceptable insulation resistance $R_{iso}$ between the DC+ conductor and Protective Earth before the EVSE is permitted to energize the output?

**Step 1 — Recall the standard formula:**

$$
R_{iso,min} = 100\,k\Omega / V \times V_{nom}
$$

The factor $100\,k\Omega/V$ comes from the IEC 61851-23 requirement that the *leakage current* under worst-case insulation failure must not exceed a defined safety threshold.

**Step 2 — Convert units:**

$$
100\,k\Omega/V = 100{,}000\,\Omega / V
$$

$$
R_{iso,min} = 100{,}000 \times 920 = 92{,}000{,}000\,\Omega = 92\,M\Omega
$$

**Step 3 — Express final answer:**

$$
\boxed{R_{iso,min} = 92\,M\Omega}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification of physical meaning:**

If a human body (modeled as $\approx 2\,k\Omega$ wet skin) were to come into contact with the energized DC+ conductor while the insulation had degraded to exactly $92\,M\Omega$, the parallel combination of body + insulation resistance would limit the fault current to a value below the let-go threshold. This is the *safety margin* built into the standard.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — CCS2 vs Competing Standards

| Feature | **CCS2** | **CCS1** | **CHAdeMO** | **GB/T** | **Tesla NACS** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Region | EU, India, AU | North America | Japan, (legacy global) | China | North America (now opening) |
| AC Backward-Compatible | Yes (Type 2) | Yes (Type 1 / J1772) | No (separate AC inlet) | Yes (single inlet, dual protocol) | Yes (Type 2 derived) |
| Max DC Power | **350 kW** | 350 kW | 400 kW (CHAdeMO 2.0) | 250 kW (GB/T 2015+) | 250 kW (V3) |
| Max Voltage | **1000 V** | 600 V (extending to 1000) | 1000 V | 1000 V | 500 V (V3) → 1000 V (V4) |
| Max Current | **500 A** (liquid-cooled) | 500 A | 400 A | 250–600 A | 631 A |
| Communication | **ISO 15118 / PLC** | ISO 15118 / PLC | CAN bus | CAN bus | Proprietary → ISO 15118 |
| Plug & Charge | Yes (PnC native) | Yes | Limited | No | Yes |
| V2G Support | Yes (ISO 15118-20) | Yes | Yes (V2H/V2G pioneer) | Pilot | Limited |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** CCS2's strategic advantage is **regional lock-in combined with technical leadership**. The EU's mandate (AFIR) plus India's 2023 mandate (all highway chargers must be CCS2) gives it near-monopoly status in two of the world's largest EV markets.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — Cable Sizing Verification

**Problem:** A 350 kW HPC station delivers 920 V DC at the EVSE output. The liquid-cooled CCS2 cable has a one-way length of 3 m, with a copper cross-section of 95 mm². The temperature rise limit is 30 K above ambient, with a 4 Ω/km conductor loop resistance. Determine whether the cable can carry the full current continuously.

**Step 1 — Compute current at full power:**

$$
I = \frac{P}{V} = \frac{350{,}000}{920} \approx 380.4\,A
$$

**Step 2 — Compute loop resistance for 3 m cable:**

$$
R_{loop} = 4 \times 10^{-3}\,\Omega/m \times 3\,m \times 2\,(go\,and\,return) = 0.024\,\Omega
$$

**Step 3 — Compute I²R loss:**

$$
P_{loss} = I^2 \times R_{loop} = (380.4)^2 \times 0.024 \approx 3{,}473\,W
$$

**Step 4 — Compute heat to be removed by coolant:**

If the station's liquid cooling system removes this heat:

$$
\dot{Q} = P_{loss} \approx 3.47\,kW
$$

For a 50/50 water-glycol coolant at $\Delta T = 10\,K$:

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{\dot{Q}}{c_p \times \Delta T} = \frac{3473}{3800 \times 10} \approx 0.091\,kg/s \approx 5.5\,L/min
$$

This is comfortably within the practical capacity of a typical HPC cable cooling jacket (typically 8–12 L/min).

$$
\boxed{I_{continuous} \approx 380\,A < 500\,A_{rated},\quad \text{cable is adequate.}}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CCS2 Vehicle Inlet — Pin Layout (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CCS2_Inlet[CCS2 Vehicle Inlet - IEC 62196-3 Configuration FF]
        direction TB
        upperBlock[Upper AC Cluster - Type 2 Derived]
        lowerBlock[Lower DC Cluster - CCS2 Specific]

        subgraph AC_Cluster[AC Section]
            L1[L1 - Phase 1]
            L2[L2 - Phase 2]
            L3[L3 - Phase 3]
            N[N - Neutral]
            PE1[PE - Protective Earth]
        end

        subgraph DC_Cluster[DC Section]
            DCP[DC Plus - High Voltage Positive]
            DCM[DC Minus - High Voltage Negative]
        end

        subgraph Signal_Cluster[Signal and Safety Cluster]
            CP[CP - Control Pilot]
            PP[PP - Proximity Pilot]
            LOCK[Locking Actuator - 12V/9V/6V]
        end

        upperBlock --> AC_Cluster
        lowerBlock --> DC_Cluster
    end

    CP <--> PLC_Mod[PLC Modem - HomePlug Green PHY 1.3 to 30 MHz OFDM]
    PP --> PP_Decode[PP Resistor Decode - Cable Current Rating]
    LOCK <--> Latch_Drv[Latch Driver - Latched 9V Unlatched 12V]

    PLC_Mod --> ISO15118[ISO 15118 Stack - V2G Application Layer]
    PP_Decode --> BMS_Logic[BMS - Charge Session Init]
    Latch_Drv --> Safety[Vehicle Charge Controller]
    ISO15118 --> Safety
    BMS_Logic --> Safety
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The CCS2 inlet is a single physical port with two functional zones. The **upper AC cluster** is for slow/Level-2 charging (Type 2 plug). The **lower DC cluster** is the *only* part used during DC fast charging. The **signal cluster** (CP, PP, Lock) is shared between both modes and is what makes the "combined" feature possible.

## 4.2 CCS2 Charging Session — Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant U as User
    participant V as EV (BMS + VCU)
    participant E as EVSE (Charger)
    participant B as Battery Pack

    U->>V: Insert CCS2 plug
    V->>V: Read PP resistor (cable rating)
    V->>E: Apply 12V to lock actuator
    E->>V: Confirm lock with 9V signal
    V->>E: Wake CP line (begin SLAC)
    E->>V: SLAC exchange (PLC handshake)
    V->>E: SDP discovery, TCP/TLS setup
    V->>E: ISO 15118 auth (Plug & Charge or EIM)
    E->>V: CableCheck + Isolation test
    V->>E: Insulation R = 92 MΩ (pass)
    E->>V: Pre-charge to V_pack - 10V
    V->>V: Close HV contactors
    V->>B: Begin CurrentDemand loop
    B-->>V: SoC, V, T telemetry every 100ms
    V->>E: CurrentDemand (updated profile)
    E->>B: Deliver P_DC = V*I
    loop Every 100 ms
        E->>V: Voltage/Current present
        V->>E: Adjust request
    end
    B-->>V: SoC = 80% (target reached)
    V->>E: StopCurrentDemand
    E->>B: Ramp I to 0, open contactor
    V->>V: Open HV contactors
    E->>V: Unlock actuator
    U->>V: Disconnect plug
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Reading Strategy:** This sequence diagram is the **answer key** for any "describe the CCS2 charging process" question. Memorize the eight major milestones: **plug insertion → lock → SLAC → TLS → CableCheck → pre-charge → energy transfer → termination**.

## 4.3 CCS2 Power Electronics — Station-Side Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GRID[3-Phase AC Grid 11kV/22kV] --> XFMR[Step-Down Transformer]
    XFMR --> AFE[Active Front-End Rectifier]
    AFE --> DC_LINK[DC Link Capacitor Bank 800V DC]
    DC_LINK --> DC_DC[Isolated DC-DC Converter]
    DC_DC --> OUTPUT[CCS2 Output 200 to 920V DC]
    OUTPUT --> CABLE[Liquid-Cooled CCS2 Cable]
    CABLE --> VEHICLE[Vehicle Inlet]
    VEHICLE --> PACK[HV Battery Pack]

    CTRL[Station Controller - SECC] -. PLC over CP .-> VEHICLE
    AFE -. Gate signals .-> CTRL
    DC_DC -. Gate signals .-> CTRL
    COOL[Cooling System - Liquid Chiller] -. Coolant flow .-> CABLE
    ISO[Isolation Monitor] -. Insulation R .-> CTRL
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Modern CCS2 High-Power Charging (HPC) stations use a **two-stage** topology: an **Active Front-End (AFE)** converts grid AC to a stable intermediate DC link (typically ~800 V), and an **isolated DC-DC converter** steps this up or down to match the EV's pack voltage (which varies dynamically with SoC). This dual-stage design is what enables the wide output voltage range (200–920 V) without excessive component stress.

## 4.4 ISO 15118 Protocol Stack (Logical Layering)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    APP[Application Layer - V2G Messages - Charge Loop - AC/DC BID]

    TRANSPORT[Transport Layer - TCP over IPv6]

    SECURITY[Security Layer - TLS 1.2 or 1.3 - X.509 Certificates]

    NETWORK[Network Layer - IPv6 - DHCPv6 - SLAAC]

    PHY[Physical Layer - PLC HomePlug Green PHY - 1.3 to 30 MHz OFDM on CP/PE]

    APP --> TRANSPORT
    TRANSPORT --> SECURITY
    SECURITY --> NETWORK
    NETWORK --> PHY
    PHY -.-> CP_PIN[CCS2 CP Pin]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**
**Q:** What is meant by the term "Combined Charging System 2 (CCS2)"? Identify the international standard it complies with.

**Model Answer (≈ 80 words):**

CCS2 (Combined Charging System 2) is an internationally harmonized DC fast-charging standard for EVs defined by **IEC 62196-3 Configuration FF** and controlled by the **CharIN e.V.** consortium. It uses a single vehicle inlet that combines a Type 2 AC plug (for slow/Level-2 charging) with two additional high-current DC power pins (DC+ and DC−). Communication is governed by **ISO 15118** over Power Line Communication (PLC). It supports up to **350 kW** at 1000 V / 500 A.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
**Q:** Differentiate between the Control Pilot (CP) and Proximity Pilot (PP) pins in a CCS2 connector. What signals does each carry?

**Model Answer (≈ 80 words):**

The **PP (Proximity Pilot)** is an analog safety interlock that uses a resistor divider to indicate (a) that a connector is physically inserted and (b) the current-carrying capacity of the attached cable. The **CP (Control Pilot)** is the *communication* line: in AC mode it carries a **±12 V PWM signal** encoding current limit (duty cycle = current), while in DC mode it carries **PLC (HomePlug Green PHY)** signals for ISO 15118 digital handshaking. PP is purely passive/analog; CP carries the intelligent data.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option I
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a CCS2 charging station and the various components involved in DC fast charging. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A BEV has a 75 kWh battery. It is charged from 20% to 80% SoC using a CCS2 DC fast charger rated at 150 kW. The average battery voltage during the session is 400 V. The charger delivers full 150 kW up to 60% SoC and then linearly tapers to 50 kW at 80% SoC. Assuming 95% Coulombic efficiency and 98% cable efficiency, calculate the total charging time. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Block Diagram Explanation:**

Refer to Section 4.3 (CCS2 Power Electronics — Station-Side Architecture). Components are:

1. **Step-down transformer** — adapts grid voltage (11/22 kV) to station bus voltage.
2. **Active Front-End (AFE) rectifier** — IGBT-based, provides bidirectional grid interface, harmonic mitigation (THD < 5%), and unity power factor.
3. **DC link capacitor bank** — energy buffer at intermediate DC bus (~800 V), decouples grid and EV sides.
4. **Isolated DC-DC converter** — typically a DAB (Dual Active Bridge) or resonant LLC; provides galvanic isolation and wide output voltage range (200–920 V).
5. **SECC (Supply Equipment Communication Controller)** — runs the ISO 15118 stack; manages PLC modem over CP.
6. **Cooling system** — liquid chiller + pump + heat exchanger; circulates coolant through cable and IGBT cold plates.
7. **Isolation monitor** — continuously measures $R_{iso}$ during charging; triggers emergency shutdown if below threshold.
8. **Safety contactors** — high-speed DC contactors; pre-charge circuit.
9. **HMI** — display, RFID reader, payment terminal.
10. **CCS2 dispenser** — liquid-cooled cable, locking actuator.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

This is identical to Derivation A in Section 3.1. Recap of key steps:

**Step 1 — Energy required by cells:**

$$
E_{cell} = 75 \times (0.80 - 0.20) = 45\,kWh
$$

**Step 2 — Phase 1 (20% → 60% at 150 kW):**

$$
E_1 = 75 \times 0.40 = 30\,kWh
$$

$$
t_1 = \frac{30}{150 \times 0.98 \times 0.95} \approx \frac{30}{139.65} \approx 0.2148\,h \approx 12.89\,min
$$

> *Logic:* Combined efficiency of cable × Coulombic = $0.98 \times 0.95 = 0.931$.

**Step 3 — Phase 2 (60% → 80% with linear taper from 150 kW to 50 kW):**

$$
E_2 = 75 \times 0.20 = 15\,kWh
$$

$$
P_{avg,2} = \frac{150 + 50}{2} = 100\,kW
$$

$$
t_2 = \frac{15}{100 \times 0.931} = \frac{15}{93.1} \approx 0.1611\,h \approx 9.67\,min
$$

**Step 4 — Total time:**

$$
t_{total} = t_1 + t_2 \approx 12.89 + 9.67 = 22.56\,\text{minutes}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{charge} \approx 22.6\,\text{minutes}}
$$

---

### Question B — Option II (Internal Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
**(a)** Explain the complete CCS2 charging session handshake — from physical plug insertion to energy transfer — highlighting the role of PP, CP, and ISO 15118 at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CCS2 DC fast charger is rated for 920 V DC output. Per IEC 61851-23, the minimum acceptable insulation resistance between the DC+ conductor and protective earth is $100\,k\Omega/V$. If the charger's insulation monitor reads $80\,M\Omega$ during pre-charge verification, determine whether the EVSE is permitted to energize the output, and justify your answer. If not, what action should the station take? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Charging Session Handshake:**

(Follow the eight-step flow from Section 2.3.)

1. **Physical insertion & PP detection** — Vehicle reads PP resistor; determines cable current rating.
2. **Locking** — Vehicle applies 12 V; station confirms latch with 9 V.
3. **CP handshake** — Begins with ±12 V digital; transitions to PLC.
4. **ISO 15118 SLAC + TLS** — Authentication (Plug & Charge via X.509 cert or EIM via RFID).
5. **CableCheck & insulation test** — Verifies $R_{iso}$ per IEC standard.
6. **Pre-charge** — Output rises to $V_{pack} - 10\,V$.
7. **Energy transfer** — CurrentDemand loop running at 100 ms cadence.
8. **Termination** — Ramp-down, contactor open, lock release.

**(b) Insulation Resistance Numerical:**

**Step 1 — Compute threshold:**

$$
R_{iso,min} = 100{,}000\,\Omega/V \times 920\,V = 92{,}000{,}000\,\Omega = 92\,M\Omega
$$

**Step 2 — Compare with measured value:**

$$
R_{iso,measured} = 80\,M\Omega < 92\,M\Omega = R_{iso,min}
$$

**Step 3 — Decision:**

$$
\boxed{\text{The EVSE is NOT permitted to energize the output.}}
$$

**Step 4 — Required action:**

The station must:
- **Abort the charging session immediately.**
- Display a fault message on the HMI ("Insulation Fault — Service Required").
- **Lock out further charging attempts** until a qualified technician performs a full dielectric withstand (hipot) test on the station.
- Log the fault in the maintenance database for predictive maintenance analysis.
- Notify the operator via the backend OCPP (Open Charge Point Protocol) telemetry.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Many students confuse CP and PP roles.** PP is the *safety interlock* (analog resistor); CP is the *communication channel* (PWM or PLC). Losing 1 mark if mixed up.
> 2. **In numerical problems, students often forget the cable efficiency $\eta_{cable}$** or the Coulombic efficiency $\eta_{chg}$, and arrive at $t = 18$ min instead of the correct ~22.6 min. Always apply both efficiencies *as a product* to the power.
> 3. **In the insulation problem, students forget to convert** $100\,k\Omega/V$ to $\Omega/V$ before multiplying. $100\,k\Omega/V$ means 100,000 Ω per volt of nominal voltage. This is a frequent 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Do not write "ISO 15118 controls the contactor directly."** ISO 15118 is a *communication protocol*; the actual contactor is closed by the EV's BMS based on parameters negotiated via ISO 15118. Be precise.
> 5. **Tapering profiles:** When a question mentions "linear taper from $P_1$ to $P_2$," students must use the **arithmetic mean** of $P_1$ and $P_2$ as the *average* power during that segment — not the geometric mean or the higher value.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — CCS2 (Module 3)**

- ☐ **CCS2 = Combo 2 = IEC 62196-3 Configuration FF** (NOT to be confused with CCS1 = Configuration EE, used in North America).
- ☐ **Single inlet, dual-mode** — upper Type 2 AC pins + lower DC+/DC− pins.
- ☐ **Max practical power: 350 kW** at up to 1000 V / 500 A (liquid-cooled cable mandatory above 200 A continuous).
- ☐ **Communication: ISO 15118** over **PLC (HomePlug Green PHY, OFDM 1.3–30 MHz)** on the **Control Pilot (CP)** pin.
- ☐ **PP (Proximity Pilot) ≠ CP (Control Pilot)** — PP = analog safety + cable rating; CP = data channel.
- ☐ **Three flagship features of ISO 15118:** (1) Plug & Charge (PnC) auto-authentication, (2) Bidirectional V2G, (3) Smart-charging tariff negotiation.
- ☐ **Pre-charge rule:** EVSE must bring DC+ to within **≤ 20 V** of the EV pack voltage before closing vehicle contactors, to prevent arcing.
- ☐ **Insulation test threshold:** $R_{iso,min} = 100\,k\Omega/V \times V_{nom}$ — commit this formula to memory.
- ☐ **Two-stage station architecture:** AFE rectifier (AC→DC) + isolated DC-DC (DC bus → EV pack voltage).
- ☐ **Charging session has 8 milestones:** Insertion → PP → Lock → SLAC/TLS → CableCheck → Pre-charge → Transfer → Terminate.
- ☐ **Tapering charge profile** — pack voltage and BMS C-rate limits cause real chargers to taper power above ~60–80% SoC; never assume constant $P$ over the full session.
- ☐ **Global relevance for KTU students:** India mandated CCS2 for all highway fast chargers in 2023 (Ministry of Heavy Industries notification); every KTU automotive placement interview is likely to test this.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
