topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT631 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** GB/T

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is GB/T? — Formal Definition

**GB/T** stands for **Guo Biao / T** (Chinese National Standards — Recommended/Voluntary). In the context of **Electric and Hybrid Vehicles (EHVs)**, GB/T refers to a comprehensive family of Chinese national standards governing **electric vehicle conductive charging systems, charging connectors, communication protocols, and interoperability** between Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs), Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicles (PHEVs) and the Electric Vehicle Supply Equipment (EVSE).

The most relevant GB/T sub-standards for EV charging are:

| GB/T Code | Title (English) | Scope |
|---|---|---|
| **GB/T 18487.1** | Conductive charging systems for electric vehicles — Part 1: General requirements | System-level architecture, charging modes (Mode 2/3/4), safety |
| **GB/T 20234.1** | Connection set for conductive charging — Part 1: General requirements | Connector general specs |
| **GB/T 20234.2** | Connection set — Part 2: AC charging coupler | AC charging plug design |
| **GB/T 20234.3** | Connection set — Part 3: DC charging coupler | DC fast-charging plug design |
| **GB/T 27930** | Communication protocol between off-board charger and BMS | CAN-based handshake for DC charging |
| **GB/T 34657** | Interoperability test specifications for EV charging | Plug-and-charge conformance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** GB/T is one of **four major international EV charging standards** a student must know — the others being **SAE J1772 / CCS1 (USA)**, **IEC 62196 / Type 2 (Europe)**, and **CHAdeMO (Japan)**. For EHV engineers, GB/T is non-negotiable because China is the **world's largest EV market (>60% global share in 2024)** and Indian OEMs exporting to China must comply.

---

## 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy — "Why GB/T Exists"

Imagine you travel from India to Japan with your phone charger. You find the plug does not fit the wall socket — that's because every region developed its own physical/electrical convention. **The exact same problem occurs with EVs**, except the stakes are much higher: instead of 5 V at 2 A, an EV battery may demand **500 V at 250 A** (≈ 125 kW) of DC power. A mismatch can cause arcing, fire, or electrocution.

**GB/T is China's "universal language" for that high-power socket.** It tells the car *exactly*:
1. What shape the plug should be (mechanical),
2. How much voltage/current the pins may carry (electrical),
3. How the car and the charger should "talk" before current starts flowing (communication protocol).

Think of GB/T 20234 as the **physical plug**, GB/T 18487 as the **rules of the conversation**, and GB/T 27930 as the **language (grammar) of that conversation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Hook (2024 Context):** India's *Bharat DC-001* and *Bharat AC-001* chargers were partly inspired by GB/T architecture, with modifications for Indian grid conditions. The **IS 17017** series (BIS) is India's equivalent, modeled on IEC 62196 but interoperable considerations with GB/T remain for export-ready EVs.

---

## 1.3 GB/T Connector Physical Layout — Quick Visual Map

The **GB/T 20234.2 (AC)** connector has **7 pins**, while **GB/T 20234.3 (DC)** connector has **9 pins** (additional 2 pins for DC+ and DC−). 

**GB/T 20234.3 DC Coupler Pin Layout:**

| Pin # | Designation | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | DC+ | High-voltage DC positive to battery |
| 2 | DC− | High-voltage DC negative (return) |
| 3 | PE | Protective Earth (chassis ground) |
| 4 | S+ | CAN-H communication (between EV and EVSE) |
| 5 | S− | CAN-L communication |
| 6 | CC1 | Connection Confirmation (proximity detection) |
| 7 | CC2 | Charging Confirmation (latch/lock state) |
| 8 | A+ | Low-voltage auxiliary power (+12 V) |
| 9 | A− | Auxiliary low-voltage ground |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unique GB/T Feature:** Unlike SAE J1772 or IEC Type 2, GB/T 20234.3 uses **two separate confirmation pins (CC1 and CC2)** — one to detect plug insertion, the other to confirm the latch is locked. This **dual-lock safety** is a hallmark of GB/T and prevents "drive-away while plugged-in" accidents.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The GB/T Charging Architecture — Four Logical Layers

GB/T 18487.1 partitions the EV charging ecosystem into **four logical layers**. Understanding these layers is mandatory for KTU questions on "Compare GB/T with SAE J1772" or "Explain GB/T charging modes."

### Layer 1 — Physical Coupling (GB/T 20234.1 / .2 / .3)
Defines connector geometry, pin diameter, creepage distance, mating cycles, IP rating (≥ IP54 when mated), and locking mechanism.

### Layer 2 — System Modes (GB/T 18487.1)
Four charging modes are defined:
- **Mode 1** — Slow AC, no communication (deprecated for EVs).
- **Mode 2** — Slow AC through an in-cable **IC-CPD** (In-Cable Control and Protective Device).
- **Mode 3** — Dedicated AC EVSE with **PWM pilot** control signal.
- **Mode 4** — Off-board DC charger (the fast-charging mode).

### Layer 3 — Communication Protocol (GB/T 27930)
For DC charging, the **off-board charger** and the **BMS** communicate via **CAN 2.0B at 250 kbps**. The protocol is **master-slave**, where the **charger is the master** and the **BMS is the slave** — *opposite* to CCS where the BMS initiates.

### Layer 4 — Interoperability & Conformance (GB/T 34657)
Defines test cases for plug-and-charge, fault tolerance, and electromagnetic compatibility.

---

## 2.2 GB/T Charging Sequence — Step-by-Step Logical Flow

The GB/T 27930 DC charging handshake is a **four-phase state machine**:

1. **Phase 1 — Handshaking (Initialisation):**
   - Charger closes contactors (no current yet).
   - Charger and BMS exchange parameter versions via CAN.
   - Maximum charging current requested by BMS.

2. **Phase 2 — Parameter Configuration:**
   - BMS sends full battery parameters: **SOC, SOH, cell voltages, temperature, total voltage**.
   - Charger replies with its capability envelope: max voltage, max current, isolation status.

3. **Phase 3 — Charging (Bulk phase):**
   - Charger regulates output current based on BMS demand.
   - BMS continuously sends **battery status (BCS)** messages every 250 ms.
   - Charging terminates when SOC ≥ target OR BMS issues stop command.

4. **Phase 4 — End of Charging (Statistics):**
   - Charger ramps current to 0, opens contactors.
   - Final energy accounting messages exchanged, then **CAN bus goes to sleep**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Charger is Master in GB/T:** The original GB/T 27930 design philosophy treats the **charger as the "intelligent supervisor"** and the **BMS as a "reporting device"**. This differs from CCS/ISO 15118 where the BMS uses **SLAC (Signal Level Attenuation Characterization)** to detect the charger and then initiates. KTU exam tip: this is a frequently tested *compare-and-contrast* point.

---

## 2.3 GB/T Pilot Signal — PWM Duty-Cycle Encoding

GB/T Mode 3 AC charging uses a **±12 V PWM pilot signal** to encode available current on a single control wire. The relationship is:

$$ I_{\text{available}} = \frac{D}{100} \times I_{\text{max, EVSE}} $$

where $D$ is the **duty cycle (%)** of the pilot waveform.

**GB/T Duty-Cycle ↔ Current Mapping Table (Key Exam Values):**

| Pilot Duty Cycle $D$ | Available Current (A) | EVSE Pilot State |
|---|---|---|
| 10 % ≤ D < 85 % | $D \times 0.6$ | Ready (Standby) |
| 85 % ≤ D ≤ 90 % | $D \times 2.5$ | Charging enabled |
| 90 % < D ≤ 97 % | $D \times 0.6$ (residual) | Fault / Limited |
| D < 3 % or D > 97 % | 0 (Invalid) | Disconnect / Error |

For a 32 A GB/T AC EVSE, a pilot duty cycle of **88 %** would indicate:

$$ I_{\text{available}} = 0.88 \times 2.5 = 2.20 \text{ A (Fault indication)} $$

For the same EVSE with $D = 86\%$:

$$ I_{\text{available}} = 0.86 \times 2.5 = 2.15 \text{ A (Fault)} $$

This shows the pilot is operating in the **fault band** — charger electronics would halt current flow.

For a **healthy 63 A GB/T AC EVSE** with pilot at $D = 80\%$:

$$ I_{\text{available}} = 0.80 \times 0.6 = 0.48 \text{ A} $$

> [!CAUTION]
> **Common Student Mistake (KTU):** Many students mis-apply the SAE J1772 formula $I = D \times 0.6$ for *all* duty cycles. GB/T explicitly defines **three distinct piecewise regions**. Always read the duty cycle first.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — GB/T

> The following table is the **single most important reference** for GB/T numerical problems in KTU ESE.

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Legend | Units | Boundary / Validity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_{\text{avail}} = \dfrac{D}{100} \times 0.6$ | $D$ = duty cycle | A | 10 % ≤ $D$ < 85 % |
| 2 | $I_{\text{avail}} = \dfrac{D}{100} \times 2.5$ | $I_{\text{avail}}$ = available | A | 85 % ≤ $D$ ≤ 90 % |
| 3 | $P_{\text{ch}} = V_{\text{batt}} \times I_{\text{ch}} \times \eta_{\text{ch}}$ | $\eta_{\text{ch}}$ = charger efficiency (≈ 0.93–0.96) | kW | DC charging |
| 4 | $t_{\text{ch}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{bat}}}{P_{\text{ch}}}$ | $E_{\text{bat}}$ = battery energy | h | Constant-power approx. |
| 5 | $E_{\text{bat}} = Q_{\text{bat}} \times V_{\text{nom}}$ | $Q$ = Ah capacity | kWh | Nominal regime |
| 6 | $V_{\text{out,max}} = V_{\text{batt,OCV}} + I_{\text{ch}} \times R_{\text{int}}$ | $R_{\text{int}}$ = internal resistance | V | Steady state |
| 7 | $R_{\text{isol}} > 100 \,\Omega/\text{V}$ | Isolation resistance per volt | $\Omega$/V | Safety — IEC 61851 mirrored |
| 8 | $T_{\text{CAN}} = 4 \times t_{\text{bit}}$ | CAN frame timing (GB/T 27930) | s | 250 kbps → $t_{\text{bit}}=4\,\mu\text{s}$ |
| 9 | $f_{\text{PWM}} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{PWM}}}$ | GB/T pilot PWM frequency | Hz | $f_{\text{PWM}} = 1000 \pm 20$ Hz |
| 10 | $I_{\text{max,pilot}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{pilot}}}{R_{\text{pilot}}}$ | Pilot pull-up: $V = +12$ V, $R = 1000\,\Omega$ typical | mA | Mode 3 |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

GB/T standards are not academic — they directly impact:
1. **EV exports to China** — Mandatory for compliance since 2016.
2. **Charging infrastructure rollout** — India's Tata Power, Ather, and BPCL EV chargers reference GB/T in their reference designs.
3. **Battery Management System (BMS) firmware** — Every BMS in a Chinese-spec EV must implement the GB/T 27930 CAN stack.
4. **Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G)** — GB/T 18487.1 Annex B defines V2G reverse power flow, making it a V2G reference standard.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Problem: GB/T Pilot Signal → Current Resolution

> **KTU-Style Worked Example (Module 3 Numerical):**
> A GB/T Mode 3 AC EVSE has a maximum rated output of 32 A single-phase. The onboard charger of a connected EV samples the pilot PWM waveform and measures a duty cycle of **30 %**. Determine:
> (a) The current the EV may safely draw.
> (b) The active power drawn if the EV battery is at 220 V nominal.
> (c) The charging time for a **40 kWh** battery (assume 100 % SOC restoration at constant power and η = 0.95).

### Given Data
- EVSE max current $I_{\text{max,EVSE}} = 32$ A
- Pilot duty cycle $D = 30\%$
- Battery nominal voltage $V_{\text{batt}} = 220$ V
- Battery capacity $E_{\text{bat}} = 40$ kWh
- Charger efficiency $\eta_{\text{ch}} = 0.95$

### Solution

**Step 1 — Identify which piecewise region $D$ falls in.**

$$ D = 30\% \quad \Rightarrow \quad 10\% \leq 30\% < 85\% $$

So we use **Region 1** of the GB/T pilot encoding:

$$ I_{\text{avail}} = \frac{D}{100} \times 0.6 $$

**Step 2 — Calculate the available current.**

$$ I_{\text{avail}} = \frac{30}{100} \times 0.6 = 0.30 \times 0.6 = 0.18 \text{ A} $$

This is *extremely* low — the EV would essentially be **idle** (≈ 0.18 A). This indicates the **EVSE is signalling a fault or limited state**, not a normal charging offer.

**Step 3 — Cross-check with Region 2 if misinterpreted.**

If a student misreads the table and applies Region 2:

$$ I_{\text{avail (wrong)}} = \frac{30}{100} \times 2.5 = 0.75 \text{ A} $$

This would also be unreasonably low for a 32 A EVSE → both interpretations lead the student to conclude a **fault condition** exists.

**Step 4 — Active power drawn by the EV battery.**

$$ P_{\text{ch, batt}} = V_{\text{batt}} \times I_{\text{avail}} \times \eta_{\text{ch}} $$

$$ P_{\text{ch, batt}} = 220 \times 0.18 \times 0.95 $$

$$ P_{\text{ch, batt}} = 37.62 \text{ W} $$

**Step 5 — Charging time to refill 40 kWh (theoretical).**

$$ t_{\text{ch}} = \frac{E_{\text{bat}}}{P_{\text{ch,batt}}} = \frac{40{,}000 \text{ Wh}}{37.62 \text{ W}} $$

$$ t_{\text{ch}} = 1063.26 \text{ h} $$

This result confirms a **non-viable charging condition** — the EVSE is in a fault state and the driver must disconnect and reattempt.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Problem: GB/T 27930 Communication Timing

> **KTU-Style Worked Example (CAN Bus Timing):**
> During GB/T 27930 DC charging, the BMS transmits a **Battery Charging Status (BCS)** message every **250 ms** with a standard 11-bit CAN ID. The CAN bus operates at **250 kbps**. The BCS payload is **8 bytes**. Calculate:
> (a) The number of BCS messages transmitted in **1 hour**.
> (b) The **bus utilization** (in %) if only BCS messages are present.

### Given Data
- Transmission period $T_{\text{BCS}} = 250$ ms
- Bus bit-rate $R_{\text{CAN}} = 250$ kbps = $250{,}000$ bits/s
- CAN ID length = 11 bits
- Standard CAN frame overhead = 47 bits (SOF + RTR + Control + CRC + ACK + EOF + Inter-frame space)
- Payload = 8 bytes = 64 bits

### Solution

**Step 1 — Total bits in one BCS frame.**

$$ L_{\text{frame}} = L_{\text{overhead}} + L_{\text{ID}} + L_{\text{payload}} $$

$$ L_{\text{frame}} = 47 + 11 + 64 = 122 \text{ bits} $$

**Step 2 — Transmission time for one frame.**

$$ t_{\text{frame}} = \frac{L_{\text{frame}}}{R_{\text{CAN}}} = \frac{122}{250{,}000} $$

$$ t_{\text{frame}} = 4.88 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 488 \, \mu\text{s} $$

**Step 3 — Number of BCS messages per hour.**

$$ N_{\text{BCS}} = \frac{3600}{T_{\text{BCS}}} = \frac{3600}{0.250} $$

$$ N_{\text{BCS}} = 14{,}400 \text{ messages/h} $$

**Step 4 — Total time spent transmitting per hour.**

$$ T_{\text{total,h}} = N_{\text{BCS}} \times t_{\text{frame}} = 14{,}400 \times 4.88 \times 10^{-4} $$

$$ T_{\text{total,h}} = 7.027 \text{ s} $$

**Step 5 — Bus utilization.**

$$ U_{\text{bus}} = \frac{T_{\text{total,h}}}{3600} \times 100\% = \frac{7.027}{3600} \times 100\% $$

$$ U_{\text{bus}} \approx 0.195\% $$

This very low utilization confirms the GB/T 27930 protocol is **extremely bandwidth-efficient**, leaving room for **multiple BMS parameter messages** (BCL, BRM, BCP, etc.) to coexist on the same bus.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison: GB/T vs. CCS vs. CHAdeMO

This matrix is the **most exam-relevant analytical table** for any "Compare EV charging standards" KTU question.

| Parameter | **GB/T (China)** | **CCS1 / CCS2 (EU-US)** | **CHAdeMO (Japan)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Governing Body | SAC (Standardization Administration of China) | SAE / IEC | TEPCO / CHAdeMO Association |
| AC Connector | GB/T 20234.2 (7-pin) | IEC 62196 Type 1 / Type 2 | — (separate AC inlet) |
| DC Connector | GB/T 20234.3 (9-pin) | Combined AC + DC (Combo 1/2) | Dedicated CHAdeMO inlet |
| Max DC Power | 250 kW (current); 900 kW planned | 350 kW (planned) | 400 kW (CHAdeMO 3.0) |
| Communication Protocol | GB/T 27930 (CAN 2.0B, 250 kbps) | ISO 15118 (PLC over CP) | CHAdeMO (CAN 2.0B) |
| Master-Slave (DC) | Charger = Master, BMS = Slave | EV = Master, Charger = Slave | EV = Master, Charger = Slave |
| Pilot Signal | ±12 V PWM (Mode 3 AC) | PWM (AC) + PLC (DC) | Dedicated CAN bus |
| V2G Support | Yes (Annex B of GB/T 18487.1) | Yes (ISO 15118-20) | Yes (CHAdeMO 2.0+) |
| Geographic Scope | China, parts of South-East Asia | North America, Europe | Japan, parts of EU |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 GB/T 27930 DC Charging Communication — Block Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **end-to-end communication state machine** of GB/T 27930 between the off-board charger and the EV BMS.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([EV Plug Inserted into EVSE])
    startA --> initDetect{CC1 and CC2\nboth LOW?}
    initDetect -- No --> waitLatch[Wait for Latch Confirmation]
    waitLatch --> initDetect
    initDetect -- Yes --> handshake[Phase 1: Handshake\nCharger sends CHM\nBMS replies BHM]
    handshake --> p2start[Phase 2: Parameter Configuration\nBMS sends BRM, BCP, BRO\nCharger sends CRM, CRO]
    p2start --> p3start[Phase 3: Bulk Charging\nCharger sends CCS\nBMS replies BCS every 250 ms]
    p3start --> termCheck{Target SOC reached\nOR Battery Temp > limit?}
    termCheck -- No --> p3start
    termCheck -- Yes --> p4start[Phase 4: End of Charging\nCharger sends CSD\nBMS replies BSD]
    p4start --> contactorOpen[Open DC Contactors\nRamp Current to Zero]
    contactorOpen --> canSleep[CAN Bus Sleep\nSession Ended]
    canSleep --> doneA([Charging Complete])

    style startA fill:#f9f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style doneA fill:#9f9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style handshake fill:#bbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style p3start fill:#ff9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style contactorOpen fill:#fbb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.2 GB/T Connector Pin Architecture — Functional Block Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GBTEVSE[GB/T 20234.3 DC Coupler - 9 Pins]
        P1[Pin 1: DC+]
        P2[Pin 2: DC-]
        P3[Pin 3: PE]
        P6[Pin 6: CC1\nProximity]
        P7[Pin 7: CC2\nLatch State]
        P4[Pin 4: S+ CAN-H]
        P5[Pin 5: S- CAN-L]
        P8[Pin 8: A+ 12V Aux]
        P9[Pin 9: A- Aux GND]
    end

    subgraph PowerFlow[High-Voltage Power Path]
        BATT[Battery Pack]
        DCPOS[DC+ Bus]
        DCNEG[DC- Bus]
    end

    subgraph CommFlow[Communication Path]
        CHARGER[Off-board Charger]
        BMS[BMS in EV]
    end

    subgraph SafetyFlow[Safety Detection Path]
        PILOT[Pilot Detector Circuit]
        LOCK[Latch Motor Actuator]
    end

    P1 --> DCPOS
    P2 --> DCNEG
    P3 --> DCPOS
    DCPOS --> BATT
    DCNEG --> BATT

    P4 --> CHARGER
    P5 --> BMS
    P6 --> PILOT
    P7 --> LOCK

    P8 --> BMS
    P9 --> BMS

    style BATT fill:#cfc,stroke:#060,stroke-width:2px
    style CHARGER fill:#ccf,stroke:#006,stroke-width:1px
    style BMS fill:#ccf,stroke:#006,stroke-width:1px
    style PILOT fill:#fbb,stroke:#a00,stroke-width:1px
    style LOCK fill:#fbb,stroke:#a00,stroke-width:1px
```

---

## 4.3 GB/T vs. SAE J1772 — Pilot Signal Reference Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pilotStart([Pilot Oscillator in EVSE])
    pilotStart --> pwmOut[Generate 1 kHz, +/-12V PWM]
    pwmOut --> dutySense[EV samples Duty Cycle D]
    dutySense --> regionCheck{D range?}
    regionCheck -- D 10 to 85% --> regionReady[Region 1: Standby\nI_avail = D x 0.6]
    regionCheck -- D 85 to 90% --> regionCh[Region 2: Charging\nI_avail = D x 2.5]
    regionCheck -- D 90 to 97% --> regionFault[Region 3: Fault\nI_avail = D x 0.6]
    regionCheck -- D less 3% OR greater 97% --> regionErr[Invalid Pilot\nEV Disables Charging]
    regionReady --> cmdOut
    regionCh --> cmdOut[Report to Onboard Charger]
    regionFault --> cmdOut
    regionErr --> cmdOut

    style pilotStart fill:#9cf,stroke:#003,stroke-width:2px
    style regionErr fill:#f99,stroke:#900,stroke-width:2px
    style cmdOut fill:#cfc,stroke:#060,stroke-width:2px
```

---

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **Model Question Paper Pattern:** Two questions of 3 marks each, answer in **80–120 words**, no diagrams mandatory but a small sketch earns bonus clarity.

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)]**
**Differentiate between GB/T 18487.1 and GB/T 27930. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
GB/T 18487.1 specifies the **general requirements for conductive EV charging systems**, defining four charging modes (1–4), system architectures, and safety/isolation limits. It is a *system-level* standard. GB/T 27930, in contrast, defines the **CAN-based communication protocol** specifically used *between an off-board DC charger and the EV's BMS during Mode 4 DC charging*. While 18487 is a *hardware/system* standard, 27930 is a *firmware/communication* standard. Both are mandatory under Chinese GB/T specifications but address different layers of the charging stack.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**
**Explain the role of the CC1 and CC2 pins in a GB/T 20234.3 DC connector. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The GB/T 20234.3 DC connector uses **two separate confirmation contacts**:
- **CC1 (Connection Confirmation 1):** Detects *physical proximity* of the plug — confirms the connector has been inserted into the vehicle inlet.
- **CC2 (Connection Confirmation 2):** Confirms *mechanical latch engagement* — verifies the connector locking mechanism is fully secured.

This dual-pin architecture provides **redundant safety verification** before the charger is permitted to close its high-voltage contactors. If either CC1 or CC2 reports an abnormal state, the EVSE will refuse to energise, preventing *arcing, drive-away-while-plugged, and partial-mating* hazards. This is **unique to GB/T** and not present in CCS or CHAdeMO connectors.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Question (14 Marks)

> **As per KTU 2024 ESE Module Choice pattern, students must attempt ONE of the two questions provided below. Each question has sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.**

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **four-phase GB/T 27930 communication sequence** between an off-board DC fast charger and the EV's Battery Management System (BMS). List the messages exchanged in each phase. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A GB/T 20234.3 DC fast charger is delivering **500 V at 200 A** to a 100 kWh EV battery. The charger efficiency is **96 %**. Compute:
   (i) The active power delivered to the battery.
   (ii) The charging time required to refill the battery from 20 % to 80 % SOC.
   (iii) The energy lost as heat during the charging session.
   **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) GB/T 27930 — Four-Phase Charging Sequence**

The GB/T 27930 protocol organizes DC charging into **four sequential phases**, executed over a **CAN 2.0B bus at 250 kbps**, with the **charger acting as the master** and the **BMS acting as the slave**.

**Phase 1 — Handshaking:**
After the connector is latched and CC1 + CC2 are confirmed, both nodes initialize. The charger broadcasts a **CHM (Charger Handshake Message)** and waits for the BMS's **BHM (Battery Handshake Message)**. No high voltage is applied yet.

**Phase 2 — Parameter Configuration:**
The BMS broadcasts **BRM (Battery Parameters), BCP (Battery Charging Parameters), BRO (Battery Ready Output)**. The charger responds with **CRM (Charger Recognition/Ready Message) and CRO (Charger Output Parameters)**. The BMS declares maximum permissible voltage/current.

**Phase 3 — Charging:**
The charger activates contactors and applies the requested current. Every 250 ms, BMS sends **BCS (Battery Charging Status)** and **BCL (Battery Charging Limits)** messages. The charger sends **CCS (Charger Charging Status)** and **CST (Charger Status)**.

**Phase 4 — Termination:**
Once target SOC is reached, BMS sends **BSD (Battery Status Data)**. Charger sends **CSD (Charger Status Data)** with stop command. Contactors open, current ramps to zero, CAN bus sleeps.

*(Block diagram already presented in Section 4.1.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution**

**Given:** $V_{\text{ch,out}} = 500$ V, $I_{\text{ch,out}} = 200$ A, $\eta_{\text{ch}} = 0.96$, $E_{\text{bat}} = 100$ kWh, $\Delta \text{SOC} = 80\% - 20\% = 60\%$.

**(i) Active power delivered to the battery:**

$$ P_{\text{DC,out}} = V \times I = 500 \times 200 = 100{,}000 \text{ W} = 100 \text{ kW} $$

Power reaching battery (after losses):

$$ P_{\text{batt}} = P_{\text{DC,out}} \times \eta_{\text{ch}} $$

$$ P_{\text{batt}} = 100 \times 0.96 = 96 \text{ kW} $$

Wait — re-reading the question: *"active power delivered to the battery"* refers to the **output power of the charger after efficiency**:

$$ \boxed{P_{\text{batt}} = 96 \text{ kW}} $$

**(ii) Charging time for 60 % SOC refill:**

Energy to be refilled:

$$ E_{\text{needed}} = 100 \text{ kWh} \times 0.60 = 60 \text{ kWh} $$

Time (assuming constant 96 kW draw):

$$ t_{\text{ch}} = \frac{E_{\text{needed}}}{P_{\text{batt}}} = \frac{60}{96} $$

$$ \boxed{t_{\text{ch}} = 0.625 \text{ h} = 37.5 \text{ minutes}} $$

**(iii) Energy lost as heat:**

Energy drawn from the grid:

$$ E_{\text{grid}} = \frac{E_{\text{needed}}}{\eta_{\text{ch}}} = \frac{60}{0.96} = 62.5 \text{ kWh} $$

Energy lost as heat:

$$ E_{\text{loss}} = E_{\text{grid}} - E_{\text{needed}} = 62.5 - 60 $$

$$ \boxed{E_{\text{loss}} = 2.5 \text{ kWh}} $$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**

**(a)** Compare the **GB/T (Chinese)**, **CCS (Western)**, and **CHAdeMO (Japanese)** DC fast-charging standards in terms of connector architecture, communication protocol, master-slave relationship, and maximum power capability. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A GB/T Mode 3 AC EVSE delivers 230 V single-phase at 32 A maximum. The onboard charger measures a pilot duty cycle of **80 %**.
   (i) Identify the **operating region** of the GB/T pilot signal.
   (ii) Compute the **available current** the EV may safely draw.
   (iii) Compute the charging time for a **24 kWh** battery at constant power with charger efficiency of **0.94**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Comparison Table**

*(The detailed table is already in Section 3.3. The student should reproduce a clean, simplified version:)*

| Feature | GB/T | CCS | CHAdeMO |
|---|---|---|---|
| Connector | 9-pin DC | Combo 1/2 | 10-pin |
| Protocol | GB/T 27930 | ISO 15118 | CHAdeMO |
| Master | Charger | EV | EV |
| Max Power | 250 kW | 350 kW | 400 kW |

**(b) Numerical Solution**

**Given:** $V = 230$ V, $I_{\text{max,EVSE}} = 32$ A, $D = 80\%$, $E_{\text{bat}} = 24$ kWh, $\eta_{\text{ch}} = 0.94$.

**(i) Operating Region:**

$$ 10\% \leq 80\% < 85\% $$

$$\Rightarrow \textbf{Region 1: Standby} \quad I_{\text{avail}} = D \times 0.6$$

**(ii) Available current:**

$$ I_{\text{avail}} = 0.80 \times 0.6 = 0.48 \text{ A} $$

**(iii) Charging time:**

Power at battery terminals:

$$ P_{\text{batt}} = V \times I_{\text{avail}} \times \eta_{\text{ch}} $$

$$ P_{\text{batt}} = 230 \times 0.48 \times 0.94 = 103.78 \text{ W} = 0.10378 \text{ kW} $$

Charging time:

$$ t_{\text{ch}} = \frac{24 \text{ kWh}}{0.10378 \text{ kW}} = 231.26 \text{ h} $$

$$\boxed{t_{\text{ch}} \approx 231.3 \text{ hours}}$$

This indicates a **fault pilot state** — the EV must not begin charging.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Duty-cycle region misidentification:** Students often blindly apply $I = D \times 0.6$ or $I = D \times 2.5$ *without* identifying the region. KTU examiners **deduct 2 marks** for this. Always state the **region first**, then substitute the formula.
> 2. **Master-Slave confusion:** GB/T is **charger-master**; CCS and CHAdeMO are **EV-master**. Mixing these is a 3-mark deduction.
> 3. **CC1 vs. CC2 confusion:** Many students describe CC1/CC2 as "charging confirmation and connection confirmation" — the **correct mapping** is CC1 = *proximity* (insertion), CC2 = *latch state*. Reversing them loses 1 mark.
> 4. **GB/T vs. GB number:** GB/T is *voluntary/recommended*, **GB is mandatory**. EHV-relevant codes are all **GB/T** family — using just "GB" in answers is technically inaccurate.
> 5. **Forgetting the BMS–charger handshake:** DC charging is *not* "plug and play"; you must describe the **four-phase state machine** for full marks on communication questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- [x] **GB/T** = Chinese National Standard (recommended/voluntary) for EV conductive charging.
- [x] Three core sub-standards: **GB/T 18487.1** (system), **GB/T 20234.2/3** (connector), **GB/T 27930** (CAN protocol).
- [x] **GB/T 20234.3 DC connector has 9 pins**; AC connector has 7 pins.
- [x] **CC1 = proximity detection; CC2 = latch confirmation** — a unique dual-pin safety feature.
- [x] Pilot duty-cycle has **three piecewise regions** — always check region before applying formula.
- [x] **GB/T 27930 master = charger**, slave = BMS (opposite to CCS and CHAdeMO).
- [x] CAN bus runs at **250 kbps, 250 ms BCS update period**.
- [x] PWM pilot frequency = **1000 Hz ± 20 Hz**, ±12 V amplitude.
- [x] GB/T supports **V2G** via Annex B of GB/T 18487.1.
- [x] GB/T 20234.3 max current ≈ 250 A continuous; max voltage ≈ 1000 V (planned).
- [x] China's GB/T is the **world's largest installed base** of DC fast chargers — globally dominant by volume.
- [x] For KTU 14-mark answers: **always combine block diagram + phase explanation + numerical**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Customised Charging Protocols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT631 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Customised Charging Protocols

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Customised Charging Protocols** are a class of intelligent, adaptive, and negotiated communication and power-delivery frameworks that govern the **bidirectional exchange of electrical energy** between an Electric Vehicle Supply Equipment (EVSE) and an Electric Vehicle (EV), where the **voltage, current, charging profile, termination criteria, and auxiliary services** (such as authentication, billing, thermal management, and grid feedback) are dynamically tailored to the **battery chemistry, state-of-charge (SoC), state-of-health (SoH), ambient conditions, user preferences, and grid constraints** of the specific vehicle and session.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this topic is positioned at the intersection of **power electronics, battery management, embedded communication systems, and smart-grid engineering**. A "customised" protocol implies that the system moves away from the legacy "dumb" IEC 61851 PWM pilot signalling (where the EVSE simply announces a fixed current limit) and adopts a **High-Level Communication (HLC)** channel — most commonly through the **ISO 15118** standard — where both the EV and the EVSE negotiate every parameter of the charging session before current begins to flow.

The word **"customised"** itself carries three engineering implications:

1. **Vehicle-side customization:** The Battery Management System (BMS) dictates the optimum Constant Current (CC), Constant Voltage (CV) thresholds, and taper-off profiles based on chemistry (LFP, NMC, NCA, LTO).
2. **Charger-side customization:** The EVSE adjusts its power output based on grid availability, time-of-use (ToU) tariffs, and load balancing.
3. **Session-side customization:** Plug & Charge, auto-authentication, scheduled departure, and V2G services are negotiated per session.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (Module 3):** This topic directly supports **Course Outcome CO3** — *"Apply the concepts of charging infrastructure, standards, and communication protocols for electric and hybrid vehicles."* It also maps to **Cognitive Level L4 (Analyze)** and **L5 (Evaluate)** under Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you walk into a restaurant and the waiter hands you a single, fixed meal without asking what you want. That is the **old, non-customised charging** approach — the charger assumes a generic profile. 

Now imagine a smart restaurant where:

- The waiter (EVSE) asks your **appetite** (SoC), your **stomach condition** (SoH / temperature), and your **allergies** (battery chemistry limits).
- The **chef** (BMS) tells the waiter how much **heat to apply** (CC phase), when to **lower the flame** (CV phase), and when to **stop cooking** (termination).
- The **restaurant manager** (Grid Operator) also whispers instructions: "This customer has a coupon — make the meal at a steady 22 kW, not 50 kW" (load balancing, ToU tariff).
- After the meal, payment happens **automatically** when you entered the door (Plug & Charge via ISO 15118).

That is **customised charging** in its essence — a fully negotiated, adaptive, and contextual energy transaction.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants, Standards & Metrics

- **Standard AC Voltage (India):** **230 V single-phase, 415 V three-phase, 50 Hz**.
- **Standard DC Fast Charging Voltages:** **200 V – 1000 V** (per IEC 61851-23 / CCS2).
- **Standard Communication Protocols:** **ISO 15118, OCPP 2.0.1, OCPI 2.2.1, DIN 70121, CHAdeMO 2.0**.
- **Pilot signal frequency:** **1 kHz** PWM (IEC 61851-1).
- **Pilot signal duty cycle encodes maximum allowed current** (e.g., **\textbf{30\% duty = 6 A}, \textbf{53\% = 20 A}, \textbf{80\% = 32 A}**).

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Architecture of a Customised Charging Protocol

A customised protocol is not a single document — it is a **stacked architecture**. Understanding this stack is critical for KTU board questions.

### Layer 1 — Physical & Electrical Interlock
- Connector geometry (Type 2 / CCS2, GB/T, CHAdeMO, NACS).
- Proximity pilot (PP) and Control Pilot (CP) pins.
- IEC 61851-1 PWM signalling establishes **maximum current handshake** before HLC begins.

### Layer 2 — Low-Level Communication
- **Power Line Communication (PLC)** over the Control Pilot wire using **HomePlug GreenPHY** chipset.
- Achieves data rates up to **~10 Mbit/s** for ISO 15118.

### Layer 3 — High-Level Communication (HLC)
- **ISO 15118-2** (AC + DC, classic).
- **ISO 15118-20** (multiplexed, supports MCS — Megawatt Charging System).
- **DIN 70121** (older DC subset, predecessor of ISO 15118-2).
- **CHAdeMO 2.0** (Japanese standard for V2G).

### Layer 4 — Application / Use Cases
- **Plug & Charge (PnC)** — automatic contract-based authentication.
- **Scheduled / Departure-Time Charging** — user sets "ready by 7 AM".
- **Bidirectional / V2G** — EV battery feeds power back.
- **Wireless (WPT) Customisation** — SAE J2954 with alignment helpers.

### Layer 5 — Backend Communication
- **OCPP 2.0.1** (Open Charge Point Protocol) — EVSE ↔ Charging Network Operator.
- **OCPI 2.2.1** (Open Charge Point Interface) — roaming between operators.

## 2.2 The 5-Step Customised Charging Negotiation Sequence

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Any customised charging session in ISO 15118 follows this **SLAC → SDP → TCP/TLS → V2G Messages → Charging Loop** sequence. Memorize this — board questions frequently ask to "describe the ISO 15118 handshake".

1. **SLAC (Signal Level Attenuation Characterization):** EV and EVSE exchange sounding signals over PLC to determine which phase pair has the best channel quality. The EV side (EVCC) sends **M-Sounds**, the EVSE (SECC) responds with **N-Sounds** at **9.6 kHz / 12.8 kHz band**, ultimately computing attenuation.
2. **SDP (Service Discovery Protocol):** Once the channel is established, the SECC advertises available services (TCP port **15118**) and the EVCC binds to it.
3. **TCP/TLS Handshake:** A **mutually authenticated TLS 1.2 / 1.3** session is set up using **X.509 certificates**. This is what enables Plug & Charge.
4. **V2G Message Exchange:** XML-encoded (in ISO 15118-2) or **EXI-encoded** (in ISO 15118-20) messages are exchanged:
   - `ChargeParameterDiscoveryReq/Res`
   - `CableCheckReq/Res` (DC only)
   - `PreChargeReq/Res`
   - `PowerDeliveryReq/Res`
   - `ChargingStatusReq/Res`
   - `SessionStopReq/Res`
5. **Closed-Loop Power Adjustment:** The EV continuously sends `CurrentDemandReq` messages (typically every **250 ms**), and the EVSE must respond within **\textbf{≤ 1 second}** with the new current limit. If the EVSE does not respond in time, the EV must **de-rate the current to zero** — a critical safety feature.

## 2.3 Customised vs Standardised Charging — Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Standardised (IEC 61851-1 only) | Customised (ISO 15118 / OCPP 2.0.1) |
|---|---|---|
| Current limit | Fixed via PWM duty cycle | Dynamically negotiated per 250 ms loop |
| Authentication | RFID card / app | Plug & Charge via X.509 cert |
| Billing | Manual/operator-wise | Automatic, contract-based |
| Load balancing | Not possible | Possible (smart charging profiles) |
| V2G | Not supported | Supported natively (ISO 15118-20) |
| Data exchanged | ~few bits (duty cycle) | kBs per session (XML/EXI) |
| Cybersecurity | None | TLS 1.2/1.3, mutual auth |
| User awareness | None | Departure time, SoC targets, tariffs |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$ | AC charging power (single phase); $\cos\phi$ is power factor | W |
| 2 | $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$ | Three-phase AC charging power | W |
| 3 | $P_{DC} = V_{bat} \cdot I_{bat}$ | DC fast charging instantaneous power | W |
| 4 | $E_{bat} = \int_0^{t} V_{bat}(\tau) \cdot I_{bat}(\tau) \, d\tau$ | Energy delivered to battery | Wh |
| 5 | $C_{rate} = \dfrac{I_{bat}}{Q_{bat}}$ | C-rate — defines charging speed | h$^{-1}$ |
| 6 | $t_{charge} = \dfrac{C_{bat} \cdot (SoC_{final} - SoC_{initial})}{I_{bat} \cdot \eta_{charge}}$ | Approx. charging time | hours |
| 7 | $D_{pilot} = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}} \times 100\%$ | PWM duty cycle of Control Pilot | % |
| 8 | $I_{max} = (D_{pilot} \cdot 0.6) \, \text{A / \%}$ (valid 10–85%) | Max current indicated by PWM (legacy mode) | A |
| 9 | $f_{SLAC} \in \{9.6, 12.8\} \, \text{kHz}$ | SLAC sounding frequencies | Hz |
| 10 | $\Delta t_{demand} \leq 1 \, \text{s}$ | ISO 15118 deadline for `CurrentDemandRes` | s |
| 11 | $P_{grid}^{max} = P_{EVSE}^{rated} - \sum_{i \neq k} P_i$ | Load-balanced EVSE power budget | W |
| 12 | $SoH = \dfrac{Q_{max}^{actual}}{Q_{max}^{rated}} \times 100\%$ | State of Health — affects allowed C-rate | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical exam tip:** Equation 8 above is the **legacy IEC 61851-1 PWM interpretation**. In a customised charging session, this is **overridden** by the ISO 15118 `PowerDeliveryReq` message, which is a 14-bit signed integer for voltage and current. Always state the override in your answers to score full marks.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

Customised protocols are the **enabling layer for**:
- **Smart Grid integration** — demand-response events can throttle thousands of EVs simultaneously.
- **Renewable absorption** — solar-heavy noon hours can soak up excess PV into EV batteries.
- **V2H / V2G** — using parked EVs as distributed storage assets worth **\$ 400+ billion** in grid-balancing services globally.
- **Fleet management** — depots can charge 50 trucks sequentially with a single 1 MW grid connection.
- **Bidirectional WPT (wireless)** — modern protocols allow alignment fine-tuning and load modulation.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Charging Time for a Custom CC-CV Profile

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** A **60 kWh** NMC battery pack in an EV is to be charged from **20% SoC to 80% SoC** using a customised CC-CV profile. The maximum CC phase current is limited by the BMS to **1.2 C**. The pack nominal voltage is **400 V** (and rises to **420 V** at 80% SoC). Charger-to-battery efficiency is **92%**. The CV phase ends when taper current drops below **C/20**. Compute the total charging time.

**Given:**
- Energy capacity: $C_{bat} = 60 \, \text{kWh}$
- Initial SoC: $SoC_i = 20\%$
- Final SoC: $SoC_f = 80\%$
- C-rate: $C_{rate} = 1.2 \, \text{C}$
- Nominal voltage: $V_{nom} = 400 \, \text{V}$
- CV-phase voltage: $V_{cv} = 420 \, \text{V}$
- Efficiency: $\eta = 0.92$
- Taper current threshold: $I_{taper} = C_{bat} / 20$

### Step 1 — Equivalent Full Charge Capacity in Ah

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{bat} &= \dfrac{C_{bat}}{V_{nom}} \\
&= \dfrac{60{,}000 \, \text{Wh}}{400 \, \text{V}} \\
&= 150 \, \text{Ah}
\end{aligned}
$$

This conversion is necessary because the **C-rate definition** in equation (5) is naturally expressed in Amperes, and the pack's energy rating is in Watt-hours.

### Step 2 — CC-Phase Current

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{cc} &= C_{rate} \cdot Q_{bat} \\
&= 1.2 \cdot 150 \\
&= 180 \, \text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — CC-Phase Energy Delivered

The CC phase typically transitions to CV around **70% SoC** in NMC chemistry. So the CC phase takes the battery from **20% to 70%**, delivering **50% of $C_{bat}$**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta E_{cc} &= 0.50 \cdot 60 \, \text{kWh} \\
&= 30 \, \text{kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — CC-Phase Duration

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{cc} &= \dfrac{\Delta E_{cc}}{V_{nom} \cdot I_{cc} \cdot \eta} \\
&= \dfrac{30{,}000 \, \text{Wh}}{400 \, \text{V} \cdot 180 \, \text{A} \cdot 0.92} \\
&= \dfrac{30{,}000}{66{,}240} \\
&= 0.4529 \, \text{h} \\
&\approx 27.2 \, \text{minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — CV-Phase End Current

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{taper} &= \dfrac{C_{rate, taper}}{1 \, \text{h}} \cdot Q_{bat} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{20} \cdot 150 \\
&= 7.5 \, \text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — CV-Phase Energy Delivered (10% SoC, from 70% to 80%)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta E_{cv} &= 0.10 \cdot 60 \\
&= 6 \, \text{kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Approximate CV-Phase Average Power

The CV-phase current ramps from **180 A** down to **7.5 A**. We approximate the mean current as the arithmetic mean (a common KTU board simplification):

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{avg,cv} &= \dfrac{180 + 7.5}{2} \\
&= 93.75 \, \text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 8 — CV-Phase Duration

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{cv} &= \dfrac{\Delta E_{cv}}{V_{cv} \cdot I_{avg,cv} \cdot \eta} \\
&= \dfrac{6{,}000 \, \text{Wh}}{420 \, \text{V} \cdot 93.75 \, \text{A} \cdot 0.92} \\
&= \dfrac{6{,}000}{36{,}225} \\
&= 0.1656 \, \text{h} \\
&\approx 9.94 \, \text{minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 9 — Total Charging Time

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{total} &= t_{cc} + t_{cv} \\
&= 27.2 + 9.94 \\
&\approx 37.1 \, \text{minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** Approximately **37 minutes** from 20% to 80% SoC.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** This 37-minute benchmark is the **"EV fast-charging sweet spot"** used by Tesla Supercharger V3 (250 kW) and Hyundai E-GMP (800 V, 350 kW) platforms. The same calculation for a **7 kW AC onboard charger** (32 A, single phase, 230 V) yields ~5.7 hours — explaining why **DC fast charging requires customisation**, while AC level 1/2 is naturally slow.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Load-Balanced Power Budgeting at a Charging Depot

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** A charging depot has a **500 kVA** grid connection. Currently, **3 EVs** are charging at the following negotiated (customised) power levels: **E1 = 150 kW, E2 = 110 kW, E3 = 90 kW**. A **4th EV** arrives requesting **150 kW**. Derive the negotiated power that the charger management system (CMS) will assign under ISO 15118 smart-charging rules, assuming equal-priority weighted sharing of remaining capacity.

**Given:**
- $S_{grid} = 500 \, \text{kVA}$, assume $\cos\phi = 0.95$, so $P_{grid}^{max} \approx 475 \, \text{kW}$.
- $P_1 = 150$, $P_2 = 110$, $P_3 = 90$, $P_4^{req} = 150$.

### Step 1 — Currently Allocated Power

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{used} &= 150 + 110 + 90 \\
&= 350 \, \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Remaining Capacity

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{rem} &= P_{grid}^{max} - P_{used} \\
&= 475 - 350 \\
&= 125 \, \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Equal-Priority Allocation

If all four EVs are weighted equally, each gets:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_i^{new} &= \dfrac{P_{grid}^{max}}{N} \\
&= \dfrac{475}{4} \\
&= 118.75 \, \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

But this requires **reducing E1, E2, E3**, which is generally avoided. The CMS instead allocates **$P_4^{new} = \min(P_4^{req}, P_{rem}) = 125 \, \text{kW}$** and informs E4 that the negotiated limit is **125 kW** via the `PowerDeliveryReq` message.

### Step 4 — Final Negotiated Profile

| EV | Requested | Negotiated |
|---|---|---|
| E1 | 150 kW | 150 kW (unchanged) |
| E2 | 110 kW | 110 kW (unchanged) |
| E3 | 90 kW | 90 kW (unchanged) |
| E4 | 150 kW | **125 kW (capped)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** The negotiated value is communicated **back to the EV** in the `PowerDeliveryRes` and `CurrentDemandRes` messages, and the EV's BMS must **never exceed** it. This is the practical manifestation of "customised" charging — no two sessions in a depot look identical.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — C-Rate vs Temperature Customisation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** A LFP cell rated **3.2 V, 100 Ah, 25 °C** has a BMS that reduces C-rate linearly from **1.5 C at 25 °C to 0.2 C at 0 °C and 0.5 C at 45 °C**. At a measured cell temperature of **10 °C**, derive the allowed C-rate.

### Linear Interpolation between 0 °C and 25 °C

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(T) &= C(0) + \dfrac{C(25) - C(0)}{25 - 0} \cdot (T - 0) \\
&= 0.2 + \dfrac{1.5 - 0.2}{25} \cdot 10 \\
&= 0.2 + \dfrac{1.3 \cdot 10}{25} \\
&= 0.2 + 0.52 \\
&= 0.72 \, \text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Allowed Charging Current

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{allowed} &= 0.72 \cdot 100 \\
&= 72 \, \text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

This current value is then transmitted in the **`ChargeParameterDiscoveryReq`** message to the EVSE as the **EVPresentVoltage** and **`EVMaxCurrent`** parameters.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Customised Charging Protocol — Layered Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Application Layer - Plug Charge - V2G - Smart Charging"] --> nodeB["ISO 15118-2 or 15118-20 - HLC Messages"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["TLS 1.2 or 1.3 - Mutual Auth via X509"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["TCP and IP over PLC"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["HomePlug GreenPHY PLC Modem"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Physical Layer - Control Pilot and PLC coupling"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Connector - CCS2 or CHAdeMO or NACS"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** This is a **Layered Functional Architecture Flow**. Each block represents a logical layer in the ISO 15118 stack, with arrows showing how a customised charging request propagates from the application layer down to the physical copper.

## 4.2 End-to-End ISO 15118 Communication Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EVCC["EVCC - EV Communication Controller"] -->|SLAC M Sounds| SECC["SECC - Supply Equipment Controller"]
    SECC -->|N Sounds| EVCC
    EVCC -->|SDP Request| SECC
    SECC -->|SDP Response| EVCC
    EVCC -->|TCP TLS Handshake| SECC
    SECC -->|X509 Cert| EVCC
    EVCC -->|ChargeParameterDiscoveryReq| SECC
    SECC -->|ChargeParameterDiscoveryRes| EVCC
    EVCC -->|CableCheckReq DC only| SECC
    EVCC -->|PreChargeReq| SECC
    SECC -->|PreChargeRes| EVCC
    EVCC -->|PowerDeliveryReq| SECC
    SECC -->|PowerDeliveryRes| EVCC
    EVCC -->|CurrentDemandReq every 250 ms| SECC
    SECC -->|CurrentDemandRes within 1 s| EVCC
    EVCC -->|SessionStopReq| SECC
```

## 4.3 Smart-Charging Decision Tree (CMS)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["EV Connects to EVSE"] --> B["SLAC and TLS Handshake"]
    B --> C{"Grid Capacity Available?"}
    C -->|"Yes"| D["Negotiate Max Power"]
    C -->|"No"| E["Apply Load Balancing Algorithm"]
    E --> F["Calculate Weighted Share"]
    F --> D
    D --> G["Send PowerDeliveryRes"]
    G --> H{"CurrentDemandLoop Every 250 ms"}
    H --> I{"EVSE Response within 1 s?"}
    I -->|"Yes"| J["Adjust Current"]
    I -->|"No"| K["De-rate to Zero - Safety"]
    J --> H
    K --> L["Session Stop"]
    J --> L
```
