# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Bank, Module 3]**

**Define customised charging protocols in the context of Electric Vehicles. List any two international standards that enable them.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Customised charging protocols are intelligent, negotiated communication and power-delivery frameworks between an EV and an EVSE in which the voltage, current, charging profile, termination criteria, and auxiliary services are dynamically tailored to the battery chemistry, SoC, SoH, ambient conditions, user preferences, and grid constraints of the specific session. **(2 marks)**

Two international standards that enable customised charging are:
1. **ISO 15118** — High-Level Communication (HLC) between EV and EVSE, supporting Plug & Charge, smart charging, and V2G.
2. **OCPP 2.0.1** — Open Charge Point Protocol for backend management, enabling remote start/stop, smart charging profiles, and firmware updates. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Bank, Module 3]**

**What is the role of SLAC (Signal Level Attenuation Characterization) in ISO 15118 customised charging?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

SLAC is the first mandatory handshake step in an ISO 15118 customised charging session, performed over the Control Pilot wire using **HomePlug GreenPHY PLC** at sounding frequencies of **9.6 kHz and 12.8 kHz**. **(1 mark)** Its role is to determine which phase pair (in three-phase installations) provides the best signal quality and lowest attenuation, ensuring robust high-level communication before the TCP/TLS handshake. **(1 mark)** Without successful SLAC, the customised HLC channel cannot be established, and charging falls back to the legacy IEC 61851-1 PWM mode. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, ESE Pattern]**

**(a) Describe in detail the ISO 15118 customised charging handshake sequence, with a focus on SLAC, SDP, TLS authentication, and the V2G message exchange loop. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The ISO 15118 customised charging handshake proceeds in the following sequential steps:

**1. SLAC (Signal Level Attenuation Characterization) — 2 marks**
Once the EV is plugged in, the EV's Communication Controller (EVCC) initiates SLAC by broadcasting **M-Sounds** (multicarrier bursts) across the three phase pairs of the PLC channel. The EVSE's Communication Controller (SECC) responds with **N-Sounds** at defined centre frequencies of **9.6 kHz and 12.8 kHz**. Both sides compute the **attenuation** of the channel, and the EVCC selects the phase pair with the **lowest attenuation**. This ensures that the subsequent high-bandwidth HLC traffic has a robust physical channel.

**2. SDP (Service Discovery Protocol) — 1 mark**
After SLAC, the EVCC broadcasts an **SDP request** to discover available services. The SECC responds with an **SDP response** advertising **TCP port 15118** (or 15118-2 multiplexed) as the entry point for HLC. This is essentially a "DNS-like" lookup for charging services.

**3. TCP and TLS Handshake (mutually authenticated) — 2 marks**
A standard TCP three-way handshake is followed by **TLS 1.2 or 1.3** session setup. Critically, the handshake is **mutually authenticated**: both the EV and the EVSE present **X.509 digital certificates**. The EV's certificate contains a **Provisioning Service Certificate (PCert)** chained to a **V2G Root CA**, which encodes the contract between the EV owner and the Mobility Operator (MO). This is what enables **Plug & Charge** — no RFID card is required.

**4. V2G Message Exchange — 2 marks**
The two sides exchange **V2G messages** in EXI (Efficient XML Interchange) format. The key sequence is:
- `ChargeParameterDiscoveryReq/Res` — exchange of supported voltage, current, and SoC targets.
- `CableCheckReq/Res` (DC only) — verify isolation.
- `PreChargeReq/Res` — bring DC bus close to battery voltage.
- `PowerDeliveryReq/Res` — authorise current flow.
- `CurrentDemandReq/Res` — closed-loop, every **250 ms**; the EV must receive `CurrentDemandRes` within **1 s**, otherwise it de-rates to zero.
- `SessionStopReq/Res` — clean termination.

---

**(b) A 75 kWh NMC battery pack in an EV is to be charged from 10% to 80% SoC using a customised CC-CV protocol. The BMS limits the CC current to 1.5 C, the pack voltage is 400 V (CV at 420 V), and the charger-to-battery efficiency is 90%. The CV phase tapers from full CC current to C/20. Compute the total charging time. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Pack Ah capacity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{bat} &= \dfrac{75{,}000}{400} = 187.5 \, \text{Ah}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — CC phase current:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{cc} &= 1.5 \cdot 187.5 = 281.25 \, \text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — CC phase SoC range (10% → 70%):**

For NMC chemistry, CC-CV transition typically occurs at 70% SoC.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta E_{cc} &= (0.70 - 0.10) \cdot 75 = 45 \, \text{kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — CC phase time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{cc} &= \dfrac{45{,}000}{400 \cdot 281.25 \cdot 0.90} \\
&= \dfrac{45{,}000}{101{,}250} \\
&= 0.4444 \, \text{h} = 26.67 \, \text{minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — CV phase end current:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{taper} &= \dfrac{187.5}{20} = 9.375 \, \text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — CV phase energy (70% → 80%):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta E_{cv} &= 0.10 \cdot 75 = 7.5 \, \text{kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — CV phase average current:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{avg,cv} &= \dfrac{281.25 + 9.375}{2} = 145.31 \, \text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — CV phase time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{cv} &= \dfrac{7{,}500}{420 \cdot 145.31 \cdot 0.90} \\
&= \dfrac{7{,}500}{54{,}927} \\
&= 0.1365 \, \text{h} = 8.19 \, \text{minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9 — Total charging time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{total} &= 26.67 + 8.19 = 34.86 \, \text{minutes} \approx \mathbf{35 \, \text{minutes}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** The total charging time is approximately **35 minutes**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) Compare the standardised IEC 61851-1 PWM pilot signalling approach with the customised ISO 15118 High-Level Communication approach. Discuss authentication, current negotiation, security, and V2G capability. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Dimension | IEC 61851-1 (PWM Pilot) | ISO 15118 (Customised) |
|---|---|---|
| **Communication** | One-way analogue PWM (1 kHz) on Control Pilot | Bi-directional digital PLC + EXI messages |
| **Current negotiation** | Fixed by duty cycle: $D = I / 0.6$ for 10–85% | Dynamic, per **250 ms** loop, per-cell BMS limits |
| **Authentication** | None / RFID / app-based | Mutual TLS 1.2/1.3, X.509, Plug & Charge |
| **Security** | None (no encryption) | Encrypted, signed, replay-protected |
| **Data exchanged** | ~6 bits (duty cycle) | kBs per session (EV status, tariffs, schedules) |
| **V2G** | Not supported | Natively supported in ISO 15118-20 |
| **Smart charging** | Not possible | Possible (load balancing, ToU tariffs) |
| **Cybersecurity** | Vulnerable to plug-relay attacks | Resilient via PKI |
| **Cost** | Very low | Higher (PLC modem, secure element) |

**Key Takeaway:** IEC 61851-1 is a **legacy "dumb" current-capable pilot** that is still mandatory as a fallback, but cannot support any modern V2G or smart-grid service. ISO 15118 is the **customised** successor and is mandated for **Plug & Charge** and **V2G** under EU AFIR regulations and the **Bharat DC-001 / CCS2** upgrades in India. **(2 marks for conclusion)**

---

**(b) A charging depot has a 750 kVA grid connection (cos φ = 0.95). Four EVs are connected with requested powers of 180 kW, 150 kW, 120 kW, and 100 kW. A fifth EV arrives requesting 200 kW. Using a weighted-priority load-balancing algorithm where the priorities are [3, 2, 2, 1, 3] for EVs 1–5, derive the negotiated power for EV 5. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Maximum real power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{grid}^{max} &= 750 \cdot 0.95 = 712.5 \, \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total requested power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{req,total} &= 180 + 150 + 120 + 100 + 200 = 750 \, \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total priority weight:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{total} &= 3 + 2 + 2 + 1 + 3 = 11
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Power per unit weight:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{unit} &= \dfrac{P_{grid}^{max}}{W_{total}} = \dfrac{712.5}{11} = 64.77 \, \text{kW / unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Allocated powers (initial):**

| EV | Priority | Allocated (kW) | Capped at request? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3 | $3 \cdot 64.77 = 194.32$ | **Capped to 180** |
| 2 | 2 | $2 \cdot 64.77 = 129.55$ | OK |
| 3 | 2 | $2 \cdot 64.77 = 129.55$ | OK (over 120) **Capped to 120** |
| 4 | 1 | $1 \cdot 64.77 = 64.77$ | OK |
| 5 | 3 | $3 \cdot 64.77 = 194.32$ | OK (under 200) |

**Step 6 — Re-balance after caps:**

Excess: $194.32 - 180 = 14.32$ kW (from EV1) and $129.55 - 120 = 9.55$ kW (from EV3). Total freed: **23.87 kW**.

Remaining demand: EV5 still asks for 200, EV2/4 are under-allocated. Redistribution adds **23.87 kW** to EV5.

**Step 7 — Final allocation for EV 5:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_5^{final} &= 194.32 + 23.87 = \mathbf{218.19 \, kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

But **EV5 only requested 200 kW**, so the **negotiated final power** is:

$$
\boxed{P_5^{final} = \mathbf{200 \, \text{kW}}}
$$

**Answer:** The negotiated power for EV 5 is **200 kW** (full demand met), and the freed capacity is re-allocated to EVs 1 and 3 which were capped. This prioritised redistribution is communicated to each EV via successive `PowerDeliveryRes` messages, completing the **customised** session.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in Customised Charging Protocol Questions:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up IEC 61851-1 with ISO 15118:** Students often describe the PWM pilot as a "customised" feature. **Marks lost:** 1–2 per occurrence. *Always clarify that IEC 61851-1 is the **legacy fallback**, and ISO 15118 is the customised layer.*
>
> 2. **Forgetting the 1-second deadline:** ISO 15118 specifies that if `CurrentDemandRes` is not received within 1 s, the EV **must de-rate to zero**. Many students omit this. **Marks lost:** 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Ignoring C-rate and SoH:** When asked about customised profiles, students write generic "fast charging" answers without considering **C-rate, SoH, temperature derating, and CV taper**. **Marks lost:** 2–3 marks.
>
> 4. **PWM duty cycle formula error:** Equation 8 is $I = D \cdot 0.6$ for duty in 10–85%. Students sometimes write $I = D \cdot 6$ (miscounting the unit conversion). **Marks lost:** 1 mark.
>
> 5. **No mention of Plug & Charge security:** The X.509 PKI infrastructure underpinning Plug & Charge is often skipped. **Marks lost:** 1–2 marks.
>
> 6. **Treating OCPP and OCPI as the same:** OCPP is EVSE ↔ Network Operator; OCPI is Network Operator ↔ Network Operator (roaming). **Marks lost:** 1 mark.
>
> 7. **No unit consistency in derivations:** Mixing kW with W, or h with min, without explicit conversion. **Marks lost:** 0.5–1 mark per inconsistency.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Customised charging = dynamic, negotiated, intelligent power delivery** based on chemistry, SoC, SoH, temperature, grid, and user.
- **IEC 61851-1 PWM** is the **legacy, non-customised** control pilot (1 kHz, duty cycle encodes max current). Always present; serves as fallback.
- **ISO 15118** is the **customised HLC layer**; uses HomePlug GreenPHY PLC over the Control Pilot wire.
- **SLAC** is the first handshake; sounding at **9.6 kHz / 12.8 kHz** to find the best phase pair.
- **Plug & Charge** relies on **mutual TLS + X.509 certificates** chained to a V2G Root CA.
- The **CurrentDemand loop runs every 250 ms**; if `CurrentDemandRes` is not received in **≤ 1 s**, the EV de-rates to **zero current** (safety).
- **OCPP 2.0.1** = EVSE ↔ Network Operator (backend), supports **smart charging profiles**.
- **OCPI 2.2.1** = Network Operator ↔ Network Operator (roaming between providers).
- **CC-CV profile:** CC at 1–1.5 C until 70% SoC (NMC) or 80% SoC (LFP), then CV taper to C/20.
- **Customisation parameters:** $V_{max}$, $I_{max}$, $C_{rate}$, $T_{cell}$, $SoC_{target}$, $t_{departure}$, tariff class.
- **V2G (ISO 15118-20)** uses the same closed loop but with **negative `PowerDeliveryReq`**.
- **Indian context:** **Bharat AC-001 (230 V, 15 A)** and **Bharat DC-001 (48 V / 72 V, 200 A)** are early custom protocols; **CCS2** is the future direction.
- **Wireless (SAE J2954)** customisation: alignment check, pad-to-pad gap, foreign object detection, and load modulation frequency.
- **Cybersecurity:** No TLS → no Plug & Charge → no V2G. The PKI is non-negotiable.
- **Interoperability:** ISO 15118-20 introduces **app-layer protocol multiplexing**, allowing multiple sessions over one PLC link (e.g., charging + V2H + fleet).
- **Remember the formulas** in the cheat sheet (Section 2.4) — they appear in 80% of numerical questions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Mantra for this topic:** Always structure your answer in three blocks — (1) **Why customisation is needed** (chemistry, grid, user), (2) **How ISO 15118 enables it** (SLAC, TLS, V2G messages), and (3) **Numerical proof** (C-rate, charging time, load balancing). This triad fetches full marks in both Part A and Part B.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Battery Box Engineering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT631 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Battery Box Engineering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Battery Box Engineering** is the multidisciplinary design and integration discipline concerned with the structural enclosure, thermal pathway, electrical isolation, mechanical mounting, and crash-energy management system that physically houses, protects, and manages the operational environment of a High-Voltage (HV) traction battery pack in an Electric Vehicle (EV) or Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV).

In KTU 2024 scheme context, Battery Box Engineering is treated as the convergence point of **mechanical structural design, thermal-fluid engineering, electrical safety, and automotive packaging** — making it a critical Core Engineering deliverable in EV architecture. The Battery Box (also referred to as the *Battery Enclosure*, *Battery Tray*, or *Pack Housing*) must simultaneously satisfy conflicting requirements: it must be **lightweight** (to preserve range), **structurally rigid** (to survive crash loading), **thermally conductive or insulated** (as design demands), **electrically isolated**, and **manufacturable at automotive volumes**.

The battery box is officially classified under the KTU 2024 syllabus under three engineering layers:

| Layer | Function | Subsystem |
|-------|----------|-----------|
| **Structural Layer** | Load-bearing, crash absorption | Sheet metal/castings, cross-members |
| **Thermal Layer** | Heat dissipation or retention | Cold plates, phase-change material, air ducts |
| **Sealing Layer** | IP-rated isolation from environment | Gaskets, sealants, vent membranes |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of the battery box like a bank vault built inside an earthquake-prone high-rise, with built-in air conditioning and leak-proof double doors — except the vault must also be as light as a hollow aluminum suitcase.**

Imagine a conventional car's fuel tank. Now imagine replacing that flammable liquid with thousands of small, energetic chemical cells (18650, 21700, or prismatic Li-ion cells). These cells:

- Contain enough stored energy to power a small house for a day
- Are extremely sensitive to **puncture, heat, water, and vibration**
- Can enter **thermal runaway** (uncontrolled exothermic reaction) if damaged
- Must be accessible for service and diagnostics

The **battery box is the protective cocoon** that solves all these challenges simultaneously. It is *not* just a metal box — it is a precision-engineered **multi-functional structural, thermal, and safety system** that transforms a collection of dangerous chemical cells into a safe, road-worthy automotive component.

A real-world analogy: **a battery box is to an EV battery pack what a chassis frame is to an internal combustion engine** — it is the foundational platform upon which all other systems are mounted, and its failure compromises every downstream function.

## 1.3 Why Battery Box Engineering Matters in KTU Examinations

The KTU 2024 Scheme PEMUT631 syllabus highlights battery box engineering as a **high-weightage, design-oriented topic** because:

1. **Safety-critical**: Battery box failures directly cause vehicle fires and passenger injuries.
2. **Performance-determining**: Box mass fraction directly reduces vehicle range.
3. **Cross-disciplinary**: Tests the student's ability to integrate mechanical, thermal, and electrical concepts.
4. **Industry-relevant**: Every OEM (Tesla, BYD, Tata, Mahindra) has proprietary battery box designs.

Key performance metrics used by the industry (and examiners) for benchmarking:

- **Volumetric Energy Density**: Target **> 250 Wh/L** at pack level
- **Gravimetric Energy Density**: Target **> 150 Wh/kg** at pack level
- **IP Rating**: Minimum **IP67**, preferred **IP68 / IP6K9K**
- **Mass fraction of enclosure**: Target **< 20%** of total pack mass
- **Crash survival**: Must withstand **> 20g** deceleration pulse
- **Operating temperature range**: **-30 °C to +60 °C** ambient
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Core Design Requirements (Hierarchical Decomposition)

Battery Box Engineering can be decomposed into the following hierarchical design requirements, each driven by an engineering first-principle:

### A. Structural / Mechanical Requirements

**A1. Crash Energy Absorption**
The battery box is the **first line of defense** during front, rear, side, and underbody impact events. In a frontal crash, the battery box must not rupture, as electrolyte leakage + electrical short = fire hazard.

The fundamental requirement is governed by **Newton's Second Law**:

$$F_{crash} = m_{pack} \cdot a_{decel}$$

where:
- $F_{crash}$ = inertial force experienced by battery pack during crash (N)
- $m_{pack}$ = total battery pack mass (kg)
- $a_{decel}$ = peak deceleration experienced (m/s²)

For a typical 400 kg EV battery pack experiencing a 25 g crash pulse:

$$F_{crash} = 400 \cdot 25 \cdot 9.81 = 98{,}100 \text{ N} \approx 98 \text{ kN}$$

This 98 kN load must be transferred safely through the box into the vehicle's crumple zones without intruding into the cell array.

**A2. Structural Stiffness and Modal Frequency**
The battery box must avoid resonance with road excitations. Road-induced vibration frequencies typically range from **0 Hz to 200 Hz** (with dominant excitation between **10 Hz and 80 Hz**). The natural frequency of the battery box structure must lie **above this excitation band** to prevent resonance amplification.

The fundamental natural frequency for a rectangular plate (simplified model of a battery box bottom plate):

$$f_{n} = \frac{\pi}{2} \sqrt{\frac{D}{\rho h L^{4}}} \cdot \left( m^{2} + n^{2} \right)^{2}$$

where:
- $D = \frac{E h^{3}}{12(1-\nu^{2})}$ = flexural rigidity of the plate (N·m)
- $E$ = Young's modulus of the box material (Pa)
- $h$ = plate thickness (m)
- $\rho$ = material density (kg/m³)
- $L$ = plate length (m)
- $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio
- $m, n$ = mode numbers (integers, fundamental mode: $m=1, n=1$)

**A3. Vibration and Shock Loading (ISO 16750-3)**
The battery box must survive:
- Random vibration profiles (PSD curves)
- Mechanical shock pulses (50 g, 6 ms half-sine)
- Drop tests (from 1 m height)

### B. Thermal Management Requirements

**B1. Heat Dissipation (Conduction)**
The dominant heat transfer path from cell to ambient is **conduction through the bottom plate to a cold plate**. Fourier's Law governs this:

$$q = \frac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{t}$$

where:
- $q$ = heat transfer rate (W)
- $k$ = thermal conductivity of the box bottom plate (W/m·K)
- $A$ = contact area between cell and plate (m²)
- $\Delta T$ = temperature difference between cell and coolant (K)
- $t$ = plate thickness (m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is **why aluminum (k ≈ 205 W/m·K) is preferred over steel (k ≈ 50 W/m·K)** for battery box bottom plates that double as cold plates.

**B2. Thermal Expansion Mismatch**
During operation, cells heat up to **40-60 °C** while the box exterior may be at **-20 °C** in winter. The differential thermal expansion can cause mechanical stress at the cell-to-box interface:

$$\varepsilon_{thermal} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

$$\sigma_{thermal} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

For aluminum ($\alpha = 23 \times 10^{-6} /K$, $E = 70$ GPa), with $\Delta T = 80$ K:

$$\sigma_{thermal} = 70 \times 10^{9} \cdot 23 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 80 = 128.8 \text{ MPa}$$

This stress must be managed via **compliant thermal interface materials (TIM)** such as gap fillers or thermal pads.

### C. Electrical Safety Requirements

**C1. Creepage and Clearance Distances**
The battery box must provide sufficient **insulation distance** between the high-voltage bus (typically **400 V or 800 V**) and the grounded enclosure (chassis). Per IEC 60664:

| System Voltage | Min. Clearance | Min. Creepage |
|---------------|----------------|---------------|
| 400 V DC | 5.0 mm | 8.0 mm |
| 800 V DC | 8.0 mm | 12.0 mm |

**C2. Isolation Resistance**
The insulation resistance between HV bus and enclosure must exceed **100 Ω/V** (per UN R100) or roughly **500 kΩ total** for a 400V system.

### D. Environmental Sealing Requirements

**D1. IP Rating Definition**

> [!NOTE]
> **IP67** = Dust-tight (6) + Immersion to 1 m for 30 min (7)
> **IP6K9K** = Dust-tight + High-pressure, high-temperature jet wash (automotive standard)

The first digit (6) refers to solid particle protection, the second (7 or 9K) refers to liquid ingress protection. Achieving IP67 requires:
- Continuous gasket bead around lid perimeter
- Sealed cable gland entries
- Gore-Tex vent membrane (to allow pressure equalization without moisture ingress)

### E. Manufacturing and Assembly Requirements

| Manufacturing Process | Application | Typical Material |
|----------------------|-------------|------------------|
| **Aluminum extrusion** | Frame rails, cross-members | 6063-T5, 6061-T6 |
| **Aluminum die casting** | Corner brackets, mounting points | ADC12, A380 |
| **Sheet metal stamping** | Lid, bottom plate | 5052, 5182 |
| **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)** | Bottom plate seams | 6061-T6 |
| **TIG/MIG welding** | Steel enclosures | DP590, DP780 |
| **Composite layup (SMC/carbon)** | Premium lightweight enclosures | Carbon fiber + epoxy |
| **Adhesive bonding** | Composite assemblies | Structural epoxy |

## 2.2 Material Selection Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The four most commonly used battery box materials in modern EVs, ranked by adoption:

### 2.2.1 Aluminum Alloys (Dominant Choice)

**Why Aluminum:**
- Excellent strength-to-weight ratio (specific stiffness comparable to steel at 1/3 density)
- Superior thermal conductivity (enables cold plate integration)
- Natural corrosion resistance (forms protective oxide layer)
- Recyclable (sustainability credentials)
- Formable and weldable

**Disadvantages:**
- Higher raw material cost than steel
- Welding requires specialized processes (FSW, MIG with Al-Si filler)
- Lower absolute stiffness than steel (requires thicker sections or ribbing)

**Specific stiffness comparison** (key lightweighting metric):

$$\frac{E}{\rho} \quad \text{(specific stiffness, units: } \frac{\text{Pa}}{\text{kg/m}^3}\text{)}$$

| Material | E (GPa) | $\rho$ (kg/m³) | E/$\rho$ (MN·m/kg) |
|----------|---------|----------------|---------------------|
| Steel (mild) | 200 | 7850 | 25.5 |
| Aluminum 6061 | 70 | 2700 | 25.9 |
| Carbon Fiber Composite | 140 | 1600 | **87.5** |
| Magnesium AZ91 | 45 | 1800 | 25.0 |

> [!NOTE]
> Note that **steel and aluminum have nearly identical specific stiffness** — so lightweighting via aluminum only works if you can thin the section without losing stiffness. This is why **stamped/extruded ribbed aluminum sections** are common in EV battery boxes.

### 2.2.2 Advanced High-Strength Steel (AHSS)

Used in **Tesla's structural battery pack** (where the box is integral to body-in-white). Dual-phase steels like **DP780** (780 MPa tensile) offer high crash energy absorption per unit cost.

### 2.2.3 Carbon Fiber Reinforced Polymer (CFRP)

Used in **BMW i3, Lucid Air, premium EVs**. Offers best specific stiffness but at **>10x cost** of aluminum.

### 2.2.4 Composite Sandwich Structures (SMC + Foam Core)

Used in mid-tier EVs where weight is critical but CFRP cost is prohibitive.

## 2.3 Thermal Management Architectures Integrated with Battery Box

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The battery box is not a passive container — in modern EVs, it is the structural backbone of the thermal management system.**

### Architecture 1: Air-Cooled Battery Box (Simplest)
- Ambient or forced air circulated around cells
- Heat dissipated through box surface fins
- Used in: **Nissan Leaf (early generations), Mitsubishi i-MiEV**
- Heat transfer coefficient: $h \approx 10-50$ W/m²·K

### Architecture 2: Liquid-Cooled Battery Box (Industry Standard)
- Cold plate integrated into bottom of box (aluminum extrusion with internal channels)
- Coolant: 50/50 water-glycol mixture
- Heat transfer coefficient: $h \approx 500-5000$ W/m²·K (10-100x better than air)
- Used in: **Tesla Model 3/Y, Hyundai Ioniq 5, Tata Nexon EV**

The cold plate heat transfer rate:

$$Q_{coldplate} = \dot{m} \cdot c_{p} \cdot \Delta T_{coolant}$$

where:
- $\dot{m}$ = coolant mass flow rate (kg/s)
- $c_p$ = coolant specific heat (J/kg·K)
- $\Delta T_{coolant}$ = coolant temperature rise across cold plate (K)

### Architecture 3: Immersion-Cooled Battery Box (Emerging)
- Cells fully submerged in dielectric fluid (e.g., 3M Novec, Shell S5/Castrol ON)
- Heat transfer coefficient: $h \approx 1000-10000$ W/m²·K
- Used in: **Tesla Cybertruck, Formula E**
- Requires sealed box design (dielectric fluid containment)

## 2.4 Crash Engineering and Energy Absorption

The battery box must absorb crash energy through controlled deformation. Two key approaches:

### 2.4.1 Crumple Zones (Front/Rear Mounting)
The battery box is connected to the vehicle frame via **energy-absorbing structures** (aluminum honeycomb, extruded tubes) that crush progressively, limiting peak deceleration transmitted to the cells.

### 2.4.2 Side Intrusion Beams
Reinforced cross-members running laterally across the box resist side-impact intrusion. Target: **< 5% intrusion** at 50 km/h side impact per Euro NCAP.

The energy absorbed by a crushing structure is:

$$E_{abs} = F_{avg} \cdot \delta_{crush}$$

where:
- $F_{avg}$ = average crush force (N)
- $\delta_{crush}$ = crush stroke (m)

For a target crash energy of 50 kJ (typical side impact), with 100 mm crush stroke:

$$F_{avg} = \frac{E_{abs}}{\delta_{crush}} = \frac{50{,}000}{0.1} = 500 \text{ kN}$$

This 500 kN must be sustained without bottoming out or buckling.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **most frequently tested formulas** for Battery Box Engineering in KTU examinations. Memorize these equations, units, and boundary conditions.

| # | Formula | Equation | Units | Application |
|---|---------|----------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Crash Inertial Force | $F_{crash} = m_{pack} \cdot a_{decel}$ | N | Crash load calculation |
| 2 | Flexural Rigidity | $D = \frac{E h^{3}}{12(1-\nu^{2})}$ | N·m | Plate stiffness |
| 3 | Natural Frequency (plate) | $f_{n} = \frac{\pi}{2}\sqrt{\frac{D}{\rho h L^{4}}}(m^{2}+n^{2})^{2}$ | Hz | Vibration analysis |
| 4 | Conduction Heat Transfer | $q = \frac{k A \Delta T}{t}$ | W | Cold plate sizing |
| 5 | Thermal Stress | $\sigma_{th} = E \alpha \Delta T$ | Pa | Thermal expansion |
| 6 | Cold Plate Heat Removal | $Q = \dot{m} c_{p} \Delta T_{coolant}$ | W | Coolant flow sizing |
| 7 | Crash Energy Absorbed | $E_{abs} = F_{avg} \cdot \delta_{crush}$ | J | Crush zone design |
| 8 | Specific Stiffness | $\frac{E}{\rho}$ | N·m/kg | Material selection |
| 9 | Specific Strength | $\frac{\sigma_{y}}{\rho}$ | N·m/kg | Lightweighting |
| 10 | Heat Transfer Coefficient (forced conv.) | $h = \frac{Nu \cdot k}{L}$ | W/m²·K | Cold plate design |
| 11 | Pressure Drop (coolant) | $\Delta P = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^{2}}{2}$ | Pa | Pump sizing |
| 12 | Buckling Load (plate) | $P_{cr} = \frac{k \pi^{2} D}{L^{2}}$ | N | Bottom plate stability |
| 13 | Cell-to-Pack Energy Density | $E_{pack} = \frac{E_{cell}}{V_{pack}}$ | Wh/L | Pack-level metric |
| 14 | Box Mass Fraction | $\eta_{mass} = \frac{m_{box}}{m_{pack,total}}$ | dimensionless | Lightweighting KPI |
| 15 | IP Rating Definition | First digit: solids, Second digit: liquids | - | Sealing spec |
| 16 | Insulation Resistance | $R_{iso} \geq 100 \cdot V_{system}$ | Ω | UN R100 compliance |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall**: Students often confuse **clearance** (straight-line distance through air) with **creepage** (distance along the insulator surface). Creepage is always **greater than or equal to** clearance for HV battery boxes. Both must be specified separately in design reports.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Crash Inertial Force on Battery Pack

**Problem Statement:** A battery pack of mass 480 kg is mounted in an EV. During a 40% offset frontal crash test, the vehicle decelerates at a peak of 28 g. Calculate the peak inertial force experienced by the battery pack and the required yield strength of the box mounting brackets.

**Given:**
- $m_{pack} = 480$ kg
- $a_{decel} = 28 \cdot g = 28 \cdot 9.81 = 274.68$ m/s²

**Step 1: Apply Newton's Second Law**

By Newton's Second Law, the inertial force experienced by a body undergoing deceleration is:

$$F_{inertial} = m \cdot a$$

Substituting the values:

$$F_{inertial} = 480 \cdot 274.68$$

$$F_{inertial} = 131{,}846.4 \text{ N} \approx 131.85 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Convert to effective g-load for design margin**

For design with a factor of safety of 1.5:

$$F_{design} = F_{inertial} \cdot n_{FoS}$$

$$F_{design} = 131.85 \cdot 1.5 = 197.78 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Required bracket cross-sectional area**

Assuming 4 mounting brackets carry the load equally:

$$A_{bracket} = \frac{F_{design}}{4 \cdot \sigma_{yield}}$$

For steel brackets with $\sigma_{yield} = 350$ MPa (typical mild steel):

$$A_{bracket} = \frac{197{,}780}{4 \cdot 350 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$A_{bracket} = 1.413 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 141.3 \text{ mm}^2$$

This corresponds to a **12 mm × 12 mm** square bracket cross-section per mounting point.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Bottom Plate Natural Frequency (Modal Analysis)

**Problem Statement:** Determine the fundamental natural frequency of an aluminum battery box bottom plate with dimensions $L = 1.5$ m (long), $b = 1.0$ m (short), thickness $h = 3$ mm. Material: Al 6061-T6 ($E = 70$ GPa, $\rho = 2700$ kg/m³, $\nu = 0.33$). Check if the box will resonate under typical road excitation (assume 25 Hz dominant).

**Step 1: Calculate Flexural Rigidity D**

$$D = \frac{E h^{3}}{12(1-\nu^{2})}$$

$$D = \frac{70 \times 10^{9} \cdot (3 \times 10^{-3})^{3}}{12 \cdot (1 - 0.33^{2})}$$

Numerator: $70 \times 10^{9} \cdot 27 \times 10^{-9} = 1890$ N·m²

Denominator: $12 \cdot (1 - 0.1089) = 12 \cdot 0.8911 = 10.6932$

$$D = \frac{1890}{10.6932} = 176.75 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 2: Apply Fundamental Mode Formula**

For the fundamental mode (m=1, n=1):

$$f_{1,1} = \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{D}{\rho h L^{4}}} \cdot (1 + 1)^{2}$$

$$f_{1,1} = \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{D}{\rho h L^{4}}} \cdot 4$$

Note: We use the **shorter dimension** $L$ to get the lower (worst-case) frequency. Let me redo using $L = 1.0$ m (short side):

$$f_{1,1} = \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{176.75}{2700 \cdot 0.003 \cdot 1.0^{4}}} \cdot 4$$

Inside the square root:

$$\frac{D}{\rho h L^{4}} = \frac{176.75}{2700 \cdot 0.003 \cdot 1} = \frac{176.75}{8.1} = 21.82 \text{ m}^{4}/\text{s}^{2}$$

$$\sqrt{21.82} = 4.671 \text{ m}^{2}/\text{s}$$

$$f_{1,1} = \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot 4.671 \cdot 4 = \frac{\pi \cdot 4.671 \cdot 4}{2}$$

$$f_{1,1} = \frac{58.71}{2} = 29.36 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3: Compare with Road Excitation**

The calculated natural frequency $f_{1,1} = 29.36$ Hz is **close to** but **above** the dominant road excitation band (10-25 Hz). 

> [!WARNING]
> **Conclusion**: The design is **marginally acceptable**. If road excitation were to extend to 30 Hz, resonance could occur. Recommendation: **add stiffening ribs** to push $f_{1,1}$ above **40 Hz**.

To double the frequency, the plate thickness must be increased by factor $\sqrt{8} \approx 2.83$ (since $f \propto h^{1.5}$ for plates), OR stiffening ribs can be added to increase effective D.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Cold Plate Heat Removal Capacity

**Problem Statement:** A battery pack generates 3.5 kW of waste heat during 1C discharge. The cold plate (integrated into the battery box bottom) uses a 50/50 water-glycol mixture entering at 25 °C and exiting at 35 °C. Calculate the required coolant mass flow rate.

**Given:**
- $Q = 3500$ W
- $\Delta T_{coolant} = 35 - 25 = 10$ K
- $c_{p, glycol-water} \approx 3500$ J/kg·K (50/50 mixture)

**Step 1: Apply Energy Balance**

The heat absorbed by coolant equals the heat rejected by battery:

$$Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_{p} \cdot \Delta T_{coolant}$$

**Step 2: Solve for Mass Flow Rate**

$$\dot{m} = \frac{Q}{c_{p} \cdot \Delta T_{coolant}}$$

$$\dot{m} = \frac{3500}{3500 \cdot 10}$$

$$\dot{m} = 0.1 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 3: Convert to Volumetric Flow Rate**

Assuming coolant density $\rho_{coolant} = 1050$ kg/m³:

$$\dot{V} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho_{coolant}} = \frac{0.1}{1050} = 9.52 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

$$\dot{V} = 9.52 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 60 \cdot 1000 = 5.71 \text{ L/min}$$

**Step 4: Select Pump**
A 12 V DC automotive pump rated for **> 10 L/min** at 1 bar pressure drop would be selected, providing 75% design margin.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Thermal Stress at Cell-Box Interface

**Problem Statement:** A prismatic Li-ion cell expands by 2% during charging at 1C rate. The cell is constrained by the battery box walls. Calculate the thermal-mechanical stress induced if the cell-box coefficient of thermal expansion mismatch is 5 × 10⁻⁶/K over a temperature rise of 60 K.

**Given:**
- $\alpha_{mismatch} = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ /K
- $\Delta T = 60$ K
- $E_{cell} = 10$ GPa (effective modulus of constrained cell stack)

**Step 1: Calculate Differential Strain**

$$\varepsilon_{thermal} = \alpha_{mismatch} \cdot \Delta T$$

$$\varepsilon_{thermal} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 60 = 3 \times 10^{-4} \text{ (dimensionless)}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Constrained Stress**

Assuming fully constrained (worst case):

$$\sigma_{thermal} = E_{cell} \cdot \varepsilon_{thermal}$$

$$\sigma_{thermal} = 10 \times 10^{9} \cdot 3 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\sigma_{thermal} = 3 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} = 3 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Compare with Cell Failure Stress**

For a typical prismatic cell, the casing yield strength is approximately **20 MPa**. Since 3 MPa < 20 MPa, the design is **safe**, but a **compliant foam pad** (compression set < 30%) is recommended between cell and box to absorb dimensional changes and prevent stress accumulation over charge-discharge cycles.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Box Mass Fraction Optimization

**Problem Statement:** A battery pack has total mass 450 kg, with cells weighing 360 kg and ancillary components (BMS, wiring, cooling) weighing 35 kg. Calculate the current box mass fraction, and determine target box mass for achieving 15% mass fraction.

**Given:**
- $m_{pack,total} = 450$ kg
- $m_{cells} = 360$ kg
- $m_{ancillary} = 35$ kg

**Step 1: Calculate Current Box Mass**

$$m_{box} = m_{pack,total} - m_{cells} - m_{ancillary}$$

$$m_{box} = 450 - 360 - 35 = 55 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Current Mass Fraction**

$$\eta_{mass} = \frac{m_{box}}{m_{pack,total}} = \frac{55}{450} = 0.122 = 12.2\%$$

**Step 3: Calculate Target Box Mass for 15% Mass Fraction**

$$m_{box,target} = 0.15 \cdot m_{pack,total} = 0.15 \cdot 450 = 67.5 \text{ kg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Current design is already **better than target** (12.2% < 15%). The current design is well-optimized. To achieve **< 10% mass fraction**, the box would need to weigh < 45 kg, requiring **CFRP or advanced sandwich composite** construction.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Design Trade-off Summary

| Design Parameter | Lightweighting Tendency | Crash Safety Tendency | Thermal Performance Tendency | Manufacturing Cost Tendency |
|------------------|-----------------------|----------------------|------------------------------|------------------------------|
| ↑ Plate thickness | Worse (heavier) | Better | Better (lower ΔT) | Higher (material) |
| ↑ Use of aluminum | Better | Same | Better (higher k) | Higher |
| ↑ Use of CFRP | Best | Better | Worse (low k) | Highest |
| ↑ Stiffening ribs | Slight worse | Better | Slightly better | Higher (tooling) |
| ↑ Cooling channel count | Worse (more coolant mass) | Same | Best | Highest |
| ↓ Box volume (compact) | Better | Worse (less crush space) | Worse | Lower |
| ↑ IP rating (sealing) | Slight worse | Same | Same | Higher |
| ↑ Mounting point count | Slight worse | Best | Same | Higher |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Insight**: When asked to "design a battery box for a given set of requirements," always begin by **explicitly stating the conflicting trade-offs** and then justify your choices using this matrix logic.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]

**Q: List any THREE primary functions of a battery box in an electric vehicle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three primary functions of a battery box in an EV are:

1. **Mechanical Protection and Crash Safety (1 mark):** The battery box provides structural integrity during crash events, ensuring the cell array remains intact and preventing thermal runaway caused by mechanical damage.

2. **Thermal Management Integration (1 mark):** The battery box houses and integrates thermal management systems (cold plates, air ducts, or immersion fluid containment) to maintain cells within their optimal operating temperature window (typically 15-40 °C).

3. **Environmental Sealing and Electrical Isolation (1 mark):** The battery box provides IP-rated protection against water, dust, and contaminants while maintaining galvanic isolation between the high-voltage cell stack and the vehicle chassis (per IEC 60664 and UN R100).

---

### Question A2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024 / Model Paper Style]

**Q: Define the IP rating IP6K9K as applicable to automotive battery boxes.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**IP6K9K** is a specialized ingress protection rating defined under **ISO 20653** for road vehicles:

- **First Digit "6"** (1 mark): Complete protection against ingress of dust (dust-tight). The "K" suffix denotes automotive-specific testing conditions.
- **Second Digit "9K"** (2 marks): Protection against high-pressure, high-temperature water jets. Specifically, water at **80 °C, 80-100 bar pressure, sprayed from 10-15 cm distance** must not penetrate the enclosure.

This rating is **more stringent than IP68** for automotive use because it specifically tests pressure-wash scenarios (e.g., car wash, off-road driving through water crossings).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — With Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination) pattern, students must answer **one full question (14 marks) per module**, with internal choice between two questions. Each question typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Style]

**Q: A battery box for a compact electric SUV is to be designed with the following requirements:**
- Pack mass: **420 kg**
- Peak crash deceleration: **25 g**
- Operating voltage: **400 V DC**
- Material: Aluminum 6061-T6 (E = 70 GPa, $\nu$ = 0.33, $\rho$ = 2700 kg/m³)
- Bottom plate dimensions: **1500 mm × 1100 mm × 3 mm**

**Part (a) [7 marks]: Calculate (i) the peak crash inertial force on the pack, (ii) the maximum stress induced in each of the 4 mounting brackets if each has a cross-section of 15 mm × 15 mm, and (iii) check whether the mounting design is safe with a factor of safety of 1.5.**

**Model Answer for Part (a):**

**(i) Peak Crash Inertial Force [2 marks]:**

$$F_{crash} = m_{pack} \cdot a_{decel} = 420 \cdot (25 \cdot 9.81)$$

$$F_{crash} = 420 \cdot 245.25 = 103{,}005 \text{ N} \approx 103 \text{ kN}$$

**(ii) Maximum Stress per Bracket [3 marks]:**

Load distributed across 4 brackets (assuming equal distribution):

$$F_{bracket} = \frac{103{,}005}{4} = 25{,}751.25 \text{ N}$$

Bracket cross-sectional area:

$$A = 15 \text{ mm} \times 15 \text{ mm} = 225 \text{ mm}^{2} = 225 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{2}$$

Stress:

$$\sigma = \frac{F_{bracket}}{A} = \frac{25{,}751.25}{225 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\sigma = 1.144 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} = 114.4 \text{ MPa}$$

**(iii) Safety Check [2 marks]:**

Yield strength of mounting bracket steel (assumed SAE 1020): $\sigma_{y} = 250$ MPa

Allowable stress with FoS = 1.5:

$$\sigma_{allow} = \frac{\sigma_{y}}{FoS} = \frac{250}{1.5} = 166.67 \text{ MPa}$$

Since $\sigma_{actual} = 114.4$ MPa < $\sigma_{allow} = 166.67$ MPa, the mounting design is **SAFE** with a margin of $166.67 - 114.4 = 52.27$ MPa.

---

**Part (b) [7 marks]: Calculate (i) the flexural rigidity D of the bottom plate, (ii) the fundamental natural frequency using the simplified plate formula, and (iii) state whether the design will resonate with road excitations in the 15-25 Hz band.**

**Model Answer for Part (b):**

**(i) Flexural Rigidity [2 marks]:**

$$D = \frac{E h^{3}}{12(1-\nu^{2})}$$

$$D = \frac{70 \times 10^{9} \cdot (3 \times 10^{-3})^{3}}{12 \cdot (1 - 0.33^{2})}$$

$$D = \frac{70 \times 10^{9} \cdot 2.7 \times 10^{-8}}{12 \cdot 0.8911}$$

$$D = \frac{1890}{10.6932} = 176.75 \text{ N·m}$$

**(ii) Fundamental Natural Frequency [3 marks]:**

Using $L = 1.1$ m (shorter dimension for worst case), $m = n = 1$:

$$f_{1,1} = \frac{\pi}{2} \sqrt{\frac{D}{\rho h L^{4}}} (m^{2}+n^{2})^{2}$$

$$f_{1,1} = \frac{\pi}{2} \sqrt{\frac{176.75}{2700 \cdot 0.003 \cdot 1.1^{4}}} \cdot 4$$

Calculate $L^{4} = 1.1^{4} = 1.4641$ m⁴:

$$\frac{D}{\rho h L^{4}} = \frac{176.75}{2700 \cdot 0.003 \cdot 1.4641} = \frac{176.75}{11.859} = 14.90$$

$$\sqrt{14.90} = 3.861$$

$$f_{1,1} = \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot 3.861 \cdot 4 = \frac{3.1416 \cdot 3.861 \cdot 4}{2}$$

$$f_{1,1} = 24.27 \text{ Hz}$$

**(iii) Resonance Check [2 marks]:**

The calculated fundamental natural frequency $f_{1,1} = 24.27$ Hz falls **within** the road excitation band of 15-25 Hz.

> [!WARNING]
> **Conclusion**: The design will resonate with road excitations, leading to amplified vibrations and potential fatigue failure. **Recommendation**: Add stiffening ribs (e.g., 20 mm deep extruded channels at 200 mm spacing) to push the natural frequency above **40 Hz**.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - June 2024 / Style]

**Q: A liquid-cooled battery pack for an electric two-wheeler has the following specifications:**
- Pack heat dissipation: **Q = 2.5 kW at 1C continuous discharge**
- Coolant: 50/50 water-glycol mixture, $c_p$ = 3500 J/kg·K
- Coolant inlet temperature: 25 °C
- Allowed coolant temperature rise: 8 K

**Part (a) [7 marks]: (i) Calculate the required coolant mass flow rate and volumetric flow rate (in L/min). (ii) If the cold plate has dimensions 600 mm × 400 mm and is 8 mm thick (aluminum, k = 205 W/m·K), determine the steady-state temperature difference between the cell bottom surface and the coolant.**

**Model Answer for Part (a):**

**(i) Mass and Volumetric Flow Rate [3 marks]:**

From energy balance:

$$\dot{m} = \frac{Q}{c_p \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{2500}{3500 \cdot 8} = 0.0893 \text{ kg/s}$$

Volumetric flow rate (coolant density $\rho \approx 1050$ kg/m³):

$$\dot{V} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \frac{0.0893}{1050} = 8.50 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

Convert to L/min:

$$\dot{V} = 8.50 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 60 \cdot 1000 = 5.10 \text{ L/min}$$

**(ii) Steady-State Temperature Difference [4 marks]:**

Heat flux through cold plate (assuming uniform heat generation):

$$q'' = \frac{Q}{A_{plate}} = \frac{2500}{0.6 \cdot 0.4} = 10{,}416.67 \text{ W/m}^{2}$$

Conduction through plate (Fourier's Law):

$$\Delta T_{plate} = \frac{q'' \cdot t}{k} = \frac{10{,}416.67 \cdot 0.008}{205}$$

$$\Delta T_{plate} = 0.406 \text{ K}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The temperature drop across the cold plate is only **0.4 K**, confirming that aluminum's high thermal conductivity easily handles this heat load. The dominant thermal resistance will be at the **cell-to-cold-plate interface** (governed by the TIM), not the plate itself.

**Total temperature difference cell-bottom to coolant:**

If a thermal interface material (TIM) with conductance $h_{TIM} = 5000$ W/m²·K is used:

$$\Delta T_{TIM} = \frac{q''}{h_{TIM}} = \frac{10{,}416.67}{5000} = 2.08 \text{ K}$$

Total $\Delta T = \Delta T_{plate} + \Delta T_{TIM} = 0.41 + 2.08 = 2.49$ K

So the cell bottom surface is approximately **2.5 K** hotter than the bulk coolant.

---

**Part (b) [7 marks]: (i) Explain with a neat sketch the four main layers of a battery box architecture. (ii) Discuss why aluminum is preferred over steel for battery box construction in modern EVs, citing at least four specific engineering reasons.**

**Model Answer for Part (b):**

**(i) Four Main Layers of Battery Box Architecture [3 marks]:**

The four main layers of a battery box are:

1. **Structural Layer**: Provides mechanical strength and crash energy absorption. Includes bottom tray, side rails, cross-members, lid, and mounting brackets.

2. **Thermal Layer**: Manages heat transfer to/from the cells. Includes cold plates (in liquid cooling), air ducts (in air cooling), and thermal interface materials (gap fillers, thermal pads).

3. **Sealing Layer**: Provides ingress protection (IP rating). Includes EPDM gaskets, O-rings, Gore-Tex vent membranes (for pressure equalization), and sealed cable glands.

4. **Electrical Isolation Layer**: Provides galvanic isolation between high-voltage bus and chassis ground. Includes insulation barriers, creepage extenders, and HV warning labels.

*Neat sketch would show a layered cross-section with cells in the center, surrounded by the structural outer skin, with cold plate beneath, gaskets around the lid perimeter, and HV busbar insulation between busbars and box wall.*

**(ii) Aluminum vs. Steel for Battery Box [4 marks]:**

| # | Engineering Reason | Explanation |
|---|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Weight Reduction (1 mark)** | Aluminum density (2700 kg/m³) is **1/3 of steel** (7850 kg/m³). For a 55 kg box, switching to aluminum saves ~37 kg, directly extending EV range by ~3-5 km per charge. |
| 2 | **Superior Thermal Conductivity (1 mark)** | Aluminum (k = 205 W/m·K) vs steel (k = 50 W/m·K). Aluminum enables efficient cold plate integration, reducing cell-to-coolant thermal resistance by ~75%. |
| 3 | **Corrosion Resistance (1 mark)** | Aluminum forms a self-healing Al₂O₃ oxide layer, eliminating need for paint/coating in unpainted interior surfaces. Critical for IP-rated battery compartments. |
| 4 | **Formability and Extrudability (1 mark)** | Aluminum 6063 extrudes into complex cross-sections (hollow rails, multi-channel cold plates) that are impossible with steel. Enables single-piece integrated designs. |

**Trade-off Acknowledged (no marks deducted, but shows depth):** Aluminum's higher raw material cost is offset by lower total system cost when lifecycle weight savings, thermal performance, and reduced assembly complexity are accounted for.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make in Battery Box Engineering Questions (and How to Avoid Them):**
>
> **Mistake 1 — Unit Confusion in Crash Force Calculation (Loss: 1-2 marks)**: Students frequently forget to multiply g by 9.81 m/s² when given acceleration in "g" units. Always write **$a = 25 \cdot 9.81 = 245.25$ m/s²** explicitly.
>
> **Mistake 2 — Confusing Clearance with Creepage (Loss: 1 mark)**: Clearance = straight-line distance through air. Creepage = path distance along insulator surface. For HV battery boxes, **creepage > clearance** is always true. Both must be specified separately.
>
> **Mistake 3 — Omitting Material Properties (Loss: 1 mark)**: When calculating plate frequency, students often forget to state E, $\rho$, and $\nu$ before substituting into the formula. Always **list given values in a "Given:" section** before calculations.
>
> **Mistake 4 — Wrong Dimension in Plate Frequency Formula (Loss: 2 marks)**: Use the **shorter plate dimension** for worst-case (lowest) natural frequency. Students often use the longer dimension, getting an erroneously high (non-conservative) frequency.
>
> **Mistake 5 — Forgetting Factor of Safety (Loss: 1 mark)**: Always apply FoS = 1.5 to 2.0 on calculated stresses when checking against yield strength. KTU examiners explicitly look for this in 14-mark design questions.
>
> **Mistake 6 — Treating Battery Box as Passive Container (Loss: 2-3 marks)**: Modern examiners expect students to recognize that the battery box is a **multi-functional structural-thermal-electrical system**, not just a metal box. Always mention integration with thermal management and crash safety.
>
> **Mistake 7 — Wrong Cold Plate Area (Loss: 1 mark)**: When computing heat flux $q''$, use the actual plate area in contact with cells, not the total battery box footprint.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Battery Box Engineering (KTU PEMUT631, Module 3)**

- [x] **Battery Box Definition**: Multi-functional enclosure providing structural, thermal, electrical, and sealing functions for HV battery pack.
- [x] **Primary Materials**: Aluminum 6061/6063 (60% of EVs), Steel DP780/DP590 (Tesla structural pack), CFRP (premium EVs), SMC composites (mid-tier).
- [x] **Key Design Metrics**: Volumetric energy density > 250 Wh/L, Gravimetric > 150 Wh/kg, IP67 minimum, Mass fraction < 20%.
- [x] **Crash Requirement**: Must survive **> 20 g** deceleration without cell intrusion; use $F = m \cdot a$ with FoS = 1.5.
- [x] **Vibration Requirement**: Natural frequency > 40 Hz to avoid resonance with road excitation (10-25 Hz dominant).
- [x] **Plate Frequency Formula**: $f_{n} = \frac{\pi}{2}\sqrt{\frac{D}{\rho h L^{4}}}(m^{2}+n^{2})^{2}$ where $D = \frac{Eh^{3}}{12(1-\nu^{2})}$.
- [x] **Thermal Conductivity Comparison**: Aluminum (205 W/m·K) > Steel (50 W/m·K) > CFRP (5 W/m·K along fiber).
- [x] **Cold Plate Sizing**: $Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$; typical coolant is 50/50 water-glycol with $c_p \approx 3500$ J/kg·K.
- [x] **Thermal Stress**: $\sigma = E \alpha \Delta T$; manage with compliant TIM (gap fillers).
- [x] **HV Clearance/Creepage**: 400V → 5mm/8mm; 800V → 8mm/12mm (per IEC 60664).
- [x] **IP Rating Standard**: Automotive uses **IP6K9K** (more stringent than IP68 for pressure-wash).
- [x] **Manufacturing Processes**: Aluminum extrusion (rails), FSW/TIG welding (seams), die casting (brackets), adhesive bonding (composites).
- [x] **Crash Load Path**: Impact → Crumple Zone → Mount Brackets → Side Rails → Box Walls → Cell Array (must remain intact).
- [x] **Thermal Management Architectures**: Air-cooled (simple), Liquid-cooled (industry standard), Immersion-cooled (emerging).
- [x] **Cell-to-Pack Energy Density**: Determined by box mass fraction; target box mass < 15% of total pack mass.
- [x] **Insulation Resistance**: $R_{iso} \geq 100 \cdot V_{system}$ per UN R100.
- [x] **Standards to Remember**: ISO 20653 (IP), IEC 60664 (clearance/creepage), UN R100 (EV safety), ISO 16750-3 (vibration), Euro NCAP (crash).
- [x] **Always Apply**: Factor of Safety (1.5-2.0), list Given data explicitly, show all calculation steps, convert units carefully.
- [x] **Emerging Trend**: Cell-to-Pack (CTP) and Cell-to-Body (CTB) architectures are eliminating the traditional module enclosure, integrating the battery box into the vehicle floor pan (e.g., BYD Blade Battery, Tesla 4680 structural pack).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Battery Management: Bus Bar Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT631 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Battery Management: Bus Bar Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Electric and Hybrid Vehicles (EHVs)**, a **Bus Bar** is defined as a rigid or flexible metallic conductor — typically fabricated from **electrolytic-grade copper (Cu)** or **aluminum (Al)** — that is engineered to conduct substantial DC currents with minimum resistive loss, minimum voltage sag, and controlled thermal behaviour between the constituent cells, modules, and external power terminals of a **High-Voltage (HV) traction battery pack**.

Within the **Battery Management System (BMS)** architecture, bus bars perform three synchronous engineering functions:

1. **Electrical interconnection** — they physically and electrically join individual 18650/21700/prismatic/pouch cells (or pre-assembled modules) in **series, parallel, or series-parallel (S/P)** configurations to realize the required pack voltage and energy capacity.
2. **Current distribution** — they distribute the discharge and regenerative-charging currents uniformly across all parallel cell strings, minimizing **current imbalance**.
3. **Thermal conduction path** — they act as passive heat spreaders, conducting I²R-generated heat away from cell terminals toward the pack's cooling plates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Bus bar design is classified under Module-3 *Battery Management* and is treated as an **integration-level** topic that bridges electrochemistry, power electronics, and mechanical packaging.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners)

Imagine a battery pack as a **multi-storey residential building**, and each individual lithium-ion cell as a **single apartment** in that building. The bus bar is analogous to the **main water pipeline (or a wide arterial road)** that runs vertically through every floor:

- Each cell (apartment) supplies or absorbs electrical energy (water flow).
- A thin wire would be like a narrow pipe — it overheats, leaks pressure (voltage), and breaks.
- A **wide, low-resistance bus bar** is like a **thick, well-insulated main pipeline** — it carries huge flow (current) with negligible pressure drop (voltage drop) and does not heat up excessively.
- A **water (thermal) leak** in the analogy corresponds to **I²R heat loss** that must be safely dissipated.

So when KTU examiners say "design a bus bar," they are essentially asking: *"What pipe diameter and material will carry a given current flow without bursting or losing too much pressure?"*

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Resistivity of Copper (at 20 °C) | $\rho_{Cu}$ | $\mathbf{1.68 \times 10^{-8} \; \Omega \cdot m}$ |
| Resistivity of Aluminum (at 20 °C) | $\rho_{Al}$ | $\mathbf{2.65 \times 10^{-8} \; \Omega \cdot m}$ |
| Temperature coefficient of Cu | $\alpha_{Cu}$ | $\mathbf{3.93 \times 10^{-3} \; /^{\circ}C}$ |
| Temperature coefficient of Al | $\alpha_{Al}$ | $\mathbf{4.03 \times 10^{-3} \; /^{\circ}C}$ |
| Typical allowable current density (Cu) | $J$ | **3 – 8 A/mm²** (continuous) |
| Typical allowable current density (Al) | $J$ | **2 – 5 A/mm²** (continuous) |
| Free convection heat-transfer coefficient | $h_c$ | **5 – 25 W/m²·K** |

> [!NOTE]
> The current-density ranges above are *engineering-rule-of-thumb* values approved in industrial HV battery pack design. KTU numerical problems will usually specify a numerical $J$ value to eliminate ambiguity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Framework of Bus Bar Design

The design of a battery-pack bus bar proceeds in **seven ordered stages**. Each stage answers one engineering question, and the answers feed the next.

- **Step 1 — Load Enumeration**
  *Establish* the maximum continuous current $I_{cont}$ and peak pulse current $I_{peak}$ (during hard acceleration or regenerative braking). For an EV traction inverter, $I_{peak}$ can be **2× to 3×** $I_{cont}$ for 10 – 30 s pulses.

- **Step 2 — Material Selection**
  Copper is preferred for its **lower resistivity, superior thermal conductivity (~400 W/m·K), and higher mechanical strength**. Aluminum is chosen when **mass minimization** outweighs the cost of the larger cross-section (aerospace and certain performance EVs).

- **Step 3 — Current-Density Budgeting**
  Choose an *allowable* current density $J_{allow}$ that ensures the steady-state temperature rise $\Delta T$ of the bus bar stays below **30 °C – 40 °C** above ambient (per ISO 6469-1 and most OEM specs).

- **Step 4 — Cross-Section Sizing (Ampacity Criterion)**
  Compute the minimum cross-sectional area using the **ampacity (current-carrying capacity) equation**:
  $$A_{min} \;=\; \frac{I_{cont}}{J_{allow}}$$

- **Step 5 — Voltage-Drop Verification**
  Compute the DC resistance and verify the percentage voltage drop stays within **1 % – 2 %** of the pack terminal voltage. This is critical for **State-of-Charge (SoC) estimation accuracy** because the BMS infers SoC partly from terminal voltage.

- **Step 6 — Thermal Verification**
  Equate the Joule heat generated to the heat dissipated by free/forced convection and solve for the steady-state temperature rise $\Delta T$. If $\Delta T$ exceeds the limit, **increase the cross-section, change the material, or add active cooling**.

- **Step 7 — Mechanical & Manufacturing Verification**
  Check bending stress, vibration tolerance (per **ISO 12405 / UN 38.3**), and feasibility of joining methods — **ultrasonic welding, laser welding, or torque-controlled bolting**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Why** behind Step 5 is subtle but exam-relevant: a high bus-bar resistance causes a **DC offset error** in the BMS voltage sensor, leading to SoC mis-estimation and accelerated cell ageing.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A \;=\; \dfrac{I}{J}$ | Cross-sectional area from allowable current density | $\text{m}^2$ or $\text{mm}^2$ |
| 2 | $R_{DC} \;=\; \dfrac{\rho L}{A}$ | DC resistance of a uniform conductor | $\Omega$ |
| 3 | $V_{drop} \;=\; I \cdot R_{DC} \;=\; \dfrac{I \rho L}{A}$ | Resistive (ohmic) voltage drop | $\text{V}$ |
| 4 | $P_{loss} \;=\; I^{2} R_{DC} \;=\; \dfrac{I^{2} \rho L}{A}$ | Joule (I²R) power loss | $\text{W}$ |
| 5 | $Q_{gen} \;=\; P_{loss}$ | Heat generation rate | $\text{W}$ |
| 6 | $\Delta T \;=\; \dfrac{I^{2} \rho L}{A \cdot h_c \cdot A_s}$ | Steady-state temperature rise (convection only) | °C or K |
| 7 | $\delta \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \rho}{\omega \mu}}$ | Skin depth (relevant for AC ripple on DC bus) | $\text{m}$ |
| 8 | $\rho(T) \;=\; \rho_{0}\,\bigl[1 + \alpha (T - T_{0})\bigr]$ | Resistivity at operating temperature $T$ | $\Omega \cdot \text{m}$ |
| 9 | $\sigma_{b} \;=\; \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I_{xx}} \;=\; \dfrac{6 M}{b h^{2}}$ | Max bending stress (rectangular section, $b$ × $h$) | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 10 | $m_{bus} \;=\; \rho_{mass} \cdot A \cdot L$ | Mass of bus bar ($\rho_{mass}$ = mass density) | $\text{kg}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> In all numerical KTU problems, treat $h_c$ as a **given constant** (e.g., 15 W/m²·K for natural convection, 50 – 200 W/m²·K for forced air). Do **not** derive it from correlations unless the question explicitly says so.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tesla Model 3/Y**: Uses a laminated flexible bus-bar harness (epoxy-encapsulated copper braid) for module-to-module links, balancing thermal expansion and ease of robotic assembly.
- **Tata Nexon EV / MG ZS EV**: Rigid nickel-plated copper bus bars with laser-welded terminations, achieving sub-1 % voltage drop at 200 A continuous.
- **Formula-E race packs**: Aluminium bus bars (lower mass) with forced-liquid cooling plates bonded to the bar surface for sub-50 °C operation at 600 A peak.

The bus bar, although a *passive* component, dictates the **continuous power rating, peak-power capability, cycle life, and safety** of the entire pack.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section presents **exhaustive derivations** of the governing equations used in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE problems on bus bar design. Every algebraic step is written out explicitly.

## 3.1 Derivation of the Ampacity Equation (Cross-Section from Current Density)

**Starting premise:** A bus bar is rated to carry a continuous current $I$ without exceeding a designer-specified current density $J_{allow}$.

**Step 1** — Current density is, by definition, current per unit cross-sectional area:

$$J \;=\; \frac{I}{A}$$

**Step 2** — Rearrange to solve for the area:

$$A \;=\; \frac{I}{J}$$

**Step 3** — Equating the operating current density to the allowable value gives the *minimum* area that satisfies the thermal/mechanical limit:

$$A_{min} \;=\; \frac{I_{cont}}{J_{allow}}$$

> *Logical check:* Larger $J_{allow}$ (more aggressive cooling) ⇒ smaller required area. ✓ Dimensionally: $A$ has units of $\text{m}^2$ if $I$ is in A and $J$ in $\text{A/m}^2$. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation of Voltage Drop Across a Bus Bar

**Step 1** — The DC resistance of a uniform conductor of length $L$, cross-section $A$, and resistivity $\rho$ is:

$$R_{DC} \;=\; \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$

**Step 2** — By Ohm's law, the ohmic voltage drop when current $I$ flows is:

$$V_{drop} \;=\; I \cdot R_{DC}$$

**Step 3** — Substitute the resistance expression:

$$V_{drop} \;=\; I \cdot \frac{\rho L}{A} \;=\; \frac{I \rho L}{A}$$

**Step 4** — The percentage voltage drop with respect to the pack terminal voltage $V_{pack}$ is:

$$\% V_{drop} \;=\; \frac{V_{drop}}{V_{pack}} \times 100 \;=\; \frac{I \rho L}{A \cdot V_{pack}} \times 100$$

> *Engineering check:* For a 400 V pack carrying 200 A through 0.3 m of copper bus bar with $A = 50 \text{ mm}^2$, we get $V_{drop} \approx 20 \text{ mV}$, i.e., **0.005 %** — well within the 1 – 2 % budget.

## 3.3 Derivation of Steady-State Temperature Rise (Thermal Sub-System)

**Step 1** — Energy balance at steady state: *heat generated = heat dissipated*:

$$Q_{gen} \;=\; Q_{diss}$$

**Step 2** — Heat generated is the Joule loss in the bar:

$$Q_{gen} \;=\; I^{2} R_{DC} \;=\; \frac{I^{2} \rho L}{A}$$

**Step 3** — Heat dissipated by free convection from the bar's surface (Newton's law of cooling) is:

$$Q_{diss} \;=\; h_c \cdot A_s \cdot \Delta T$$

where $A_s$ is the lateral (cooling) surface area of the bar and $\Delta T = T_{bar} - T_{ambient}$.

**Step 4** — Equate the two:

$$\frac{I^{2} \rho L}{A} \;=\; h_c \, A_s \, \Delta T$$

**Step 5** — Solve for $\Delta T$:

$$\Delta T \;=\; \frac{I^{2} \rho L}{A \cdot h_c \cdot A_s}$$

**Step 6** — For a rectangular bar of width $b$ and height $h$ (with $A = bh$ and $A_s \approx 2(b+h)L$):

$$\Delta T \;=\; \frac{I^{2} \rho}{b h \cdot h_c \cdot 2(b + h)}$$

> *Logical check:* Doubling the current quadruples $\Delta T$ (because $I^2$ is in the numerator). Doubling the cross-section halves $\Delta T$. ✓

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Full KTU-Style Design)

**Problem statement:** Design a **copper** bus bar for an EV battery pack with the following specifications:

| Given Parameter | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Continuous current | $I_{cont}$ | **500 A** |
| Bus bar length (cell-to-terminal) | $L$ | **0.20 m** |
| Allowable current density (Cu, natural convection) | $J_{allow}$ | **5 A/mm²** |
| Convective heat-transfer coefficient | $h_c$ | **15 W/m²·K** |
| Maximum allowable temperature rise | $\Delta T_{max}$ | **40 °C** |
| Resistivity of Cu at 20 °C | $\rho$ | $1.68 \times 10^{-8} \; \Omega\!\cdot\!\text{m}$ |

**Sub-task (a) — Find the minimum cross-sectional area:**

Apply Equation 1:

$$A_{min} \;=\; \frac{I_{cont}}{J_{allow}} \;=\; \frac{500 \; \text{A}}{5 \; \text{A/mm}^2} \;=\; 100 \; \text{mm}^2$$

Choose a practical rectangular cross-section: $\;b = 10 \; \text{mm},\; h = 10 \; \text{mm}\;$ (giving $A = 100 \; \text{mm}^2$). ✓

**Sub-task (b) — Compute the DC resistance and the percentage voltage drop (assume $V_{pack} = 400$ V):**

$$R_{DC} \;=\; \frac{\rho L}{A} \;=\; \frac{1.68 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.20}{100 \times 10^{-6}} \;=\; 3.36 \times 10^{-5} \; \Omega$$

$$V_{drop} \;=\; I \cdot R_{DC} \;=\; 500 \times 3.36 \times 10^{-5} \;=\; 1.68 \times 10^{-2} \; \text{V} \;=\; 16.8 \; \text{mV}$$

$$\% V_{drop} \;=\; \frac{16.8 \times 10^{-3}}{400} \times 100 \;=\; 4.2 \times 10^{-3} \;\%$$

This is **negligibly small** and well within the 1 – 2 % KTU/OEM budget. ✓

**Sub-task (c) — Compute the steady-state temperature rise:**

Surface area of the bar: $A_s = 2(b + h) \cdot L = 2(0.010 + 0.010) \times 0.20 = 8.0 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^2$.

Power loss: $P_{loss} = I^2 R = 500^2 \times 3.36 \times 10^{-5} = 8.4 \; \text{W}$.

$$\Delta T \;=\; \frac{P_{loss}}{h_c \cdot A_s} \;=\; \frac{8.4}{15 \times 8.0 \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; 70 \;^{\circ}\text{C}$$

> ⚠ **Design conflict:** $\Delta T = 70 \;^{\circ}\text{C}$ **exceeds** the 40 °C limit. The 10 × 10 mm bar is electrically sufficient but **thermically inadequate** in free convection.

**Sub-task (d) — Redesign to satisfy $\Delta T \leq 40 \;^{\circ}\text{C}$:**

Iterate on cross-section. Try $b = 10 \; \text{mm},\; h = 16 \; \text{mm}$ ⇒ $A = 160 \; \text{mm}^2$:

- $R_{DC} = (1.68 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.20) / (160 \times 10^{-6}) = 2.1 \times 10^{-5} \; \Omega$
- $P_{loss} = 500^2 \times 2.1 \times 10^{-5} = 5.25 \; \text{W}$
- $A_s = 2(0.010 + 0.016) \times 0.20 = 1.04 \times 10^{-2} \; \text{m}^2$
- $\Delta T = 5.25 / (15 \times 1.04 \times 10^{-2}) = 33.6 \;^{\circ}\text{C}$  ✓

**Final design:** A **10 mm × 16 mm copper bus bar**, $\Delta T = 33.6 \;^{\circ}\text{C}$, $V_{drop} = 10.5 \; \text{mV}$.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix (Decision Map)

| Design Driver | Equation to Consult | If Violated, Action |
|---|---|---|
| Current-carrying capacity (ampacity) | $A = I/J$ | Increase $A$ or switch to Cu |
| Voltage drop (BMS accuracy) | $V_{drop} = I\rho L/A$ | Increase $A$ or shorten $L$ |
| Steady-state temperature rise | $\Delta T = I^2 \rho L / (A h_c A_s)$ | Increase $A$, add cooling, or switch material |
| Skin-effect loss (AC ripple) | $\delta = \sqrt{2\rho/(\omega\mu)}$ | Use stranded/litz conductors or thin laminations |
| Mechanical bending | $\sigma_b = 6M/(bh^2)$ | Increase $h$ (depth in bending direction) |
| Mass budget | $m = \rho_{mass} A L$ | Switch to Al if mass-constrained |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

Below are two Mermaid diagrams designed strictly within the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE rendering rules: every node identifier is alphanumeric-prefixed, labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text (no markdown formatting inside double quotes), and there are no reserved-keyword collisions.

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Bus Bar's Role in the BMS Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cellA1[Cell 1 Positive Terminal] --> bbS1
    cellA2[Cell 2 Positive Terminal] --> bbS1
    cellA3[Cell 3 Positive Terminal] --> bbS1
    cellA4[Cell 4 Positive Terminal] --> bbS1
    bbS1[Series Bus Bar Module A] --> modA[Module A Output]
    cellB1[Cell 5 Negative Terminal] --> bbP1
    cellB2[Cell 6 Negative Terminal] --> bbP1
    bbP1[Parallel Bus Bar String 1] --> modB[Module B Input]
    modA --> cont1[HV Contactor Positive]
    modB --> cont2[HV Contactor Negative]
    cont1 --> fuseprot[Pyro Fuse and Current Sensor]
    cont2 --> fuseprot
    fuseprot --> invout[Inverter DC Link Terminals]
    invout --> bmslink[BMS Master Controller]
    bmslink -. monitors .-> cellA1
    bmslink -. monitors .-> bbS1
```

*Reading guide:* The diagram traces the **electrical current path** from individual cells → series/parallel bus bars → module outputs → contactors → protection devices → inverter. The dotted arrows depict the BMS's *sensing* channel (voltage and temperature taps).

## 4.2 Bus Bar Design & Verification Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA1[Step 1: Define I continuous and I peak] --> stepA2[Step 2: Select Material Copper or Aluminium]
    stepA2 --> stepA3[Step 3: Choose J allowable from thermal limit]
    stepA3 --> stepA4[Step 4: Compute A min using A = I slash J]
    stepA4 --> stepA5[Step 5: Compute R DC using R = rho L over A]
    stepA5 --> stepA6[Step 6: Compute V drop and percent V drop]
    stepA6 --> stepA7{V drop within 1 to 2 percent}
    stepA7 -- No --> stepA8[Increase A or shorten L]
    stepA8 --> stepA5
    stepA7 -- Yes --> stepA9[Step 7: Compute Delta T from convection balance]
    stepA9 --> stepA10{Delta T within limit}
    stepA10 -- No --> stepA11[Increase A, switch to Cu, or add forced cooling]
    stepA11 --> stepA9
    stepA10 -- Yes --> stepA12[Step 8: Mechanical and vibration check]
    stepA12 --> stepA13[Step 9: Finalise cross-section, joining method, plating]
    stepA13 --> stepA14[Design Complete]
```

*Reading guide:* This is the **engineering decision tree** a KTU examiner expects you to know. The two **diamond decision blocks** are the critical gates (voltage-drop gate and temperature-rise gate) that determine whether iteration is required.

## 4.3 Cross-Section Geometry — Current Density and Heat-Flux Visual

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    width1[Width b of Bus Bar] --> geom1[Cross Section Area A = b times h]
    height1[Height h of Bus Bar] --> geom1
    geom1 --> geom2[Surface Area As = 2 times b plus h times L]
    geom2 --> conv[Heat Loss by Convection: Q = hc times As times Delta T]
    joule1[Joule Heat Q gen = I squared R DC] --> bal[Energy Balance at Steady State]
    conv --> bal
    bal --> delt1[Temperature Rise Delta T]
```
