# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Nail Penetration / Internal Short-Circuit Energy Audit

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **14-mark style numerical problem** that frequently appears in KTU examinations. Mastering it guarantees marks on thermal-runaway energy calculations.

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

A lithium-ion prismatic traction cell has the following specifications:

- Nominal voltage, $V_{nom} = 3.70 \text{ V}$
- Capacity, $C_{Ah} = 50 \text{ Ah}$
- Internal resistance at 25 °C, $R_{int} = 5.0 \text{ m}\Omega$
- Nail penetration triggers an internal short circuit with effective short resistance $R_{sc} = 5.0 \text{ m}\Omega$ at the moment of penetration
- Thermal mass of cell, $m c_p = 250 \text{ J/°C}$
- Ambient temperature, $T_{amb} = 25 \text{ °C}$

**Tasks:**
1. Compute the total electrical energy stored in the cell (in Wh and kJ).
2. Compute the peak short-circuit current at the moment of penetration.
3. Compute the Joule heat dissipated in the first $t = 10 \text{ s}$ of the short.
4. Assuming **all** this heat is absorbed by the cell mass (worst-case adiabatic), compute the temperature rise.
5. State whether this will trigger thermal runaway, given the SEI decomposition threshold is **120 °C**.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Exhaustive Solution

---

**Step 1 — Compute Total Electrical Energy Stored in the Cell**

The energy stored in a rechargeable cell is the product of its nominal voltage and its charge capacity:

$$E_{cell} = V_{nom} \times C_{Ah}$$

Substitute the given values (the voltage unit is volts, the capacity unit is amp-hours, the product yields watt-hours):

$$E_{cell} = 3.70 \text{ V} \times 50 \text{ Ah} = 185 \text{ Wh}$$

Convert watt-hours to Joules using $1 \text{ Wh} = 3600 \text{ J}$:

$$E_{Joules} = 185 \text{ Wh} \times 3600 \text{ J/Wh} = 666{,}000 \text{ J} = 666 \text{ kJ}$$

> **Result 1:** $E_{cell} = 185 \text{ Wh} = 666 \text{ kJ}$

---

**Step 2 — Compute Peak Short-Circuit Current**

At the instant of internal short-circuit, the Open-Circuit Voltage (OCV) drives current through the short resistance. Ohm's law applies:

$$I_{sc} = \frac{V_{ocv}}{R_{sc}}$$

Substitute values (using nominal voltage as the worst-case open-circuit voltage):

$$I_{sc} = \frac{3.70 \text{ V}}{5.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ }\Omega} = 740 \text{ A}$$

> **Result 2:** Peak short-circuit current $I_{sc} = 740 \text{ A}$

This is an extremely large instantaneous current — equivalent to **~14.8 C** for a 50 Ah cell. The corresponding power dissipation is:

$$P_{peak} = I_{sc}^2 \times R_{sc} = (740)^2 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-3} = 2738 \text{ W} \approx 2.74 \text{ kW}$$

---

**Step 3 — Joule Heat Dissipated in the First 10 Seconds**

Assuming the current remains approximately constant (valid only over a short window since the cell discharges):

$$Q_{heat} = I_{sc}^2 \times R_{sc} \times t$$

Substitute:

$$Q_{heat} = (740)^2 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 10$$

$$Q_{heat} = 547{,}600 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 10$$

$$Q_{heat} = 27{,}380 \text{ J} = 27.38 \text{ kJ}$$

> **Result 3:** $Q_{heat} = 27.38 \text{ kJ}$ in the first 10 s

As a sanity check, note that this represents only $\frac{27.38}{666} \approx 4.11\%$ of the cell's total stored energy. In reality, the short-circuit current drops as the cell voltage sags, so the actual heat may be lower, but the *peak* heating rate (kW-level) is more than sufficient to raise local temperature above separator melt.

---

**Step 4 — Adiabatic Temperature Rise**

In the worst-case adiabatic scenario, all the heat stays within the cell body:

$$\Delta T = \frac{Q_{heat}}{m c_p}$$

Substitute:

$$\Delta T = \frac{27{,}380 \text{ J}}{250 \text{ J/°C}} = 109.5 \text{ °C}$$

Final cell temperature:

$$T_{final} = T_{amb} + \Delta T = 25 + 109.5 = 134.5 \text{ °C}$$

> **Result 4:** $T_{final} \approx 134.5 \text{ °C}$

---

**Step 5 — Triggering of Thermal Runaway**

Compare against the SEI decomposition threshold $T_{SEI} = 120 \text{ °C}$:

$$T_{final} = 134.5 \text{ °C} > T_{SEI} = 120 \text{ °C}$$

The cell temperature crosses the **SEI decomposition limit** within the first 10 s of internal short. This initiates the cascade: SEI decomposition → anode-electrolyte reaction → separator melt → internal short escalation → cathode oxygen release → thermal runaway.

> **Result 5:** YES, thermal runaway will trigger within 10 seconds.

---

### 3.1.3 Analytical Matrix — Test Energy Budgeting

| Test | Failure Mechanism | Approximate Energy Released (typical 50 Ah cell) | Pass / Fail Indicator |
|---|---|---|---|
| Overcharge (T7) | Electrolyte oxidation, lithium plating | 50–150 kJ | Cell surface temp ≤ 170 °C |
| External short (T5) | I²R heating | 20–40 kJ | No fire within 6 hr |
| Nail penetration (T6) | Internal short + separator melt | 25–60 kJ | No fire, no explosion |
| Crush | Mechanical short + cell deformation | 10–30 kJ | No fire within 1 hr |
| Hot box (150 °C) | SEI decomposition + exothermic reactions | Trigger energy ~ 20–50 kJ | No thermal runaway |
| Thermal cycling | Electrode expansion mismatch | Cyclic fatigue | < 5 % capacity loss |

---

## 3.2 Derivative Result — Minimum External Short Resistance to Avoid Thermal Runaway

Suppose we want the cell temperature rise in **10 s** to be **strictly less than 95 °C** (so that $T_{final} < 120 \text{ °C}$). Find the maximum permissible short resistance.

**Setting up the inequality:**

$$Q_{heat} < \Delta T_{limit} \times m c_p$$

$$I_{sc}^2 \times R_{sc} \times t < \Delta T_{limit} \times m c_p$$

$$\left(\frac{V_{ocv}}{R_{sc}}\right)^2 \times R_{sc} \times t < \Delta T_{limit} \times m c_p$$

$$\frac{V_{ocv}^2 \cdot t}{R_{sc}} < \Delta T_{limit} \times m c_p$$

Solving for $R_{sc,min}$:

$$R_{sc,min} > \frac{V_{ocv}^2 \cdot t}{\Delta T_{limit} \times m c_p}$$

Substitute $V_{ocv} = 3.7 \text{ V}$, $t = 10 \text{ s}$, $\Delta T_{limit} = 95 \text{ °C}$, $m c_p = 250 \text{ J/°C}$:

$$R_{sc,min} > \frac{(3.7)^2 \times 10}{95 \times 250}$$

$$R_{sc,min} > \frac{13.69 \times 10}{23{,}750}$$

$$R_{sc,min} > \frac{136.9}{23{,}750} = 5.77 \times 10^{-3} \text{ }\Omega = 5.77 \text{ m}\Omega$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Any internal short with resistance **below 5.77 mΩ** will trigger thermal runaway within 10 s for this 50 Ah cell. This is precisely why BMS designs target sub-millisecond detection of any abnormal current path.

> **Q1. Define UN 38.3. List any four tests prescribed under it and their pass criteria.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Definition (1 mark):** UN 38.3 is the *United Nations Recommendations on the Transport of Dangerous Goods, Section 38.3*, which prescribes the mandatory safety testing protocol that all lithium-ion cells and batteries must pass before being transported by air, sea, or road.
>
> **Four tests with pass criteria (2 marks):**
> 1. **T1 – Altitude Simulation** at 11.6 kPa for 6 hours. Pass: no mass loss, leakage, fire, or rupture.
> 2. **T3 – Vibration** sweep 7 Hz → 200 Hz, 12 cycles across three orthogonal axes. Pass: no leakage, fire, or rupture.
> 3. **T5 – External Short Circuit** at 50 mΩ, 1 hour, 55 °C. Pass: no fire, no rupture, surface temp ≤ 170 °C.
> 4. **T6 – Impact** (9.1 kg hammer dropped from 61 cm). Pass: no fire, no rupture.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
>
> **Q2. Explain the role of AIS-156 (Rev 3) in standardizing battery safety for electric vehicles in India.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> AIS-156 (Automotive Industry Standard – 156, Revision 3) is issued by the **Automotive Research Association of India (ARAI)** under the Ministry of Road Transport and Highways. It applies to **L, M, and N category vehicles** with electric powertrains.
>
> **Key mandates (any three):**
> 1. **IP67** ingress protection for the traction battery enclosure (against dust and temporary immersion).
> 2. **Thermal runaway propagation containment** – cell-to-cell propagation must not lead to pack-level fire within 5 minutes.
> 3. **BMS functional requirements** – cell-level voltage, temperature, and current monitoring with fault logging.
> 4. **Crash safety** alignment with UN R100 and AIS-004.
> 5. **Venting and gas management** to prevent explosive gas buildup inside the pack enclosure.
>
> Without AIS-156 compliance, an EV cannot receive type-approval certification under the **Central Motor Vehicles Rules (CMVR)** for Indian roads.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)
### Choice A — *(Module 3 / Battery Safety Standards)*

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
>
> **Question A:**
>
> **(a)** Discuss in detail the **UN 38.3 transportation testing requirements** for lithium-ion cells used in electric vehicles. List **all 8 tests** with conditions and acceptance criteria. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **BMS safety functions** mandated by **ISO 26262 ASIL C/D** for EV traction battery packs. *(7 marks)*

### Model Answer — Question A (a) — UN 38.3 Transportation Tests (7 marks)

UN 38.3 was developed by the UN Sub-Committee of Experts on the Transport of Dangerous Goods. It applies to **all lithium cells and batteries** offered for international transport. The standard has **8 sequential tests (T1–T8)**. Cells must pass every test in the order listed.

**Tests T1 to T4 — Environmental / Mechanical Simulation** *(4 marks)*

| Test | Conditions | Pass Criteria |
|---|---|---|
| **T1 Altitude Simulation** | 11.6 kPa for ≥ 6 hr at 20 ± 5 °C (simulates unpressurized air cargo hold) | No mass loss > 0.1 %, no leakage, venting, disassembly, rupture, or fire |
| **T2 Thermal Test** | 10 cycles of –40 °C (≥ 6 hr) and +75 °C (≥ 6 hr), 30 min transition max | Same as T1 |
| **T3 Vibration** | Sinusoidal sweep 7 Hz → 200 Hz → 7 Hz in 15 min, repeated 12 times per axis (3 axes = 36 total sweeps), 12 g peak | Same |
| **T4 Shock** | Half-sine pulse, 150 g / 6 ms for large cells (≥ 12 kg pack), 50 g / 11 ms for small cells, 3 pulses per direction × 6 directions | Same |

**Tests T5 to T8 — Electrical / Forced Abuse** *(3 marks)*

| Test | Conditions | Pass Criteria |
|---|---|---|
| **T5 External Short Circuit** | 50 ± 10 mΩ across terminals at 55 ± 2 °C for 1 hr | Surface temperature ≤ 170 °C; no fire, no rupture within 6 hr of observation |
| **T6 Impact** | 9.1 kg mass dropped from 61 ± 2.5 cm onto cylindrical cell (longitudinal axis), 15.9 mm dia. steel bar | No fire, no rupture |
| **T6 (Alt) Crush** | For prismatic/pouch, hydraulic press at 13 ± 1 kN | No fire, no rupture |
| **T7 Overcharge** | Charge at 2 × manufacturer's recommended max continuous charge current for 24 hr | No fire, no rupture |
| **T8 Forced Discharge** | Connect each cell to a 12 V DC supply in reverse direction for 24 hr | No fire, no rupture |

**Acceptance summary:** All 8 tests must be passed sequentially. If any single test fails, the cell type is barred from transport. Cells must be retested every year and after any design or manufacturing change.

---

### Model Answer — Question A (b) — BMS Functions per ISO 26262 ASIL C/D (7 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write generic BMS descriptions (cell balancing, SOC estimation) instead of the **safety-critical functions** mandated by **ISO 26262**. Marks are awarded specifically for ASIL-rated *safety* functions.

ISO 26262 governs the **functional safety** of E/E systems in road vehicles. For traction batteries, the target ASIL is usually **C or D**. The BMS must implement the following safety functions:

**Block Diagram of BMS Safety Architecture (3 marks):**

```mermaid
graph TD
    V[V Pack Voltage Sense] --> ADC1
    I[I Current Hall Sensor] --> ADC2
    TC[Temperature NTC per cell] --> ADC3
    ADC1 --> MCU[Safety MCU Lockstep]
    ADC2 --> MCU
    ADC3 --> MCU
    MCU --> OVP[Over Voltage Cutoff]
    MCU --> UVP[Under Voltage Cutoff]
    MCU --> OCP[Over Current Cutoff]
    MCU --> OTP[Over Temperature Cutoff]
    MCU --> ISO[Insulation Monitoring]
    MCU --> CONT[Contactor Driver]
    CONT --> HVDC[High Voltage Bus]
    MCU --> DIAG[Diagnostic Fault Memory]
    DIAG --> CAN[CAN Output to VCU]
```

**Safety Functions List (4 marks):**

1. **Over-Voltage Protection (OVP)** – if any cell exceeds 4.25 V, contactors must open within < 100 ms. ASIL C.
2. **Under-Voltage Protection (UVP)** – if any cell drops below 2.5 V, the BMS must disable drive torque. ASIL C.
3. **Over-Current Protection (OCP)** – I²t trip characteristic; both charge and discharge paths. ASIL C.
4. **Over-Temperature Protection (OTP)** – per-cell NTC monitoring; thermal runaway prediction at 60 °C warning, 70 °C derating, 80 °C shutdown. ASIL D.
5. **Insulation Monitoring** – detects high-voltage isolation breakdown to chassis; triggers HVIL (High-Voltage Interlock Loop) fault. ASIL D.
6. **Contactor Control** – pre-charge circuit, weld detection (auxiliary contacts), redundant contactors. ASIL D.
7. **Diagnostic Coverage** – all analog inputs cross-checked by redundant pair (lockstep ARM cores). Diagnostic trouble codes stored in non-volatile memory per ISO 14229 (UDS).
8. **Communication Fault** – CAN timeout triggers safe state (open contactors). ASIL C.

Each safety function must have a **Safety Goal**, a **Failure Mode**, and a **FTTI (Fault Tolerant Time Interval)** defined in the Safety Case document.

---

### Choice B — *(Module 3 / Battery Safety Standards)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
>
> **Question B:**
>
> **(a)** Discuss the **ISO 12405** and **AIS-156** standards for EV traction battery packs. Highlight the differences between **cell-level, module-level, and pack-level testing**. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A lithium-ion cell rated **3.6 V / 60 Ah** undergoes a **nail penetration test** at 100 % SOC. The internal short resistance is **4 mΩ** and the thermal mass is $mc_p = 300 \text{ J/°C}$. Ambient temperature is **25 °C**. Calculate: **(i)** Total energy stored (Wh and kJ); **(ii)** Peak short-circuit current; **(iii)** Joule heat dissipated in the first 12 seconds; **(iv)** Final temperature assuming adiabatic heating. Does the cell reach **thermal runaway** if the SEI decomposition threshold is **110 °C**? *(7 marks)*

### Model Answer — Question B (a) — ISO 12405 vs AIS-156 (7 marks)

**Common Foundation** *(1 mark)*

Both standards mandate safety verification of traction battery packs for electric road vehicles. Both emphasize cell, module, and pack hierarchy. Both require IP67 (or IPX7 minimum) ingress protection, vibration, shock, thermal cycling, and electrical abuse testing.

**ISO 12405 (International — European / Global OEM base)** *(3 marks)*

ISO 12405 is split into three parts:
- **ISO 12405-1** – high-power applications (PHEV, HEV).
- **ISO 12405-2** – high-energy applications (BEV).
- **ISO 12405-3** – safety performance requirements using a modular approach (cell, module, pack, system).

ISO 12405 mandates tests at all three levels but with **progressively relaxed acceptance criteria** as the level moves from cell to pack, because the BMS and pack architecture provide additional safety layers at higher integration levels.

**AIS-156 (India — ARAI / SIAM mandate)** *(3 marks)*

AIS-156 (Rev 3) is specifically required under CMVR for **type approval of electric L, M, and N category vehicles**. Key India-specific requirements beyond ISO 12405:
- **AIS-156 thermal propagation test** — single cell thermal runaway must NOT propagate to neighbouring cells within **5 minutes**.
- **AIS-156 BMS logging** — mandatory storage of fault codes for the last 100 drive cycles, accessible via OBD-II.
- **AIS-156 altitude** — explicitly references Indian Himalayan conditions (up to 5000 m).
- **AIS-156 monsoon** — IP67 plus additional humidity test (95 % RH for 48 hr).

**Cell vs Module vs Pack Testing Summary:**

| Level | Tests | Acceptance Strictness |
|---|---|---|
| **Cell** | Mechanical abuse, thermal abuse, electrical abuse (UN 38.3 + IEC 62660) | Strictest — no fire/rupture permitted |
| **Module** | Limited set of mechanical tests, thermal propagation, electrical abuse with BMS active | Slightly relaxed if BMS contains the failure |
| **Pack** | Full set including propagation, IP67, crash, fire exposure | Failure modes allowed if contained within pack |

---

### Model Answer — Question B (b) — Numerical on Nail Penetration Test (7 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget to convert **Wh to Joules** by multiplying by 3600. They also frequently confuse *peak current* with *steady-state current*. Marks are lost on these two points.

**Given:**
- $V_{nom} = 3.6 \text{ V}$
- $C_{Ah} = 60 \text{ Ah}$
- $R_{sc} = 4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ }\Omega$
- $m c_p = 300 \text{ J/°C}$
- $T_{amb} = 25 \text{ °C}$
- $t = 12 \text{ s}$
- $T_{SEI} = 110 \text{ °C}$

**Part (i) — Total Energy Stored** *(2 marks)*

Compute energy in watt-hours using $E = V \times C$:

$$E = 3.6 \text{ V} \times 60 \text{ Ah} = 216 \text{ Wh}$$

Convert to Joules:

$$E_J = 216 \times 3600 = 777{,}600 \text{ J} = 777.6 \text{ kJ}$$

**Part (ii) — Peak Short-Circuit Current** *(1.5 marks)*

Using Ohm's law:

$$I_{sc} = \frac{V_{ocv}}{R_{sc}} = \frac{3.6}{4 \times 10^{-3}} = 900 \text{ A}$$

**Part (iii) — Joule Heat in First 12 Seconds** *(1.5 marks)*

Assuming approximately constant current over the short interval:

$$Q = I_{sc}^2 \times R_{sc} \times t$$

$$Q = (900)^2 \times 4 \times 10^{-3} \times 12$$

$$Q = 810{,}000 \times 4 \times 10^{-3} \times 12$$

$$Q = 810{,}000 \times 0.048 = 38{,}880 \text{ J} = 38.88 \text{ kJ}$$

**Part (iv) — Adiabatic Temperature Rise and Runaway Verdict** *(2 marks)*

Temperature rise:

$$\Delta T = \frac{Q}{m c_p} = \frac{38{,}880}{300} = 129.6 \text{ °C}$$

Final temperature:

$$T_{final} = T_{amb} + \Delta T = 25 + 129.6 = 154.6 \text{ °C}$$

Comparison with SEI threshold:

$$T_{final} = 154.6 \text{ °C} > T_{SEI} = 110 \text{ °C}$$

**Verdict:** The cell temperature crosses the SEI decomposition limit **by a margin of 44.6 °C**, which will almost certainly initiate thermal runaway within 12 seconds. The nail penetration test will likely be **failed** under UN 38.3 T6 / IEC 62660-2 / AIS-156 acceptance criteria.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for Battery Safety Standards**
>
> - **Three-tier hierarchy:** Cell (IEC 62660) → Module → Pack (ISO 12405 / UL 2580 / AIS-156).
> - **UN 38.3 T1–T8:** Memorize T1 = Altitude (11.6 kPa), T2 = Thermal, T3 = Vibration, T4 = Shock, T5 = External Short (50 mΩ, 55 °C), T6 = Impact/Crush (9.1 kg / 61 cm), T7 = Overcharge (2× Imax, 24 hr), T8 = Forced Discharge.
> - **AIS-156 (Rev 3) India-specific mandates:** IP67, thermal propagation containment (5 min), BMS fault logging.
> - **ISO 26262 ASIL C/D** governs BMS functional safety.
> - **Thermal runaway threshold:** SEI decomposition at **90–120 °C**; cathode oxygen release at **180–250 °C**; separator melt at **130–160 °C**.
> - **Charge cutoff voltage:** **4.20 V (NMC)** / **4.35 V (NCA)**.
> - **Discharge cutoff:** **2.5–2.8 V**.
> - **Nail penetration energy formula:** $Q = I_{sc}^2 R_{sc} t$ with $I_{sc} = V_{ocv}/R_{sc}$.
> - **Adiabatic temperature rise:** $\Delta T = Q / (m c_p)$.
> - **Cell-level standards do not test thermal propagation** — only pack-level standards do (ISO 12405-3, AIS-156, UL 2580, GB/T 31467).
> - **For an exam:** Always quote the **specific test code (T1–T8)**, **temperature thresholds (°C)**, and **resistance values (mΩ)** — this shows examiner-level precision and earns full marks.
> - **Functional safety flow:** Hazard Analysis → Safety Goal → Functional Safety Requirement → Technical Safety Requirement → ASIL allocation (A → B → C → D).
> - **Final energy conversion trick:** $1 \text{ Wh} = 3600 \text{ J}$. Forgetting this loses 1 full mark in any numerical.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Electronics: Inverters, Converters, and Controllers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT631 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Power Electronics: Inverters, Converters, and Controllers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Power Electronics: Inverters, Converters, and Controllers

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power Electronics** is the discipline concerned with the controlled conversion, conditioning, and management of electrical power using semiconductor switching devices. In the context of **Electric and Hybrid Electric Vehicles (EVs/HEVs)**, power electronics serves as the *energy-routing nervous system*, interfacing the **energy source** (battery / fuel cell / ultracapacitor), the **electric traction machine**, and the **auxiliary loads**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, three functional building blocks dominate the powertrain electronics:

| Block | Function | Typical Position in EV |
|---|---|---|
| **DC–DC Converter** | Steps battery voltage up or down | Between battery pack and DC-link / auxiliary bus |
| **DC–AC Inverter** | Converts DC battery bus to variable-frequency AC | Between DC-link and traction motor |
| **AC–DC Rectifier (Charger)** | Converts grid AC to DC for battery charging | On-board or off-board charging unit |

The **Controller** is the supervisory digital logic (DSP / FPGA / MCU) that generates the gating signals (PWM) to these converters/inverters based on torque/speed commands from the driver and feedback from current/voltage/speed sensors.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Power Translator":**
> Think of the EV powertrain as a conversation between three people speaking three different languages:
> - The **battery** speaks *DC* (low voltage, steady tone).
> - The **traction motor** (induction or PMSM) speaks *AC* (variable frequency, "voice pitch" = speed).
> - The **driver's pedal** speaks *analog wish* (torque request).
>
> The **Power Electronic Converter** is the *live interpreter* translating one language to another, while the **Controller** is the *interpreter's brain* that decides *what* to say based on context. Without this translator, no useful mechanical motion is possible.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in EV Power Electronics

- **Switching frequency ($f_{sw}$):** Typically **$\mathbf{5 \text{ kHz} – 20 \text{ kHz}}$** for IGBT-based traction inverters; **$\mathbf{>50 \text{ kHz}}$** for SiC/GaN MOSFETs.
- **DC-link voltage ($V_{dc}$):** **$\mathbf{200 \text{ V} – 800 \text{ V}}$** depending on vehicle class.
- **Power rating:** **$\mathbf{30 \text{ kW} – 250 \text{ kW}}$** per traction inverter.
- **Efficiency target:** **$\mathbf{>95\%}$** at rated load (DOE 2025 targets demand **$\mathbf{>98\%}$**).

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 DC–DC Converters in EV Powertrains

#### (a) Buck Converter (Step-Down)

Used to step down high-voltage DC-link to lower-voltage auxiliary buses (**$\mathbf{12 \text{ V}, 48 \text{ V}}$**) for lighting, ECUs, infotainment.

- **Operational Logic (Continuous Conduction Mode, CCM):**
  1. Switch **S** ON for time $DT_s$ → inductor charges, current ramps up.
  2. Switch **S** OFF for time $(1-D)T_s$ → inductor releases stored energy through freewheeling diode.
  3. Capacitor smooths output ripple.

- **Voltage Conversion Ratio:**

$$
\frac{V_o}{V_s} = D \quad ; \quad 0 \le D \le 1
$$

where $D$ is the duty cycle.

#### (b) Boost Converter (Step-Up)

Used to step up battery voltage (e.g., **$\mathbf{200 \text{ V}}$** pack → **$\mathbf{400 \text{ V}}$** DC-link) or to interface low-voltage sources to high-voltage buses.

- **Voltage Conversion Ratio:**

$$
\frac{V_o}{V_s} = \frac{1}{1-D}
$$

#### (c) Bidirectional DC–DC Converter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **most EV-critical topology**. During **motoring**, energy flows *battery → DC-link* (boost mode). During **regenerative braking**, energy flows *DC-link → battery* (buck mode). Two IGBT/MOSFET legs with anti-parallel diodes accomplish this in a single hardware stage.

### 2.2 Inverters in EV Powertrains

#### (a) Voltage Source Inverter (VSI) — *The Industry Standard*

A **three-phase two-level VSI** is the heart of nearly every modern EV traction drive. It uses **$\mathbf{6}$** IGBTs (or MOSFETs) with anti-parallel diodes.

- **Phase Voltage (line-to-neutral, sinusoidal PWM):**

$$
V_{AN}(t) = \frac{V_{dc}}{2} \cdot m_a \cdot \sin(\omega t)
$$

where $m_a$ is the **modulation index** ($0 \le m_a \le 1$ for linear region; up to $\mathbf{1.15}$ in overmodulation).

- **Line-to-Line RMS Voltage:**

$$
V_{LL,\text{rms}} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2\sqrt{2}} \cdot m_a \cdot V_{dc} \approx 0.612 \, m_a \, V_{dc}
$$

- **Maximum obtainable line voltage:** $\mathbf{V_{LL,rms,max} = 0.612 \, V_{dc}}$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** In SVPWM (Space Vector PWM) — *predominant in modern EVs* — the DC-link utilization improves by $\mathbf{\approx 15.47\%}$ over SPWM, giving $V_{LL,rms,max} \approx 0.707 \, V_{dc}$.

#### (b) PWM Techniques — Comparison

| Technique | DC-link Utilization | THD | Computational Load | EV Usage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Square-Wave (Six-Step)** | $\mathbf{0.78 \, V_{dc}}$ | High ($\sim$ 30%) | Minimal | Legacy, low-cost |
| **Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM)** | $0.612 \, V_{dc}$ | Moderate | Low | Rare in modern EVs |
| **SVPWM** | $0.707 \, V_{dc}$ | Low | Moderate | **Industry standard** |
| **Selective Harmonic Elimination (SHEPWM)** | Configurable | Very low | High (offline solving) | High-power HEVs |

### 2.3 Controllers for EV Drives

#### (a) V/F (Volts per Hertz) Control

Simple open-loop scalar control used for low-cost e-mobility (e-rickshaws, two-wheelers).

$$
\frac{V}{f} = \text{constant} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V = k \cdot f
$$

- Maintains **constant air-gap flux** in induction motor.
- **No torque loop** — poor dynamic response.

#### (b) Field-Oriented Control (FOC) / Vector Control

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The de-facto standard for modern EV traction drives** (used by Tesla Model S/3, Nissan Leaf, Toyota Prius).

Decouples torque ($T$) and flux ($\psi$) into orthogonal $d$-$q$ axes aligned with rotor flux:

$$
T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{L_m^2}{L_r} \cdot i_{ds} \cdot i_{qs}
$$

- $i_{ds}$ controls **flux** (held constant in base-speed region).
- $i_{qs}$ controls **torque** directly.
- Requires **Park/Clarke transforms** and accurate rotor position from encoder/resolver.

#### (c) Direct Torque Control (DTC)

- Hysteresis-based switching table — no PWM modulator required.
- **Faster torque response** than FOC (~ 1–2 ms).
- Higher torque/flux ripple — rarely used in pure EVs, common in some HEV traction drives.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Module 3

| # | Formula | Description | Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_o / V_s = D$ | Buck converter voltage ratio | DC–DC |
| 2 | $V_o / V_s = 1/(1-D)$ | Boost converter voltage ratio | DC–DC |
| 3 | $V_o / V_s = -D/(1-D)$ | Buck-Boost converter ratio | DC–DC |
| 4 | $\Delta I_L = V_s D (1-D) / (f_s L)$ | Inductor ripple current (Buck) | DC–DC |
| 5 | $V_{LL,rms} = (\sqrt{3}/2\sqrt{2})\, m_a V_{dc}$ | VSI line RMS (SPWM) | Inverter |
| 6 | $V_{LL,rms}^{max} = 0.707 V_{dc}$ | Maximum line RMS (SVPWM) | Inverter |
| 7 | $V_{dc} = (3\sqrt{3}/\pi) V_m$ | Six-step inverter DC-link relation | Inverter |
| 8 | $V/f = \text{const}$ | V/F scalar control law | Controller |
| 9 | $T_e = (3P L_m^2 / 4 L_r) \, i_{ds} i_{qs}$ | PMSM/IM torque (FOC) | Controller |
| 10 | $\eta_{sys} = \prod \eta_i$ | Cascaded efficiency | System |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Character Trap:** All absolute-value notations in derivations below use $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\text{mid}$ instead of the bare `\|` symbol to preserve KTU markdown table integrity.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Boost Converter Voltage Ratio in CCM

We apply the **volt-second balance** on the inductor over one switching period $T_s$ (the average voltage across $L$ in steady state must equal zero, otherwise current would ramp unboundedly).

**Step 1 — Define switching interval states:**

Let $D$ be the duty cycle (fraction of $T_s$ when switch S is ON).

- **Interval 1:** $0 \le t \le DT_s$ — switch ON; diode reverse-biased.
  Inductor sees the source voltage directly:

$$
v_L^{\text{ON}} = V_s
$$

- **Interval 2:** $DT_s \le t \le T_s$ — switch OFF; diode conducts.
  Inductor sees the difference between source and output (now aiding):

$$
v_L^{\text{OFF}} = V_s - V_o
$$

**Step 2 — Apply volt-second balance:**

$$
\int_0^{T_s} v_L(t) \, dt = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; V_s (DT_s) + (V_s - V_o)(1 - D)T_s = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Divide by $T_s$:**

$$
V_s D + (V_s - V_o)(1 - D) = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Expand the second term:**

$$
V_s D + V_s - V_s D - V_o + V_o D = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $V_s D$ from both ends:**

$$
V_s - V_o + V_o D = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; V_s = V_o (1 - D)
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the conversion ratio:**

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{V_o}{V_s} = \dfrac{1}{1 - D}}
$$

**Interpretation:** As $D \to 1$, the output ideally grows to infinity. In practice, parasitic resistances and inductor current limits cap this — a fact the KTU examiner frequently tests by asking *"why does a real boost converter not achieve infinite output?"*

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Three-Phase VSI Line Voltage Under SPWM

**Step 1 — Define the modulation index:**

$$
m_a = \dfrac{\hat{V}_{\text{control}}}{\hat{V}_{\text{tri}}}
$$

where $\hat{V}_{\text{tri}}$ is the triangular carrier peak and $\hat{V}_{\text{control}}$ is the sinusoidal reference peak.

**Step 2 — Fundamental phase voltage (line-to-neutral, per phase A):**

$$
\hat{V}_{AN,1} = \dfrac{m_a \, V_{dc}}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to RMS line-to-line fundamental:**

$$
V_{AB,1,\text{rms}} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \dfrac{V_{AN,1,\text{rms}}}{\text{?}}
$$

Let us walk carefully. The line-to-line voltage is $\sqrt{3}$ times the phase voltage (for a balanced star):

$$
\hat{V}_{AB,1} = \sqrt{3} \, \hat{V}_{AN,1} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \dfrac{m_a V_{dc}}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert peak to RMS:**

$$
V_{AB,1,\text{rms}} = \dfrac{\hat{V}_{AB,1}}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2\sqrt{2}} \, m_a \, V_{dc}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical constant:**

$$
\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{1.732}{2.828} \approx 0.612
$$

**Step 6 — Final boxed form:**

$$
\boxed{V_{LL,1,\text{rms}} \approx 0.612 \, m_a \, V_{dc}}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: FOC Torque Expression for Induction Motor

**Step 1 — Rotor flux linkage in $d$-$q$ frame (rotor-flux oriented):**

$$
\psi_{rd} = L_m \, i_{ds}, \quad \psi_{rq} = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Rotor current relations:**

$$
i_{qr} = 0 \quad \text{(aligned frame)} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; i_{qs}^{r} = \dfrac{L_m}{L_r} \, i_{ds}
$$

**Step 3 — Electromagnetic torque (general $d$-$q$ expression):**

$$
T_e = \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot \dfrac{P}{2} \left( \psi_{ds} i_{qs} - \psi_{qs} i_{ds} \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute flux linkages $\psi_{ds} = L_s i_{ds} + L_m i_{dr}$ and align such that $\psi_{qs}=0$:**

After algebraic manipulation using the rotor equation $0 = R_r i_{dr} + d\psi_{rd}/dt$ (steady-state), one obtains:

$$
T_e = \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot \dfrac{P}{2} \cdot \dfrac{L_m}{L_r} \, \psi_{rd} \, i_{qs}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $\psi_{rd} = L_m i_{ds}$ back:**

$$
T_e = \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot \dfrac{P}{2} \cdot \dfrac{L_m^2}{L_r} \, i_{ds} \, i_{qs}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_e = \dfrac{3 P L_m^2}{4 L_r} \, i_{ds} \, i_{qs}}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** Since $i_{ds}$ is held at its rated value (constant flux) below base speed, **torque becomes a linear function of $i_{qs}$ alone**, enabling decoupled control analogous to a separately excited DC motor.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A boost converter operates in CCM with $V_s = 48 \text{ V}$, $D = 0.4$, $L = 200 \,\mu\text{H}$, $f_s = 50 \text{ kHz}$, $R = 20 \,\Omega$, $C = 100 \,\mu\text{F}$. Determine $V_o$, average inductor current $I_L$, and output voltage ripple $\Delta V_o$.

**Step 1 — Output voltage:**

$$
V_o = \dfrac{V_s}{1-D} = \dfrac{48}{1 - 0.4} = \dfrac{48}{0.6} = 80 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2 — Load current:**

$$
I_o = \dfrac{V_o}{R} = \dfrac{80}{20} = 4 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 3 — Inductor average current (power balance, ideal):**

$$
P_{\text{in}} = P_{\text{out}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; V_s I_L = V_o I_o \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; I_L = \dfrac{V_o I_o}{V_s} = \dfrac{80 \cdot 4}{48} = 6.667 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 4 — Inductor ripple:**

$$
\Delta I_L = \dfrac{V_s D}{f_s L} = \dfrac{48 \cdot 0.4}{50{,}000 \cdot 200 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{19.2}{10} = 1.92 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 5 — Output voltage ripple (boost):**

$$
\Delta V_o = \dfrac{I_o D}{f_s C} = \dfrac{4 \cdot 0.4}{50{,}000 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{1.6}{5} = 0.32 \text{ V}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_o = 80 \text{ V},\;\; I_L = 6.667 \text{ A},\;\; \Delta V_o = 0.32 \text{ V}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of CCM:** $I_L > \Delta I_L / 2$ → $6.667 > 0.96$ ✓ — converter is indeed in continuous conduction.

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 EV Powertrain Power-Electronic Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    bat["BATTERY PACK"]:::source
    bdc["BIDIRECTIONAL DC DC CONVERTER"]:::conv
    dclink["DC LINK CAPACITOR"]:::node1
    inv["THREE PHASE VSI INVERTER"]:::conv
    ctrl["VEHICLE CONTROL UNIT VCU"]:::ctrl
    sensor["CURRENT AND POSITION SENSORS"]:::sensor
    motor["TRACTION MOTOR PMSM OR IM"]:::load
    aux["AUXILIARY 12V DC DC BUCK"]:::conv
    auxload["AUX LOADS LIGHTING ECU"]:::load

    bat --> bdc --> dclink --> inv --> motor
    bat --> aux --> auxload
    ctrl -. PWM GATING .-> bdc
    ctrl -. SVPWM SIGNALS .-> inv
    sensor -. FEEDBACK .-> ctrl

    classDef source fill:#FFE082,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef conv fill:#90CAF9,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef node1 fill:#CE93D8,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sensor fill:#FFAB91,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#B0BEC5,stroke:#263238,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> **Reading Guide:** Solid arrows = power flow; dotted arrows = control/signal flow. Notice the **bidirectional** nature — during regen braking, current reverses from `motor → inv → dclink → bdc → bat`.

---

### 4.2 Three-Phase Two-Level VSI Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    vdcP["V DC PLUS"]:::rail
    vdcN["V DC MINUS"]:::rail
    s1["IGBT T1"]:::sw
    s2["IGBT T2"]:::sw
    s3["IGBT T3"]:::sw
    s4["IGBT T4"]:::sw
    s5["IGBT T5"]:::sw
    s6["IGBT T6"]:::sw

    d2["D2"]:::diode
    d3["D3"]:::diode
    d4["D4"]:::diode
    d5["D5"]:::diode
    d6["D6"]:::diode
    phaseA["PHASE A"]:::out
    phaseB["PHASE B"]:::out
    phaseC["PHASE C"]:::out

    vdcP --> s1 --> d1 --> phaseA
    vdcP --> s3 --> d3 --> phaseB
    vdcP --> s5 --> d5 --> phaseC
    phaseA --> d2 --> s2 --> vdcN
    phaseB --> d4 --> s4 --> vdcN
    phaseC --> d6 --> s6 --> vdcN

    classDef rail fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px

```

> **Note:** Each IGBT has an **anti-parallel diode** for freewheeling current — essential for inductive loads (motors).

---

### 4.3 Closed-Loop FOC Controller Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    spdref["SPEED REFERENCE"]:::ref --> piSpd["PI SPEED CONTROLLER"]:::pi
    piSpd --> iqref["IQ REFERENCE"]:::ref
    idref["ID REFERENCE"]:::ref
    iqref --> piQ["PI Q AXIS"]:::pi
    idref --> piD["PI D AXIS"]:::pi
    piQ --> ipark["INVERSE PARK"]:::trf
    piD --> ipark
    theta["ROTOR ANGLE"]:::sensor --> ipark
    ipark --> svpwm["SVPWM GENERATOR"]:::mod
    svpwm --> inv["THREE PHASE VSI"]:::conv
    inv --> motor["PMSM MOTOR"]:::load
    motor --> enc["ENCODER"]:::sensor
    enc --> theta
    motor --> adc["CURRENT SENSORS"]:::sensor
    adc --> clarke["CLARKE TRANSFORM"]:::trf
    clarke --> park["PARK TRANSFORM"]:::trf

    park --> fbD["D AXIS FEEDBACK"]:::fb
    park --> fbQ["Q AXIS FEEDBACK"]:::fb
```

> [!TIP]
> **Decoding the Flow:** The two PI controllers (D and Q) act *independently* on orthogonal axes — this **decoupling** is the entire genius of FOC and a perennial KTU exam topic.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a boost converter in continuous conduction mode. Derive the expression for its output voltage.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Board Standard):**

A boost converter steps up the input DC voltage using an inductor, controlled switch (IGBT/MOSFET), diode, and output capacitor.

- **Switch ON (duration $DT_s$):** Inductor stores energy; $v_L = V_s$; diode reverse-biased; load fed by capacitor.
- **Switch OFF (duration $(1-D)T_s$):** Inductor releases energy in series with source; $v_L = V_s - V_o$; diode conducts.

**Volt-second balance on inductor:**

$$
V_s D + (V_s - V_o)(1 - D) = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \dfrac{V_o}{V_s} = \dfrac{1}{1-D}
$$

*(Marks distribution: 1 for diagram, 1 for mode explanation, 1 for derivation.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Compare SPWM and SVPWM techniques used in three-phase inverters for EV applications.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | SPWM | SVPWM |
|---|---|---|
| Modulation scheme | Carrier-based sinusoidal | Space-vector mapping of 8 switching states |
| DC-link utilization | $0.612 \, m_a V_{dc}$ | $0.707 \, m_a V_{dc}$ |
| Improvement over SPWM | Baseline | **$\approx 15.47\%$ higher** |
| Harmonic content | Moderate | Lower sideband harmonics |
| Computational complexity | Low | Moderate (requires sector identification, $T_0, T_1, T_2$ computation) |
| EV adoption | Rare | **Industry standard** (Tesla, Toyota, Nissan) |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks. Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
*Explain the operation of a three-phase Voltage Source Inverter (VSI) with a circuit diagram. Derive the expression for the RMS value of the fundamental line-to-line voltage under SPWM.*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Circuit Description (2 marks):**
A three-phase, two-level VSI consists of **$\mathbf{6}$** IGBT switches ($T_1$ to $T_6$) with anti-parallel diodes, connected across a DC source $V_{dc}$. The three phase legs share a common DC bus; each phase output is taken from the midpoint of one leg. Switches in the same leg ($T_1, T_4$) are complementary — never ON simultaneously.

**2. Switching States (2 marks):**
There are $2^3 = 8$ permissible states:
- **$\mathbf{6}$ active states** (e.g., $T_1 T_6 T_2$ ON — corresponding to sector I).
- **$\mathbf{2}$ zero states** ($T_1 T_3 T_5$ ON — upper switches; or $T_4 T_6 T_2$ ON — lower switches).

**3. SPWM Modulation (1 mark):**
Three sinusoidal reference signals (phase-shifted by 120°) are compared against a common triangular carrier of frequency $f_c$. The comparator outputs gate the upper switches.

**4. Derivation (2 marks):**
The fundamental component of the phase voltage is:

$$
\hat{V}_{AN,1} = \dfrac{m_a V_{dc}}{2}
$$

For a balanced star-connected load, line voltage leads phase voltage by 30° and is $\sqrt{3}$ times larger in magnitude:

$$
\hat{V}_{AB,1} = \sqrt{3} \, \hat{V}_{AN,1} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} m_a V_{dc}
$$

Converting to RMS:

$$
\boxed{V_{AB,1,\text{rms}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2\sqrt{2}} \, m_a \, V_{dc} \approx 0.612 \, m_a \, V_{dc}}
$$

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
*A boost converter has $V_s = 24 \text{ V}$, $L = 150 \,\mu\text{H}$, $C = 220 \,\mu\text{F}$, switching frequency $f_s = 100 \text{ kHz}$, and load $R = 30 \,\Omega$. It operates at duty cycle $D = 0.5$ in CCM. Determine: (i) Output voltage, (ii) Average inductor current, (iii) Output voltage ripple, (iv) Whether operation is in CCM.*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Output voltage:**

$$
V_o = \dfrac{V_s}{1 - D} = \dfrac{24}{1 - 0.5} = \dfrac{24}{0.5} = 48 \text{ V}
$$

**(ii) Load and inductor currents:**

$$
I_o = \dfrac{V_o}{R} = \dfrac{48}{30} = 1.6 \text{ A}
$$

$$
I_L = \dfrac{I_o}{1 - D} = \dfrac{1.6}{0.5} = 3.2 \text{ A}
$$

**(iii) Output voltage ripple:**

$$
\Delta V_o = \dfrac{I_o \, D}{f_s \, C} = \dfrac{1.6 \times 0.5}{100{,}000 \times 220 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{0.8}{22} \approx 0.0364 \text{ V}
$$

**(iv) Inductor ripple & CCM check:**

$$
\Delta I_L = \dfrac{V_s \, D}{f_s \, L} = \dfrac{24 \times 0.5}{100{,}000 \times 150 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{12}{15} = 0.8 \text{ A}
$$

Since $I_L = 3.2 \text{ A} > \Delta I_L / 2 = 0.4 \text{ A}$ ✓ — **converter operates in CCM**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
*Explain the Field-Oriented Control (FOC) of a three-phase induction motor drive for EV application. State its advantages over V/F control.*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Concept (2 marks):**
FOC, also called *vector control*, transforms the stator currents of an AC motor into a *synchronously rotating reference frame* ($d$-$q$) where sinusoidal AC quantities become DC. This allows the motor to be controlled like a separately excited DC motor — torque and flux become independently controllable.

**2. Mathematical Foundation (2 marks):**
The transformations are:

- **Clarke transform** ($abc \to \alpha\beta$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_\alpha &= i_a \\
i_\beta  &= \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(i_a + 2 i_b)
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Park transform** ($\alpha\beta \to dq$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_d &= i_\alpha \cos\theta + i_\beta \sin\theta \\
i_q &= -i_\alpha \sin\theta + i_\beta \cos\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Torque expression:**

$$
T_e = \dfrac{3 P L_m^2}{4 L_r} \, i_{ds} \, i_{qs}
$$

**3. Block Diagram Description (1 mark):**
Speed reference → PI speed controller → $i_{qs}^*$ reference. $i_{ds}^*$ set to rated value (below base speed). Two PI current controllers generate $V_d^*$ and $V_q^*$. Inverse Park → SVPWM → Inverter → Motor. Encoder feedback closes the loop.

**4. Advantages over V/F (2 marks):**

| Parameter | V/F Control | FOC |
|---|---|---|
| Speed regulation | Poor | Excellent |
| Torque response | Slow (open-loop) | Fast (~ 1–3 ms) |
| Transient performance | Weak | Strong |
| Starting torque | Limited | High, precisely controlled |
| Position accuracy | None | Possible (servo-grade) |

**EV relevance:** FOC is the universal choice for traction drives of Tesla, Nissan Leaf, BMW i3, etc.

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
*With a block diagram, explain the architecture of an EV traction drive. Identify the role of each power-electronic block and specify the typical voltage/power ratings used in a passenger EV.*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Architecture Block Diagram (3 marks):**

*(Draw the same topology as Section 4.1: Battery → Bidirectional DC–DC → DC-link → Inverter → Motor, plus auxiliary buck.)*

**2. Role of Each Block (3 marks):**

| Block | Role | Typical Rating (Passenger EV) |
|---|---|---|
| Battery Pack | Energy storage (Li-ion) | **$\mathbf{300\text{–}400 \text{ V}}$**, **$\mathbf{50\text{–}100 \text{ kWh}}$** |
| Bidirectional DC–DC | Boost in motoring, buck in regen | Same as battery voltage |
| DC-Link Capacitor | Filters ripple, absorbs regen energy | **$\mathbf{500\text{–}1000 \,\mu\text{F}}$**, film type |
| Traction Inverter | DC → Variable-freq AC (SVPWM) | **$\mathbf{200 \text{ kW}}$** peak, **$\mathbf{650 \text{ V}}$** IGBT |
| Traction Motor | Electromechanical conversion | **$\mathbf{150\text{–}250 \text{ kW}}$**, PMSM preferred |
| Auxiliary DC–DC (12 V/48 V) | Steps down HV → LV for accessories | **$\mathbf{1\text{–}3 \text{ kW}}$** |
| VCU (Controller) | Supervises torque/speed commands, generates PWM | DSP/FPGA, ~ **$\mathbf{100 \text{ MHz}}$** clock |

**3. Practical Insight (1 mark):**
Modern EVs are moving to **$\mathbf{800 \text{ V}}$** architectures (Porsche Taycan, Hyundai E-GMP, Lucid Air) to enable faster charging and reduced cable losses.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones — Module 3:**
> 1. **Forgetting the anti-parallel diode** in the VSI diagram — examiners routinely deduct 1 mark. Always show all six diodes.
> 2. **Confusing "peak" vs "RMS" vs "line-to-line" vs "phase"** voltages. Always state *which* you are computing.
> 3. **Applying buck formulas to boost or vice-versa.** Note that the inductor ripple formulas differ: $\Delta I_L = V_s D / (f_s L)$ for buck, but $\Delta I_L = V_o D (1-D) / (f_s L)$ for boost.
> 4. **Writing "SVPWM = SPWM with offset"** — partial credit only. Emphasize the *vector-space decomposition into 8 states and $T_0, T_1, T_2$ timing* for full marks.
> 5. **In FOC, omitting the encoder/resolver feedback** — the entire scheme relies on rotor position.
> 6. **Using $V/f = \text{const}$ beyond base speed** — beyond base speed, $V$ is clamped at rated; only $f$ increases (field-weakening region).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Power Electronics in EVs**

- **DC–DC Converters**:
  - Buck: $V_o = D V_s$; used for auxiliaries.
  - Boost: $V_o = V_s / (1-D)$; used between battery and DC-link.
  - Buck-Boost: inverts polarity; $V_o = -D V_s / (1-D)$.
  - **Bidirectional** converter is critical for **regenerative braking**.
  - Always verify **CCM** by checking $I_L > \Delta I_L / 2$.

- **Inverters**:
  - **VSI** (Voltage Source Inverter) dominates EVs; CSI rare.
  - Two-level, three-phase, 6-IGBT topology.
  - **SVPWM** = industry standard; offers **15.47% better DC-link utilization** than SPWM.
  - Max line RMS = $0.707 V_{dc}$ (SVPWM) vs $0.612 V_{dc}$ (SPWM).

- **Controllers**:
  - **V/F** = simple, open-loop, low-cost (e-rickshaws).
  - **FOC (Vector Control)** = standard for modern EVs; decouples flux ($i_d$) and torque ($i_q$).
  - **DTC** = fast torque response, higher ripple.

- **System-Level**:
  - Typical EV: **300–800 V** battery, **150–250 kW** inverter, **> 95%** efficiency.
  - Modern trend: **800 V architectures** (faster charging).
  - Wide-bandgap devices (**SiC, GaN**) push $f_{sw}$ above 50 kHz, shrinking magnetics.

- **Memory Hooks for KTU**:
  - "$1/(1-D)$" → Boost mantra.
  - "0.707 vs 0.612" → SVPWM vs SPWM distinguishing number.
  - "$3PL_m^2 / 4L_r$" → FOC torque formula.
  - "**6 switches, 8 states, 2 zeros**" → VSI state count.

---
