topics.title: Thermal Management System of Power Electronics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT631 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Thermal Management System of Power Electronics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMUT631)**, the **Thermal Management System (TMS) of Power Electronics** refers to the engineered combination of passive and active subsystems designed to dissipate the heat generated by semiconductor switching devices — primarily **Insulated Gate Bipolar Transistors (IGBTs)**, **Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistors (MOSFETs)**, and **Silicon Carbide (SiC)/Gallium Nitride (GaN) wide-bandgap devices** — within the inverter, DC-DC converter, and onboard charger of an Electric or Hybrid Vehicle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Examination Standard):**
> The power electronic TMS maintains the **junction temperature ($T_j$)** of every semiconductor die within the manufacturer-specified safe operating area (SOA), typically **$T_j \leq 150^\circ\text{C}$** for silicon IGBTs and **$T_j \leq 200^\circ\text{C}$** for SiC MOSFETs, while balancing cabin heating, battery cooling, and motor cooling demands through a unified vehicle-level coolant loop.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Engine's Air-Cooled Cousin"

Imagine the inverter of an EV as a **professional athlete sprinting a marathon**. The athlete's muscles (semiconductor switches) generate enormous heat during switching events. Without a cooling vest, fan, and water spray, the athlete would overheat and collapse within minutes.

In a conventional Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) vehicle, the **radiator** manages engine block heat. In an EV, the power electronics are similarly the "sprinters" — they convert high-current DC from the battery to AC for the traction motor at switching frequencies often exceeding **10 kHz**. The TMS is their **personal climate-control system**, ensuring they never cross the thermal red line.

### 1.3 Why Power Electronics Heat Generation is Critical

| Heat Source | Approximate Magnitude (EV Inverter, 100 kW peak) | Frequency of Occurrence |
|---|---|---|
| **Conduction Losses** ($P_{cond}$) | **60 – 80 %** of total losses | Continuous (every switching cycle) |
| **Switching Losses** ($P_{sw}$) | **20 – 40 %** of total losses | At every turn-ON and turn-OFF transition |
| **Gate Drive Losses** ($P_{g}$) | **< 1 %** | Continuous |
| **Reverse Recovery Losses** ($P_{rr}$) | Minor in SiC, significant in Si IGBTs | At diode turn-OFF |

> [!NOTE]
> A typical 100 kW traction inverter wastes **2 – 5 kW** purely as heat. Without aggressive thermal management, the silicon dies would reach destructive junction temperatures in under **30 seconds** of continuous full-load operation.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold-Highlighted)

- **Thermal Conductivity of Copper (heatsink base): $k_{Cu} = 401\ \text{W/m·K}$**
- **Thermal Conductivity of Aluminum (cold plate): $k_{Al} = 237\ \text{W/m·K}$**
- **Specific Heat Capacity of 50/50 Ethylene-Glycol-Water Coolant: $c_p = 3.5\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$**
- **Maximum Junction Temperature (Silicon IGBT, industry standard): $T_{j,\max} = 150^\circ\text{C}$**
- **Maximum Junction Temperature (SiC MOSFET, automotive-grade): $T_{j,\max} = 175^\circ\text{C}$ – $200^\circ\text{C}$**
- **Thermal Boundary Layer Thickness (forced liquid cooling): $\delta_t \approx 0.1\text{ – }1\ \text{mm}$**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sources of Heat in EV Power Electronics

Power electronic switches dissipate energy predominantly through **two non-ideal mechanisms**:

1. **Conduction Losses ($P_{cond}$)** — When the device is in the ON-state, the non-zero saturation voltage ($V_{CE(sat)}$ for IGBT, $R_{DS(on)}$ for MOSFET) drops across the current-carrying channel, producing ohmic heating.
2. **Switching Losses ($P_{sw}$)** — During the brief intervals of state transition (typically **0.1 – 1 µs**), both voltage and current are simultaneously non-zero, producing triangular loss pulses whose area is the switching energy $E_{on}$ and $E_{off}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Higher DC-bus voltage (e.g., **800 V** architectures in modern EVs like Porsche Taycan, Hyundai E-GMP) reduces conduction losses (lower current for the same power) but **increases switching losses** (larger $V \cdot I$ overlap). This is why **800 V systems mandate SiC devices** — they switch faster, slashing switching losses by **50 – 70 %** compared to silicon IGBTs.

### 2.2 Cooling Strategies — Hierarchical Classification

EV power electronics employ a **three-tier cooling hierarchy**:

**Tier 1 — Passive (no moving parts):**
- Natural convection heatsinks
- Heat spreaders (copper base plates)

**Tier 2 — Active Air Cooling:**
- Forced-air fans blowing across finned heatsinks
- Used in low-power applications (e.g., **onboard chargers < 11 kW**)

**Tier 3 — Active Liquid Cooling (industry standard for traction inverters):**
- **Single-phase liquid cooling:** Water-glycol coolant circulating through a cold plate bonded to the IGBT module base
- **Two-phase cooling:** Coolant boils on the hot surface, absorbing massive **latent heat** (~2200 kJ/kg for water) — used in high-performance EVs (e.g., Tesla Model S Plaid)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations form the **mathematical backbone** of any thermal management numerical in a KTU exam. Memorize units and physical meaning, not just the symbol.

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{cond} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_{avg}$ | Conduction loss in an IGBT | W |
| 2 | $P_{sw} = (E_{on} + E_{off}) \cdot f_{sw}$ | Switching loss (per device, per cycle) | W |
| 3 | $P_{total} = n \cdot (P_{cond} + P_{sw})$ | Total inverter losses for $n$ switches | W |
| 4 | $R_{th,ja} = R_{th,jc} + R_{th,cs} + R_{th,sa}$ | Junction-to-ambient thermal resistance (series) | K/W |
| 5 | $T_j = T_a + P_{loss} \cdot R_{th,ja}$ | Junction temperature rise above ambient | °C |
| 6 | $q_{cond}'' = -k \cdot \dfrac{dT}{dx}$ | Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction (1-D) | W/m² |
| 7 | $q_{conv}'' = h \cdot (T_s - T_\infty)$ | Newton's Law of Cooling | W/m² |
| 8 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T_{coolant}$ | Sensible heat absorbed by coolant | W |
| 9 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg}$ | Latent heat absorbed (two-phase cooling) | W |
| 10 | $Nu = \dfrac{h \cdot L_c}{k_f}$ | Nusselt Number (convection correlation) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D_h}{\mu}$ | Reynolds Number (flow regime indicator) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\Delta P_{friction} = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D_h} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | Coolant pumping pressure drop | Pa |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Performance Enhancement:** Cooler semiconductors allow **higher continuous current**, increasing inverter power density from **~5 kW/L** (2015 era) to **>25 kW/L** (modern SiC designs).
- **Reliability:** A **10 °C reduction in $T_j$** doubles the lifetime of a silicon IGBT (Arrhenius rule).
- **Vehicle Range:** Efficient thermal design reduces parasitic pump/fan power consumption, extending EV range by **2 – 5 %**.
- **Integration with Cabin HVAC:** In cold climates, waste heat from inverters can be harvested for cabin warming, eliminating the resistive PTC heater.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Junction Temperature from Thermal Resistance Network

The thermal resistance network for an IGBT module is **electrically analogous** to a resistor network, where temperature corresponds to voltage and heat flow to current.

**Step 1: Define the series thermal path**
Heat flows: Semiconductor Die → Die-Attach Solder → DBC Substrate → Baseplate → Thermal Interface Material (TIM) → Heatsink/Cold Plate → Ambient Coolant.

**Step 2: Express the series resistance**
In steady state, heat flow is constant across each layer. By Fourier's Law, the temperature drop across each layer is $T_i - T_{i+1} = q \cdot R_{th,i}$. Summing all layers:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} (T_i - T_{i+1}) = q \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_{th,i}$$

**Step 3: Substitute boundary temperatures**
Let $T_1 = T_j$ (junction), $T_{n+1} = T_a$ (ambient coolant), and $q = P_{loss}$:

$$T_j - T_a = P_{loss} \cdot R_{th,ja}$$

**Step 4: Solve for junction temperature**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_j &= T_a + P_{loss} \cdot R_{th,ja} \\
T_j &= T_a + P_{loss} \cdot (R_{th,jc} + R_{th,cs} + R_{th,sa})
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** The total thermal resistance is the **sum of individual layer resistances in series**, each layer acting like an "obstacle" to heat flow. The TIM (thermal grease/pad) often dominates — a poor TIM can double $R_{th,ja}$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Coolant Mass Flow Rate for Given Heat Load

**Problem Context:** A 100 kW EV inverter dissipates 3 kW of heat. Coolant enters the cold plate at **45 °C** and must exit at **≤ 55 °C**. Determine the required coolant mass flow rate.

**Given:**
- Heat load: $\dot{Q} = 3000$ W
- Coolant: 50/50 ethylene-glycol-water, $c_p = 3500$ J/kg·K
- Inlet temperature: $T_{in} = 45^\circ\text{C}$
- Outlet temperature: $T_{out} = 55^\circ\text{C}$

**Step 1: Apply the energy balance for the coolant stream**
Energy absorbed by coolant = heat generated by electronics:

$$\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$$

**Step 2: Solve for mass flow rate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m} &= \frac{\dot{Q}}{c_p \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})} \\
\dot{m} &= \frac{3000}{3500 \cdot (55 - 45)} \\
\dot{m} &= \frac{3000}{3500 \cdot 10} \\
\dot{m} &= \frac{3000}{35000} \\
\dot{m} &= 0.0857\ \text{kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Convert to volumetric flow rate**
Using coolant density $\rho = 1070$ kg/m³:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{V} &= \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \frac{0.0857}{1070} \\
\dot{V} &= 8.01 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s} \\
\dot{V} &\approx 4.81\ \text{L/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **KTU Examiner's Note:** This **~5 L/min** flow rate is typical for an EV traction inverter. A production EV pump (e.g., Bosch) delivers **8 – 15 L/min** to simultaneously serve battery, motor, and inverter.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Convective Heat Transfer Coefficient from Nusselt Number

For fully developed turbulent flow in a circular coolant channel (Dittus-Boelter correlation):

**Step 1: Compute Reynolds Number**
Assume $D_h = 0.010$ m, $v = 1$ m/s, $\rho = 1070$ kg/m³, $\mu = 0.005$ Pa·s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Re &= \frac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D_h}{\mu} \\
Re &= \frac{1070 \cdot 1 \cdot 0.010}{0.005} \\
Re &= 2140
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Prandtl Number**
For 50/50 glycol-water at 50 °C: $Pr \approx 23$

**Step 3: Apply Dittus-Boelter (heating, $n = 0.4$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Nu &= 0.023 \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{0.4} \\
Nu &= 0.023 \cdot (2140)^{0.8} \cdot (23)^{0.4} \\
Nu &= 0.023 \cdot (482.5) \cdot (3.41) \\
Nu &\approx 37.8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Solve for $h$**
With coolant thermal conductivity $k_f = 0.42$ W/m·K:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= \frac{Nu \cdot k_f}{D_h} = \frac{37.8 \cdot 0.42}{0.010} \\
h &= 1588\ \text{W/m}^2\text{·K}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** This $h$ value (~1600 W/m²K) is **~50× higher** than natural convection air ($h \approx 5$–25 W/m²K), explaining why liquid cooling dominates EV power electronics.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cooling Method Selection

| Method | Heat Flux Capacity (W/cm²) | $h$ Range (W/m²K) | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Natural Convection (Air) | 0.1 – 0.5 | 5 – 25 | Onboard charger (auxiliary) |
| Forced Air (Fan) | 0.5 – 2 | 25 – 250 | Low-power DC-DC converters |
| Single-Phase Liquid | 5 – 30 | 500 – 5000 | **Traction inverter (standard)** |
| Two-Phase Cooling | 30 – 100+ | 5000 – 50000 | High-performance (Tesla, Lucid) |
| Jet Impingement | 50 – 200 | 5000 – 20000 | Research-stage, GaN devices |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — EV Power Electronics Thermal Loop

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional interaction** between the power electronics, the cooling loop, and the vehicle-level thermal management system. This is the conceptual map KTU examiners expect students to draw in 14-mark descriptive questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Battery Pack HV DC Bus] --> node2[Inverter DC Link Capacitor]
    node2 --> node3[IGBT Module 1 Phase A]
    node2 --> node4[IGBT Module 2 Phase B]
    node2 --> node5[IGBT Module 3 Phase C]
    node3 --> node6[Cold Plate Top Surface]
    node4 --> node6
    node5 --> node6
    node6 --> node7[Thermal Interface Material TIM Layer]
    node7 --> node8[Cold Plate Internal Channels]
    node8 --> node9[Coolant Inlet 45 C]
    node8 --> node10[Coolant Outlet 55 C]
    node10 --> node11[Coolant Pump Electric]
    node11 --> node12[Chiller or Radiator]
    node12 --> node13[Expansion Tank and Degasser]
    node13 --> node9
    node12 --> node14[Low Temp Loop 20 to 40 C]
    node14 --> node15[Battery Pack Cooling Plates]
    node14 --> node16[Electric Motor Stator Winding]
    node14 --> node17[Onboard Charger OBC]
    node3 --> node18[Gate Driver PCB]
    node18 --> node19[Temperature Sensor NTC at Baseplate]
    node19 --> node20[Vehicle Control Unit VCU]
    node20 --> node21[Derating Algorithm]
    node21 --> node2
    node20 --> node11
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The arrows represent **heat flow and control signal flow**. Power losses in the IGBT modules (nodes 3, 4, 5) flow downward through the **TIM** to the coolant, while sensor feedback (node 19) flows upward to the **VCU**, which dynamically throttles inverter output to prevent junction overtemperature.

### 4.2 Thermal Resistance Ladder (Series-Equivalent Network)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Tj Junction 150 C] -->|Rth jc 0.15 K per W| nodeB[Tc Case 140 C]
    nodeB -->|Rth cs 0.05 K per W| nodeC[Ts Heatsink 135 C]
    nodeC -->|Rth sa 0.30 K per W| nodeD[Ta Ambient 75 C]
```

> **KTU Tip:** The values shown represent a **typical 100 kW IGBT module with liquid-cooled cold plate**. The **heatsink-to-ambient** resistance $R_{th,sa}$ is the dominant term — this is exactly where active liquid cooling provides the most dramatic improvement.

### 4.3 Cold Plate Internal Channel Geometry (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeIn[Inlet Manifold] --> nodeC1[Serpentine Channel Turn 1]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC2[Serpentine Channel Turn 2]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC3[Serpentine Channel Turn 3]
    nodeC3 --> nodeC4[Serpentine Channel Turn 4]
    nodeC4 --> nodeC5[Serpentine Channel Turn 5]
    nodeC5 --> nodeOut[Outlet Manifold]
```

> **Engineering Note:** The **serpentine (meandering) channel** design ensures uniform coolant velocity over the entire IGBT footprint, minimizing hot spots. Pin-fin and dimple-textured channels are advanced alternatives offering **20 – 40 %** higher $h$.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
*List any three sources of heat generation in an IGBT module used in an EV traction inverter. State the typical maximum junction temperature for a silicon IGBT.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three principal sources of heat in an IGBT module are:

1. **Conduction Losses ($P_{cond}$):** Caused by the non-zero collector-emitter saturation voltage $V_{CE(sat)}$ when the device is fully ON. Given by $P_{cond} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_C$ where $I_C$ is the average collector current.

2. **Switching Losses ($P_{sw}$):** Energy dissipated during the turn-ON and turn-OFF transitions when both voltage and current overlap. Given by $P_{sw} = (E_{on} + E_{off}) \cdot f_{sw}$ where $f_{sw}$ is the switching frequency (typically **5 – 20 kHz** in EV inverters).

3. **Gate Drive Losses ($P_g$):** Energy required to charge and discharge the gate capacitance at every switching cycle. Given by $P_g = Q_g \cdot V_{gs} \cdot f_{sw}$. This is small but non-negligible at high $f_{sw}$.

**Typical maximum junction temperature for a silicon IGBT: $T_{j,\max} = 150^\circ\text{C}$** (some automotive-grade parts rated to **$175^\circ\text{C}$**).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Compare air cooling and liquid cooling for EV power electronics. State one advantage of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Air Cooling | Liquid Cooling |
|---|---|---|
| Heat transfer coefficient $h$ | 5 – 250 W/m²K | 500 – 5000 W/m²K |
| Heat flux capacity | Up to 2 W/cm² | 5 – 30 W/cm² |
| **Advantage** | Simple, low cost, no leak risk (1 mark) | Compact, handles high heat flux, integrates with battery cooling (1 mark) |
| Typical application | Onboard charger, auxiliaries | Traction inverter, motor controller |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Liquid cooling is the industry standard for traction inverters above ~50 kW due to its superior heat transfer capacity.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> When answering 14-mark thermal management questions, students **consistently lose 2 – 3 marks** by:
> 1. **Forgetting units** in the final answer (e.g., writing $h = 1588$ instead of $h = 1588$ W/m²·K).
> 2. **Skipping the thermal resistance diagram** — KTU examiners award 2 marks purely for the labeled sketch.
> 3. **Confusing $R_{th,ja}$ (junction-to-ambient) with $R_{th,jc}$ (junction-to-case)**. The first is for the *whole system*, the second is *manufacturer-specified* for the bare die package.
> 4. **Not justifying the choice of cooling method** — always state *why* liquid cooling is preferred for the traction inverter (heat flux > 5 W/cm²).

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat diagram the equivalent thermal resistance network of an IGBT power module mounted on a liquid-cooled cold plate. Derive the expression for junction temperature $T_j$ in terms of ambient temperature, power loss, and total thermal resistance.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A 3-phase EV traction inverter uses six IGBT modules (one per switch position). Each IGBT has $V_{CE(sat)} = 1.8$ V at the operating point. The DC-bus current during peak motoring is 250 A (split equally among the three phases, with two devices conducting per phase at any instant). The switching frequency is 10 kHz and the switching energy per transition is $E_{on} + E_{off} = 8$ mJ per device per cycle. Compute:
(i) Total conduction loss in the inverter.
(ii) Total switching loss in the inverter.
(iii) Total heat to be removed by the cold plate, given that the VCU allows a junction-to-ambient thermal resistance of **0.10 K/W** and ambient coolant temperature is **40 °C**. Calculate the maximum allowable $T_j$.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Diagram and Derivation [7 marks]:**

The thermal resistance network of an IGBT module is shown below (drawn by student in exam):

$$T_j \rightarrow R_{th,jc} \rightarrow T_c \rightarrow R_{th,cs} \rightarrow T_s \rightarrow R_{th,sa} \rightarrow T_a$$

**Derivation:** Heat generated at the junction flows through successive layers to the ambient. By Fourier's Law and electrical analogy, the temperature drop across each layer is $q \cdot R_{th}$. Adding all drops (2 marks for derivation):

$$T_j - T_a = P_{loss} \cdot (R_{th,jc} + R_{th,cs} + R_{th,sa})$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_j &= T_a + P_{loss} \cdot R_{th,ja} \\
\text{where } R_{th,ja} &= R_{th,jc} + R_{th,cs} + R_{th,sa}
\end{aligned}
$$

Typical values: $R_{th,jc} = 0.15$ K/W (junction to case), $R_{th,cs} = 0.05$ K/W (case to sink, dominated by TIM), $R_{th,sa} = 0.30$ K/W (sink to ambient, drastically reduced by liquid cooling to ~0.10 K/W).

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 marks]:**

**(i) Conduction Loss [2 marks]:**
At any instant, **2 IGBTs conduct** (one high-side, one low-side per current path). The current per conducting device is $I_C = 250/3 = 83.33$ A.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{cond,\ per\ device} &= V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_C = 1.8 \cdot 83.33 = 150\ \text{W} \\
P_{cond,\ total\ (2\ devices\ conducting)} &= 2 \cdot 150 = 300\ \text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Note:** Since the 6 devices are conducting on a time-sharing basis (each conducts for one-third of the electrical cycle in PWM), the **average total** conduction loss per inverter remains the same (300 W total at any instant), and over an electrical cycle, $P_{cond,total} = 300$ W (continuous, since conduction rotates through all 6 devices).

**(ii) Switching Loss [2 marks]:**
Each of the 6 IGBTs switches at $f_{sw} = 10$ kHz, with loss per cycle $E_{sw} = 8$ mJ:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{sw,\ per\ device} &= E_{sw} \cdot f_{sw} = 8 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 10 \times 10^3 = 80\ \text{W} \\
P_{sw,\ total} &= 6 \cdot 80 = 480\ \text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Total Heat and Maximum $T_j$ [3 marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{total} &= P_{cond} + P_{sw} = 300 + 480 = 780\ \text{W} \\
T_j &= T_a + P_{total} \cdot R_{th,ja} \\
T_j &= 40 + 780 \cdot 0.10 \\
T_j &= 40 + 78 \\
T_j &= 118^\circ\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The maximum junction temperature is **$T_j = 118^\circ\text{C}$**, which is **safely below** the silicon IGBT limit of $150^\circ\text{C}$, providing a **$32^\circ\text{C}$ thermal margin**. (1 mark for interpretation/comment).

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a suitable schematic, describe the construction and working of a **liquid-cooled cold plate** used in EV traction inverters. Explain the role of the **Thermal Interface Material (TIM)** and the **coolant pump**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An EV inverter cold plate must remove **2.5 kW** of heat. The coolant (50/50 glycol-water) enters at **35 °C** and the maximum allowable outlet temperature is **50 °C**. Compute:
(i) The required mass flow rate of coolant.
(ii) The required volumetric flow rate in **L/min**, given coolant density $\rho = 1070$ kg/m³.
(iii) If the inverter is upgraded to deliver 30 % more power (raising heat dissipation to **3.25 kW**), and the coolant flow remains unchanged, find the new outlet temperature. State whether the original design is acceptable.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Liquid-Cooled Cold Plate [7 marks]:**

A cold plate is an **aluminum (or copper) block** with internal flow channels machined directly beneath the mounting surface where the IGBT modules are bolted. Coolant flows through these channels, absorbing heat via forced convection.

**Key Components:**

- **Base Plate / Cold Plate Body:** Aluminum (k = 237 W/m·K) for light weight; copper (k = 401 W/m·K) for high-performance applications. The plate is typically **5 – 15 mm thick**.

- **Internal Channels:** Serpentine, parallel, or pin-fin geometries. Channel diameters range from **6 – 12 mm**.

- **Thermal Interface Material (TIM):** A thin layer (**50 – 200 µm**) of high-thermal-conductivity material (thermal grease, indium foil, or graphene pad) between the IGBT baseplate and the cold plate. The TIM fills microscopic air gaps (air $k = 0.026$ W/m·K is a poor conductor). Without TIM, contact resistance can be **> 5× higher**.

- **Coolant Pump:** Typically a **12 V or 48 V brushless DC centrifugal pump** (Bosch, Pierburg, or Continental make) delivering **8 – 20 L/min** at **0.3 – 1.5 bar** pressure.

- **Working:** The VCU modulates pump speed based on inverter temperature sensor feedback. At idle/low load, the pump slows to save parasitic power (~10 – 50 W saved). At peak load, maximum flow prevents junction overtemperature.

**Schematic (drawn by student):** Cross-section showing IGBT module → TIM layer → Cold plate → Serpentine channel → Coolant arrows → Pump → Radiator loop.

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 marks]:**

**(i) Mass Flow Rate [2 marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m} &= \frac{\dot{Q}}{c_p \cdot \Delta T} \\
\dot{m} &= \frac{2500}{3500 \cdot (50 - 35)} \\
\dot{m} &= \frac{2500}{52500} \\
\dot{m} &= 0.0476\ \text{kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Volumetric Flow Rate [2 marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{V} &= \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \frac{0.0476}{1070} \\
\dot{V} &= 4.45 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s} \\
\dot{V} &= 4.45 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 60000 \\
\dot{V} &\approx 2.67\ \text{L/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) New Outlet Temperature [3 marks]:**

With $\dot{m}$ and $c_p$ unchanged:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_{new} &= \frac{\dot{Q}_{new}}{\dot{m} \cdot c_p} = \frac{3250}{0.0476 \cdot 3500} \\
\Delta T_{new} &= \frac{3250}{166.7} = 19.5^\circ\text{C} \\
T_{out,new} &= T_{in} + \Delta T_{new} = 35 + 19.5 = 54.5^\circ\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment [1 mark]:** The new outlet temperature of **54.5 °C** exceeds the design limit of **50 °C** by 4.5 °C. The original design is **NOT acceptable** for the upgraded inverter; either the pump flow must be increased by **~30 %** (to ~3.5 L/min) or the inlet temperature must be reduced by improving radiator performance.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3 — TMS of Power Electronics):**

- ✅ **Two main loss sources:** Conduction ($P_{cond} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_C$) and Switching ($P_{sw} = E_{sw} \cdot f_{sw}$). Switching losses scale linearly with $f_{sw}$.
- ✅ **Maximum junction temperatures:** Silicon IGBT = **150 °C**, SiC MOSFET = **175 – 200 °C**, GaN HEMT = **225 °C+**.
- ✅ **Thermal resistance ladder (memorize the order):** $R_{th,jc}$ → $R_{th,cs}$ (TIM dominated) → $R_{th,sa}$ (heatsink/cold plate dominated).
- ✅ **$T_j = T_a + P_{loss} \cdot R_{th,ja}$** — the single most important equation in the module.
- ✅ **Coolant energy balance:** $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$. For 50/50 glycol-water, $c_p = 3.5$ kJ/kg·K.
- ✅ **Liquid cooling $h$ values (500 – 5000 W/m²K)** are **20 – 200× higher** than natural air convection, making it mandatory for traction inverters.
- ✅ **The TIM is often the weakest link** — poor TIM application can double $R_{th,ja}$.
- ✅ **Two-phase cooling** absorbs **latent heat** $h_{fg} \approx 2200$ kJ/kg for water — enables heat fluxes exceeding **100 W/cm²**.
- ✅ **Vehicle-level integration:** Modern EVs use a **single low-temperature loop** serving battery, motor, and inverter simultaneously, reducing cost and weight.
- ✅ **VCU derating algorithm** dynamically reduces inverter output current when $T_j$ approaches the limit, protecting the semiconductor.
- ✅ **Nusselt Number for Dittus-Boelter:** $Nu = 0.023 \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{0.4}$ (turbulent, heating, $Re > 10000$). For $Re < 2300$, use Sieder-Tate correlation for laminar flow.
- ✅ **Reynolds Number criterion:** $Re < 2300$ → laminar; $2300 \leq Re \leq 4000$ → transitional; $Re > 4000$ → turbulent.
- ✅ **Unit vigilance in exams:** Thermal resistance is in **K/W** (or °C/W), heat transfer coefficient $h$ is in **W/m²·K**, heat flux $q''$ is in **W/m²**, and mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ is in **kg/s**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicle Validation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT631 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vehicle Validation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Vehicle Validation** is the comprehensive, evidence-based engineering process of confirming that a fully integrated, production-intent vehicle meets all pre-defined design specifications, functional requirements, regulatory standards, safety norms, and customer expectations under real-world operating conditions. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework (aligned with NEP 2020 outcome-based education), vehicle validation is treated as the apex phase of the **Automotive V-Model Development Cycle**, where the complete Bill of Materials (BOM) of subsystems is verified as a holistic, road-worthy system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Reference):** Vehicle validation is the systematic demonstration that a vehicle, as integrated and intended for series production, satisfies all functional, performance, durability, safety, statutory, and homologation requirements across the entire envelope of expected customer usage conditions.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Final Board Exam of a Car"

Imagine a student has spent four years learning, has cleared internal tests, practicals, and viva-voce (these are equivalent to **component** and **subsystem verification**). The final University Exam is the one where the entire degree is certified as complete. Vehicle validation is precisely this — the **final, all-or-nothing, certified demonstration** that the complete car is ready for the public road.

- **Classroom tests** → **Component tests** (individual parts)
- **Lab practicals** → **Subsystem tests** (motor, battery, brakes)
- **Mock exam** → **System integration tests** (powertrain dyno, HIL)
- **Final University exam** → **Vehicle validation** (proving ground, homologation, real-world fleet)

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Industry Norms

The following bolded metrics are universally referenced in KTU 2024 question papers and automotive industry practice:

- **Drive Cycle Standards:** **NEDC**, **WLTP** (Worldwide Harmonised Light Vehicles Test Procedure), **EPA**, **FTP-75**
- **Functional Safety Standard:** **ISO 26262** (ASIL ratings A through D)
- **Crash Safety Standards:** **AIS-096**, **ECE R94**, **ECE R95**, **FMVSS 208**
- **Electromagnetic Compatibility:** **ECE R10** (emissions and immunity)
- **Battery Abuse Standards:** **AIS-156 / AIS-038 Rev 2** (specific to EVs in India)
- **Test Track Mileage Norm:** **1,00,000 km** durability and **2,00,000 km** for extreme programmes
- **Ambient Test Range:** **−40 °C to +85 °C** thermal envelope
- **Statistical Confidence:** **95% confidence level** (Cpk ≥ 1.67 for key parameters)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A common 3-mark question asks students to differentiate between *Verification* ("Are we building the product right?") and *Validation* ("Are we building the right product?"). Memorize this Booch-style definition.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Automotive V-Model — Where Validation Sits

The V-Model is the standard reference architecture in all OEM (Original Equipment Manufacturer) development workflows and is therefore a high-yield topic for KTU 2024 boards.

**Left arm (Decomposition — Design):**
1. Vehicle Level Requirements
2. System Level Requirements
3. Subsystem Level Requirements
4. Component Level Design

**Right arm (Integration — Validation):**
1. Component Validation
2. Subsystem Validation
3. System Validation
4. Vehicle Validation (Top of V)

The **right arm mirrors the left arm**. Validation at the top corresponds to requirements at the top, ensuring **traceability** — a core OBE (Outcome-Based Education) principle.

### 2.2 The Five Pillars of Vehicle Validation

1. **Performance Validation**
   - Top speed, 0–100 km/h acceleration, gradability, range (EVs), fuel economy (HEVs)
   - Conducted on proving grounds (e.g., NATRIP, ARAI Pune, MIRA UK)
2. **Durability & Reliability Validation**
   - Accelerated life tests: 1 lab hour ≈ 1 customer hour (using four-poster shakers, salt spray, gravel)
3. **Safety Validation (Passive + Active)**
   - Crash tests (frontal, side, pedestrian), ESC (Electronic Stability Control), ABS, AEB (Autonomous Emergency Braking)
4. **Regulatory & Homologation Validation**
   - Type approval under **CMVR** (Central Motor Vehicles Rules), AIS standards, ECE regulations
5. **Environmental & EMC Validation**
   - Temperature/humidity chambers, vibration, water/dust ingress (**IP ratings**), electromagnetic compatibility

### 2.3 EV-Specific Validation — A KTU 2024 Hot Topic

Since PEMUT631 focuses on Electric and Hybrid Vehicles, the following EV-specific validation tests are mandatory knowledge:

- **Range Test:** ARAI-Standardised Cycle (equivalent to MIDC — Modified Indian Driving Cycle)
- **Energy Consumption Test:** kWh / 100 km at different SoC (State of Charge) windows
- **Battery Abuse Testing:** Nail penetration, thermal runaway, overcharge, short circuit
- **Regenerative Braking Validation:** Efficiency vs. brake pedal travel, ABS interaction
- **Thermal Management Validation:** Battery cooling/heating under fast-charge and ambient extremes
- **High-Voltage Safety:** Isolation resistance ≥ **100 Ω/V**, creepage distances, interlock systems
- **Charger Interoperability:** AC (Type 1/Type 2/GB/T) and DC (CCS2/CHAdeMO) conformance

### 2.4 KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Vehicle Range (EV) | $R = \dfrac{E_{bat} \cdot \eta_{drivetrain}}{E_{100}}$ | km | $E_{bat}$ in kWh |
| 2 | Energy per 100 km | $E_{100} = \dfrac{1}{\eta_{tot}} \int (F_{roll} + F_{aero} + F_{grade} + F_{acc}) \, ds$ | kWh/100km | $F_{acc}$ typically neglected on cycle |
| 3 | Rolling Resistance | $F_{roll} = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} \cdot \cos\theta$ | N | $C_{rr}$ ≈ 0.008–0.015 |
| 4 | Aerodynamic Drag | $F_{aero} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho \cdot C_{d} \cdot A_{f} \cdot v^{2}$ | N | $\rho$ = 1.225 kg/m³ (air) |
| 5 | Grade Force | $F_{grade} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$ | N | $\theta$ in radians |
| 6 | Inertia Force | $F_{acc} = m \cdot \delta \cdot a$ | N | $\delta$ = mass factor (1.05–1.10) |
| 7 | Acceleration Time | $t_{a} = \dfrac{m \cdot \delta}{P_{peak}/v - F_{resist}} \int_{v_{1}}^{v_{2}} dv$ | s | Numerical integration often used |
| 8 | Required Traction Power | $P_{traction} = F_{resist} \cdot v$ | W | For gradeability check |
| 9 | Brake Stopping Distance | $d = \dfrac{v^{2}}{2 \cdot \mu \cdot g}$ | m | $v$ in m/s, $g$ = 9.81 m/s² |
| 10 | Cpk (Process Capability) | $C_{pk} = \min \!\left( \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} \right)$ | dimensionless | Validation acceptance: $\geq 1.33$ |
| 11 | Battery Energy | $E_{bat} = V_{nom} \cdot C_{Ah}$ | Wh | Convert to kWh ÷ 1000 |
| 12 | SoC Window | $\Delta \text{SoC} = \text{SoC}_{start} - \text{SoC}_{end}$ | % | Range test must report this |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked for energy consumption, do NOT forget to multiply by the **regenerative-braking recovery factor** (typically 0.05 to 0.25 of total tractive energy). Skipping it is the #1 reason for full-mark loss in Part B derivations.

### 2.5 Validation Facilities Used by OEMs

| Facility | Purpose | Test Type |
|----------|---------|-----------|
| Chassis Dynamometer (Dyno) | Range & energy on simulated cycles | Indoor |
| Climatic Chamber (Hot/Cold) | Soak, cold-start, AC performance | Environmental |
| Salt Spray Cabinet | Corrosion (≥ 1000 hrs) | Durability |
| Crash Test Facility | Frontal, side, rear | Safety |
| 4-Poster Shaker | Suspension NVH, road load simulation | Durability |
| EMC Anechoic Chamber | Radiated/conducted emissions | Regulatory |
| Battery Abuse Lab | Nail, crush, overcharge, thermal runaway | EV Safety |
| Proving Ground (e.g., NATRIP) | High-speed, durability tracks | Vehicle |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — EV Range from Battery Energy (Foundation Equation)

The most fundamental KTU 2024 derivation. We begin with the energy balance of an electric vehicle traversing distance $R$ on a flat road at constant speed $v$.

**Step 1 — Identify the resisting forces (neglect inertia and grade):**
The total resistance force $F_{resist}$ on a level road is the sum of rolling resistance and aerodynamic drag.

$$
F_{resist} = F_{roll} + F_{aero}
$$

**Step 2 — Expand each term:**

$$
F_{resist} = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} \cdot \cos\theta \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2} \rho \cdot C_{d} \cdot A_{f} \cdot v^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Mechanical work required over distance R (Joules):**

$$
W_{mech} = F_{resist} \cdot R
$$

**Step 4 — Account for drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{tot}$:**

The mechanical energy that must leave the battery is divided by the round-trip efficiency of the motor, transmission, and inverter.

$$
E_{bat} = \dfrac{W_{mech}}{\eta_{tot}}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the energy expression:**

$$
E_{bat} = \dfrac{F_{resist} \cdot R}{\eta_{tot}}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for R (the vehicle range):**

$$
R = \dfrac{E_{bat} \cdot \eta_{tot}}{F_{roll} + F_{aero}}
$$

This is the canonical KTU 2024 range equation. For variable-speed cycles, $F_{resist}$ is integrated over the drive cycle.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Acceleration Time (0 to $v_{f}$)

Used for validating the **0–100 km/h (or 0–60 mph) acceleration** spec. Newton's second law on the vehicle:

$$
m \cdot \delta \cdot \dfrac{dv}{dt} = F_{tractive} - F_{resist}
$$

**Step 1 — Tractive force from peak motor power $P_{peak}$:**

The motor can deliver a peak tractive force that depends on speed; for simplicity, take the force needed at each instant as $P_{peak}/v$ (limited by the power envelope).

$$
F_{tractive} = \dfrac{P_{peak}}{v}
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange the equation of motion:**

$$
dt = \dfrac{m \cdot \delta \, v \, dv}{P_{peak} - F_{resist} \, v}
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from $v_1$ to $v_2$:**

$$
t_a = \int_{v_{1}}^{v_{2}} \dfrac{m \cdot \delta \, v}{P_{peak} - F_{resist} \, v} \, dv
$$

**Step 4 — Constant-speed simplification** (assume $F_{resist}$ constant over the speed range):

$$
t_a = \dfrac{m \cdot \delta}{F_{resist}} \int_{v_{1}}^{v_{2}} \dfrac{v \, dv}{\dfrac{P_{peak}}{F_{resist}} - v}
$$

**Step 5 — Final closed-form result:**

$$
t_a = -\dfrac{m \cdot \delta}{F_{resist}} \left[ \left( \dfrac{P_{peak}}{F_{resist}} - v \right) \ln \left( \dfrac{P_{peak}}{F_{resist}} - v \right) + v \right]_{v_{1}}^{v_{2}}
$$

In KTU exams, numerical evaluation with the trapezoidal rule is acceptable as a substitute for the closed form.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Required Traction Power for a Given Grade

Used for **gradability** validation (e.g., 30% grade at 30 km/h).

**Step 1 — Total resistance on a grade $\theta$:**

$$
F_{resist}(\theta) = m g C_{rr} \cos\theta + m g \sin\theta + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_{d} A_{f} v^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Required mechanical power at speed $v$ (in m/s):**

$$
P_{mech} = F_{resist}(\theta) \cdot v
$$

**Step 3 — Required electrical power from the battery:**

$$
P_{elec} = \dfrac{P_{mech}}{\eta_{mot} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{trans}}
$$

This output must be **less than** the motor's continuous rating for sustained operation. If $P_{elec} > P_{cont}$, the test fails the validation spec.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Validation Test vs. KPI Tracked

| Test Type | Key KPI | Accept Criterion | Test Standard |
|-----------|---------|------------------|---------------|
| Cold-start (EV) | Time to ready, range at −20°C | ≥ 70% nominal range | AIS-156 |
| Range (EV) | km on MIDC / WLTP | ≥ declared value − 5% | AIS-039 |
| Energy Economy | kWh/100 km | ≤ declared + 5% | ARAI Std. |
| Acceleration 0–60 | Seconds | ≤ declared value | SAE J1494 |
| Top Speed | km/h | ≥ declared value | ECE R68 |
| Brake Stopping | Distance from 100 → 0 km/h | ≤ 50 m (typical) | ECE R13H |
| Crash Frontal | HIC, chest deflection | Within AIS-096 limits | AIS-096 |
| Water Intrusion | IP rating | IP67 for HV components | ISO 20653 |
| EMC | Radiated emission dBµV/m | Below ECE R10 limit | ECE R10 |
| Battery Thermal Runaway | Time to propagation | ≥ 5 minutes warning | AIS-156 |

---

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 2024 Style)

> **Problem:** An electric car has $m = 1500$ kg, $C_{d} = 0.28$, $A_{f} = 2.2$ m², $C_{rr} = 0.010$, $\eta_{tot} = 0.85$, and a usable battery of **40 kWh**. Compute the **WLTP range** at a constant **80 km/h** on a flat road. Assume $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ and $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Solution:**

$$
v = \dfrac{80 \times 1000}{3600} = 22.22 \; \text{m/s}
$$

$$
F_{roll} = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.010 = 147.15 \; \text{N}
$$

$$
F_{aero} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.28 \times 2.2 \times (22.22)^{2}
$$

$$
F_{aero} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.28 \times 2.2 \times 493.83 = 186.39 \; \text{N}
$$

$$
F_{resist} = 147.15 + 186.39 = 333.54 \; \text{N}
$$

$$
E_{100} = \dfrac{F_{resist} \times 100{,}000}{\eta_{tot} \times 3.6 \times 10^{6}} \;\; \text{kWh/100 km}
$$

Converting mechanical work to kWh: 1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J.

$$
\text{Work per 100 km} = 333.54 \times 100{,}000 = 3.3354 \times 10^{7} \; \text{J}
$$

$$
E_{100} = \dfrac{3.3354 \times 10^{7}}{0.85 \times 3.6 \times 10^{6}} = 10.89 \; \text{kWh/100 km}
$$

$$
\text{Range} = \dfrac{40}{10.89} \times 100 = 367.3 \; \text{km}
$$

**Validation Acceptance:** A real WLTP result on this spec is typically **~330–360 km** (cycle is not constant speed), so the calculated value passes the validation envelope.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Automotive V-Model — Position of Vehicle Validation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle Level Requirements] --> B[System Level Requirements]
    B --> C[Subsystem Level Requirements]
    C --> D[Component Level Design]
    D --> D1[Battery Cell Design]
    D --> D2[Motor Design]
    D --> D3[Brake System Design]
    D --> D4[Chassis Design]
    D1 --> E1[Component Validation]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E1
    E1 --> E2[Subsystem Validation]
    E2 --> E3[System Validation]
    E3 --> E4[Vehicle Validation on Proving Ground]
    E4 --> E5[Homologation and Type Approval]
    E5 --> F[Series Production Release]
    A -.-> E4
    B -.-> E3
    C -.-> E2
```

### 4.2 Vehicle Validation Process Flow (End-to-End)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Build Validation Proto VP1] --> B[Engineering Validation EVT]
    B --> C[Design Validation DVP and S]
    C --> D[Pre Production Validation PPT]
    D --> E[Homologation and Certification]
    E --> F[Mass Production Start of Production SOP]

    subgraph "Pillar 1: Performance"
    P1[Top Speed] --> P2[Range and Energy] --> P3[Acceleration]
    end

    subgraph "Pillar 2: Durability"
    Q1[4 Poster Shaker] --> Q2[Stone Chip] --> Q3[Corrosion 1000 hr]
    end

    subgraph "Pillar 3: Safety"
    R1[Frontal Crash] --> R2[Side Crash] --> R3[Pedestrian Protection]
    end

    subgraph "Pillar 4: Environmental"
    S1[Cold Start minus 20C] --> S2[Hot Soak plus 50C] --> S3[EMC Chamber]
    end

    subgraph "Pillar 5: EV Specific"
    T1[Nail Penetration] --> T2[Thermal Runaway] --> T3[IP67 Immersion]
    end

    B --> P1
    B --> P2
    B --> P3
    C --> Q1
    C --> Q2
    C --> Q3
    C --> R1
    C --> R2
    C --> R3
    D --> S1
    D --> S2
    D --> S3
    D --> T1
    D --> T2
    D --> T3
```

### 4.3 Validation Test Pyramid — Level and Cost

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Component Test Lab] -->|Lower Cost| L2[Subsystem Test HIL]
    L2 -->|Medium Cost| L3[System Test Dyno Bench]
    L3 -->|Higher Cost| L4[Full Vehicle Test Proving Ground]
    L4 -->|Highest Cost| L5[Fleet Test Real World Customers]

    L1 -.->|Many Prototypes| X1[Hundreds of units]
    L2 -.->|Fewer Prototypes| X2[Dozens of units]
    L3 -.->|Limited Prototypes| X3[Tens of units]
    L4 -.->|Few Prototypes| X4[5 to 10 units]
    L5 -.->|Very Few| X5[2 to 3 units]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Diagram Insight:** Notice the inverse relationship between test level and number of prototypes. The higher up the pyramid (towards vehicle validation), the fewer prototypes you build, because each test is more expensive and more comprehensive.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3 Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Vehicle Verification and Vehicle Validation. Provide one example test for each.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Verification:** Confirms that the product is built correctly as per the design specifications. Question answered: *"Are we building the product right?"* Example: Checking the bolt torque on the wheel hub against the drawing (3,500 ± 50 Nm).
- **Validation:** Confirms that the right product has been built to meet customer needs. Question answered: *"Are we building the right product?"* Example: A 1,00,000 km durability test on a proving ground to confirm that the car survives real-world Indian road conditions for the warranted life.
- Key distinction: Verification is **specification-driven**; Validation is **usage-driven**.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any three EV-specific validation tests mandated by AIS-156 (India).**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Nail penetration test** on a fully-charged cell to ensure no fire/explosion.
2. **Thermal runaway propagation test** — to confirm that thermal runaway in one cell does not cascade to adjacent cells within 5 minutes.
3. **Overcharge and over-discharge test** to confirm BMS (Battery Management System) intervention.
4. (Any one of: IP67 immersion, vibration test, drop test, external short circuit.)

---

### Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Adapted]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a neatly labelled diagram, explain the **Automotive V-Model** of product development. Highlight the position of *Vehicle Validation* and explain the four levels of testing that build up to it.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An electric car with the following data must be validated for **range** at a constant 60 km/h on a flat road:
- Mass $m = 1200$ kg, $C_{d} = 0.30$, $A_{f} = 2.1$ m², $C_{rr} = 0.012$
- Drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{tot} = 0.82$
- Usable battery energy $E_{bat} = 35$ kWh
- Air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

Compute the validated range in km and the energy consumption in kWh/100 km.

**Model Solution (a):**
- Draw the V-Model (mirror image): left arm (decomposition) — Vehicle Requirements → System Requirements → Subsystem Requirements → Component Design; right arm (integration) — Component Validation → Subsystem Validation → System Validation → Vehicle Validation.
- Explain **traceability** — each validation level corresponds to a requirement level on the left.
- Position of Vehicle Validation: top right corner, post-system-validation.
- Four levels: (i) Component (cell, IC, sensor); (ii) Subsystem (BMS, inverter, brake); (iii) System (powertrain on dyno); (iv) Full Vehicle (proving ground + homologation).
- Conclude: Vehicle validation is the apex acceptance gate before SOP (Start of Production).

**Model Solution (b):**
- $v = 60 \times 1000/3600 = 16.67$ m/s
- $F_{roll} = 1200 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 = 141.26$ N
- $F_{aero} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.1 \times (16.67)^2 = 107.13$ N
- $F_{resist} = 248.39$ N
- Mechanical work for 1 km = $248.39 \times 1000 = 2.4839 \times 10^5$ J
- Energy required from battery per km = $\dfrac{2.4839 \times 10^5}{0.82 \times 3.6 \times 10^6} = 0.0841$ kWh/km
- **Energy consumption:** $E_{100} = 8.41$ kWh/100 km
- **Range:** $R = 35 / 0.0841 = 416.2$ km

**Validation Acceptance:** If the marketing claim was 400 km, the calculated 416 km passes with 4% margin (typical ARAI acceptance is ±5%).

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **five pillars of vehicle validation** with suitable examples for each. Why is environmental testing (Pillar 5) particularly critical for Electric Vehicles?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A two-wheeler electric vehicle with $m = 130$ kg, $C_{d} = 0.45$, $A_{f} = 0.5$ m², $C_{rr} = 0.015$, $\eta_{tot} = 0.78$, and battery $E_{bat} = 3.0$ kWh must achieve a guaranteed range of **80 km** at 45 km/h. Calculate whether the design **passes the validation acceptance** (assume ±5% tolerance on declared range).

**Model Solution (a):**
- **Pillar 1 — Performance:** Range, top speed, gradability. Example: WLTP cycle run on chassis dyno.
- **Pillar 2 — Durability:** 4-poster shaker, 1000 hr salt spray. Example: 1,00,000 km accelerated durability.
- **Pillar 3 — Safety:** Frontal and side crash, ESC, ABS. Example: AIS-096 frontal offset crash at 56 km/h.
- **Pillar 4 — Regulatory/Homologation:** CMVR type approval, ARAI certification. Example: AIS-156 for battery.
- **Pillar 5 — Environmental/EMC:** −40 °C cold start, +85 °C hot soak, IP67, ECE R10 EMC.
- **Why critical for EV:** Battery performance is highly temperature-sensitive (range drops 30–50% at −20 °C); HV isolation resistance must be maintained under humidity; EMC affects BMS readings and motor controller.

**Model Solution (b):**
- $v = 45 \times 1000/3600 = 12.5$ m/s
- $F_{roll} = 130 \times 9.81 \times 0.015 = 19.13$ N
- $F_{aero} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.45 \times 0.5 \times (12.5)^2 = 21.53$ N
- $F_{resist} = 40.66$ N
- Energy/km = $\dfrac{40.66 \times 1000}{0.78 \times 3.6 \times 10^6} = 0.01448$ kWh/km
- **Calculated Range:** $R = 3.0 / 0.01448 = 207.2$ km
- **Declared Range:** 80 km
- **Tolerance band:** 80 × 0.95 = 76 km (lower limit) to 80 × 1.05 = 84 km (upper limit)
- **Validation Verdict:** Calculated 207.2 km >> 84 km → **DESIGN PASSES** validation with a huge safety margin. Engineers may consider down-sizing the battery to 1.5 kWh for cost optimisation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing Verification with Validation:** Writing "checking bolt torque" as a validation example is a guaranteed −2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{tot}$:** Range derivations without $\eta_{tot}$ lose 3–4 marks.
> 3. **Not converting kW to W or km/h to m/s:** This is the single biggest arithmetic error in Part B; double-check units first.
> 4. **Confusing NEDC with WLTP:** NEDC is obsolete; WLTP is current. Saying "NEDC" in 2024 papers is marked wrong.
> 5. **Missing the SoC window:** A range test that consumes the battery from 100% to 5% is NOT the same as 100% to 0% — always declare the SoC window.
> 6. **Skipping regenerative braking credit:** In real EV tests, the test cycle result is 10–20% better than the resistive-force calculation; stating the latter as the final answer without acknowledging regen loses marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Validation** is the final acceptance phase; **Verification** is the design-conformance phase — never confuse them.
- The **V-Model** is the standard reference for explaining where validation sits; draw it cleanly in the exam.
- **Five pillars:** Performance, Durability, Safety, Regulatory/Homologation, Environmental/EMC.
- For **EVs**, additional tests are: range (WLTP/MIDC), energy consumption, battery abuse (AIS-156), HV isolation, thermal management, regen efficiency, and EMC.
- The **canonical range equation** is $R = \dfrac{E_{bat} \cdot \eta_{tot}}{F_{roll} + F_{aero}}$ — memorize the numerator and denominator in this exact form.
- **$C_{pk} \geq 1.33$** is the typical validation acceptance threshold; **$C_{pk} \geq 1.67$** is preferred for safety-critical.
- The **tolerance band** for declared range is typically **±5%** for ARAI-style certifications.
- **Cost pyramid:** Higher validation level = fewer prototypes = more expensive per test.
- Always state **assumptions** (flat road, no wind, constant speed, no accessories) before solving a range or energy problem.
- **Cold-weather range penalty** in EVs can be 30–50% — always mention this in long-answer questions.
- **Homologation** is the regulatory sub-set of validation that produces the type-approval certificate; without it, the car cannot be sold legally.
- **Traceability** is the OBE mantra: every validation test must trace back to a written requirement.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: System Integration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT631 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** System Integration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of System Integration in Electric & Hybrid Vehicles

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**System Integration** in the context of Electric and Hybrid Electric Vehicles (EVs/HEVs) is the **holistic engineering discipline** that unifies the mechanical, electrical, electronic, thermal, and control subsystems of a vehicle into a single, optimized, and coherent operating entity. It involves the architectural design, communication networking, energy management, and functional co-ordination of all major powertrain and chassis sub-assemblies to deliver the intended vehicle-level performance, safety, efficiency, and drivability targets defined by the Original Equipment Manufacturer (OEM).

Mathematically, it can be represented as the **integration operator** $\mathcal{I}$ acting on the set of all vehicle subsystems $\mathcal{S}$:

$$
\mathcal{V} = \mathcal{I}\big(\mathcal{S}_{mech} \cup \mathcal{S}_{elec} \cup \mathcal{S}_{elec} \cup \mathcal{S}_{ctrl} \cup \mathcal{S}_{therm} \cup \mathcal{S}_{comm}\big)
$$

Where the integrated vehicle $\mathcal{V}$ is greater than the simple sum of its parts due to **synergistic cross-subsystem coupling**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEMUT631 Module 4):**
> System Integration refers to the **architectural synthesis** of the electric drivetrain, energy storage system (ESS), Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) [for HEVs], power electronics, on-board chargers, thermal management modules, and supervisory controllers into a unified vehicle platform governed by a **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU)** via standardized communication protocols such as **CAN 2.0B / CAN FD / Automotive Ethernet**.

## 1.2 Key Subsystems Involved in Integration

For KTU examination purposes, the student must recognize the following **6 core subsystem blocks** that participate in system integration:

| # | Subsystem | Primary Function | KTU Designation |
|---|-----------|------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Energy Storage System (ESS) | Battery + BMS | $\mathcal{S}_{ess}$ |
| 2 | Electric Machine & Inverter | Traction motor drive | $\mathcal{S}_{em}$ |
| 3 | ICE + Transmission (HEVs) | Range extender / primary mover | $\mathcal{S}_{ice}$ |
| 4 | Power Electronics & DC-DC | Voltage conversion | $\mathcal{S}_{pe}$ |
| 5 | Thermal Management | Battery, motor, inverter cooling | $\mathcal{S}_{therm}$ |
| 6 | VCU / Supervisory Controller | Energy management strategy | $\mathcal{S}_{ctrl}$ |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body:**
> Imagine the human body. The heart (battery), lungs (ICE), muscles (electric motor), nerves (CAN bus), and brain (VCU) each work perfectly in isolation. However, walking requires the *integrated* coordination of all of them simultaneously. If the brain sends signals too slowly (communication latency) or the lungs fail to deliver oxygen (energy starvation), the body collapses. **System Integration in EVs is exactly this** — making sure that the battery delivers the right power at the right time, the motor responds to driver demand, the ICE supplements energy when needed, and the cooling system removes heat — all orchestrated by the VCU over a real-time communication network.

## 1.4 Why System Integration is a Mandatory KTU Module

In modern EVs, neglecting integration leads to:

- **Packaging inefficiency** — components occupy excessive volume, reducing cabin/boot space.
- **Thermal hot-spots** — battery cells degrade 2× faster above **45 °C**.
- **Communication bottlenecks** — message latency exceeding **10 ms** causes jerky torque transitions.
- **EMC interference** — high-frequency inverter switching (typically **5–20 kHz**) corrupts low-voltage sensor signals.
- **Reduced drive range** — uncoordinated energy flow reduces **Wh/km** efficiency by **15–25 %**.

Hence, KTU 2024 scheme dedicates this module to teach the **top-down design methodology** that binds these subsystems together.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Layers of System Integration

System integration in EVs/HEVs is conventionally organized into **four hierarchical layers**. KTU examiners frequently test this layered understanding.

### Layer 1 — Hardware Integration Layer (Physical)
This is the lowest physical layer. It deals with:
- Mechanical packaging of battery modules, motor, inverter, and DC-DC converter.
- High-voltage (HV) cabling routing and **creepage distances** (typically $\geq 8$ mm for **400 V** systems per IEC 60664).
- Low-voltage (LV) **12 V / 24 V** auxiliary network.
- Crash structure and HV interlock loop (**HVIL**).

### Layer 2 — Energy Integration Layer (Power Flow)
Concerned with **power flow management** between energy sources and sinks. Uses the well-known **backward-facing** and **forward-facing** power equations:

$$
P_{demand}(t) = P_{batt}(t) + P_{ice}(t) \cdot \eta_{ice} - P_{aux}(t) - P_{loss}(t)
$$

Where:
- $P_{demand}(t)$ = instantaneous traction power demand at wheels
- $P_{batt}(t)$ = power drawn from battery
- $P_{ice}(t)$ = mechanical power from ICE
- $\eta_{ice}$ = ICE thermal efficiency
- $P_{aux}(t)$ = auxiliary loads (HVAC, lighting, infotainment)
- $P_{loss}(t)$ = combined drivetrain + thermal losses

### Layer 3 — Communication Integration Layer
Uses serial communication protocols. The dominant standard is the **Controller Area Network (CAN)** with bit-rates of:
- **Classical CAN:** 125 kbps to 1 Mbps
- **CAN FD:** up to **5 Mbps**
- **Automotive Ethernet:** up to **1 Gbps** (used in modern ADAS platforms)

### Layer 4 — Control Integration Layer (Software)
The **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU)** sits at the apex. It runs the **Energy Management Strategy (EMS)** — either rule-based or optimization-based — to decide:
- Power split between ICE and motor (HEVs)
- Battery State of Charge (SOC) target window
- Regenerative braking intensity
- Mode selection: EV / Hybrid / Charge-Depleting / Charge-Sustaining

## 2.2 Integration Topologies

The KTU 2024 syllabus recognizes the following **canonical integration topologies**:

| Topology | Configuration | KTU Example |
|----------|--------------|-------------|
| Series Hybrid | Engine → Generator → Battery → Motor → Wheels | Chevrolet Volt (Gen 1) |
| Parallel Hybrid | Engine + Motor → Common Driveline | Honda Insight (Gen 1) |
| Series-Parallel (PS) | Both paths active, planetary gear | Toyota Prius (THS) |
| BEV | Battery → Inverter → Motor → Wheels | Tata Nexon EV |
| FCEV | Fuel Cell + Battery → Inverter → Motor | Toyota Mirai |
| PHEV | Series + Parallel with plug-in charging | Toyota Prius Prime |

## 2.3 Communication Bus Arbitration

The CAN protocol uses **CSMA/CR** (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Resolution) via a non-destructive bit-wise arbitration. The priority of a message is determined by its **11-bit (or 29-bit) identifier**. The message with the **lowest numerical ID wins** arbitration.

The bus arbitration time $t_{arb}$ is:

$$
t_{arb} = \frac{N_{bits} \cdot t_q}{1}
$$

Where:
- $N_{bits}$ = number of arbitration bits (typically **11** for standard CAN)
- $t_q$ = time quantum, defined as:

$$
t_q = 2 \cdot \frac{1}{f_{clk}} \cdot BRP
$$

Where $BRP$ is the **Baud Rate Prescaler** and $f_{clk}$ is the CAN controller clock frequency (typically **8 MHz, 16 MHz, or 40 MHz**).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **definitive formula reference** for any KTU numerical on System Integration. Memorize every row.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Typical Value |
|---|---------|---------|-------|---------------|
| 1 | Total Drivetrain Efficiency | $\eta_{sys} = \eta_{batt} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{mot} \cdot \eta_{trans}$ | dimensionless | **0.75 – 0.88** |
| 2 | Power Demand at Wheels | $P_w = F_t \cdot v = (M g f_r + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 + M a) \cdot v$ | W | vehicle dependent |
| 3 | Rolling Resistance Force | $F_r = M g f_r \cos\theta$ | N | $f_r \approx 0.008$–$0.015$ |
| 4 | Aerodynamic Drag Force | $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$ | N | $C_d A \approx 0.6$–$0.8$ m² |
| 5 | Climbing Force | $F_c = M g \sin\theta$ | N | depends on slope |
| 6 | Acceleration Force | $F_a = M a$ | N | $a$ in m/s² |
| 7 | Battery Energy | $E_{batt} = V_{nom} \cdot Q_{Ah} \cdot 3600$ | J | or in Wh: $E = V \cdot Q$ |
| 8 | Battery Usable SOC Window | $\Delta SOC = SOC_{max} - SOC_{min}$ | dimensionless | typically **0.8** (20 % – 100 %) |
| 9 | Battery C-rate | $C = \dfrac{I_{discharge}}{Q_{Ah}}$ | h⁻¹ | EV: **1C – 3C** |
| 10 | Heat Generated in Battery | $Q_{batt} = I^2 R_{int} = I^2 \cdot \dfrac{V_{nom}}{P_{max}}$ | W | thermal model dependent |
| 11 | Time Quantum (CAN) | $t_q = 2 \cdot BRP \cdot \dfrac{1}{f_{clk}}$ | s | typically **125 ns – 1 μs** |
| 12 | CAN Bit Time | $t_{bit} = t_{q} \cdot (1 + t_{seg1} + t_{seg2})$ | s | nominal bit time |
| 13 | Maximum Bus Length (CAN) | $L_{max} = \dfrac{t_{prop}}{v_{prop} \cdot 5 \text{ ns/m}}$ | m | up to **40 m at 1 Mbps** |
| 14 | Torque-Speed (Motor) | $P_{mot} = T_{mot} \cdot \omega_{mot}$ | W | constant-power region |
| 15 | Regenerative Braking Recovery | $E_{regen} = \int \eta_{reg} \cdot F_{brake} \cdot v \, dt$ | J | $\eta_{reg} \approx 0.6$–$0.8$ |

## 2.5 The 3 Pillars of Integration Engineering

System Integration rests on **three engineering pillars**, all of which are KTU-favorite short-answer topics:

### Pillar 1 — Functional Integration
Combining multiple electronic functions onto fewer **ECU (Electronic Control Unit)** hardware platforms. Modern EVs use **zonal architectures** where one zone controller handles lighting, doors, climate, and infotainment. This reduces wiring harness mass by **20–30 %** and improves reliability.

### Pillar 2 — Energy Integration
Implementing smart **Energy Management Strategies (EMS)** to split power between sources. Common EMS algorithms:
- **Rule-based (deterministic)** — e.g., "if SOC < 30 % then start ICE"
- **ECMS (Equivalent Consumption Minimization Strategy)** — instantaneous optimization
- **DP (Dynamic Programming)** — global offline optimization
- **RL (Reinforcement Learning)** — modern AI-based online learning

### Pillar 3 — Thermal Integration
A unified thermal loop using **glycol coolant** shared between battery pack, inverter, and motor. The coolant flow rate $Q$ is given by:

$$
Q = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}{\rho \cdot h_{fg}}
$$

Where $\dot{m}$ is mass flow rate, $c_p$ is specific heat, and $\Delta T$ is temperature rise across the heat exchanger.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total System Efficiency of an Integrated EV Powertrain

This is a frequently-asked KTU 14-mark derivation. We derive the **overall drivetrain efficiency** for a series hybrid or BEV powertrain from first principles.

### Statement
The total efficiency $\eta_{sys}$ of an integrated electric powertrain is the **product of efficiencies** of every energy-conversion stage from the battery terminals to the wheel contact patch.

### Given
- Battery coulombic efficiency: $\eta_{batt} = 0.98$
- Inverter efficiency: $\eta_{inv} = 0.96$
- Motor efficiency: $\eta_{mot} = 0.94$
- Transmission efficiency: $\eta_{trans} = 0.97$

### Step 1 — Define the energy flow path
For a BEV, the energy path is:
Battery (DC) → Inverter (DC→AC) → Motor (Electrical→Mechanical) → Transmission (Mechanical) → Wheels

### Step 2 — Write power at each node
Let the mechanical power at the wheel be $P_w$.

Power input to motor (electrical) is:
$$
P_{mot,in} = \frac{P_w}{\eta_{mot} \cdot \eta_{trans}}
$$

Power input to inverter (DC side) is:
$$
P_{inv,in} = \frac{P_{mot,in}}{\eta_{inv}} = \frac{P_w}{\eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{mot} \cdot \eta_{trans}}
$$

Power output of battery (at terminals) is:
$$
P_{batt,out} = \frac{P_{inv,in}}{\eta_{batt}} = \frac{P_w}{\eta_{batt} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{mot} \cdot \eta_{trans}}
$$

### Step 3 — Define system efficiency
By definition:
$$
\eta_{sys} = \frac{P_w}{P_{batt,out}}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute
$$
\eta_{sys} = \eta_{batt} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{mot} \cdot \eta_{trans}
$$

### Step 5 — Numerical evaluation
$$
\eta_{sys} = 0.98 \times 0.96 \times 0.94 \times 0.97
$$

Breaking the multiplication:
$$
0.98 \times 0.96 = 0.9408
$$

$$
0.9408 \times 0.94 = 0.88435
$$

$$
0.88435 \times 0.97 = 0.85782
$$

### Result
$$
\boxed{\eta_{sys} \approx 0.858 \text{ or } 85.8 \,\%}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This ~86 % is **3–4× higher** than a conventional ICE vehicle (~25–35 %), which is the fundamental reason EVs are thermodynamically superior.

## 3.2 Derivation: Energy Management for a Series Hybrid

A **series hybrid** has ICE coupled *only* to a generator. The traction motor is the *only* path to the wheels. We wish to minimize equivalent fuel consumption.

### Energy balance at the DC bus
$$
P_{batt}(t) + P_{gen}(t) = \dfrac{P_{mot}(t)}{\eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{mot}} + P_{aux}(t)
$$

Where $P_{gen}(t) = \eta_{gen} \cdot P_{ice}(t)$ is the generator output power.

### Equivalent fuel rate (ECMS strategy)
The **equivalent fuel consumption** of the battery is:
$$
\dot{m}_{eq,batt} = \dot{m}_{fuel,batt} = \dfrac{P_{batt} \cdot s}{Q_{lhv}}
$$

Where:
- $s$ = equivalence factor (dimensionless, typically **1.5 – 2.5**)
- $Q_{lhv}$ = lower heating value of fuel (gasoline: **44 MJ/kg**)

### Total instantaneous fuel rate
$$
\dot{m}_{tot} = \dfrac{P_{ice}}{Q_{lhv}} + s \cdot \dfrac{P_{batt}}{Q_{lhv}}
$$

### Optimization objective
$$
\min_{P_{ice}(t)} \int_{0}^{T} \dot{m}_{tot}(t) \, dt
$$

Subject to SOC constraint:
$$
SOC(t) \in [SOC_{min}, SOC_{max}]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU, the student should remember the form of the ECMS cost function: a linear combination of ICE fuel rate and a *battery-equivalent* fuel rate, weighted by the equivalence factor $s$. This is a high-frequency exam topic.

## 3.3 Derivation: CAN Bus Time Quantum and Bit Time

### Step 1 — Identify the source clock
Assume the CAN peripheral is clocked at $f_{clk} = 40$ MHz.

### Step 2 — Set the Baud Rate Prescaler
For BRP = 4:
$$
t_q = 2 \cdot \dfrac{BRP}{f_{clk}} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{4}{40 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
t_q = 2 \cdot 1 \times 10^{-7} = 2 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} = 200 \text{ ns}
$$

### Step 3 — Define nominal bit time
For a bit rate of **500 kbps**, the nominal bit time is:
$$
t_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{500 \times 10^{3}} = 2 \, \mu s
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $t_{seg1} + t_{seg2}$
$$
t_{bit} = t_q \cdot (1 + t_{seg1} + t_{seg2})
$$

$$
2 \times 10^{-6} = 2 \times 10^{-7} \cdot (1 + t_{seg1} + t_{seg2})
$$

$$
1 + t_{seg1} + t_{seg2} = 10
$$

$$
t_{seg1} + t_{seg2} = 9
$$

A common choice is $t_{seg1} = 6$ and $t_{seg2} = 3$ (sample point at 70 %), but $t_{seg1} = 5, t_{seg2} = 4$ (sample point at 60 %) is also valid.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Mode Selection Logic in a PHEV

The following table shows the **mode arbitration logic** inside a typical VCU. KTU often asks to draw or describe this matrix.

| Vehicle Speed | SOC (%) | Torque Demand | Accelerator Pedal | VCU Mode |
|---------------|---------|---------------|-------------------|----------|
| 0–20 km/h | > 25 % | Low | < 30 % | **EV (Pure Electric)** |
| 0–20 km/h | > 25 % | High | > 80 % | **EV + Boost (Motor + ICE)** |
| 20–80 km/h | < 25 % | Any | Any | **Hybrid (Charge-Sustaining)** |
| 80–120 km/h | Any | High | > 70 % | **ICE Primary + Motor Assist** |
| Decelerating | Any | Negative | 0 % | **Regenerative Braking** |
| Stationary, plugged | Any | None | 0 % | **Grid Charging (AC→DC)** |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical: Integrated EV Power Demand

> **Problem (KTU-style):** An electric vehicle has the following parameters:
> - Mass $M = 1500$ kg
> - Rolling resistance coefficient $f_r = 0.012$
> - Drag coefficient $C_d = 0.29$
> - Frontal area $A = 2.2$ m²
> - Air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³
> - Grade $\theta = 0°$ (flat road)
> - Acceleration $a = 1.0$ m/s²
> - Speed $v = 30$ m/s (~108 km/h)
> - Drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{sys} = 0.86$
>
> **Find:** (a) Traction force required, (b) Power at wheels, (c) Power demanded from battery.

### Solution

**Part (a) — Forces**

Rolling resistance:
$$
F_r = M g f_r = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 = 176.58 \text{ N}
$$

Aerodynamic drag:
$$
F_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.29 \times 2.2 \times 900
$$

Step-by-step:
$$
1.225 \times 0.29 = 0.35525
$$

$$
0.35525 \times 2.2 = 0.78155
$$

$$
0.78155 \times 900 = 703.395
$$

$$
F_d = 703.4 \text{ N}
$$

Acceleration force:
$$
F_a = M a = 1500 \times 1.0 = 1500 \text{ N}
$$

Total traction force (flat road, no grade):
$$
F_t = F_r + F_d + F_a = 176.58 + 703.4 + 1500 = 2379.98 \text{ N} \approx 2380 \text{ N}
$$

**Part (b) — Power at wheels**
$$
P_w = F_t \cdot v = 2380 \times 30 = 71{,}400 \text{ W} \approx 71.4 \text{ kW}
$$

**Part (c) — Power at battery terminals**
$$
P_{batt} = \frac{P_w}{\eta_{sys}} = \frac{71{,}400}{0.86} = 83{,}023.3 \text{ W} \approx 83.0 \text{ kW}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This single problem integrates the **Formula Sheet entries #1, #2, #3, #4, #5, and #6** — exactly the kind of integrated question KTU 2024 scheme asks in 14-mark form.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Define System Integration as applied to Electric and Hybrid Vehicles. List any four major subsystems that are integrated in a modern EV."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

System Integration in EVs/HEVs is the engineering discipline of combining and coordinating all major vehicle subsystems — mechanical, electrical, electronic, thermal, and control — into a single, optimized operating unit governed by a Vehicle Control Unit (VCU). (2 marks)

The four major subsystems integrated are:
1. Energy Storage System (Battery + BMS)
2. Traction Motor + Inverter
3. Power Electronics (DC-DC converter, On-board Charger)
4. Vehicle Control Unit with CAN-based communication network (1 mark)

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"What is the role of CAN protocol in EV system integration? Mention its typical bit rate."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Controller Area Network (CAN)** is the primary in-vehicle communication bus used to integrate all ECUs (Electronic Control Units) of an EV/HEV. It enables real-time, prioritized, message-based data exchange between the VCU, BMS, motor inverter, BMS, charger, and other modules over a **twisted-pair differential bus**, eliminating the need for complex point-to-point wiring. (2 marks)

The classical CAN bit rate is **125 kbps to 1 Mbps**, while CAN-FD can go up to **5 Mbps**. (1 mark)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **system architecture of a Series-Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle**. Identify and describe the function of the **Planetary Gear Set (Power Split Device)** in this architecture.

**(b) [7 marks]** A BEV has the following parameters during a constant-speed cruise at $v = 25$ m/s on a flat road:
- Vehicle mass $M = 1200$ kg
- $C_d A = 0.65$ m², $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³
- Rolling coefficient $f_r = 0.010$
- No grade, no acceleration
- $\eta_{batt} = 0.98$, $\eta_{inv} = 0.95$, $\eta_{mot} = 0.93$, $\eta_{trans} = 0.96$

Calculate: (i) Traction force, (ii) Power at wheels, (iii) Power drawn from battery, (iv) Total drivetrain efficiency.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Series-Parallel HEV Architecture (7 marks)**

A **Series-Parallel Hybrid** (e.g., Toyota Hybrid System / THS) uses a **planetary gear set (PGS)** as the Power Split Device (PSD). The PGS has three ports:
- **Sun gear** — connected to Motor-Generator 1 (MG1)
- **Planet carrier** — connected to the Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)
- **Ring gear** — connected to Motor-Generator 2 (MG2) and the driveline

**Functions of the PGS (3 marks):**
1. Continuously variable power split between ICE and MG2 without a stepped transmission.
2. MG1 acts as a **regulator**, absorbing the difference between ICE speed and vehicle speed, thus controlling the engine operating point.
3. Acts as a **torque summing device** at the ring gear: $T_{ring} = T_{ICE} + T_{MG2}$ (in appropriate sign convention).

**Modes of operation (2 marks):**
- **EV mode** — ICE off, MG2 alone drives the vehicle.
- **Hybrid mode** — ICE runs at optimal point, MG1 regulates speed, MG2 adds/subtracts torque.
- **Regeneration** — MG2 acts as a generator during braking.
- **Charging mode** — ICE drives MG1 to charge the battery while MG2 propels the vehicle.

**Block diagram should include** ICE, MG1, MG2, PGS, Battery, Inverter, VCU, Wheels, and signal arrows. (2 marks)

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**(i) Traction Force**

Rolling resistance:
$$
F_r = M g f_r = 1200 \times 9.81 \times 0.010 = 117.72 \text{ N}
$$

Aerodynamic drag:
$$
F_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.65 \times 625
$$

$$
= 0.6 \times 0.65 \times 625 = 0.39 \times 625 = 243.75 \text{ N}
$$

No grade, no acceleration, so:
$$
F_t = F_r + F_d = 117.72 + 243.75 = 361.47 \text{ N}
$$

**(ii) Power at wheels**
$$
P_w = F_t \cdot v = 361.47 \times 25 = 9036.75 \text{ W} \approx 9.04 \text{ kW}
$$

**(iii) Power drawn from battery**
$$
\eta_{sys} = \eta_{batt} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{mot} \cdot \eta_{trans}
$$

$$
= 0.98 \times 0.95 \times 0.93 \times 0.96
$$

Step-by-step:
$$
0.98 \times 0.95 = 0.931
$$

$$
0.931 \times 0.93 = 0.86583
$$

$$
0.86583 \times 0.96 = 0.83120
$$

$$
\eta_{sys} \approx 0.831
$$

$$
P_{batt} = \frac{P_w}{\eta_{sys}} = \frac{9036.75}{0.831} \approx 10{,}874.6 \text{ W} \approx 10.87 \text{ kW}
$$

**(iv) Total drivetrain efficiency**
$$
\boxed{\eta_{sys} \approx 83.1 \,\%}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice — Alternative]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **CAN bus protocol architecture** used in EV system integration. With a relevant equation, derive the **time quantum** and **nominal bit time**, given a clock frequency $f_{clk} = 40$ MHz, $BRP = 5$, $t_{seg1} = 13$, $t_{seg2} = 2$.

**(b) [7 marks]** Describe the **Energy Management Strategies (EMS)** used in HEVs. Compare **Rule-Based** and **ECMS** approaches with suitable equations.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — CAN Bus Architecture (7 marks)**

The CAN protocol is a **multi-master, message-prioritized, differential serial bus** standardized under **ISO 11898**. It uses a **non-return-to-zero (NRZ)** bit encoding with **bit stuffing**. (2 marks)

**Architecture layers (2 marks):**
- **Physical layer** — twisted pair with 120 Ω termination
- **Data link layer** — frame structure: SOF, ID (11/29 bits), RTR, Control, Data (0–8 bytes), CRC, ACK, EOF
- **Application layer** — CANopen, J1939 (commercial vehicles), or OEM-proprietary

**Time Quantum Derivation (3 marks):**
$$
t_q = 2 \cdot \dfrac{BRP}{f_{clk}} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{5}{40 \times 10^{6}} = 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} = 250 \text{ ns}
$$

**Nominal bit time:**
$$
t_{bit} = t_q \cdot (1 + t_{seg1} + t_{seg2}) = 250 \times 10^{-9} \cdot (1 + 13 + 2)
$$

$$
= 250 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 16 = 4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 4 \, \mu s
$$

**Bit rate:**
$$
BR = \frac{1}{t_{bit}} = \frac{1}{4 \times 10^{-6}} = 250{,}000 \text{ bps} = 250 \text{ kbps}
$$

---

**Part (b) — EMS Comparison (7 marks)**

| Feature | Rule-Based | ECMS (Equivalent Consumption Minimization) |
|---------|-----------|--------------------------------------------|
| **Strategy** | Deterministic IF-THEN rules | Instantaneous optimization of equivalent cost |
| **Real-time?** | Yes, fast | Yes, real-time |
| **Optimality** | Sub-optimal | Near-optimal |
| **Tuning** | Heuristic thresholds | Equivalence factor $s$ |
| **SOC handling** | Implicit (rule triggers) | Explicit via cost function |
| **Computation** | Negligible | Low (scalar minimization) |
| **Use case** | Production vehicles | Research, PHEVs |

**Rule-Based Example (2 marks):**
- IF SOC > 30 % AND torque demand < 50 % → EV mode
- IF SOC < 30 % OR torque demand > 70 % → Start ICE
- IF brake pedal > 5 % → Regen mode

**ECMS Cost Function (2 marks):**
$$
J(t) = \dot{m}_{fuel,ICE}(t) + s(t) \cdot \dot{m}_{eq,batt}(t)
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{eq,batt} = \dfrac{P_{batt}(t)}{Q_{lhv}}
$$

Where $s(t)$ is the time-varying equivalence factor, often adapted as:
$$
s(t) = s_0 - k \cdot (SOC(t) - SOC_{ref})
$$

This adaptive form penalizes deviations from a reference SOC.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in numerical problems** — ALWAYS write N, W, m/s, etc. (-1 mark per mistake)
> 2. **Confusing $C_d$ and $C_d A$** — Some problems give $C_d A$ directly. Read the problem statement twice.
> 3. **Mixing up gear ratio in planetary systems** — In Toyota THS, the planet carrier is *not* always the slowest member; it depends on whether MG1 is absorbing or generating.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s** — Multiply km/h by $\frac{5}{18}$ or divide by 3.6. KTU penalizes this.
> 5. **Wrong $g$** — Use **9.81 m/s²**, not 10 m/s². Examiners deduct 0.5 marks.
> 6. **Treating the system efficiency as additive** — It is **multiplicative**, never additive.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **System Integration = unification** of mechanical, electrical, electronic, thermal, and control subsystems under a VCU.
- [ ] **Six core subsystems** to memorize: ESS, Motor/Inverter, ICE, Power Electronics, Thermal, VCU.
- [ ] **Four architectural layers**: Hardware, Energy, Communication, Control.
- [ ] **System efficiency = product** of all stage efficiencies: $\eta_{sys} = \eta_{batt} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{mot} \cdot \eta_{trans} \approx 0.83$–$0.88$.
- [ ] **Five canonical topologies**: Series, Parallel, Series-Parallel (PS), BEV, FCEV, PHEV.
- [ ] **Power split device** in Toyota THS is a **planetary gear set** with three ports (Sun, Carrier, Ring).
- [ ] **CAN bus** uses CSMA/CR arbitration; bit rate up to **1 Mbps (Classical)**, **5 Mbps (FD)**, **1 Gbps (Ethernet)**.
- [ ] **Time quantum** $t_q = 2 \cdot BRP / f_{clk}$.
- [ ] **Bit time** $t_{bit} = t_q (1 + t_{seg1} + t_{seg2})$.
- [ ] **EMS types**: Rule-Based, ECMS, Dynamic Programming, Reinforcement Learning.
- [ ] **ECMS cost function** is a weighted sum of ICE fuel rate and equivalent battery fuel rate.
- [ ] **Standard battery SOC window** is **20 % – 80 %** (or 30 % – 70 %) for longevity.
- [ ] **C-rate** of an EV battery is typically **1C – 3C** during normal operation, peaks at **5C–10C** during regen.
- [ ] **Traction force** = $F_r + F_d + F_c + F_a$ (sum of all four resistive forces).
- [ ] **Use $g = 9.81$ m/s²**, not 10, in all numericals.
- [ ] **Convert km/h → m/s** by multiplying with $\frac{5}{18}$.
- [ ] **Regenerative braking** recovers up to **60 % – 80 %** of kinetic energy.
- [ ] **Thermal integration** uses a single shared coolant loop across battery, inverter, and motor.
- [ ] **Functional integration** reduces wiring harness mass by **20 % – 30 %** via zonal ECUs.
- [ ] **High-voltage (HV) interlock loop (HVIL)** is a mandatory safety feature in all modern EVs.
- [ ] **$Q_{lhv}$ for gasoline = 44 MJ/kg**; for diesel = **42.5 MJ/kg**; for hydrogen = **120 MJ/kg**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
