topics.title: Controller Area Networking (CAN) and Vector Tools Simulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT631 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Controller Area Networking (CAN) and Vector Tools Simulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Controller Area Network (CAN)** is a robust, multi-master, serial communication bus protocol originally developed by **Bosch (1986)** and standardized as **ISO 11898**. It is specifically engineered for real-time distributed control systems where multiple **Electronic Control Units (ECUs)** must exchange critical data over a single twisted-pair differential bus without the need for a host computer.

In the context of **Electric and Hybrid Vehicles (EV/HV)**, CAN serves as the **central nervous system** of the vehicle, enabling deterministic, priority-based communication between the **Battery Management System (BMS)**, **Motor Controller (Inverter)**, **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU)**, **On-Board Charger (OBC)**, and **DC-DC Converter**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** CAN is the de facto standard for in-vehicle networking (IVN) defined in **SAE J1939** for heavy-duty vehicles and **ISO 15765 (CAN-FD extension)** for passenger EVs. Students must master the CAN 2.0A (Standard, 11-bit ID) and CAN 2.0B (Extended, 29-bit ID) frame formats.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **conference room with multiple experts** (ECUs) sitting around a circular table, all sharing a **single open microphone** (the CAN bus). The rules are simple but brilliant:

- Only **one person (node)** can speak at a time.
- If two experts start speaking simultaneously, the one with the **higher priority message** (lower CAN ID number) is allowed to continue, while the other immediately **backs off** and waits — this is called **non-destructive bitwise arbitration**.
- Everyone in the room **hears every message** and decides for themselves whether to act on it.
- A strict **chairman (the bus protocol)** checks every spoken sentence for errors. If an error is found, the speaker is interrupted and the sentence is repeated automatically.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** Unlike Ethernet (address-based), CAN is **message-prioritized**. The data doesn't carry the sender/receiver address — instead, every message has a **Content-based Identifier** that defines its priority and meaning.

### 1.3 Core Physical Constants & Standards

- **Standard Baud Rates:** **125 kbps, 250 kbps, 500 kbps, 1 Mbps** (High-Speed CAN, ISO 11898-2)
- **Bus Voltage Levels:**
  - **Dominant Bit (Logic 0):** CAN_H ≈ **3.5 V**, CAN_L ≈ **1.5 V** → Differential ≈ **2 V**
  - **Recessive Bit (Logic 1):** CAN_H ≈ **2.5 V**, CAN_L ≈ **2.5 V** → Differential ≈ **0 V**
- **Maximum Bus Length at 1 Mbps:** **40 meters** (with proper termination)
- **Maximum Nodes on Bus:** **30+** (electrically limited by transceiver load)
- **Termination Resistors:** **120 Ω** at each physical end of the bus (characteristic impedance matching)

![CAN Bus Physical Topology — Linear with Termination]
```
[ECU 1]----[ECU 2]----[ECU 3]---- ... ----[ECU N]
   |                                            |
  120Ω                                        120Ω
   |                                            |
  GND                                         GND
```

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Layered Architecture of CAN (OSI Mapping)

CAN is a **Layer 1 (Physical) and Layer 2 (Data Link)** protocol. Higher layers (like CANopen, J1939) sit on top.

| OSI Layer | CAN Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Layer 7 - Application | OEM Specific (UDS, J1939-71) | Vehicle diagnostics, torque request |
| Layer 2 - Data Link | **CAN Core (ISO 11898-1)** | Framing, arbitration, CRC, ACK |
| Layer 1 - Physical | **ISO 11898-2 (HS-CAN)** | Differential signaling, transceiver |

### 2.2 CAN 2.0A Standard Frame Structure (Most Exam-Relevant)

The standard CAN data frame consists of **7 distinct fields**. KTU examiners frequently ask students to draw and label these.

1. **SOF (Start of Frame):** 1 dominant bit — synchronizes all nodes.
2. **Arbitration Field (11-bit ID + RTR):** Defines message priority. Lower ID = Higher priority.
3. **Control Field (IDE + r0 + DLC):** 6 bits. **DLC (Data Length Code)** specifies number of data bytes (0–8).
4. **Data Field:** 0 to **8 bytes** of payload (64 bits maximum in Classic CAN).
5. **CRC Field (15-bit CRC + 1 delimiter):** Cyclic Redundancy Check for error detection.
6. **ACK Field:** 2 bits. Any receiving node that received the message correctly overwrites the recessive ACK with a dominant bit.
7. **EOF (End of Frame):** 7 recessive bits.

### 2.3 Non-Destructive Bitwise Arbitration — The Heart of CAN

This is the **single most important theoretical concept** for KTU exams.

- All nodes transmit **bit-by-bit simultaneously** during the arbitration phase.
- The bus is wired such that a **dominant bit (0) overrides a recessive bit (1)**.
- If a node transmits a recessive bit but reads back a dominant bit, it knows a higher-priority node is transmitting → it **immediately stops** and becomes a receiver.
- The arbitration **never destroys any data** — hence "non-destructive." The higher-priority message wins without delay.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why CAN IDs Must be Unique:** Two nodes with the same ID would cause a permanent arbitration failure (bus error). Every CAN ID on a vehicle network must be assigned uniquely.

### 2.4 Error Handling (5 Error Types — KTU Hot Topic)

CAN implements **5 distinct error detection mechanisms** working in parallel:

1. **Bit Monitoring** — Transmitter compares its sent bit with the actual bus level.
2. **Bit Stuffing Rule** — After 5 consecutive identical bits, a stuff bit of opposite polarity is inserted. If not, an error is flagged.
3. **CRC Check** — Receiver recalculates CRC and compares.
4. **Form Check** — Fixed-format fields (CRC, EOF, intermission) must follow strict rules.
5. **ACK Check** — Transmitter expects a dominant bit in ACK slot from at least one receiver.

Each CAN node maintains two counters:
- **TEC (Transmit Error Counter)** — incremented on transmit errors.
- **REC (Receive Error Counter)** — incremented on receive errors.

Based on counter values, a node transitions through three states: **Error Active → Error Passive → Bus Off**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` notation inside tables (NEVER the pipe character) to avoid breaking markdown syntax.

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bit Time** | $t_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{\text{Baud Rate}}$ | seconds | Determines maximum bus latency |
| **Max Bus Length (rule of thumb)** | $L_{max} \approx \dfrac{50 \times 10^{6}}{\text{Baud Rate}}$ | meters | HS-CAN physical layer design |
| **Propagation Delay (signal)** | $t_{prop} = \dfrac{L \times 5\,\text{ns/m}}{v_{signal}}$ | seconds | Critical for arbitration timing |
| **Bit Stuffing Efficiency** | $\eta_{stuff} = \dfrac{N_{data}}{N_{data} + N_{stuff}}$ | ratio | Useful payload percentage |
| **Propagation Time Budget** | $t_{prop} \le \dfrac{t_{bit}}{2}$ (× bus length in time quanta) | seconds | Ensures correct sampling at 1 Mbps |
| **CRC Polynomial (CAN 2.0A)** | $g(x) = x^{15} + x^{14} + x^{10} + x^{8} + x^{7} + x^{4} + x^{3} + 1$ | — | Hamming Distance = 6 |
| **Bus Termination Resistance** | $R_{term} = Z_{0} = 120\,\Omega$ | ohms | Prevents reflections at 1 Mbps |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in EV/HV Applications

- **BMS ↔ VCU:** SOC, temperature, voltage, current broadcast every 10–100 ms.
- **VCU ↔ Inverter:** Torque command, motor speed feedback at 1–5 ms cycle.
- **OBD-II Port:** Diagnostic access via CAN at **500 kbps** using **UDS (Unified Diagnostic Services, ISO 14229)**.
- **Charging Communication:** **ISO 15118** (PLC) and **CAN-based charging protocols** between EVSE and EV.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Bus Length vs. Baud Rate Relationship

The bus length is fundamentally limited by the **propagation delay** of the signal relative to the **bit time**. For arbitration to work correctly, the signal must propagate from the farthest node to the nearest node **within half the bit time** (so a transmitting node can sample its own bit before another node potentially overrides it).

**Step 1:** Define the bit time in seconds for a given baud rate.

$$
t_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{\text{Baud Rate}}
$$

*Conversion Logic:* A bit at 500 kbps lasts 2 µs; at 1 Mbps it lasts 1 µs. The faster the bus, the shorter the bit, the less time available for signal travel.

**Step 2:** The electromagnetic signal propagates through twisted-pair cable at roughly **2/3 the speed of light**, giving a typical propagation velocity of approximately **5 ns/m**.

$$
v_{prop} \approx 2 \times 10^{8}\, \text{m/s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad t_{prop\_per\_meter} \approx 5\, \text{ns/m}
$$

**Step 3:** For correct sampling, the **round-trip propagation delay** (signal travels from transmitter to farthest node and back) must not exceed half the bit time.

$$
2 \times L_{max} \times 5\, \text{ns/m} \le \dfrac{t_{bit}}{2}
$$

**Step 4:** Solve algebraically for the maximum length.

$$
L_{max} \times 10\, \text{ns/m} \le \dfrac{1}{2 \times \text{Baud Rate}}
$$

$$
L_{max} \le \dfrac{1}{20 \times 10^{-9} \times \text{Baud Rate}}
$$

$$
L_{max} \le \dfrac{50 \times 10^{6}}{\text{Baud Rate}}\, \text{(in meters)}
$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate for a typical 1 Mbps automotive CAN bus.

$$
L_{max} \le \dfrac{50 \times 10^{6}}{1 \times 10^{6}} = 50\, \text{m}
$$

After accounting for transceiver delays and safety margins, the practical maximum is standardized at **40 meters**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Bit Timing Decomposition (Time Quanta)

CAN controllers divide each bit into **Time Quanta (TQ)** based on the peripheral clock. For an **STM32-class MCU** at 36 MHz with a CAN baud rate of 500 kbps:

**Step 1:** Determine the total Time Quanta per bit (Tq_per_bit).

$$
\text{Tq\_per\_bit} = \dfrac{f_{clock}}{\text{Baud Rate}} = \dfrac{36 \times 10^{6}}{500 \times 10^{3}} = 72\, \text{Tq/bit}
$$

**Step 2:** Partition the bit into four standard segments as per **ISO 11898-1**.

$$
t_{bit} = t_{SYNC\_SEG} + t_{PROP\_SEG} + t_{PHASE\_SEG1} + t_{PHASE\_SEG2}
$$

| Segment | Typical Tq | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| SYNC_SEG | 1 Tq | Edge synchronization |
| PROP_SEG | 6 Tq | Compensates physical propagation delay |
| PHASE_SEG1 | 7 Tq | Phase error correction (slow edge) |
| PHASE_SEG2 | 7 Tq | Phase error correction (fast edge) |

**Step 3:** Verify the SJW (Synchronization Jump Width).

$$
\text{SJW} = \min(\text{PHASE\_SEG1}, \text{PHASE\_SEG2}) = 7\, \text{Tq}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute Sample Point position.

$$
\text{SP}\% = \dfrac{\text{SYNC} + \text{PROP} + \text{PHASE\_SEG1}}{\text{Total Tq}} \times 100 = \dfrac{1+6+7}{72} \times 100 \approx 87.5\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** The sample point is recommended at **75% to 90%** for noise immunity. Most automotive OEMs target **80% ± 5%**.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: CAN vs. Other Vehicle Bus Protocols

| Parameter | CAN 2.0B | CAN-FD | LIN | FlexRay | Automotive Ethernet |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Max Baud Rate | **1 Mbps** | **5 Mbps** | 20 kbps | 10 Mbps | 1000 Mbps |
| Payload | 8 bytes | **64 bytes** | 8 bytes | 254 bytes | 1500 bytes |
| Arbitration | CSMA/CR | CSMA/CR | Polling | TDMA | Full-duplex switch |
| Topology | Bus | Bus | Bus | Star/Bus | Star |
| Cost | Low | Low-Med | Very Low | High | Medium |
| EV Application | Body, Chassis | Powertrain, Charging | Mirrors, Seats | Drive-by-wire | ADAS, Infotainment |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 CAN Frame Format — Standard 2.0A (Detailed Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeSOF["SOF 1 bit Dominant"]
    nodeID["Arbitration Field 11 bit ID"]
    nodeRTR["RTR 1 bit"]
    nodeIDE["IDE 1 bit Dominant"]
    nodeR0["r0 1 bit"]
    nodeDLC["DLC 4 bits"]
    nodeDATA["Data Field 0 to 64 bits"]
    nodeCRC15["CRC 15 bits"]
    nodeCRCDEL["CRC Delimiter 1 bit"]
    nodeACKSLOT["ACK Slot 1 bit"]
    nodeACKDEL["ACK Delimiter 1 bit"]
    nodeEOF["EOF 7 bits Recessive"]
    nodeIFS["IFS 3 bits"]

    nodeSOF --> nodeID --> nodeRTR --> nodeIDE --> nodeR0 --> nodeDLC --> nodeDATA --> nodeCRC15 --> nodeCRCDEL --> nodeACKSLOT --> nodeACKDEL --> nodeEOF --> nodeIFS
```

### 4.2 CAN Bus Physical Topology with Termination

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeTerm1["Termination Resistor 120 Ohm"]
    nodeECU1["ECU 1 BMS"]
    nodeECU2["ECU 2 VCU"]
    nodeECU3["ECU 3 Inverter"]
    nodeECU4["ECU 4 OBC"]
    nodeTerm2["Termination Resistor 120 Ohm"]
    nodeCANH["CAN High Wire"]
    nodeCANL["CAN Low Wire"]

    nodeTerm1 --- nodeECU1
    nodeECU1 --- nodeECU2
    nodeECU2 --- nodeECU3
    nodeECU3 --- nodeECU4
    nodeECU4 --- nodeTerm2

    nodeCANH -. Twisted Pair .- nodeCANL
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Vector Tools Simulation Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Database Definition .dbc file"]
    nodeB["CANdb++ Editor Signal and Message Mapping"]
    nodeC["CANoe Configuration Simulation Setup"]
    nodeD["CAPL Programming Node Behavior Scripts"]
    nodeE["Real ECU or Virtual ECU VN5600"]
    nodeF["Trace Window Bus Traffic Logging"]
    nodeG["Analysis Bus Statistics Error Counters"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG -. Feedback Loop .-> nodeD
```

### 4.4 Non-Destructive Arbitration Process (Bit-by-Bit Logic)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeStart["Both Nodes Begin Transmitting Simultaneously"]
    nodeBit1["Node A sends ID bit 0 Dominant"]
    nodeBit2["Node B sends ID bit 1 Recessive"]
    nodeRead["Both Read Back Bus Level"]
    nodeWin["Node A Wins Bus Access Continues Transmission"]
    nodeBack["Node B Detects Loss Switches to Receiver Mode"]

    nodeStart --> nodeBit1
    nodeStart --> nodeBit2
    nodeBit1 --> nodeRead
    nodeBit2 --> nodeRead
    nodeRead --> nodeWin
    nodeRead --> nodeBack
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

Explain the concept of **non-destructive bitwise arbitration** in CAN. Why is it called "non-destructive"?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

In CAN, multiple nodes may attempt to transmit simultaneously. Each node monitors the bus while transmitting. The bus is designed such that a **dominant bit (0)** overwrites a **recessive bit (1)**. When two nodes transmit simultaneously, the one sending a dominant bit while the other sends a recessive bit gains control of the bus — the recessive node detects the mismatch and immediately stops transmitting, becoming a receiver.

It is called **"non-destructive"** because the arbitration process resolves the contention **without corrupting the winning message** and **without any time loss** — the higher-priority message continues transmission seamlessly while the lower-priority message is automatically retried once the bus becomes free.

*Mark Distribution:* 1 mark for dominance logic, 1 mark for arbitration rule, 1 mark for "non-destructive" justification.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, KTU Sample Paper]**

List any **three error detection mechanisms** used in the CAN protocol.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Bit Monitoring (Bit Error Detection):** Every transmitter compares its transmitted bit with the actual bus level. A mismatch (other than during arbitration or ACK) triggers a bit error.

2. **CRC (Cyclic Redundancy Check):** The transmitter appends a 15-bit CRC computed from the frame data using the polynomial $g(x) = x^{15} + x^{14} + x^{10} + x^{8} + x^{7} + x^{4} + x^{3} + 1$. The receiver recalculates and compares.

3. **Bit Stuffing Check:** After 5 consecutive identical bits, a stuff bit of opposite polarity must appear. Violation indicates a **stuffing error**.

*(Alternative valid answers: Form Error, ACK Error, CRC Error.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

**Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, End Semester Evaluation]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **CAN standard frame format** (CAN 2.0A). List each field with its bit length. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An EV powertrain uses CAN 2.0B at **500 kbps** to communicate between the BMS, VCU, and inverter. Calculate:
- (i) The **bit time** in microseconds.
- (ii) The **maximum allowable bus length** using the propagation delay formula.
- (iii) The **sample point percentage** if the bit is divided into SYNC_SEG = 1 Tq, PROP_SEG = 6 Tq, PHASE_SEG1 = 7 Tq, PHASE_SEG2 = 7 Tq. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Standard CAN 2.0A Frame Structure:

| Field | Bit Length | Description |
|---|---|---|
| SOF | 1 | Start of Frame (dominant) |
| Arbitration Field | 12 | 11-bit ID + 1-bit RTR |
| Control Field | 6 | IDE + r0 + 4-bit DLC |
| Data Field | 0–64 | 0 to 8 bytes of payload |
| CRC Field | 16 | 15-bit CRC + 1 delimiter |
| ACK Field | 2 | 1-bit ACK slot + 1 delimiter |
| EOF | 7 | End of Frame (recessive) |
| IFS | 3 | Interframe Space |

The total frame length ranges from **47 bits (no data)** to **111 bits (8 data bytes)**.

**(b) Calculations:**

**(i) Bit Time:**
$$
t_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{500 \times 10^{3}} = 2\, \mu s
$$

**(ii) Maximum Bus Length:**
$$
L_{max} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^{6}}{500 \times 10^{3}} = 100\, \text{m (theoretical)}
$$

Accounting for transceiver delays (~30 ns each end) and safety margin, the practical limit is approximately **80–90 meters** (Standard recommends up to **100 m at 500 kbps**).

**(iii) Sample Point:**
$$
\text{Total Tq} = 1 + 6 + 7 + 7 = 21\, \text{Tq}
$$
$$
\text{SP}\% = \dfrac{1 + 6 + 7}{21} \times 100 = \dfrac{14}{21} \times 100 \approx 66.67\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part B):** Students commonly lose marks by:
> - **Forgetting the RTR bit** in the Arbitration field count (it is 12 bits total, not 11).
> - **Confusing the CRC polynomial** with Ethernet's CRC-32.
> - **Misidentifying the sample point** — it is measured from the START of the bit to the sampling instant, NOT from the end.
> - **Writing "L_max = 50 m" for 500 kbps** without showing the derivation. KTU award full marks only when the formula $L_{max} = 50 \times 10^6 / \text{Baud Rate}$ is explicitly stated.

---

**Question B: [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary Exam]**

**(a)** Explain the **five error detection mechanisms** in the CAN protocol. Describe how the **TEC and REC counters** transition a node through the **Error Active → Error Passive → Bus Off** states. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Vector CANoe / CANalyzer toolchain** used for CAN bus simulation in EV development. Draw a block diagram showing the workflow from **.dbc database creation to trace analysis**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Five Error Detection Mechanisms:**

1. **Bit Error:** Transmitter compares its sent bit with bus level. Mismatch (excluding arbitration and ACK) = bit error.
2. **Bit Stuffing Error:** More than 5 consecutive bits of same polarity without a stuff bit.
3. **CRC Error:** Receiver's recalculated CRC does not match the transmitted 15-bit CRC.
4. **Form Error:** Fixed-format fields (CRC delimiter, ACK delimiter, EOF) contain illegal dominant bits.
5. **ACK Error:** Transmitter does not see a dominant bit in the ACK slot (meaning no receiver acknowledged).

**Error State Transitions:**

- **Error Active:** Normal state. Node can transmit **Active Error Flags** (6 dominant bits). Initial TEC and REC = 0.
- **Error Passive:** Reached when **TEC ≥ 128 OR REC ≥ 128**. Node transmits **Passive Error Flags** (6 recessive bits) and must wait additional suspend time before retransmission.
- **Bus Off:** Reached when **TEC ≥ 256**. Node completely disconnects from the bus, requires **host MCU intervention or 128 occurrences of 11 recessive bits** to recover.

**TEC/REC Update Rules:**
- Successful transmission: TEC decrements (unless already 0).
- Successful reception: REC decrements (if between 1 and 127).
- Error detected: corresponding counter incremented by 8 (or by 1 for receiver during transmitter error).

**(b) Vector Tools Simulation Workflow:**

**Vector CANoe** is the industry-standard tool for developing, testing, and analyzing CAN-based ECUs in EVs.

| Stage | Tool | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Database | **CANdb++** | Define messages, signals, ECUs in `.dbc` file |
| 2. Simulation Setup | **CANoe Configurator** | Configure baud rate, nodes, channels |
| 3. Node Behavior | **CAPL Browser** | Script ECUs in CAPL (C-like) language |
| 4. Hardware I/O | **VN1600 / VN7640 Interface** | Connect PC to real CAN bus |
| 5. Testing | **Test Feature Set / vTESTstudio** | Automated test sequences |
| 6. Analysis | **Trace Window / Graphics Window** | Visualize bus traffic, signals |
| 7. Diagnostics | **CANdela / DiVa** | UDS diagnostic validation |

**Workflow Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.3 Mermaid diagram above.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part B - Vector Tools):** Common mistakes:
> - Writing **"CANalyzer" and "CANoe" interchangeably** — they are distinct products (CANalyzer is read-only analysis; CANoe supports full simulation).
> - Forgetting the role of the **`.dbc` (Database CAN) file** — this is the *single most important file* in any Vector workflow.
> - Not mentioning **CAPL** (Communication Access Programming Language) — KTU expects it as the scripting layer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE:**

- **CAN Originator:** Bosch (1986), Standardized by **ISO 11898**.
- **Frame Types:** **Data Frame, Remote Frame, Error Frame, Overload Frame** (memorize the role of each).
- **CAN 2.0A** = 11-bit ID (Standard); **CAN 2.0B** = 29-bit ID (Extended).
- **Maximum Data Payload:** **8 bytes** (Classic CAN); **64 bytes** (CAN-FD).
- **Bus Voltage:** Dominant (CAN_H ≈ 3.5 V, CAN_L ≈ 1.5 V); Recessive (both ≈ 2.5 V).
- **Termination:** **120 Ω** at both ends — NEVER in the middle.
- **Bit Stuffing Rule:** Insert opposite-polarity bit after **5 identical bits** in sequence.
- **Lower CAN ID = Higher Priority** (counter-intuitive but critical).
- **Arbitration is non-destructive** — no data loss during contention.
- **5 Error Types:** Bit, Stuff, CRC, Form, ACK.
- **CRC Polynomial:** $x^{15} + x^{14} + x^{10} + x^{8} + x^{7} + x^{4} + x^{3} + 1$ (Hamming Distance = 6).
- **Error States:** Error Active → Error Passive (TEC/REC ≥ 128) → Bus Off (TEC ≥ 256).
- **Recommended Sample Point:** **75%–90%** of bit time.
- **Bus Length Rule:** $L_{max} \approx 50 \times 10^{6} / \text{Baud Rate}$ meters.
- **Vector Toolchain:** **CANdb++ → CANoe → CAPL → VN1600 → Trace/Analysis**.
- **EV-Specific CAN IDs:** BMS (0x18FF50F4 typical J1939), VCU (0x18FF01F4), Inverter torque request (0x18FF0CF4).
- **Common Vehicle Protocols:** **SAE J1939** (heavy EVs), **OSEK/AUTOSAR** (ECU OS), **UDS ISO 14229** (diagnostics).
- **Diagnostic Port:** **OBD-II** standardized to use CAN at **500 kbps (pins 6 & 14)** since **2008** for all US vehicles.

---

> **END OF NOTES — PEMUT631 Module 4: CAN and Vector Tools Simulation**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicle Sensors Specific to EV: Sensors Interfaced to the ECUs in the Vehicle Network
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT631 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vehicle Sensors Specific to EV: Sensors Interfaced to the ECUs in the Vehicle Network

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of an Electric Vehicle (EV), **vehicle sensors** are electro-mechanical or solid-state transducers that continuously convert physical, chemical, or electrical quantities of the powertrain, chassis, and occupant environment into standardized electrical signals (analog voltage, current, PWM, frequency, or digital protocols such as SPI/I²C). These signals are routed to one or more **Electronic Control Units (ECUs)** embedded within the **in-vehicle network (IVN)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ECU (Electronic Control Unit):** A microprocessor-based embedded module responsible for acquiring sensor data, executing control algorithms (e.g., field-oriented control of the traction motor), and actuating drivers (IGBT/MOSFET gate signals, relay coils, contactors).

**Vehicle Network** refers to the standardized, multiplexed digital communication bus (CAN, LIN, FlexRay, Automotive Ethernet) that interconnects distributed ECUs — replacing the legacy point-to-point wiring harness.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition: The Human Nervous System**
> Think of an EV as a human body:
> - **Sensors** are the *sensory organs* (skin, eyes, proprioceptors). A current sensor is the "nerve ending" that feels current; a resolver is the "inner ear" that knows shaft position.
> - **ECUs** are the *brain and spinal cord* — the BMS is the autonomic nervous system, the VCU (Vehicle Control Unit) is the conscious brain making decisions.
> - **The Vehicle Network (CAN bus)** is the *spinal cord and nervous tracts* — a high-speed "highway" that ferries sensory information as standardized messages between organs.
> Without sensors, the EV is blind and numb; without ECUs, it cannot think; without a network, the limbs cannot coordinate.

## 1.3 Classification of EV-Specific Sensors

| Domain | Sensors | Primary ECU |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy Storage (BMS)** | Cell voltage, pack current, cell temperature | BMS-ECU |
| **Traction Motor** | Resolver/Encoder, phase current, motor temp | Motor Controller (MCU) |
| **Vehicle Dynamics** | Wheel speed, steering angle, IMU, yaw rate | VCU / ESC-ECU |
| **Driver Input** | Accelerator pedal (APS), brake pedal (BPS), gear selector | VCU |
| **Thermal Management** | Coolant temp, flow rate, ambient temp | HVAC / TMS-ECU |
| **ADAS & Environment** | Radar, LiDAR, Camera, Ultrasonic, GPS+IMU | ADAS-ECU / Gateway |
| **Charging** | CCS/Type-2 pilot signals, insulation monitoring | OBC / Charger-ECU |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The focus is on *which* sensors interface with the *BMS, MCU, and VCU* and *how* their data is serialized onto the multiplexed bus.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Battery Management System (BMS) Sensors

### 2.1.1 Cell Voltage Sensor
- **Principle:** Resistive differential amplifier + analog multiplexer.
- **Range:** **0 V – 5 V** per Li-ion cell (nominal 3.6 V–3.7 V).
- **Accuracy required:** ±**2 mV** for accurate State-of-Charge (SoC) estimation.
- **Topology:** Daisy-chained AFE (Analog Front End) such as LTC6811, BQ79616.

### 2.1.2 Pack Current Sensor — Hall-Effect vs. Shunt
- **Hall-Effect Sensor:** Galvanically isolated; bandwidth typically **DC – 100 kHz**.
- **Shunt Resistor:** Low-side or in-line; bandwidth > **1 MHz**; introduces I²R loss.

Hall voltage equation:

$$V_{H} \;=\; \frac{R_{H} \cdot I \cdot B}{t}$$

where $R_H$ is the Hall coefficient, $I$ the control current, $B$ the magnetic flux density, and $t$ the sensor thickness.

### 2.1.3 Temperature Sensor (NTC Thermistor)
- **Steinhart–Hart equation** (the most accurate three-parameter model):

$$\frac{1}{T} \;=\; A + B \ln(R) + C \big[\ln(R)\big]^{3}$$

where $T$ is in Kelvin and $R$ is the thermistor resistance in $\Omega$.

For board-level approximations, the **β-parameter equation** is sufficient:

$$R(T) \;=\; R_{0} \cdot \exp\!\left[\beta\!\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_{0}}\right)\right]$$

## 2.2 Traction Motor Sensors

### 2.2.1 Resolver (Rotor Position)
- **Why resolver, not optical encoder?** EV traction motors run at **>15 000 rpm** with severe vibration and **180 °C** temperatures; resolvers are electromagnetic and rugged.
- Excited with a **10 kHz sinusoidal carrier** $V_{exc} = V_{0}\sin(\omega_{c} t)$.
- The two-phase windings produce:

$$V_{\sin} \;=\; k \cdot V_{0}\sin(\omega_{c} t)\sin(\theta)$$

$$V_{\cos} \;=\; k \cdot V_{0}\sin(\omega_{c} t)\cos(\theta)$$

- The MCU decodes the envelope $\theta$ via an **RDC (Resolver-to-Digital Converter)** such as the AD2S1210.

### 2.2.2 Phase Current Sensing
- Performed by **3-phase current transducers** (LEM LA-series) or inline shunts.
- Bandwidth requirement: ≥ **50 kHz** for FOC switching at 10–20 kHz PWM.

## 2.3 Driver-Input and Vehicle Dynamics Sensors

| Sensor | Output Type | ECU Pinout |
|---|---|---|
| Accelerator Pedal Position (APS) | Dual-redundant **0–5 V analog** | VCU ADC channels |
| Brake Pedal Position (BPS) | Hall-effect **PWM or analog** | VCU / ESC-ECU |
| Wheel Speed Sensor | **Active or passive, 0–2.5 kHz** | ESC-ECU |
| Steering Angle Sensor | **SPI / analog differential** | EPS-ECU / VCU |

## 2.4 The In-Vehicle Network (Multiplexed Bus)

### 2.4.1 CAN (Controller Area Network) — Dominant EV Bus
- ISO 11898 standard, differential signalling on **CAN_H and CAN_L**.
- Two states: **Dominant (0)** — $V_{diff} = 2$ V; **Recessive (1)** — $V_{diff} = 0$ V.
- Standard data rates: **125 kbps** (body), **500 kbps** (powertrain), **1 Mbps** (chassis).
- Frame format (Data Frame): SOF, Arbitration (11-bit ID), RTR, Control, Data (0–8 B), CRC, ACK, EOF.

### 2.4.2 CAN Bit Timing Equation

$$t_{bit} \;=\; t_{SYNC\_SEG} + t_{PROP\_SEG} + t_{PHASE\_SEG1} + t_{PHASE\_SEG2}$$

The Time Quantum is generated from the CAN clock:

$$t_{q} \;=\; \frac{1}{f_{CAN\_CLK}} \cdot BRP$$

where $BRP$ is the Baud-Rate Prescaler.

Maximum achievable bit rate:

$$f_{bit,\,max} \;=\; \frac{1}{8 \cdot t_{q}}$$

(Minimum one tq per segment, 4 segments → 8 tq/bit is the smallest legal CAN bit time.)

### 2.4.3 Network Latency for a Periodic CAN Message

$$t_{latency} \;=\; t_{frame} + t_{prop} + t_{TX} + t_{RX} + t_{RX\_processing}$$

where $t_{frame} = (stuff\_bits + header + data + CRC + ACK + EOF) \cdot t_{bit}$.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_H = R_H \cdot I \cdot B / t$ | Hall-effect output voltage | V |
| 2 | $R(T) = R_0 \exp\!\big[\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)\big]$ | NTC thermistor resistance | $\Omega$ |
| 3 | $V_{\sin} = k V_0 \sin(\omega_c t)\sin\theta$ | Resolver sine winding output | V |
| 4 | $V_{\cos} = k V_0 \sin(\omega_c t)\cos\theta$ | Resolver cosine winding output | V |
| 5 | $t_{bit} = t_{SYNC} + t_{PROP} + t_{PH1} + t_{PH2}$ | CAN bit time | s |
| 6 | $t_q = (BRP)/f_{CAN\_CLK}$ | CAN time quantum | s |
| 7 | $f_{bit,max} = 1/(8 t_q)$ | Max CAN bit rate | bps |
| 8 | $R_{th} = \Delta T / P$ | Thermal resistance (junction) | K/W |
| 9 | $SoC\% = (Q_{used}/Q_{rated})\times 100$ | State of Charge (Coulomb counting) | % |
| 10 | $P = I^2 R_{shunt}$ | Power loss in shunt resistor | W |

> Real-world utility: These equations govern **safety-critical decisions** — overcurrent shutdown of the BMS, regenerative-braking cut-off via resolver zero-crossing, and torque-vectoring in ESC — all happening on millisecond timescales across a 30–40 °C underhood environment.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: CAN Bit Rate Selection for a 500 kbps Powertrain Bus

A designer chooses a CAN clock of $f_{CAN\_CLK}$ = **40 MHz** and requires $f_{bit}$ = **500 kbps**.

**Step 1 — Compute nominal bit time.**

$$t_{bit} = \frac{1}{f_{bit}} = \frac{1}{500{,}000} = 2 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s} = 2\ \mu\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Decide total time quanta per bit.**
The conventional choice for 500 kbps is **16 tq per bit** (gives fine resolution for the propagation delay and sampling point).

$$t_{q} = \frac{t_{bit}}{N_{tq}} = \frac{2\ \mu\text{s}}{16} = 0.125\ \mu\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Baud-Rate Prescaler.**

$$BRP = t_{q} \cdot f_{CAN\_CLK} - 1$$

$$BRP = (0.125 \times 10^{-6})(40 \times 10^{6}) - 1 = 5 - 1 = 4$$

**Step 4 — Allocate segments.**
For a **75 % sampling point**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{SYNC\_SEG} &= 1\,t_q = 0.125\ \mu\text{s} \\
t_{PROP\_SEG} &= 3\,t_q = 0.375\ \mu\text{s} \\
t_{PHASE\_SEG1} &= 8\,t_q = 1.000\ \mu\text{s} \\
t_{PHASE\_SEG2} &= 4\,t_q = 0.500\ \mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Sample point = $(1+3+8)/16 = 12/16 = 75\%$. ✔

**Step 5 — Verification.**

$$
t_{bit} = 1 + 3 + 8 + 4 = 16\,t_q = 2\ \mu\text{s} = \frac{1}{500\,\text{kbps}}\ \checkmark
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Coulomb-Counting SoC Using Current-Sensor Output

The current sensor outputs an analog voltage $V_{out} = G \cdot I_{pack}$ where $G$ = **40 mV/A**.

**Step 1 — ADC conversion.**
With a 12-bit ADC and $V_{ref} = 5$ V, the LSB weight is:

$$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{12}} = \frac{5}{4096} = 1.2207\ \text{mV/LSB}$$

**Step 2 — Compute current from ADC reading.**

$$I_{pack} = \frac{N_{ADC} \cdot V_{LSB}}{G} = \frac{N_{ADC} \cdot 1.2207\ \text{mV}}{40\ \text{mV/A}}$$

For an ADC reading of $N_{ADC} = 2000$:

$$I_{pack} = \frac{2000 \times 1.2207 \times 10^{-3}}{40 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{2.4414}{0.040} = 61.035\ \text{A}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate to obtain charge transferred over $\Delta t$ = 1 s.**

$$\Delta Q = I_{pack} \cdot \Delta t = 61.035 \times 1 = 61.035\ \text{C}$$

Convert to Ah:

$$\Delta Q_{Ah} = \frac{61.035}{3600} = 0.016954\ \text{Ah}$$

**Step 4 — Update SoC for a 100 Ah pack.**

$$SoC_{new} = SoC_{old} - \frac{\Delta Q_{Ah}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100\% = 80\% - \frac{0.016954}{100}\times 100 = 79.983\%$$

> This discrete integration is repeated every 10 ms by the BMS, then the SoC is broadcast over **CAN ID 0x6B1** every 100 ms to the VCU.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Sensor-to-ECU Map with Protocol

| Subsystem | Sensor | Signal Output | Signal Conditioning | Local Link to AFE | Multiplexed Bus to ECU | ECU Consumer |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Battery pack** | Cell V (96 cells) | 0–5 V differential | Differential amp + MUX | Daisy-chain SPI | **CAN 500 kbps** (0x6B0) | BMS-ECU |
| **Battery pack** | Hall current | 0–5 V | Anti-alias LPF, ADC | I²C to AFE | **CAN 500 kbps** | BMS-ECU |
| **Battery pack** | 16× NTC temp | 10 k$\Omega$ at 25 °C | Wheatstone + ADC | Daisy-chain SPI | **CAN 500 kbps** | BMS-ECU |
| **Traction motor** | Resolver | ±10 V sin/cos at 10 kHz | RDC chip AD2S1210 | SPI to MCU | **CAN-FD 2 Mbps** (0x1A0) | MCU-ECU |
| **Traction motor** | 3-phase LEM | ±12 V | Isolation + ADC | SPI | **CAN-FD 2 Mbps** | MCU-ECU |
| **Traction motor** | Stator PT100 | RTD bridge | Instrumentation amp | SPI | **CAN-FD 2 Mbps** | MCU-ECU |
| **VCU inputs** | APS dual Hall | 0–5 V (redundant) | Redundancy check | Analog in | **CAN 500 kbps** (0x201) | VCU |
| **VCU inputs** | BPS Hall | PWM 0–100 % | RC filter | Analog in | **CAN 500 kbps** (0x2B1) | VCU |
| **Chassis** | Wheel speed | 0–2.5 kHz square | Comparator | Local timer | **CAN 500 kbps** (0xC0) | ESC-ECU |
| **ADAS** | Front radar | Ethernet data | PHY | Automotive Eth | **100BASE-T1** | ADAS-ECU |
| **Body** | Door/light | On/off | LIN transceiver | LIN | **LIN 19.2 kbps** | BCM-ECU |
| **Thermal** | Coolant temp | NTC | Voltage divider | Local | **CAN 500 kbps** | TMS-ECU |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two buses?** Powertrain/CAN-FD must be **deterministic and high-speed** (steering, braking, torque). Body/LIN can be **slow and cheap** (mirrors, window lift). Gateway ECU bridges the two domains.

## 3.4 Numerical Example: Thermal Runaway Detection Latency

A 96s2p battery pack has 192 cells. Each NTC is read sequentially by the AFE. The SPI daisy-chain at **1 MHz** with 16-bit words takes:

$$t_{scan} = 192 \times 16 \times \frac{1}{1 \times 10^{6}} = 3.072\ \text{ms}$$

The CAN message is then transmitted every 100 ms. Therefore, the **maximum detection latency** between a thermal event and the BMS reacting is:

$$t_{detect} = t_{scan} + t_{CAN} + t_{process} \approx 3.07 + 0.5 + 2.0 = 5.57\ \text{ms}$$

This is well below the **10 ms** thermal-runaway safety budget mandated by ISO 6469-1.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an EV Sensor Network

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1[Pack V Sensor x96] --> nodeA1[BMS AFE LTC6811]
    node2[Hall I Sensor] --> nodeA1
    node3[NTC Temp x16] --> nodeA1
    node4[Insulation Monitor] --> nodeA1
    nodeA1 --> nodeA2[BMS Microcontroller]
    nodeA2 --> nodeCAN1[CAN Transceiver TJA1051]
    node5[Resolver] --> nodeB1[RDC Chip AD2S1210]
    node6[Phase I x3 LEM] --> nodeB1
    node7[PT100 Stator] --> nodeB1
    nodeB1 --> nodeB2[Motor Control MCU TMS320]
    nodeB2 --> nodeCAN2[CAN-FD Transceiver]
    node8[APS Hall x2] --> nodeC1[VCU ADC]
    node9[BPS Hall] --> nodeC1
    node10[Steering Angle] --> nodeC1
    nodeC1 --> nodeC2[VCU Processor]
    nodeC2 --> nodeCAN3[CAN Transceiver]
    nodeCAN1 --> nodeBUS((CAN BUS\n500 kbps\nDifferential))
    nodeCAN2 --> nodeBUS
    nodeCAN3 --> nodeBUS
    node11[Wheel Speed x4] --> nodeD1[ESC ECU]
    nodeD1 --> nodeBUS
    nodeBUS --> nodeGW[Gateway ECU]
    node12[Radar] --> nodeE1[ADAS ECU]
    node13[Camera] --> nodeE1
    nodeE1 --> nodeETH((100BASE-T1\nAutomotive Eth))
    nodeETH --> nodeGW
    nodeGW --> nodeOBD[OBD-II Port\nDiagnostics]
    node14[Mirror LIN node] --> nodeF1[LIN Master BCM ECU]
    nodeF1 --> nodeGW
```

## 4.2 Single-Sensor-to-ECU Signal Chain (Resolver Example)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Brushless Motor Shaft] --> node2[Resolver Rotor Winding Excited 10 kHz]
    node2 --> node3[Stator Winding SIN Output]
    node2 --> node4[Stator Winding COS Output]
    node3 --> node5[Buffer Op-Amp]
    node4 --> node6[Buffer Op-Amp]
    node5 --> node7[Anti-Alias LPF 50 kHz]
    node6 --> node8[Anti-Alias LPF 50 kHz]
    node7 --> node9[ADC Channel 1 MCU]
    node8 --> node10[ADC Channel 2 MCU]
    node9 --> node11[Atan2 Calculation in DSP]
    node10 --> node11
    node11 --> node12[Rotor Angle theta deg]
    node12 --> node13[Park and Clarke Transform FOC]
    node13 --> node14[IGBT Gate Signals 3-phase Inverter]
    node14 --> node15[Traction Motor]
```

## 4.3 CAN Frame Transmission on the Bus

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[BMS-ECU] --> node2[CAN TX Buffer]
    node2 --> node3[Arbitration Phase ID 0x6B1]
    node3 --> node4[CAN BUS Dominant or Recessive]
    node4 --> node5[VCU RX Acceptance Filter 0x6B1]
    node5 --> node6[VCU RX Buffer]
    node6 --> node7[Application Layer]
    node8[Motor MCU] --> node9[CAN TX ID 0x1A0]
    node9 --> node4
    node4 --> node10[ESC RX Filter 0x1A0]
    node10 --> node11[Torque Arbitration]
```

## 4.4 ECU Hardware Layer Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1[Application Layer Diagnostic and Control]
    node2[CANopen or Autosar Runtime]
    node3[CAN Data Link Layer]
    node4[CAN Physical Layer ISO 11898]
    node5[CAN Transceiver TJA1051]
    node6[CAN Bus Differential Pair Twisted 120 ohm Term]
    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of a resolver in an electric vehicle traction motor control. Why is it preferred over an optical encoder in EV applications?**

**Model Answer:**
A resolver is an electromagnetic rotary position sensor that provides the absolute angular position of the rotor shaft to the Motor Control Unit (MCU). The rotor is excited by a **10 kHz sinusoidal carrier**; the stator produces two modulated sine and cosine windings whose envelope decodes to rotor angle $\theta$ via the function $\theta = \tan^{-1}(V_{\sin}/V_{\cos})$.

It is preferred over an optical encoder in EVs because:
- (i) **Temperature tolerance** up to **155 °C** vs. encoder's 85 °C LED limit.
- (ii) **Vibration robustness** — no fragile disc or photodiodes.
- (iii) **IP-rated sealing** — operates in oil-cooled motor housings.
- (iv) **EMI immunity** at high switching frequencies.

---

### Question 2
`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**List any four types of sensors used in the Battery Management System (BMS) of an EV and state the physical quantity each one measures.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Sensor | Quantity Measured |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cell voltage sensor (e.g., LTC6811) | Terminal voltage of each cell (0–5 V) |
| 2 | Hall-effect current transducer (e.g., LEM LA 100-P) | Pack current (DC – 100 kHz) |
| 3 | NTC thermistor (10 k$\Omega$ at 25 °C) | Cell surface or tab temperature (−40 to +125 °C) |
| 4 | Insulation monitoring device (IMD) | Insulation resistance between HV bus and chassis (k$\Omega$/M$\Omega$) |

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 4 - PEMUT631]`

**a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **sensor-to-ECU signal chain** for a Battery Management System (BMS) of an EV. List the typical sensors interfaced and explain how their data is multiplexed onto the CAN bus. (7 marks)

**b)** A CAN bus is driven by a 40 MHz clock and must operate at 500 kbps. The desired sampling point is 75 %. Design the bit timing and compute the Baud-Rate Prescaler (BRP) and the four segment lengths. (7 marks)

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

A BMS monitors every cell, module, and the full pack. The signal chain is:

1. **Physical layer (Transducers):**
   - **Voltage sensors:** Each of the 96 series cells is connected to the differential inputs of an Analog Front End (AFE) like the LTC6811-1. The AFE contains a 16-bit $\Delta\Sigma$ ADC.
   - **Current sensor:** A LEM Hall-effect transducer (LA 100-P) outputs $\pm 50$ mA proportional to $\pm 100$ A; this is converted to $0$–**5 V** by a burden resistor and amplifier.
   - **Temperature sensors:** 16 NTC thermistors (10 k$\Omega$ at 25 °C) connected in a Wheatstone bridge; an instrumentation amp outputs 0–5 V to the AFE.

2. **Signal conditioning (AFE stage):**
   - **Differential amplifiers** with high CMRR (>80 dB) reject the 96s common-mode voltage.
   - **Anti-alias low-pass filters** with $-3$ dB at **5 kHz** precede the ADC.
   - The **$\Delta\Sigma$ ADC** oversamples at $f_s = 1$ MHz and decimates to 1 ksps.

3. **Local link:**
   - AFEs are **daisy-chained via SPI** (1 MHz) to the BMS microcontroller, which collects 192 cells × 16-bit data within 3 ms.

4. **Multiplexed bus to other ECUs:**
   - The BMS MCU serialises the aggregated data into **CAN 2.0B frames** using a TJA1051 transceiver.
   - **Message ID 0x6B0** carries cell voltages; **0x6B1** carries SoC; **0x6B2** carries temperatures; broadcast every **100 ms** at 500 kbps.
   - The VCU, MCU and gateway ECUs filter and accept these IDs.

The block diagram is shown in the architecture in SECTION 4.1.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

Given: $f_{CAN\_CLK} = 40$ MHz, $f_{bit} = 500$ kbps, sampling point SP = 75 %.

**Step 1:** Nominal bit time.

$$t_{bit} = \frac{1}{500{,}000} = 2 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s} = 2\ \mu\text{s}$$

**Step 2:** Choose $N_{tq} = 16$ tq per bit (standard for 500 kbps).

$$t_{q} = \frac{t_{bit}}{N_{tq}} = \frac{2\ \mu\text{s}}{16} = 0.125\ \mu\text{s}$$

**Step 3:** BRP.

$$BRP = t_{q} \cdot f_{CAN\_CLK} - 1 = (0.125 \times 10^{-6})(40 \times 10^{6}) - 1 = 4$$

**Step 4:** Distribute 16 tq to reach 75 % SP.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{SYNC\_SEG} &= 1\,t_q = 0.125\ \mu\text{s} \\
t_{PROP\_SEG} &= 3\,t_q = 0.375\ \mu\text{s} \\
t_{PHASE\_SEG1} &= 8\,t_q = 1.000\ \mu\text{s} \\
t_{PHASE\_SEG2} &= 4\,t_q = 0.500\ \mu\text{s} \\
\text{Total} &= 16\,t_q = 2\ \mu\text{s}\ \checkmark \\
\text{SP} &= (1+3+8)/16 = 75\%\ \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget the **−1 offset** in the BRP equation. Register value `BRP` in the hardware register is one less than the actual divider. Writing `BRP = t_q × f_CLK` (without the −1) will cost 1 mark.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
`[KTU University Exam - June 2024 - Module 4 - PEMUT631]`

**a)** Explain the **operating principle of a resolver** used as a rotor position sensor in an EV traction motor. Derive the expressions for the sin and cos windings and show how the absolute rotor angle $\theta$ is extracted. (7 marks)

**b)** A Hall-effect current sensor with sensitivity **40 mV/A** measures the pack current. A 12-bit ADC with reference **5 V** reads the value **2048**. Calculate the measured current. If the pack capacity is **100 Ah** and the initial SoC is **85 %**, what is the new SoC after **10 minutes** of continuous current? (7 marks)

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

A resolver is a rotary transformer with one **rotor winding** (primary, excited) and two **stator windings** (secondaries, displaced by 90° in space).

**Excitation:** The rotor is supplied with a high-frequency sinusoid:

$$V_{exc}(t) = V_{0}\sin(\omega_{c} t)$$

where $\omega_c = 2\pi \times 10{,}000\ \text{rad/s}$.

**Coupling:** The coupling between the rotor and stator depends on the relative angular displacement $\theta$ between the rotor axis and each stator coil. By Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, the induced EMF in the sine winding (placed along the sin axis) is:

$$V_{\sin}(t) = k \cdot V_{0}\sin(\omega_{c} t)\sin(\theta)$$

and in the cosine winding (rotated 90° spatially):

$$V_{\cos}(t) = k \cdot V_{0}\sin(\omega_{c} t)\cos(\theta)$$

**Demodulation:** The RDC (e.g., AD2S1210) multiplies each signal by $V_{exc}$, then low-pass-filters to remove the $2\omega_c$ component. The envelopes become $k V_0 \sin\theta$ and $k V_0 \cos\theta$.

**Angle extraction:** The ratio of envelopes yields the tangent:

$$\tan(\theta) = \frac{V_{\sin,\text{envelope}}}{V_{\cos,\text{envelope}}}$$

$$\boxed{\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{V_{\sin}}{V_{\cos}}\right)}$$

A **Type-II tracking loop** in the RDC provides absolute position with **16-bit resolution** (≈ 0.005°) and tracks angular velocities up to **125 000 rpm**.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Step 1 — ADC LSB weight.**

$$V_{LSB} = \frac{5}{2^{12}} = \frac{5}{4096} = 1.2207\ \text{mV/LSB}$$

**Step 2 — Compute sensor output voltage.**

$$V_{out} = N_{ADC} \cdot V_{LSB} = 2048 \times 1.2207 \times 10^{-3} = 2.5000\ \text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Compute current.**

$$I = \frac{V_{out}}{G} = \frac{2.5000}{40 \times 10^{-3}} = 62.5\ \text{A}$$

**Step 4 — Charge transferred in 10 minutes.**

$$t = 10\ \text{min} = \frac{10}{60} = 0.1667\ \text{h}$$

$$\Delta Q = I \cdot t = 62.5 \times 0.1667 = 10.4167\ \text{Ah}$$

**Step 5 — New SoC.**

$$SoC_{new} = SoC_{old} - \frac{\Delta Q}{Q_{rated}} \times 100\% = 85\% - \frac{10.4167}{100} \times 100\%$$

$$SoC_{new} = 85\% - 10.4167\% = 74.5833\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Sign Convention:** The above assumes **discharge current** is positive. If the problem states "regenerative charging," you must add the charge: $SoC_{new} = 85\% + 10.42\% = 95.42\%$. Failure to verify the sign direction of $I$ costs 1 mark.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Unit Confusion:** Many students confuse **Ah** with **A·s (coulombs)**. Remember: $1$ Ah $= 3600$ C. The integration of current in **time** yields coulombs; the integration in **hours** yields ampere-hours directly for SoC calculations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Sensors in EV** are classified into **BMS** (cell V, pack I, T, IMD), **Motor** (resolver, phase I, PT100), **Chassis** (wheel speed, steering, IMU), and **ADAS** (radar, LiDAR, camera).
- The **Resolver** is the **de facto standard** for traction-motor rotor position due to robustness; the angle is recovered by $\theta = \tan^{-1}(V_{\sin}/V_{\cos})$ after synchronous demodulation.
- **Hall-effect current sensors** are isolated, but **shunt resistors** give the highest bandwidth; sensitivity is in mV/A (typical **40 mV/A**).
- **NTC thermistors** follow $R(T) = R_0 \exp[\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)]$; cell monitoring uses **Steinhart–Hart** for accuracy.
- The **in-vehicle network** is the **CAN bus** (ISO 11898) at **500 kbps** for powertrain; **CAN-FD** at **2 Mbps** for higher payload; **LIN** at **19.2 kbps** for low-speed body.
- CAN bit time: $t_{bit} = t_{SYNC} + t_{PROP} + t_{PH1} + t_{PH2}$; minimum legal bit time is **8 tq**; $BRP$ is **one less** than the divider.
- **SoC update** uses Coulomb counting: $SoC = SoC_{old} - (I \cdot \Delta t)/Q_{rated} \times 100\%$.
- **Redundancy is mandatory** in safety-critical signals: APS uses dual Hall sensors with 2-out-of-2 voting; brake uses two independent paths (mechanical + electrical).
- **Signal chain** = Transducer → Anti-alias LPF → ADC → AFE (SPI/I²C) → ECU MCU → CAN Transceiver → CAN Bus → Other ECUs.
- **Gateway ECU** bridges powertrain CAN, body LIN, and 100BASE-T1 automotive Ethernet.
- **EMC compliance:** CAN bus requires **120 $\Omega$** termination at each end of the differential twisted pair; mismatches cause bit errors and arbitration loss.
- **Diagnostics:** All ECUs implement UDS (ISO 14229) over CAN — error codes, DTCs, and live data accessible via the OBD-II port.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hardware and Software Interfaces: Implementation Challenges and Examples to Solve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT631 - ELECTRIC AND HYBRID VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hardware and Software Interfaces: Implementation Challenges and Examples to Solve

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Electric and Hybrid Vehicles (EHVs), **Hardware and Software Interfaces (HSI)** refer to the precisely defined electrical, electronic, mechanical, and logical boundaries through which the **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU)**, **Battery Management System (BMS)**, **Motor Control Unit (MCU)**, sensors, and actuators exchange signals, data, and control commands. The interface is governed by physical layer standards (voltage levels, connector pinouts, EMI/EMC compliance) and logical layer protocols (CAN, LIN, FlexRay, Automotive Ethernet, SPI, I²C).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Hardware-Software Interface (HSI) is the integrated set of physical connectors, signal conditioning circuits, communication protocols, embedded firmware, and real-time operating system (RTOS) services that permit heterogeneous electronic control units (ECUs) inside an electric or hybrid powertrain to sense, compute, actuate, and communicate deterministically within stringent automotive timing budgets (typically 1 ms – 100 ms loops).*

### 1.2 Constituent Subsystems in an EHV

| Subsystem | Hardware Element | Software Element | Interface Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy Storage | Li-ion pack, contactors, current shunt | State-of-Charge (SOC) algorithms | CAN / SPI to VCU |
| Traction Motor | 3-phase PMSM, resolver, inverter | Field-Oriented Control (FOC) code | Analog/Digital + CAN |
| Vehicle Control | 32-bit/64-bit microcontroller, ADC, PWM | RTOS scheduler, control laws | Multi-bus gateway |
| Driver Interface | Accelerator pedal, brake, display | HMI firmware, dashboard logic | LIN / CAN |
| Charger | On-board charger (OBC), DC-DC converter | Charge profile firmware | CAN / PWM pilot |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Automotive Nervous System"

Imagine the EV as a **human body**:

- The **battery pack** is the *heart* — it pumps energy.
- The **VCU** is the *cerebellum* — it coordinates reflexes in real time.
- The **sensors** are the *sensory nerves* (eyes, skin) that detect pedal position, wheel speed, temperature, current, and voltage.
- The **actuators** (inverter, contactor, motor) are the *muscles* that produce motion.
- The **HSI** is the *synapse* — the chemical-electric junction where the nerve (hardware signal) meets the brain's instruction (software command).

Just as a damaged synapse causes uncoordinated motion in a human, a poorly designed HSI causes jerky acceleration, brake interference, communication dropouts, or even unsafe behavior in an EV.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric to Remember:** Automotive-grade ECUs must operate across a **temperature range of -40 °C to +85 °C** (under-hood) and tolerate supply voltage transients from **6 V to 18 V** on a 12 V rail (per ISO 7637-2). Failure to meet these standards is a common KTU exam trap.

## 1.4 Why HSI is the Heart of EHV Design

The HSI must simultaneously satisfy **four contradictory demands**:

1. **Determinism** — braking and torque commands must execute within microseconds.
2. **Robustness** — survive vibration, humidity, salt spray, and EMI.
3. **Safety** — comply with **ISO 26262** (functional safety) at ASIL-C or ASIL-D.
4. **Scalability** — allow new features (regenerative braking, V2G) to be added without redesign.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners often begin questions with the line *"With a neat block diagram, explain the hardware-software interface of an EV powertrain."* Memorize the **VCU ↔ BMS ↔ MCU ↔ HMI** four-node block and you will always score the diagram marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Architecture of an EHV HSI

The interface is best understood as a **four-layer stack**, modelled after the OSI layers but simplified for embedded use.

### Layer 1 — Physical Layer (Hardware Signaling)

- Differential signalling on **CAN-H / CAN-L** at **2.5 V ± 1.0 V** (recessive) and **3.5 V / 1.5 V** (dominant) for classical CAN.
- Terminating resistors of **120 Ω** at each end of the bus.
- Twisted-pair cabling with characteristic impedance of **120 Ω** to suppress common-mode noise.

### Layer 2 — Data Link Layer (Protocol Framing)

- Standard **CAN 2.0B** frames: 11-bit or 29-bit identifier, 0–8 byte payload, **CRC-15** checksum, ACK slot.
- Bus arbitration is **non-destructive, CSMA/CD+AMP** — the lower-ID message wins.
- Bit timing is divided into **Sync, Propagation, Phase 1, Phase 2** segments, summing to the Nominal Bit Time.

### Layer 3 — Application Layer (Software Logic)

- **AUTOSAR** (AUTomotive Open System ARchitecture) standardizes the basic software (BSW) — OS, COM, MEM, ECU abstraction.
- Application software components (SWC) communicate via **Run-Time Environment (RTE)**.
- Real-time scheduling uses **OSEK / VDX** or **POSIX** priorities.

### Layer 4 — Diagnostic & Calibration Layer

- **UDS (Unified Diagnostic Services, ISO 14229)** for fault reading (DTC), clear, programming.
- **CCP / XCP** for measurement and calibration of internal variables via CAN.
- **CAN FD** (Flexible Data-rate, up to 64-byte payload, 5 Mbit/s) supersedes classical CAN in modern EVs.

## 2.2 Key Hardware Building Blocks

### 2.2.1 Signal Conditioning

Raw sensor outputs are rarely directly fed to the MCU. They pass through:

1. **Anti-aliasing filter** — typically a 2nd-order Butterworth with cutoff $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$.
2. **Level shifter** — translates 0–12 V sensor signal to 0–3.3 V ADC range.
3. **Isolation amplifier** — galvanic isolation up to **5 kV** for high-voltage battery monitoring.

### 2.2.2 Power Electronics Interface

- **Gate drivers** (e.g., UCC21750) provide ±15 V to IGBTs/MOSFETs, with **desaturation protection** in < 2 μs.
- **DC-link capacitors** of value chosen by $C = \frac{I_{rms}}{2 \pi f_{sw} \cdot \Delta V_{dc}}$ absorb inverter ripple.

### 2.2.3 Communication Transceivers

- **CAN transceiver** (e.g., TJA1050, TCAN334) converts TTL logic to differential bus levels.
- **Isolation**: digital isolators (ADuM1411) provide **2.5 kV RMS** barrier between ECU and bus.

## 2.3 Key Software Building Blocks

| Software Layer | Function | Typical Tool / API |
|---|---|---|
| Bootloader | Flash programming over UDS | Vector DaVinci, EB tresos |
| RTOS (e.g., FreeRTOS, AUTOSAR OS) | Task scheduling, interrupts | OSEK, POSIX threads |
| Device Drivers | ADC, PWM, SPI, CAN peripherals | HAL (Hardware Abstraction Layer) |
| COM Stack | CAN/CAN-FD framing, PDU routing | AUTOSAR COM, CANopen |
| Application SWC | Torque calculation, SOC estimator | Simulink-generated C code |
| Diagnostic Stack | UDS, DTC, memory | ISO 14229 |
| Calibration (CCP/XCP) | Real-time variable tuning | INCA, CANape |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **high-yield** for KTU Part B numerical or analytical sub-questions. They are standard textbook expressions, not fabricated.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bus Utilization } (U) &= \frac{N \cdot t_{msg}}{T_{cycle}} \leq 0.30 \quad (\text{30% rule for CAN}) \\
\text{CAN Bit Time } t_b &= t_{SYNC} + t_{PROP} + t_{PHS1} + t_{PHS2} \\
\text{Bus Bit Rate } &= \frac{1}{t_b} \\
\text{Bus Length Max } L_{max} &= \frac{v_{prop} \cdot t_{PROP}}{2} \quad \text{(with } v_{prop} \approx 2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s)} \\
\text{Battery SOC (Coulomb Counting)} \quad SOC(t) &= SOC(t_0) - \frac{1}{C_{rated}} \int_{t_0}^{t} I(\tau) \, d\tau \\
\text{Cell Balancing Current} \quad I_{bal} &= \frac{V_{cell} - V_{ref}}{R_{bal}} \\
\text{Thermal Time Constant} \quad \tau_{th} &= R_{th} \cdot C_{th} \\
\text{Gate Drive Power} \quad P_g &= Q_g \cdot V_{gs} \cdot f_{sw} \\
\text{Sampling Rate (Nyquist)} \quad f_s &\geq 2 \cdot f_{max, signal} \\
\text{CPU Utilization Bound (Liu & Layland)} \quad U_{LL} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Writing $\frac{1}{R_{th} \cdot C_{th}}$ for the thermal time constant. The correct relation is $\tau_{th} = R_{th} \cdot C_{th}$ (units: °C/W × J/°C = seconds).

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of HSI Design

A well-engineered HSI:

- Reduces **time-to-market** by 30–40 % via modular AUTOSAR components.
- Enables **over-the-air (OTA)** software updates by standardizing the bootloader-diagnostic interface.
- Facilitates **functional safety** certification (ASIL-D) through deterministic scheduling.
- Lowers **wiring harness mass** by up to 20 % when CAN-FD replaces point-to-point analog links.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — CAN Bus Maximum Length vs. Bit Rate

Classical CAN 2.0B is a synchronous serial bus. The propagation time $t_{prop}$ along the cable must be less than half a bit time to allow clean arbitration.

**Given:**
- Bit rate $R_b$ (bit/s)
- Propagation velocity along twisted pair $v_{prop} \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s (≈ 2/3 the speed of light)
- Safety margin: $t_{prop} \leq t_b / 2$

**Step 1:** Bit time is the inverse of bit rate.
$$
t_b = \frac{1}{R_b}
$$

**Step 2:** Propagational delay across cable of length $L$ is
$$
t_{prop} = \frac{L}{v_{prop}}
$$

**Step 3:** Applying the arbitration constraint $t_{prop} \leq t_b / 2$,
$$
\frac{L}{v_{prop}} \leq \frac{1}{2 R_b}
$$

**Step 4:** Solving for $L$,
$$
L \leq \frac{v_{prop}}{2 R_b}
$$

**Step 5:** Substituting numerical values for $v_{prop} = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s:
$$
L_{max} \, [\text{m}] \leq \frac{1 \times 10^8}{R_b}
$$

**Result table:**

| Bit Rate $R_b$ | $t_b$ | $L_{max}$ (theoretical) | Practical $L_{max}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 Mbit/s | 1 μs | 50 m | 25 m |
| 500 kbit/s | 2 μs | 100 m | 50 m |
| 250 kbit/s | 4 μs | 200 m | 100 m |
| 125 kbit/s | 8 μs | 400 m | 250 m |

## 3.2 Derivation — SOC by Coulomb Counting (with Efficiency)

The State of Charge (SOC) is the ratio of remaining charge to rated capacity. In practice, charging/discharging is not 100 % efficient due to internal resistance and side reactions.

**Starting Definition:**
$$
SOC(t) = SOC(t_0) + \frac{1}{C_{rated}} \int_{t_0}^{t} \eta \cdot I(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

where $\eta$ is the Coulombic efficiency (≈ 1 for discharge, < 1 for charge at high C-rate).

**Step 1 — Discretize** for digital implementation with sampling period $\Delta t$:
$$
SOC[k+1] = SOC[k] + \frac{\eta \cdot I[k] \cdot \Delta t}{C_{rated}}
$$

**Step 2 — Define units carefully.** $C_{rated}$ in Ampere-hours, $I$ in Amperes, $\Delta t$ in hours.

**Step 3 — Numerical Example.** A 50 Ah battery starts at 80 % SOC. Discharge at 25 A for 0.5 h with $\eta = 1$.

$$
\Delta SOC = \frac{1 \cdot 25 \cdot 0.5}{50} = 0.25 = 25 \%
$$
$$
SOC_{final} = 80\% - 25\% = 55\%
$$

**Step 4 — Error Analysis.** Coulomb counting suffers from **current-sensor offset** and **integration drift**. A typical approach pairs it with **Open-Circuit Voltage (OCV)** correction at rest:
$$
SOC_{corrected} = SOC_{coulomb} + K_p \cdot (OCV_{measured} - OCV_{model})
$$

This forms a **PI estimator** of the SOC, often implemented in the BMS software layer.

## 3.3 Derivation — RTOS Schedulability (Liu & Layland Bound)

For $n$ periodic tasks with worst-case execution time $C_i$ and period $T_i$, the Liu–Layland necessary and sufficient condition for Rate Monotonic Scheduling is:

$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)
$$

**Step 1:** Compute the bound for $n = 3$:
$$
U_{max} = 3 (2^{1/3} - 1) = 3 (1.2599 - 1) = 0.7798
$$

**Step 2:** Practical schedulability threshold is **~70 %** (asymptotic bound $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$).

**Step 3:** Suppose we have three EHV tasks:

| Task | Function | $C_i$ | $T_i$ | $C_i / T_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | Torque loop (motor control) | 0.2 ms | 1 ms | 0.200 |
| $\tau_2$ | CAN message transmit | 0.5 ms | 5 ms | 0.100 |
| $\tau_3$ | BMS update | 1.0 ms | 10 ms | 0.100 |
| **Total** |  |  |  | **0.400** |

Since $0.400 \leq 0.7798$, the task set is **schedulable** with 38 % margin.

## 3.4 Derivation — Anti-Aliasing Filter Design

A resolver produces a sinusoidal signal at carrier frequency $f_c = 10$ kHz. The MCU's ADC samples it at $f_s$. The signal's maximum frequency content is $f_{sig, max} = 2$ kHz.

**Step 1:** Apply Nyquist: $f_s \geq 2 \cdot 2000 = 4000$ Hz. Choose $f_s = 20$ kHz.

**Step 2:** Choose filter cutoff $f_{co}$ slightly below $f_s/2 = 10$ kHz, e.g., $f_{co} = 8$ kHz.

**Step 3:** 2nd-order Butterworth RC topology:
$$
f_{co} = \frac{1}{2 \pi R C}
$$

Choose $C = 10$ nF, then:
$$
R = \frac{1}{2 \pi (8000)(10 \times 10^{-9})} \approx 1989 \, \Omega \approx 2 \, \text{k}\Omega
$$

**Step 4:** Verify attenuation at Nyquist:
$$
A_{dB} = 10 \log_{10} \left( 1 + \left( \frac{f_s/2}{f_{co}} \right)^4 \right) = 10 \log_{10} (1 + 1.25) \approx 2.2 \, \text{dB}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Real systems use **oversampling** ($f_s \gg f_{sig,max}$) and digital decimation filtering, which relaxes the analog anti-aliasing requirement and is a common KTU analytical sub-question.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common HSI Challenges and Resolutions

| # | Challenge | Root Cause | Hardware Countermeasure | Software Countermeasure |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | CAN bus-off error | Dominant bit stuck low | Add TVS diode, twisted pair, common-mode choke | Implement CAN error counters & auto-recovery |
| 2 | Torque ripple at low speed | Inverter dead-time, cogging | Increase PWM resolution (12-bit) | Use space-vector modulation, FOC |
| 3 | SOC drift over time | Current sensor offset | Use Hall-effect + shunt hybrid | Combine coulomb counting with OCV correction |
| 4 | EMI-induced ADC glitch | Switching converter noise | Add RC filter, ground plane, ferrite bead | Apply median filter to ADC samples |
| 5 | Bootloader brick | Power loss during flash | Add external watchdog + dual-bank flash | Implement A/B partition + CRC verification |
| 6 | Latency in braking | CAN backlog, OS jitter | Use CAN-FD or FlexRay | Use priority-inheritance mutex, pre-emption |
| 7 | Thermal runaway in pack | Cell imbalance | Add fuse, vent, thermistor per cell | Run thermal model + active balancing |
| 8 | Ground shift | 12 V ↔ 400 V common ground | Galvanic isolation (ISO 1050) | Software debounce on differential signals |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 EHV Powertrain Hardware-Software Interface — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DRIVER["DRIVER INTERFACE LAYER"]
        Pedal["Accelerator Pedal Sensor"]
        Brake["Brake Pressure Sensor"]
        Switch["Mode Selector Switch"]
    end

    subgraph VCU["VEHICLE CONTROL UNIT"]
        MPU["32-bit MCU - e.g. S32K3"]
        RTOS["AUTOSAR OS / RTOS Kernel"]
        SWC["Application Software Components"]
    end

    subgraph COMM["COMMUNICATION BUS LAYER"]
        CAN1["CAN-FD Bus A - Powertrain"]
        CAN2["CAN-FD Bus B - Chassis"]
        ETH["Automotive Ethernet 100BASE-T1"]
        LIN["LIN Bus - Body"]
    end

    subgraph POWERTRAIN["POWERTRAIN ACTUATORS"]
        BMS["Battery Management System"]
        MCU["Motor Control Unit - Inverter"]
        DC["DC-DC Converter"]
        OBC["On-Board Charger"]
    end

    subgraph SENSE["SENSOR NETWORK"]
        Curr["Current Shunt / Hall Sensor"]
        Volt["Voltage Divider Network"]
        Temp["NTC Thermistor Array"]
        Res["Resolver / Encoder"]
    end

    Pedal -->|Analog 0-5V| MPU
    Brake -->|Analog 0-5V| MPU
    Switch -->|Digital GPIO| MPU

    MPU -->|PWM gate signals| MCU
    MPU -->|Contactor coil drive| BMS
    MPU -->|Enable / disable| DC
    MPU -->|CAN-FD messages| CAN1
    MPU -->|CAN-FD messages| CAN2

    CAN1 --> BMS
    CAN1 --> MCU
    CAN1 --> OBC
    CAN2 --> Brake["Brake-by-Wire ECU"]
    ETH --> ADAS["ADAS Domain Controller"]
    LIN --> BCM["Body Control Module"]

    Curr -->|4-20 mA| BMS
    Volt -->|0-5 V scaled| BMS
    Temp -->|Resistance| BMS
    Res -->|Sin/Cos differential| MCU

    BMS -->|SOC, SOH, faults| CAN1
    MCU -->|Speed, torque, temp| CAN1

    RTOS --> MPU
    SWC --> RTOS
```

## 4.2 AUTOSAR Layered Software Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph APP["APPLICATION LAYER"]
        SWC1["SWC - Torque Manager"]
        SWC2["SWC - SOC Estimator"]
        SWC3["SWC - Thermal Manager"]
    end

    subgraph RTE["RUNTIME ENVIRONMENT RTE"]
        RTE1["Sender-Receiver Interface"]
        RTE2["Client-Server Interface"]
    end

    subgraph BSW["BASIC SOFTWARE BSW"]
        COM["COM Stack - CAN / CAN-FD"]
        OS["AUTOSAR OS - OSEK"]
        MCAL["MCAL - ADC, PWM, GPT, SPI"]
        ECU["ECU Abstraction Layer"]
        CD["Complex Drivers"]
        MEM["Memory Services - NvM"]
        DCM["Diagnostic - UDS / DEM"]
    end

    SWC1 --> RTE1
    SWC2 --> RTE1
    SWC3 --> RTE2
    RTE1 --> COM
    RTE2 --> MEM
    COM --> ECU
    OS --> MCAL
    ECU --> MCAL
    CD --> ECU
    MEM --> DCM
```

## 4.3 CAN Bus Topology with Termination

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ECU1["VCU Node\n120 ohm internal"] --- BUS["Twisted Pair Bus Line\n120 ohm characteristic impedance"]
    ECU2["BMS Node"] --- BUS
    ECU3["MCU Node"] --- BUS
    ECU4["HMI / Cluster Node\n120 ohm internal"] --- BUS

    BUS_TERMINATION_LEFT["Termination Resistor\nRT = 120 ohm"] --- BUS
    BUS_TERMINATION_RIGHT["Termination Resistor\nRT = 120 ohm"] --- BUS
```

## 4.4 Hardware-Software Interface Implementation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    REQ["System Requirements\nASIL-C or ASIL-D"] --> SPEC["Interface Specification\n- Signals\n- Timing\n- Safety Goals"]
    SPEC --> HW_DESIGN["Hardware Design\n- Schematic\n- PCB Layout\n- EMC"]
    SPEC --> SW_DESIGN["Software Design\n- Architecture\n- Modules\n- APIs"]
    HW_DESIGN --> HW_TEST["HIL Testing\nPower-on, Burn-in"]
    SW_DESIGN --> UNIT_TEST["Unit Testing"]
    UNIT_TEST --> SW_INT["Software Integration"]
    HW_TEST --> SYS_INT["System Integration"]
    SW_INT --> SYS_INT
    SYS_INT --> VAL["Validation\n- Vehicle dyno\n- Track tests"]
    VAL --> PROD["Production Sign-off"]
```

## 4.5 Battery Management System HSI — Cell Monitoring IC Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CELL1["Cell 1\n3.7 V"] --> AFE1["AFE Chip 1\n- Voltage ADC\n- Coulomb Counter"]
    CELL2["Cell 2\n3.7 V"] --> AFE1
    CELL3["Cell 3\n3.7 V"] --> AFE1
    CELL4["Cell 4\n3.7 V"] --> AFE2["AFE Chip 2\n- Voltage ADC\n- Coulomb Counter"]
    CELL5["Cell 5\n3.7 V"] --> AFE2
    CELL6["Cell 6\n3.7 V"] --> AFE2

    AFE1 -->|SPI Daisy Chain| AFE2
    AFE2 -->|SPI to Isolator| ISO["Digital Isolator\n2.5 kV"]
    ISO -->|SPI 4-wire| BMS_MCU["BMS Microcontroller\n- SOC algorithm\n- Balancing logic\n- CAN interface"]
    BMS_MCU -->|CAN-FD| VCU_BUS["Vehicle CAN Bus"]

    TEMP["NTC Thermistor per cell"] --> AFE1
    TEMP2["NTC Thermistor per cell"] --> AFE2

    SHUNT["Current Shunt\nin pack return path"] --> AFE1
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Define Hardware-Software Interface in the context of an Electric Vehicle powertrain. List any two physical-layer communication standards used.**

**Model Answer:**
The Hardware-Software Interface (HSI) in an EV powertrain is the set of electrical, electronic, and logical boundaries through which ECUs (VCU, BMS, MCU) exchange signals and data with sensors, actuators, and other controllers.

Two physical-layer standards used are:
1. **CAN 2.0B / CAN-FD** (ISO 11898) — differential, twisted-pair, 120 Ω terminated, up to 1 Mbit/s (classical) or 5 Mbit/s (CAN-FD).
2. **LIN (Local Interconnect Network)** — single-wire UART-based, up to 19.2 kbit/s, used for body and comfort electronics (e.g., HMI, mirrors).

*(Acceptable alternatives: FlexRay, Automotive Ethernet 100BASE-T1, SPI, I²C.)* **[3 marks]**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: What is the "30 % rule" for CAN bus utilization? Why is it followed in EV powertrain design?**

**Model Answer:**
The **30 % rule** states that the bus utilization (total time occupied by all messages in one cycle) should not exceed **30 %** of the total cycle time, leaving **70 %** margin for retries, error frames, and bus-off recovery.

In EV powertrain design, this rule ensures:
- Sufficient bandwidth for **diagnostic messages** (UDS) during runtime.
- Time for **error recovery** when a dominant bit error forces retransmission.
- Headroom for **adding new ECUs** (e.g., telematics, ADAS) without redesigning the network.
- Predictable **worst-case latency** for safety-critical messages like brake and torque commands. **[3 marks]**

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the hardware-software interface architecture of an Electric Vehicle powertrain. Discuss in detail the implementation challenges related to (a) electromagnetic compatibility (EMC) and (b) real-time software scheduling, suggesting suitable remedies. [14 marks]**

#### (a) Block Diagram & Architecture — 7 marks

The EHV powertrain HSI is organized as **four inter-connected layers** (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):

1. **Sensor Layer** — accelerator pedal (dual-redundant potentiometers/Hall), resolver on motor, current shunt, NTC thermistors, isolation voltage monitors.
2. **ECU Layer** — VCU (master), BMS, MCU, OBC, DC-DC converter. Each ECU has a 32-bit automotive MCU (e.g., NXP S32K3, TI TMS570, Infineon AURIX).
3. **Communication Layer** — CAN-FD backbone split into **powertrain CAN** (500 kbit/s) and **chassis CAN** (500 kbit/s), with a central gateway; LIN for body, Ethernet for ADAS.
4. **Actuator Layer** — 3-phase inverter driving PMSM, contactors in HV battery, brake-by-wire actuator, dashboard HMI.

**Hardware flow:** Sensors → ADC → MCU → FOC/SOC algorithm → PWM/CAN output → Inverter/Motor/Contactor.

**Software flow:** Bootloader → AUTOSAR OS → RTE → Application SWCs (Torque Manager, SOC Estimator, Thermal Manager, Energy Management) → BSW (COM, MCAL) → Peripherals.

#### (b) Implementation Challenges & Remedies — 7 marks

**Challenge 1 — EMC/EMI:**
- High $di/dt$ (e.g., 1000 A/μs) from the inverter switching at 10–20 kHz creates radiated and conducted emissions that corrupt analog sensor signals.
- *Remedies:* Add common-mode chokes on CAN lines; use shielded twisted-pair; separate analog and digital grounds with a single-point connection; place RC snubbers across MOSFETs; comply with **CISPR 25** (vehicle-component EMI) and **ISO 7637-2** (transient immunity).

**Challenge 2 — Real-Time Scheduling:**
- Multiple tasks (torque loop at 1 ms, CAN transmit at 5 ms, BMS at 100 ms) compete for CPU; missed deadlines cause unsafe torque commands.
- *Remedies:* Use a **pre-emptive RTOS** (AUTOSAR OS or FreeRTOS) with **Rate Monotonic Scheduling**; verify schedulability using the **Liu–Layland bound** $U \leq n(2^{1/n}-1)$; assign the highest priority to the torque loop; use priority-inheritance mutexes to prevent priority inversion; isolate interrupt service routines (ISRs) and keep them under 10 % of CPU.

**Conclusion:** A robust EHV HSI demands co-design of hardware (signal integrity, isolation, EMC) and software (deterministic scheduling, modular AUTOSAR) from day one, validated by HIL testing and ISO 26262 ASIL certification.

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 — Alternative]

**Q: Explain the role of the CAN protocol in interfacing ECUs of a hybrid electric vehicle. With relevant formulas, derive the maximum allowable bus length for a 500 kbit/s CAN network. Discuss the implementation challenges of in-vehicle networking and how they are mitigated. [14 marks]**

#### (a) Role of CAN in HEV & Maximum Bus Length Derivation — 7 marks

**Role of CAN:**
- Multi-master serial bus enabling **peer-to-peer** communication between VCU, BMS, MCU, HCU, and HMI without a host.
- **Non-destructive bitwise arbitration** ensures highest-priority message wins without delay.
- Built-in **CRC-15, ACK, and error confinement** ensure data integrity — critical for safety.
- Standard 11-bit or extended 29-bit identifier; payload up to 8 bytes (CAN 2.0B).

**Derivation of Maximum Bus Length:**

*Given:* Bit rate $R_b = 500$ kbit/s, propagation velocity $v_{prop} = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s.

*Step 1:* Bit time $t_b = 1 / R_b = 1 / 500\,000 = 2 \, \mu s$.

*Step 2:* Arbitration constraint — propagation time must be less than half a bit time:
$$
t_{prop} \leq \frac{t_b}{2} = 1 \, \mu s
$$

*Step 3:* Substituting $t_{prop} = L / v_{prop}$,
$$
\frac{L}{2 \times 10^8} \leq 1 \times 10^{-6}
$$

*Step 4:* Solving,
$$
L \leq 200 \, \text{m} \quad \text{(theoretical)}
$$

*Step 5:* Apply a practical derating factor of 0.5 to account for connectors, stubs, and safety margin:
$$
L_{practical} \leq 100 \, \text{m}
$$

This matches the ISO 11898 recommendation for 500 kbit/s. **[7 marks]**

#### (b) Implementation Challenges & Mitigations — 7 marks

1. **Bus-off & Error Frames:** A faulty node can flood the bus. *Mitigation:* Implement error-passive recovery with auto-reconnect after 128 occurrences of 11 recessive bits.
2. **Latency under high load:** With 30+ ECUs, message backlog can exceed 10 ms. *Mitigation:* Migrate to CAN-FD (64-byte payload, 5 Mbit/s) or segment into multiple sub-networks.
3. **Cable harness cost & weight:** 2–4 km of wiring in a modern car. *Mitigation:* Use **Automotive Ethernet** (100BASE-T1, 100 Mbit/s over single twisted pair) for ADAS and infotainment.
4. **Security:** CAN frames are not encrypted. *Mitigation:* Use **SecOC** (AUTOSAR Secure Onboard Communication) with MAC and freshness counter.
5. **EMI on CAN lines:** Inverter switching injects common-mode noise. *Mitigation:* Twisted pair, 120 Ω termination at both ends, common-mode choke, and shielded cable for HV areas.
6. **Clock drift across ECUs:** Each node has its own oscillator. *Mitigation:* Use **Sync** segment in bit timing (typically 1 TQ) to resynchronize on every falling edge of the bus. **[7 marks]**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse** $v_{prop} = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s with the speed of light ($3 \times 10^8$ m/s). The factor 2/3 accounts for the velocity factor in polyethylene-insulated twisted pair.
> 2. **Do not forget** the 0.5 derating factor when quoting "practical" bus length — many students lose 1 mark by stating 200 m for 500 kbit/s.
> 3. **Always draw the diagram** even if the question doesn't ask for one. A clean VCU ↔ BMS ↔ MCU ↔ HMI block diagram fetches 2–3 easy marks.
> 4. **For SOC questions**, mention the dual approach (coulomb counting + OCV correction) — full marks require both methods, not just one.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pin this to your wall before the exam!**

### ⚡ Hardware Layer
- [ ] Classical CAN: 1 Mbit/s max, 8-byte payload, 120 Ω termination at both ends.
- [ ] CAN-FD: 5 Mbit/s, 64-byte payload, 11-bit or 29-bit ID, single-wire not allowed.
- [ ] **30 % bus utilization rule** — leaves 70 % margin for safety, retries, and future expansion.
- [ ] **Maximum bus length at 500 kbit/s ≈ 100 m** (practical); at 1 Mbit/s ≈ 25 m.
- [ ] **Twisted-pair characteristic impedance = 120 Ω**; **propagation velocity ≈ 2 × 10⁸ m/s**.
- [ ] **Differential voltage levels:** recessive = 2.5 V; dominant = 3.5 V (CAN-H) / 1.5 V (CAN-L).
- [ ] **Galvanic isolation up to 2.5 kV** between HV battery monitoring and 12 V ECU.
- [ ] Automotive temperature range: **-40 °C to +85 °C** (under-hood: -40 °C to +125 °C).
- [ ] **EMC standards:** CISPR 25 (emissions), ISO 7637-2 (transients), ISO 11452 (radiated immunity).

### ⚙️ Software Layer
- [ ] **AUTOSAR** = BSW (Basic Software) + RTE (Run-Time Environment) + ASWC (Application Software Components).
- [ ] **OSEK/VDX OS** supports fixed-priority pre-emptive scheduling.
- [ ] **Liu–Layland schedulability bound** $U \leq n(2^{1/n}-1)$; practical limit ~70 %.
- [ ] **Torque loop is the highest-priority task** (1 ms period in PMSM drives).
- [ ] **UDS (ISO 14229)** is the standard for diagnostics — services 0x19 (ReadDTC), 0x14 (ClearDTC), 0x27 (SecurityAccess).
- [ ] **CCP/XCP** for measurement and calibration of internal ECU variables.
- [ ] **SOC estimation** combines coulomb counting (short-term) with OCV correction (long-term).

### 🛡️ Safety & Standards
- [ ] **ISO 26262** — functional safety; ASIL-D required for traction inverter, ASIL-C for BMS.
- [ ] **ISO 11898** — CAN physical and data link layer.
- [ ] **ISO 14229** — UDS.
- [ ] **AUTOSAR SecOC** — secure onboard communication with MAC.
- [ ] **Bootloader** must support **dual-bank flash** for fail-safe OTA updates.

### 🔑 Top 5 KTU Memory Hooks
1. **"30 – 70 – 30"** → 30 % bus utilization, 70 % margin, 30 °C temp rise allowable per cell.
2. **"120 – 2e8 – 2.5"** → 120 Ω termination, 2 × 10⁸ m/s propagation, 2.5 kV isolation.
3. **"ASIL-D"** → Drive (traction) and Battery = D; Cluster = B; Body = A.
4. **"1 ms – 5 ms – 100 ms"** → Torque / CAN / BMS typical task periods.
5. **"FOC – SOC – SOH"** → Field-Oriented Control, State of Charge, State of Health — the three core algorithms in any EHV.
